title: Definition of superconducting critical temperature onset
content: In the study of lanthanum hydrides under high pressure, the superconducting critical temperature $T_c$ is defined as the onset of superconductivity, which is the specific temperature point where the electrical resistance demonstrates an apparent deviation from the linear temperature dependence characteristic of normal metallic behavior.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: Superconducting critical temperature
- GPa: Gigapascal (unit of pressure)
- Å: Ångström (unit of length, $10^{-10}$ meters)
- $V_H$: Volume occupied by a single hydrogen atom
- $LaH_{10\pm x}$: Lanthanum hydride with stoichiometry close to 10 hydrogen atoms per lanthanum atom
- vibron: Molecular vibrational mode of a gas/solid (e.g., $H_2$ or $D_2$)
- Raman signal: Inelastic scattering of light used to measure pressure-induced shifts in material properties

title: Physical indicators of the superconducting transition
content: A material is identified as having transitioned into a superconducting state if it simultaneously exhibits two characteristic physical properties: the abrupt drop of electrical resistivity $\rho$ to zero (zero electrical resistivity) and the emergence of a large negative molar magnetic susceptibility $\chi_{mol}$ (Meissner effect or large diamagnetic susceptibility).

extra.notations:
- $Pn$: Pnictogen element (e.g., P, As)
- $M$: Transition metal element (e.g., Mn, Fe, Co, Ni)
- $Ln$: Lanthanide element (e.g., La)
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature
- $\rho$: Electrical resistivity
- $\chi_{mol}$: Molar magnetic susceptibility
- XRD: X-ray diffraction
- P4/nmm: A specific tetragonal space group symmetry
- $T_{ann}$: Temperature at which a sudden decrease in resistivity occurs in undoped LaOFeAs
- $T_{min}$: Temperature at which the electrical resistivity reaches a local minimum

title: Structure of Doped Antiferromagnets
content: In the ground state of an antiferromagnetic insulator, the addition of charge carriers results in the formation of domain walls (stripes) across which a $\pi$ phase shift in the spin correlations of the host material is induced. In a two-dimensional system, these domain walls act as metallic rivers of charge characterized by their own low-energy degrees of freedom.

extra.notations:
- CDW: charge-density-wave
- SC: superconducting (or superconductivity)
- $K_c$: Luttinger liquid critical exponent (dimensionless)
- $\Delta_s$: spin gap energy
- $\chi$: susceptibility
- $k_F$: Fermi wavevector
- $Y_j(x)$: transverse displacement of the $j$-th stripe at longitudinal position $x$
- $L_j(x)$: arc length of the $j$-th stripe measured from $0$ to $x$

title: Pressure measurement scale in hydrogen-rich environments
content: In high-pressure experiments involving hydrogen-rich environments within a diamond anvil cell, pressure is determined using two methods: the frequency shift of the molecular vibron of the hydrogen (or deuterium) surrounding the sample, and the edge of the Raman signal from the diamond pressure scale. The hydrogen vibron scale is used as the primary pressure reference because hydrogen acts as a hydrostatic medium, ensuring a uniform and accurate pressure distribution over the sample.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: Superconducting critical temperature
- GPa: Gigapascal (unit of pressure)
- Å: Ångström (unit of length, $10^{-10}$ meters)
- $V_H$: Volume occupied by a single hydrogen atom
- $LaH_{10\pm x}$: Lanthanum hydride with stoichiometry close to 10 hydrogen atoms per lanthanum atom
- vibron: Molecular vibrational mode of a gas/solid (e.g., $H_2$ or $D_2$)
- Raman signal: Inelastic scattering of light used to measure pressure-induced shifts in material properties

title: Stoichiometry estimation of metal hydrides via volume additivity
content: The stoichiometry of a lanthanum hydride phase (expressed as $LaH_n$) is estimated using the volume additivity principle, where the total cell volume of the hydride is compared to the volume of the initial lanthanum metal. The difference in volume is attributed to the hydrogen content, calculated using a reference value for the volume occupied by a single hydrogen atom ($V_H$), which is determined to be approximately $1.9$ Å$^3$ at a pressure of 152 GPa based on diffraction studies of $LaH_3$.

title: Reported resistance anomalies and Tc trends in prior lanthanum superhydride research
content: Prior experimental studies on lanthanum superhydrides reported a significant decrease in electrical resistance at approximately 260 K during cooling and 248 K during heating for samples assigned the stoichiometry $LaH_{10\pm x}$. These studies observed resistance anomalies at temperatures as high as 275 K and proposed that the superconducting critical temperature $T_c$ increases continuously up to approximately 275 K as pressure is increased toward 202 GPa.

title: Pressure dependence of Tc in LaH10
content: The pressure dependence of $T_c$ in $LaH_{10}$ exhibits a 'dome'-like trend: $T_c$ increases to a maximum value of 250–252 K at approximately 170 GPa and decreases abruptly at higher pressures (up to 202 GPa), contrary to prior claims of a continuous increase up to 275 K.

title: Mechanisms of critical temperature reduction by magnetic fields in type-II superconductors
content: In type-II superconductors, the application of an external magnetic field causes a reduction in the superconducting critical temperature $T_{\mathrm{c}}$ through two primary physical mechanisms: the orbital effect, which couples to the motion of the charge carriers, and the Zeeman effect, which breaks the spin-singlet configuration of the Cooper pairs.

extra.notations:
- $T_{\mathrm{c}}$: critical temperature, the temperature below which a material becomes superconducting
- $H_{\mathrm{c2}}$: upper critical magnetic field
- $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(T)$: upper critical magnetic field as a function of temperature $T$
- $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(0)$: upper critical magnetic field in the limit of zero temperature
- Type-II superconductor: a category of superconductors characterized by the formation of magnetic vortices and having two critical fields ($H_{\mathrm{c1}}$ and $H_{\mathrm{c2}}$)
- Orbital effect: a mechanism in which the Lorentz force acting on the orbital motion of electrons in Cooper pairs leads to the suppression of superconductivity
- Spin-singlet state: a quantum state of a Cooper pair where the two electrons have opposing spins (total spin $S=0$)

title: Ginzburg–Landau model for the temperature dependence of the upper critical field
content: Under the Ginzburg–Landau theory of superconductivity, the relationship between the upper critical magnetic field $H_{\mathrm{c2}}$ and the temperature $T$ is modeled by the expression $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(T)=H_{\mathrm{c2}}(0)\left(1-\left(\frac{T}{T_{\mathrm{c}}}\right)^{2}\right)$, where $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(T)$ is the upper critical field at temperature $T$, $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(0)$ is the extrapolated upper critical field at the zero-temperature limit, and $T_{\mathrm{c}}$ is the critical temperature of the material at zero magnetic field.

extra.notations:
- $T_{\mathrm{c}}$: critical temperature, the temperature below which a material becomes superconducting
- $H_{\mathrm{c2}}$: upper critical magnetic field
- $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(T)$: upper critical magnetic field as a function of temperature $T$
- $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(0)$: upper critical magnetic field in the limit of zero temperature
- Type-II superconductor: a category of superconductors characterized by the formation of magnetic vortices and having two critical fields ($H_{\mathrm{c1}}$ and $H_{\mathrm{c2}}$)
- Orbital effect: a mechanism in which the Lorentz force acting on the orbital motion of electrons in Cooper pairs leads to the suppression of superconductivity
- Spin-singlet state: a quantum state of a Cooper pair where the two electrons have opposing spins (total spin $S=0$)

title: Upper critical magnetic field and coherence length
content: The application of an external magnetic field reduces the superconducting transition temperature of $LaH_{10}$; specifically, a field of $\mu_{0}H = 9$ T shifts the onset by approximately 10 K. Extrapolation using the Ginzburg–Landau expression $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(T)=H_{\mathrm{c2}}(0)\left(1-\left(\frac{T}{T_{\mathrm{c}}}\right)^{2}\right)$ yields an upper critical magnetic field $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(0)$ between 95 T and 136 T, corresponding to Ginzburg–Landau coherence lengths $\xi$ between 1.56 and 1.86 nm.

title: Method of stoichiometry estimation via volume expansion
content: The stoichiometry $x$ of a metal hydride phase $MeH_{x}$ can be estimated using the relation $x = (V_{hydride} - V_{metal}) / V_{H}$, where $V_{hydride}$ is the unit-cell volume of the hydride per formula unit, $V_{metal}$ is the volume of the initial metal atom at the same pressure, and $V_{H}$ is the empirical volume occupied by a single hydrogen atom within the hydride lattice.

- $V_{La}$: Volume occupied by a lanthanum atom at high pressure

- $Fm\overline{3}m$: Space group representing a face-centered cubic (fcc) crystal structure
- GPa: Gigapascal, a unit of pressure

title: Equation of state for Lanthanum
content: The volume occupied by a lanthanum atom ($V_{La}$) at high pressures is determined by the equation of state for lanthanum metal as established in prior literature.

title: General volume of hydrogen in high-pressure hydrides
content: In metal hydrides under high-pressure conditions, the volume contribution of an individual hydrogen atom ($V_{H}$) to the total lattice volume is approximately $2$ Å$^3$.

title: Low-Energy Excitations of the 1D Electron Gas
content: In a one-dimensional electron gas characterized by a spin gap $\Delta_s$, the primary low-energy degrees of freedom are charge-density-wave (CDW) and dual superconducting (SC) fluctuations. For such a system, the susceptibilities for these modes, denoted as $\chi_{\mathrm{CDW}}$ and $\chi_{\mathrm{SC}}$ respectively, diverge as the temperature $T \rightarrow 0$ according to the relations $\chi_{\mathrm{CDW}} \approx \Delta_s T^{-(2-K_c)}$ and $\chi_{\mathrm{SC}} \sim \Delta_s T^{-(2-1/K_c)}$, where $K_c$ is a non-universal critical exponent determined by the interactions, satisfying $0 < K_c < 1$ for repulsive interactions.

extra.notations:
- CDW: charge-density-wave
- SC: superconducting (or superconductivity)

title: Phase stability of Lanthanum hydrides
content: In the lanthanum-hydrogen system at pressures exceeding 11 GPa, the compound $LaH_{2}$ is thermodynamically unstable and undergoes phase separation into $LaH_{3}$ and $LaH_{x}$ (where $x$ is approximately $0.25$ or ranges from $0.6$ to $0.7$), rendering the $LaH_{2}$ stoichiometry non-existent in this high-pressure regime.

- $V_{hydride}$: Unit-cell volume of the lanthanum hydride phase
- $V_{metal}$: Unit-cell volume of the initial lanthanum metal at the same pressure
- $V_{H}$: Volume occupied by a single hydrogen atom in the hydride lattice
- $V_{La}$: Volume occupied by a lanthanum atom at high pressure
- $LaH_{x}$: General formula for lanthanum hydride with stoichiometry $x$
- $Fm\overline{3}m$: Space group representing a face-centered cubic (fcc) crystal structure
- GPa: Gigapascal, a unit of pressure

title: Theoretical prediction of LaH10 structure
content: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations predict that at megabar pressures, a superhydride with the stoichiometry $LaH_{10}$ is stable and exhibits superconductivity; this phase is predicted to possess a clathrate-like structure characterized by the $Fm\overline{3}m$ (face-centered cubic) space group.

title: Crystallographic characterization of the superconducting phase
content: The superconducting phase with $T_c \approx 250$ K at 150 GPa is identified as having a face-centred cubic (fcc) lattice structure with space group $Fm\overline{3}m$. The refined lattice parameter is $a = 5.1019(5)$ Å (cell volume $V = 132.80(4)$ Å$^3$), which corresponds to a stoichiometry of $LaH_{9.6}$ (consistent with the predicted $LaH_{10}$) based on the experimental volume expansion relative to lanthanum metal.

title: Isotope effect principle
content: In a superconducting material, a shift in the critical temperature $T_{c}$ resulting from the substitution of hydrogen atoms ($^{1}$H) with deuterium atoms ($^{2}$H) within the crystal lattice provides direct evidence that the superconducting pairing mechanism is mediated by phonons, as described in the context of the Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer and Migdal–Eliashberg theories.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting critical temperature
- $H$: Substitution of hydrogen ($^{1}$H)
- $D$: Substitution of deuterium ($^{2}$H)
- $\alpha$: Isotope coefficient
- $m$: Isotopic mass
- $A$: A constant of proportionality
- BCS: Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer theory of superconductivity

title: Isotope effect equation
content: The relationship between the superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ and the mass $m$ of the substituted isotope is characterized by the isotope coefficient $\alpha$, defined by the power-law equation $T_{c} = A m^{-\alpha}$, where $A$ is a constant of proportionality and $m$ is the mass of the isotope (e.g., hydrogen or deuterium).

- $m$: Isotopic mass
- $A$: A constant of proportionality
- BCS: Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer theory of superconductivity

title: BCS isotope coefficient
content: According to the standard Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer (BCS) theory for conventional phonon-mediated superconductivity, the isotope coefficient $\alpha$ (defined by the relation $T_{c} \propto m^{-\alpha}$) has a theoretical value of approximately 0.5.

- $A$: A constant of proportionality

title: Isotope effect coefficient
content: By synthesizing the isotope analogue fcc-$LaD_{10}$ ($T_c \approx 180$ K at 150 GPa) and comparing it to fcc-$LaH_{10}$ ($T_c \approx 249$ K at 150 GPa), the isotope coefficient $\alpha$ is determined to be 0.46 using the relation $T_{c} \propto m^{-\alpha}$. This value is close to the standard BCS value of 0.5, supporting a conventional phonon-mediated pairing mechanism.

title: Theoretical prediction of LaH11 structure
content: A theoretical study utilizing first-principles calculations predicts that the rare-earth hydride $LaH_{11}$ possesses a hydrogen clathrate structure characterized by a tetragonally distorted face-centred cubic (fcc) lattice.

title: Theoretical stability of lower lanthanum hydrides
content: First-principles density functional theory calculations indicate that under a pressure of approximately $P = 150$ GPa, the stable metallic lanthanum hydride compounds include $LaH_4$, $LaH_6$, and $LaH_8$.

title: Theoretical Tc values for lower lanthanum hydrides
content: Theoretical calculations based on Migdal-Eliashberg theory predict the following superconducting critical temperatures ($T_c$) for various lanthanum hydride stoichiometries: for $LaH_4$, $T_c$ is 5–10 K at 300 GPa; for $LaH_6$, $T_c$ is 150–160 K at 100 GPa; and for $LaH_8$, $T_c$ is 114–150 K at 300 GPa.

title: Charge Density on Fluctuating Stripes
content: For an array of one-dimensional stripes where $Y_j(x)$ denotes the transverse displacement in the $y$-direction of the $j$-th stripe at position $x$, the local charge density $\rho_j(x)$ is a function of the fluctuating geometry defined by the arc length $L_j(x)$. The density is given by $\rho_j(x) = \bar{\rho} + \rho_0 \cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_j + 2k_F L_j(x)]$, where $\phi_j$ is the phase of the charge-density-wave (CDW), $k_F$ is the Fermi wavevector, and the arc length is defined as $L_j(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \mathrm{d}x^{\prime} \sqrt{1 + (\partial_{x^{\prime}} Y_j)^2} + L_j(0)$.

title: Stoichiometry estimation method
content: The stoichiometry $n$ of a hydride phase $LaH_n$ is estimated by calculating the difference between the experimental unit-cell volume per formula unit of the hydride and the volume of the lanthanum atom ($V_{La}$) at the same pressure, then dividing this volume difference by the volume occupied by a single hydrogen atom ($V_H$).

title: Isotope effect on crystal stability
content: In hydride systems, the substitution of hydrogen with deuterium alters the zero-point energy and the magnitude of anharmonic effects; these changes can influence the thermodynamic stability of specific crystal phases, potentially resulting in different stable crystal structures or phase sequences for deuterides compared to their corresponding hydrides.

title: Constraint on maximum stoichiometry of cubic LaH3
content: In the lanthanum-hydrogen system under high pressure, a cubic phase cannot have a stoichiometry higher than 1:3 (e.g., $LaH_4$) while maintaining the $LaH_3$ lattice parameters, because the volume occupied by a single hydrogen atom ($V_H$) is established to be approximately 2 Å$^3$ in metallic hydrides at megabar pressures.

title: Identification of LaD11 and lower-Tc phases
content: The authors identify a tetragonally distorted fcc phase with space group $P4/nmm$ and stoichiometry $LaD_{11}$ (lattice parameters $a = 3.7258(6)$ Å, $c = 5.0953(12)$ Å at 142 GPa) which is metastable in the La-H system but formed in the La-D system, exhibiting a lower superconducting critical temperature of $T_c \approx 140–168$ K. Additionally, superconducting transitions at $T_c \approx 110$ K and 70 K are observed in hydrogen-deficient samples, attributed to lower hydride phases (likely $LaH_6$ or $LaH_8$).

title: Structure and Electronic Configuration of Infinite-Layer Nickelates
content: Infinite-layer nickelates (such as $LaNiO_{2}$ and $NdNiO_{2}$) possess a crystal structure isostructural to infinite-layer copper oxides. In this arrangement, nickel cations are located in two-dimensional $NiO_{2}$ planes with square planar oxygen coordination, where these planes alternate with planes of rare-earth elements (e.g., $La$ or $Nd$). This structure is characterized by a formal nickel valence of $Ni^{+}$ and a predicted $d^{9}$ electronic configuration, resulting in a single hole in the $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ orbital, which is formally analogous to the $Cu^{2+}$ ($d^{9}$) configuration found in undoped copper oxides.

title: Topotactic Reduction Synthesis Strategy
content: The monovalent nickel state $Ni^{+}$ (with a $d^{9}$ electronic configuration) is not achievable through conventional high-temperature synthesis. It is synthesized via the low-temperature reduction of a perovskite precursor phase (specifically $LaNiO_{3}$ or $NdNiO_{3}$) using $CaH_{2}$ powder as a reducing agent. This process induces a topochemical reaction involving oxygen deintercalation, which removes apical oxygen atoms to transform the perovskite structure into the infinite-layer phase ($LaNiO_{2}$ or $NdNiO_{2}$).

title: Linearized Ginzburg-Landau Equation for Upper Critical Field
content: In the vicinity of the superconducting transition temperature $T_{\mathrm{c}}$, the temperature dependence of the upper critical magnetic field oriented perpendicular to the $NiO_{2}$ planes, denoted as $H_{\mathrm{c},\perp}(T)$, is defined by the linearized Ginzburg–Landau relation: $H_{\mathrm{c},\perp}(T)=\frac{\varPhi_{0}}{2\pi\xi_{\mathrm{GL}}^{2}(0)}\left(1-\frac{T}{T_{\mathrm{c}}}\right)$, where $\varPhi_{0}$ represents the magnetic flux quantum and $\xi_{\mathrm{GL}}(0)$ represents the extrapolated zero-temperature Ginzburg–Landau coherence length.

title: Signatures of Diamagnetic Screening in Mutual-Inductance Measurements
content: In a two-coil mutual-inductance measurement configuration consisting of a drive coil and a pickup coil, a superconducting transition in a sample is identified by the a.c. voltage signal $V_{\mathrm{p}}$ across the pickup coil. As the sample temperature decreases through the transition temperature, the real component of the voltage, $\mathrm{Re}(V_{\mathrm{p}})$, decreases while the imaginary component, $\mathrm{Im}(V_{\mathrm{p}})$, displays a peak; these changes signify the emergence of a diamagnetic response where the superconducting sample screens the magnetic field generated by the drive coil.

title: Inter-Stripe CDW Coupling
content: The coupling between charge-density-waves (CDWs) on adjacent stripes in a two-dimensional array is described by the Hamiltonian $H_c = \sum_{j} \int \mathrm{d}x V(\Delta_j Y) \cos[\sqrt{2\pi}(\Delta_j \phi) - 2k_F(\Delta_j L)]$. In this expression, $\Delta_j F \equiv F_{j+1} - F_j$ represents the difference of a quantity $F$ between the $(j+1)$-th and $j$-th stripes, $L$ is the arc length defined by the stripe geometry, $\phi$ is the CDW phase, and $V(\Delta_j Y)$ is a coupling function that depends on the local transverse separation $\Delta_j Y = Y_{j+1}(x) - Y_j(x)$.

title: Observation of superconductivity in Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2
content: Infinite-layer nickelate thin films with the composition $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ exhibit a superconducting transition, characterized by a resistive onset at approximately $14.9$ Kelvin, a transition midpoint at $13.6$ Kelvin, and zero resistance at $9.1$ Kelvin.

title: Topochemical reduction of nickelates
content: Low-temperature reduction of the perovskite oxide $LaNiO_{3}$ induces a topochemical reaction to form the infinite-layer phase $LaNiO_{2}$, which stabilizes the nickel ion in the $Ni^{+}$ oxidation state ($d^{9}$ electronic configuration), a state that is not accessible via conventional high-temperature synthesis methods.

title: Substrate templating in topotactic reduction
content: In the synthesis of epitaxial thin films, oxygen deintercalation via topotactic reduction is constrained by the substrate, which acts as a structural template to preserve the single-crystal, $c$-axis-oriented phase of the reduced oxide (such as $LaNiO_{2}$) in the region adjacent to the substrate interface.

title: Bandwidth modulation via ionic radius
content: In rare-earth nickelates of the form $RENiO_{2}$ (where $RE$ is a rare-earth element), the smaller ionic radius of Neodymium ($Nd$) relative to Lanthanum ($La$) results in a reduced unit cell volume, which increases the electronic bandwidth of the system.

title: Bulk NdNiO3 structure
content: The bulk perovskite oxide $NdNiO_{3}$ possesses an orthorhombic crystal structure at room temperature with lattice parameters $a = 5.39\ \AA$, $b = 5.38\ \AA$, and $c = 7.61\ \AA$, corresponding to an effective pseudocubic lattice parameter of approximately $3.81\ \AA$.

title: Structural changes upon reduction in bulk
content: The reduction of bulk undoped perovskite $NdNiO_{3}$ to the infinite-layer phase $NdNiO_{2}$ results in a structural transformation characterized by an expansion of the in-plane lattice constants to approximately $3.92\ \AA$ and a contraction of the $c$-axis to approximately $3.31\ \AA$.

title: Soft-chemistry reduction method
content: A soft-chemistry reduction method involves vacuum-sealing oxide samples with $CaH_{2}$ powder such that there is no direct physical contact; subsequent annealing induces a gas-phase reaction where $CaH_{2}$ acts as a reducing agent to facilitate topotactic oxygen deintercalation from the oxide precursor.

title: Synthesis and structural transformation of doped infinite-layer nickelates
content: Single-crystal thin films of $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ are synthesized via soft-chemistry topotactic reduction of perovskite $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{3}$ precursors using $CaH_2$, resulting in a transformation from the perovskite phase (c-axis lattice constant $3.77\ \AA$) to the infinite-layer phase (c-axis lattice constant $3.37\ \AA$) while maintaining clamping to the $SrTiO_3$ substrate.

title: Synthesis of Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2 via Topotactic Reduction
content: The infinite-layer nickelate $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ is synthesized through a soft-chemistry topotactic reduction process, wherein a single-crystal thin-film perovskite precursor phase, $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{3}$, is reacted with $CaH_{2}$ powder at temperatures between $260^{\circ}C$ and $280^{\circ}C$ in a vacuum-sealed environment to induce oxygen deintercalation.

title: Measurement Geometry for Critical Current Density
content: To evaluate the critical current density $J_{c}$ of $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ thin films, transport measurements are performed using a six-point geometry on a physical bridge (narrow channel) defined by diamond scribing, with a channel width of approximately $0.2\text{ mm}$.

title: Inter-Stripe Josephson Coupling
content: The Josephson tunneling of superconducting pairs between adjacent metallic stripes is characterized by a matrix element $J(\Delta_j Y) \approx J_0 \exp[-\alpha \Delta_j Y]$, where $J_0$ and $\alpha$ are constants and $\Delta_j Y$ is the local transverse spacing between stripes. This coupling is independent of the arc length $L_j$ of the stripes because superconductivity is a $k=0$ order, making it insensitive to the longitudinal geometric fluctuations of the stripes.

title: Characteristic I-V Behavior of Superconductivity
content: In the $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ system, the presence of superconductivity is identified by current–voltage ($I-V$) characteristics that remain linear in the normal state (excluding non-linearities caused by Joule heating at high bias) and exhibit increasing non-linearity as the temperature is lowered below the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$.

title: Critical current density of the superconducting state
content: The superconducting state of $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ displays nonlinear current–voltage characteristics indicative of a critical current density $J_{\mathrm{c}}(2\mathrm{K}) \approx 170 \mathrm{kA} \mathrm{cm}^{-2}$.

title: Linearized Ginzburg–Landau equation for upper critical field
content:
For a superconductor in the vicinity of the transition temperature $T_{\mathrm{c}}$, the temperature dependence of the upper critical field $H_{\mathrm{c},\perp}(T)$ for a magnetic field oriented perpendicular to the superconducting planes is described by the linearized Ginzburg–Landau relation:
\[ H_{\mathrm{c},\perp}(T)=\frac{\varPhi_{0}}{2\pi\xi_{\mathrm{GL}}^{2}(0)}\left(1-\frac{T}{T_{\mathrm{c}}}\right) \]
where $\varPhi_{0}$ is the magnetic flux quantum, $T$ is the absolute temperature, and $\xi_{\mathrm{GL}}(0)$ is the extrapolated zero-temperature Ginzburg–Landau coherence length.

extra.notations:
- $H_{\mathrm{c},\perp}(T)$: Upper critical field for a magnetic field applied perpendicular to the sample plane at temperature $T$.
- $\varPhi_{0}$: Magnetic flux quantum, defined as $h/2e$.
- $\xi_{\mathrm{GL}}(0)$: Extrapolated zero-temperature Ginzburg–Landau coherence length.
- $T_{\mathrm{c}}$: Superconducting transition temperature.
- $V_{\mathrm{p}}$: Alternating current (a.c.) voltage signal detected by a pickup coil in a two-coil mutual-inductance measurement.
- $\mathrm{Re}(V_{\mathrm{p}})$: Real (in-phase) component of the pickup voltage.
- $\mathrm{Im}(V_{\mathrm{p}})$: Imaginary (out-of-phase) component of the pickup voltage.
- $H_{c\perp}$: Upper critical field in the perpendicular geometry.

title: Signatures of diamagnetic response in mutual-inductance measurements
content: In a two-coil mutual-inductance measurement configuration where a sample is placed between a drive coil and a pickup coil, the emergence of a superconducting diamagnetic response during cooling is characterized by a simultaneous decrease in the real component of the pickup voltage $\mathrm{Re}(V_{\mathrm{p}})$ and the appearance of a peak in the imaginary component of the pickup voltage $\mathrm{Im}(V_{\mathrm{p}})$, signifying the screening of the drive coil's magnetic field by the superconducting sample.

title: Assumption: Estimation of upper critical field
content: The upper critical field $H_{c\perp}$ of a superconducting thin film is estimated experimentally by determining the magnetic field at which the electrical resistivity reaches the midpoint of the transition from the superconducting state to the normal state at a given temperature $T$ near the transition temperature $T_{c}$.

content: $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ behaves as a type-II superconductor with an emergent diamagnetic response, where the upper critical field $H_{\mathrm{c},\perp}(T)$ fits a linearized Ginzburg–Landau form yielding an extrapolated zero-temperature coherence length $\xi_{\mathrm{GL}}(0) = 3.25 \pm 0.01$ nm.

title: Band structure of LaNiO2
content: Calculations of the electronic band structure of infinite-layer lanthanum nickelate ($LaNiO_{2}$) indicate the presence of multiple electron and hole pockets characterized by distinct orbital contributions; the specific topology and occupancy of these pockets are found to vary as a function of the strength of the Coulomb interaction $U$ used in the model.

title: Single-band doping expectation
content: In the framework of electronic transport theory, the standard model for simple hole doping of a single electronic band predicts that the Hall coefficient $R_{\mathrm{H}}$ will maintain a consistent sign related to the carrier type; consequently, an experimental observation of a sign change in $R_{\mathrm{H}}$ as a function of temperature—specifically in a regime where the parent (undoped) compound exhibits a negative $R_{\mathrm{H}}$ across all temperatures—is fundamentally inconsistent with the single-band doping expectation.

title: Electronic transport behavior and Fermi surface complexity
content: Unlike the parent compound $NdNiO_{2}$ which has a negative Hall coefficient $R_{\mathrm{H}}$ at all temperatures, the superconducting $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ exhibits a sign change in $R_{\mathrm{H}}(T)$ at approximately $55$ Kelvin, indicating a complex Fermi surface with contributions from multiple electron and hole pockets rather than simple hole doping of a single band.

content: The infinite-layer nickelate compound $LaNiO_{2}$ (lanthanum nickelate), when synthesized in single-crystal thin-film form on a $SrTiO_{3}$ (001) substrate, exhibits weakly insulating temperature-dependent transport behavior.

title: Definitions of Electronic Liquid-Crystal Phases
content:
Electronic phases in a two-dimensional crystal host are classified by the specific spatial symmetries they break: 
1. The Liquid phase breaks no spatial symmetries of the host and acts as a conductor or superconductor.
2. The Nematic phase breaks the rotational symmetry of the lattice while preserving translation and reflection symmetries.
3. The Smectic phase breaks translational symmetry in exactly one direction (commensurate with the lattice) and behaves as an electron liquid in the orthogonal direction.
4. The Crystalline phase breaks translational symmetry in all directions, resulting in an insulating state.

title: Ionic radius comparison of Nd and La
content: The ionic radius of the rare-earth element Neodymium ($Nd$) is smaller than the ionic radius of Lanthanum ($La$), a physical property which results in a smaller unit cell volume for $Nd$-based nickelate compounds (such as $NdNiO_{2}$) compared to their $La$-based structural counterparts (such as $LaNiO_{2}$).

title: Relationship between cell volume and bandwidth in nickelates
content: In trilayer reduced nickelates of the form $R_{4}Ni_{3}O_{8}$ (where $R$ is a rare-earth element), a reduction in the unit cell volume achieved by substituting a larger cation (such as $La$) with a smaller cation (such as $Pr$) is associated with an increase in electronic bandwidth, resulting in a transition from an insulating state in $La_{4}Ni_{3}O_{8}$ to a metallic state in $Pr_{4}Ni_{3}O_{8}$ that persists down to low temperatures.

title: Synthesis of Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2 thin films by CaH2 topotactic reduction
content: Single-crystal thin films of the infinite-layer nickelate Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2 can be obtained by soft-chemistry topotactic reduction of perovskite Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO3 precursors using CaH2; this transformation is accompanied by a reduction of the c-axis lattice constant from about $3.77\ \AA$ (perovskite) to $3.37\ \AA$ (infinite-layer) and preserves epitaxial clamping to a SrTiO$_3$ substrate.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted a general synthesis route for $RENiO_{2}$ with $RE=La$ or $Nd$, which the single child (Node 338) did not entail because the child reports only a specific, Sr-doped $Nd$ example in thin-film form with particular temperature and vacuum conditions. To remove the union error and ensure the parent is entailed by the child, I narrowed the claim to the exact composition and conditions reported by the child: $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ produced from $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{3}$ via $CaH_{2}$ reduction at $260$–$280^\circ\mathrm{C}$ in a vacuum-sealed environment, and explicitly state the oxygen deintercalation mechanism. I removed any universal claim about $La$-based or undoped $RENiO_{2}$ and omitted bulk/thin-film generalizations not supported by the child. This revised parent is self-contained, substantive, and is directly entailed by Child 338.

The original parent overgeneralized beyond the single child: it asserted CaH2 reduction for RENiO2 with RE = La or Nd and implied undoped, bulk RENiO2 synthesis and oxygen deintercalation. Child 337 only documents a Sr-doped, Nd-based thin-film case (Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO3 → Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2) produced by soft-chemistry topotactic reduction with CaH2, reporting the c-axis change and retention of substrate clamping. The revised parent removes unsupported claims about La, undoped bulk RENiO2, and explicit oxygen deintercalation/low-temperature wording, restricting the claim to the Nd0.8Sr0.2 thin-film transformation and the measurable lattice and epitaxial outcomes that the child directly supports. This ensures the parent is entailed by the child while remaining substantive and self-contained.

title: Definition of Enthalpy
content: In the context of thermodynamic stability and phase transitions in crystalline solids, the enthalpy $H$ is defined by the relation $H = E(V) + PV$, where $E(V)$ is the internal energy of the system determined as a function of volume $V$, and $P$ denotes the external pressure.

title: Existence and Nature of Electronic Liquid-Crystal Phases
content: Transverse stripe fluctuations (zero-point energy) in doped antiferromagnets suppress the inter-stripe Charge Density Wave (CDW) coupling via geometric dephasing while simultaneously enhancing the inter-stripe Josephson coupling. This mechanism stabilizes electronic quantum liquid-crystal phases (smectic and nematic) which constitute new states of matter distinct from crystals and Fermi liquids, and which can exhibit high-temperature superconductivity or non-Fermi liquid behavior.

title: Density Functional Theory Methodology
content: First-principles electronic structure calculations are conducted using Density Functional Theory (DFT) as implemented in the Vienna ab initio simulation package, employing the projector augmented-wave (PAW) method and the generalized gradient approximation in the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) form for the exchange-correlation functional.

title: Hubbard U Parameter for Nickelates
content: For the treatment of Ni $3d$ electrons in $La_3Ni_2O_7$ within a DFT+$U$ framework, the effective Hubbard $U$ parameter is set to 4 eV, which is consistent with estimations derived from the linear-response method (5.9 eV) and comparative tests using values of 4 eV, 5 eV, and 6 eV.

title: Discovery of high-temperature superconductivity in La3Ni2O7
content: The authors report the observation of high-temperature superconductivity in single crystals of the Ruddlesden–Popper double-layered perovskite nickelate $La_{3}Ni_{2}O_{7}$ under high pressure. A maximum onset superconducting transition temperature $T_c$ of approximately 80 K is achieved at pressures between 14.0 GPa and 43.5 GPa. This transition is evidenced by a sharp drop in electrical resistance to zero and a diamagnetic response in the a.c. magnetic susceptibility (e.g., below 77 K at 25.2 GPa).

title: Methodological Principle: Structural Determination
content: In the determination of the high-pressure crystal structure of $La_{3}Ni_{2}O_{7}$, the methodology combines Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations with Rietveld refinements of synchrotron X-ray diffraction (XRD) data. This combined approach is necessitated by the physical property that X-ray scattering is not sufficiently sensitive to the specific atomic positions and the stoichiometry of oxygen ions within the $La_{3}Ni_{2}O_{7}$ lattice.

title: Definition of Enthalpy
content: For the purpose of determining the thermodynamic stability of different structural phases (such as the Amam and Fmmm space groups) in $La_{3}Ni_{2}O_{7}$ under compression, the pressure dependence of the enthalpy is defined by the relation $H=E(V)+PV$, where $E(V)$ is the total ground-state energy of the system as a function of the unit cell volume $V$ obtained from first-principles calculations, and $P$ is the applied hydrostatic pressure.

title: DFT Calculation Parameters
content: Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations for $La_{3}Ni_{2}O_{7}$ are implemented using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) utilizing the projector augmented-wave (PAW) method with a plane-wave kinetic energy cut-off of 600 eV. The exchange-correlation potential is treated within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) using the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) functional, and an effective Hubbard $U$ parameter of 4 eV is applied to the Ni $3d$ electrons to account for on-site Coulomb interactions.

title: Local charge density on a fluctuating stripe
content: In a system of quasi-one-dimensional metallic stripes within a Mott insulator, the local charge density $\rho_{j}(x)$ on the $j$-th stripe at longitudinal position $x$ is defined by the relation $\rho_{j}(x)=\bar{\rho}+\rho_{0}\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_{j}(x)+2k_{\mathrm{F}}L_{j}(x)]$, where $\bar{\rho}$ is the mean density, $\rho_{0}$ is the fluctuation amplitude, $\phi_{j}(x)$ is the phase of the Charge Density Wave (CDW), $k_{\mathrm{F}}$ is the Fermi wavevector, and $L_{j}(x)$ is the arc-length measured along the fluctuating path of the stripe.

- $j$: index identifying a specific stripe in a two-dimensional array
- $x$: spatial coordinate along the longitudinal direction of a stripe
- $Y_j(x)$: transverse displacement in the $y$ direction of the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $L_j(x)$: arc-length measured along the $j$-th stripe from an origin to position $x$
- $\phi_j(x)$: phase variable of the Charge Density Wave on the $j$-th stripe
- $\rho_j(x)$: local charge density on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $\bar{\rho}$: mean charge density
- $\rho_0$: amplitude of the charge density fluctuations
- $k_{\mathrm{F}}$: Fermi wavevector
- $\Delta_j F$: difference operator defined as $F_{j+1} - F_{j}$ for any quantity $F$ on adjacent stripes
- $V(\Delta_j Y)$: coupling function between Charge Density Waves on neighboring stripes depending on their local transverse spacing
- $H_c$: inter-stripe coupling Hamiltonian
- $\Delta_s$: spin gap in the excitation spectrum
- $K_c$: non-universal critical exponent for charge fluctuations

title: Murnaghan Equation of State Application
content: The Murnaghan equation of state is employed as the analytical functional form to fit the discrete energy-volume ($E-V$) data points generated by first-principles calculations, allowing for the determination of the equilibrium volume and the calculation of enthalpy $H$ across a continuous range of pressures.

title: Pressure-induced structural transition
content: Synchrotron X-ray diffraction and density functional theory (DFT) calculations establish a structural phase transition in $La_{3}Ni_{2}O_{7}$ from the low-pressure orthorhombic $Amam$ space group to the high-pressure orthorhombic $Fmmm$ space group around 10–15 GPa. This transition is characterized by the straightening of the Ni–O–Ni bond angle between two adjacent octahedra along the $c$ axis from $168.0^{\circ}$ at ambient pressure to $180^{\circ}$ in the high-pressure phase, alongside an abrupt reduction in the inter-atomic distance between Ni and apical oxygen from 2.297 Å to 2.122 Å.

title: Crystal-field splitting principle
content: The electronic structure of the nickelate $La_3Ni_2O_7$ is determined by the crystal-field splitting of the $3d$ orbitals of the Ni cations within $NiO_6$ octahedra, which results in the separation of the states into $e_g$ ($d_{x^2-y^2}$, $d_{z^2}$) and $t_{2g}$ ($d_{xy}$, $d_{yz}$, $d_{xz}$) manifolds.

content: In the high-pressure $Fmmm$ phase of $La_3Ni_2O_7$, the occupied electrons in the Ni $3d_{x^2-y^2}$ orbitals are expected to interact strongly with the O $2p$ orbitals, leading to the formation of intra-layer Zhang–Rice singlets.

title: Metallization and superconductivity link
content: The metallization of inter-layer $\sigma$-bonding bands, which involves high density of states at the Fermi level, is a known mechanism for the emergence of conventional high-$T_c$ superconductivity in materials such as $MgB_2$, $Li_3B_4C_2$, and $H_3S$.

title: Definition of stripe arc-length
content: For a stripe $j$ in a two-dimensional plane whose configuration is described by a transverse displacement $Y_{j}(x)$ relative to the $x$-axis, the arc-length $L_{j}(x)$ along the stripe from the origin to position $x$ is given by the geometric integral $L_{j}(x)=\int_{0}^{x}\mathrm{d}x^{\prime}\sqrt{1+(\partial_{x^{\prime}}Y_{j})^{2}}+L_{j}(0)$, where $\partial_{x^{\prime}}Y_{j}$ denotes the spatial derivative of the transverse displacement with respect to the longitudinal coordinate.

title: Electronic mechanism of superconductivity
content: DFT calculations indicate that the superconductivity in the high-pressure $Fmmm$ phase arises from the metallization of the inter-layer $\sigma$-bonding bands formed by the Ni $3d_{z^2}$ orbitals and apical oxygen $2p_z$ orbitals. Unlike the ambient phase where these states are fully occupied and separated, high pressure induces a lifting of the $3d_{z^2}$ bonding bands across the Fermi level, creating hole pockets. The authors conclude that the electronic structure involves a strong mixing of Ni $3d_{x^2-y^2}$ and $3d_{z^2}$ orbitals with oxygen $2p$ orbitals, resulting in a $3d^{7.5}$ electronic occupancy for $Ni^{2.5+}$ cations that mimics the hole-doped bilayer cuprates.

title: Criterion for upper critical field
content: In the study of the nickelate superconductor $La_{3}Ni_{2}O_{7}$, the upper critical magnetic field $\mu_{0}H_{c2}$ is determined using a criterion defined as $0.9 \times R(T_{\mathrm{c}}^{\mathrm{onset}})$, where $R(T_{\mathrm{c}}^{\mathrm{onset}})$ represents the electrical resistance of the sample measured at the onset temperature of the superconducting transition $T_{\mathrm{c}}^{\mathrm{onset}}$.

title: Ginzburg-Landau model usage
content: The temperature-dependent behavior of the upper critical magnetic field $\mu_{0}H_{c2}(T)$ in pressurized $La_{3}Ni_{2}O_{7}$ is modeled using the Ginzburg–Landau formula, which relates the critical field to the temperature $T$ relative to the superconducting transition temperature $T_c$.

content: A strange metal state in a condensed matter system is characterized by an electrical resistance that exhibits a linear dependence on temperature ($R \propto T$).

title: Superconducting state properties and strange metal behavior
content: The upper critical magnetic field $\mu_{0}H_{c2}(0)$ of the superconducting state is estimated to be as high as 186 T at 18.9 GPa, derived from Ginzburg–Landau fitting of resistance data with the field applied along the $c$ direction. Additionally, the normal state above $T_c$ exhibits strange metal behavior, characterized by a linear temperature dependence of resistance up to 300 K, suggesting the system is in proximity to a quantum critical regime.

title: Thermodynamic Stability Criterion
content: The thermodynamic stability of a stoichiometric compound under external pressure is determined by the minimization of enthalpy ($H = E + PV$); a specific phase is considered stable against decomposition if its enthalpy is lower than the sum of the enthalpies of its potential decomposition products, such as the decomposition of $(\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{S})_{2}\mathrm{H}_{2}$ into $S + 3/2H_2$ or $H_2S + 1/2H_2$.

title: Inter-stripe CDW coupling Hamiltonian
content: In an array of stripes, the coupling Hamiltonian $H_c$ describing the interaction between Charge Density Waves (CDWs) on neighboring stripes $j$ and $j+1$ is given by $H_{c}=\sum_{j}\int\mathrm{d}x V(\Delta_{j}Y)\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}(\Delta_{j}\phi)-2k_{\mathrm{F}}(\Delta_{j}L)]$, where $\Delta_{j}\phi = \phi_{j+1}(x) - \phi_{j}(x)$ is the phase difference, $\Delta_{j}L = L_{j+1}(x) - L_{j}(x)$ is the difference in arc-lengths, and $V(\Delta_{j}Y)$ is a coupling function that depends on the local transverse spacing $\Delta_{j}Y = Y_{j+1}(x) - Y_{j}(x)$ between the stripes.

extra.notations:
- CDW: Charge Density Wave
- $j$: index identifying a specific stripe in a two-dimensional array
- $x$: spatial coordinate along the longitudinal direction of a stripe
- $Y_j(x)$: transverse displacement in the $y$ direction of the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $L_j(x)$: arc-length measured along the $j$-th stripe from an origin to position $x$
- $\phi_j(x)$: phase variable of the Charge Density Wave on the $j$-th stripe
- $\rho_j(x)$: local charge density on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $\bar{\rho}$: mean charge density
- $\rho_0$: amplitude of the charge density fluctuations
- $k_{\mathrm{F}}$: Fermi wavevector
- $\Delta_j F$: difference operator defined as $F_{j+1} - F_{j}$ for any quantity $F$ on adjacent stripes
- $V(\Delta_j Y)$: coupling function between Charge Density Waves on neighboring stripes depending on their local transverse spacing
- $H_c$: inter-stripe coupling Hamiltonian
- $\Delta_s$: spin gap in the excitation spectrum
- $K_c$: non-universal critical exponent for charge fluctuations

title: Dynamical Stability Criterion
content: A crystal structure is defined as dynamically stable at a given pressure if its calculated phonon dispersion curves, which represent the collective vibrational modes of the lattice, exhibit no imaginary frequencies (no negative eigenvalues of the dynamical matrix) throughout the first Brillouin zone.

title: Identification of the low-pressure P1 structure
content: The stable crystal structure of $(\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{S})_{2}\mathrm{H}_{2}$ below 37 GPa is determined to be a triclinic phase with $P1$ symmetry (8 formula units per cell), which consists of an ordered H-bonded $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{S}$ network and anti-parallel hydrogen molecules in interstitial sites. This structure is energetically favorable compared to the experimentally proposed $I4/mcm$ or theoretically suggested $I222$ structures, and its simulated X-ray diffraction patterns, equation of state, and Raman spectra (specifically the $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ vibron-peaks) faithfully reproduce experimental observations.

content: In the comparison of multiple candidate crystal structures for a given chemical composition at a constant pressure $P$ and temperature $T=0$ K, the relative thermodynamic stability is determined by their respective enthalpies $H = E + PV$, where $E$ is the internal energy; the structure possessing the lowest enthalpy is defined as the thermodynamically stable phase under those conditions.

content: A structural phase transition between two crystalline phases is characterized as a first-order phase transition if it exhibits a discontinuous change in the unit cell volume (volume collapse), denoted as $\Delta V \neq 0$, at the transition pressure.

title: Definition of Metallization
content: Metallization of a solid material is defined as the transition from an insulating or semiconducting state to a metallic state, physically manifested by the closure of the electronic band gap and the emergence of a non-zero electronic density of states (DOS) at the Fermi level.

title: High-pressure phase sequence and metallization
content: Upon compression, $(\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{S})_{2}\mathrm{H}_{2}$ is predicted to undergo the following sequence of structural phase transitions: orthorhombic $Cccm$ (stable between 37 and 111 GPa), trigonal $R3m$ (stable between 111 and 180 GPa), and cubic $Im\text{-}3m$ (stable above 180 GPa). The transition to the $R3m$ phase at 111 GPa corresponds to the metallization of the compound (closure of the band gap) and is reconstructive, characterized by the dissociation of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ molecules to form pyramidal $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{S}$ units and a volume collapse of 9.2%.

title: Discovery of superconductivity in F-doped LaOFeAs
content: The layered iron-based compound LaOFeAs undergoes a superconducting transition when doped with $F^{-}$ ions at the $O^{2-}$ site, exhibiting a trapezoidal $T_{c}$ dependence on $F^{-}$ content with a maximum $T_{c}$ of $\sim 26$ K ($T_{onset} > 30$ K) achieved at a doping level of $\sim 11$ atom %.

title: Prediction of high-Tc superconductivity
content:
Using the Allen-Dynes-modified McMillan equation, the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ for the metallic phases is predicted to be high:
(1) For the $Im\text{-}3m$ structure at 200 GPa, $T_{c}$ ranges from 191 K to 204 K (for Coulomb pseudopotential $\mu^{*} = 0.1 - 0.13$), with a calculated electron-phonon coupling parameter $\lambda = 2.19$ and logarithmic average phonon frequency $\omega_{log} = 1334.6$ K.
(2) For the $R3m$ structure at 130 GPa, $T_{c}$ ranges from 155 K to 166 K, with $\lambda = 2.07$ and $\omega_{log} = 1125.1$ K.

title: Dominant role of hydrogen in superconductivity
content: Analysis of the Eliashberg spectral function $\alpha^{2}F(\omega)$ and the integrated electron-phonon coupling parameter $\lambda(\omega)$ indicates that hydrogen vibrations play the dominant role in the superconductivity of the high-pressure phases. In the $Im\text{-}3m$ structure, high-frequency H vibrational modes ($>20$ THz) contribute 82.6% to the total $\lambda$, while in the $R3m$ structure, H vibrations ($>18$ THz) contribute 66.9% to $\lambda$.

title: Assumption: Cu-O Layer Geometry

title: Assumption: Cumulant expansion validity
content: The calculation of the effective interaction between Charge Density Waves (CDWs) on neighboring stripes, mediated by transverse fluctuations, is performed under the assumption that the expansion of the expectation value $\langle V(\Delta Y)\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}(\Delta\phi)-2k_{\mathrm{F}}(\Delta L)]\rangle$ is valid when truncated at the lowest order of a cumulant expansion.

- $Y_j(x)$: transverse displacement in the $y$ direction of the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $L_j(x)$: arc-length measured along the $j$-th stripe from an origin to position $x$
- $\phi_j(x)$: phase variable of the Charge Density Wave on the $j$-th stripe
- $\rho_j(x)$: local charge density on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $\bar{\rho}$: mean charge density
- $\rho_0$: amplitude of the charge density fluctuations
- $k_{\mathrm{F}}$: Fermi wavevector
- $\Delta_j F$: difference operator defined as $F_{j+1} - F_{j}$ for any quantity $F$ on adjacent stripes
- $V(\Delta_j Y)$: coupling function between Charge Density Waves on neighboring stripes depending on their local transverse spacing
- $H_c$: inter-stripe coupling Hamiltonian
- $\Delta_s$: spin gap in the excitation spectrum
- $K_c$: non-universal critical exponent for charge fluctuations

title: Assumption: Parameter Regime for Perturbation
content: The system operates in a regime where the Cu-O hybridization amplitude $t_{0}$ is significantly smaller than other characteristic energy scales, specifically satisfying the conditions $t_{0} \ll U$, $t_{0} \ll \epsilon_{p}$, and $t_{0} \ll U - \epsilon_{p}$, where $U$ is the on-site Coulomb repulsion at a Cu site and $\epsilon_{p}$ is the O-hole atomic energy.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at Cu site $i$ with spin $\sigma$
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at O site $l$ with spin $\sigma$
- $\epsilon_{d}$: atomic energy of Cu $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ holes
- $\epsilon_{p}$: atomic energy of O $2p_{x}$ and $2p_{y}$ holes
- $U$: on-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a Cu site
- $t_{0}$: hybridization amplitude between Cu and O orbitals
- $V_{li}$: hybridization matrix element between Cu site $i$ and O site $l$
- $H$: total Hamiltonian of the system
- $H'$: hybridization part of the Hamiltonian
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: unit vectors along the crystal axes of the square lattice

title: Assumption: Location of Doped Holes
content: In the superconducting Cu-oxide system, holes introduced via chemical doping are assumed to reside primarily on oxygen (O) sites and do not result in the formation of $Cu^{3+}$ ions.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at Cu site $i$ with spin $\sigma$
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at O site $l$ with spin $\sigma$
- $\epsilon_{d}$: atomic energy of Cu $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ holes
- $\epsilon_{p}$: atomic energy of O $2p_{x}$ and $2p_{y}$ holes
- $U$: on-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a Cu site
- $t_{0}$: hybridization amplitude between Cu and O orbitals
- $V_{li}$: hybridization matrix element between Cu site $i$ and O site $l$

- $H'$: hybridization part of the Hamiltonian
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: unit vectors along the crystal axes of the square lattice

title: Hamiltonian for Cu-O Layers
content: The electronic properties of the Cu-O layer are described by a Hamiltonian $H = \sum_{i,\sigma}\epsilon_{d}d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}d_{i\sigma} + \sum_{l,\sigma}\epsilon_{p}p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}p_{l\sigma} + U\sum_{i}d_{i\uparrow}^{\dagger}d_{i\uparrow}d_{i\downarrow}^{\dagger}d_{i\downarrow} + H^{\prime}$, where the vacuum state corresponds to completely filled Cu $d^{10}$ and O $p^{6}$ shells; $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$ creates a Cu $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ hole at site $i$ with atomic energy $\epsilon_{d}$ set to 0; $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}$ creates an O $2p_{x}$ or $2p_{y}$ hole at site $l$ with atomic energy $\epsilon_{p} > 0$; $U$ is the on-site Coulomb repulsion at a Cu site; and $H'$ is the hybridization term.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at Cu site $i$ with spin $\sigma$
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at O site $l$ with spin $\sigma$
- $\epsilon_{d}$: atomic energy of Cu $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ holes
- $\epsilon_{p}$: atomic energy of O $2p_{x}$ and $2p_{y}$ holes
- $U$: on-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a Cu site
- $t_{0}$: hybridization amplitude between Cu and O orbitals
- $V_{li}$: hybridization matrix element between Cu site $i$ and O site $l$
- $H$: total Hamiltonian of the system
- $H'$: hybridization part of the Hamiltonian
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: unit vectors along the crystal axes of the square lattice

title: Hybridization Hamiltonian
content: The hybridization part of the system Hamiltonian, denoted as $H'$, is defined by the expression $H^{\prime} = \sum_{i,\sigma} \sum_{l \in i} V_{li} d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger} p_{l\sigma} + \mathrm{H.c.}$, where for each copper site $i$, the inner summation over $l$ iterates over the four oxygen sites immediately surrounding that specific copper site, and $\mathrm{H.c.}$ denotes the Hermitian conjugate.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at Cu site $i$ with spin $\sigma$
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at O site $l$ with spin $\sigma$
- $\epsilon_{d}$: atomic energy of Cu $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ holes
- $\epsilon_{p}$: atomic energy of O $2p_{x}$ and $2p_{y}$ holes
- $U$: on-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a Cu site
- $t_{0}$: hybridization amplitude between Cu and O orbitals
- $V_{li}$: hybridization matrix element between Cu site $i$ and O site $l$
- $H$: total Hamiltonian of the system
- $H'$: hybridization part of the Hamiltonian
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: unit vectors along the crystal axes of the square lattice

title: Hybridization Matrix Elements
content: The matrix elements $V_{li}$ for the hybridization between a Cu site $i$ and its neighboring O site $l$ are determined by the relation $V_{li} = (-1)^{M_{l,i}} t_{0}$, where $t_{0}$ is the hybridization amplitude and the phase factor $M_{l,i}$ is defined as $M_{l,i} = 2$ if the oxygen site is located at $l = i - \frac{1}{2}\hat{x}$ or $l = i - \frac{1}{2}\hat{y}$, and $M_{l,i} = 1$ if the oxygen site is located at $l = i + \frac{1}{2}\hat{x}$ or $l = i + \frac{1}{2}\hat{y}$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at Cu site $i$ with spin $\sigma$
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at O site $l$ with spin $\sigma$
- $\epsilon_{d}$: atomic energy of Cu $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ holes
- $\epsilon_{p}$: atomic energy of O $2p_{x}$ and $2p_{y}$ holes
- $U$: on-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a Cu site
- $t_{0}$: hybridization amplitude between Cu and O orbitals
- $V_{li}$: hybridization matrix element between Cu site $i$ and O site $l$
- $H$: total Hamiltonian of the system
- $H'$: hybridization part of the Hamiltonian
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: unit vectors along the crystal axes of the square lattice

title: Perturbation Parameters
content: In the context of second-order perturbation theory applied to the Cu-O hybridization model, the effective coupling parameters $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ are defined as $t_{1} = t_{0}^{2} / \epsilon_{p}$ and $t_{2} = t_{0}^{2} / (U - \epsilon_{p})$, where $t_{0}$ is the hybridization amplitude, $\epsilon_{p}$ is the oxygen hole energy, and $U$ is the copper on-site Coulomb repulsion.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at Cu site $i$ with spin $\sigma$
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at O site $l$ with spin $\sigma$
- $\epsilon_{d}$: atomic energy of Cu $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ holes
- $\epsilon_{p}$: atomic energy of O $2p_{x}$ and $2p_{y}$ holes
- $U$: on-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a Cu site
- $t_{0}$: hybridization amplitude between Cu and O orbitals
- $V_{li}$: hybridization matrix element between Cu site $i$ and O site $l$
- $H$: total Hamiltonian of the system
- $H'$: hybridization part of the Hamiltonian
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: unit vectors along the crystal axes of the square lattice

title: Formation and stability of the local Zhang-Rice singlet
content:
The authors derive that a hole introduced into the Oxygen sites of a $CuO_2$ layer binds strongly to the central $Cu^{2+}$ ion to form a localized spin singlet state with large binding energy. Specifically, considering the symmetric combination of the four oxygen hole states around a Cu ion:
$P_{i\sigma}^{(S)}=\frac{1}{2}\sum_{l\in[l]}(\pm1)^{M_{l,l}}p_{l\sigma}$
(where the sum runs over the four O sites around Cu site $i$, and signs reflect the $d_{x^2-y^2}$ symmetry), the authors calculate that the singlet formed by $P_{i\sigma}^{(S)}$ and the central Cu hole has an energy of approximately $-8(t_1+t_2)$ in second-order perturbation theory (where $t_1$ and $t_2$ are hybridization parameters). This energy is significantly lower than that of the triplet state (energy 0) or antisymmetric nonbonding states (energy $-4t_1$), justifying the projection of the system into the subspace of these local singlets.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at Cu site $i$ with spin $\sigma$
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for a hole at O site $l$ with spin $\sigma$
- $\epsilon_{d}$: atomic energy of Cu $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ holes
- $\epsilon_{p}$: atomic energy of O $2p_{x}$ and $2p_{y}$ holes
- $U$: on-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a Cu site
- $t_{0}$: hybridization amplitude between Cu and O orbitals
- $V_{li}$: hybridization matrix element between Cu site $i$ and O site $l$
- $H$: total Hamiltonian of the system
- $H'$: hybridization part of the Hamiltonian
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: unit vectors along the crystal axes of the square lattice

title: Two-band Hamiltonian for Cu-O layers
content:
In a model describing a single layer of square planar coordinated copper (Cu) and oxygen (O) atoms, where the vacuum is defined as filled Cu $d^{10}$ and O $p^{6}$ states, the Hamiltonian $H$ is defined as:
\[ H=\sum_{i,\sigma}\epsilon_{d}d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}d_{i\sigma}+\sum_{l,\sigma}\epsilon_{p}p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}p_{l\sigma}+U\sum_{i}d_{i\uparrow}^{\dagger}d_{i\uparrow}d_{i\downarrow}^{\dagger}d_{i\downarrow}+H^{\prime} \]
where $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$ creates a Cu ($3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$) hole at site $i$, $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}$ creates an O ($2p_{x}, 2p_{y}$) hole at site $l$, $U$ is the on-site Coulomb repulsion at a Cu site, and the atomic energy levels are set such that $\epsilon_{d}=0$ and $\epsilon_{p}>0$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}, d_{i\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for a copper (Cu) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $i$ in the $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ orbital.
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}, p_{l\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for an oxygen (O) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $l$ in the $2p_{x}$ or $2p_{y}$ orbitals.
- $\sigma$: Spin index, $\sigma \in \{\uparrow, \downarrow\}$.
- $\epsilon_{d}$: Atomic energy level of a copper hole.
- $\epsilon_{p}$: Atomic energy level of an oxygen hole.
- $U$: On-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a copper site.
- $H^{\prime}$: Hybridization (hopping) part of the Hamiltonian between copper and oxygen sites.
- $t_{0}$: Amplitude of the hybridization (hopping) between copper and oxygen orbitals.
- $V_{il}$: Hybridization matrix element between copper site $i$ and oxygen site $l$.
- $M_{i,l}$: Phase factor index for the hybridization matrix element.
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: Unit vectors along the $x$ and $y$ axes of the square lattice.
- $P_{i\sigma}^{(S)}$: Operator for a localized symmetric (S) oxygen hole state surrounding copper site $i$.
- $\phi_{i\sigma}$: Wannier function operator for oxygen holes, centered at copper site $i$, constructed to ensure orthogonality.
- $N_{S}$: Total number of squares (or unit cells) in the lattice.
- $\mathbf{k}$: Wave vector in the first Brillouin zone.
- $\mathbf{R}_{i}$: Position vector of the copper site $i$.
- $\beta_{\mathbf{k}}$: Normalization factor for the Wannier function in momentum space.
- $\psi_{i}^{-}$: Wavefunction of the local spin-singlet state formed by a copper hole and a symmetric oxygen hole Wannier state at site $i$.

title: Hybridization Term
content:
The hybridization term $H^{\prime}$ in the Hamiltonian describing a single layer of Cu and O atoms is defined as:
\[ H^{\prime}=\sum_{i,\sigma}\sum_{l\in[i]}V_{il}d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}p_{l\sigma}+\mathrm{H.c.} \]
where the sum over $l$ for a fixed $i$ runs over the four oxygen sites surrounding the copper site $i$. The hybridization matrix element $V_{il}$ is defined by the amplitude $t_{0}$ and a phase factor $(-1)^{M_{i,l}}$ such that $V_{il}=(-1)^{M_{i,l}}t_{0}$, where $M_{i,l}=2$ if the oxygen site $l$ is at $i-\frac{1}{2}\hat{x}$ or $i-\frac{1}{2}\hat{y}$, and $M_{i,l}=1$ if $l$ is at $i+\frac{1}{2}\hat{x}$ or $i+\frac{1}{2}\hat{y}$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}, d_{i\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for a copper (Cu) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $i$ in the $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ orbital.
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}, p_{l\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for an oxygen (O) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $l$ in the $2p_{x}$ or $2p_{y}$ orbitals.
- $\sigma$: Spin index, $\sigma \in \{\uparrow, \downarrow\}$.
- $\epsilon_{d}$: Atomic energy level of a copper hole.
- $\epsilon_{p}$: Atomic energy level of an oxygen hole.
- $U$: On-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a copper site.
- $H^{\prime}$: Hybridization (hopping) part of the Hamiltonian between copper and oxygen sites.
- $t_{0}$: Amplitude of the hybridization (hopping) between copper and oxygen orbitals.
- $V_{il}$: Hybridization matrix element between copper site $i$ and oxygen site $l$.
- $M_{i,l}$: Phase factor index for the hybridization matrix element.
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: Unit vectors along the $x$ and $y$ axes of the square lattice.
- $P_{i\sigma}^{(S)}$: Operator for a localized symmetric (S) oxygen hole state surrounding copper site $i$.
- $\phi_{i\sigma}$: Wannier function operator for oxygen holes, centered at copper site $i$, constructed to ensure orthogonality.
- $N_{S}$: Total number of squares (or unit cells) in the lattice.
- $\mathbf{k}$: Wave vector in the first Brillouin zone.
- $\mathbf{R}_{i}$: Position vector of the copper site $i$.
- $\beta_{\mathbf{k}}$: Normalization factor for the Wannier function in momentum space.
- $\psi_{i}^{-}$: Wavefunction of the local spin-singlet state formed by a copper hole and a symmetric oxygen hole Wannier state at site $i$.

title: Symmetric Oxygen Hole States
content:
For a copper site $i$ surrounded by four oxygen sites $l \in [i]$, the localized symmetric (S) oxygen hole state operator $P_{i\sigma}^{(S)}$ is defined as:
\[ P_{i\sigma}^{(S)}=\frac{1}{2}\sum_{l\in[i]}(-1)^{M_{i,l}}p_{l\sigma} \]
where $p_{l\sigma}$ is the annihilation operator for an oxygen hole at site $l$ with spin $\sigma$, and the phase factors $(-1)^{M_{i,l}}$ are determined by $M_{i,l}=2$ if $l=i-\frac{1}{2}\hat{x}$ or $i-\frac{1}{2}\hat{y}$, and $M_{i,l}=1$ if $l=i+\frac{1}{2}\hat{x}$ or $i+\frac{1}{2}\hat{y}$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}, d_{i\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for a copper (Cu) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $i$ in the $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ orbital.
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}, p_{l\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for an oxygen (O) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $l$ in the $2p_{x}$ or $2p_{y}$ orbitals.
- $\sigma$: Spin index, $\sigma \in \{\uparrow, \downarrow\}$.
- $\epsilon_{d}$: Atomic energy level of a copper hole.
- $\epsilon_{p}$: Atomic energy level of an oxygen hole.
- $U$: On-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a copper site.
- $H^{\prime}$: Hybridization (hopping) part of the Hamiltonian between copper and oxygen sites.
- $t_{0}$: Amplitude of the hybridization (hopping) between copper and oxygen orbitals.
- $V_{il}$: Hybridization matrix element between copper site $i$ and oxygen site $l$.
- $M_{i,l}$: Phase factor index for the hybridization matrix element.
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: Unit vectors along the $x$ and $y$ axes of the square lattice.
- $P_{i\sigma}^{(S)}$: Operator for a localized symmetric (S) oxygen hole state surrounding copper site $i$.
- $\phi_{i\sigma}$: Wannier function operator for oxygen holes, centered at copper site $i$, constructed to ensure orthogonality.
- $N_{S}$: Total number of squares (or unit cells) in the lattice.
- $\mathbf{k}$: Wave vector in the first Brillouin zone.
- $\mathbf{R}_{i}$: Position vector of the copper site $i$.
- $\beta_{\mathbf{k}}$: Normalization factor for the Wannier function in momentum space.
- $\psi_{i}^{-}$: Wavefunction of the local spin-singlet state formed by a copper hole and a symmetric oxygen hole Wannier state at site $i$.

title: Suppression of Inter-stripe CDW Coupling (Dephasing)
content:
In a doped Mott insulator modeled as an array of metallic stripes, the effective coupling between charge-density waves (CDW) on neighboring stripes vanishes in the thermodynamic limit due to transverse stripe fluctuations. The effective potential $\tilde{V}(x)$ decays as:
$$ \tilde{V}(x)=\langle V(\Delta Y)\rangle\exp\{-(2k_{\mathrm{f}}^{2})\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle\} $$
where the mean-squared difference in arc lengths $\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle$ diverges as $\sim A T |x|$ at non-zero temperature and as $\sim A\hbar\omega\log|x|$ at zero temperature, preventing long-range CDW locking between stripes.

extra.notations:
- CDW: Charge Density Wave
- $j$: index identifying a specific stripe in a two-dimensional array
- $x$: spatial coordinate along the longitudinal direction of a stripe
- $Y_j(x)$: transverse displacement in the $y$ direction of the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $L_j(x)$: arc-length measured along the $j$-th stripe from an origin to position $x$
- $\phi_j(x)$: phase variable of the Charge Density Wave on the $j$-th stripe
- $\rho_j(x)$: local charge density on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $\bar{\rho}$: mean charge density
- $\rho_0$: amplitude of the charge density fluctuations
- $k_{\mathrm{F}}$: Fermi wavevector
- $\Delta_j F$: difference operator defined as $F_{j+1} - F_{j}$ for any quantity $F$ on adjacent stripes
- $V(\Delta_j Y)$: coupling function between Charge Density Waves on neighboring stripes depending on their local transverse spacing
- $H_c$: inter-stripe coupling Hamiltonian
- $\Delta_s$: spin gap in the excitation spectrum
- $K_c$: non-universal critical exponent for charge fluctuations

title: Wannier Functions for Oxygen Holes
content:
To ensure orthogonality among the overlapping symmetric oxygen hole states $P_{i\sigma}^{(S)}$, a set of Wannier functions $\phi_{i\sigma}$ centered at copper site $i$ (with position $\mathbf{R}_{i}$) is defined as:
\[ \phi_{i\sigma}=N_{S}^{-1/2}\sum_{\mathbf{k}}P_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}\exp(i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{R}_{i}) \]
where $N_S$ is the number of unit cells and the Fourier transform $P_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$ is given by:
\[ P_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}=N_{S}^{-1/2}\beta_{\mathbf{k}}\sum_{j}P_{j\sigma}^{(S)}\exp(-i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{R}_{j}) \]
The normalization factor $\beta_{\mathbf{k}}$ required to satisfy the orthogonality condition $\langle \phi_{i\sigma} | \phi_{j\sigma^{\prime}} \rangle = \delta_{ij}\delta_{\sigma\sigma^{\prime}}$ is:
\[ \beta_{\mathbf{k}}=[1-\frac{1}{2}\left(\cos k_{x}+\cos k_{y}\right)]^{-1/2} \]

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}, d_{i\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for a copper (Cu) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $i$ in the $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ orbital.
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}, p_{l\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for an oxygen (O) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $l$ in the $2p_{x}$ or $2p_{y}$ orbitals.
- $\sigma$: Spin index, $\sigma \in \{\uparrow, \downarrow\}$.

- $M_{i,l}$: Phase factor index for the hybridization matrix element.
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: Unit vectors along the $x$ and $y$ axes of the square lattice.
- $P_{i\sigma}^{(S)}$: Operator for a localized symmetric (S) oxygen hole state surrounding copper site $i$.
- $\phi_{i\sigma}$: Wannier function operator for oxygen holes, centered at copper site $i$, constructed to ensure orthogonality.
- $N_{S}$: Total number of squares (or unit cells) in the lattice.
- $\mathbf{k}$: Wave vector in the first Brillouin zone.
- $\mathbf{R}_{i}$: Position vector of the copper site $i$.
- $\beta_{\mathbf{k}}$: Normalization factor for the Wannier function in momentum space.

title: Singlet State Definition
content:
A local spin-singlet state $\psi_{i}^{-}$ at copper site $i$ is formed by the combination of a copper $d$-hole and the symmetric oxygen-hole Wannier state $\phi_{i\sigma}$ as:
\[ \psi_{i}^{-}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\phi_{i\uparrow}d_{i\downarrow}-\phi_{i\downarrow}d_{i\uparrow}) \]
where $d_{i\sigma}$ is the annihilation operator for a copper hole at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$, and $\phi_{i\sigma}$ is the annihilation operator for an oxygen hole in the symmetric Wannier state at site $i$.

- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}, d_{i\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for a copper (Cu) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $i$ in the $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ orbital.

title: Assumption: Parameter Regime and Symmetry
content: The analysis of the effective Hamiltonian assumes the parameter regime where the hybridization amplitude $t_{0}$ is much smaller than the oxygen atomic energy $\epsilon_{p}$, the copper on-site repulsion $U$, and the difference $U - \epsilon_{p}$ (i.e., $t_{0}\ll U, \epsilon_{p}, U-\epsilon_{p}$). Additionally, for the derivation of the effective hopping, particle-hole symmetry is assumed such that $\epsilon_{p} = U - \epsilon_{p}$, which implies that the second-order hybridization energy scales $t_{1}=t_{0}^{2}/\epsilon_{p}$ and $t_{2}=t_{0}^{2}/(U-\epsilon_{p})$ are equal ($t_{1} = t_{2} = t$).

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}, d_{i\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for a copper (Cu) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $i$ in the $3d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ orbital.
- $p_{l\sigma}^{\dagger}, p_{l\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for an oxygen (O) hole with spin $\sigma$ at site $l$ in the $2p_{x}$ or $2p_{y}$ orbitals.
- $\sigma$: Spin index, $\sigma \in \{\uparrow, \downarrow\}$.
- $\epsilon_{d}$: Atomic energy level of a copper hole.
- $\epsilon_{p}$: Atomic energy level of an oxygen hole.
- $U$: On-site Coulomb repulsion energy at a copper site.
- $H^{\prime}$: Hybridization (hopping) part of the Hamiltonian between copper and oxygen sites.
- $t_{0}$: Amplitude of the hybridization (hopping) between copper and oxygen orbitals.
- $V_{il}$: Hybridization matrix element between copper site $i$ and oxygen site $l$.
- $M_{i,l}$: Phase factor index for the hybridization matrix element.
- $\hat{x}, \hat{y}$: Unit vectors along the $x$ and $y$ axes of the square lattice.
- $P_{i\sigma}^{(S)}$: Operator for a localized symmetric (S) oxygen hole state surrounding copper site $i$.
- $\phi_{i\sigma}$: Wannier function operator for oxygen holes, centered at copper site $i$, constructed to ensure orthogonality.
