title: Hamiltonian of the 1DEG
content: The Hamiltonian density of the one-dimensional electron gas ($1DEG$) representing a metallic stripe is $\mathcal{H}_{1DEG}=\mathcal{H}_{0}+\mathcal{H}_{1}$. The noninteracting part is $\mathcal{H}_{0}=i v_{F}\sum_{\sigma}\left[\psi_{1,\sigma}^{\dagger}\partial_{x}\psi_{1,\sigma}-\psi_{2,\sigma}^{\dagger}\partial_{x}\psi_{2,\sigma}\right]-\mu\sum_{\alpha,\sigma}\left[\psi_{\alpha,\sigma}^{\dagger}(x)\psi_{\alpha,\sigma}(x)\right]$, where $v_{F}$ is the Fermi velocity, $\mu$ is the chemical potential, and $\psi_{\alpha,\sigma}^{\dagger}(x)$ creates an electron with spin $\sigma$ on the right- ($\alpha=1$) or left- ($\alpha=2$) moving branch. The interaction part is $\mathcal{H}_{1}=g_{2}\sum_{\sigma,\sigma^{\prime}}\psi_{1,\sigma}^{\dagger}\psi_{2,\sigma^{\prime}}^{\dagger}\psi_{2\sigma,\sigma^{\prime}}\psi_{1,\sigma}+g_{1}\sum_{\sigma,\sigma^{\prime}}\psi_{1,\sigma}^{\dagger}\psi_{2,\sigma^{\prime}}^{\dagger}\psi_{1,\sigma^{\prime}}\psi_{2,\sigma}+g_{3}[\psi_{1,\downarrow}^{\dagger}\psi_{1,\uparrow}^{\dagger}\psi_{2,\downarrow}\psi_{2,\uparrow}e^{i(4k_{F}-G)x}+\mathrm{H.c.}]$, where $g_{1}, g_{2}, g_{3}$ are coupling constants, $k_{F}$ is the Fermi wave vector, and $G$ is a reciprocal lattice vector.

extra.notations:
- $1DEG$: One-dimensional electron gas representing a metallic stripe.
- $\alpha=1, 2$: Indices for right-moving and left-moving electron branches, respectively.
- $\psi_{\alpha,\sigma}^{\dagger}(x)$: Creation operator for an electron with branch index $\alpha$ and spin $\sigma$ at position $x$.
- $v_{F}$: Fermi velocity of the 1DEG.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential of the 1DEG.
- $g_{1}, g_{2}, g_{3}$: Coupling constants for backscattering, forward scattering, and umklapp scattering in the 1DEG.
- $k_{F}$: Fermi wave vector.
- $G$: Reciprocal lattice vector.
- $\widetilde{\cdot}$: Denotes a parameter or field associated with the environment (e.g., $\widetilde{v}_{c}$ is the environmental charge velocity).
- $\widetilde{\Delta}_{c}, \widetilde{\Delta}_{s}$: Charge and spin gaps of the environment.
- $\phi_{s}, \phi_{c}$: Bosonic spin and charge phase fields.
- $\theta_{s}, \theta_{c}$: Dual bosonic phase fields (conjugate to the spatial derivatives of $\phi_{s}, \phi_{c}$).
- $P^{\dagger}$: Singlet-pair creation operator on the metallic stripe.
- $\bar{\mathcal{P}}$: Singlet-pair operator for the environment.
- $K_{s}, K_{c}$: Luttinger liquid exponents for spin and charge sectors.
- $a$: Short-distance cutoff (lattice spacing).
- $J_{s}, V_{s}, J_{c}, V_{c}, t_{sp}, t_{tp}$: Coupling constants for interaction and pair hopping between the 1DEG and its environment.

title: Hamiltonian of the Environment
content: The environment of the metallic stripe is modeled as a second interacting one-dimensional electron gas with a Hamiltonian density $\mathcal{H}_{env}$ of the same functional form as $\mathcal{H}_{1DEG}$, with environmental parameters $\widetilde{v}_{c}$, $\widetilde{v}_{s}$, $\widetilde{g}_{i}$, and $\widetilde{\mu}$. The environment is characterized as a Mott insulator with a charge gap $\widetilde{\Delta}_{c}$ produced by strong commensurability ($4\widetilde{k}_{F}=\widetilde{G}$) and a propagation velocity for charge excitations $\widetilde{v}_{c}$ that is much smaller than the Fermi velocity $v_{F}$ of the 1DEG.

extra.notations:
- $1DEG$: One-dimensional electron gas representing a metallic stripe.
- $\alpha=1, 2$: Indices for right-moving and left-moving electron branches, respectively.
- $\psi_{\alpha,\sigma}^{\dagger}(x)$: Creation operator for an electron with branch index $\alpha$ and spin $\sigma$ at position $x$.
- $v_{F}$: Fermi velocity of the 1DEG.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential of the 1DEG.
- $g_{1}, g_{2}, g_{3}$: Coupling constants for backscattering, forward scattering, and umklapp scattering in the 1DEG.
- $k_{F}$: Fermi wave vector.
- $G$: Reciprocal lattice vector.
- $\widetilde{\cdot}$: Denotes a parameter or field associated with the environment (e.g., $\widetilde{v}_{c}$ is the environmental charge velocity).
- $\widetilde{\Delta}_{c}, \widetilde{\Delta}_{s}$: Charge and spin gaps of the environment.
- $\phi_{s}, \phi_{c}$: Bosonic spin and charge phase fields.
- $\theta_{s}, \theta_{c}$: Dual bosonic phase fields (conjugate to the spatial derivatives of $\phi_{s}, \phi_{c}$).
- $P^{\dagger}$: Singlet-pair creation operator on the metallic stripe.
- $\bar{\mathcal{P}}$: Singlet-pair operator for the environment.
- $K_{s}, K_{c}$: Luttinger liquid exponents for spin and charge sectors.
- $a$: Short-distance cutoff (lattice spacing).
- $J_{s}, V_{s}, J_{c}, V_{c}, t_{sp}, t_{tp}$: Coupling constants for interaction and pair hopping between the 1DEG and its environment.

title: Impurity phase identification
content: The compound iron arsenide ($FeAs$), which may appear as an impurity phase in the synthesis of $LaOFeAs$, is helimagnetic and possesses a unique X-ray diffraction pattern with specific Bragg peaks that are distinguishable from the diffraction peaks of the $P4/nmm$ $LaOFeAs$ phase.

extra.notations:
- $LaOMPn$: A class of quaternary oxypnictides where $La$ is Lanthanum, $O$ is Oxygen, $M$ is a transition metal (Mn, Fe, Co, or Ni), and $Pn$ is a pnictogen (P or As).
- $P4/nmm$: A specific tetragonal space group symmetry.
- $FeAs$: Iron arsenide, which can occur as an impurity phase in the synthesis of iron-based oxypnictides.
- Vegard's volume rule: An empirical heuristic stating that a linear relation exists, at constant temperature, between the crystal lattice parameter of an alloy and the concentrations of the constituent elements.
- $x$: The molar fraction of a dopant (specifically $F^{-}$ substituting for $O^{2-}$ in $La[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs$).

title: Charge Density in Fluctuating Stripes
content: Consider a system of stripes indexed by $j$. The local charge density $\rho_j(x)$ at position $x$ along the $j$-th stripe, which is subject to a transverse displacement $Y_j(x)$, is defined by the relation $\rho_{j}(x) = \bar{\rho} + \rho_{0} \cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_{j}(x) + 2k_{\mathrm{F}}L_{j}(x)]$, where $\phi_j(x)$ represents the phase of the charge-density-wave, $k_F$ is the Fermi wavevector, and $L_j(x)$ is the arc length measured along the undulating stripe.

extra.notations:
- $K_c$: Luttinger liquid parameter for the charge degrees of freedom
- $\Delta_s$: Spin gap magnitude
- $\chi_{\mathrm{CDW}}$: Charge-density-wave susceptibility
- $\chi_{\mathrm{SC}}$: Superconducting susceptibility
- $k_F$: Fermi wavevector
- $\phi_j(x)$: Phase field of the charge-density-wave on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $Y_j(x)$: Transverse displacement of the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $L_j(x)$: Arc length along the $j$-th stripe from the origin to position $x$
- $\rho_j(x)$: Local charge density on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $\bar{\rho}$: Average charge density
- $\rho_0$: Amplitude of the charge-density-wave fluctuation
- $\Delta_j F$: Finite difference operator defined as $F_{j+1} - F_j$
- $V(\Delta_j Y)$: Coupling potential between charge-density-waves on neighboring stripes $j$ and $j+1$
- $J(\Delta_j Y)$: Josephson tunneling matrix element for superconducting pairs between neighboring stripes
- $J_0$: Base Josephson coupling constant
- $\alpha$: Exponential decay constant for inter-stripe tunneling
- $\hbar\bar{\omega}$: Characteristic energy scale of transverse stripe zero-point fluctuations

title: Interaction Hamiltonian and Pair Hopping
content: The interaction Hamiltonian density between the 1DEG and its environment is $\mathcal{H}_{int} = J_{s}\vec{j}_{s}\cdot\vec{\widetilde{j}}_{s} + V_{s}\vec{S}\cdot\vec{\widetilde{S}} + J_{c}j_{c}\widetilde{j}_{c} + V_{c}\rho\widetilde{\rho} + \mathcal{H}_{pair}$. Here $\vec{j}_{s}$ and $\vec{\widetilde{j}}_{s}$ are spin-current operators, $\vec{S}$ and $\vec{\widetilde{S}}$ are spin-density operators, $j_{c}$ and $\widetilde{j}_{c}$ are charge-current operators, and $\rho$ and $\widetilde{\rho}$ are charge-density operators. The pair transfer term is $\mathcal{H}_{pair} = t_{sp}[P^{\dagger}\bar{\mathcal{P}} + \mathrm{H.c.}] + t_{tp}\sum_{m=-1}^{1}[P_{m}^{\dagger}\bar{\mathcal{P}}_{m} + \mathrm{H.c.}]$, where $P^{\dagger}(x) \equiv \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[\psi_{1,\uparrow}^{\dagger}(x)\psi_{2,\downarrow}^{\dagger}(x) + \psi_{2,\uparrow}^{\dagger}(x)\psi_{1,\downarrow}^{\dagger}(x)]$ is the singlet-pair creation operator on the stripe and $\bar{\mathcal{P}}$ is the corresponding operator for the environment.

extra.notations:
- $1DEG$: One-dimensional electron gas representing a metallic stripe.
- $\alpha=1, 2$: Indices for right-moving and left-moving electron branches, respectively.
- $\psi_{\alpha,\sigma}^{\dagger}(x)$: Creation operator for an electron with branch index $\alpha$ and spin $\sigma$ at position $x$.
- $v_{F}$: Fermi velocity of the 1DEG.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential of the 1DEG.
- $g_{1}, g_{2}, g_{3}$: Coupling constants for backscattering, forward scattering, and umklapp scattering in the 1DEG.
- $k_{F}$: Fermi wave vector.
- $G$: Reciprocal lattice vector.
- $\widetilde{\cdot}$: Denotes a parameter or field associated with the environment (e.g., $\widetilde{v}_{c}$ is the environmental charge velocity).
- $\widetilde{\Delta}_{c}, \widetilde{\Delta}_{s}$: Charge and spin gaps of the environment.
- $\phi_{s}, \phi_{c}$: Bosonic spin and charge phase fields.
- $\theta_{s}, \theta_{c}$: Dual bosonic phase fields (conjugate to the spatial derivatives of $\phi_{s}, \phi_{c}$).
- $P^{\dagger}$: Singlet-pair creation operator on the metallic stripe.
- $\bar{\mathcal{P}}$: Singlet-pair operator for the environment.
- $K_{s}, K_{c}$: Luttinger liquid exponents for spin and charge sectors.
- $a$: Short-distance cutoff (lattice spacing).
- $J_{s}, V_{s}, J_{c}, V_{c}, t_{sp}, t_{tp}$: Coupling constants for interaction and pair hopping between the 1DEG and its environment.

title: Bosonization Representation
content: The fermionic fields $\psi_{s,\sigma}^{\dagger}(x)$ are represented in terms of bosonic phase fields $\Phi_{s,\sigma}(x)$ as $\psi_{s,\sigma}^{\dagger}(x) = (2\pi a)^{-1/2}\exp\{i\Phi_{s,\sigma}(x)\}$. The total Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H} = \mathcal{H}_{c} + \mathcal{H}_{s} + \mathcal{H}_{pair}$ is expressed using spin fields $\phi_{s} = (\phi_{\downarrow} - \phi_{\uparrow})/\sqrt{2}$ and charge fields $\phi_{c} = (\phi_{\downarrow} + \phi_{\uparrow})/\sqrt{2}$, where $\phi_{\sigma}$ and their conjugate momenta $\Pi_{\sigma}$ (related to $\theta_{\sigma}$) satisfy $[\phi_{\sigma}(x), \Pi_{\sigma^{\prime}}(x^{\prime})] = i\delta(x-x^{\prime})$. In this representation, the singlet pair hopping term is $\mathcal{H}_{pair} = \frac{t_{sp}}{\pi^{2}a^{2}}\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}(\theta_{c} - \bar{\theta}_{c})]\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_{s}]\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\widetilde{\phi}_{s}]$, where $a$ is the lattice spacing and $\theta_{c}, \bar{\theta}_{c}$ are the dual charge fields for the stripe and environment respectively.

title: Unitary Transformation
content: A unitary transformation is defined by the operator $U_{\lambda} = \exp[-i\lambda\int dx\theta_{c}(x)\partial_{x}\widetilde{\phi}_{c}(x)]$. This transformation shifts the fields according to $U_{\lambda}^{\dagger}\widetilde{\Pi}_{c}U_{\lambda} = \widetilde{\Pi}_{c} + \lambda\Pi_{c}$ and $U_{\lambda}^{\dagger}\phi_{c}U_{\lambda} = \phi_{c} - \lambda\widetilde{\phi}_{c}$, where $\Pi_{c}$ and $\widetilde{\Pi}_{c}$ are conjugate momenta to $\phi_{c}$ and $\widetilde{\phi}_{c}$ respectively. For specific values of $\lambda$ and coupling constants $J_{c}$ and $V_{c}$, this transformation diagonalizes the quadratic charge part of the Hamiltonian and renormalizes the effective velocities $v_{c}, \widetilde{v}_{c}$ and Luttinger exponents $K_{c}, \widetilde{K}_{c}$.

title: Assumption: Spin Gap in Environment
content: The environment is assumed to possess a spin gap $\widetilde{\Delta}_{s}$, resulting either from a pre-existing structural feature (such as spin ladders) or induced by a relevant negative backscattering coupling $\widetilde{g}_{1} < 0$. The presence of this spin gap implies that the fluctuations of the environmental spin field $\widetilde{\phi}_{s}$ are quenched, allowing $\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\widetilde{\phi}_{s}]$ to be replaced by its non-zero vacuum expectation value $\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\widetilde{\phi}_{s}]\rangle$.

title: Assumption: Relevant Interactions
content: Under the renormalization-group flow toward the paired-spin-liquid fixed point, and based on the physics of antiferromagnetic environments, the interactions $J_{s}$, $V_{s}$, and $t_{tp}$ are considered irrelevant and are set to $J_{s} = V_{s} = t_{tp} = 0$. The interactions that remain relevant for the low-energy physics of the system are the singlet pair hopping $t_{sp}$ and the charge couplings $V_{c}$ and $J_{c}$.

title: Pairing on a 1DEG stripe induced by an environmental spin gap via pair tunneling
content:
In a model of a one-dimensional electron gas (1DEG) representing a metallic stripe coupled to an active antiferromagnetic environment, pairing on the stripe is induced when a spin gap in the environment is generated and communicated to the stripe via pair tunneling (pair transfer) between stripe and environment. The pair-tunneling coupling (bare $J_{sp}$, renormalized to $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ by environmental spin fluctuations) sets a crossover pairing temperature $T_{pair}$ at which the spin gap $\Delta_s(T)$ develops. In the regime
      $$
        \mathcal{J}_{sp}\left[\mathcal{J}_{sp}/W\right]^{1/3} \ll \varepsilon^* \ll \mathcal{J}_{sp},
      $$
    the mean-field analysis gives the scaling relations $T_{pair}\sim \varepsilon^*$ and, at $T=0$, $\Delta_s(0)\sim [\mathcal{J}_{sp}]^2/\varepsilon^*$. Thus, pair tunneling between the stripe and the environment mediates the onset of a spin-gap-driven pairing scale on the stripe.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted a specific pairing mechanism (a spin gap generated in the environment that is communicated to the stripe via pair hopping) that the single child (Node 637) did not claim or derive. Node 637 only supports the statements that, after a unitary spin–charge decoupling, the charge sector remains Gaussian with an unrenormalized charge velocity and therefore an unrenormalized Drude weight, and that heavy physics is confined to the spin sector. I removed the unsupported causal pairing claims and the explicit spin-gap/pair-hopping mechanism, leaving only the factual claims that are entailed by every child. This revised parent is self-contained and now matches the content of Child 637.

The original parent included two claims not supported by the single child: an explicit $t_{sp}[P^\dagger\bar{\mathcal{P}}+\mathrm{H.c.}]$ Hamiltonian term in that notation and the substantive assertion that the mechanism "establishes a high pairing scale without a large mass renormalization, despite strong Coulomb repulsion." Child 645 uses a pseudospin description with couplings $J_{sp}$ and $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ and provides mean-field scaling for $T_{pair}$ and $\Delta_s(0)$, but it does not state the specific $t_{sp}$ notation nor demonstrate the claim about negligible mass renormalization under strong Coulomb repulsion. To ensure the parent is entailed by the child, I removed the unsupported Hamiltonian notation and the unsupported statement about mass renormalization, retained only the intersectional, substantive physical claims that the child does support (pair-tunneling-mediated transfer of a spin gap to the stripe, the role of $J_{sp}/\mathcal{J}_{sp}$, and the quoted scaling relations in the specified regime), and restated them in a self-contained form.

extra.notations:
- $1DEG$: one-dimensional electron gas (metallic stripe)
      - $J_{sp}$: bare pair-tunneling coupling between stripe and environment
      - $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: effective (renormalized) pair-tunneling coupling
      - $\varepsilon^*$: renormalized charge-transfer energy
      - $T_{pair}$: pairing crossover temperature where the spin gap develops
      - $\Delta_s(T)$: temperature-dependent spin gap on the stripe/environment
      - $W$: electronic bandwidth scale appearing in the renormalization regime

title: General Hamiltonian of the 1DEG in an Active Environment
content: The total Hamiltonian density of a one-dimensional electron gas ($1DEG$) coupled to an active environment is defined as $\mathcal{H} = \mathcal{H}_{1DEG} + \mathcal{H}_{env} + \mathcal{H}_{int} + \mathcal{H}_{Coul}$. In the bosonized representation, where the $1DEG$ and environment are modeled as interacting one-dimensional gases, the Hamiltonian decomposes into charge ($c$) and spin ($s$) sectors, along with a pair hopping term, such that $\mathcal{H} = \mathcal{H}_{c} + \mathcal{H}_{s} + \mathcal{H}_{pair}$. The dynamics are described by bosonic fields $\phi_{c,s}(x)$ and their conjugate momenta $\Pi_{c,s}(x)$.

extra.notations:
- $1DEG$: One-dimensional electron gas (representing a metallic stripe).
- $\mathcal{H}_{1DEG}$: Hamiltonian density of the one-dimensional electron gas.
- $\mathcal{H}_{env}$: Hamiltonian density of the active environment (modeled as an interacting 1DEG).
- $\mathcal{H}_{int}$: Hamiltonian density describing density and current couplings between the stripe and the environment.
- $\mathcal{H}_{Coul}$: Hamiltonian density for long-range Coulomb interactions.
- $\mathcal{H}_{pair}$: Singlet pair hopping Hamiltonian density.
- $\phi_{c,s}(x)$: Bosonic fields for the charge ($c$) and spin ($s$) sectors of the stripe.
- $\bar{\phi}_{c,s}(x)$ or $\widetilde{\phi}_{s}(x)$: Bosonic fields for the charge and spin sectors of the environment.
- $\theta_{c}(x)$: Dual field to $\phi_{c}(x)$, related to the charge phase.
- $\Pi_{c,s}(x)$: Conjugate momenta for the charge and spin fields.
- $t_{sp}$: Singlet pair transfer amplitude (hopping strength).
- $a$: Lattice cutoff/short-distance regulator.
- $\rho(x)$: Long-wavelength charge density operator relative to the background.
- $V_{c}$: Coupling constant for the density-density interaction between the stripe and environment.
- $U_{\lambda}$: Unitary operator for the decoupling transformation with parameter $\lambda$.
- $\widetilde{v}_{c}$: Propagation velocity of charge excitations in the environment.
- $\bar{K}_{c}$: Luttinger parameter characterizing the environmental charge sector.
- $\varepsilon^{*}$: Renormalized excitation energy of a holon pair in the environment.
- $\tau_{R}$: Pseudospin operators at lattice site $R$.
- $\xi_p$: Lattice constant of the pseudospin array.

title: Bosonized Pair Hopping Hamiltonian
content: The singlet pair hopping Hamiltonian density $\mathcal{H}_{pair}$, which describes the transfer of hole pairs between a metallic stripe (represented by unbarred fields) and its environment (represented by barred/tilded fields), is expressed in the bosonized representation as $\mathcal{H}_{pair} = \left(\frac{t_{sp}}{\pi^{2}a^{2}}\right)\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}(\theta_{c}-\bar{\theta}_{c})]\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_{s}]\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\widetilde{\phi}_{s}]$. Here, $\theta_{c}$ and $\bar{\theta}_{c}$ are the dual charge fields of the stripe and environment respectively, $\phi_{s}$ and $\widetilde{\phi}_{s}$ are the corresponding spin fields, $a$ is the lattice cutoff, and $t_{sp}$ is the singlet pair transfer amplitude.

title: Charge Density and Coupling
content: The long-wavelength charge density relative to the background for the $1DEG$ is defined as $\rho(x) = -\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\partial_{x}\phi_{c}$, where $\phi_{c}$ is the charge bosonic field. The interaction Hamiltonian between the stripe and the environment includes a density-density coupling term of the form $V_{c}\rho\widetilde{\rho}$, where $V_{c}$ is the coupling strength and $\widetilde{\rho}$ is the environmental charge density.

title: Unitary Decoupling Transformation
content: A unitary transformation $U_{\lambda} = \exp\left[-i\lambda\int d x\theta_{c}(x)\partial_{x}\widetilde{\phi}_{c}(x)\right]$ is defined to decouple the charge modes of the $1DEG$ from the environment, where $\lambda$ is a transformation parameter, $\theta_{c}$ is the dual charge field of the $1DEG$, and $\widetilde{\phi}_{c}$ is the charge field of the environment. The transformation shifts the fields as $U_{\lambda}^{\dagger}\widetilde{\Pi}_{c}(x)U_{\lambda} = \widetilde{\Pi}_{c}(x) + \lambda\Pi_{c}(x)$ and $U_{\lambda}^{\dagger}\phi_{c}(x)U_{\lambda} = \phi_{c}(x) - \lambda\widetilde{\phi}_{c}(x)$, where $\Pi_{c}$ and $\widetilde{\Pi}_{c}$ are conjugate momenta. For the specific choice $\lambda=1$, this transformation eliminates the $\theta_c$ dependence in Hamiltonian terms involving the difference $(\bar{\theta}_c - \theta_c)$.

title: Arc Length Definition
content: For a stripe $j$ modeled as an elastic string with a transverse displacement $Y_j(x)$ relative to the $x$-axis, the arc length $L_j(x)$ from the origin to position $x$ is defined by the integral $L_{j}(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \mathrm{d}x^{\prime} \sqrt{1 + (\partial_{x^{\prime}}Y_{j})^{2}} + L_{j}(0)$.

- $\chi_{\mathrm{CDW}}$: Charge-density-wave susceptibility

- $k_F$: Fermi wavevector
- $\phi_j(x)$: Phase field of the charge-density-wave on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$

- $L_j(x)$: Arc length along the $j$-th stripe from the origin to position $x$

- $\bar{\rho}$: Average charge density
- $\rho_0$: Amplitude of the charge-density-wave fluctuation
- $\Delta_j F$: Finite difference operator defined as $F_{j+1} - F_j$
- $V(\Delta_j Y)$: Coupling potential between charge-density-waves on neighboring stripes $j$ and $j+1$
- $J(\Delta_j Y)$: Josephson tunneling matrix element for superconducting pairs between neighboring stripes
- $J_0$: Base Josephson coupling constant
- $\alpha$: Exponential decay constant for inter-stripe tunneling
- $\hbar\bar{\omega}$: Characteristic energy scale of transverse stripe zero-point fluctuations

title: Assumption: Physical Properties of the Antiferromagnetic Environment
content: The environment is assumed to be an antiferromagnet where hole motion is frustrated, resulting in a small propagation velocity $\widetilde{v}_{c}$ for charge excitations and a small Luttinger parameter $\bar{K}_{c}$ due to strong effective interactions between holes. Under these conditions, the kinetic energy (bandwidth) of holon pairs in the environment is neglected, and these excitations are characterized by a single renormalized energy scale $\varepsilon^{*}$.

title: Definition of Pseudospin Operators
content: The charge degrees of freedom of the environment are represented by discrete pseudospin operators $\tau_{R}$ defined on a lattice with constant $\xi_p$. The eigenvalue $\tau_{R}^{z} = +1/2$ indicates the presence of a holon pair in the environment near site $R$, while $\tau_{R}^{z} = -1/2$ indicates its absence; the shifted operator $\tau_{R}^{z} + 1/2$ represents the local holon pair density.

- $\bar{K}_{c}$: Luttinger parameter characterizing the environmental charge sector.
- $\varepsilon^{*}$: Renormalized excitation energy of a holon pair in the environment.
- $\tau_{R}$: Pseudospin operators at lattice site $R$.
- $\xi_p$: Lattice constant of the pseudospin array.

title: Unrenormalized stripe charge sector in the decoupling limit
content: In the decoupling limit produced by a unitary transformation that separates spin and charge degrees of freedom, the stripe (one-dimensional electron gas) charge sector is a Gaussian Luttinger-liquid with a soundlike dispersion whose charge velocity $v_c$ is not renormalized by the coupling to the environment; consequently the zero-temperature Drude weight (superfluid phase stiffness) of the stripe is unrenormalized, while the strong-coupling/"heavy" renormalizations are confined to the spin sector.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted a specific transformed Hamiltonian produced by a unitary $U_{1}$ and detailed coupling terms (product of cosine spin fields, explicit $\partial_x\phi_c$ coupling to $\tau_R^z$). Child 645 does not derive or state that Hamiltonian; it reports the pseudospin representation ($\tau_R^z$ labels holon‑pair occupation), identifies $J_{sp}$ and $\varepsilon^{*}$, and gives mean‑field scaling relations for $T_{pair}$ and $\Delta_s(0)$. To ensure the parent is entailed by the child, I removed the unsupported technical claims about the explicit $U_{1}$ transformation and the detailed Hamiltonian terms, and kept only the substantive assertions that are present in the child: the pseudospin labeling, the roles of $J_{sp}$ and $\varepsilon^{*}$, and the mean‑field scaling relations. The revised parent is self‑contained, factual, and fully supported by Child 645.

The original parent asserted a general unitary-transformed effective Hamiltonian and parameter-dependent terms (nonzero $\Delta V_c$ and general $\varepsilon^*$) that are not supported by Child 618, which explicitly works in the decoupling limit $\Delta V_c=0$ and at $\varepsilon^*=0$. To ensure the parent is entailed by Child 618, I removed all claims about a general unitary transformation, any $\Delta V_c$-dependent coupling, and nonzero $\varepsilon^*$, and restricted the statement to the decoupling/$\varepsilon^*=0$ regime. The revised parent contains only claims present in Child 618: the pseudospin representation, the identification $\tau_R^z=+1/2$ with a holon pair, the exact solvability and fully gapped paired-spin-liquid ground state in that limit, and the induced spin-gap formula with the definition of $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ and exponent $\alpha$. This eliminates the union errors and makes the parent both substantive and entailed by the child.

The original parent included specific model-building claims (a pseudospin mapping of environmental charge fluctuations, the identification $\tau_R^z=+1/2$ with a holon pair, and an explicit transformed Hamiltonian with $J_{sp}\tau^x\cos[\ldots]$ and a $\{\varepsilon^*-2\sqrt{2/\pi}\Delta V_c\partial_x\phi_c\}[\tau_R^z+1/2]$ coupling) that are not supported by the single child (Node 637). To ensure every child entails the parent, those unsupported, detailed assertions were removed. The revised parent keeps only the intersectional, substantive physical claims that the child does make: in the decoupling limit the stripe charge sector remains Gaussian with unrenormalized $v_c$ and Drude weight, and the heavy/strong-coupling physics resides in the spin sector. This rewrite therefore fixes the union errors and makes the parent fully entailed by the child.

extra.notations:
- $v_c$: charge propagation velocity of the stripe (1DEG)
      - Drude weight: zero-temperature charge stiffness (superfluid phase stiffness)
      - 1DEG: one-dimensional electron gas (stripe)

title: Transformed Pseudospin Hamiltonian
content:
In the context of a one-dimensional electron gas ($1DEG$) coupled to an active environment, the transformed Hamiltonian of the pseudospin model, $U_1^{\dagger}H_{pseudo}U_1$, is expressed in terms of pseudospin operators $\tau_R$ (where $\tau_R^z = +1/2$ indicates the presence of a holon pair at site $R$ and $-1/2$ its absence) as follows:
$ U_1^{\dagger}H_{pseudo}U_1 = H_{1DEG} + \bar{H}_s + \sum_R J_{sp} \tau_R^x \cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_s] \cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\bar{\phi}_s] + \sum_R \left\{ \varepsilon^* - 2\sqrt{2/\pi}\Delta V_c \partial_x \phi_c \right\} [\tau_R^z + 1/2] $
where $H_{1DEG}$ is the Hamiltonian of the $1DEG$ stripe, $\bar{H}_s$ is the Hamiltonian for environmental spin degrees of freedom, $J_{sp}$ is the pair hopping coupling, $\phi_s$ and $\bar{\phi}_s$ are the spin fields of the $1DEG$ and environment respectively, $\phi_c$ is the charge field of the $1DEG$, $\varepsilon^*$ is the renormalized excitation energy, and $\Delta V_c$ is the charge interaction parameter.

extra.notations:
- $1DEG$: One-dimensional electron gas (representing a metallic stripe)
- $\tau_R$: Pseudospin operator at site $R$, where $\tau_R^z = +1/2$ signifies a holon pair in the environment and $-1/2$ signifies its absence
- $H_{1DEG}$: Hamiltonian of the 1DEG/stripe charge and spin degrees of freedom
- $\bar{H}_s$: Hamiltonian for the environmental spin degrees of freedom
- $J_{sp}$: Pair hopping coupling between the stripe and the environment
- $\phi_s$: Spin field of the 1DEG
- $\bar{\phi}_s$: Spin field of the environment
- $\phi_c$: Charge field of the 1DEG
- $\varepsilon^*$: Renormalized charge transfer energy (energy cost to move a pair of holes)
- $\Delta V_c$: Effective charge interaction parameter between the 1DEG and the environment
- $v_s, \tilde{v}_s$: Spin velocities for the stripe and environment, respectively
- $K_s, \tilde{K}_s$: Luttinger liquid spin exponents for the stripe and environment, respectively
- $a$: Lattice cutoff/constant
- $g_1, \tilde{g}_1$: Backscattering coupling constants for the stripe and environment, respectively
- $\overline{\Delta}_s$: Induced spin gap in the 1DEG
- $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: Effective coupling defined as $J_{sp}\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\tilde{\phi}_s]\rangle$
- $\alpha$: Scaling exponent for the spin gap, equal to $2/(4-K_s)$

title: Spin Hamiltonian Components
content:
For a system consisting of a metallic stripe and its environment, the general spin Hamiltonian is defined as $\mathcal{H}_s \equiv \mathcal{H}_s^0 + \mathcal{H}_s^1 + \mathcal{H}_s^2$. The non-interacting part $\mathcal{H}_s^0$ describes the quadratic fluctuations of the spin fields $\phi_s$ (stripe) and $\tilde{\phi}_s$ (environment) and their conjugate momenta $\Pi_s, \tilde{\Pi}_s$:
$ \mathcal{H}_s^0 = \frac{v_s}{2}\left[ K_s\Pi_s^2 + \frac{1}{K_s}(\partial_x \phi_s)^2 \right] + \frac{\tilde{v}_s}{2}\left[ \tilde{K}_s\tilde{\Pi}_s^2 + \frac{1}{\tilde{K}_s}(\partial_x \tilde{\phi}_s)^2 \right] $
The interaction part $\mathcal{H}_s^2$ contains non-linear cosine terms that can lead to gaps:
$ \mathcal{H}_s^2 = \frac{2g_1}{(2\pi a)^2}\cos[\sqrt{8\pi}\phi_s] + \frac{2\tilde{g}_1}{(2\pi a)^2}\cos[\sqrt{8\pi}\tilde{\phi}_s] + \dots $
where $v_s, \tilde{v}_s$ are spin velocities, $K_s, \tilde{K}_s$ are critical exponents, $g_1, \tilde{g}_1$ are backscattering couplings, and $a$ is the lattice cutoff.

title: Solution of the Sine-Gordon Model
content:
For a spin field governed by a sine-Gordon Hamiltonian with effective pair-tunneling coupling $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$, the excitation spectrum consists of massive solitons and antisolitons with energies $E_s(k) = \pm\sqrt{(v_s k)^2 + \overline{\Delta}_s^2}$. The spin gap $\overline{\Delta}_s$ scales with the coupling $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ according to the relation:
$ \overline{\Delta}_s \sim \frac{v_s}{a} \left[ \frac{\mathcal{J}_{sp} a}{v_s} \right]^\alpha $
where the exponent $\alpha = 2/(4 - K_s)$ is determined by the Luttinger liquid parameter $K_s$, provided $K_s < 4$.

title: Assumption: Decoupling Limit and Zero Charge Transfer
content: The system is analyzed in a "decoupling limit" where the effective charge interaction $\Delta V_c$ is zero and the renormalized charge transfer energy $\varepsilon^*$ is zero, which allows the $1DEG$ charge degrees of freedom to decouple from the pseudospin and spin dynamics.

title: Assumption: Large Environmental Spin Gap
content: The environmental spin sector is assumed to possess a pre-existing large spin gap $\tilde{\Delta}_s$, characterized by a Luttinger parameter $\tilde{K}_s < 1$ and a large negative backscattering coupling $\tilde{g}_1$, such that the environmental spin field $\tilde{\phi}_s$ is pinned and its fluctuations are negligible at low energies.

title: Exact solution and generation of a paired spin liquid state
content:
For the pseudospin model in the decoupling limit ($\Delta V_c = 0$) and at zero charge transfer energy ($\varepsilon^* = 0$), the authors demonstrate an exact solution where the ground state is a fully gapped "paired spin liquid." In the case where the environment has a pre-existing large spin gap $\bar{\Delta}_s$, the induced spin gap $\Delta_s$ in the 1DEG is given by:
      $ \Delta_{s} \sim \frac{v_{s}}{a}\bigg[\frac{\mathcal{J}_{s p}a}{v_{s}}\bigg]^{\alpha} $
      where $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ is the effective coupling constant, $v_s$ is the spin velocity, and $\alpha = 2/(4-K_s)$. This demonstrates that the spin gap is a robust consequence of the coupling to the environment, identifying the "pairing" as the formation of a spin gap in the 1DEG.

title: Bosonization of Fermionic Fields
content: In a one-dimensional system, the fermion fields $\psi_{s,\sigma}$ at branch $s \in \{1, 2\}$ with spin $\sigma \in \{\uparrow, \downarrow\}$ are represented by Bose fields $\phi_{\sigma}$ and their duals $\theta_{\sigma}$ as $\psi_{s,\sigma}^{\dagger}(x) = (2\pi a)^{-1/2} \exp\{i\sqrt{\pi}[\theta_{\sigma}(x) \mp \phi_{\sigma}(x)]\}$, where the minus sign applies to $s=1$ and the plus sign to $s=2$. These are decomposed into charge ($c$) and spin ($s$) sectors via $\phi_{c,s}(x) = [\phi_{\uparrow}(x) \pm \phi_{\downarrow}(x)]/\sqrt{2}$ and $\Pi_{c,s}(x) = [\Pi_{\uparrow}(x) \pm \Pi_{\downarrow}(x)]/\sqrt{2}$, where $\Pi$ is the conjugate momentum to $\phi$. The resulting charge density $\rho(x)$, charge current $j_c(x)$, spin density $S^z(x)$, and spin current $j_s^z(x)$ are given by $\rho(x) = -\sqrt{2/\pi}\partial_x\phi_c$, $j_c(x) = \sqrt{2/\pi}\Pi_c$, $S^z(x) = -\sqrt{1/2\pi}\partial_x\phi_s$, and $j_s^z(x) = \sqrt{1/2\pi}\Pi_s$.

extra.notations:
- $s$: branch index (1 for right-moving, 2 for left-moving)
- $\sigma$: spin index ($\uparrow, \downarrow$)
- $x$: spatial coordinate
- $a$: short-distance cutoff (lattice constant)
- $\theta_{c,s}, \phi_{c,s}$: dual bosonic fields for charge ($c$) and spin ($s$) sectors
- $\Pi_{c,s}$: conjugate momentum fields to $\phi_{c,s}$
- $v_{c,s}$: propagation velocities for charge and spin excitations
- $K_{c,s}$: Luttinger liquid parameters/critical exponents

- $t_{sp}, t_{tp}$: singlet and triplet pair hopping amplitudes
- $J_{sp}$: effective pair-tunneling matrix element
- $\tau_R^x, \tau_R^z$: pseudospin-1/2 operators representing environmental charge occupancy at site $R$
- $\varepsilon^*$: renormalized energy cost for charge transfer to the environment
- $V_c, J_c$: charge-density and charge-current coupling constants
- $H_{1DEG}$: Hamiltonian of the one-dimensional electron gas (metallic stripe)

title: Inter-stripe CDW Coupling Hamiltonian
content: The coupling Hamiltonian $H_c$ describing the interaction between charge-density-waves on adjacent stripes in a two-dimensional array is given by $H_{c} = \sum_{j} \int \mathrm{d}x V(\Delta_{j}Y) \cos[\sqrt{2\pi}(\Delta_{j}\phi) - 2k_{\mathrm{F}}(\Delta_{j}L)]$, where $\Delta_j F = F_{j+1} - F_j$ denotes the difference in a quantity $F$ between neighboring stripes, $L_j$ is the arc length, $\phi_j$ is the charge-density-wave phase, and $V(\Delta_j Y)$ is a coupling function dependent on the local transverse separation $\Delta_j Y = Y_{j+1} - Y_j$.

extra.notations:
- $K_c$: Luttinger liquid parameter for the charge degrees of freedom
- $\Delta_s$: Spin gap magnitude
- $\chi_{\mathrm{CDW}}$: Charge-density-wave susceptibility
- $\chi_{\mathrm{SC}}$: Superconducting susceptibility
- $k_F$: Fermi wavevector
- $\phi_j(x)$: Phase field of the charge-density-wave on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $Y_j(x)$: Transverse displacement of the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $L_j(x)$: Arc length along the $j$-th stripe from the origin to position $x$
- $\rho_j(x)$: Local charge density on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $\bar{\rho}$: Average charge density
- $\rho_0$: Amplitude of the charge-density-wave fluctuation
- $\Delta_j F$: Finite difference operator defined as $F_{j+1} - F_j$
- $V(\Delta_j Y)$: Coupling potential between charge-density-waves on neighboring stripes $j$ and $j+1$
- $J(\Delta_j Y)$: Josephson tunneling matrix element for superconducting pairs between neighboring stripes
- $J_0$: Base Josephson coupling constant
- $\alpha$: Exponential decay constant for inter-stripe tunneling
- $\hbar\bar{\omega}$: Characteristic energy scale of transverse stripe zero-point fluctuations

title: Pair Hopping Hamiltonian
content: The interaction between a one-dimensional metallic stripe (1DEG) and its environment is modeled by a pair hopping Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H}_{pair} = t_{sp}[P^{\dagger}\tilde{\mathcal{P}} + \mathrm{H.c.}] + t_{tp}\sum_{m=-1}^{1}[P_m^{\dagger}\tilde{\mathcal{P}}_m + \mathrm{H.c.}]$, where $P^{\dagger}(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[\psi_{1,\uparrow}^{\dagger}(x)\psi_{2,\downarrow}^{\dagger}(x) + \psi_{2,\uparrow}^{\dagger}(x)\psi_{1,\downarrow}^{\dagger}(x)]$ is the singlet-pair creation operator for the 1DEG and $\tilde{\mathcal{P}}$ denotes the corresponding pair operator in the environment.

title: Unitary Transformation U_lambda
content: For a system of two coupled one-dimensional sectors (1DEG and environment), a unitary transformation $U_{\lambda} = \exp[-i\lambda \int dx \theta_c(x) \partial_x \tilde{\phi}_c(x)]$ shifts the fields such that $U_{\lambda}^{\dagger}\tilde{\Pi}_c U_{\lambda} = \tilde{\Pi}_c + \lambda \Pi_c$, $U_{\lambda}^{\dagger}\phi_c U_{\lambda} = \phi_c - \lambda \tilde{\phi}_c$, and leaves $\tilde{\phi}_c$ and $\Pi_c$ invariant, where $\theta, \phi$ and $\tilde{\theta}, \tilde{\phi}$ are the bosonic fields of the 1DEG and environment respectively.

title: Pseudospin Representation of Environmental Charge
content: In the limit of small kinetic energy for environmental excitations, the charge degrees of freedom of the environment are mapped to a lattice of pseudospins $\vec{\tau}_R$ with spacing $\xi_p$, where $\tau_R^z = 1/2$ indicates the presence of a holon pair at site $R$ and $\tau_R^z = -1/2$ indicates its absence. The unitary transformation $U$ that decouples the charge sector of the 1DEG (with $\lambda=1$) is represented in this basis as $U = \exp(-i\sqrt{2\pi}\sum_R \tau_R^z \theta_c)$.

title: Transformed Pseudospin Hamiltonian
content: Applying the unitary transformation $U$ to the pseudospin Hamiltonian $H_{pseudo}$ in the decoupling limit (where the transformed charge-charge coupling $\Delta V_c = 0$) yields $U^{\dagger}H_{pseudo}U = H_{1DEG} + \tilde{H}_s + \sum_R J_{sp} \tau_R^x \cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_s] \cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\tilde{\phi}_s] + \sum_R \varepsilon^* [\tau_R^z + 1/2]$, where $\tilde{H}_s$ is the environmental spin Hamiltonian and $J_{sp} \tau_R^x$ originates from the transformation of the pair hopping term.

title: Definition of Tau Symmetry
content: The Hamiltonian $H_{pseudo}$ possesses a discrete $Z_2$ symmetry, designated $\tau$ symmetry, defined by the joint transformation of the 1DEG spin field $\phi_s$ and the environmental dual fields $\tilde{\theta}_c, \tilde{\theta}_s$ such that $\tilde{\theta}_c \to \tilde{\theta}_c + \sqrt{\pi/2}$, $\tilde{\theta}_s \to \tilde{\theta}_s + \sqrt{\pi/2}$, and $\phi_s \to \phi_s + \sqrt{\pi/2}$.

- $\varepsilon^*$: renormalized energy cost for charge transfer to the environment
- $V_c, J_c$: charge-density and charge-current coupling constants
- $H_{1DEG}$: Hamiltonian of the one-dimensional electron gas (metallic stripe)

title: Constraint on Continuous Symmetry Breaking
content: In one-dimensional systems, the Hohenberg-Coleman-Mermin-Wagner theorem dictates that a continuous symmetry cannot be spontaneously broken at any non-zero temperature, and at zero temperature, long-range order is restricted to discrete symmetries.

title: Composite nonlocal order parameter and Broken Z(2) symmetry
content:
The authors identify that the superconducting order in this model is characterized by a composite order parameter $O_{comp}$ that cannot be expressed as a local function of the original physical fields. Defined via the unitary transformation $U_1$, the order parameter is:
$ O_{comp} = U_{1}\widetilde{\psi}_{1,\downarrow}\widetilde{\psi}_{2,\downarrow}U_{1}^{\dagger} = (2\pi a)^{-1}\exp[-i\sqrt{2\pi}(\widetilde{\theta}_{c}-\theta_{c}+i\widetilde{\phi}_{s})] $
The authors establish that long-range composite order implies a spontaneously broken discrete $Z(2)$ symmetry (termed $\tau$ symmetry) in the ground state, distinct from the usual space-time symmetries, with an order parameter correlation function that exhibits long-range order at zero temperature:
$ \langle U^{\dagger}\tau_{R}^{x}\tau_{R}^{ x},U\rangle \approx (m_{\tau})^{2}\exp[-|R-R^{\prime}|/\xi_{\tau}(T)] $
where $\xi_{\tau}(T)$ diverges as $T \to 0$.

title: Bosonized Hamiltonian decomposition
content: The total Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H}$ of a one-dimensional electron gas (1DEG) coupled to an active environment is decomposed into independent charge ($c$) and spin ($s$) sectors, plus a pair transfer term: $\mathcal{H}=\mathcal{H}_{c}+\mathcal{H}_{s}+\mathcal{H}_{pair}$. Here, $\mathcal{H}_{c}$ describes the charge degrees of freedom of both the 1DEG and the environment, $\mathcal{H}_{s}$ describes their spin degrees of freedom, and $\mathcal{H}_{pair}$ represents the hopping of electron pairs between the 1DEG and the environment.

extra.notations:
- $c, s$: Indices for charge and spin degrees of freedom, respectively.
- $\mathcal{H}_{c}, \mathcal{H}_{s}$: Hamiltonians for the charge and spin sectors of the one-dimensional electron gas (1DEG) and its environment.
- $\phi_{c}, \theta_{c}$: Boson field and its dual field representing charge degrees of freedom in the 1DEG.
- $\Pi_{c}$: Canonical momentum conjugate to the charge field $\phi_{c}$.
- $\widetilde{\phi}_{c}, \widetilde{\theta}_{c}$: Boson field and its dual field representing charge degrees of freedom in the environment.
- $\widetilde{\Pi}_{c}$: Canonical momentum conjugate to the environmental charge field $\widetilde{\phi}_{c}$.
- $v_{c}, K_{c}$: Charge velocity and Luttinger liquid exponent for the 1DEG.
- $\widetilde{v}_{c}, \widetilde{K}_{c}$: Charge velocity and Luttinger liquid exponent for the environment.
- $V_{c}, J_{c}$: Coupling constants for charge density-density and current-current interactions between the 1DEG and the environment.
- $a$: Short-distance cutoff or lattice spacing.
- $k_{F}, G$: Fermi wave vector of the 1DEG and the reciprocal lattice vector, respectively.
- $g_{3}, \widetilde{g}_{3}$: Umklapp scattering coupling constants for the 1DEG and the environment.
- $\widetilde{\mu}$: Chemical potential in the environment.
- $t_{sp}, t_{tp}$: Singlet and triplet pair hopping (tunneling) amplitudes between the 1DEG and the environment.

- $U_{\lambda}$: Unitary transformation operator.
- $\Delta V_{c}, \Delta J_{c}$: Renormalized charge coupling constants after the unitary transformation $U_{\lambda}$.
- $v_{c}\gamma, K_{c}\gamma$: Rescaled charge velocity and Luttinger exponent for the 1DEG after transformation.
- $\mu^{*}$: Effective chemical potential in the nearly commensurate regime.

title: Charge Hamiltonian Definition
content: The charge sector of the 1DEG and its environment is modeled by the Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H}_{c}=\mathcal{H}_{c}^{0}+\mathcal{H}_{c}^{1}+\mathcal{H}_{c}^{2}$. The quadratic parts for the individual systems are $\mathcal{H}_{c}^{0}=\frac{v_{c}}{2}[K_{c}\Pi_{c}^{2}+\frac{1}{K_{c}}(\partial_{x}\phi_{c})^{2}]+\frac{\widetilde{v_{c}}}{2}[\widetilde{K}_{c}\widetilde{\Pi}_{c}^{2}+\frac{1}{\widetilde{K}_{c}}(\partial_{x}\widetilde{\phi}_{c})^{2}]$, where ($v_c, K_c$) and ($\widetilde{v}_c, \widetilde{K}_c$) are the velocities and Luttinger parameters for the 1DEG and environment charge modes. The interaction term is $\mathcal{H}_{c}^{1}=\frac{2J_{c}}{\pi}\Pi_{c}\widetilde{\Pi}_{c}+\frac{2V_{c}}{\pi}\partial_{x}\phi_{c}\partial_{x}\widetilde{\phi}_{c}$ with coupling constants $V_c, J_c$. The nonlinear part $\mathcal{H}_{c}^{2}=\frac{2g_{3}}{(2\pi a)^{2}}\cos[\sqrt{8\pi}\phi_{c}-(4k_{F}-G)x]+\frac{2\widetilde{g_{3}}}{(2\pi a)^{2}}\cos[\sqrt{8\pi}\widetilde{\phi}_{c}]-\widetilde{\mu}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\partial_{x}\widetilde{\phi}_{c}$ includes umklapp scattering and chemical potential effects.

title: Unitary Transformation Definition
content: A unitary transformation is defined by the operator $U_{\lambda}=\exp[-i\lambda\int d x\theta_{c}(x)\partial_{x}\widetilde{\phi}_{c}(x)]$, where $\theta_c(x)$ is the dual field to the 1DEG charge field $\phi_c(x)$, and $\widetilde{\phi}_c(x)$ is the boson field representing the charge degrees of freedom in the environment. This transformation is parameterized by $\lambda$ to decouple or modify the interaction terms between the 1DEG and the environment.

title: Josephson Tunneling Matrix Element
content: The Josephson tunneling matrix element $J(\Delta_j Y)$, representing the hopping of superconducting pairs between adjacent stripes $j$ and $j+1$, is determined by the local transverse spacing $\Delta_j Y = Y_{j+1} - Y_j$ according to the relation $J(\Delta_{j}Y) \approx J_{0} \exp[-\alpha\Delta_{j}Y]$, where $J_0$ and $\alpha$ are constant parameters. This matrix element is independent of the stripe arc length $L_j$.

extra.notations:
- $K_c$: Luttinger liquid parameter for the charge degrees of freedom
- $\Delta_s$: Spin gap magnitude
- $\chi_{\mathrm{CDW}}$: Charge-density-wave susceptibility
- $\chi_{\mathrm{SC}}$: Superconducting susceptibility
- $k_F$: Fermi wavevector
- $\phi_j(x)$: Phase field of the charge-density-wave on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $Y_j(x)$: Transverse displacement of the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $L_j(x)$: Arc length along the $j$-th stripe from the origin to position $x$
- $\rho_j(x)$: Local charge density on the $j$-th stripe at position $x$
- $\bar{\rho}$: Average charge density
- $\rho_0$: Amplitude of the charge-density-wave fluctuation
- $\Delta_j F$: Finite difference operator defined as $F_{j+1} - F_j$
- $V(\Delta_j Y)$: Coupling potential between charge-density-waves on neighboring stripes $j$ and $j+1$
- $J(\Delta_j Y)$: Josephson tunneling matrix element for superconducting pairs between neighboring stripes
- $J_0$: Base Josephson coupling constant
- $\alpha$: Exponential decay constant for inter-stripe tunneling
- $\hbar\bar{\omega}$: Characteristic energy scale of transverse stripe zero-point fluctuations

title: Transformation of Charge Fields
content: Under the action of the unitary transformation $U_{\lambda}=\exp[-i\lambda\int d x\theta_{c}(x)\partial_{x}\widetilde{\phi}_{c}(x)]$, the boson fields and their conjugate momenta are shifted as follows: $U_{\lambda}^{\dagger}\widetilde{\Pi}_{c}(x)U_{\lambda} = \widetilde{\Pi}_{c}(x)+\lambda\Pi_{c}(x)$, $U_{\lambda}^{\dagger}\widetilde{\phi}_{c}(x)U_{\lambda} = \widetilde{\phi}_{c}(x)$, $U_{\lambda}^{\dagger}\Pi_{c}(x)U_{\lambda} = \Pi_{c}(x)$, and $U_{\lambda}^{\dagger}\phi_{c}(x)U_{\lambda} = \phi_{c}(x)-\lambda\widetilde{\phi}_{c}(x)$.

title: Renormalization of Charge Interactions
content: The unitary transformation $U_{\lambda}$ transforms the charge interaction parameters $V_c$ and $J_c$ into effective parameters $\Delta V_{c}=V_{c}-\frac{\pi}{2}\frac{\lambda v_{c}}{K_{c}}$ and $\Delta J_{c}=J_{c}-\frac{\pi}{2}\lambda\widetilde{v}_{c}\widetilde{K}_{c}$, where $v_c, K_c$ are the 1DEG charge velocity and Luttinger parameter, and $\widetilde{v}_c, \widetilde{K}_c$ are the environmental charge velocity and Luttinger parameter.

title: Decoupling Limit Definition
content: The decoupling limit of the charge Hamiltonian is achieved when the parameters are chosen such that the quadratic coupling between the 1DEG and the environment is diagonalized. This occurs when $\lambda=2V_{c}K_{c}/(\pi v_{c})$ and $J_{c}=-\widetilde{v}_{c}\widetilde{K}_{c}K_{c}V_{c}/v_{c}$. A specific "decoupling point" frequently utilized is $\lambda=1$, which corresponds to the case where the transformed interaction parameters $\Delta V_c$ and $\Delta J_c$ vanish.

title: Effect of Transformation on Pair Hopping at Lambda=1
content: At the specific transformation parameter $\lambda=1$, the unitary transformation $U_{1}$ acts on the charge phase difference as $U_{1}^{\dagger}(\widetilde{\theta}_{c}-\theta_{c})U_{1}=\widetilde{\theta}_{c}$. Consequently, the transformed pair hopping Hamiltonian $U_{1}^{\dagger}\mathcal{H}_{pair}U_{1}$ becomes independent of the 1DEG charge phase $\theta_c$, effectively decoupling the 1DEG charge degrees of freedom from the pair-transfer dynamics.

title: Charge Hamiltonian in Incommensurate Limit
content: In the strongly incommensurate limit of the 1DEG, characterized by a large effective chemical potential $\mu^{*} = v_{c}(4k_{F}-G)/(4K_{c})$, the umklapp scattering term in the Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H}_{c}^{2}$ (proportional to $g_3$) oscillates rapidly and can be neglected in the low-energy effective theory.

title: Drude Weight Definition
content: The Drude weight, which characterizes the superfluid phase stiffness of the 1DEG collective charge modes, is determined by the unrenormalized charge velocity $v_c$ and Luttinger parameter $K_c$ according to the relation $D \propto v_c K_c$. In the absence of an energy gap in the charge sector, this quantity represents the stiffness against phase fluctuations.

title: Absence of mass renormalization for charge degrees of freedom
content: The authors derive that, in the decoupling limit where the unitary transformation separates spin and charge, the charge degrees of freedom of the 1DEG are described by a Gaussian model with a soundlike dispersion and velocity $v_c$ that is unrenormalized by the interactions with the environment. Consequently, the Drude weight (or zero-temperature superfluid phase stiffness) is unrenormalized, avoiding the large mass renormalization typically associated with strong-coupling models (such as heavy-fermion behavior), while the "heavy" physics is confined solely to the spin degrees of freedom.

title: Effective Pseudospin Hamiltonian
content:
In a model of a one-dimensional electron gas ($1DEG$) coupled to a magnetic insulating environment where the environmental spin degrees of freedom are integrated out due to a large preexisting spin gap, the effective Hamiltonian $H_{pseudo}$ is defined as:
\[ H_{pseudo}=H_{1DEG}+\sum_{R}\mathcal{J}_{sp}\tau_{R}^{x}+\sum_{R}\left[\varepsilon+2V_{c}\rho_{R}\right]\left[\tau_{R}^{z}+1/2\right] \]
Here, $H_{1DEG}$ is the 1DEG Hamiltonian, $\tau_{R}^{x,z}$ are pseudospin operators representing environmental charge transfer states at site $R$, $\rho_R$ is the local charge density of the 1DEG, and $V_c$ is the coupling between the 1DEG density and environmental charge. The effective coupling is defined by $\mathcal{J}_{s p}\equiv J_{s p}\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\widetilde{\phi}_{s}]\rangle$, where $\widetilde{\phi}_{s}$ is the environmental spin field. The renormalized charge transfer energy is given by $\varepsilon^* = \varepsilon - 2V_c$.

extra.notations:
- $1DEG$: One-Dimensional Electron Gas, representing a metallic stripe in the model.
- $H_{1DEG}$: Hamiltonian of the 1DEG.
- $\tau_{R}$: Pseudospin operators at position $R$, where $\tau^x$ represents charge transfer and $\tau^z$ represents the state of a holon pair in the environment.
- $\rho_{R}$: Charge density of the 1DEG at position $R$.
- $J_{sp}$: Bare pair-tunneling coupling constant between the 1DEG and the environment.
- $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: Effective coupling constant renormalized by environmental spin fluctuations.
- $\widetilde{\phi}_{s}$: Dual bosonic field representing the spin degrees of freedom in the environment.
- $\varepsilon$: Bare energy required to transfer a pair of holes from the 1DEG to the environment.
- $V_{c}$: Forward-scattering Coulomb interaction between the 1DEG and the environment.
- $\varepsilon^*$: Renormalized charge transfer energy.
- $\Delta_{s}(T)$: Temperature-dependent spin gap in the 1DEG.
- $\delta(T)$: Temperature-dependent creation energy of a pseudospin flip.
- $\phi_{s}$: Bosonic field representing the spin degrees of freedom in the 1DEG.
- $E_{b}(T)$: Binding energy scale for the pseudospin excitations.
- $\beta$: Inverse temperature $1/T$.
- $a$: Lattice constant or short-distance cutoff.
- $K_s$: Luttinger liquid parameter for the spin degrees of freedom in the 1DEG.
- $\alpha$: Exponent characterizing the spin correlations, defined as $2/(4-K_s)$.
- $v_s$: Spin-wave velocity in the 1DEG.
- $\rho(E_F)$: Density of states at the Fermi level.
- $T_{pair}$: Crossover temperature at which the spin gap $\Delta_s$ begins to deviate from its $T=0$ value.

title: Mean-Field Equations for the Pseudospin Model
content:
Under the mean-field approximation for the pseudospin model, the self-consistent state of the system is determined by the temperature-dependent spin gap $\Delta_{s}(T)$ and the pseudospin flip energy $\delta(T)$ according to the following equations:
\[ \delta(T)=(2\mathcal{J}_{s p}/\pi)\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_{s}]\rangle \]
\[ \Delta_{s}(T)=\overline{\Delta}_{s}[2\langle\tau_{R}^{x}\rangle]^{\alpha} \]
\[ \langle U^{\dagger}\tau_{R}^{x}U\rangle=(\delta/4E_{b})\tanh[\beta E_{b}(T)] \]
\[ E_{b}(T)=\sqrt{(\varepsilon^{*})^{2}+(\delta/2)^{2}} \]
where $\beta = 1/T$, $U$ is a unitary transformation decoupling the charge modes, and $\overline{\Delta}_{s}$ is the spin gap value at $T=0$ and $\varepsilon^{*}=0$.

title: Definitions of Electronic Liquid Crystal Phases
content:
The zero-temperature phases of a doped Mott insulator on a two-dimensional square lattice are classified by the breaking of spatial point-group and translational symmetries: 
1. The Liquid phase breaks no spatial symmetries and acts as a conductor or superconductor. 
2. The Nematic phase breaks the four-fold rotational symmetry ($C_4$) of the lattice while preserving translation and reflection symmetries. 
3. The Smectic phase breaks translational symmetry in exactly one direction while maintaining liquid-like properties in the orthogonal direction. 
4. The Crystalline phase breaks translational symmetry in all directions, resulting in an insulating electronic solid.

extra.notations:
- $K_c$: Luttinger liquid parameter for the charge degrees of freedom
- $\Delta_s$: Spin gap magnitude
- $\chi_{\mathrm{CDW}}$: Charge-density-wave susceptibility
- $\chi_{\mathrm{SC}}$: Superconducting susceptibility
- $k_F$: Fermi wavevector

- $\bar{\rho}$: Average charge density

- $\Delta_j F$: Finite difference operator defined as $F_{j+1} - F_j$
- $V(\Delta_j Y)$: Coupling potential between charge-density-waves on neighboring stripes $j$ and $j+1$
- $J(\Delta_j Y)$: Josephson tunneling matrix element for superconducting pairs between neighboring stripes
- $J_0$: Base Josephson coupling constant
- $\alpha$: Exponential decay constant for inter-stripe tunneling
- $\hbar\bar{\omega}$: Characteristic energy scale of transverse stripe zero-point fluctuations

title: Sine-Gordon Expectation Value at Low Temperature
content:
In the regime where the temperature $T$ is small compared to the 1DEG spin gap ($\Delta_{s}(T) \gg T$), the expectation value of the cosine term in the sine-Gordon theory of the 1DEG spin degrees of freedom is approximated by:
\[ \langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_{s}]\rangle\approx(\pi a\overline{\delta}/2\mathcal{J}_{s p})[2\langle U^{\dagger}\tau_{R}^{x}U\rangle]^{(2\alpha-1)} \]
where $a$ is the lattice constant, $\overline{\delta}$ is the zero-temperature pseudospin flip energy, and $\alpha = 2/(4-K_s)$ is the correlation exponent determined by the 1DEG Luttinger parameter $K_s$.

title: Spin Correlation Exponent
content: For a 1DEG with repulsive interactions where spin-rotation invariance is preserved, the Luttinger parameter $K_s$ approaches 1 at low energy, resulting in the spin correlation exponent $\alpha = 2/(4-K_s)$ taking the specific value of $\alpha = 2/3$.

title: Zero-Field Gap Scales
content:
At the decoupling limit where the renormalized charge transfer energy $\varepsilon^*=0$, the characteristic energy scales for the spin gap $\overline{\Delta}_s$ and the pseudospin flip energy $\overline{\delta}$ are defined by:
\[ \overline{\Delta}_{s}\sim\frac{v_{s}}{a}\bigg[\frac{\mathcal{J}_{s p}a}{v_{s}}\bigg]^{\alpha} \]
\[ \overline{\delta}=(\mathcal{J}_{s p}/\pi a)\langle\cos(\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_{s})\rangle\approx\rho(E_{F})\overline{\Delta}_{s}^{2} \]
where $v_s$ is the spin velocity and $\rho(E_F)$ is the density of states. For $\alpha=2/3$, these scales follow the power-law relations $\overline{\Delta}_{s}\sim\mathcal{J}_{sp}^{2/3}$ and $\overline{\delta}\sim\mathcal{J}_{sp}^{1/3}$.

title: Definition of Pairing Temperature
content: The crossover temperature $T_{pair}$ is defined as the thermal scale at which the 1DEG spin gap $\Delta_{s}(T)$ undergoes a significant reduction from its zero-temperature value $\Delta_s(0)$ due to thermal fluctuations of the pseudospin and spin fields.

title: Assumption: Large Environmental Spin Gap
content: The environment is assumed to possess a large, preexisting spin gap $\widetilde{\Delta}_s$, such that for energies $E \ll \widetilde{\Delta}_s$, the environmental spin field $\widetilde{\phi}_s$ is effectively pinned, allowing the environmental spin degrees of freedom to be integrated out and replaced by the constant expectation value $\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\widetilde{\phi}_{s}]\rangle$ in the effective coupling $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$.

title: Temperature scales and dependence on charge transfer energy
content: Solving the mean-field equations for the pseudospin model, the authors determine the dependence of the pairing crossover temperature $T_{pair}$ (where the spin gap $\Delta_s(T)$ develops) on the charge transfer energy $\varepsilon^*$. They find that for small $\varepsilon^*$, $T_{pair}$ is an increasing function of $\varepsilon^*$, reaching a maximum when $\varepsilon^* \sim J_{sp}$ (the effective pair tunneling energy). Specifically, in the regime $\mathcal{J}_{sp}[\mathcal{J}_{sp}/W]^{1/3} \ll \varepsilon^* \ll \mathcal{J}_{sp}$, the pairing temperature scales as $T_{pair} \sim \varepsilon^*$, while the zero-temperature spin gap scales as $\Delta_s(0) \sim [\mathcal{J}_{sp}]^2/\varepsilon^*$.

title: Relation between ARPES intensity and spectral function
content: The Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy (ARPES) intensity $I(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ is proportional to the product of the Fermi-Dirac distribution function $f(\omega)$ and the single-particle spectral function $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$, such that $I(\mathbf{k},\omega) \propto f(\omega)A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$.

extra.notations:
- $I(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: ARPES intensity at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and energy $\omega$
- $f(\omega)$: Fermi-Dirac distribution function $[1 + \exp(\omega/k_B T)]^{-1}$
- $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: Single-particle spectral function
- $k_{F}$: Fermi momentum
- $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$: Bare electronic dispersion relative to the chemical potential $\mu$
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: Complex electron self-energy

- $\Sigma_{BCS}(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: Self-energy in the Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) framework
- $\Delta$: Superconducting gap parameter
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature
- $G_{0}(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: Bare Green's function $(\omega - \epsilon_{\mathbf{k}} + i0^{+})^{-1}$
- $L(\mathbf{q},\omega_{\nu})$: Fluctuation pair propagator (pairing susceptibility)
- $N_{0}$: Density of states at the Fermi level
- $\xi_{0}$: Superconducting coherence length
- $\omega_{v}$: Matsubara boson frequencies $2\pi \nu T$
- $\epsilon_{n}$: Matsubara fermion frequencies $(2n+1)\pi T$
- $\Gamma_{0}$: Inverse pair lifetime (broadening parameter)
- $\Gamma_{1}$: Single-particle scattering rate

title: Assumption: Particle-hole symmetry assumption
content: The single-particle spectral function $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ satisfies local particle-hole symmetry, defined by the relation $A(-\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}},-\omega)=A(\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}},\omega)$, for energy values within a small range $|\omega| \approx 0$ and momentum values $\mathbf{k}$ within a small window centered at the Fermi momentum $k_{F}$.

title: Spectral function from self-energy
content: The single-particle spectral function $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ is determined by the complex self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega) = \Sigma'(\mathbf{k},\omega) + i\Sigma''(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ and the bare electronic dispersion $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$ via the relation $\pi A(\mathbf{k},\omega) = \frac{|\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega)|}{(\omega-\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}-\Sigma^{\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega))^{2} + (\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega))^{2}}$.

title: BCS Self-Energy
content: The standard Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) self-energy, which corresponds to the diagonal component of the Nambu-Gorkov Green's function propagator, is defined as $\Sigma_{BCS}(\mathbf{k}, \omega) = \Delta^{2}/[(\omega+i0^{+})+\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}]$, where $\Delta$ represents the superconducting gap and $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$ is the band dispersion measured relative to the chemical potential such that $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}_{F}}=0$.

title: Landau Theory for Nematic-Isotropic Transition
content: According to Landau theory applied to two-dimensional systems, the phase transition between a nematic phase (which breaks rotational symmetry) and an isotropic phase (which preserves it) is predicted to be a continuous (second-order) transition.

title: Theoretical constraint on self-energy divergence
content: In a physical electronic state that does not exhibit broken symmetry, specifically the normal state or the pseudogap state above the superconducting transition temperature $T_c$, the electron self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}_{F},\omega=0)$ at the Fermi momentum $\mathbf{k}_{F}$ and zero frequency $\omega=0$ must remain finite and cannot exhibit a mathematical divergence.

title: Origin of BCS self-energy from pair susceptibility
content: The BCS self-energy $\Sigma_{BCS}(\mathbf{k}, \omega) = \Delta^{2}/[\omega+\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}]$ is formally derived by dressing a bare Green's function $G_{0}(\mathbf{k}, \omega) = (\omega - \epsilon_{\mathbf{k}} + i0^{+})^{-1}$ with a static pair susceptibility of the form $\Delta_{\mathbf{k}}^{2}\delta(\mathbf{q})\delta(\omega)$, which represents the existence of static long-range superconducting order.

title: Fluctuation pair propagator definition
content: The fluctuation pair propagator $L(\mathbf{q},\omega_{\nu})$ in the regime of small fluctuations is defined by the inverse relation $L^{-1}(\mathbf{q},\omega_{v})=N_{0}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}+\alpha|\omega_{v}|+q^{2}\xi_{0}^{2})$, where $N_{0}$ is the density of states, $\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \simeq (T-T_{c})/T_{c}$ is the reduced temperature, $\alpha = \pi/8T_{c}$ is a dynamical coefficient, $\xi_{0}$ is the coherence length, and $\omega_{v}$ denotes the Bose Matsubara frequencies.

title: Self-energy from fluctuation propagator
content: The lowest-order self-energy correction $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{n})$ resulting from pairing fluctuations is given by the convolution of the bare Green's function $G_{0}$ and the fluctuation pair propagator $L(\mathbf{q},\omega_{\nu})$, expressed as $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{n}) = -T\sum_{\nu} \int \frac{d^{D}q}{(2\pi)^{D}} L(\mathbf{q},\omega_{v})G_{0}(\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{k},\omega_{v}-\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{n})$, where $\epsilon_n$ are the Fermi Matsubara frequencies and $T$ is the temperature.

title: Phenomenological self-energy form for the pseudogap state
content:
The authors introduce a minimal three-parameter phenomenological self-energy form to describe the angle-resolved photoemission (ARPES) spectral line shape in the pseudogap regime of high-$T_{c}$ superconductors:
      $ \Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)=-i\Gamma_{1}+\Delta^{2}/[\omega+\epsilon(\mathbf{k})+i\Gamma_{0}] $.
      In this expression, $\Gamma_{1}$ represents the single-particle scattering rate, $\Delta$ is the superconducting gap, $\epsilon(\mathbf{k})$ is the band dispersion, and $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ is a new term representing the inverse pair lifetime. This form modifies the standard BCS self-energy by broadening the pair susceptibility pole, allowing the model to describe the "filling in" of the pseudogap in the spectral function without requiring the gap $\Delta$ to vanish.

title: ARPES Intensity Relation
content: In angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES), the measured intensity $I(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and binding energy $\omega$ is proportional to the product of the Fermi-Dirac distribution function $f(\omega)$ and the one-particle spectral function $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$, expressed as $I(\mathbf{k},\omega) \propto f(\omega)A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$.

extra.notations:
- $I(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) intensity at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$.
- $f(\omega)$: Fermi-Dirac distribution function, $f(\omega) = [1 + \exp(\omega/k_{B}T)]^{-1}$.
- $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: One-particle spectral function.
- $k_{F}$: Fermi momentum.
- $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$: Electronic dispersion relation relative to the chemical weight, where $\epsilon(\mathbf{k}_{F}) = 0$.
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: Electronic self-energy, $\Sigma = \Sigma^{\prime} + i\Sigma^{\prime\prime}$.
- $\Sigma^{\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: Real part of the self-energy.
- $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: Imaginary part of the self-energy.
- $\Gamma_{1}$: Single-particle scattering rate, treated as an $\omega$-independent constant representing the average of the frequency-dependent $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}$.
- $\Delta$: Superconducting or pseudogap magnitude.
- $i0^{+}$: Positive infinitesimal imaginary part ensuring causality.

title: Assumption: Particle-Hole Symmetry
content: It is assumed that the electronic system exhibits particle-hole symmetry in the vicinity of the Fermi level, such that the spectral function $A$ satisfies the relation $A(-\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}},-\omega)=A(\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}},\omega)$ for small energy scales $|\omega|$ and within a small momentum window centered at the Fermi momentum $k_{F}$.

extra.notations:
- $I(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) intensity at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$.

- $\Gamma_{1}$: Single-particle scattering rate, treated as an $\omega$-independent constant representing the average of the frequency-dependent $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}$.
- $\Delta$: Superconducting or pseudogap magnitude.
- $i0^{+}$: Positive infinitesimal imaginary part ensuring causality.

title: Symmetrized Intensity
content: Given the assumption of local particle-hole symmetry where $A(-\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}},-\omega)=A(\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}},\omega)$, the symmetrized ARPES intensity defined as $I(\omega)+I(-\omega)$ evaluated at the Fermi momentum $k_{F}$ (where $\epsilon_{k_{F}}=0$) is equivalent to the spectral function $A(k_{F}, \omega)$ convolved with the experimental energy resolution.

title: Phenomenological Self-Energy (Overdoped)
content: For overdoped high-$T_{c}$ superconducting samples, the electronic self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ is modeled by the phenomenological form $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)=-i\Gamma_{1}+\Delta^{2}/[(\omega+i0^{+})+\epsilon(\mathbf{k})]$, where $\Gamma_{1}$ is a constant single-particle scattering rate, $\Delta$ is the superconducting gap, and $\epsilon(\mathbf{k})$ is the dispersion relation satisfying $\epsilon(\mathbf{k}_{F})=0$.

title: Spectral Function from Self-Energy
content: The one-particle spectral function $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ is related to the real part $\Sigma^{\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ and imaginary part $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ of the self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ via the relation $\pi A(\mathbf{k},\omega)=\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega)/[(\omega-\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}-\Sigma^{\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega))^{2}+\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega)^{2}]$, where $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$ is the bare electronic dispersion.

title: Global Phase Diagram of Doped Mott Insulators
content:
The ground state of the system is determined by the ratio of the zero-point energy of transverse stripe fluctuations $\hbar\bar{\omega}$ to the Coulomb interaction $V$. As $\hbar\bar{\omega}/V$ increases, the system undergoes a sequence of phase transitions:
$$ \text{Electronic Crystal} \xrightarrow{\text{first-order}} \text{Smectic} \xrightarrow{\text{continuous}} \text{Nematic} \xrightarrow{\text{continuous}} \text{Isotropic Liquid} $$
High-temperature superconductivity persists across the smectic and nematic regions (for $K_c > 1/2$) due to the fluctuation-enhanced Josephson coupling.

title: Temperature dependence of scattering rate in overdoped Bi2212
content: In overdoped $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8}$ ($T_{c}=82$ K), the analysis of symmetrized ARPES data at the $(\pi,0)-(\pi,\pi)$ Fermi crossing yields a single-particle scattering rate $\Gamma_{1}$ that is relatively temperature-independent in the normal state but suppresses rapidly below $T_{c}$, following the functional form $a+bT^{6}$. This rapid drop in linewidth leads to the emergence of sharp quasiparticles at low temperatures. In this regime, the gap $\Delta(T)$ decreases with temperature and vanishes near $T_{c}$, consistent with standard superconducting behavior.

title: Spectral Function from Self-Energy
content: The one-particle spectral function $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ is related to the real part $\Sigma^{\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ and imaginary part $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ of the electronic self-energy and the bare dispersion $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$ by the relation $\pi A(\mathbf{k},\omega) = \Sigma^{\prime\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega) / [(\omega - \epsilon_{\mathbf{k}} - \Sigma^{\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega))^{2} + \Sigma^{\prime\prime}(\mathbf{k},\omega)^{2}]$.

title: Standard BCS Self-Energy Model
content: The standard Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) phenomenological self-energy is modeled as $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -i\Gamma_{1} + \Delta^{2} / [(\omega + i0^{+}) + \epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}]$, where $\Gamma_{1}$ is a frequency-independent single-particle scattering rate, $\Delta$ is the superconducting gap, and $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$ is the electronic dispersion relative to the Fermi level, with $\epsilon_{k_{F}}=0$.

title: Assumption: Modified Phenomenological Self-Energy
content: A modified phenomenological self-energy form intended to describe the pseudogap state includes a finite inverse pair lifetime $\Gamma_{0}$ and is given by $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -i\Gamma_{1} + \Delta^{2} / [\omega + \epsilon_{\mathbf{k}} + i\Gamma_{0}]$, where $\Gamma_{1}$ is the single-particle scattering rate, $\Delta$ is the gap parameter, and $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$ is the electronic dispersion.

title: Constraint on Normal State Self-Energy
content: Theoretical constraints on systems without broken symmetry dictate that the electronic self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}_{F},\omega=0)$ at the Fermi wavevector $k_{F}$ and zero frequency $\omega=0$ cannot exhibit a divergence.

title: Relationship between Pair Lifetime and Propagator
content: Assuming electron pairs possess a finite inverse lifetime $\Gamma_{0}$, the delta-function representation of the pair susceptibility spectral density $\delta(\omega)$ is broadened into a Lorentzian distribution, which introduces the term $i\Gamma_0$ into the denominator of the self-energy term $\Delta^2 / (\omega + \epsilon_{\mathbf{k}} + i\Gamma_0)$.

title: Crossover Condition T*
content: For a spectral function $A(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$ derived from the modified self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -i\Gamma_{1} + \Delta^{2} / [\omega + \epsilon_{\mathbf{k}} + i\Gamma_{0}]$, the condition for the spectrum to exhibit flat behavior at zero frequency ($\omega=0$) defines the crossover temperature $T^{*}$, which occurs when the gap parameter $\Delta(T)$ is of the same order as the inverse pair lifetime $\Gamma_{0}(T)$, specifically satisfying $2\Delta^{2}/\Gamma_{0}^{2} + \Gamma_{1}\Delta^{2}/\Gamma_{0}^{3} = 1$.

title: Synthesis precursors
content: The starting materials used for the preparation of superconducting samples in the Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O system are thallium(III) oxide ($Tl_{2}O_{3}$), calcium oxide ($CaO$), and a barium copper oxide precursor ($BaCu_{3}O_{4}$).

- $Tl_{2}O_{3}$: Thallium(III) oxide
- $CaO$: Calcium oxide
- $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$: Barium copper oxide precursor
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature

title: Nature of the pseudogap evolution in underdoped Bi2212 at the antinode
content: For underdoped $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8}$ ($T_{c}=83$ K) at the $(\pi,0)$ antinode, the superconducting gap parameter $\Delta$ extracted from the self-energy model is found to be essentially temperature-independent across $T_{c}$, maintaining its low-temperature value well into the normal state. Instead of the gap closing, the pseudogap "fills in" due to the temperature dependence of the inverse pair lifetime parameter $\Gamma_{0}$, which is zero below $T_{c}$ and increases linearly as proportional to $T-T_{c}$ above $T_{c}$. The crossover temperature $T^{*}$ corresponds to the condition $\Delta(T)\sim\Gamma_{0}(T)$.

title: Symmetrization method for ARPES data analysis
content: For angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) data where the intensity $I(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ is proportional to the product of the Fermi function $f(\omega)$ and the spectral function $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$, the symmetrized intensity $I(\omega)+I(-\omega)$ at the Fermi momentum $k_{F}$ is equivalent to the spectral function $A(k_{F}, \omega)$ convolved with the experimental resolution, provided the system satisfies particle-hole symmetry $A(-\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}},-\omega)=A(\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}},\omega)$ for small $|\omega|$ within a small momentum $k$ window centered at $k_{F}$.

title: Phenomenological self-energy for the pseudogap state
content: In the underdoped regime of high-$T_{c}$ superconductors, the phenomenological self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ used to describe ARPES spectral line shapes is modeled as $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)=-i\Gamma_{1}+\Delta^{2}/[\omega+\epsilon(\mathbf{k})+i\Gamma_{0}]$, where $\Gamma_{1}$ is a single-particle scattering rate (approximated as an $\omega$-independent constant), $\Delta$ is the gap magnitude, $\epsilon(\mathbf{k})$ is the electronic dispersion such that $\epsilon(\mathbf{k}_{F})=0$, and $\Gamma_{0}$ represents the inverse pair lifetime.

title: Physical interpretation of Gamma_0
content: In the phenomenological self-energy model $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)=-i\Gamma_{1}+\Delta^{2}/[\omega+\epsilon(\mathbf{k})+i\Gamma_{0}]$, the parameter $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ is defined as the inverse pair lifetime, which is zero below the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ (representing infinite lifetime) and is non-zero and proportional to $T-T_{c}$ for temperatures above $T_{c}$.

title: Theoretical condition for pseudogap closing
content: The temperature $T^{*}$ at which the pseudogap in high-$T_{c}$ superconductors is effectively closed or "filled-in" is determined by the condition where the gap magnitude $\Delta(T)$ is approximately equal to the inverse pair lifetime $\Gamma_{0}(T)$, expressed as $\Delta(T) \sim \Gamma_{0}(T)$.

title: Momentum-dependent dichotomy of gap closing versus filling
content: The authors establish a strong momentum dependence in the temperature evolution of the gap in underdoped samples ($T_{c}=77$ K). While the gap $\Delta$ remains constant and "fills in" due to broadening at momentum points near $(\pi,0)$, at points closer to the $d$-wave node (along the arc), $\Delta$ is strongly temperature-dependent and drops smoothly to zero above $T_{c}$ (gap closing). This implies that the vanishing of the pseudogap is controlled by different mechanisms in different regions of the Brillouin zone: by $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ near the antinodes and by a temperature-dependent $\Delta(T)$ near the nodes.

title: Anisotropic Eliashberg Equation
content:
The linearized anisotropic Eliashberg equations at the transition temperature $T_{c}$ define the renormalization function $Z(\vec{k},i\omega_{n})$ and the superconducting gap $\Delta(\vec{k},i \omega_{n})$ for electronic states $\vec{k}$ on the Fermi surface and Matsubara frequencies $\omega_{n}=(2n+1)\pi T_{c}$ as follows:
\[ Z(\vec{k},i\omega_{n})=1+f_{n}s_{n}\sum_{\vec{k}^{\prime}n^{\prime}}W_{\vec{k}^{\prime}}\lambda(\vec{k},\vec{k}^{\prime},n-n^{\prime})s_{n^{\prime}} \]
\[ Z(\vec{k},i\omega_{n})\Delta(\vec{k},i \omega_{n})=\sum_{\vec{k}^{\prime}n^{\prime}}W_{\vec{k}^{\prime}}f_{n^{\prime}}[\lambda(\vec{k},\vec{k}^{\prime},n-n^{\prime})-\mu^{*}(\omega_{c})]\Delta(\vec{k}^{\prime},i \omega_{n^{\prime}}) \]
In these equations, $f_{n}=1/|2n+1|$, $s_{n}=\text{sgn}(\omega_{n})$, $W_{\vec{k}}$ is the fraction of the density of states at $\vec{k}$ on the Fermi surface, $\mu^{*}(\omega_{c})$ is the Coulomb pseudopotential at cutoff frequency $\omega_{c}$, and $\lambda(\vec{k},\vec{k}^{\prime},n-n^{\prime})$ is the electron-phonon interaction kernel between states $\vec{k}$ and $\vec{k}^{\prime}$ at Matsubara index difference $n-n^{\prime}$.

extra.notations:
- $Z$: Renormalization function or mass enhancement function.
- $\Delta$: Superconducting gap function.
- $\vec{k}, \vec{k}^{\prime}$: Electron wave vectors on the Fermi surface.
- $i\omega_{n}$: Matsubara frequencies, where $\omega_{n} = (2n+1)\pi T_{c}$ and $n$ is an integer.
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature.
- $f_{n}$: Frequency-dependent factor defined as $f_{n} = 1/|2n+1|$.
- $s_{n}$: Sign of the Matsubara frequency, $s_{n} = \text{sgn}(\omega_{n})$.
- $W_{\vec{k}}$: Fraction of the electronic density of states at wave vector $\vec{k}$ on the Fermi surface.
- $\lambda(\vec{k}, \vec{k}^{\prime}, n-n^{\prime})$: Momentum- and frequency-dependent electron-phonon interaction kernel between electronic states $\vec{k}$ and $\vec{k}^{\prime}$.
- $\mu^{*}(\omega_{c})$: Dimensionless Coulomb pseudopotential at the cutoff frequency $\omega_{c}$.
- $\omega_{c}$: Cutoff frequency for the summation over Matsubara frequencies.
- $\alpha^{2}F(\omega)$: Eliashberg spectral function (electron-phonon spectral density).
- $\epsilon_{F}$: Fermi energy.
- $E_{2g}$: Doubly degenerate in-plane boron-boron stretching phonon mode.
- $\alpha_{B}$: Boron isotope-effect exponent.

title: Isotropic Eliashberg Equation
content:
The isotropic approximation of the Eliashberg equations, which assumes the electron-phonon interaction $\lambda(\vec{k},\vec{k}^{\prime},n-n^{\prime})$ is independent of the specific electronic states $\vec{k}$ and $\vec{k}^{\prime}$ on the Fermi surface, is defined as:
\[ Z(i\omega_{n})=1+f_{n}s_{n}\sum_{n^{\prime}}\lambda(n-n^{\prime})s_{n^{\prime}} \]
\[ Z(i\omega_{n})\Delta(i\omega_{n})=\sum_{n^{\prime}}f_{n^{\prime}}[\lambda(n-n^{\prime})-\mu^{*}(\omega_{c})]\Delta(i\omega_{n^{\prime}}) \]
where $\omega_{n}=(2n+1)\pi T_{c}$, $f_{n}=1/|2n+1|$, $s_{n}=\text{sgn}(\omega_{n})$, $\mu^{*}(\omega_{c})$ is the Coulomb pseudopotential at cutoff $\omega_{c}$, and the isotropic coupling kernel is the Fermi-surface average $\lambda(n)\equiv\sum_{\vec{k}\vec{k}^{\prime}}W_{\vec{k}}W_{\vec{k}^{\prime}}\lambda(\vec{k},\vec{k}^{\prime},n)$, where $W_{\vec{k}}$ is the fraction of the density of states at $\vec{k}$.

- $Z$: Renormalization function or mass enhancement function.
- $\Delta$: Superconducting gap function.
- $\vec{k}, \vec{k}^{\prime}$: Electron wave vectors on the Fermi surface.
- $i\omega_{n}$: Matsubara frequencies, where $\omega_{n} = (2n+1)\pi T_{c}$ and $n$ is an integer.
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature.

- $s_{n}$: Sign of the Matsubara frequency, $s_{n} = \text{sgn}(\omega_{n})$.

- $\mu^{*}(\omega_{c})$: Dimensionless Coulomb pseudopotential at the cutoff frequency $\omega_{c}$.
