title: Diagnostic Signature of Superconductivity
content: A diamagnetic shift in magnetic susceptibility ($\chi$), characterized by a negative change in the material's response to an applied magnetic field, is a physical property used to identify and confirm the presence of a superconducting state in a material.

extra.notations:
- $\chi$: magnetic susceptibility, a measure of how much a material will become magnetized in an applied magnetic field.
- $Pb$: Lead, a standard elemental superconductor.

title: Lead Reference Standard
content: Lead ($Pb$) serves as a standard reference material for superconductivity; the magnitude of the diamagnetic signal (magnetic susceptibility shift) measured in a $Pb$ sample is used as a benchmark to normalize the diamagnetic signal of a test sample of similar physical dimensions, thereby quantifying the superconducting fraction of the test sample.

extra.notations:
- $\chi$: magnetic susceptibility, a measure of how much a material will become magnetized in an applied magnetic field.
- $Pb$: Lead, a standard elemental superconductor.

title: Magnetic Confirmation of Superconductivity
content: Magnetic susceptibility ($\chi$) measurements confirm the superconducting state with a diamagnetic shift initiating at 91 K, where the diamagnetic signal at 4.2 K corresponds to 24% of the superconducting signal of a lead (Pb) reference sample of similar dimensions.

extra.notations:
- $\chi$: magnetic susceptibility, a measure of how much a material will become magnetized in an applied magnetic field.
- $Pb$: Lead, a standard elemental superconductor.

title: Resistive Definition of Upper Critical Field
content: The upper critical magnetic field $H_{c2}(T_{c})$ for a superconductor is defined resistively by identifying the temperature at which the electrical resistance $R$ decreases by a specific percentage from its value in the normal state, with common criteria being a 10% reduction or a 50% reduction of the normal-state resistance.

extra.notations:
- $H_{c2}(T)$: Upper critical magnetic field as a function of temperature $T$.
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature.
- $R$: Electrical resistance.
- $H_{c2}(0)$: Upper critical magnetic field extrapolated to zero temperature ($T = 0$ K).
- $dH_{c2}/dT|_{T_c}$: The slope of the upper critical field with respect to temperature evaluated near the transition temperature $T_{c}$.

title: Weak-Coupling Limit Estimation
content: In the weak-coupling limit of the theory of superconductivity, the upper critical magnetic field at zero temperature, $H_{c2}(0)$, is estimated using the relationship $H_{c2}(0) \approx -0.69 T_{c} (dH_{c2}/dT)|_{T_c}$, where $(dH_{c2}/dT)|_{T_c}$ is the temperature derivative (slope) of the upper critical field measured near the transition temperature $T_{c}$.

extra.notations:
- $H_{c2}(T)$: Upper critical magnetic field as a function of temperature $T$.
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature.
- $R$: Electrical resistance.
- $H_{c2}(0)$: Upper critical magnetic field extrapolated to zero temperature ($T = 0$ K).
- $dH_{c2}/dT|_{T_c}$: The slope of the upper critical field with respect to temperature evaluated near the transition temperature $T_{c}$.

title: Assumption: Neglect of Low-Field Curvature
content: To approximate a linear slope for the temperature dependence of the upper critical magnetic field near the transition temperature, $(dH_{c2}/dT)|_{T_c}$, the upward (positive) curvature of the $H_{c2}$ versus temperature relationship typically observed at very low magnetic field strengths is neglected.

extra.notations:
- $H_{c2}(T)$: Upper critical magnetic field as a function of temperature $T$.
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature.
- $R$: Electrical resistance.
- $H_{c2}(0)$: Upper critical magnetic field extrapolated to zero temperature ($T = 0$ K).
- $dH_{c2}/dT|_{T_c}$: The slope of the upper critical field with respect to temperature evaluated near the transition temperature $T_{c}$.

title: Estimation of Upper Critical Field
content: The upper critical field $H_{c2}(0)$ is estimated to range between 80 and 180 T in the weak-coupling limit, derived from resistively determined slopes $dH_{c2}/dT$ near $T_{c}$ of 1.3 T/K (using the 50% resistance drop criterion) and 3 T/K (using the 10% resistance drop criterion).

extra.notations:
- $H_{c2}(T)$: Upper critical magnetic field as a function of temperature $T$.
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature.
- $R$: Electrical resistance.
- $H_{c2}(0)$: Upper critical magnetic field extrapolated to zero temperature ($T = 0$ K).
- $dH_{c2}/dT|_{T_c}$: The slope of the upper critical field with respect to temperature evaluated near the transition temperature $T_{c}$.

title: Structure of La-Ba-Cu-O Superconductors
content: The superconductivity observed in the Lanthanum-Barium-Copper-Oxygen (La-Ba-Cu-O) system is attributed to the presence of the $\mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{NiF}_{4}$-type crystal structure phase.

title: Structure of La-Sr-Cu-O Superconductors
content: The Lanthanum-Strontium-Copper-Oxygen (La-Sr-Cu-O) compound system crystallizes in the $\mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{NiF}_{4}$ structure and generally exhibits a higher superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ and a narrower (sharper) superconducting transition width compared to the Lanthanum-Barium-Copper-Oxygen (La-Ba-Cu-O) system.

title: Effect of Annealing on Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O
content: In the Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O superconducting system, the process of annealing samples at $450^{\circ}\text{C}$ in an environment of flowing oxygen results in a slight improvement of the superconducting properties, such as the transition temperature or resistance profile.

title: Effect of Phase Purity on Tc in La-Based Systems
content: In the Lanthanum-Barium-Copper-Oxygen (La-Ba-Cu-O) and Lanthanum-Strontium-Copper-Oxygen (La-Sr-Cu-O) systems, reducing the superconducting transition width by increasing the phase purity of the $\mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{NiF}_{4}$ structure results in a reduction of the onset superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$, while the temperature of the main transition remains constant near 37 K.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: Superconducting transition temperature
- $\mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{NiF}_{4}$ structure: A specific tetragonal crystal structure common in layered perovskites
- La-Ba-Cu-O: Lanthanum-Barium-Copper-Oxygen compound system
- La-Sr-Cu-O: Lanthanum-Strontium-Copper-Oxygen compound system

title: Conjecture: Hypothesis on Mixed Phases
content: The high-temperature superconductivity observed in Lanthanum-Barium-Copper-Oxygen (La-Ba-Cu-O) and Lanthanum-Strontium-Copper-Oxygen (La-Sr-Cu-O) systems, which exhibits a significant increase in $T_{c}$ under hydrostatic pressure, is hypothesized to be associated with one or more of the following: interfacial effects between metallic and insulating layers in mixed-phase samples, concentration fluctuations within the $\mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{NiF}_{4}$ phase, strong superconducting interactions arising from mixed valence states of copper, or a previously unidentified phase.

extra.notations:

title: Structural Phase Characteristics
content: X-ray powder diffraction patterns indicate that the Y-Ba-Cu-O samples consist of multiple phases that are uncharacteristic of the $\mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{NiF}_{4}$ structure associated with previously known La-based high-temperature superconductors.

title: The Two-Dimensional Hubbard Hamiltonian
content: The Hubbard Hamiltonian for electrons on a two-dimensional square lattice is defined by a hopping integral $t$ connecting neighboring sites with separation $a$, a constant on-site Coulomb repulsion energy $U$, and a temperature-dependent chemical potential $\mu$. The chemical potential $\mu$ is a parameter adjusted to maintain a fixed electronic filling $n$.

extra.notations:
- $t$: Hopping integral between neighboring sites.
- $a$: Spatial separation between neighboring sites on a square lattice.
- $U$: On-site Coulomb repulsion strength.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential.
- $n$: Electronic filling (average number of electrons per site).
- $\Phi$: Free-energy functional (Luttinger-Ward functional).
- $G$: Dressed single-particle Green's function.
- $\Sigma$: Single-particle self-energy.
- $L$: Particle-hole or particle-particle response function.
- $\mathcal{U}$: External potential.
- $1, 2, 1^{\prime}, 2^{\prime}$: Space-time (or position-time) coordinates in the many-body formalism.

title: Baym's Conserving Approximation Scheme
content: A conserving approximation, which satisfies microscopic conservation laws for particle number, energy, and momentum, is defined by the following formal procedure: (a) construct a free-energy functional $\Phi[G, U]$ as a functional of the dressed single-particle Green's function $G$ and the interaction $U$; (b) define the single-particle self-energy $\Sigma$ via functional differentiation such that $\Sigma(1,2) = \delta\Phi / \delta G(2,1)$, where $1$ and $2$ represent space-time coordinates; and (c) determine the particle-hole or particle-particle response functions $L$ by evaluating the functional derivative of the Green's function $G$ with respect to an external potential $\mathcal{U}$ in the limit of vanishing external potential, expressed as $L(1,2;1^{\prime},2^{\prime}) = \delta G(1,1^{\prime}) / \delta\mathcal{U}(2^{\prime},2) \mid_{\mathcal{U}=0}$.

extra.notations:
- $t$: Hopping integral between neighboring sites.
- $a$: Spatial separation between neighboring sites on a square lattice.
- $U$: On-site Coulomb repulsion strength.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential.
- $n$: Electronic filling (average number of electrons per site).
- $\Phi$: Free-energy functional (Luttinger-Ward functional).
- $G$: Dressed single-particle Green's function.
- $\Sigma$: Single-particle self-energy.
- $L$: Particle-hole or particle-particle response function.
- $\mathcal{U}$: External potential.
- $1, 2, 1^{\prime}, 2^{\prime}$: Space-time (or position-time) coordinates in the many-body formalism.

title: Properties of Phi-Derivable Approximations
content: In the context of many-body theory, correlation functions generated through the $\Phi$-derivative method (where $\Phi$ is the Luttinger-Ward functional) possess the following properties: they satisfy microscopic conservation laws (particle number, energy, and momentum), are consistent with Luttinger's theorem regarding the volume of the Fermi surface, and describe Fermi-liquid behavior at low temperatures within the normal state of the system.

extra.notations:
- $t$: Hopping integral between neighboring sites.
- $a$: Spatial separation between neighboring sites on a square lattice.
- $U$: On-site Coulomb repulsion strength.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential.
- $n$: Electronic filling (average number of electrons per site).
- $\Phi$: Free-energy functional (Luttinger-Ward functional).
- $G$: Dressed single-particle Green's function.
- $\Sigma$: Single-particle self-energy.
- $L$: Particle-hole or particle-particle response function.
- $\mathcal{U}$: External potential.
- $1, 2, 1^{\prime}, 2^{\prime}$: Space-time (or position-time) coordinates in the many-body formalism.

title: Padé Approximants
content: In numerical many-body physics, $N$-point Padé approximants serve as a method for numerical analytic continuation, allowing for the reconstruction of real-frequency correlation functions from discrete data obtained on the imaginary frequency (Matsubara) axis.

extra.notations:
- $t$: Hopping integral between neighboring sites.
- $a$: Spatial separation between neighboring sites on a square lattice.
- $U$: On-site Coulomb repulsion strength.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential.
- $n$: Electronic filling (average number of electrons per site).
- $\Phi$: Free-energy functional (Luttinger-Ward functional).
- $G$: Dressed single-particle Green's function.
- $\Sigma$: Single-particle self-energy.
- $L$: Particle-hole or particle-particle response function.
- $\mathcal{U}$: External potential.
- $1, 2, 1^{\prime}, 2^{\prime}$: Space-time (or position-time) coordinates in the many-body formalism.

title: Development of the Self-Consistent Fluctuation-Exchange Approximation
content:
The authors develop and implement a "fluctuation-exchange approximation" for the two-dimensional Hubbard model, which is a conserving approximation derived from a "free-energy" functional $\Phi$. The approximation is constructed by summing specific classes of diagrams:
      1. The lowest order $\Phi$ diagram (Hartree-Fock).
      2. $\Phi$ ring diagrams representing the interaction of longitudinal spin and density fluctuations.
      3. Transverse spin fluctuations.
      4. Particle-particle fluctuations.

      The single-particle self-energy $\Sigma$ and the particle-hole (or particle-particle) response functions $L$ are generated via functional differentiation:
      $ \Sigma(1,2)=\delta\Phi/\delta G(2,1) $ and $ L(1,2;1^{\prime},2^{\prime})=\delta G(1,1^{\prime})/\delta\mathcal{U}(2^{\prime},2)\mid_{U=0} $.

      This method satisfies microscopic conservation laws (particle number, energy, momentum), is consistent with Luttinger's theorem, and is solved self-consistently treating full frequency and momentum dependence using a discretized Brillouin zone and analytic continuation to Matsubara frequencies.

extra.notations:
- $t$: Hopping integral between neighboring sites.
- $a$: Spatial separation between neighboring sites on a square lattice.
- $U$: On-site Coulomb repulsion strength.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential.
- $n$: Electronic filling (average number of electrons per site).
- $\Phi$: Free-energy functional (Luttinger-Ward functional).
- $G$: Dressed single-particle Green's function.
- $\Sigma$: Single-particle self-energy.
- $L$: Particle-hole or particle-particle response function.
- $\mathcal{U}$: External potential.
- $1, 2, 1^{\prime}, 2^{\prime}$: Space-time (or position-time) coordinates in the many-body formalism.

title: Baym's Conserving Approximation Scheme
content: A conserving approximation for an interacting electron system, consistent with microscopic conservation laws for particle number, energy, and momentum, is generated from a free-energy functional $\Phi$ that depends on the dressed single-particle Green’s function $G$ and the interaction $U$. The single-particle self-energy $\Sigma$ is defined by the functional derivative of $\Phi$ with respect to $G$ as $\Sigma(1,2)=\delta\Phi/\delta G(2,1)$, where the indices represent space-time or momentum-frequency coordinates. The associated response functions $L$ (representing particle-hole or particle-particle correlations) are generated by evaluating the change in the self-consistent Green's function $G$ in the presence of an infinitesimal external potential $\mathcal{U}$, defined by the functional derivative $L(1,2;1^{\prime},2^{\prime})=\delta G(1,1^{\prime})/\delta\mathcal{U}(2^{\prime},2)\mid_{\mathcal{U}=0}$.

extra.notations:
- $G$: dressed single-particle Green's function
- $U$: on-site Coulomb repulsion or interaction potential
- $\Phi$: free-energy functional of $G$ and $U$
- $\Sigma$: single-particle self-energy
- $L$: response function (particle-hole or particle-particle)
- $\mathcal{U}$: external potential
- $t$: hopping integral between neighboring sites
- $a$: lattice constant (separation between neighboring sites)
- $\mu$: chemical potential
- $n$: electronic filling factor
- $K$: Bethe-Salpeter kernel
- $v$: effective two-particle interaction (particle-hole or particle-particle)
- $\mathbf{Q}$: zone-boundary wave vector, where $a\mathbf{Q}=(\pi,\pi)$
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: singlet pairing state with d-wave symmetry

title: Two-Dimensional Hubbard Model Definition
content: The two-dimensional Hubbard model for electrons on a square lattice is defined by a Hamiltonian featuring a hopping integral $t$ connecting nearest-neighbor sites separated by distance $a$, a local on-site Coulomb repulsion $U$, and a chemical potential $\mu$. The chemical potential $\mu$ is a temperature-dependent parameter adjusted to maintain a specific electronic filling $n$, representing the average number of electrons per lattice site.

extra.notations:
- $G$: dressed single-particle Green's function
- $U$: on-site Coulomb repulsion or interaction potential
- $\Phi$: free-energy functional of $G$ and $U$
- $\Sigma$: single-particle self-energy
- $L$: response function (particle-hole or particle-particle)
- $\mathcal{U}$: external potential
- $t$: hopping integral between neighboring sites
- $a$: lattice constant (separation between neighboring sites)
- $\mu$: chemical potential
- $n$: electronic filling factor
- $K$: Bethe-Salpeter kernel
- $v$: effective two-particle interaction (particle-hole or particle-particle)
- $\mathbf{Q}$: zone-boundary wave vector, where $a\mathbf{Q}=(\pi,\pi)$
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: singlet pairing state with d-wave symmetry

title: Conductivity of Tl-Ca-Cu-O
content: Samples in the Tl-Ca-Cu-O chemical system, when prepared using thermal and atmospheric conditions similar to those used for the Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O system (such as heating to $880$–$910^{\circ}\text{C}$ in flowing oxygen), exhibit semiconductor-like electrical resistivity behavior down to the temperature of liquid nitrogen ($77$ K) rather than metallic or superconducting behavior.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: Superconducting transition temperature (critical temperature).
- Tl-Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca/Ba-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium/Barium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.
- Tl-Ca-Cu-O: Thallium-Calcium-Copper-Oxygen chemical system.

title: Bethe-Salpeter Instability Condition
content: In an interacting electron system, a phase transition or crossover instability is identified when the leading eigenvalue of the Bethe-Salpeter kernel $K$ reaches unity. The kernel $K$ is defined as the product of two self-consistent single-particle Green's functions $G$ and an effective two-particle interaction $v$, where $v$ is derived from the functional derivatives of the free-energy functional $\Phi$ corresponding to either the particle-hole or particle-particle scattering channel.

- $G$: dressed single-particle Green's function
- $U$: on-site Coulomb repulsion or interaction potential
- $\Phi$: free-energy functional of $G$ and $U$
- $\Sigma$: single-particle self-energy

- $\mathcal{U}$: external potential
- $t$: hopping integral between neighboring sites
- $a$: lattice constant (separation between neighboring sites)
- $\mu$: chemical potential
- $n$: electronic filling factor
- $K$: Bethe-Salpeter kernel
- $v$: effective two-particle interaction (particle-hole or particle-particle)
- $\mathbf{Q}$: zone-boundary wave vector, where $a\mathbf{Q}=(\pi,\pi)$
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: singlet pairing state with d-wave symmetry

title: Order Parameter Symmetry Channels
content: Different types of electronic ordering are associated with the development of scattering poles (divergences) in specific channels of the two-particle correlation functions: singlet and triplet superconductivity are associated with poles in the particle-particle channel, while spin and charge density ordering are associated with poles in the particle-hole channel. In the context of the two-dimensional Hubbard model near half-filling ($n \approx 1$), antiferromagnetic ordering corresponds to a scattering pole in the particle-hole channel at or near the zone-boundary wave vector $a\mathbf{Q}=(\pi,\pi)$, and d-wave superconductivity corresponds to a scattering pole in the particle-particle channel with singlet $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ symmetry.

title: Phase Diagram of the 2D Hubbard Model
content:
Based on the Bethe-Salpeter equation eigenvalues computed with the fluctuation-exchange approximation for the two-dimensional Hubbard model with Coulomb repulsion $U/t=4$:
1. An antiferromagnetic state is favored for electronic fillings $n$ deviating by less than $\sim 6\%$ from half-filling ($n=1.0$).
2. A d-wave superconducting state (singlet $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ symmetry) is favored for deviations between $6\%$ and $18\%$ from half-filling.
3. The transition temperatures are defined by the condition where the leading eigenvalue of the Bethe-Salpeter kernel reaches unity.

title: Baym's Conserving Approximation Scheme
content: A conserving approximation, which ensures consistency with microscopic conservation laws for particle number, energy, and momentum, is generated by defining a free-energy functional $\Phi[G, U]$ as a function of the dressed single-particle Green’s function $G$ and the interaction $U$; the single-particle self-energy $\Sigma(1,2)$ is then derived via functional differentiation such that $\Sigma(1,2)=\delta\Phi/\delta G(2,1)$, and $G$ is determined self-consistently.

title: Compositional and Thermal Stability Characteristics
content: The addition of calcium to the Tl-Ba-Cu-O system stabilizes the superconducting phase against annealing; annealing at $450^{\circ}C$ in flowing oxygen slightly improves the superconductivity of Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O samples, whereas it depresses or destroys the transition in Tl–Ba–Cu–O samples, and Tl–Ca–Cu–O samples without barium exhibit semiconductor-like behavior down to liquid-nitrogen temperatures.

title: Assumption: Bandwidth Scaling Assumption
content: For the purpose of converting dimensionless temperature units to Kelvin, the bare bandwidth of the two-dimensional Hubbard model, given by $8t$, is assumed to be $1$ eV.

title: Suppression of Superconducting Transition Temperature
content: The maximum predicted d-wave superconducting transition temperature derived from the self-consistent conserving approximation is of order $0.01t$. For a bare bandwidth $8t=1$ eV, this corresponds to $T_{d}\sim15$ K. This result is an order of magnitude smaller than estimates based on the random-phase-approximation (RPA) which neglect self-energies, indicating that the inclusion of the self-energy significantly suppresses the transition temperature.

title: Definition of the 2D Hubbard Model
content: The Hubbard Hamiltonian for electrons moving on a two-dimensional square lattice is defined by three parameters: a hopping integral $t$ that connects neighboring sites with spatial separation $a$, an on-site Coulomb repulsion $U$, and a temperature-dependent chemical potential $\mu$ which is adjusted to maintain a fixed electronic filling $n$.

title: Construction of Conserving Approximations
content: A "conserving approximation" for a many-body system, which maintains consistency with microscopic conservation laws for particle number, energy, and momentum, is constructed by defining a free-energy functional $\Phi[G, U]$ as a function of the dressed single-particle Green's function $G$ and the interaction $U$. The single-particle self-energy $\Sigma$ is then derived through functional differentiation as $\Sigma(1,2) = \delta\Phi / \delta G(2,1)$, where the indices represent space-time or momentum-frequency coordinates.

- $t$: Hopping integral between neighboring sites in the Hubbard model.

title: Definition of the Fluctuation-Exchange Approximation
content: The "fluctuation-exchange approximation" is a specific conserving approximation for the Hubbard model in which the free-energy functional $\Phi$ is defined as the sum of diagrams representing specific physical processes: the Hartree-Fock contribution, ring diagrams representing the interaction of longitudinal spin and density fluctuations, diagrams for transverse spin fluctuations, and diagrams for particle-particle fluctuations.

title: Quantum Monte Carlo as a Benchmark
content: Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) techniques are a numerical framework for calculating electronic correlation functions in models like the Hubbard model; while they provide exact results within statistical errors, their application is limited to relatively small system sizes and high temperatures, and they primarily provide data for correlations in imaginary time $\tau$ rather than real-time dynamics.

title: Validation Against Quantum Monte Carlo
content:
The accuracy of the fluctuation-exchange approximation is established by comparing the calculated imaginary-time Green's function $G(\tau)$ with Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) results. For $U/t=4$ and $T/t=0.25$:
      1. The approximate Green's function is particularly accurate at half-filling ($n=1.00$).
      2. At $n=0.88$, the approximation remains similar to QMC but underestimates $G$ at intermediate values of $\tau$.

title: Two-Dimensional Hubbard Model Definition
content: The two-dimensional Hubbard model is defined on a square lattice where the Hamiltonian describes electrons characterized by a hopping integral $t$ connecting neighboring sites with spatial separation $a$, an on-site Coulomb repulsion $U$ representing the energy cost for two electrons to occupy the same site, and a temperature-dependent chemical potential $\mu$ that is adjusted to maintain a constant electronic filling $n$.

title: Conserving Approximation Framework
content: A conserving approximation for a many-body system is generated using a self-consistent framework where a free-energy functional $\Phi$ is defined as a function of the dressed single-particle Green's function $G$ and the interaction $U$. The single-particle self-energy $\Sigma$ is derived from the functional derivative $\Sigma(1,2) = \delta\Phi/\delta G(2,1)$. The corresponding particle-hole or particle-particle response functions $L$ are generated by determining $G$ in the presence of an external potential $\mathcal{U}$ and evaluating the functional derivative $L(1,2;1^{\prime},2^{\prime}) = \delta G(1,1^{\prime})/\delta\mathcal{U}(2^{\prime},2)|_{\mathcal{U}=0}$, ensuring the satisfaction of microscopic conservation laws.

title: Hamiltonian for long-wavelength phase fluctuations
content: The Hamiltonian $H$ governing the thermodynamic effects of long-wavelength phase fluctuations at low temperature is defined by the kinetic energy of the superfluid as $H = \frac{1}{2}m^{*}n_{s}(0)\int\mathrm{d}r v_{s}^{2}$, where $n_{s}(0)$ is the zero-temperature superfluid density, $m^{*}$ is the effective mass of an electron, and $v_{s}$ is the superfluid velocity defined by the relation $v_{s} = \hbar\nabla\theta/2m^{*}$ where $\theta$ is the periodic phase angle.

- $\theta$: Superconducting phase angle (a periodic variable with period $2\pi$).
- $\nabla$: Gradient operator.

- $\xi_{\perp}$: Coherence length perpendicular to the layers.
- $V_{0}$: Phase stiffness energy scale.
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: Maximum phase-ordering temperature.
- $A$: Dimensionless constant depending on short-distance physics.

title: Dynamic Magnetic Susceptibility and Paramagnon Dispersion
content: The absorptive part of the magnetic susceptibility, $\operatorname{Im}\chi(\mathbf{q},\omega)$, calculated for $n=0.96$ and $T/t=0.20$, exhibits a sharp quasielastic peak at the wave vector $a\mathbf{Q}^{*}=(\pi,\pi)$. The results show a softening of the paramagnon dispersion curve at the zone center and zone boundary. The calculated velocity in the $(1,1)$ direction is approximately $0.35t/a^{-1}$ ($1.4t/\mathring{A}^{-1}$ for a lattice constant of $4\ \mathring{A}$).

title: Formula for Tunneling Conductance
content: For electron tunneling between a probe metal (material 2) characterized by a constant density of states $N_2(0)$ and a material with many-body effects (material 1), the differential tunneling conductance $g(V)$ as a function of voltage $V$ is given by $g(V)=4\pi e^{2}|M|^{2}N_{2}(0)\sum_{\mathbf{k}}A_{1}(\mathbf{k},-e V)$, where $M$ is the tunneling matrix element assumed to be momentum-independent and $A_1(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$ is the single-particle spectral function of material 1 at wavevector $\mathbf{k}$ and energy $\omega$.

extra.notations:
- $g(V)$: Differential tunneling conductance at voltage $V$
- $e$: Elementary charge
- $M$: Tunneling matrix element
- $N_{2}(0)$: Density of states of the probe metal at the Fermi level
- $A_{1}(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: Single-particle spectral function of the material under study
- $T_{1}^{-1}$: Nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate
- $\chi_{m}^{+-}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Transverse magnetic susceptibility at wavevector $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $I(\omega)$: Raman scattering intensity at frequency shift $\omega$
- $n(\omega)$: Bose-Einstein distribution function $[\exp(\hbar\omega/k_B T)-1]^{-1}$
- $\varepsilon^{-1}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Inverse dielectric function
- $P(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Polarizability
- $\sigma(\omega)$: Total complex optical conductivity
- $\sigma_1(\omega)$: Drude-like contribution to the optical conductivity
- $\sigma_2(\omega)$: Direct absorption contribution to the optical conductivity
- $\omega_p$: Plasma frequency
- $\Sigma(\omega)$: Retarded one-particle self-energy
- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Polarizability of charge and spin density excitations
- $\rho$: Electrical resistivity
- $G(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: Retarded one-particle Green's function

title: Formula for Nuclear Relaxation Rate
content: The nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate $T_{1}^{-1}$ is proportional to the low-frequency limit of the sum over all wavevectors $\mathbf{q}$ of the imaginary part of the transverse magnetic susceptibility $\chi_{m}^{+-}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ weighted by temperature $T$, expressed by the relation $T_{1}^{-1}\sim-\lim_{\omega\to0}\frac{T}{\omega}\sum_{\mathbf{q}}\operatorname{Im}\chi_{m}^{+-}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$.

title: Formula for Raman Scattering Intensity
content: The Raman scattering intensity $I(\omega)$ at frequency shift $\omega$ is proportional to the product of the Bose factor term $[1+n(\omega)]$ and the imaginary part of the inverse dielectric function $\operatorname{Im}\varepsilon^{-1}(0,\omega)$, which for the polarizability $P(0,\omega)$ scales as $-(T/\omega)\operatorname{Im}P(0,\omega)$ in the low-frequency regime $\omega < T$ and as $-\operatorname{Im}P(0,\omega)$ in the high-frequency regime $\omega > T$.

title: Decomposition of Optical Conductivity
content: The optical conductivity $\sigma(\omega)$ is decomposed into a sum of a Drude-like contribution $\sigma_{1}(\omega) \sim \operatorname{Im}\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}/[\omega+i\Sigma(\omega)]$ and a direct absorption term $\sigma_{2}(\omega) \sim -\omega\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$, where $\omega_p$ is the plasma frequency, $\Sigma(\omega)$ is the retarded one-particle self-energy, and $\tilde{P}(0, \omega)$ is the polarizability of specific charge and spin density excitations at zero momentum.

title: Resistivity Relation
content: The electrical resistivity $\rho$ is related to the transport relaxation time $\tau$ and the plasma frequency $\omega_p$ via the expression $\rho=\left(\omega_{p}^{2}\tau\right)^{-1}$.

content: The single-particle spectral function $A(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$ is defined as the imaginary part of the retarded one-particle Green's function $G(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$ scaled by $-1/\pi$, such that $A(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -\frac{1}{\pi}\operatorname{Im}G(\mathbf{k},\omega)$.

title: Assumption: Experimental Anomalies in Cu-O Superconductors
content: The normal state of copper-oxide (Cu-O) superconductors consistently exhibits a set of universal experimental anomalies: (1) electrical resistivity $\rho(T)$ is linear in temperature $T$; (2) tunneling conductance $g(V)$ is linear in voltage $|V|$ following $g_0 + g_1|V|$; (3) nuclear relaxation rate $T_1^{-1}$ follows a linear temperature dependence plus a constant $aT + b$; (4) Raman scattering displays a frequency-independent background intensity up to approximately 0.5 eV; (5) optical conductivity $\sigma(\omega)$ features a peak at zero frequency with width $\sim T$ and an $\omega^{-1}$ decay up to an ultraviolet cutoff $\omega_c$; and (6) thermal conductivity $\kappa(T)$ is approximately temperature independent.

title: Crystal structure of LaOFeAs
content: The quaternary oxypnictide $LaOFeAs$ possesses a layered crystal structure characterized by the tetragonal space group $P4/nmm$, wherein the unit cell contains two molecules ($Z=2$). The structure is composed of $(\mathrm{La}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2})$ layers and $(\mathrm{Fe}_{2}\mathrm{As}_{2})$ layers; the $(\mathrm{Fe}_{2}\mathrm{As}_{2})$ layer is sandwiched between the $(\mathrm{La}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2})$ layers and functions as the primary conduction path for charge carriers.

- $x$: The molar fraction of a dopant element (e.g., Fluorine) substituting a host element (e.g., Oxygen).
- $T_c$: Superconducting transition temperature.
- Vegard's volume rule: An empirical rule stating that at constant temperature, a linear relation exists between the crystal lattice parameters of an alloy and the concentrations of the constituent elements.

title: Relation between superfluid density and penetration depth
content: The zero-temperature superfluid density $n_{s}(0)$ is related to the zero-temperature magnetic penetration depth $\lambda(0)$ through the expression $4\pi n_{s}(0) = m^{*}(c/e\lambda(0))^{2}$, where $m^{*}$ is the effective mass of an electron, $c$ is the speed of light, and $e$ is the electron charge.

- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: Maximum phase-ordering temperature.
- $A$: Dimensionless constant depending on short-distance physics.

title: Assumption: Existence of Fermi Surface
content: Based on high-resolution photoemission spectroscopy experiments, Cu-O materials possess an energy distribution of electronic states that is consistent with the existence of a Fermi surface within an experimental resolution of 20 meV.

title: Conjecture: Marginal Fermi Liquid Hypothesis (The Ansatz)
content: There exist charge and spin density excitations contributing to a polarizability $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ whose imaginary part $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ is essentially momentum-independent over a wide range of $\mathbf{q}$ and follows a frequency-to-temperature scaling such that $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega) \sim -N(0)(\omega/T)$ for $|\omega| < T$ and $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega) \sim -N(0)\operatorname{sgn}\omega$ for $|\omega| > T$, where $N(0)$ is the unrenormalized one-particle density of states.

title: Marginal Fermi Liquid Hypothesis for Polarizability
content:
The universal anomalies in the normal state of Cu-O high-temperature superconductors are explained by the hypothesis that charge and spin density excitations exist with a polarizability $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ that is constant over a wide range of momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and has the frequency dependence:
\[ \operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim\left\{\begin{aligned}&-N(0)(\omega/T)\quad\mathrm{for}\quad|\omega|<T,\\ &-N(0)\operatorname{sgn}\omega\quad\mathrm{for}\quad|\omega|>T,\end{aligned}\right. \]
where $N(0)$ is the unrenormalized one-particle density of states and $T$ is the temperature.

title: Hypothesis on Polarizability Spectrum
content:
In the normal state of Cu-O high-temperature superconductors, there exist charge-density and spin-density excitations such that the absorptive part of the polarizability, $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$, where $\mathbf{q}$ is the momentum and $\omega$ is the frequency, is characterized by the following functional form:
\[ \mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim\left\{\begin{aligned}&-N(0)(\omega/T)\quad\mathrm{for}\quad|\omega|<T,\\ &-N(0)\mathrm{sgn}\omega\quad\mathrm{for}\quad|\omega|>T,\end{aligned}\right. \]
where $N(0)$ is the unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level and $T$ is the temperature.

- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Polarizability function as a function of momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$.

- $N(0)$: Unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level.

- $z_{k}$: Quasiparticle weight (renormalization factor) at wave vector $\mathbf{k}$.
- $\mathrm{Re}\Sigma$: Real part of the self-energy.
- $E_{k}$: Quasiparticle energy defined by the pole of the one-particle Green's function.

title: Assumption: Momentum Dependence of Excitations
content: The charge-density and spin-density excitations characterized by the polarizability $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$—where $\mathbf{q}$ is the momentum and $\omega$ is the frequency—are assumed to exist either uniformly over a wide range of momentum $\mathbf{q}$ or are weighted by a smooth function of $\mathbf{q}$ that remains non-zero over a substantial portion of the Brillouin zone.

title: Mechanism of Self-Energy Generation
content: The retarded one-particle self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$, which describes the many-body corrections to a particle with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$, is generated by the exchange of charge-density and spin-density fluctuations, where these fluctuations are defined by the polarizability $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$ as specified in the hypothesis regarding its absorptive part $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$.

extra.notations:
- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Polarizability function as a function of momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$.
- $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Absorptive (imaginary) part of the polarizability.
- $N(0)$: Unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level.

- $\mathbf{q}$: Momentum vector.

- $\mathrm{Re}\Sigma$: Real part of the self-energy.
- $E_{k}$: Quasiparticle energy defined by the pole of the one-particle Green's function.

title: Definition of Quasiparticle Weight
content:
For a system described by a retarded one-particle self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$, the quasiparticle weight $z_{k}$ at wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ is defined by the frequency derivative of the real part of the self-energy ($\mathrm{Re}\Sigma$) evaluated at the quasiparticle energy $E_{k}$ according to the relation:
\[ z_{k}^{-1}=\left[1-\frac{\partial \mathrm{Re}\Sigma}{\partial \omega}\right]_{\omega=E_{k}} \]
where $E_{k}$ is the energy solution for the quasiparticle state.

extra.notations:
- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Polarizability function as a function of momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$.
- $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Absorptive (imaginary) part of the polarizability.
- $N(0)$: Unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level.
- $T$: Absolute temperature.
- $\mathbf{q}$: Momentum vector.
- $\omega$: Frequency.
- $\mathrm{sgn}\omega$: Signum function of $\omega$.
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: Retarded one-particle self-energy as a function of wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$.
- $z_{k}$: Quasiparticle weight (renormalization factor) at wave vector $\mathbf{k}$.
- $\mathrm{Re}\Sigma$: Real part of the self-energy.
- $E_{k}$: Quasiparticle energy defined by the pole of the one-particle Green's function.

title: Derivation of Marginal Fermi Liquid Self-Energy and Quasiparticle Weight
content:
Based on the proposed polarizability spectrum, the retarded one-particle self-energy due to the exchange of charge and spin fluctuations is derived as:
\[ \Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)\sim g^{2}N^{2}(0)\left(\omega\ln\frac{x}{\omega_{c}}-i\frac{\pi}{2}x\right) \]
where $x = \max(|\omega|, T)$, $\omega_{c}$ is an ultraviolet cutoff, and $g$ is a coupling constant. Consequently, the quasiparticle weight $z_{k}$ vanishes logarithmically as the energy $E_{k} \rightarrow 0$:
\[ z_{k}^{-1}=[1-\delta\operatorname{Re}\Sigma/\delta\omega]_{\omega=E_{k}}\sim\ln|\omega_{c}/E_{k}| \]
This logarithmic vanishing of $z_{k}$ characterizes the system as a marginal Fermi liquid, where the single-particle spectral function $A(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$ is broader than a normal Fermi liquid and carries substantial weight in the wings due to an $\omega^{-1}$ tail.

extra.notations:
- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Polarizability function as a function of momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$.
- $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: Absorptive (imaginary) part of the polarizability.
- $N(0)$: Unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level.
- $T$: Absolute temperature.
- $\mathbf{q}$: Momentum vector.
- $\omega$: Frequency.
- $\mathrm{sgn}\omega$: Signum function of $\omega$.
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: Retarded one-particle self-energy as a function of wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$.
- $z_{k}$: Quasiparticle weight (renormalization factor) at wave vector $\mathbf{k}$.
- $\mathrm{Re}\Sigma$: Real part of the self-energy.
- $E_{k}$: Quasiparticle energy defined by the pole of the one-particle Green's function.

title: Assumption: Marginal Fermi Liquid Hypothesis
content:
The Marginal Fermi Liquid model hypothesizes that, over a wide range of momentum $\mathbf{q}$, there exist charge- and spin-density excitations contributing to the charge and spin polarizability $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$ such that the imaginary part $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ follows the universal form:
$\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim -N(0)(\omega/T)$ for $|\omega| < T$, and $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim -N(0)\mathrm{sgn}\omega$ for $|\omega| > T$, where $N(0)$ is the unrenormalized one-particle density of states and $T$ is the temperature.

title: Relationship between Nuclear Relaxation Rate and Magnetic Susceptibility
content:
For a metallic system, the nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate $T_{1}^{-1}$ is formally related to the imaginary part of the transverse magnetic susceptibility $\chi_{m}^{+-}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$ by the expression:
$T_{1}^{-1}\sim-\lim_{\omega\to0}\frac{T}{\omega}\sum_{\mathbf{q}}\operatorname{Im}\chi_{m}^{+-}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$, where $T$ is the temperature and the summation is performed over the momentum $\mathbf{q}$ in the Brillouin zone.

title: Properties of the X-Y model
content: A statistical mechanics model consisting of phase variables defined in spatial regions of linear dimension $a$ is known to describe the long-distance physics of the "X-Y" model, which undergoes a transition to a low-temperature phase-ordered state characterized by a specific energy scale determined by the phase stiffness.

title: Experimental Behavior of Nuclear Relaxation Rate in YBCO
content: Experimental measurements of the in-plane Cu nuclear relaxation rate $T_{1}^{-1}$ in the high-temperature superconductor $YBa_{2}Cu_{3}O_{7}$ are characterized by the functional form $T_{1}^{-1}\sim a T+b$, where $a$ is a coefficient approximately twice the value predicted by the Korringa law and $b$ is a temperature-independent constant.

title: Decomposition of Optical Conductivity
content: The optical conductivity $\sigma(\omega)$ of a system can be expressed as the sum of a Drude contribution $\sigma_{1}(\omega)$ and a direct absorption contribution $\sigma_{2}(\omega)$ derived from polarizability excitations $\tilde{P}$. The direct absorption term is defined as $\sigma_{2}(\omega)\sim-\omega\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$, while the Drude term, renormalized by the retarded one-particle self-energy $\Sigma(\omega)$, is given by $\sigma_{1}(\omega)\sim\mathrm{Im}\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}/[\omega+i\Sigma(\omega)]$, where $\omega_p$ is the plasma frequency and $\omega$ is the frequency.

title: Experimental Constraints on Optical Conductivity in Cu-O Materials
content: Experimental observations of the frequency-dependent optical conductivity $\sigma(\omega)$ in copper-oxide (Cu-O) based materials reveal a characteristic profile including a peak at zero frequency with a width proportional to the temperature $T$, a subsequent slow increase on an energy scale defined by $T$, and an eventual decline as $\omega^{-1}$ relative to an ultraviolet cutoff frequency $\omega_{c}$.

title: Relation of Raman Scattering to Polarizability
content: The Raman scattering intensity $I(\omega)$ is proportional to the imaginary part of the inverse dielectric function $\varepsilon^{-1}(0, \omega)$ and the Bose-Einstein factor $[1+n(\omega)]$, such that $I(\omega)\sim-[1+n(\omega)]\mathrm{Im}\varepsilon^{-1}(0,\omega)$; in the regime where frequency $\omega$ exceeds temperature $T$ ($\omega > T$), this intensity is directly proportional to the imaginary part of the polarizability $\operatorname{Im} P(0, \omega)$.

content: The success of the phenomenology based on the proposed $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ in fitting experimental data (specifically the nuclear relaxation rate $T_{1}^{-1}$ and optical conductivity $\sigma(\omega)$) imposes the constraint that charge and spin degrees of freedom must have the same functional form and similar magnitudes for their susceptibilities in the metallic phase of Cu-O materials.

title: General Tunneling Current Formula
content:
In a tunneling experiment involving two materials, the tunneling current $I$ as a function of the applied bias voltage $V$ is given by the formula:
\[ I=4\pi e\sum_{\mathbf{k},\mathbf{k}^{\prime}}|M_{\mathbf{k},\mathbf{ k}^{\prime}}|^{2}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\frac{d\omega_{1}}{2\pi}A_{1}(\mathbf{k},\omega_{1})\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\frac{d\omega_{2}}{2\pi}A_{2}(\mathbf{k}^{\prime},\omega_{2})[f(\omega_{1})-f(\omega_{2})]\delta(\omega_{1}-\omega_{2}+e V) \]
where $e$ is the elementary charge, $M_{\mathbf{k},\mathbf{k}^{\prime}}$ is the tunneling matrix element between momentum states $\mathbf{k}$ in material 1 and $\mathbf{k}^{\prime}$ in material 2, $A_{1}(\mathbf{k},\omega_{1})$ and $A_{2}(\mathbf{k}^{\prime},\omega_{2})$ are the single-particle spectral functions for the respective materials, and $f(\omega)$ is the Fermi-Dirac distribution function.

extra.notations:
- $I$: tunneling current
- $e$: elementary charge
- $\mathbf{k}, \mathbf{k}^{\prime}$: electron wave vectors (momentum) for material 1 and material 2
- $M_{\mathbf{k}, \mathbf{k}^{\prime}}$: tunneling matrix element between states $\mathbf{k}$ and $\mathbf{k}^{\prime}$
- $\omega_{1}, \omega_{2}$: energy variables (frequencies)
- $A_{1}(\mathbf{k}, \omega_{1})$: spectral function of material 1
- $A_{2}(\mathbf{k}^{\prime}, \omega_{2})$: spectral function of material 2
- $f(\omega)$: Fermi-Dirac distribution function
- $V$: applied bias voltage
- $\delta(x)$: Dirac delta function
- $g(V)$: differential tunneling conductance ($dI/dV$)
- $g_{0}, g_{1}$: coefficients for the linear voltage dependence of conductance
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy
- $g$: coupling constant in the marginal Fermi liquid model
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized single-particle density of states at the Fermi level
- $T$: absolute temperature
- $x$: energy scale defined as $\max(|\omega|, T)$
- $\omega_{c}$: ultraviolet energy cutoff
- $G(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: retarded single-particle Green's function
- $\operatorname{Im}$: imaginary part of a complex quantity
- $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$: single-particle band energy relative to the Fermi level
- $N_{2}(0)$: density of states of material 2 at the Fermi level

title: Experimental Tunneling Conductance
content:
The normal-state differential tunneling conductance $g(V) = dI/dV$ of Copper-Oxide (Cu-O) based superconductors is empirically observed to follow a linear voltage dependence:
\[ g(V) \sim g_{0}+g_{1}|V| \]
This relationship holds for bias voltages $|V|$ ranging from $0$ to approximately $100$ meV, where the coefficients $g_{0}$ and $g_{1}$ exhibit very weak temperature dependence.

title: Marginal Fermi Liquid Self-Energy
content:
Under the marginal Fermi liquid hypothesis, the retarded one-particle self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ resulting from the exchange of charge and spin fluctuations is characterized by the form:
\[ \Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)\sim g^{2}N^{2}(0)\left(\omega\ln\frac{x}{\omega_{c}}-i\frac{\pi}{2}x\right) \]
In this expression, $x$ is defined as the maximum of the absolute frequency and the temperature, $x = \max(|\omega|, T)$, $\omega_{c}$ is an ultraviolet energy cutoff, $g$ is a dimensionless coupling constant, and $N(0)$ represents the unrenormalized single-particle density of states at the Fermi level.

title: Relation of Spectral Function to Green's Function
content:
For a many-body system, the single-particle spectral function $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ is related to the imaginary part of the retarded one-particle Green's function $G(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ by the relation:
\[ A(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -\frac{1}{\pi}\operatorname{Im}G(\mathbf{k},\omega) \]
where $\mathbf{k}$ is the momentum and $\omega$ is the frequency.

title: One-Particle Green's Function Form
content:
The retarded one-particle Green's function $G(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ for an interacting electronic system is expressed as:
\[ G(\mathbf{k},\omega)=\frac{1}{\omega-\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}-\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)} \]
where $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$ is the single-particle band energy measured relative to the chemical potential and $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ is the retarded complex self-energy accounting for many-body interactions.

title: Interpretation of Tunneling Conductance
content:
The experimentally observed linear dependence of the tunneling conductance $g(V) \sim g_{0}+g_{1}|V|$ implies that the imaginary part of the self-energy scales as $\operatorname{Im}\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega) \sim |\omega|$ in the energy range extending to at least 0.1 eV, consistent with the derived marginal Fermi liquid self-energy. The tunneling conductance is given by:
      \[ g(V)=4\pi e^{2}|M|^{2}N_{2}(0)\sum_{\mathbf{k}}A_{1}(\mathbf{k},-e V) \]
      indicating nonanalytic behavior of the one-particle density of states at the chemical potential.

title: Definition of Nuclear Relaxation Rate
content: In the context of Cu-O high-temperature superconductors, the nuclear longitudinal relaxation rate $T_{1}^{-1}$ is related to the imaginary part of the transverse magnetic susceptibility $\chi_{m}^{+}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ by the relation $T_{1}^{-1} \sim -\lim_{\omega\to0} \frac{T}{\omega} \sum_{\mathbf{q}} \operatorname{Im} \chi_{m}^{+}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$, where $T$ is the temperature, $\omega$ is the frequency, and $\mathbf{q}$ is the wave vector.

content: The total transverse magnetic susceptibility $\chi_{m}^{+}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ in the marginal Fermi liquid model is expressed as the sum of two distinct components: a contribution from renormalized particle-hole excitations calculated without vertex corrections, and a contribution denoted as $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$, which represents the leading frequency contribution of renormalized particle-hole excitations including the complete vertex.

title: Relation between Constant Term b and Fluctuation Time
content: The temperature-independent term $b$ appearing in the fit of the nuclear relaxation rate $T_{1}^{-1} \sim aT + b$ is defined in terms of a characteristic fluctuation time $\tau_{m}$ and the hyperfine coupling constant $|H_{\mathrm{h f}}|$ by the equation $b \equiv 2\pi |H_{\mathrm{h f}}|^{2} \tau_{m}$.

title: Assumption: The Marginal Fermi Liquid Hypothesis
content: The marginal Fermi liquid phenomenology assumes the existence of charge- and spin-density excitations characterized by a polarizability $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ whose imaginary part $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ is approximately independent of momentum $\mathbf{q}$ over a substantial range and follows the frequency and temperature dependence: $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega) \sim -N(0)(\omega/T)$ for $|\omega| < T$, and $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega) \sim -N(0)\mathrm{sgn}(\omega)$ for $|\omega| > T$, where $N(0)$ is the unrenormalized one-particle density of states and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

title: Assumption: Observation of Hyperfine Coupling
content: In the analysis of nuclear relaxation data for Cu-O superconductors, the magnitude of the hyperfine coupling constant $|H_{\mathrm{h f}}|$ is a known parameter obtained from independent experimental measurements.

title: Mechanism for Nuclear Relaxation Rate Anomalies
content:
The nuclear relaxation rate $T_{1}^{-1}$, observed to fit the form $aT + b$, is derived from the transverse magnetic susceptibility $\chi_{m}^{+}$ containing two contributions:
1. A term proportional to $T$ (up to logarithmic corrections) arising from particle-hole pairs renormalized by the marginal Fermi liquid self-energy.
2. A temperature-independent term $b$ arising directly from the contribution of $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}$ (the proposed excitation spectrum), leading to a characteristic fluctuation time $\tau_{m} \sim 1 \mathrm{eV}^{-1}$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{1}^{-1}$: Nuclear longitudinal relaxation rate

title: Assumption: Short-distance spatial cutoff
content: In the calculation of the Hamiltonian $H = \frac{1}{2}m^{*}n_{s}(0)\int\mathrm{d}r v_{s}^{2}$, the system is discretized into regions of linear dimension $a$, acting as a short-distance spatial cutoff such that the spatial integral $\int\mathrm{d}r$ is treated as a discrete sum and the gradient operator $\nabla$ in the superfluid velocity $v_{s} = \hbar\nabla\theta/2m^{*}$ is treated as a finite difference.

extra.notations:
- $H$: Hamiltonian governing the thermodynamic effects of long-wavelength phase fluctuations.
- $m^{*}$: Effective mass of an electron.
- $n_{s}(0)$: Superfluid density at zero temperature.
- $v_{s}$: Superfluid velocity.
- $\hbar$: Reduced Planck constant.
- $\theta$: Superconducting phase angle (a periodic variable with period $2\pi$).
- $\nabla$: Gradient operator.
- $\lambda(0)$: Magnetic penetration depth at zero temperature.
- $c$: Speed of light in vacuum.
- $e$: Elementary charge.
- $a$: Short-distance spatial cutoff (linear dimension of a region).
- $d$: Average spacing between layers in a quasi-two-dimensional system.
- $\xi_{\perp}$: Coherence length perpendicular to the layers.
- $V_{0}$: Phase stiffness energy scale.
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: Maximum phase-ordering temperature.
- $A$: Dimensionless constant depending on short-distance physics.

title: Conjecture: Marginal Fermi Liquid Hypothesis
content:
The normal state properties of Cu-O superconductors are governed by the existence of charge- and spin-density excitations whose imaginary part of the polarizability, $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$, follows the universal scaling form:
\[ \mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim\left\{\begin{aligned}&-N(0)(\omega/T)\quad\mathrm{for}\quad|\omega|<T,\\ &-N(0)\mathrm{sgn}\omega\quad\mathrm{for}\quad|\omega|>T,\end{aligned}\right. \]
where $N(0)$ is the unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level and $T$ is the temperature.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary part of the charge and spin polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level
- $T$: absolute temperature
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $\sigma(\omega)$: frequency-dependent optical conductivity
- $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$: plasma frequency

title: Assumption: Smooth Momentum Dependence
content: The polarizability $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ is defined such that it is multiplied by a smooth function of momentum $\mathbf{q}$ which remains nonzero over a substantial portion of the Brillouin zone, ensuring that the resulting retarded one-particle self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ remains qualitatively consistent with a momentum-independent form.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary part of the charge and spin polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level
- $T$: absolute temperature
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $\sigma(\omega)$: frequency-dependent optical conductivity
- $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$: plasma frequency

title: Extended Drude Model
content:
The optical conductivity $\sigma(\omega)$ of a metallic system characterized by a frequency-dependent electronic self-energy $\Sigma(\omega)$ is given by the extended Drude formula:
\[ \sigma(\omega)\sim\operatorname{Im}\frac{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}{\omega+i\Sigma(\omega)} \]
where $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$ is the plasma frequency of the material.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary part of the charge and spin polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level
- $T$: absolute temperature
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $\sigma(\omega)$: frequency-dependent optical conductivity
- $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$: plasma frequency

title: Direct Absorption Conductivity
content:
In addition to Drude-like transport, the direct absorption contribution to the total optical conductivity $\sigma(\omega)$ resulting from an excitation spectrum with polarizability $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ at the long-wavelength limit ($\mathbf{q} \to 0$) is proportional to:
\[ \sigma(\omega)\sim-\omega\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega) \]

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary part of the charge and spin polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level
- $T$: absolute temperature
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $\sigma(\omega)$: frequency-dependent optical conductivity
- $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$: plasma frequency

title: Optical Sum Rule
content: The integrated oscillator strength of the constituent conductivity terms, representing the total area under the optical conductivity spectrum $\sigma(\omega)$, must sum to the total band structure value determined by the square of the plasma frequency, $\omega_{p}^{2}$.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary part of the charge and spin polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level
- $T$: absolute temperature
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $\sigma(\omega)$: frequency-dependent optical conductivity
- $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$: plasma frequency

title: Two-Component Optical Conductivity
content:
The optical conductivity $\sigma(\omega)$ is described as the sum of two terms, $\sigma(\omega)=\sigma_{1}(\omega)+\sigma_{2}(\omega)$, which fits experimental data when:
\[ \sigma_{1}(\omega)\sim\operatorname{Im}\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}/[\omega+i\Sigma(\omega)] \]
is a Drude contribution renormalized by the frequency-dependent self-energy $\Sigma(\omega)$, and
\[ \sigma_{2}(\omega)\sim-\omega\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega) \]
is a direct absorption term due to the proposed excitation spectrum.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary part of the charge and spin polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level
- $T$: absolute temperature
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k}, \omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $\sigma(\omega)$: frequency-dependent optical conductivity
- $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$: plasma frequency

title: Marginal Fermi Liquid Hypothesis (Polarizability)
content:
In the marginal Fermi liquid phenomenology of Cu-O high-temperature superconductors, there exist charge- and spin-density excitations that contribute to both the charge and spin polarizability. The absorptive (imaginary) part of this polarizability contribution, denoted as $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$, is hypothesized to follow the universal form:
$$ \mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim\left\{\begin{aligned}&-N(0)(\omega/T)\quad\mathrm{for}\quad|\omega|<T,\\ &-N(0)\mathrm{sgn}\omega\quad\mathrm{for}\quad|\omega|>T,\end{aligned}\right. $$
where $N(0)$ is the unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the chemical potential and $T$ is the temperature.

extra.notations:
- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: contribution of charge- and spin-density excitations to the polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the chemical potential
- $T$: temperature
- $\omega_{c}$: soft ultraviolet frequency cutoff
- $V(q)$: bare interaction at momentum $q$
- $\varepsilon^{-1}(q, \omega)$: inverse dielectric function
- $P(0, \omega)$: polarizability at zero momentum

title: Definition of Superconductive Pairing Kernel
content: Within the framework of superconductivity theory applied to Cu-O materials, the particle-particle scattering amplitude, which serves as the pairing kernel, is approximated by the expression $V(q) \times \mathrm{Re} \varepsilon^{-1}(q, \omega)$, where $V(q)$ represents the bare interaction and $\varepsilon^{-1}(q, \omega)$ is the inverse dielectric function of the system.

extra.notations:
- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: contribution of charge- and spin-density excitations to the polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the chemical potential
- $T$: temperature
- $\omega_{c}$: soft ultraviolet frequency cutoff
- $V(q)$: bare interaction at momentum $q$
- $\varepsilon^{-1}(q, \omega)$: inverse dielectric function
- $P(0, \omega)$: polarizability at zero momentum

title: Relation of Dielectric Function to Polarizability
content: The imaginary part of the inverse dielectric function $\mathrm{Im} \varepsilon^{-1}(0, \omega)$, as probed by Raman scattering experiments in the normal state of Cu-O superconductors, is proportional to the imaginary part of the polarizability $P(0, \omega)$ at zero momentum, such that $\mathrm{Im} \varepsilon^{-1}(0, \omega) \sim \mathrm{Im} P(0, \omega)$.

extra.notations:
- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: contribution of charge- and spin-density excitations to the polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the chemical potential
- $T$: temperature
- $\omega_{c}$: soft ultraviolet frequency cutoff
- $V(q)$: bare interaction at momentum $q$
- $\varepsilon^{-1}(q, \omega)$: inverse dielectric function
- $P(0, \omega)$: polarizability at zero momentum

title: Assumption: Frequency Cutoff and q-independence
content: The polarizability contribution $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$ is assumed to be independent of the momentum $\mathbf{q}$ over a substantial range of the Brillouin zone. Furthermore, the functional form of the excitation spectrum defined by $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim -N(0)(\omega/T)$ for $|\omega|<T$ and $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim -N(0)\mathrm{sgn}\omega$ for $|\omega|>T$ is assumed to extend up to a soft ultraviolet frequency cutoff $\omega_{c}$, which is of the order of a few tenths of an eV.

- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q}, \omega)$: contribution of charge- and spin-density excitations to the polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$

- $P(0, \omega)$: polarizability at zero momentum

title: Assumption: Phase ordering temperature proportionality
content: The maximum phase-ordering temperature $T_{\theta}^{max}$ is directly proportional to the phase stiffness energy scale $V_{0}$ through the relation $T_{\theta}^{max} = A V_{0}$, where $V_{0} = \hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)a/4m^{*}$ (expressed using the cutoff length $a$ and effective mass $m^{*}$) and $A$ is a dimensionless constant of the order of unity determined by the specific short-distance physics of the system.

- $H$: Hamiltonian governing the thermodynamic effects of long-wavelength phase fluctuations.
- $m^{*}$: Effective mass of an electron.
- $n_{s}(0)$: Superfluid density at zero temperature.
- $v_{s}$: Superfluid velocity.

title: Stability Constraint
content: In addition to the marginal Fermi liquid excitations characterized by the polarizability $\tilde{P}$, there exist contributions from other electronic processes that ensure the static inverse dielectric function satisfies the stability requirement $\mathrm{Re}\varepsilon^{-1}(q,0)>0$.

title: Attractive Pairing Interaction
content: The proposed excitation spectrum $\operatorname{Im} P(0, \omega)$ leads, via the Kramers-Kronig relation, to a negative (attractive) contribution to the real part of the inverse dielectric function $\operatorname{Re}\varepsilon^{-1}(q,\omega)-1$ for frequencies $\omega < \omega_c$. This implies that the marginal Fermi liquid excitations are responsible for s-wave superconductive pairing.

title: Order Parameter Symmetry and Phase Shift for d-wave Pairing
content: For a superconductor exhibiting $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ pairing symmetry, the superconducting order parameter is described by the form $\Delta(k_{x},k_{y})=\Delta_{0}[\cos(k_{x}a)-\cos(k_{y}a)]$, where $\Delta_{0}$ is the gap amplitude and $a$ is the lattice constant. This mathematical form implies that there is an intrinsic phase shift $\delta_{ab}=\pi$ between the superconducting order parameter sampled in the orthogonal crystal directions $a$ and $b$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: A superconducting pairing state symmetry characterized by nodes in the energy gap and a phase that changes sign between orthogonal axes.
- $\Delta(k_{x},k_{y})$: The superconducting order parameter as a function of the wave vector $k$ in the $x$-$y$ plane.
- $\Delta_{0}$: The maximum magnitude of the superconducting order parameter.
- $k_{x}, k_{y}$: Components of the electron wave vector.
- $a$: The lattice constant of the crystal.
- $\delta_{ab}$: The intrinsic phase shift of the superconducting order parameter between pairs tunneling in the orthogonal $a$ and $b$ crystal directions.
- $s$-wave: A superconducting pairing state symmetry where the order parameter is isotropic or anisotropic but maintains the same sign (phase) in all directions.
- dc SQUID: Direct current Superconducting Quantum Interference Device; a loop containing two Josephson junctions used to measure extremely weak magnetic fields.
- $\phi_{a}, \phi_{b}$: Gauge-invariant phase differences across Josephson junctions located on the $a$ and $b$ crystal faces, respectively.
- $\Phi$: Total magnetic flux linking the SQUID loop.
- $\Phi_{0}$: The magnetic flux quantum, defined as $h/2e$.
- $I_{c}$: The critical current of the SQUID, representing the maximum supercurrent it can sustain.
- $I_{0}$: The critical current of an individual Josephson junction in a symmetric SQUID.
- $\Phi_{\mathrm{ext}}$: Externally applied magnetic flux.
- $\alpha$: Fractional critical current asymmetry, defined as $\alpha=(I_{1}-I_{2})/(I_{1}+I_{2})$.
- $\eta$: Fractional inductance asymmetry, defined as $\eta=(L_{1}-L_{2})/(L_{1}+L_{2})$.
- $L$: Total self-inductance of the SQUID loop.

title: Phase Shift for s-wave Pairing
content: For a superconductor exhibiting $s$-wave pairing symmetry (whether isotropic or anisotropic), the phase of the superconducting order parameter is uniform across all directions in $k$-space. This symmetry implies that the intrinsic phase shift $\delta_{ab}$ between the orthogonal crystal directions $a$ and $b$ is equal to zero.

title: Tunneling Probability Directionality
content: In a Josephson junction, the probability of electron tunneling is not isotropic but is strongly peaked for electron wave vectors that are oriented perpendicular to the plane of the junction face.

title: Phase Coherence Constraint in Corner SQUIDs
content: For a dc SQUID interferometer constructed by connecting two Josephson junctions located on the orthogonal $a$ and $b$ faces of a superconducting crystal, the requirement of phase coherence around the superconducting loop imposes the constraint $\phi_{a} - \phi_{b} + 2\pi\Phi/\Phi_{0} + \delta_{ab} = 0$. In this expression, $\phi_{a}$ and $\phi_{b}$ are the gauge-invariant phase differences across the junctions on the $a$ and $b$ faces, $\Phi$ is the total magnetic flux threading the loop, $\Phi_{0}$ is the magnetic flux quantum, and $\delta_{ab}$ is the intrinsic phase shift between pairs tunneling in the $a$ and $b$ directions.

title: Symmetric SQUID Critical Current Modulation
content: In the limit of zero loop inductance, a symmetric dc SQUID with two Josephson junctions each having a critical current $I_{0}$ exhibits a maximum supercurrent (total critical current $I_{c}$) that modulates as a function of the applied magnetic flux $\Phi_{\mathrm{ext}}$ according to the relation $I_{c}=2I_{0}|\cos(\pi\Phi_{\mathrm{ext}}/\Phi_{0}+\delta_{ab}/2)|$, where $\delta_{ab}$ is the intrinsic phase shift between the two junctions.

- $\eta$: Fractional inductance asymmetry, defined as $\eta=(L_{1}-L_{2})/(L_{1}+L_{2})$.
- $L$: Total self-inductance of the SQUID loop.

title: Effect of Asymmetry on SQUID Flux
content: In an asymmetric dc SQUID characterized by a fractional critical current asymmetry $\alpha$ and an inductance asymmetry $\eta$, the total magnetic flux $\Phi$ linking the SQUID loop depends on the bias current $I$. Near the critical current, this flux varies linearly with $I$ and can be approximated as $\Phi \approx \frac{1}{2}(\alpha + \eta)LI$, which for specific experimental geometries is observed to scale such that the shift in the flux modulation pattern is approximately $\frac{1}{3}(\alpha + \frac{1}{2})\Phi_{0}$.

title: Relationship Between Resistance and Critical Current
content: In the noise-rounded regime of the current-voltage ($I$-$V$) characteristic of a dc SQUID, where the SQUID is biased at a current near or below the nominal critical current, an increase in the SQUID critical current $I_{c}$ results in a corresponding decrease in the measured dynamic resistance $dV/dI$. Consequently, a maximum in the critical current modulation corresponds to a minimum in the dynamic resistance.

title: Experimental evidence for pi-phase shift in YBCO order parameter via corner SQUIDs
content: The authors experimentally determined the relative phase of the superconducting order parameter between orthogonal $a$ and $b$ directions in single crystals of $YBa_{2}Cu_{3}O_{6.8}$ using bimetallic YBCO-Pb dc SQUIDs configured on the crystal corners. By measuring the dynamic resistance versus applied magnetic flux $\Phi$ and extrapolating the flux value at minimum resistance to zero bias current, they observed intercepts for seven corner SQUIDs ranging from $0.3\Phi_{0}$ to $0.6\Phi_{0}$. These results cluster around $\Phi_{0}/2$, providing evidence for an intrinsic phase shift $\delta_{ab} \approx \pi$, which characterizes the $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ pairing state, as opposed to the $\delta_{ab} = 0$ shift expected for $s$-wave symmetry.

title: Assumption: Definition of cutoff length a for quasi-two-dimensional systems
content: For quasi-two-dimensional superconducting systems, the short-distance spatial cutoff length $a$ is defined as the larger of the average interlayer spacing $d$ or the term $\sqrt{\pi\xi_{\perp}}$, where $\xi_{\perp}$ is the coherence length perpendicular to the superconducting layers.

title: Phase constraint in a dc SQUID
content: In a dc SQUID consisting of Josephson junctions on the orthogonal $a$ and $b$ faces of a crystal, the condition for phase coherence around the loop with self-inductance $L$ and circulating current $J$ is given by the constraint $\phi_{a} - \phi_{b} + 2\pi\Phi/\Phi_{0} + \delta_{ab} = 0$. Here, $\phi_{a}$ and $\phi_{b}$ are the gauge-invariant phase differences across the junctions, $\Phi_{0}$ is the flux quantum, $\delta_{ab}$ is the intrinsic phase shift inside the crystal between pairs tunneling in the $a$ and $b$ directions, and the total magnetic flux is $\Phi = \Phi_{\mathrm{ext}} + LJ$, where $\Phi_{\mathrm{ext}}$ is the applied flux.

title: Critical current modulation of a symmetric SQUID
content: For a symmetric dc SQUID with two Josephson junctions each having a critical current $I_{0}$, in the limit of zero loop inductance ($L \approx 0$), the maximum total supercurrent $I_{c}$ modulates as a function of the applied magnetic flux $\Phi_{\mathrm{ext}}$ according to the relation $I_{c} = 2I_{0}|\cos(\pi\Phi_{\mathrm{ext}}/\Phi_{0} + \delta_{ab})|$, where $\Phi_{0}$ is the flux quantum and $\delta_{ab}$ is the intrinsic phase shift between the tunneling directions of the two junctions.

title: Order parameter and phase shift in d-wave symmetry
content: In a superconductor with $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ pairing symmetry, the superconducting order parameter is described by $\Delta(k_{x},k_{y}) = \Delta_{0}[\cos(k_{x}a) - \cos(k_{y}a)]$. This specific symmetry results in an intrinsic phase shift $\delta_{ab} = \pi$ between the superconducting order parameter phases sampled in the orthogonal $a$ and $b$ crystallographic directions.

title: Linear flux-current relation in asymmetric SQUIDs
content: For an asymmetric dc SQUID biased near its critical current, characterized by a fractional critical current asymmetry $\alpha = (I_{1}-I_{2})/(2I_{0})$ and an inductance asymmetry $\eta = (L_{1}-L_{2})/L$, the total magnetic flux $\Phi$ linking the SQUID loop varies linearly with the bias current $I$ according to the approximation $\Phi = \frac{1}{2}(\alpha + \eta)LI = \frac{1}{3}(\alpha + \frac{1}{2})\Phi_{0}$, where $L$ is the total loop inductance and $\Phi_{0}$ is the flux quantum.

title: Effect of trapped magnetic flux vortices
content: The presence of magnetic flux vortices trapped near a Josephson junction reduces the junction's critical current and alters the SQUID's asymmetry, thereby changing the slope of the current-dependent phase shift in the resistance versus flux characteristics. Additionally, a vortex trapped near the SQUID loop contributes a static magnetic flux, resulting in a parallel translation (shift) of the resistance versus flux modulation curves along the flux axis.

title: Control measurements using edge SQUIDs
content: In control experiments using "edge SQUIDs," where both Josephson junctions are fabricated on the same face of the YBCO crystal, the authors found that the extrapolated zero-bias flux intercepts distributed about $0$. This demonstrates that the phase shift of order $\pi$ observed in the corner SQUIDs is specific to the geometry sampling orthogonal $k$-space directions and is not an artifact of the measurement setup, although deviations from ideal values in both geometries are attributed to trapped magnetic flux vortices.

title: Relationship between critical current and dynamic resistance
content: In a Josephson junction or SQUID biased with a constant current in the noise-rounded region of the current-voltage characteristic (specifically the region below the resistance maximum), the dynamic resistance $R$ is inversely related to the critical current $I_{c}$, such that an increase in $I_{c}$ corresponds to a decrease in $R$.

title: Tunneling probability directionality
content: In a Josephson tunnel junction, the tunneling probability for Cooper pairs is strongly peaked for electron wave vectors that are oriented perpendicular to the plane of the junction face.

title: Theoretical derivation of zero-temperature phase stiffness
content:
The authors derive a specific expression for the zero-temperature 'phase stiffness' $V_{0}$, which sets the energy scale for phase fluctuations in a superconductor. By identifying the short-distance spatial cutoff $a$ (related to the coherence length or interlayer spacing) and utilizing the relation between superfluid density $n_{s}(0)$ and the penetration depth $\lambda(0)$, the authors establish the formula:
$V_{0}=\frac{(\hbar c)^{2}a}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)}$
where $\lambda(0)$ is the zero-temperature penetration depth, $c$ is the speed of light, and $e$ is the electron charge. For quasi-two-dimensional systems, the authors specify that $a$ represents the larger of the average spacing between layers $d$ or $\sqrt{\pi\xi_{\perp}}$, and this stiffness determines the maximum phase-ordering temperature $T_{\theta}^{max} = A V_{0}$ (where $A$ is a dimensionless constant of order 1).

title: Standard Fraunhofer diffraction pattern
content: For a spatially uniform Josephson junction, the modulation of the critical current $I_{c}$ as a function of the magnetic flux $\Phi$ threading the junction follows a Fraunhofer diffraction pattern described by the equation $I_{c}(\Phi)=I_{0}|\sin(\pi\Phi/\Phi_{0})/(\pi\Phi/\Phi_{\mathrm{0}})|$, where $I_0$ is the maximum critical current and $\Phi_0$ is the magnetic flux quantum.

extra.notations:
- $I_{c}$: Critical current of a Josephson junction or SQUID
- $R$: Dynamic resistance
- $k_{x}, k_{y}$: Components of the electron wave vector in the $a-b$ plane
- $\Delta(k_{x},k_{y})$: Superconducting order parameter as a function of wave vector
- $\Delta_{0}$: Maximum amplitude of the superconducting gap
- $a$: Lattice constant in the $a-b$ plane
- $\delta_{ab}$: Intrinsic phase shift of the order parameter between the $a$ and $b$ crystal directions
- $\Phi$: Magnetic flux threading a Josephson junction or SQUID loop
- $\Phi_{0}$: Superconducting flux quantum, approximately $2.07 \times 10^{-15}$ Wb
- $I_{0}$: Maximum critical current (zero-flux limit for a uniform junction)
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: A superconducting pairing state symmetry characterized by a $cos(k_{x}a) - cos(k_{y}a)$ functional form

title: Theoretical critical current modulation for a d-wave corner junction
content: For a single Josephson junction fabricated on the corner of a crystal with $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ pairing symmetry, where tunneling occurs into orthogonal directions ($a$ and $b$ faces) characterized by an intrinsic $\pi$ phase shift, the critical current $I_{c}$ as a function of magnetic flux $\Phi$ is given by the functional form $I_{c}(\Phi)=I_{0}|\sin^{2}(\pi\Phi/2\Phi_{0})/(\pi\Phi/\Phi_{0})|$, assuming equal contributions from the $a$ and $b$ components.

- $I_{c}$: Critical current of a Josephson junction or SQUID
- $R$: Dynamic resistance

- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: A superconducting pairing state symmetry characterized by a $cos(k_{x}a) - cos(k_{y}a)$ functional form

title: Single junction diffraction pattern modulation
content: The authors demonstrated that a single YBCO-Pb Josephson junction fabricated on the corner of a YBCO crystal (tunneling into both $a$ and $b$ directions) exhibits a resistance maximum at zero applied flux, corresponding to a minimum in critical current. This contrasts with a single junction on the crystal edge, which displays a resistance minimum at zero flux consistent with a standard Fraunhofer diffraction pattern ($I_{c}(\Phi) \propto |\sin(\pi\Phi/\Phi_{0})/(\pi\Phi/\Phi_{0})|$). The corner junction behavior supports the presence of a $\pi$ phase shift between orthogonal directions, consistent with destructive interference predicted for $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ symmetry.
