title: Three-orbital Hamiltonian structure
content: The physical model for copper-oxide planes is represented by a general Hamiltonian $H$ formulated in a basis of three orbitals per unit cell ($d, p_x, p_y$), expressed as the sum of kinetic energy and interaction terms: $H = K + H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)} + H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$. Here, $K$ represents the kinetic energy of the electrons, $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}$ denotes the local on-site interactions on the Cu and O orbitals, and $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$ denotes the nearest-neighbor interactions between the Cu and O orbitals.

extra.notations:
- $d$: Copper (Cu) $d$-orbital
- $p_{x}, p_{y}$: Oxygen (O) $p$-orbitals
- $H$: Total Hamiltonian of the system
- $K$: Kinetic energy part of the Hamiltonian
- $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}$: Local interaction part of the Hamiltonian
- $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$: Nearest-neighbor interaction part of the Hamiltonian
- $\mathbf{k}$: Wave vector in the Brillouin zone
- $\sigma$: Spin index
- $\epsilon_{d}$: Energy difference between Cu and O orbitals
- $n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Number operator for Cu $d$-orbitals
- $t_{pd}$: Hopping parameter between Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals
- $t_{pp}$: Hopping parameter between O $p$ and O $p$ orbitals
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}$, $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for Cu $d$-orbitals
- $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}$, $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for O $p_x$-orbitals
- $a$: Lattice constant
- $V$: Nearest-neighbor interaction strength between Cu and O
- $x$: Doping level (deviation from half-filling), where $x > 0$ for holes and $x < 0$ for electrons
- $U_d, U_p$: On-site repulsion energies for Cu and O orbitals respectively
- $R, \phi$: Amplitude and phase of the complex mean-field order parameter

title: Kinetic energy and geometric factors
content: For a copper-oxide system with lattice constant $a$, the kinetic energy operator $K$ in the basis of $d, p_x, p_y$ orbitals is defined as $K = \sum_{\mathbf{k},\sigma} \epsilon_{d}n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma} + 2t_{pd}d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}^{+}[s_{x}(k)p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma} + s_{y}(k)p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}] - 4t_{pp}s_{x}(k)s_{y}(k)p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma} + \mathrm{H.c.}$, where $\epsilon_d$ is the energy difference between Copper and Oxygen orbitals, $t_{pd}$ and $t_{pp}$ are the hopping parameters, and $d^{+}$ and $p^{+}$ are the respective creation operators. The geometric structure factors are defined as $s_{x}(k) = \sin(k_{x}a/2)$, $s_{y}(k) = \sin(k_{y}a/2)$, $c_{x}(k) = \cos(k_{x}a/2)$, $c_{y}(k) = \cos(k_{y}a/2)$, and $s_{xy}^{2}(k) = \sin^{2}(k_{x}a/2) + \sin^{2}(k_{y}a/2)$.

extra.notations:
- $d$: Copper (Cu) $d$-orbital
- $p_{x}, p_{y}$: Oxygen (O) $p$-orbitals
- $H$: Total Hamiltonian of the system
- $K$: Kinetic energy part of the Hamiltonian
- $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}$: Local interaction part of the Hamiltonian
- $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$: Nearest-neighbor interaction part of the Hamiltonian

- $t_{pd}$: Hopping parameter between Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals
- $t_{pp}$: Hopping parameter between O $p$ and O $p$ orbitals
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}$, $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for Cu $d$-orbitals
- $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}$, $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Creation and annihilation operators for O $p_x$-orbitals
- $a$: Lattice constant
- $V$: Nearest-neighbor interaction strength between Cu and O
- $x$: Doping level (deviation from half-filling), where $x > 0$ for holes and $x < 0$ for electrons
- $U_d, U_p$: On-site repulsion energies for Cu and O orbitals respectively
- $R, \phi$: Amplitude and phase of the complex mean-field order parameter

title: Nearest-neighbor interaction Hamiltonian
content: In the three-orbital model for copper oxides, the nearest-neighbor interaction Hamiltonian $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$ between the Cu $d$ orbitals and O $p_x, p_y$ orbitals is given by the expression $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)} = 2V \sum_{\mathbf{k}\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\mathbf{q},\sigma\sigma^{\prime}} c_{x}(q) d_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}\sigma}^{+} d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma^{\prime}} p_{x\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{q}\sigma^{\prime}}^{+} p_{x\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\sigma^{\prime}} + (x \rightarrow y)$, where $V$ is the interaction strength, $c_x(q) = \cos(q_x a / 2)$, and the term $(x \rightarrow y)$ represents the identical summation structure for the $y$-direction orbitals using $c_y(q) = \cos(q_y a / 2)$.

title: Assumption: Energy level approximation
content: To simplify calculations for the copper-oxide model, the renormalized energy difference $\epsilon_{d}$ between the Copper (Cu) $d$ orbitals and the Oxygen (O) $p$ orbitals is assumed to be zero ($\epsilon_{d} = 0$).

title: Assumption: Renormalization of hopping parameters
content: In the physical limit where the on-site repulsion energies $U_{d}$ and $U_{p}$ are significantly larger than the hopping parameters $t_{pd}$ and $t_{pp}$ (i.e., $(U_{d}, U_{p}) \gg (t_{pd}, t_{pp})$), the hopping parameters are effectively renormalized by the doping level $|x|$ (the deviation from half-filling in the conduction band) such that $t_{pd} \rightarrow \bar{t}_{pd} = t_{pd} |x|$ and $t_{pp} \rightarrow \bar{t}_{pp} = t_{pp} |x|$, where $x > 0$ denotes hole doping and $x < 0$ denotes electron doping.

title: Assumption: Mean-field order parameter ansatz
content: A complex mean-field order parameter $Re^{i\phi}$, with amplitude $R$ and phase $\phi$, is defined through the mean-field decomposition of the nearest-neighbor interaction $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$ as $Re^{i\phi} \equiv \frac{V}{2} \sum_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} (s_{x}(k) \langle d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+} p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma} \rangle - s_{y}(k) \langle d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+} p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma} \rangle)$, where $s_{x,y}(k) = \sin(k_{x,y}a/2)$ and $\langle \cdot \rangle$ denotes the expectation value. This parameter describes a circulating current (CC) phase that breaks time-reversal symmetry.

title: Superconducting order parameter and Mean-Field Theory
content: In the context of the BCS-Eliashberg mean-field theory of superconductivity, the superconducting state is described by a complex order parameter consisting of an amplitude and a phase. Within this framework, the phase is considered unimportant for the determination of the transition temperature $T_{c}$, and the mean-field transition temperature $T^{MF}$ represents the characteristic temperature below which local pairing of electrons becomes significant.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: The actual superconducting transition temperature.
- $T^{MF}$: The mean-field transition temperature, characterizing the scale where local pairing becomes significant.
- $H$: Hamiltonian governing long-wavelength phase fluctuations.
- $m^{*}$: Effective mass of an electron.
- $n_{s}(0)$: Superfluid density at zero temperature.
- $v_{s}$: Superfluid velocity.
- $\theta$: Phase angle of the complex superconducting order parameter.
- $\hbar$: Reduced Planck constant.
- $V_{0}$: Phase stiffness energy scale at zero temperature.
- $a$: Length scale characterizing the size of regions where the superconducting order parameter is well-defined locally.
- $\lambda(0)$: Magnetic penetration depth at zero temperature.
- $c$: Speed of light in vacuum.
- $e$: Elementary charge.
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: Maximum phase-ordering temperature calculated by ignoring disordering effects of non-phase degrees of freedom.
- $A$: Dimensionless constant of order unity depending on short-distance cutoff details.
- $d$: Average spacing between layers in a quasi-two-dimensional system.
- $\xi_{\perp}$: Superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers.

title: Assumption: Low doping approximation
content: The derivation of the critical doping level $|x_c|$, which identifies the quantum-critical point in the copper-oxide system, is performed under the assumption of the low-doping limit, where the absolute deviation from half-filling $|x|$ is much less than one ($|x| \ll 1$).

title: Derivation of critical doping for the Circulating Current phase
content:
The author derives the critical doping $|x_c|$ defining the quantum-critical point for the transition to the Circulating Current (CC) phase. By minimizing the mean-field energy of a three-orbital Hamiltonian with a complex order parameter $Re^{i\phi}$ (representing current flows), the critical doping is calculated as:
$|x_{c}|\approx\frac{1}{2}\left(V/t_{pd}\right)\left(0.25+0.5t_{pp}/t_{pd}\right)$
where $V$ is the nearest-neighbor interaction strength, and $t_{pd}, t_{pp}$ are the hopping parameters. The transition is identified as belonging to the Ising universality class ($\phi_{0} = \pm \pi/2$), and the transition temperature $T_p(x)$ is predicted to scale as $T_{p}/E_{F} \sim |x_{c} - x|^{1/2}$.

title: Model Hamiltonian
content: For copper oxides, the three-orbital model Hamiltonian $H$ in the basis of $d$, $p_x$, and $p_y$ orbitals per unit cell is defined as $H = K + H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)} + H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$. The kinetic energy term is $K = \sum_{\mathbf{k},\sigma} \epsilon_{d} n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma} + 2t_{pd} d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}^{\dagger} [s_{x}(k)p_{x k\sigma} + s_{y}(k)p_{y k\sigma}] - 4t_{pp} s_{x}(k)s_{y}(k) p_{x k\sigma}^{\dagger} p_{y k\sigma} + \mathrm{H.c.}$, where $s_{x,y}(k) = \sin(k_{x,y}a/2)$ and $a$ is the lattice constant. The local interaction term is $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)} = \sum_{i,\sigma} U_{d} n_{di\sigma} n_{di-\sigma} + U_{p}(n_{pxi\sigma} n_{pxi-\sigma} + n_{pyi\sigma} n_{pyi-\sigma})$, representing on-site repulsions. The nearest-neighbor interaction between Cu and O orbitals is $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)} = 2V \sum_{\mathbf{k}\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\mathbf{q},\sigma\sigma^{\prime}} c_{x}(q) d_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}\sigma}^{\dagger} d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma^{\prime}} p_{x\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\mathbf{q}-\mathbf{q}\sigma^{\prime}} p_{x\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\sigma^{\prime}} + (x \to y)$, where $c_{x}(q) = \cos(q_{x}a/2)$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}^{+}$: Creation operator for a hole in the Cu $d$-orbital with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x k\sigma}, p_{y k\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for holes in the O $p_x$ and $p_y$ orbitals with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for holes in $d$, $p_x$, and $p_y$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $\epsilon_{d}$: Energy of the Cu $d$-orbital relative to the O $p$-orbitals.
- $t_{pd}$: Hopping integral between nearest-neighbor Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pp}$: Hopping integral between nearest-neighbor O $p$ orbitals.
- $U_{d}, U_{p}$: On-site Coulomb repulsion on Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals, respectively.
- $V$: Nearest-neighbor Coulomb repulsion between Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals.
- $s_{x,y}(k)$: Geometric sine form factors defined as $\sin(k_{x}a/2)$ and $\sin(k_{y}a/2)$.
- $c_{x,y}(q)$: Geometric cosine form factors defined as $\cos(q_{x}a/2)$ and $\cos(q_{y}a/2)$.
- $a$: Lattice constant of the copper-oxide plane.
- $\mathrm{H.c.}$: Hermitian conjugate.
- $|x|$: Deviation from half-filling (doping concentration) in the conduction band.
- $\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}$: Operator representing phase fluctuations of the complex order parameter at wavevector $\mathbf{q}$.
- $c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a quasiparticle in the conduction band with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$, spin $\sigma$, and order parameter parameter $\theta$.

title: Geometric Form Factors
content: In the context of the three-orbital model for copper oxides with lattice constant $a$, the geometric form factors related to the relative phases of the $x$ and $y$ orbitals in the unit cell are defined as $s_{x}(k) = \sin(k_{x}a/2)$, $s_{y}(k) = \sin(k_{y}a/2)$, $c_{x}(k) = \cos(k_{x}a/2)$, $c_{y}(k) = \cos(k_{y}a/2)$, and the combined sine form factor is $s_{xy}^{2}(k) = \sin^{2}(k_{x}a/2) + \sin^{2}(k_{y}a/2)$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}^{+}$: Creation operator for a hole in the Cu $d$-orbital with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x k\sigma}, p_{y k\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for holes in the O $p_x$ and $p_y$ orbitals with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for holes in $d$, $p_x$, and $p_y$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $\epsilon_{d}$: Energy of the Cu $d$-orbital relative to the O $p$-orbitals.
- $t_{pd}$: Hopping integral between nearest-neighbor Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pp}$: Hopping integral between nearest-neighbor O $p$ orbitals.
- $U_{d}, U_{p}$: On-site Coulomb repulsion on Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals, respectively.
- $V$: Nearest-neighbor Coulomb repulsion between Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals.
- $s_{x,y}(k)$: Geometric sine form factors defined as $\sin(k_{x}a/2)$ and $\sin(k_{y}a/2)$.
- $c_{x,y}(q)$: Geometric cosine form factors defined as $\cos(q_{x}a/2)$ and $\cos(q_{y}a/2)$.
- $a$: Lattice constant of the copper-oxide plane.
- $\mathrm{H.c.}$: Hermitian conjugate.
- $|x|$: Deviation from half-filling (doping concentration) in the conduction band.
- $\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}$: Operator representing phase fluctuations of the complex order parameter at wavevector $\mathbf{q}$.
- $c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a quasiparticle in the conduction band with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$, spin $\sigma$, and order parameter parameter $\theta$.

title: Assumption: Mean-field Renormalization for Large Repulsion
content: In the limit where the on-site Coulomb repulsions $(U_d, U_p)$ are much larger than the hopping integrals $(t_{pd}, t_{pp})$, the effects of large repulsion are accounted for in a mean-field approximation for a low density of holes by renormalizing the hopping parameters to $\bar{t}_{pd} = t_{pd} |x|$ and $\bar{t}_{pp} = t_{pp} |x|$, where $|x|$ represents the doping concentration defined as the deviation from half-filling in the conduction band.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}^{+}$: Creation operator for a hole in the Cu $d$-orbital with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x k\sigma}, p_{y k\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for holes in the O $p_x$ and $p_y$ orbitals with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for holes in $d$, $p_x$, and $p_y$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $\epsilon_{d}$: Energy of the Cu $d$-orbital relative to the O $p$-orbitals.
- $t_{pd}$: Hopping integral between nearest-neighbor Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pp}$: Hopping integral between nearest-neighbor O $p$ orbitals.
- $U_{d}, U_{p}$: On-site Coulomb repulsion on Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals, respectively.
- $V$: Nearest-neighbor Coulomb repulsion between Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals.
- $s_{x,y}(k)$: Geometric sine form factors defined as $\sin(k_{x}a/2)$ and $\sin(k_{y}a/2)$.
- $c_{x,y}(q)$: Geometric cosine form factors defined as $\cos(q_{x}a/2)$ and $\cos(q_{y}a/2)$.

- $c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a quasiparticle in the conduction band with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$, spin $\sigma$, and order parameter parameter $\theta$.

title: Assumption: Ansatz for Stability of Phase Fluctuations
content: To restore the stability of the system against phase fluctuations $\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}$ (where $\mathbf{q}$ is the fluctuation wavevector) which would otherwise lead to instabilities in momentum space, it is assumed that the expectation values of the fluctuation operators $\langle\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}\rangle$ and the inter-state correlation operators $\langle c_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q},\theta\sigma}^{\dagger} c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}\rangle$ (where $c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}$ is the conduction band operator) are finite for a range of $\mathbf{q}$ around zero, resulting in the formation of new one-particle eigenstates.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}^{+}$: Creation operator for a hole in the Cu $d$-orbital with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x k\sigma}, p_{y k\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for holes in the O $p_x$ and $p_y$ orbitals with wavevector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for holes in $d$, $p_x$, and $p_y$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.

- $t_{pp}$: Hopping integral between nearest-neighbor O $p$ orbitals.
- $U_{d}, U_{p}$: On-site Coulomb repulsion on Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals, respectively.

title: Assumption: Brillouin-Wigner Approximation
content: The Brillouin-Wigner self-consistent approximation is employed to relate the expectation value of the quasiparticle operators to the phase fluctuation order parameter such that $\langle c_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q},\theta\sigma}^{\dagger} c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma} \rangle = g(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}) \langle \delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}} \rangle / (E_{k} - E_{k+q})$, where $E_k$ denotes the renormalized one-particle eigenvalues of the new eigenstates, $g(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q})$ is the coupling vertex between the electrons and the fluctuations, and $c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}$ is the annihilation operator for the conduction band state.

title: Theoretical form of the single-particle pseudogap
content:
The author calculates the single-particle spectrum in the CC phase by accounting for the stability of phase fluctuations via a self-consistent Born-Wigner approximation. The resulting dispersion relation exhibits a gap $D(k)$ distinct from the d-wave superconducting gap:
$E_{k}=\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}\pm D(k)$
with the momentum dependence
$D(k)=D_{0}[\cos(k_{x}a)-\cos(k_{y}a)]^{2}$
where $D_0$ is of order $\theta_{0}^{2}\bar{t}_{pd}^{2}\rho(0)$ and estimated to be approximately 20 meV.

title: Three-orbital Hamiltonian
content: For a copper-oxide lattice model, the total Hamiltonian $H$ is defined in the basis of three orbitals per unit cell ($d, p_x, p_y$) as $H=K+H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}+H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$, where $K$ represents the kinetic energy, $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}$ accounts for local interactions, and $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$ accounts for nearest-neighbor interactions.

extra.notations:
- $d, p_x, p_y$: Atomic orbitals within a unit cell of the copper-oxide (CuO) lattice.
- $K$: Kinetic energy operator.
- $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}, H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$: Interaction parts of the Hamiltonian representing local and nearest-neighbor interactions respectively.
- $\mathbf{k}$: Wave vector in the Brillouin zone.
- $\sigma$: Electron spin index.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for $d$, $p_x$, and $p_y$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}, p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}, p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}$: Creation operators for electrons in the $d$, $p_x$, and $p_y$ orbitals.
- $\epsilon_d$: Energy difference between the $d$ and $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pd}, t_{pp}$: Bare hopping integrals between $p$-$d$ and $p$-$p$ orbitals.
- $a$: Lattice constant.
- $U_d, U_p$: On-site Coulomb repulsion strengths for $d$ and $p$ orbitals.
- $V$: Nearest-neighbor interaction strength between Cu and O orbitals.
- $x$: Doping level (deviation from half-filling).
- $\bar{t}_{pd}, \bar{t}_{pp}$: Renormalized hopping parameters under mean-field approximation.
- $R, \phi$: Magnitude and phase of the complex mean-field order parameter for circulating currents.
- $\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}$: Fluctuations of the phase $\phi$ at wave vector $\mathbf{q}$.
- $c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for an electron in the conduction band state for a given $\theta = R/(2\bar{t}_{pd})$.
- $E_k$: Single-particle eigenvalues in the phase-fluctuation-stabilized state.

title: Kinetic Energy Term
content: The kinetic energy operator $K$ for a three-orbital model ($d, p_x, p_y$) is defined as $K=\sum_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}\epsilon_{d}n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma}+2t_{p d}d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}^{+}[s_{x}(k)p_{x k\sigma}+s_{y}(k)p_{y k\sigma}]-4t_{p p}s_{x}(k)s_{y}(k)p_{x k\sigma}^{+}p_{y k\sigma}+\mathrm{H.c.}$, where $\epsilon_d$ is the energy difference between the $d$ and $p$ orbitals (set to zero for simplification), $t_{pd}$ and $t_{pp}$ are the hopping parameters, and the phase factors are defined as $s_{x}(k)=\sin(k_{x}a/2)$, $s_{y}(k)=\sin(k_{y}a/2)$, $c_{x}(k)=\cos(k_{x}a/2)$, $c_{y}(k)=\cos(k_{y}a/2)$, and $s_{xy}^{2}(k)=\sin^{2}(k_{x}a/2)+\sin^{2}(k_{y}a/2)$.

extra.notations:
- $d, p_x, p_y$: Atomic orbitals within a unit cell of the copper-oxide (CuO) lattice.
- $K$: Kinetic energy operator.
- $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}, H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$: Interaction parts of the Hamiltonian representing local and nearest-neighbor interactions respectively.
- $\mathbf{k}$: Wave vector in the Brillouin zone.

- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for $d$, $p_x$, and $p_y$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}, p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}, p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}$: Creation operators for electrons in the $d$, $p_x$, and $p_y$ orbitals.

- $V$: Nearest-neighbor interaction strength between Cu and O orbitals.
- $x$: Doping level (deviation from half-filling).

- $E_k$: Single-particle eigenvalues in the phase-fluctuation-stabilized state.

title: Hamiltonian for phase fluctuations
content: The Hamiltonian $H$ describing the thermodynamic effects of long-wavelength phase fluctuations at low temperature in a superfluid is defined as the kinetic energy of the superfluid: $H = \frac{1}{2}m^{*}n_{s}(0)\int\mathrm{d}r v_{s}^{2}$. Here, $v_{s} = \hbar\nabla\theta / 2m^{*}$ is the superfluid velocity, $\theta$ is the periodic phase angle, $m^{*}$ is the effective mass of an electron, and $n_{s}(0)$ is the superfluid density at zero temperature.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: The actual superconducting transition temperature.
- $T^{MF}$: The mean-field transition temperature, characterizing the scale where local pairing becomes significant.
- $H$: Hamiltonian governing long-wavelength phase fluctuations.
- $m^{*}$: Effective mass of an electron.
- $n_{s}(0)$: Superfluid density at zero temperature.
- $v_{s}$: Superfluid velocity.
- $\theta$: Phase angle of the complex superconducting order parameter.
- $\hbar$: Reduced Planck constant.
- $V_{0}$: Phase stiffness energy scale at zero temperature.
- $a$: Length scale characterizing the size of regions where the superconducting order parameter is well-defined locally.
- $\lambda(0)$: Magnetic penetration depth at zero temperature.
- $c$: Speed of light in vacuum.
- $e$: Elementary charge.
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: Maximum phase-ordering temperature calculated by ignoring disordering effects of non-phase degrees of freedom.
- $A$: Dimensionless constant of order unity depending on short-distance cutoff details.
- $d$: Average spacing between layers in a quasi-two-dimensional system.
- $\xi_{\perp}$: Superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers.

title: Interaction Terms
content: The interaction components of the Hamiltonian $H$ are given by $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}=\sum_{i,\sigma}U_{d}n_{di\sigma}n_{di-\sigma}+U_{p}(n_{pxi\sigma}n_{pxi-\sigma}+n_{pyi\sigma}n_{pyi-\sigma})$, representing on-site repulsions on Cu ($U_d$) and O ($U_p$) orbitals, and $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}=2V\sum_{\mathbf{k}\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\mathbf{q},\sigma\sigma^{\prime}}c_{x}(q)d_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}\sigma}^{+}d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma^{\prime}}p_{x\mathbf{k}^{\prime}-\mathbf{q}\sigma^{\prime}}^{+}p_{x\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\sigma^{\prime}} + (x \to y)$, representing the nearest-neighbor interaction $V$ between Cu and O orbitals, where $c_x(q) = \cos(q_x a/2)$.

title: Assumption: Mean-field Approximation for Large Repulsion
content: In the limit of large on-site repulsions $(U_{d}, U_{p}) \gg (t_{pd}, t_{pp})$ and for a low density of holes or electrons $|x|$, the kinetic parameters of the Hamiltonian are renormalized as $t_{pd} \rightarrow \bar{t}_{pd} = t_{pd} |x|$ and $t_{pp} \rightarrow \bar{t}_{pp} = t_{pp} |x|$.

title: Assumption: Circulating Current Order Parameter
content: To seek a mean-field solution that breaks time-reversal symmetry without breaking translational symmetry, a complex mean-field order parameter $Re^{i\phi}$ is introduced to decompose the nearest-neighbor interaction $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$, defined as $Re^{i\phi} \equiv \frac{V}{2}\sum_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} [s_{x}(k)\langle d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}\rangle - s_{y}(k)\langle d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}\rangle]$, where $s_{x,y}(k) = \sin(k_{x,y} a/2)$.

title: Assumption: Stability Ansatz
content: To restore stability against phase fluctuations in the circulating current phase, it is assumed that the expectation values of phase fluctuations $\langle\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}\rangle$ and the inter-state correlations $\langle c_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q},\theta\sigma}^{+}c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}\rangle$ are finite for a range of wave vectors $\mathbf{q}$ around zero, resulting in new eigenstates annihilated by the operator $\gamma_{k\sigma} = \left(c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}+\sum_{q\neq0}u_{k,\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}}c_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}\theta\sigma}\right) / M_{k}$, where $c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}$ is the conduction band operator and $M_k$ is a normalization factor.

content: The self-consistency of the phase fluctuations is determined using the Brillouin-Wigner approximation, which states that the correlation function satisfies $\langle c_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q},\theta\sigma}^{+}c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}\rangle = g(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q})\langle\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}\rangle / (E_{k}-E_{k+q})$, where $E_k$ denotes the renormalized single-particle eigenvalues and $g(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q})$ is the coupling vertex derived from the interaction Hamiltonian.

title: Density of states in the pseudogap phase
content:
The single-particle density of states $\rho_{cc}(\omega)$ derived from the calculated dispersion relation in the CC phase is found to be suppressed below the normal state value $\rho(0)$ for all energies $|\omega| < D$ without the logarithmic singularity characteristic of d-wave superconductors. The analytic form is:
$\rho_{c c}(\omega)=\rho(0)\frac{2}{\pi}\mathrm{a r c s i n}\left(\left|\frac{\omega}{D}\right|^{1/2}\right),\quad\left|\frac{\omega}{D}\right|\leq1$
which exhibits an $|\omega|^{1/2}$ dependence for low energies.

- $x$: Doping level (deviation from half-filling).
- $\bar{t}_{pd}, \bar{t}_{pp}$: Renormalized hopping parameters under mean-field approximation.
- $R, \phi$: Magnitude and phase of the complex mean-field order parameter for circulating currents.
- $\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}$: Fluctuations of the phase $\phi$ at wave vector $\mathbf{q}$.
- $c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for an electron in the conduction band state for a given $\theta = R/(2\bar{t}_{pd})$.
- $E_k$: Single-particle eigenvalues in the phase-fluctuation-stabilized state.

title: General Hamiltonian
content: In a model for copper oxides defined in a basis of three orbitals per unit cell (copper $d$, oxygen $p_{x}$, and oxygen $p_{y}$), the general Hamiltonian $H$ is expressed as the sum of a kinetic energy operator $K$ and two interaction terms $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}$ and $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$ such that $H = K + H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)} + H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a copper $d$ orbital with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}, p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for copper $d$, oxygen $p_{x}$, and oxygen $p_{y}$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Number operator for a copper $d$ orbital in momentum space.
- $t_{pd}$: Hopping parameter between copper $d$ and oxygen $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pp}$: Hopping parameter between oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals.
- $a$: Lattice constant of the copper-oxide plane.
- $\mathrm{H.c.}$: Hermitian conjugate.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential.
- $|x|$: Magnitude of doping (deviation from half filling) in the conduction band.
- $f(\epsilon)$: Fermi-Dirac distribution function.

title: Kinetic Energy Operator
content: The kinetic energy operator $K$ for a three-orbital model of copper oxides ($d, p_x, p_y$) is defined as $K = \sum_{\mathbf{k},\sigma} \{ \epsilon_{d}n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma} + 2t_{pd}d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}^{\dagger}[s_{x}(\mathbf{k})p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma} + s_{y}(\mathbf{k})p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}] - 4t_{pp}s_{x}(\mathbf{k})s_{y}(\mathbf{k})p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{\dagger}p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma} + \mathrm{H.c.} \}$, where $\epsilon_d$ is the energy difference between copper and oxygen orbitals, $t_{pd}$ and $t_{pp}$ are hopping parameters, and the phase factors are $s_{x}(\mathbf{k}) = \sin(k_{x}a/2)$ and $s_{y}(\mathbf{k}) = \sin(k_{y}a/2)$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a copper $d$ orbital with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}, p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for copper $d$, oxygen $p_{x}$, and oxygen $p_{y}$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Number operator for a copper $d$ orbital in momentum space.
- $t_{pd}$: Hopping parameter between copper $d$ and oxygen $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pp}$: Hopping parameter between oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals.
- $a$: Lattice constant of the copper-oxide plane.
- $\mathrm{H.c.}$: Hermitian conjugate.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential.
- $|x|$: Magnitude of doping (deviation from half filling) in the conduction band.
- $f(\epsilon)$: Fermi-Dirac distribution function.

title: Local Interaction Hamiltonian
content: The local interaction Hamiltonian $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}$ representing on-site Coulomb repulsions on copper and oxygen orbitals is given by $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)} = \sum_{i,\sigma} \{ U_{d}n_{di\sigma}n_{di-\sigma} + U_{p}(n_{pxi\sigma}n_{pxi-\sigma} + n_{pyi\sigma}n_{pyi-\sigma}) \}$, where $U_d$ is the on-site repulsion energy for copper $d$ orbitals and $U_p$ is the on-site repulsion energy for oxygen $p$ orbitals.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a copper $d$ orbital with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}, p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for copper $d$, oxygen $p_{x}$, and oxygen $p_{y}$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Number operator for a copper $d$ orbital in momentum space.
- $t_{pd}$: Hopping parameter between copper $d$ and oxygen $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pp}$: Hopping parameter between oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals.
- $a$: Lattice constant of the copper-oxide plane.
- $\mathrm{H.c.}$: Hermitian conjugate.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential.
- $|x|$: Magnitude of doping (deviation from half filling) in the conduction band.
- $f(\epsilon)$: Fermi-Dirac distribution function.

title: Nearest Neighbor Interaction Hamiltonian
content: The nearest neighbor interaction Hamiltonian $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$ between copper and oxygen orbitals is defined as $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)} = 2V \sum_{\mathbf{k}\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\mathbf{q},\sigma\sigma^{\prime}} \{ c_{x}(\mathbf{q})d_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}\sigma}^{\dagger}d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma^{\prime}}p_{x\mathbf{k}^{\prime}-\mathbf{q}\sigma^{\prime}}^{\dagger}p_{x\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\sigma^{\prime}} + c_{y}(\mathbf{q})d_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}\sigma}^{\dagger}d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma^{\prime}}p_{y\mathbf{k}^{\prime}-\mathbf{q}\sigma^{\prime}}^{\dagger}p_{y\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\sigma^{\prime}} \}$, where $V$ is the interaction strength and $c_{x,y}(\mathbf{q}) = \cos(q_{x,y}a/2)$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a copper $d$ orbital with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}, p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for copper $d$, oxygen $p_{x}$, and oxygen $p_{y}$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Number operator for a copper $d$ orbital in momentum space.

- $t_{pp}$: Hopping parameter between oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals.
- $a$: Lattice constant of the copper-oxide plane.
- $\mathrm{H.c.}$: Hermitian conjugate.
- $\mu$: Chemical potential.
- $|x|$: Magnitude of doping (deviation from half filling) in the conduction band.
- $f(\epsilon)$: Fermi-Dirac distribution function.

title: Phase stiffness energy scale
content: The characteristic energy scale for classical phase fluctuations in a superconductor is defined as the zero-temperature phase stiffness $V_{0} = \hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)a / 4m^{*}$. In this expression, $n_{s}(0)$ is the zero-temperature superfluid density, $m^{*}$ is the electron effective mass, and $a$ is a spatial length scale characterizing the size of regions where the superconducting order parameter is locally well-defined.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: The actual superconducting transition temperature.
- $T^{MF}$: The mean-field transition temperature, characterizing the scale where local pairing becomes significant.
- $H$: Hamiltonian governing long-wavelength phase fluctuations.
- $m^{*}$: Effective mass of an electron.
- $n_{s}(0)$: Superfluid density at zero temperature.
- $v_{s}$: Superfluid velocity.
- $\theta$: Phase angle of the complex superconducting order parameter.
- $\hbar$: Reduced Planck constant.
- $V_{0}$: Phase stiffness energy scale at zero temperature.
- $a$: Length scale characterizing the size of regions where the superconducting order parameter is well-defined locally.
- $\lambda(0)$: Magnetic penetration depth at zero temperature.
- $c$: Speed of light in vacuum.
- $e$: Elementary charge.
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: Maximum phase-ordering temperature calculated by ignoring disordering effects of non-phase degrees of freedom.
- $A$: Dimensionless constant of order unity depending on short-distance cutoff details.
- $d$: Average spacing between layers in a quasi-two-dimensional system.
- $\xi_{\perp}$: Superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers.

title: Assumption: Low Doping Mean-Field Approximation
content: In the strong correlation limit where the on-site repulsions $U_d$ and $U_p$ are much larger than the hopping parameters $t_{pd}$ and $t_{pp}$, a mean-field approximation for a low density of holes is characterized by the renormalization of the hopping parameters such that $t_{pd} \rightarrow \bar{t}_{pd} = t_{pd} |x|$ and $t_{pp} \rightarrow \bar{t}_{pp} = t_{pp} |x|$, where $|x|$ denotes the doping deviation from half filling.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a copper $d$ orbital with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}, p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for copper $d$, oxygen $p_{x}$, and oxygen $p_{y}$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Number operator for a copper $d$ orbital in momentum space.

title: Assumption: Ansatz for Stability Restoration
content: To restore stability against phase fluctuations in the circulating current phase, it is assumed that the expectation values of the phase fluctuation operator $\langle\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}\rangle$ and the correlation function $\langle c_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q},\theta\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}\rangle$ are finite for a range of wave vectors $\mathbf{q}$ around zero, which defines new quasiparticle eigenstates $\gamma_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$ of the form $\gamma_{\mathbf{k}\sigma} = (c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma} + \sum_{\mathbf{q}\neq0} u_{\mathbf{k},\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}}c_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}\theta\sigma}) / M_{\mathbf{k}}$, where $M_{\mathbf{k}}$ is a normalization factor and the coefficients $u_{\mathbf{k},\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}}$ are non-zero only for states where both the original conduction band energies $\epsilon(\mathbf{k})$ and $\epsilon(\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q})$ are within an energy range $\Omega_{0}^{0}$ of the chemical potential $\mu$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a copper $d$ orbital with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}, p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: Number operators for copper $d$, oxygen $p_{x}$, and oxygen $p_{y}$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.

- $t_{pp}$: Hopping parameter between oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals.

title: Assumption: Brillouin-Wigner Approximation
content: The self-consistency of the coupling between conduction electrons and phase fluctuations $\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}$ is evaluated using the Brillouin-Wigner approximation, which relates the correlation function to the coupling function $g(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q})$ and the renormalized single-particle eigenvalues $E_{\mathbf{k}}$ by the relation $\langle c_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q},\theta\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{\mathbf{k}\theta\sigma}\rangle = g(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q})\langle\delta\phi_{\mathbf{q}}\rangle / (E_{\mathbf{k}} - E_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}})$.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a copper $d$ orbital with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}, p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.

title: Thermodynamic power laws in the CC phase
content: Based on the derived density of states, the author predicts specific power-law dependencies for thermodynamic quantities in the CC phase for temperatures $T \ll T_{p}(x)$. Specifically, the specific heat is predicted to scale as $C_{v} \sim T^{3/2}$ and the magnetic susceptibility as $\chi \sim T^{1/2}$, with the ratio $T\chi/C_{v}$ remaining nearly independent of temperature.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operator for a copper $d$ orbital with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}, p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}$: Annihilation operators for oxygen $p_{x}$ and $p_{y}$ orbitals with wave vector $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.

title: Three-orbital Hamiltonian for Copper Oxides
content: In a model for copper oxides based on three orbitals per unit cell (one copper $d$ orbital and two oxygen orbitals $p_{x}, p_{y}$), the total Hamiltonian $H$ is defined as the sum of three terms $H=K+H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}+H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$. The kinetic energy term is given by $K = \sum_{\mathbf{k},\sigma} \{ \epsilon_{d}n_{d\mathbf{k}\sigma} + 2t_{pd}d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}^{+}[s_{x}(k)p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma} + s_{y}(k)p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma}] - 4t_{pp}s_{x}(k)s_{y}(k)p_{x\mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}p_{y\mathbf{k}\sigma} + \mathrm{H.c.} \}$, where $s_{x,y}(k) = \sin(k_{x,y}a/2)$, $a$ is the lattice constant, and $n$ denotes particle number operators. The local interaction on Cu and O orbitals is $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)} = \sum_{i,\sigma} \{ U_{d}n_{di\sigma}n_{di-\sigma} + U_{p}(n_{pxi\sigma}n_{pxi-\sigma} + n_{pyi\sigma}n_{pyi-\sigma}) \}$, where $U_d$ and $U_p$ are on-site repulsions. The nearest-neighbor interaction between Cu and O orbitals is $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)} = 2V \sum_{\mathbf{k}\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\mathbf{q},\sigma\sigma^{\prime}} \{ c_{x}(q)d_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}\sigma}^{+}d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma^{\prime}}p_{x\mathbf{k}^{\prime}-\mathbf{q}\sigma^{\prime}}^{+}p_{x\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\sigma^{\prime}} + c_{y}(q)d_{\mathbf{k}+\mathbf{q}\sigma}^{+}d_{\mathbf{k}\sigma^{\prime}}p_{y\mathbf{k}^{\prime}-\mathbf{q}\sigma^{\prime}}^{+}p_{y\mathbf{k}^{\prime}\sigma^{\prime}} \}$, where $c_{x,y}(q) = \cos(q_{x,y}a/2)$ and $V$ is the interaction strength.

extra.notations:
- $d, p_{x}, p_{y}$: atomic orbitals (one copper $d$-orbital and two oxygen $p$-orbitals) per unit cell in the CuO$_2$ plane.
- $H$: total model Hamiltonian.
- $K$: kinetic energy operator.
- $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}$: Hamiltonian term for local (on-site) Coulomb interactions.
- $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(2)}$: Hamiltonian term for nearest-neighbor Coulomb interactions between Cu and O.
- $d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}^{+}, d_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}$: creation and annihilation operators for a hole in the Cu $d$-orbital with momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $p_{x \mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}, p_{x \mathbf{k}\sigma}$ ($p_{y \mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}, p_{y \mathbf{k}\sigma}$): creation and annihilation operators for a hole in the O $p_x$ ($p_y$) orbital with momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: particle number operators for Cu $d$, O $p_x$, and O $p_y$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $\epsilon_{d}$: renormalized energy difference between the Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pd}$: hopping integral between Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pp}$: hopping integral between O $p_x$ and O $p_y$ orbitals.
- $s_{x}(k), s_{y}(k)$: trigonometric factors defined as $\sin(k_{x}a/2)$ and $\sin(k_{y}a/2)$ respectively, where $a$ is the lattice constant.
- $c_{x}(q), c_{y}(q)$: trigonometric factors defined as $\cos(q_{x}a/2)$ and $\cos(q_{y}a/2)$ respectively.
- $U_{d}, U_{p}$: on-site repulsion energies for Cu and O orbitals respectively.
- $V$: nearest-neighbor repulsion energy between Cu and O orbitals.
- $\mathrm{H.c.}$: Hermitian conjugate.
- $x$: doping concentration, defined as the deviation from half filling.
- $\bar{t}_{pd}, \bar{t}_{pp}$: renormalized hopping parameters in the presence of large on-site repulsions.

title: Assumption: Large Repulsion Renormalization
content: For the three-orbital model of copper oxides where the on-site repulsions $U_d$ (for Cu) and $U_p$ (for O) are much larger than the hopping parameters $t_{pd}$ and $t_{pp}$ (i.e., $(U_{d}, U_{p}) \gg (t_{pd}, t_{pp})$), the effect of these repulsions at low density of charge carriers is approximated by a mean-field renormalization of the hopping parameters. Specifically, the hopping parameters $t_{pd}$ and $t_{pp}$ are replaced by $\bar{t}_{pd} = t_{pd} |x|$ and $\bar{t}_{pp} = t_{pp} |x|$, where $|x|$ represents the doping level defined as the deviation from half filling.

- $d, p_{x}, p_{y}$: atomic orbitals (one copper $d$-orbital and two oxygen $p$-orbitals) per unit cell in the CuO$_2$ plane.
- $H$: total model Hamiltonian.
- $K$: kinetic energy operator.
- $H_{\mathrm{int}}^{(1)}$: Hamiltonian term for local (on-site) Coulomb interactions.

- $p_{x \mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}, p_{x \mathbf{k}\sigma}$ ($p_{y \mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}, p_{y \mathbf{k}\sigma}$): creation and annihilation operators for a hole in the O $p_x$ ($p_y$) orbital with momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: particle number operators for Cu $d$, O $p_x$, and O $p_y$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.

- $U_{d}, U_{p}$: on-site repulsion energies for Cu and O orbitals respectively.
- $V$: nearest-neighbor repulsion energy between Cu and O orbitals.

- $\bar{t}_{pd}, \bar{t}_{pp}$: renormalized hopping parameters in the presence of large on-site repulsions.

title: Assumption: Translational Symmetry of Mean-Field
content: In the mean-field analysis of the three-orbital Hamiltonian for copper oxides (comprising $d$, $p_x$, and $p_y$ orbitals), the search for a non-superconducting order parameter is restricted to solutions that do not break the translational symmetry of the lattice. Under this constraint, the resulting mean-field Hamiltonian $H_{mf}$ maintains the same functional form as the kinetic energy operator $K$, effectively only modifying its numerical coefficients.

title: Assumption: Energy Difference Simplification
content: In the three-orbital Hamiltonian model for copper oxides involving Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals, the renormalized energy difference $\epsilon_{d}$ between the Cu and the O orbitals is assumed to be zero ($\epsilon_{d} = 0$) to simplify the calculations, under the condition that the physical value satisfies $\epsilon_{d} \lesssim O(t_{pd})$, where $t_{pd}$ is the Cu-O hopping integral.

title: Coupling of Lattice Defects to Ising Order
content: In a system that undergoes a phase transition breaking both rotational and time-reversal symmetry (such as the circulating current phase in copper oxides described by an Ising-type transition), lattice defects—including interstitials, dislocations, or grain boundaries—couple to the $d=2$ Ising order parameter in the same functional form as an external field.

- $p_{x \mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}, p_{x \mathbf{k}\sigma}$ ($p_{y \mathbf{k}\sigma}^{+}, p_{y \mathbf{k}\sigma}$): creation and annihilation operators for a hole in the O $p_x$ ($p_y$) orbital with momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $n_{di\sigma}, n_{pxi\sigma}, n_{pyi\sigma}$: particle number operators for Cu $d$, O $p_x$, and O $p_y$ orbitals at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $\epsilon_{d}$: renormalized energy difference between the Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pd}$: hopping integral between Cu $d$ and O $p$ orbitals.
- $t_{pp}$: hopping integral between O $p_x$ and O $p_y$ orbitals.
- $s_{x}(k), s_{y}(k)$: trigonometric factors defined as $\sin(k_{x}a/2)$ and $\sin(k_{y}a/2)$ respectively, where $a$ is the lattice constant.
- $c_{x}(q), c_{y}(q)$: trigonometric factors defined as $\cos(q_{x}a/2)$ and $\cos(q_{y}a/2)$ respectively.
- $U_{d}, U_{p}$: on-site repulsion energies for Cu and O orbitals respectively.
- $V$: nearest-neighbor repulsion energy between Cu and O orbitals.
- $\mathrm{H.c.}$: Hermitian conjugate.
- $x$: doping concentration, defined as the deviation from half filling.
- $\bar{t}_{pd}, \bar{t}_{pp}$: renormalized hopping parameters in the presence of large on-site repulsions.

title: Symmetry-based smearing of the transition
content: The author concludes that because the CC phase breaks the discrete rotational invariance of the lattice (Ising order) in addition to time-reversal symmetry, lattice defects such as interstitials or dislocations couple to the order parameter like external fields. Consequently, it is predicted that no thermodynamically sharp phase transition can occur at the transition temperature $T_{p}(x)$.

title: Hubbard Model Hamiltonian
content: The Hubbard model Hamiltonian $H$ is defined as $H=-t\sum_{\langle i j\rangle\sigma}(c_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{j\sigma}+c_{j\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{i\sigma})+U\sum_{i}n_{i\uparrow}n_{i\downarrow}-\mu\sum_{i\sigma}c_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{i\sigma}$, where $t$ is the near-neighbor one-electron hopping parameter, $U$ is the on-site Coulomb interaction strength, $\langle i j\rangle$ denotes summation over nearest-neighbor site pairs, $\sigma$ is the spin index, $c_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$ and $c_{i\sigma}$ are the electron creation and annihilation operators at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$, $n_{i\sigma} = c_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{i\sigma}$ is the number operator, and $\mu$ is the chemical potential.

- $c_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$ ($c_{i\sigma}$): creation (annihilation) operator for an electron at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$
- $n_{i\sigma}$: number operator $c_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{i\sigma}$ for site $i$ and spin $\sigma$
- $\mu$: chemical potential
- $\langle i j\rangle$: summation over nearest-neighbor pairs
- $\sigma$: spin index ($\uparrow$ or $\downarrow$)

- $K$: combined cluster momentum, Matsubara frequency, and spin variable $(\mathbf{K}, i\omega_n, \sigma)$

- $\bar{\mathbf{k}}$: lattice wave vector within a coarse-graining cell

title: Cluster Green's Functions Definitions
content: For a cluster of size $N_c$ in the Dynamic Cluster Approximation at temperature $T$, the finite-temperature cluster one-particle Green's function $G_{c\sigma}$ and two-particle Green's function $G_{c2\sigma_4\cdots\sigma_1}$ are defined as $G_{c\sigma}(X_{2},X_{1})=-\langle T_{\tau}c_{\sigma}(X_{2})c_{\sigma}^{\dagger}(X_{1})\rangle$ and $G_{c2\sigma_{4}\cdots\sigma_{1}}(X_{4},X_{3};X_{2},X_{1})=-\langle T_{\tau}c_{\sigma_{4}}(X_{4})c_{\sigma_{3}}(X_{3})c_{\sigma_{2}}^{\dagger}(X_{2})c_{\sigma_{1}}^{\dagger}(X_{1})\rangle$, where $X_{\ell} = (\mathbf{X}_{\ell}, \tau_{\ell})$ denotes a site $\mathbf{X}_{\ell}$ in the DCA cluster and imaginary time $\tau_{\ell}$, $T_{\tau}$ is the $\tau$-ordering operator, and $c_{\sigma}^{(\dagger)}(X)$ destroys (creates) a particle on the cluster with spin $\sigma$. In the frequency and momentum domain, these are denoted as $G_{c}(K)$ and $G_{c2}(K_{4}, K_{3}; K_{2}, K_{1})$ respectively, where $K = (\mathbf{K}, i\omega_{n}, \sigma)$ represents the cluster momentum $\mathbf{K}$, Matsubara frequency $i\omega_n$, and spin $\sigma$.

title: Cluster Four-Point Vertex Extraction
content: The cluster four-point vertex $\Gamma(K_{4},K_{3};K_{2},K_{1})$ is extracted from the cluster one-particle Green's function $G_c$ and two-particle Green's function $G_{c2}$ via the relation $G_{c2}(K_{4},K_{3};K_{2},K_{1})=-G_{c}(K_{1})G_{c}(K_{2})[\delta_{K_{1},K_{4}}\delta_{K_{2},K_{3}}-\delta_{K_{1},K_{3}}\delta_{K_{2},K_{4}}]+\frac{T}{N}\delta_{K_{1}+K_{2},K_{3}+K_{4}}G_{c}(K_{4})G_{c}(K_{3})\Gamma(K_{4},K_{3};K_{2},K_{1})G_{c}(K_{2})G_{c}(K_{1})$, where $T$ is the temperature, $N$ is the number of lattice sites, and $K_i$ are cluster variables encompassing momentum, Matsubara frequency, and spin.

title: Bethe-Salpeter Eigenvalue Equation (DCA)
content: In the Dynamic Cluster Approximation (DCA), the Bethe-Salpeter equation for the eigenvalues $\lambda_{\alpha}$ and eigenfunctions $\phi_{\alpha}$ in the particle-particle channel is given by $-\frac{T}{N_{c}}\sum_{K^{\prime}}\Gamma^{pp}(K,-K;K^{\prime},-K^{\prime})\bar{\chi}_{0}^{pp}(K^{\prime})\phi_{\alpha}(K^{\prime})=\lambda_{\alpha}\phi_{\alpha}(K)$, where $T$ is the temperature, $N_c$ is the cluster size, and $K = (\mathbf{K}, i\omega_n)$ represents cluster momentum and Matsubara frequency. The coarse-grained particle-particle susceptibility $\bar{\chi}_{0}^{pp}(K^{\prime})$ is defined as $\bar{\chi}_{0}^{pp}(K^{\prime})=\frac{N_{c}}{N}\sum_{\bar{\mathbf{k}}^{\prime}}G_{1}(\mathbf{K}^{\prime}+\bar{\mathbf{k}}^{\prime},i\omega_{n^{\prime}})G_{1}(-\mathbf{K}^{\prime}-\bar{\mathbf{k}}^{\prime},-i\omega_{n^{\prime}})$, where $G_1$ is the lattice single-particle Green's function, $N$ is the total number of lattice sites, and $\bar{\mathbf{k}}^{\prime}$ denotes the lattice wave vectors within the coarse-graining cell associated with the cluster momentum $\mathbf{K}^{\prime}$.

content: The numerical calculations for the Hubbard model are performed with an interaction strength to hopping ratio of $U/t = 4$, and the chemical potential $\mu$ is constrained such that the average electron occupancy per site $\langle n_{i} \rangle$ is $0.85$.

title: Assumption: DCA Approximation
content: The Dynamic Cluster Approximation (DCA) assumes that irreducible quantities, specifically the irreducible particle-particle vertex $\Gamma^{pp}$ and the eigenfunctions $\phi_{\alpha}$ of the Bethe-Salpeter equation, are independent of the lattice wave vectors $\bar{\mathbf{k}}$ within a coarse-graining cell centered at a cluster momentum $\mathbf{K}$, allowing the lattice problem to be mapped onto an effective $N_c$-site cluster problem where short-range correlations are captured by diagrams constructed from coarse-grained Green's functions.

title: Symmetry and Saturation of the Pairing Eigenstate
content: For the two-dimensional Hubbard model with interaction $U/t=4$ and filling $\langle n \rangle = 0.85$, calculated via a 24-site dynamic cluster approximation (DCA), the leading eigenvalue of the particle-particle Bethe-Salpeter equation corresponds to a singlet eigenfunction $\phi_{d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}}(\mathbf{K}, \omega_{n})$ that exhibits $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ symmetry and is even in Matsubara frequency $\omega_{n}$. This pairing eigenvalue increases as temperature decreases and saturates when the leading antiferromagnetic particle-hole eigenvalue (characterized by momentum $\mathbf{Q} = (\pi, \pi)$ and spin $S=1$) stops growing.

title: Extraction of Cluster Four-Point Vertex
content: The cluster four-point vertex $\Gamma$ is related to the cluster one-particle Green's function $G_{c}$ and the cluster two-particle Green's function $G_{c2}$ in the frequency-momentum domain by the relation: $G_{c2}(K_{4},K_{3};K_{2},K_{1})=-G_{c}(K_{1})G_{c}(K_{\mathrm{2}})\times[\delta_{K_{1},K_{4}}\delta_{K_{2},K_{3}}-\delta_{K_{1},K_{3}}\delta_{K_{2},K_{4}}]+\frac{T}{N}\delta_{K_{1}+K_{2},K_{3}+K_{4}}G_{c}(K_{4})G_{c}(K_{\mathrm{3}})\times\Gamma(K_{4},K_{3};K_{2},K_{1})\times G_{c}(K_{2})G_{c}(K_{\mathrm{1}})$, where $K = (\mathbf{K}, i\omega_{n}, \sigma)$ denotes the cluster wave vector $\mathbf{K}$, Matsubara frequency $\omega_{n}$, and spin $\sigma$, $T$ is the temperature, and $N$ is the number of lattice sites.

title: Bethe-Salpeter Equation for Particle-Particle Channel
content: In the Dynamical Cluster Approximation, the eigenvalues $\lambda_{\alpha}$ and eigenfunctions $\phi_{\alpha}$ in the particle-particle channel are determined by the Bethe-Salpeter equation $-\frac{T}{N_{c}}\sum_{K^{\prime}}\Gamma^{p p}(K,-K;K^{\prime},-K^{\prime})\bar{\chi}_{0}^{p p}(K^{\prime})\phi_{\alpha}(K^{\prime})=\lambda_{\alpha}\phi_{\alpha}(K)$, where $\Gamma^{pp}$ is the irreducible particle-particle vertex, $N_c$ is the cluster size, $T$ is the temperature, $K$ and $K^{\prime}$ are cluster wave-vector/frequency variables, and the coarse-grained pair susceptibility is defined as $\bar{\chi}_{0}^{pp}(K^{\prime})=\frac{N_{c}}{N}\sum_{\bar{k}^{\prime}}G_{1}(\mathbf{K}^{\prime}+\bar{\mathbf{k}}^{\prime},i\omega_{n^{\prime}})G_{1}(-\mathbf{K}^{\prime}-\bar{\mathbf{k}}^{\prime},-i\omega_{n^{\prime}})$, where $G_{1}$ is the lattice single-particle Green's function and $\bar{k}^{\prime}$ represents lattice wave vector fluctuations within a coarse-grained cell.

title: Assumption: DCA Coarse-Graining Assumption
content: Within the Dynamical Cluster Approximation (DCA), it is assumed that irreducible quantities, specifically the irreducible particle-particle vertex $\Gamma^{pp}$ and the Bethe-Salpeter eigenfunction $\phi_{\alpha}$, depend only on the cluster wave vectors $K$ and are independent of the lattice wave vector fluctuations $\bar{k}$ within a coarse-grained cell of the Brillouin zone.

title: Assumption: Channel Separation
content: Given the rotational invariance of the Hubbard model, the particle-particle interaction channel is separable into singlet and triplet components, and the particle-hole interaction channel is separable into a magnetic component (spin $S=1$) and a charge density component ($S=0$).

content: The momentum transfer $q$ associated with an interaction between two states characterized by cluster wave-vector/frequency variables $K$ and $K^{\prime}$ is defined as the difference $q = K - K^{\prime}$.

title: Momentum and Energy Dependence of the Pairing Vertex
content: The irreducible particle-particle vertex $\Gamma^{pp}(K, K')$ increases as the momentum transfer $q = K - K'$ increases, consistent with a $d$-wave pairing interaction. The frequency dependence of $\Gamma^{pp}$ is governed by the same characteristic energy scale found in the spin susceptibility $\chi(\mathbf{Q}, \omega_{m})$, such that the vertex decreases when the energy transfer exceeds this scale.

title: Two-Dimensional Hubbard Model Hamiltonian
content: The Hamiltonian $H$ for the two-dimensional Hubbard model is defined by the relation $H = -t\sum_{\langle i j\rangle\sigma}(c_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{j\sigma}+c_{j\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{i\sigma})+U\sum_{i}n_{i\uparrow}n_{i\downarrow}-\mu\sum_{i\sigma}c_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{i\sigma}$, where $t$ represents the near-neighbor one-electron hopping energy, $U$ denotes the on-site Coulomb interaction strength, $\mu$ is the chemical potential, $\langle i j\rangle$ indicates summation over nearest-neighbor pairs, $c_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$ and $c_{i\sigma}$ are the electron creation and annihilation operators at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$, and $n_{i\sigma} = c_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}c_{i\sigma}$ is the number operator.

title: Dynamical Cluster Approximation (DCA) Fundamental Assumption
content: The Dynamical Cluster Approximation (DCA) maps a lattice model onto a periodic cluster of size $N_{c} = L_{c}^{2}$ embedded in a self-consistent host, under the assumption that short-range quantities, specifically the self-energy and its functional derivatives (the irreducible vertex functions), are representable as Feynman diagrams constructed using the coarse-grained cluster Green's function $G_{c}(\mathbf{K})$.

title: Cluster Green's Function Definitions
content: In the Dynamical Cluster Approximation, the cluster one-particle Green's function $G_{c\sigma}(X_{2},X_{1})$ and two-particle Green's function $G_{c2\sigma_{4}\cdots\sigma_{1}}(X_{4},X_{3};X_{2},X_{1})$ are defined in the imaginary-time ($\tau$) formalism as $G_{c\sigma}(X_{2},X_{1}) = -\langle T_{\tau}c_{\sigma}(X_{2})c_{\sigma}^{\dagger}(X_{1})\rangle$ and $G_{c2\sigma_{4}\cdots\sigma_{1}}(X_{4},X_{3};X_{2},X_{1}) = -\langle T_{\tau}c_{\sigma_{4}}(X_{4})c_{\sigma_{3}}(X_{3}) c_{\sigma_{2}}^{\dagger}(X_{2})c_{\sigma_{1}}^{\dagger}(X_{1})\rangle$, where $X_{\ell} = (\mathbf{X}_{\ell}, \tau_{\ell})$ denotes a combined cluster site index $\mathbf{X}_{\ell}$ and imaginary time $\tau_{\ell}$, and $T_{\tau}$ is the time-ordering operator.

title: Relation of Full Vertex to Green's Functions
content: The cluster four-point vertex $\Gamma$ is related to the two-particle Green's function $G_{c2}$ and the single-particle Green's function $G_{c}$ through the equation $G_{c2}(K_{4},K_{3};K_{2},K_{1}) = -G_{c}(K_{1})G_{c}(K_{\mathrm{2}}) [\delta_{K_{1},K_{4}}\delta_{K_{2},K_{3}}-\delta_{K_{1},K_{3}}\delta_{K_{2},K_{4}}] + \frac{T}{N}\delta_{K_{1}+K_{2},K_{3}+K_{4}}G_{c}(K_{4})G_{c}(K_{\mathrm{3}}) \times \Gamma(K_{4},K_{3};K_{2},K_{1}) \times G_{c}(K_{2})G_{c}(K_{\mathrm{1}})$, where $K$ represents the combined momentum, Matsubara frequency, and spin index $(\mathbf{K}, i\omega_{n}, \sigma)$, $T$ is the temperature, and $N$ is the number of lattice sites.

title: Decomposition of the Irreducible Particle-Particle Vertex
content: The even frequency, even momentum component of the irreducible particle-particle vertex, defined as $\Gamma_{\mathrm{even}}^{pp}(K,K^{\prime}) = \frac{1}{2}[\Gamma^{\mathrm{pp}}(K,K^{\prime}) + \Gamma^{\mathrm{pp}}(K,-K^{\prime})]$, is decomposable into a fully irreducible two-fermion vertex $\Lambda_{\mathrm{irr}}(K,K^{\prime})$ and contributions from particle-hole exchange channels according to $\Gamma_{\mathrm{even}}^{pp}(K,K^{\prime}) = \Lambda_{\mathrm{irr}}(K,K^{\prime}) + \frac{1}{2}\Phi_{d}(K,K^{\prime}) + \frac{3}{2}\Phi_{m}(K,K^{\prime})$, where $\Phi_{d}$ and $\Phi_{m}$ represent the charge density ($S=0$) and magnetic ($S=1$) particle-hole channels, respectively.

title: Definition of Particle-Hole Exchange Channels
content: The charge density channel $\Phi_{d}$ and magnetic channel $\Phi_{m}$ are defined using the full vertex $\Gamma$ and the irreducible particle-hole vertex $\Gamma^{\mathrm{ph}}$ via the relation $\Phi_{d/m}(K,K^{\prime}) = \frac{1}{2}[\Gamma_{d/m}(K-K^{\prime};K^{\prime},-K) - \Gamma_{d/m}^{\mathrm{ph}}(K-K^{\prime};K^{\prime},-K) + \Gamma_{d/m}(K+K^{\prime};-K^{\prime},-K) - \Gamma_{d/m}^{\mathrm{ph}}(K+K^{\prime};-K^{\prime},-K)]$, where $K$ and $K^{\prime}$ are momentum-frequency indices.

title: Dominance of Magnetic Exchange in the Pairing Interaction
content:
By decomposing the even-momentum irreducible particle-particle vertex $\Gamma_{\mathrm{even}}^{pp}$ into a fully irreducible two-fermion vertex $\Lambda_{\mathrm{irr}}$ and reducible particle-hole exchange contributions via the relation:
$ \Gamma_{\mathrm{even}}^{pp}(K,K^{\prime})=\Lambda_{\mathrm{irr}}(K,K^{\prime})+\frac{1}{2}\Phi_{d}(K,K^{\prime})+\frac{3}{2}\Phi_{m}(K,K^{\prime}) $
where $\Phi_{d}$ and $\Phi_{m}$ denote the charge density ($S=0$) and magnetic ($S=1$) particle-hole channels respectively, it is shown that the dominant contribution to the pairing interaction arises from the magnetic exchange channel $\Phi_{m}$, whereas the charge density channel $\Phi_{d}$ and the fully irreducible vertex $\Lambda_{\mathrm{irr}}$ are small and effectively flat in momentum space.

title: ARPES Selection Rules and Geometry
content: In an ARPES experimental geometry where the synchrotron radiation, the sample surface normal, and the detected photoelectrons all lie in a single horizontal plane, the radiation is linearly polarized either horizontally ($p$ polarization) or vertically ($s$ polarization). For a high-symmetry line in the tetragonal Brillouin zone of a crystal, such as the $(0,0)-(\pi,\pi)$ line, which corresponds to a structural mirror plane, the dipole transition matrix elements dictate that photoemission intensity from an initial state with even parity relative to that mirror plane is detected only using $p$-polarized light, whereas intensity from an initial state with odd parity is detected only using $s$-polarized light.

extra.notations:
- $p$ polarization: Linear polarization of radiation where the electric field vector lies in the plane of incidence (horizontal plane in the specified experimental geometry).
- $s$ polarization: Linear polarization of radiation where the electric field vector is perpendicular to the plane of incidence (vertical plane in the specified experimental geometry).
- ARPES: Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy.
- $\sigma^+$: Left circular polarization of light.
- $(0,0)-(\pi,\pi)$: A high-symmetry nodal line in the Brillouin zone of a tetragonal cell, also denoted as $\Gamma Y$ or $\Gamma X$ depending on the specific crystal orientation.
- ZRS: Zhang-Rice singlet, a many-body state describing a hole in a $CuO_2$ plane.
- Initial state: The electronic state of the system before the absorption of a photon in the photoemission process.
- Final state: The state of the system and the emitted photoelectron after the absorption of a photon.

title: Parity of Zhang-Rice Singlet States
content: In the context of high-$T_c$ cuprate superconductors, the Zhang-Rice singlet (ZRS) states, which characterize the main electronic bands, possess odd parity with respect to the mirror plane defined by the $(0,0)-(\pi,\pi)$ high-symmetry line in the tetragonal unit cell.

title: Experimental demonstration of orbital parity switching between Main and Shadow Fermi Surfaces
content:
Using angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) with full polarization analysis on pristine and Pb-doped $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8+\delta}$ (Bi2212), it is shown that the Main Fermi Surface (MFS) and Shadow Fermi Surface (SFS) exhibit opposite polarization symmetries along the $\Gamma Y$ nodal direction. Specifically, for the $\Gamma Y$ cut (measured with photon energy $h\nu = 100$ eV):
      - The Main Band (MB) initial states are odd with respect to the $\Gamma Y$ plane (visible with $s$ polarization, suppressed with $p$ polarization).
      - The Shadow Band (SB) initial states are even with respect to the same plane (visible with $p$ polarization, suppressed with $s$ polarization).
      In contrast, along the orthogonal $\Gamma X$ direction ($h\nu = 40$ eV), both MB and SB show identical polarization behavior, originating from initial states which are odd with respect to reflection in $\Gamma X$.

- ARPES: Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy.
- $\sigma^+$: Left circular polarization of light.
- $(0,0)-(\pi,\pi)$: A high-symmetry nodal line in the Brillouin zone of a tetragonal cell, also denoted as $\Gamma Y$ or $\Gamma X$ depending on the specific crystal orientation.
- ZRS: Zhang-Rice singlet, a many-body state describing a hole in a $CuO_2$ plane.
- Initial state: The electronic state of the system before the absorption of a photon in the photoemission process.
- Final state: The state of the system and the emitted photoelectron after the absorption of a photon.

title: ARPES Selection Rules in High-Symmetry Geometry
content: Consider an Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy (ARPES) experimental geometry where the incident synchrotron radiation, the sample surface normal, and the detected photoelectrons all lie within a single horizontal plane. If measurements are performed along a high-symmetry line of the crystal that constitutes a structural mirror plane (such as the nodal $(0,0)-(\pi,\pi)$ line in a tetragonal system), the initial electronic states can be classified by their parity (even or odd) with respect to that mirror plane. In this configuration, photoemission intensity from an initial state with even parity is detected only when using $p$-polarized radiation, while intensity from an initial state with odd parity is detected only when using $s$-polarized radiation.

title: LEED Systematic Extinctions and Glide-Mirror Symmetry
content: In Low Energy Electron Diffraction (LEED), the observation of systematic extinctions—defined as the absence of specific superstructure diffraction spots (indices) regardless of the primary electron beam energy—unambiguously identifies the presence of non-symmorphic translational symmetry elements, such as glide-mirror planes, in the surface crystal structure.

title: Parity Transformation of Wavefunction Products under Structural Distortion
content: In a quantum mechanical system where an initial undistorted wavefunction $\phi(x, y)$ is odd (antisymmetric) with respect to a spatial coordinate $x$ (i.e., $\phi(-x, y) = -\phi(x, y)$), and a structural distortion is introduced such that the new wavefunction $\phi_{D}(x, y)$ can be modeled as $\phi_{D}(x, y) = D(x)\phi(x, y)$, the parity of the distortion-induced component depends on the form of $D(x)$. If the distortion function $D(x)$ is dominated by a linear term proportional to $x$ (which is an odd function), the resulting product term $x\phi(x, y)$ exhibits even parity (symmetric) with respect to $x$, since the product of two odd functions is an even function.

title: Identification of specific orthorhombic distortion via LEED extinctions
content: Low energy electron diffraction (LEED) measurements on modulation-free (Bi,Pb)-2212 reveal systematic extinctions of superstructure spots indexed as $(h = \text{odd}, k = 0)$, independent of beam energy. These extinctions demonstrate that the underlying orthorhombic distortion preserves only a single nodal mirror plane running parallel to $xz$, while the $yz$ plane acts as a glide-mirror plane. This specific symmetry breaking (shift of Bi atoms in BiO layers along the $x$ direction) provides the structural basis for the parity swap observed in the electronic states.

title: Theoretical confirmation of SFS parity via one-step photoemission simulations
content:
DFT-based one-step photoemission calculations performed on the average distorted structure of modulation-free Bi2212 reproduce the experimental polarization dependence. The simulations confirm that:
- Along the $\Gamma Y$ azimuth, the SFS exhibits a parity swap relative to the MFS (SFS intensity follows the polarization opposite to the MFS).
- Along the $\Gamma X$ azimuth, the SFS follows the intensity distribution of the MFS.
This confirms that the parity alteration is an intrinsic part of the photoionization matrix element derived from the real, distorted orthorhombic structure.

title: Distortion Wave Function Ansatz
content: In a system with a real orthorhombic structural distortion, the total electronic wave function $\phi_{D}(x, y)$ can be modeled as $\phi_{D}(x, y) = D(x)\phi(x, y)$, where $\phi(x, y)$ represents the undistorted wave function of the tetragonal system and $D(x)$ represents the distortion function along the $x$ coordinate. For small distortions, the distortion function can be approximated by a Taylor expansion $D(x) \approx 1 + xD'(0)$, such that $\phi_{D}(x, y) \approx \phi(x, y) + xD'(0)\phi(x, y)$.

title: Assumption: Distortion Parity Property
content: In the expansion of the distorted wave function $\phi_{D}(x, y) \approx \phi(x, y) + xD'(0)\phi(x, y)$, the term $xD'(0)\phi(x, y)$ is defined as the component responsible for the shadow band (SB). If the spatial coordinate $x$ is an odd function relative to $x=0$ and the undistorted wave function $\phi(x, y)$ is also an odd function in $x$, then their product $x\phi(x, y)$ is an even function, implying a parity swap for the shadow band state relative to the main band state.

title: Structural origin of the Shadow Fermi Surface
content: Based on the convergence of the polarization-dependent ARPES data, LEED extinctions, and ab initio simulations, the Shadow Fermi Surface in Bi2212 (and by analogy Bi2201) is proven to be of structural origin. It arises from bulk orthorhombic distortions from tetragonal symmetry, primarily located in the BiO planes but affecting the $CuO_2$ plane states, rather than arising from magnetic mechanisms such as antiferromagnetic fluctuations.

title: ARPES Selection Rules for Mirror Planes
content: In an angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) experimental geometry where the synchrotron radiation, the sample surface normal, and the detected photoelectrons all lie in a single horizontal plane, initial electronic states associated with a high-symmetry line acting as a structural mirror plane are classified by their parity as even or odd with respect to that plane. Under these conditions, photoemission intensity from an even initial state is detected only using $p$ polarization (where the electric field vector is in the horizontal plane), while intensity from an odd initial state is detected only using $s$ polarization (where the electric field vector is perpendicular to the horizontal plane).

- $h\nu$: photon energy
- $E_{F}$: Fermi energy

title: Symmetry of Zhang-Rice Singlet States
content: The Zhang-Rice singlet (ZRS) states, which characterize the main band (MB) of the electronic structure in cuprate superconductors, possess odd parity with respect to reflection across both the $(0, 0)-(\pi, \pi)$ ($\Gamma Y$) mirror plane and the orthogonal ($\Gamma X$) mirror plane within the idealized 2D tetragonal Brillouin zone of the crystal.

title: Wavefunction Distortion Model
content: The electronic wavefunction of a structurally distorted system, $\phi_{D}(x, y)$, can be modeled as the product of a spatial distortion function $D(x)$ and the undistorted wavefunction $\phi(x, y)$, such that $\phi_{D}(x, y) = D(x)\phi(x, y)$. For a small distortion expanded as a Taylor series $D(x) = 1 + xD'(0) + \ldots$, the total wavefunction is approximated by $\phi_{D}(x, y) \approx \phi(x, y) + xD'(0)\phi(x, y)$. If the spatial coordinate function $x$ and the undistorted wavefunction $\phi(x, y)$ are both odd functions with respect to the variable $x$, the product term $x\phi(x, y)$—which represents the component responsible for the backfolded shadow band states—is an even function with respect to $x$, resulting in a parity swap relative to the undistorted state.

title: Tetragonal Symmetry Equivalence
content: In an ideal tetragonal unit cell of a crystal such as $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8+\delta}$, the $(0,0)-(\pi,\pi)$ direction (labeled $\Gamma Y$) and the orthogonal $(0,0)-(\pi,-\pi)$ direction (labeled $\Gamma X$) are equivalent high-symmetry lines that function as structural mirror planes for the electronic initial states.

title: Requirement for TRS Breaking Studies
content: Experimental investigations and theoretical claims regarding the spontaneous breaking of time reversal symmetry (TRS) in Bismuth-based cuprate superconductors, such as $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8+\delta}$, fundamentally rely on the assumption that the $(0,0)-(\pi,0)$ planes (and by symmetry the equivalent nodal planes) act as true structural mirror planes for the electronic states of the $CuO_{2}$ planes in the system.

title: Absence of (0,0)-(pi,0) mirror planes and implications for Time Reversal Symmetry
content: The established orthorhombic distortion implies that the $(0,0)-(\pi,0)$ planes are not true mirror planes for the $CuO_{2}$ plane states, even in modulation-free samples. Consequently, experimental claims of spontaneous time reversal symmetry (TRS) breaking that rely on the assumption of these planes acting as mirror planes in Bi-based cuprates are identified as questionable.

title: Doping mechanism in Bi-2212
content: In the high-temperature cuprate superconductor $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8+\delta}$, hole-doped superconductivity is induced by the addition of a number $\delta$ of nonstoichiometric oxygen atoms per unit cell; each such nonstoichiometric oxygen dopant atom acts as an impurity with a charge of $-2e$.

title: Visibility of stoichiometric oxygen in STM
content: In scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) studies of $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8+\delta}$, the stoichiometric oxygen atoms within the bismuth-oxide (BiO) layer are invisible in low-bias topographic images because their electronic contributions to the BiO band structure are located at energies far from the Fermi energy.

title: Properties of Bi-on-Sr defect
content: The chemical substitution of a bismuth ($Bi$) atom onto a strontium ($Sr$) site within the $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8+\delta}$ crystal structure creates a positively charged defect (specifically $Bi^{3+}$ residing on a $Sr^{2+}$ site) that produces a localized electronic impurity state observed at an energy $E = +1.7$ eV relative to the Fermi level.

title: Relation between superconducting gap and hole density
content: For the cuprate superconductor $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8+\delta}$, the average magnitude of the superconducting energy gap $\bar{\Delta}$ is related to the hole density $p$ per $CuO_{2}$ plaquette by the empirical relation $\bar{\Delta} \approx [75 - 275 (\pm 10\%) \times p]$ meV.

title: Identification of oxygen dopant impurity states
content: The authors identified the atomic-scale locations of individual oxygen dopant atoms in $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8+\delta}$ by detecting a specific electronic impurity state characterized by a broad differential conductance peak at $V = -0.96$ V, located approximately $1.9$ \AA\ laterally from the nearest Bi atom. The identification is established by the observation that the spatial density $n$ of these features correlates with the average superconducting gap magnitude $\bar{\Delta}$ according to the relation $\bar{\Delta} \approx [75 - 600 (\pm 15\%) \times n]$ meV, consistent with each impurity state contributing $\sim 2$ holes to the $CuO_2$ plane.

title: Dopant Charge and Impurity State Hypothesis
content: In the high-temperature cuprate superconductor $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8+\delta}$ (Bi-2212), hole doping is implemented by the addition of $\delta$ nonstoichiometric oxygen atoms per unit cell to the parent insulator. Each such nonstoichiometric oxygen dopant atom acts as a charged impurity with an electrostatic charge of $-2e$.

title: Break Junction Gap-Doping Relation
content: For the cuprate superconductor $Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8+\delta}$, experimental break junction studies establish a linear relationship between the average superconducting energy gap magnitude $\bar{\Delta}$ and the hole density $p$ per $CuO_2$ plaquette, expressed as $\bar{\Delta} \approx [75 - 275 (\pm 10\%) \times p]$ meV.

title: Hole Accumulation Model Prediction
content: The "hole accumulation" theoretical model for cuprate superconductors assumes that the $-2e$ electrostatic charge of a dopant oxygen atom is poorly screened; this produces a repulsive potential for electrons, resulting in a local accumulation of holes at the dopant site. This model predicts that such a local increase in hole density $p$ will cause a localized reduction in the superconducting energy gap $\Delta$ in the vicinity of the dopant atom.

title: Definition of Normalized Cross-Correlation
content: The normalized cross-correlation $C_{fg}(\vec{R})$ between two 2D images represented by functions $f(\vec{r})$ and $g(\vec{r})$ is defined as $C_{fg}(\vec{R}) = \frac{\int [f(\vec{r}^{\prime})-\bar{f}] \times [g(\vec{r}^{\prime}+\vec{R})-\bar{g}] d^{2}r}{\sqrt{A_{ff}(0)A_{gg}(0)}}$, where $\bar{f}$ and $\bar{g}$ are the spatial averages of the respective images. The term $A_{ff}(\vec{R})$ denotes the autocorrelation function of $f$, defined as $A_{ff}(\vec{R}) = \int [f(\vec{r}^{\prime})-\bar{f}] \times [f(\vec{r}^{\prime}+\vec{R})-\bar{f}] d^{2}r$.

title: Dopant defects as the source of nanoscale electronic disorder
content: The authors demonstrated that the identified oxygen dopant defects are the primary atomic-scale source of the nanoscale electronic disorder in the superconducting energy gap $\Delta(\vec{r})$, based on a strong spatial cross-correlation between dopant defect images $O(\vec{r})$ and gap maps $\Delta(\vec{r})$. Specifically, the positive cross-correlation $C[O(\vec{r}) : \Delta(\vec{r})] \approx +0.3$ indicates that superconducting gap magnitudes are locally increased near dopant atoms, a finding that refutes theoretical models attributing the disorder solely to dopant-induced electrostatic hole accumulation, which would require reduced gaps in hole-rich regions.

title: Relationship between LDOS and Differential Conductance
content: In a scanning tunneling microscopy experiment, the Local Density of States (LDOS) at a spatial position $\vec{r}$ and energy $E=eV$ (where $e$ is the elementary charge and $V$ is the bias voltage) is proportional to the differential tunneling conductance $g(\vec{r}, V)$, defined as $g(\vec{r}, V) \equiv \frac{dI}{dV}\big|_{\vec{r},V}$, where $I$ is the tunneling current.

extra.notations:
- $LDOS(\vec{r}, E)$: Local Density of States at spatial position $\vec{r}$ and energy $E$.
- $g(\vec{r}, V)$: Differential tunneling conductance at position $\vec{r}$ and bias voltage $V$.
- $I(V_B)$: Tunneling current at sample bias voltage $V_B$.
- $dI/dV$: Derivative of current with respect to voltage.
- $e$: Elementary charge.
- $z(\vec{r}, V_B)$: Vertical tip displacement (topographic height) at position $\vec{r}$ for a constant-current setpoint at bias $V_B$.
- $z_0$: Exponential tunneling scale factor (decay length).
- $C$: Instrument-dependent constant.
- $\delta\rho(\vec{r})$: Spatial variation in charge density.
- $C_{fg}(\vec{R})$: Normalized cross-correlation between two-dimensional images $f$ and $g$ at relative displacement $\vec{R}$.
- $A_{ff}(\vec{R})$: Auto-correlation function of image $f$ at displacement $\vec{R}$.
- $\bar{f}$: Spatial average of the image $f(\vec{r})$.
- $n$: Spatial density of impurity states per $CuO_2$ plaquette.

title: Tunneling Current Equation
content: The tunneling current $I(V_B)$ measured at a sample bias voltage $V_B$ and spatial position $\vec{r}$, characterized by a vertical tip displacement $z(\vec{r}, V_B)$ and an exponential tunneling factor $z_0$, is proportional to the product of the tunneling matrix element and the integrated density of states: $I(V_B) \propto \exp[-z(\vec{r}, V_B)/z_0] \times \int_{0}^{eV_B} LDOS(E, \vec{r}) dE$.
