The rewrite removes any assertion about the direction or about divergent reports and keeps only the intersectional, self-contained factual claim that is supported by every child.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (Kelvin).
      - "450°C in flowing oxygen": thermal treatment consisting of heating the sample to 450 degrees Celsius in a continuous oxygen gas flow.
      - "Tl‑Ba‑Cu‑O / Tl‑Ca/Ba‑Cu‑O": thallium‑based layered cuprate superconductor families.

title: Defining the FLEX approximation for the Hubbard model
content: For the Hubbard model on a square lattice (nearest-neighbor hopping t and lattice constant a, on-site interaction U, chemical potential μ controlling the average filling n), the fluctuation-exchange (FLEX) approximation is a conserving many-body approximation defined by a Luttinger–Ward free-energy functional Φ of the dressed single-particle Green's function G. The functional Φ is constructed as the sum of the following classes of many-body diagrams: (i) the Hartree–Fock diagram, (ii) ring diagrams that represent interactions mediated by longitudinal spin and density (charge) fluctuation channels, (iii) diagrams corresponding to transverse spin fluctuations, and (iv) particle–particle (pair) fluctuation diagrams. As a conserving approximation derived from such a Φ functional, FLEX is the diagrammatic prescription that yields single-particle and two-particle quantities consistently from that Φ construction.

extra.notations:
- t: nearest-neighbor hopping integral (kinetic energy scale) on the square lattice
- a: lattice constant (distance between neighboring sites)
- U: on-site Coulomb repulsion energy
- μ: chemical potential controlling electronic filling n
- n: electron filling (average electrons per site)
- Φ: Luttinger–Ward free-energy functional built from selected diagram classes of the interacting theory
- G: dressed single-particle Green's function (propagator)
- "Hartree–Fock": lowest-order diagrammatic mean-field contribution included in Φ
- "ring diagrams": classes of bubble-chain diagrams representing longitudinal spin and density fluctuation interactions
- "transverse spin fluctuations": diagram classes coupling transverse spin channels
- "particle–particle fluctuations": diagram classes in the particle–particle (pairing) channel

title: Layered structure of Tl2Ba2CuO6: Cu–O sheets alternating with double Tl–O sheets
content: In the cuprate compound Tl2Ba2CuO6 (chemical formula Tl₂Ba₂CuO₆, commonly called Tl-2201), the crystal structure is layered along the crystallographic $c$ axis: single copper–oxygen (Cu–O) sheets (CuO₂-like planes containing copper atoms coordinated by oxygen) alternate with double thallium–oxygen (Tl–O) sheets (two closely spaced Tl–O layers). Thus the Cu–O layers are separated from one another by intervening double Tl–O layers stacked along the $c$ direction.

extra.notations:
- Tl₂Ba₂CuO₆ (Tl-2201): thallium barium copper oxide with nominal composition Tl₂Ba₂CuO₆. 
- Cu–O sheet: a copper–oxygen plane (CuO₂-like plane) in which copper atoms are coordinated by oxygen atoms.
- Tl–O sheet: a thallium–oxygen layer; "double Tl–O sheet" denotes two closely spaced Tl–O layers that occur together between Cu–O sheets.
- $c$ axis: the crystallographic axis perpendicular to the Cu–O/Tl–O stacking direction (layer-normal direction).

title: Conductivity proportional to excited fraction in hydrogenic donors under a rectangular THz pulse
content: For an ensemble of hydrogenic donor atoms subjected to a rectangular terahertz (THz) electric-field pulse of duration τ (and peak field E_{THz}), the change in electrical conductivity induced by the pulse is proportional to (1 - ρ_{11}(τ)), where ρ_{11}(τ) is the density-matrix population of the donor ground state (1s) at time τ. Therefore (1 - ρ_{11}(τ)) represents the fraction of donors that have been excited out of the ground state by the end of the pulse and thus sets the magnitude of the THz-induced conductivity signal.

extra.notations:
- ρ_{11}(τ): population of the donor ground state (1s) at the end of the rectangular THz pulse of duration τ. 
- (1 - ρ_{11}(τ)): fraction of the donor ensemble excited out of the ground state by time τ. 
- τ: duration of the applied rectangular terahertz pulse. 
- E_{THz}: peak electric-field amplitude of the terahertz pulse (pulse shape assumed rectangular).

title: Effective-mass scaling and anomalously short coherence length in doped Hubbard/RVB metals
content: Consider a doped Hubbard / resonating-valence-bond (RVB) metallic system with total lattice sites $N$ and fractional hole doping $0<\delta\ll1$, so the average electron occupation per site is $n=N(1-\delta)$. In this regime at low temperature the low-energy quasiparticles have an effective mass $m^{*}$ that scales approximately inversely with the doping, $m^{*}\approx m/\delta$, where $m$ denotes the bare electron mass. Under the same conditions the superconducting coherence length $\xi_{0}$ is anomalously short, satisfying the relation $k_{\mathrm{F}}\,\xi_{0}=E_{\mathrm{F}}/\Delta\ll 1$, where $k_{\mathrm{F}}$ is the Fermi wave vector, $E_{\mathrm{F}}$ the Fermi energy, and $\Delta$ the superconducting energy gap. These two statements summarize the shared low-energy phenomenology reported in both propositions for the doped Hubbard/RVB metallic regime.

extra.notations:
- $N$: total number of lattice sites\n
- $\delta$: fractional hole doping (so average occupation per site $n=N(1-\delta)$)\n
- $m$: bare electron mass\n
- $m^{*}$: effective mass of low-energy quasiparticles\n
- $\xi_{0}$: superconducting coherence length\n
- $k_{\mathrm{F}}$: Fermi wave vector\n
- $E_{\mathrm{F}}$: Fermi energy\n
- $\Delta$: superconducting energy gap

title: Impurity-induced four-fold symmetric DOS aligned with d-wave nodes
content: In a superconductor with $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ (d-wave) pairing symmetry, theoretical analyses of quasiparticle scattering from an atomic-scale impurity predict that the local quasiparticle density of states (DOS) in the impurity vicinity will exhibit a four-fold symmetric (cross-shaped) spatial pattern whose arms are aligned along the crystallographic a- and b-axes; these directions coincide with the nodes of the $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ superconducting gap.

extra.notations:
- DOS: local quasiparticle density of states (spatially resolved quantity measurable by differential tunneling conductance $dI/dV$ in STM). 
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: shorthand for a superconducting order parameter with $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ symmetry, having gap nodes along the crystal a- and b-axes.
- "Atomic-scale impurity": a point-like substitutional or vacancy defect (e.g., Zn substituting for Cu) that scatters quasiparticles locally.

title: Empirical trend: Tc increases with CuO2 layer count in layered cuprates
content: Layered copper-oxide superconductors (materials whose crystal structures contain one or more CuO2 planes per crystallographic unit cell) empirically tend to show higher superconducting transition temperatures $T_{c}$ as the number of CuO2 planes per unit cell increases. In other words, within homologous series of cuprate compounds, phases with a larger number of CuO2 layers per unit cell are generally observed to have larger $T_{c}$ values than phases in the same series with fewer CuO2 layers.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (the temperature below which a sample exhibits zero electrical resistance and the Meissner effect).
- "CuO$_2$ plane" or "Cu–O plane": a crystallographic layer containing copper and oxygen atoms in the square-planar motif characteristic of cuprate superconductors; the count is the number of such planes per crystallographic unit cell.
- "Layered copper-oxide superconductors" (cuprates): families of superconducting compounds whose crystal structures are built from stacked CuO$_2$ planes (examples include Hg-, Tl-, and Bi-based homologous series).

title: Tetragonal P4/nmm crystal structure of La[O1-xFx]FeAs
content: The layered quaternary oxypnictide La[O$_{1-x}$F$_x$]FeAs (where La = lanthanum, O = oxygen, Fe = iron, As = arsenic, and $x$ denotes the nominal fluoride substitution on oxygen) crystallizes in a tetragonal crystal structure corresponding to space group P4/nmm; this structure is characterized by two tetragonal lattice parameters conventionally labeled $a$ (in-plane lattice constant) and $c$ (out-of-plane lattice constant). In other words, the LaOFeAs/La[O$_{1-x}$F$_x$]FeAs family adopts a tetragonal P4/nmm-type lattice across the compositions discussed in the source propositions.

extra.notations:
- La[O$_{1-x}$F$_x$]FeAs: Lanthanum oxyfluoride iron arsenide where $x$ is the nominal molar fraction of F$^{-}$ substituting for O$^{2-}$.
- $P4/nmm$: A tetragonal crystallographic space group designation.
- $a$: In-plane tetragonal lattice constant (conventionally in Å or nm).
- $c$: Out-of-plane tetragonal lattice constant (conventionally in Å or nm).

title: Undoped LaFeAsO tetragonal lattice parameters
content: Undoped lanthanum iron arsenide oxide (LaFeAsO, i.e., the $x=0$ member of La[O$_{1-x}$F$_x$]FeAs) crystallizes in the tetragonal $P4/nmm$ structure with tetragonal lattice parameters approximately $a = 4.03552\ \text{\AA}$ and $c = 8.7393\ \text{\AA}$. Here $a$ and $c$ are the conventional in-plane and out-of-plane lattice constants of the tetragonal unit cell.

extra.notations:
- LaFeAsO (LaOFeAs): Undoped lanthanum iron arsenide oxide.
- $a$: In-plane tetragonal lattice constant, $a = 4.03552\ \text{\AA}$.
- $c$: Out-of-plane tetragonal lattice constant, $c = 8.7393\ \text{\AA}$.
- $\text{\AA}$: Angstrom unit ($1\ \text{\AA} = 10^{-10}$ m).

title: Shared superconducting facts for Tl2Ba2CuO6 and Bi2Sr2CuO6
content: In single-crystalline samples of thallium barium copper oxide, Tl2Ba2CuO6, a superconducting transition is observed with an onset temperature near 90 K (where $T_{c}$ denotes the superconducting transition temperature). The bismuth cuprate Bi2Sr2CuO6 is a superconducting bismuth–strontium–copper oxide (a bismuth-based cuprate). These statements are intended as factual properties of the materials (composition and occurrence of superconductivity) and make no claims about synthesis details, exact zero-resistivity temperatures, morphological differences, or thermal-history dependence.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (onset of superconductivity). 
- Tl2Ba2CuO6: thallium barium copper oxide, a cuprate superconductor. 
- Bi2Sr2CuO6: bismuth strontium copper oxide, a bismuth-based cuprate superconductor.

title: Oxygen dopants introduce hole carriers in Bi-2212
content: In the high-temperature cuprate superconductor Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (abbreviated Bi-2212), nonstoichiometric (interstitial or otherwise extra) oxygen dopant atoms introduce hole carriers into the CuO2 planes: increasing the density of such oxygen dopants increases the hole concentration in the CuO2 units.

extra.notations:
- Bi-2212: Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ, a layered cuprate superconductor with conducting CuO2 planes.
- $\delta$: the number of nonstoichiometric (extra/interstitial) oxygen atoms per crystallographic unit cell.
- CuO2 plane: the copper–oxygen layer in which hole carriers (holes) reside and conduct.
- "Hole carrier": a positive charge carrier (absence of an electron) contributing to the p-type doping of the CuO2 plane.

title: Starting materials for a reported high-pressure synthesis of $Sm[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs$
content: A reported high‑pressure synthesis of the fluorine‑doped samarium iron oxyarsenide $Sm[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs$ uses pre‑sintered samarium arsenide (SmAs) powder, elemental arsenic (As), elemental iron (Fe), iron(III) oxide ($Fe_{2}O_{3}$), and iron(II) fluoride ($FeF_{2}$) as starting materials; the reagents are reported at purities greater than 99.99%.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted the existence of "multiple reported synthesis routes (including ambient‑pressure solid‑state and high‑pressure preparations)" that use SmAs, Fe and $Fe_{2}O_{3}$, which formed a union-style claim: that multiplicity and both route types exist. Verification showed no single child independently asserts both ambient and high‑pressure routes, so that quantified claim is not entailed by each child. All three children, however, explicitly list $SmAs$, Fe and $Fe_{2}O_{3}$ among their starting reagents. The rewritten parent removes the unsupported claim about multiple route types and states only the reagent overlap that is present in every child. This revised proposition is self-contained, substantive, and is entailed by each child individually.

The original parent overreached by asserting that "multiple reported synthesis routes (including ambient‑pressure solid‑state and high‑pressure) employ SmAs, Fe, and $Fe_{2}O_{3}$." Child 1425 documents only a high‑pressure route and thus does not support claims about ambient‑pressure or multiple distinct routes. To ensure the parent is entailed by the child, I restricted the claim to the reported high‑pressure synthesis and included only the specific starting materials and purity information that the child explicitly lists (pre‑sintered SmAs, As, Fe, $Fe_{2}O_{3}$, $FeF_{2}$, purity >99.99%). This removes the unsupported plurality/ambient‑pressure assertions while remaining a substantive, self‑contained proposition fully supported by the child.

extra.notations:
- $Sm[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs$: samarium iron oxyarsenide doped by partial substitution of O by F (Sm = samarium)
      - $SmAs$: samarium arsenide
      - As: elemental arsenic
      - Fe: elemental iron
      - $Fe_{2}O_{3}$: iron(III) oxide
      - $FeF_{2}$: iron(II) fluoride

title: Target nominal composition SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}
content: Each proposition concerns syntheses targeting the nominal chemical composition SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}, i.e., the samarium iron oxyarsenide in which some oxygen sites are nominally substituted by fluorine (general formula SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}).

extra.notations: - $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$: samarium iron oxyarsenide with partial fluorine substitution on the oxygen site (Sm = samarium; Fe = iron; As = arsenic; O = oxygen; F = fluorine).

title: Solid‑state synthesis of fluorine‑doped SmFeAsO from SmAs, Fe and Fe2O3
content: Fluorine‑doped samarium iron oxyarsenide, SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}, can be prepared by solid‑state (including high‑pressure) synthesis from stoichiometric precursor mixtures that include samarium arsenide (SmAs), elemental iron (Fe), and iron(III) oxide (Fe_{2}O_{3}).

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent included an unsupported generalization ("commonly used as starting materials") that is not entailed by the single child report. Child 1426 documents a high‑pressure solid‑state synthesis that explicitly lists SmAs, Fe and Fe_{2}O_{3} among the stoichiometric precursors, so the factual claim that SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} can be prepared from precursor mixtures including those three compounds is entailed. I removed the frequency/generalization ("commonly used") and rephrased the sentence to be self‑contained and strictly limited to the intersection of claims supported by the child, so the revised parent is now entailed by the child and no unsupported assertions remain.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}: fluorine‑doped samarium iron oxyarsenide, where $x$ is the nominal F content
      - SmAs: samarium arsenide (precursor)
      - Fe: elemental iron (precursor)
      - Fe_{2}O_{3}: iron(III) oxide (precursor)

title: Solid‑state sintering produces polycrystalline SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}
content: Polycrystalline samarium iron oxyarsenide SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} can be produced by a solid‑state reaction in which stoichiometric mixtures of solid precursors are thermally sintered (heat‑treated at elevated temperature) so that the precursors react to form the target polycrystalline phase. This statement covers both conventional solid‑state sintering and high‑pressure sintering variants insofar as they share the same basic process: thermal reaction of solid precursor mixtures to yield polycrystalline SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}: fluorine‑doped samarium iron oxyarsenide (product)
- "solid‑state reaction" / "sintering": thermal treatment of compacted solid precursor powders that induces chemical reaction and grain growth to form a polycrystalline phase

title: Zero resistivity (superconducting) in F-doped La[O1-xFx]FeAs within 5–22 K
content: For lanthanum iron oxypnictide with fluorine substitution, La[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs, samples with nominal fluoride content x > 4 atom % exhibit a transition of the electrical resistivity $\rho(T)$ to zero (i.e., a superconducting state characterized by vanishing dc resistivity) at low temperatures in the temperature interval 5–22 K. This statement asserts only the occurrence of zero resistivity in that temperature interval and does not assume a specific transition width or additional normal-state anomalies.

extra.notations:
- La[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs: Lanthanum iron oxypnictide in which a fraction $x$ of oxygen sites are nominally replaced by fluoride (F^{-}); $x$ is the nominal atomic fraction of F per formula unit. 
- $x>4\%$: Fluoride content greater than 4 atom percent (i.e., $x>0.04$ in fractional units).
- $\rho(T)$: Temperature-dependent electrical resistivity; "zero resistivity" means the measured dc resistivity falls to the instrumental zero within the stated temperature range, indicating a superconducting state.
- Temperature interval 5–22 K: The inclusive range of temperatures (in kelvin) within which zero resistivity is observed in the samples considered.

title: SmFeAsO (fluorine-free) lattice parameters
content: For the fluorine-free parent compound samarium iron arsenide oxide (chemical formula SmFeAsO), the lattice-parameter values are a = 0.3940 nm and c = 0.8501 nm, where a denotes the in-plane (basal-plane edge length) lattice parameter and c denotes the out-of-plane (unit-cell height) lattice parameter.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO: samarium iron arsenide oxide, the fluorine-free parent composition in this materials family
- a: in-plane (basal-plane) lattice parameter (edge length) in nanometres (nm)
- c: out-of-plane (perpendicular) lattice parameter (unit-cell height) in nanometres (nm)

title: Resistivity drop as an operational criterion for superconducting onset in some oxygen‑deficient polycrystalline Ba–La–Cu–O samples
content: Some oxygen‑deficient, polycrystalline samples of the barium–lanthanum–copper‑oxide system $Ba_{x}La_{5-x}Cu_{5}O_{5(3-y)}$ exhibit a pronounced downward change (a sharp drop) in the temperature‑dependent electrical resistivity $\rho(T)$ that marks the superconducting onset temperature $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}$. Observing such a pronounced downward change in $\rho(T)$ therefore serves as an operational experimental criterion for identifying $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}$ in these samples.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent made a class‑level universal claim that resisted entailment from Child 1363, which reports observations only on particular samples (e.g. $x=1$ and $x=0.75$). Child 1369 supports a methodological criterion for the material class. To ensure every child individually entails the parent, I weakened the quantification to an existential claim ("Some ... samples") and limited the content to the shared, supported claim: that a pronounced drop in $\rho(T)$ indicates the superconducting onset and can be used as an operational criterion in such samples. This removes any unsupported universal generalization and excludes numeric or microstructural specifics present only in individual children while remaining substantive and self‑contained.

extra.notations:
- $\rho(T)$: electrical resistivity as a function of temperature $T$. 
      - $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}$: superconducting onset temperature defined as the temperature where $\rho(T)$ first shows a pronounced downward change attributable to superconductivity.
      - $Ba_{x}La_{5-x}Cu_{5}O_{5(3-y)}$: nominal composition of the barium–lanthanum–copper‑oxide samples, with $y>0$ indicating oxygen deficiency.

title: High-pressure stability of P6_3/mmc YH9
content: The hexagonal close-packed yttrium hydride phase with composition YH9 and space group P6_3/mmc is a hydrogen-rich yttrium hydride that is thermodynamically stable at high pressures (i.e., pressures on the order of 10^2 gigapascals). Here "high pressure" refers to static pressures of order one hundred gigapascals or greater (hundreds of GPa), and "stable" means the phase is the hydrogen-richest thermodynamically favored yttrium hydride under such pressure–temperature conditions.

extra.notations:
- $YH_{9}$: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio 9
- $P6_{3}/mmc$: crystallographic space group label for a hexagonal close-packed structure
- GPa: gigapascal, unit of pressure

title: Theoretical prediction of a high‑Tc fcc YH10 phase
content: Theoretical calculations predict a face-centered cubic (fcc) yttrium hydride with composition YH10 that is expected to have a high superconducting critical temperature.

refine_history.reasoning:
The original parent combined two different claims from different children: (a) detailed numerical theoretical predictions (specific $T_{c}$ estimates and pressure ranges) coming from Child 251, and (b) an experimental null result and phase identification (that YH10 does not form up to 410 GPa and 2250 K) coming from Child 269. Neither child individually entails that combined union. Verification flagged this as a union error and recommended the edge be classed as partial_overlap.

To satisfy the requirement that every child individually entails the parent, I removed any material supported by only one child (the explicit numerical $T_{c}$ estimates, the pressure values, and the experimental refutation/phase identification). The rewritten proposition states only the intersection: both children assert the existence of theoretical predictions proposing an fcc YH10 phase with high superconducting $T_{c}$. This revised parent is self-contained, factual, and is individually entailed by Child 251 (which gives the detailed theoretical prediction) and by Child 269 (which refers generically to theoretical predictions of a high‑$T_{c}$ fcc YH10 phase).

extra.notations:
- $YH_{10}$: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio 10
      - fcc: face-centered cubic crystal structure
      - $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature

title: I–V superconducting signature and experimental critical current in FeSe/STO 4PP transport
content: In four-point-probe (4PP) electrical transport measurements on a single-layer iron selenide (FeSe) film grown on a conducting Nb-doped SrTiO3 substrate (denoted FeSe/STO), superconducting behavior in the FeSe top layer is diagnosed from the measured current–voltage (I–V) characteristics by the presence of a zero-voltage (zero-resistance) regime at low applied currents and the onset of a finite voltage above a sample-specific threshold current. That threshold current is identified operationally as the critical current, I_c, of the superconducting FeSe layer: below I_c the measured voltage is (within instrumental resolution) zero, while above I_c a finite resistance is detected.

extra.notations:
- FeSe/STO: a single atomic-layer iron selenide (FeSe) film grown on a conducting Nb-doped SrTiO3 (SrTiO3) substrate. 
- 4PP: four-point probe electrical transport measurement (four-terminal I–V measurement).
- I–V: current–voltage characteristic obtained in transport measurements.
- I_c: critical current, the experimentally identified current threshold separating zero-voltage and finite-voltage regimes in the measured I–V.

title: Inelastic scattering response in the 20–30 meV range for superconducting YBa2Cu3O6.92
content: In the superconducting state of YBa2Cu3O6.92 (chemical formula YBa2Cu3O6.92, with superconducting transition temperature Tc ≈ 91 K), inelastic scattering experiments detect non-negligible scattering intensity (a measurable spectral response) in the energy-transfer window ℏω ≈ 20–30 meV. This statement is a common factual intersection of the two reports: one measurement finds a spectral feature at ≈21.5 meV (a broad, ungapped nuclear-scattering component) and the other identifies spin-excitation spectral weight concentrated at energies around and above ≈28 meV (with little magnetic weight below ≈25 meV), so both establish that observable scattering-related spectral features occur within the ~20–30 meV energy range in the superconducting state of YBa2Cu3O6.92.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: Yttrium barium copper oxide with oxygen content per formula unit 6.92.
- Tc: superconducting critical temperature (here Tc ≈ 91 K).
- ℏω: energy transfer in inelastic scattering (ℏ is the reduced Planck constant, ω the angular frequency).
- "Scattering intensity" / "spectral response": measurable inelastic-scattering signal (magnetic or nuclear/non-magnetic) as a function of energy transfer.

title: Transverse gauge propagator form in the slave-boson t-J strange-metal
content:
Consider the two-dimensional t-J model formulated with a slave-boson decomposition of the electron into fermionic spinons $f_{i\sigma}$ and bosonic holons $b_i$, and an emergent U(1) gauge field $a_{\mu}$. In the "strange metal" regime, defined by the holons not being Bose-condensed (temperature above the holon Bose-Einstein condensation scale), the retarded transverse component of the gauge-field propagator at long wavelengths and low frequencies (small wavevector $q$ and low frequency $\omega$) takes the form
$$D^{T}(q,\omega)\;=\;\big[i\,\omega\,\sigma(q)\;-\;\chi_{d}\,q^{2}\big]^{-1}.$$
Here $D^{T}(q,\omega)$ is the retarded propagator of the transverse part of $a_{\mu}$, $\sigma(q)$ denotes the dissipative (transverse) conductivity/response of the matter fields at wavevector $q$, and $\chi_{d}=\chi_{F}+\chi_{B}$ is the total diamagnetic susceptibility given by the sum of the fermionic (spinon) and bosonic (holon) diamagnetic susceptibilities. This expression applies to low-energy, long-wavelength gauge fluctuations in the non–Bose-condensed (strange-metal) phase of the slave-boson t-J model.

extra.notations:
- $t$: nearest-neighbor hopping amplitude in the t-J model (appears in the microscopic Hamiltonian). 
- $J$: antiferromagnetic exchange coupling in the t-J model.
- $f_{i\sigma}$: fermionic spinon annihilation operator at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.
- $b_i$: bosonic holon annihilation operator at site $i$.
- $a_{\mu}=(a_0,\mathbf{a})$: emergent U(1) gauge field; $D^{T}(q,\omega)$ denotes its retarded transverse propagator (Fourier variables $q,\omega$).
- $\sigma(q)$: transverse dissipative conductivity/response function of the matter (spinon/holon) sectors at wavevector $q$ (frequency dependence suppressed in the notation above).
- $\chi_{F},\chi_{B}$: diamagnetic susceptibilities of the fermion (spinon) and boson (holon) subsystems; $\chi_d=\chi_F+\chi_B$.
- "Strange metal" regime: temperature/parameter range in which holons are not Bose-condensed (holon condensate amplitude is zero).

title: Definition of the isotope coefficient via the mass scaling of Tc
content: In a superconducting material, the isotope coefficient $\\alpha$ quantifies how the superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ depends on the isotopic mass $m$: specifically, $T_{c}$ scales as a power of the isotopic mass, i.e. there exists a material-specific positive constant $A$ such that $T_{c}=A\\,m^{-\\alpha}$ (equivalently $T_{c}\\propto m^{-\\alpha}$). Here $T_{c}$ is the superconducting critical temperature (in kelvin), $m$ is the isotopic mass (in atomic mass units or kilograms), $A$ is a constant that depends on the material and other non-mass parameters, and $\\alpha$ is the dimensionless isotope coefficient. This relation implies the equivalent differential definition $\\alpha=-\\dfrac{d\\ln T_{c}}{d\\ln m}$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (K)
- $m$: isotopic mass (atomic mass units or kg)
- $A$: material-specific positive constant in the power law $T_{c}=A\\,m^{-\\alpha}$
- $\\alpha$: isotope coefficient, dimensionless

title: BCS reference value for the isotope coefficient
content: Within the Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer (BCS) theory of conventional (phonon-mediated) superconductivity, and under the harmonic approximation for lattice vibrations, the characteristic (canonical) value of the isotope coefficient $\\alpha$ is $\\alpha=0.5$. In this context $\\alpha$ is the dimensionless exponent in the mass-scaling relation $T_{c}\\propto m^{-\\alpha}$, so the canonical BCS/harmonic scaling corresponds to $T_{c}\\propto m^{-1/2}$.

extra.notations:
- BCS: Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer theory of phonon-mediated superconductivity
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (K)
- $m$: isotopic mass
- $\\alpha$: isotope coefficient; canonical BCS/harmonic value $\\alpha=0.5$

title: Meissner effect in magnesium diboride (MgB2)
content: Magnesium diboride (MgB2) undergoes a superconducting transition at a critical temperature $T_{c}$ and, below $T_{c}$, exhibits magnetic flux expulsion (the Meissner effect).

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent mixed a general methodological/epistemic claim ("observation of the Meissner effect in magnetization measurements constitutes experimental evidence ...") and measurement-protocol details (e.g., characteristic drops in $M$ or $\chi$ under FC) with a factual statement about MgB2. The child (Node 1583) asserts that MgB2 is superconducting and exhibits zero resistance and magnetic flux expulsion below $T_{c}$, but it does not make the methodological claim about what observational signatures "constitute evidence" nor mention magnetization-measurement signatures or the FC protocol. To ensure the parent is entailed by the child, I removed all methodological and measurement-specific language and kept only the physical fact common to both nodes: that MgB2 becomes superconducting and shows the Meissner effect below its critical temperature. This rewritten parent is self-contained, substantive, and is directly entailed by Child 1583.

extra.notations:
- MgB2: magnesium diboride, a layered binary compound.
      - $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (the temperature below which superconducting properties appear).
      - Meissner effect: expulsion of magnetic flux from the bulk of a material upon entering the superconducting state.

title: MgB2 is a superconductor exhibiting the Meissner effect
content: Magnesium diboride (MgB2) is a superconductor that undergoes a superconducting phase transition at a critical temperature $T_{c}$; below $T_{c}$ the superconducting state is accompanied by magnetic flux expulsion (the Meissner effect).

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent combined several concrete claims from different children (a numeric $T_{c}=39\\,$K, an explicit statement of zero electrical resistance, and the Meissner effect). Verification showed a union error: Child 683 supplies the empirical $T_{c}$ value (and by domain convention implies superconductivity), while Child 1331 describes the Meissner effect as the confirmation method for superconductivity in materials "such as MgB2" but does not assert the numeric $T_{c}$ or explicitly state zero resistance. To ensure the parent is entailed by every child individually I removed the unsupported numeric value and the explicit claim of zero electrical resistance, keeping only the intersection: that MgB2 is a superconductor with a superconducting transition at a critical temperature $T_{c}$ and that the superconducting state is associated with magnetic flux expulsion (the Meissner effect). This revised proposition contains only claims supported by both children (Child 683 via its explicit $T_{c}$ statement and the domain convention that associates $T_{c}$ with superconducting phenomenology; Child 1331 via its statement that the Meissner effect accompanies/confirms the superconducting phase in materials like MgB2), is self-contained, and avoids the prior union error.

extra.notations:
- MgB2: magnesium diboride
      - $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (the temperature of the superconducting phase transition)
      - Meissner effect: magnetic flux expulsion from the bulk upon entering the superconducting state

title: Common scaling form of absorptive polarizability in Cu–O normal state
content:
In the normal (non-superconducting) state of copper–oxide high-temperature superconductors, there exist collective charge-density and spin-density excitations whose absorptive polarizability Im\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega) (the imaginary part of the contribution to the electronic polarizability from these density excitations, as a function of momentum \mathbf{q} and frequency \omega) has the universal, temperature-dependent scaling form
        $$\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim\begin{cases}
        -N(0)\,(\omega/T), & \text{for }|\omega|<T,\\[4pt]
        -N(0)\,\mathrm{sgn}(\omega), & \text{for }|\omega|>T,
        \end{cases}$$
        where $N(0)$ denotes the (unrenormalized) one-particle density of states at the Fermi level and $T$ is the absolute temperature. This statement makes no additional claims about specific theoretical embedding (e.g., marginal Fermi liquid) or about explaining particular experimental anomalies; it records only the shared empirical/theoretical scaling form of Im\tilde{P} that both propositions assert.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary (absorptive) part of the polarizability contribution from charge- and spin-density excitations, as a function of momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$.
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level (per spin, as used in the cited phenomenology).
- $T$: absolute temperature (energy units such that $k_B=1$ implicitly in the scaling statement).
- $\mathrm{sgn}(\omega)$: sign function of $\omega$ (equal to $+1$ for $\omega>0$, $-1$ for $\omega<0$).

title: Common frequency dependence and comparable magnitude of charge and spin contributions to Im \tilde{P}
content: In the metallic (normal) state of copper–oxide high-temperature superconductors, the charge-density and spin-density components of the absorptive polarizability $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ share the same functional dependence on frequency $\omega$ (at given momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and temperature) and have comparable magnitudes.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted a specific two-regime, temperature-dependent scaling law and an explicit proportionality to $N(0)$; the verifier flagged that the sole child (Node 914) does not state those concrete formulae or normalization. Node 914 only supports the weaker, shared claim that a proposed form of $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}$ that fits experiments requires charge and spin contributions to have the same functional form and similar magnitudes. The rewritten parent removes the unsupported piecewise scaling and the $N(0)$ proportionality and keeps only the intersectional, substantive claim that is directly entailed by the child.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary (absorptive) part of the polarizability contribution from charge- and spin-density excitations.
      - $\mathbf{q}$: momentum vector.
      - $\omega$: frequency.

title: Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8: body-centered tetragonal symmetry
content: Thallium-barium-calcium-copper oxide with nominal composition Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (the '2212' phase) crystallizes in a body-centered tetragonal lattice belonging to space group I4/mmm (International Tables for Crystallography space group no. 139). This statement asserts the crystal symmetry and space-group assignment for the compound without specifying unit-cell dimensions or local coordination details.

extra.notations:
- Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8: chemical formula for the thallium-barium-calcium-copper oxide '2212' superconducting phase.
- I4/mmm: International space-group symbol for a body-centered tetragonal space group (space-group number 139).

title: Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8: body-centered tetragonal symmetry
content: Thallium-barium-calcium-copper oxide with nominal composition Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (the '2212' phase) crystallizes in a body-centered tetragonal lattice belonging to space group I4/mmm (International Tables for Crystallography space group no. 139). This statement asserts the crystal symmetry and space-group assignment for the compound without committing to specific lattice parameter values or local coordination geometries.

extra.notations:
- Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8: chemical formula for the thallium-barium-calcium-copper oxide '2212' superconducting phase.
- I4/mmm: International space-group symbol for a body-centered tetragonal space group (space-group number 139).

title: Crystalline structure of MgB2 (common content)
content: Magnesium diboride (chemical formula MgB2) crystallizes in a hexagonal unit cell with space group P6/mmm (Hermann–Mauguin symbol for the hexagonal symmetry) and lattice constants a = 3.086 Å (basal-plane lattice parameter) and c = 3.524 Å (axis perpendicular to the basal plane). The crystal structure consists of alternating planar layers: planar boron layers with a honeycomb (graphite-like) topology in which each boron atom is bonded to three nearest boron neighbors at equal distances, and layers of magnesium atoms interleaved between those boron layers along the c-axis.

extra.notations:
- MgB2: magnesium diboride (chemical formula MgB2). 
- P6/mmm: Hermann–Mauguin symbol for the relevant hexagonal space group (number 191). 
- a: basal-plane lattice constant (in ångströms, Å). 
- c: lattice constant along the hexagonal axis (in Å).

title: Marginal Fermi liquid: low-energy scale and coupling to broad charge and spin fluctuations
content:
The marginal Fermi liquid (MFL) phenomenology for a metallic system posits that low-energy electron dynamics are controlled by the energy scale
$x=\max(|\omega|,T)$, where $\omega$ is frequency and $T$ the absolute temperature, and that electrons are coupled to broad (weakly momentum-dependent) charge- and spin-density fluctuations.
This coupling produces anomalous, non-Fermi-liquid single-particle behavior: the retarded one-particle self-energy acquires a singular dependence on frequency (stronger than the quadratic Fermi-liquid form) and quasiparticle coherence is suppressed at low energies, i.e., the quasiparticle weight is reduced as the excitation energy approaches zero.

extra.notations:
- $x$: energy scale defined by $x=\max(|\omega|,T)$, with $\omega$ frequency and $T$ temperature.
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized single-particle density of states at the Fermi level (used in MFL prefactors).
- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: contribution of charge and spin density excitations to the polarizability; in MFL these fluctuations are broadly distributed in momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and controlled by $x$.
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy; in MFL it shows anomalous (non-quadratic) frequency dependence and leads to reduced quasiparticle weight as energy $\to 0$.

title: Superconductivity of H3S-like hydrogen–sulfur compounds near 10^2 GPa
content: Hydrogen–sulfur compounds with stoichiometry close to H3S become metallic and exhibit superconductivity with critical temperatures on the order of $10^{2}\,$K (roughly 150–205\,K) when compressed to pressures of order $100$–$200\,$GPa.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted broad macroscopic claims (pressure-driven metallization and observed superconductivity near 10^2 GPa for H2S, related hydrides, and elemental sulfur) that are not supported by the single child (Node 1529). Verification flagged union errors: the child only establishes a microscopic, mechanism-level claim for Im-3m/R3m H3S-like phases (dominance of high-frequency hydrogen phonons in the electron–phonon coupling). To ensure every child entails the parent, I removed all unsupported, broader claims about metallization, $T_{c}$ at ~10^2 GPa, H2S generally, and elemental sulfur. The revised parent is a self-contained, substantive physical claim that matches exactly the content asserted by the child and omits the overgeneralizations.

The original parent asserted superconductivity and metallization for both hydrogen-derived sulfur hydrides and elemental sulfur at pressures of order $10^{2}$\,GPa. The single child (Node 1530) supports only the claim about H3S-like hydrogen–sulfur compounds and says nothing about elemental sulfur, producing a union error and a misclassification (subsumption vs. partial overlap). I removed the unsupported claim about elemental sulfur and restricted the parent to the intersection: H3S-like hydrogen–sulfur compounds become metallic and superconducting near 100–200\,GPa with $T_{c}$ on the order of $10^{2}$\,K. The revised parent is self-contained, substantive, and is entailed by the child. This fixes the union error and tightness problem while preserving the scientifically meaningful core claim.

extra.notations:
- $P$: applied pressure, in gigapascals (GPa)
      - $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature, in kelvin (K)
      - H3S-like: hydrogen–sulfur stoichiometries close to H3S (e.g., formulations described as (H2S)2H2 or H3S)

title: Dependence of inter-stripe CDW coupling on transverse spacing and arc-length differences
content:
Consider a two-dimensional array of parallel metallic stripes (e.g., stripes in a doped
Mott insulator) labeled by an integer index j. Let Y_j(x) denote the stripe's transverse
displacement in the direction perpendicular to the stripe at longitudinal coordinate x,
and let L_j(x) denote the arc length measured along stripe j from a fixed origin to x.
Define the nearest-neighbor transverse separation Δ_j Y(x) ≡ Y_{j+1}(x) − Y_j(x) and the
arc-length difference Δ_j L(x) ≡ L_{j+1}(x) − L_j(x). The interaction that couples CDW
order on stripe j to stripe j+1 depends on these local geometrical differences via a
coupling function V(Δ_j Y), i.e., the strength (or prefactor) of the inter-stripe CDW
interaction is a function of the local transverse spacing Δ_j Y and depends parametrically
on the arc-length mismatch Δ_j L. In other words, inter-stripe CDW coupling is not a
purely local constant but is controlled by the geometric variables Δ_j Y(x) and Δ_j L(x).

extra.notations:
- Δ_j Y(x): = Y_{j+1}(x) − Y_j(x), the transverse spacing between stripe j and j+1 at longitudinal position x
- Δ_j L(x): = L_{j+1}(x) − L_j(x), the difference in arc length measured along adjacent stripes from a common origin to x
- V(Δ_j Y): coupling function; a position-dependent prefactor that sets the strength of the inter-stripe CDW interaction as a function of the local transverse separation
- CDW: charge-density-wave order on an individual metallic stripe

title: Q-dependent magnetic scattering separable from nuclear background in YBa2Cu3O6.92
content: In YBa2Cu3O6.92, the magnetic component of neutron scattering varies with momentum transfer along the antiferromagnetic ridge at wave vectors of the form $(1/2,1/2,l)$ (r.l.u.) and can be empirically distinguished from the more slowly varying nuclear scattering background by performing momentum-space (Q) scans across that ridge.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent conjoined two claims supported by different children: (A) that Q-scans across the $(1/2,1/2,l)$ ridge allow empirical isolation of the nuclear background (supported by Child 1444) and (B) that the magnetic signal appears as an energy-localized resonance with a reduced q-width and a (001) intensity modulation (specifics from Child 1450). This produced a union error because no single child entailed the full conjunction. I removed the specific spectral and numeric details (energy-localized peak, q-width values, explicit (001) modulation) and retained only the intersection supported by both children: that the magnetic scattering is Q-dependent along the $(1/2,1/2,l)$ rod and can be separated from the nuclear background via Q-scans. The revised parent is therefore entailed by each child individually and is self-contained and substantive.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: yttrium barium copper oxide with oxygen content $x=0.92$.
      - Q: momentum-transfer (scattering) vector in reciprocal space.
      - (1/2,1/2,l): antiferromagnetic ridge in reciprocal lattice units (r.l.u.).

title: Nuclear background in YBa2Cu3O6+x neutron scattering
content: In YBa2Cu3O6+x (e.g., $x\approx 0.92$), inelastic neutron scattering contains a measurable nuclear (non-magnetic) contribution to the energy-dependent scattering intensity that is distinct from magnetic spin-excitation scattering.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted additional interpretive claims (a specific three-component energy decomposition, an energy-independent background term, and a procedural Q-dependent method for separating nuclear and magnetic signals) that are not supported by Child 1578 alone. Child 1578 only establishes (i) measurable inelastic intensity in the $\sim$20–30 meV window, (ii) a broad, ungapped nuclear-like feature near ≈21.5 meV, and (iii) that magnetic weight lies mainly at ≈28 meV and above. I removed the unsupported modeling and Q-dependent empirical-method assertions and kept only the factual spectral observations that are entailed by the child. The revised parent is therefore fully entailed by Child 1578 and contains only claims in the intersection.

The original parent merged two distinct claims from different children (a Q-dependent separation method from Node 1444 and a specific three-component energy model from Node 1467). That union failed verification because no single child entailed both claims. To fix this, I retained only the intersection that every child supports: that neutron inelastic scattering in YBa2Cu3O6+x includes a nuclear (non-magnetic) contribution to the measured, energy-dependent intensity which is distinct from magnetic spin excitations. I removed the unsupported-by-all specifics (the three-component spectral decomposition and the claim that those components were characterized specifically via Q-scans along the $(1/2,1/2,l)$ rod). The revised parent is self-contained, factual, and entailed by each child individually.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6+x: yttrium barium copper oxide with oxygen content per formula unit 6+x.
      - $\hbar\omega$: energy transfer in inelastic scattering ($\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant).
      - nuclear (non-magnetic) contribution: component of the inelastic scattering intensity not arising from spin excitations.

title: Presence of an energy-localized neutron-scattering peak in YBa2Cu3O6.92
content: In YBa2Cu3O6.92, inelastic neutron scattering measurements within the studied energy range exhibit at least one energy-localized (spectrally narrow) peak in the dynamical response.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted additional specific claims (a spectrally narrow discrete resonance, momentum-space sharpness of magnetic features, and a broad background extending across the entire investigated range) that are not supported by child 1578 alone. Child 1578 only establishes measurable scattering in the ≈20–30 meV window and reports two distinct contributions: a broad nuclear-like component near ≈21.5 meV and magnetic spectral weight concentrated near ≈28 meV. The revised parent removes claims about spectral narrowness, momentum sharpness, and full-range background extent, restricting the proposition to the observations that every child (here node 1578) actually entails. This yields a self-contained, substantive statement that is entailed by the child.

The original parent combined two claims that were supported by different children (a resonance-like, spectrally narrow magnetic peak only reported in Node 1450, and broader/nuclear background components only reported in Node 1467), producing a union-error. To ensure the parent is entailed by every child, I removed any claim about broad or nuclear-related background components and retained only the intersection: both children report at least one energy-localized spectral peak in the measured neutron-scattering response (Node 1450 reports a narrow magnetic peak near 41 meV; Node 1467 reports a peaked nuclear contribution near ≈20 meV). The revised parent is concise, self-contained, and is individually entailed by each child.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: yttrium barium copper oxide with oxygen content per formula unit 6.92
      - $\hbar\omega$: energy transfer in inelastic scattering ($\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant, $\omega$ the angular frequency)
      - "Energy-localized (spectrally narrow) peak": a pronounced feature in energy transfer whose width is small compared to the overall investigated energy window

title: Fluorine-doped LaFeAsO superconducts near 26 K
content: The fluorine-substituted lanthanum iron oxyarsenide LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs — where La is lanthanum, O is oxygen, F is fluorine, Fe is iron, As is arsenic, and x denotes the fraction of oxygen sites replaced by fluorine — is a layered iron-based compound that becomes superconducting upon fluorine doping; experimental measurements report a superconducting critical temperature T_c of approximately 26 kelvin under the reported doping conditions.

extra.notations:
- LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs: lanthanum iron oxyarsenide with fluorine substituting oxygen at fraction x. 
- T_c: superconducting critical temperature (in kelvin).
- "Approximately 26 K": indicates experimental transition temperatures reported near 26 K (order-one uncertainty in the last digit).

title: Experimental superconductivity in F-doped LaFeAsO around 26 K
content: Fluorine-doped lanthanum iron oxyarsenide LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs (with Fe forming square-lattice layers) is an experimentally observed superconductor; measurements report superconducting transition temperatures on the order of 26 kelvin (experimental values reported at or near 26 K under the dopings studied).

extra.notations:
- LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs: lanthanum iron oxyarsenide with fluorine substitution fraction x on oxygen sites.
- Fe^{2+} square-lattice sheets: the iron atoms form approximately square planar layers in the crystal structure (common descriptor of the conducting planes in these compounds).
- T_c: superconducting critical temperature (kelvin).

title: fcc LaH10-like phase at megabar pressures
content: At pressures of order 150–170 gigapascals (GPa), a lanthanum hydride phase adopts a face-centered cubic crystal structure with space group Fm-3m (notation $Fm\\overline{3}m$). The formal stoichiometry of this cubic phase is close to LaH10 (i.e., approximately ten hydrogen atoms per lanthanum). Experimental structure refinements and theoretical calculations consistently identify this fcc phase as the cubic metallic LaH10-type phase (the observed lattice symmetry and volumetric behavior are consistent with the LaH10 structural model).

extra.notations:
- $LaH_{10}$: lanthanum decahydride, the nominal composition with ten H atoms per La. 
- $Fm\\overline{3}m$: crystallographic space group symbol for a face-centered cubic (fcc) lattice.
- GPa: gigapascal, a unit of pressure (1 GPa = 10^9 pascals).

title: SO(5) superspin identification of superconducting and antiferromagnetic order
content: In the SO(5) unification framework, define a five-component superspin vector n_a = (n_1,n_2,n_3,n_4,n_5). This superspin simultaneously represents the d-wave superconducting and antiferromagnetic order parameters: the components n_1 and n_5 are the two real degrees of freedom of the d-wave superconducting order (they correspond to the real and imaginary parts of the superconducting order parameter Δ), while the components n_2, n_3, n_4 are the three components of the antiferromagnetic (staggered spin-density) order parameter. Concretely, the superconducting components are the two real components of the complex d-wave order parameter (so that n_1 = "real(Δ)" and n_5 = "imag(Δ)"), and n_2–n_4 encode the three spin components of the AF order. This identification is the common scientific content shared by the listed propositions (independent of whether a particular text supplies a microscopic fermionic sum for Δ, an AF ordering wavevector, or additional SO(5) algebra elements).

extra.notations:
- n_a: five-component "superspin" vector whose components label order-parameter degrees of freedom (a = 1,...,5). 
- Δ: complex d-wave superconducting order parameter (the two real components of Δ correspond to n_1 and n_5). 
- n_1 = real(Δ), n_5 = imag(Δ): the two real SC components (often written as $n_1=\Delta^\dagger+\Delta$, $n_5=-i(\Delta^\dagger-\Delta)$ in microscopic presentations). 
- n_2,n_3,n_4: the three spin components of the antiferromagnetic (staggered) order parameter. 
- "SO(5) unification": the theoretical framework that treats the five components as a unified order-parameter vector.

title: Im-3m H3S as a ~200 K superconductor under high pressure
content: Under high static pressure (gigapascal-scale), a crystalline phase of sulfur trihydride (chemical formula H$_3$S) with body-centered-cubic Im-3m symmetry exists and is superconducting with a superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ close to 200 K. Here H$_3$S denotes sulfur trihydride (three hydrogen atoms per sulfur atom), Im-3m denotes the body-centered-cubic space-group symmetry, "high pressure" refers to pressures in the gigapascal range relevant to high-pressure synthesis and measurements, and $T_{c}$ is the superconducting critical temperature at which zero-resistance superconductivity onsets.

extra.notations:
- H$_3$S: sulfur trihydride (stoichiometry three H atoms per S atom). 
- Im-3m: body-centered-cubic space-group designation (crystal symmetry).
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (kelvin).
- P: pressure (gigapascal, GPa).

title: Ba–Fe–As compound crystallizes in ThCr2Si2 (I4/mmm)
content: A representative compound from the barium–iron–arsenic (Ba–Fe–As) family crystallizes in the ThCr2Si2-type crystal structure, i.e., a body-centered tetragonal structure described by the space group I4/mmm (International Tables for Crystallography No. 139). This claim asserts only the crystallographic assignment (structure type and space group) for a Ba–Fe–As composition and does not specify lattice parameters, stoichiometric variations, or the number of formula units per cell.

extra.notations:
- ThCr2Si2: A conventional name for a tetragonal structure type characteristic of many AT2X2 compounds (A = large cation, T = transition metal, X = p-block element).
- I4/mmm: Hermann–Mauguin symbol for space group No. 139, a body-centered tetragonal space group.
- "Ba–Fe–As family": Compounds whose composition is built from barium (Ba), iron (Fe) and arsenic (As), including stoichiometric BaFe2As2 and low-level doped variants such as BaFe1.8Co0.2As2.

title: Phase stiffness and short-distance cutoff in quasi-2D superconductors
content: In quasi-two-dimensional superconductors, the zero-temperature phase stiffness V0 is the characteristic energy scale for long-wavelength superconducting phase fluctuations. When computing V0 one introduces a short-distance spatial cutoff a chosen as the larger of the inter-layer spacing d and a length of order the coherence length perpendicular to the layers, $\xi_{\perp}$ (different sources may differ by an order-unity numerical factor in how the $\xi_{\perp}$-based length is defined).

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent mixed two claims: (A) a prescription that the cutoff a is the larger of d and a length set by $\xi_{\perp}$, which is supported by both children, and (B) an explicit formula expressing $V_{0}$ in terms of $\lambda(0)$, which only Child 122 provides. Child 104 does not state the quantitative relation to $\lambda(0)$, so the parent as written committed a union error. To ensure every child entails the parent, I removed the unsupported explicit formula and kept only the intersection: that V0 is the zero-temperature phase-stiffness energy scale and that the short-distance cutoff a is taken as the larger of d and a length of order $\xi_{\perp}$ (allowing order-unity convention differences). The revised proposition is fully self-contained and is entailed by both Child 104 and Child 122.

extra.notations:
- V0: zero-temperature phase stiffness (energy scale for long-wavelength phase fluctuations)
      - a: short-distance spatial cutoff chosen for computing V0
      - d: average spacing between superconducting layers
      - $\xi_{\perp}$: superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers

title: Zero‑temperature phase stiffness as the energy scale for phase fluctuations
content: The zero-temperature phase stiffness $V_{0}$ is the characteristic energy scale associated with superconducting phase fluctuations.

refine_history.reasoning: Verification showed a union error: the original parent combined two claims that occur separately in the children (Child 126 gives a microscopic definition/formula for $V_{0}$, while Child 111 asserts that phase‑ordering temperatures scale as $T_{\theta}^{max}=A\,V_{0}$). No single child entails both claims. To ensure every child individually entails the parent, I removed the temperature‑scale and the explicit microscopic‑formula claims and retained only the intersection supported by both children: that $V_{0}$ is the zero‑temperature phase‑stiffness energy scale associated with superconducting phase fluctuations. This revised, self‑contained proposition is entailed by Child 126 (which defines $V_{0}$ as the characteristic energy scale for phase fluctuations) and by Child 111 (which treats $V_{0}$ as the phase‑stiffness energy scale).

extra.notations: - $V_{0}$: zero-temperature phase stiffness (energy scale for phase fluctuations)

title: Phase-ordering and mean-field temperatures are upper bounds; strong phase fluctuations suppress Tc
content: Define $T_{c}$ as the actual superconducting transition temperature, $T^{MF}$ as the mean-field transition temperature (the temperature scale where local pairing is established in mean-field theory), and $T_{\theta}^{max}$ as the maximal phase-ordering temperature obtained when one ignores disordering effects of other degrees of freedom. For a superconducting system, both $T_{\theta}^{max}$ and $T^{MF}$ act as upper bounds on the observed transition temperature $T_{c}$ (i.e., $T_{c}\leq \min\{T_{\theta}^{max},T^{MF}\}$) because effects omitted when defining these scales (such as phase disordering and other thermally- or quantum-active non-phase degrees of freedom) can reduce the actual ordering temperature. In regimes where phase fluctuations are strong — specifically when the phase-ordering scale is comparable to the observed $T_{c}$ — phase fluctuations can be the dominant mechanism that depresses $T_{c}$ significantly below the mean-field value $T^{MF}$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (defined above)
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field transition temperature (temperature scale for local pairing in mean-field theory)
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: maximum phase-ordering temperature computed while ignoring certain disordering effects (a theoretical upper bound)

title: In low hole-doping cuprates strong phase fluctuations suppress Tc below mean-field
content: In cuprate superconductors at low hole concentration (the underdoped regime), strong thermodynamic and/or quantum phase fluctuations dominate the determination of the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$. As a result, the actual $T_{c}$ can be substantially lower than the mean-field transition temperature $T^{MF}$ (i.e., mean-field theory fails to predict the observed $T_{c}$ in this low-doping regime). Here $T^{MF}$ denotes the temperature scale for local pairing obtained from mean-field treatments, and "strong phase fluctuations" means that the phase-ordering energy scale is comparable to the observed $T_{c}$ so that phase disordering controls the transition.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field transition temperature (local-pairing scale in mean-field theory)
- "Underdoped"/"low hole concentration": doping regime of the CuO$_2$ planes with hole concentration much smaller than optimal

title: Phase-fluctuation omission makes $T_{\theta}^{max}$ and $T^{MF}$ upper bounds on $T_{c}$
content: The theoretical phase-ordering temperature $T_{\theta}^{max}$ and the mean-field transition temperature $T^{MF}$ both serve as theoretical upper bounds on the observed superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ because these theoretical scales omit phase-fluctuation effects that can reduce the actual ordering temperature.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent combined two claims that were not both supported by every child: (a) an explicit conditional that when $T_{\theta}^{max}/T_{c}\sim 1$ phase fluctuations dominate (present in Child 113 but not in Child 128), and (b) the omission of "other temperature-dependent non-phase degrees of freedom" (present in Child 128 but not in Child 113). This produced a union error. To ensure the parent is entailed by every child, I removed the conditional and the additional non-phase-degree wording and retained only the intersectional claim supported by both children: that $T_{\theta}^{max}$ and $T^{MF}$ are theoretical upper bounds on $T_{c}$ because they neglect phase-fluctuation effects. This rewritten proposition is self-contained, factual, and is entailed individually by both Child 113 and Child 128.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature
      - $T^{MF}$: mean-field transition temperature (local-pairing scale in mean-field theory)
      - $T_{\theta}^{max}$: theoretical maximum phase-ordering temperature

title: Regimes where phase fluctuations dominate cause mean-field failure for Tc
content: Define $T_{c}$ as the observed superconducting transition temperature, $T^{MF}$ as the mean-field transition temperature, and $T_{\theta}^{max}$ as the phase-ordering temperature. In regimes where the phase-ordering scale is comparable to the observed $T_{c}$ (for example, low carrier-doping regimes in unconventional superconductors), thermodynamic and/or quantum phase fluctuations can dominate the physics controlling $T_{c}$; consequently the observed $T_{c}$ can be substantially lower than $T^{MF}$ and mean-field theory fails to account for the transition temperature.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field transition temperature
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: phase-ordering temperature (upper bound on $T_{c}$)

title: Phase fluctuations can suppress the observed superconducting Tc below mean-field estimates
content: Strong fluctuations of the superconducting phase can reduce the observed superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ below mean-field estimates, causing mean-field descriptions of the transition to fail in regimes where phase fluctuations are dominant.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent conflated two claims supported by different children: (a) that specific mean-field scales such as $T^{MF}$ or $T^{max}_\theta$ act as upper bounds when fluctuations are neglected (from Child 128), and (b) that in low-doping or similar regimes phase fluctuations dominate and cause mean-field theory to fail (from Child 1420). That union made the parent stronger than what either child individually entails. I removed the child-specific details (explicit mention of $T^{max}_\theta$, the low-doping example, and the thermal/quantum qualifier) and kept only the intersection: both children support the general claim that strong phase fluctuations can suppress the observed $T_c$ below mean-field estimates and thereby invalidate mean-field descriptions when fluctuations dominate. The revised parent is self-contained and entailed by each child individually.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature
      - $T^{MF}$: mean-field transition temperature / mean-field estimate of $T_{c}$

title: CaH2 topotactic reduction converts Nd-containing perovskite nickelates to infinite-layer NiO2-based phases
content: Low-temperature topotactic reduction of Nd-containing perovskite nickelates (where the precursor chemical formula is of the form Nd1−xSrxNiO3 or NdNiO3) by treatment with calcium hydride (CaH2) powder induces oxygen deintercalation (removal of lattice oxygen, including apical oxygens) and converts the perovskite structure into the infinite-layer NiO2-based structure (chemical formula Nd1−xSrxNiO2 or NdNiO2). This transformation is accomplished under soft-chemistry, low-temperature conditions and yields an infinite-layer phase characterized by NiO2 planes and the absence of the perovskite apical oxygen coordination.

extra.notations:
- $Nd_{1-x}Sr_{x}NiO_{3}$ or $NdNiO_{3}$: perovskite precursor containing neodymium (possibly Sr-doped). 
- $Nd_{1-x}Sr_{x}NiO_{2}$ or $NdNiO_{2}$: resulting infinite-layer phase containing NiO2 planes and lacking apical oxygens.
- CaH$_2$: calcium hydride powder used as a low-temperature reducing/topotactic reagent that effects oxygen deintercalation.
- oxygen deintercalation: removal of oxygen atoms from the lattice (including apical oxygens) during the topochemical conversion from perovskite to infinite-layer structures.

title: Five-orbital in-plane tight-binding Hamiltonian for LaFeAsO (unfolded Brillouin zone)
content:
For the iron-pnictide compound LaFeAsO (and electron-doped variants LaFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}), the low-energy electronic structure of the Fe layer can be described by a two-dimensional, five-orbital tight-binding Hamiltonian on an unfolded (effective) square Brillouin zone. Written in real-space orbital language,
$$
H_{0}=\sum_{i,j}\sum_{\mu,\nu}\sum_{\sigma}\big[\,t(x_{i}-x_{j},y_{i}-y_{j};\mu,\nu)\,c^{\dagger}_{i\mu\sigma}c_{j\nu\sigma}+\text{H.c.}\,\big]
+\sum_{i,\mu,\sigma}\varepsilon_{\mu}\,n_{i\mu\sigma},
$$
where $c^{\dagger}_{i\mu\sigma}$ creates an electron with spin $\sigma$ in orbital $\mu$ at Fe site $i$, $n_{i\mu\sigma}=c^{\dagger}_{i\mu\sigma}c_{i\mu\sigma}$, $t(\Delta x,\Delta y;\mu,\nu)$ are orbital-resolved hopping integrals between sites displaced by $(\Delta x,\Delta y)$, and $\varepsilon_{\mu}$ are orbital-dependent on-site energies. The orbital index $\mu\in\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ denotes the five Fe $3d$-derived orbitals with the usual symmetry labels: $d_{3Z^{2}-R^{2}}$, $d_{XZ}$, $d_{YZ}$, $d_{X^{2}-Y^{2}}$, and $d_{XY}$. This Hamiltonian is the standard minimal lattice representation used to reproduce and analyze the Fe-layer band structure obtained from first-principles calculations.

extra.notations:
- $c^{\dagger}_{i\mu\sigma}$: creation operator for an electron of spin $\sigma$ in orbital $\mu$ at Fe site $i$.
- $n_{i\mu\sigma}=c^{\dagger}_{i\mu\sigma}c_{i\mu\sigma}$: number operator.
- $t(\Delta x,\Delta y;\mu,\nu)$: hopping integral between orbital $\mu$ at a reference site and orbital $\nu$ at a site displaced by $(\Delta x,\Delta y)$ on the effective square lattice.
- $\varepsilon_{\mu}$: orbital-dependent on-site energy for orbital $\mu$.
- Orbital index mapping: $\mu=1:d_{3Z^{2}-R^{2}},\;2:d_{XZ},\;3:d_{YZ},\;4:d_{X^{2}-Y^{2}},\;5:d_{XY}$.

title: Five-band (Fe 3d) description of LaFeAsO
content: The low-energy electronic description of LaFeAsO is built from five Fe 3d-derived electronic degrees of freedom: orbitals (or bands) corresponding to the symmetry labels $d_{3Z^{2}-R^{2}}$, $d_{XZ}$, $d_{YZ}$, $d_{X^{2}-Y^{2}}$, and $d_{XY}$. Minimal theoretical models of the Fe layer therefore employ a five-band (five-orbital) representation in which each Fe 3d symmetry furnishes one low-energy orbital/band relevant to the material's electronic structure near the Fermi level.

extra.notations:
- "Five-band": one electronic degree of freedom (orbital/band) per each Fe $3d$ symmetry listed above.
- The statement refers to low-energy (near-Fermi-level) electronic degrees of freedom relevant for LaFeAsO and related electron-doped compounds.

title: Charge-order period is half the spin-order period in La-based stripe phases
content: In hole-doped, layered lanthanum-based transition-metal oxides that exhibit stripe order (coexisting spatially modulated spin and charge densities in the transition-metal-oxide planes), the charge-density modulation ("charge stripes") has a spatial period equal to one half of the spin modulation period. Equivalently, if the magnetic (spin) modulation is characterized in reciprocal space by an incommensurability parameter $\varepsilon$ (measured as a shift from a structural Bragg point in reciprocal-lattice units), then the charge-order peaks appear at wave-vector shifts of magnitude $2\varepsilon$, indicating a charge modulation wavelength that is half the wavelength of the spin modulation.

extra.notations:
- $\varepsilon$: the magnetic incommensurability parameter — the magnitude (in reciprocal-lattice units) of the spin-order wave-vector shift from a nearby structural Bragg point. 
- "Charge stripes" / "charge-density modulation": a spatially periodic modulation of the electronic charge density in the transition-metal-oxide planes (e.g., $CuO_{2}$ or $NiO_{2}$ planes).
- "Stripe order": coexistence of spatially modulated spin density (spin stripes) and charge density (charge stripes) in the same layered oxide.
- "Reciprocal-space positions"/wave vectors: coordinates in reciprocal lattice units where superlattice (charge-order) peaks are observed; a charge peak at magnitude $2\varepsilon$ is twice the shift of the spin-order peak at $\varepsilon$.

title: SO(5) generators and π operators with d-wave form factor
content: In the SO(5) framework applied to cuprate high-temperature superconductors, the SO(5) Lie algebra has ten antisymmetric generators L_{ab} (indices a,b=1..5) satisfying the standard commutation relations of so(5). Among these ten generators there exist six operators denoted \pi_\alpha and \pi_\alpha^\dagger (with \alpha labeling the three spin components) that act to rotate antiferromagnetic order parameters into d-wave superconducting order parameters and vice versa. The microscopic description uses electron creation and annihilation operators c_{p\sigma}^\dagger,c_{p\sigma} with momentum p and spin \sigma, and employs the d-wave form factor g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y and the antiferromagnetic ordering vector Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi) in the operator constructions that relate the AF and SC sectors.

extra.notations:
- L_{ab}: antisymmetric generators of the SO(5) Lie algebra, a,b\in\{1,\dots,5\}
- \pi_\alpha,\pi_\alpha^\dagger: six SO(5) generators that rotate antiferromagnetic and d-wave superconducting order (index \alpha=\{x,y,z\})
- c_{p\sigma}^\dagger,c_{p\sigma}: electron creation/annihilation operators with momentum p and spin \sigma
- g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y: d-wave pairing form factor
- Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi): antiferromagnetic ordering vector

title: Common SO(5) content: π generators and d-wave form factor
content: For cuprate superconductors described in an SO(5) framework, the so(5) algebra consists of ten antisymmetric generators L_{ab}. Among them are six \pi_\alpha and \pi_\alpha^\dagger operators that serve to rotate antiferromagnetic order parameters into d-wave superconducting order parameters. The microscopic formulation involves electron creation and annihilation operators c_{p\sigma}^\dagger,c_{p\sigma}, the d-wave form factor g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y, and the antiferromagnetic ordering vector Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi); these ingredients appear in the operator-level constructions that relate the AF and SC sectors.

extra.notations:
- \pi_\alpha,\pi_\alpha^\dagger: SO(5) generators rotating AF and SC order (\alpha labels spin components)
- g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y: d-wave form factor
- Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi): antiferromagnetic ordering vector
- c_{p\sigma}^\dagger,c_{p\sigma}: electron creation/annihilation operators

title: Shared SO(5) statement: π operators and d-wave form factor
content: Within the SO(5) description of cuprate/high-T_c systems, the algebra includes six \pi_\alpha and \pi_\alpha^\dagger generators that implement rotations between antiferromagnetic and d-wave superconducting order parameters. The microscopic language uses electron operators c_{p\sigma}^\dagger,c_{p\sigma}, the d-wave form factor g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y, and the antiferromagnetic ordering vector Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi) in constructing operators that link the AF and SC sectors.

extra.notations:
- \pi_\alpha,\pi_\alpha^\dagger: generators rotating AF and d-wave SC order
- g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y: d-wave form factor
- Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi): antiferromagnetic ordering vector
- c_{p\sigma}^\dagger,c_{p\sigma}: electron creation/annihilation operators

title: Intersection: SO(5) π generators and d-wave microscopic ingredients
content: In the SO(5) description relevant to cuprates, the algebra contains six \pi_\alpha and \pi_\alpha^\dagger operators that rotate antiferromagnetic order into d-wave superconducting order. The microscopic representation uses electron creation/annihilation operators c_{p\sigma}^\dagger,c_{p\sigma}, the d-wave form factor g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y, and the antiferromagnetic ordering vector Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi) when relating AF and SC sectors.

extra.notations:
- \pi_\alpha,\pi_\alpha^\dagger: SO(5) generators rotating AF and SC order
- g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y: d-wave form factor
- Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi): antiferromagnetic ordering vector
- c_{p\sigma}^\dagger,c_{p\sigma}: electron creation/annihilation operators

title: In situ X-ray diffraction identifies fcc La lattice consistent with cubic LaH$_{10}$
content: In situ X-ray diffraction measurements on a lanthanum superhydride sample synthesized at megabar pressures (order 10^2 GPa) and high temperature (of order 10^3 K) show that the lanthanum (La) atoms occupy a face-centered cubic (fcc) lattice. These diffraction results are consistent with the theoretically predicted cubic metallic LaH$_{10}$ structure (lanthanum decahydride or LaH$_{10\pm x}$, i.e., a lanthanum-rich superhydride with approximately ten hydrogen atoms per lanthanum). In other words, X-ray structural data identify a cubic fcc La sublattice in the synthesized superhydride that matches the expected cubic LaH$_{10}$ phase.

extra.notations:
- La: lanthanum (chemical element)
- LaH$_{10}$: lanthanum superhydride with approximately ten hydrogen atoms per lanthanum (lanthanum decahydride)
- LaH$_{10\pm x}$: lanthanum superhydride with small stoichiometric deviations in the hydrogen content
- fcc: face-centered cubic crystal lattice (each cubic cell has lattice points at the cube corners and at the centers of all faces)
- in situ X-ray diffraction: X-ray diffraction measurements performed on the sample under the same high-pressure, high-temperature conditions used for synthesis
- GPa: gigapascal, unit of pressure (10^9 pascals)
- K: kelvin, unit of temperature

title: d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} pairing implies a π phase shift between orthogonal crystal directions
content: In a crystalline superconductor, denote by Δ(k_x,k_y) the complex superconducting order parameter as a function of in-plane wavevector components k_x and k_y, and denote by δ_{ab} the intrinsic phase difference between the order-parameter values sampled along two orthogonal crystallographic directions labeled a and b. The pairing symmetry called d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} is characterized by a sign change of the order parameter under a 90° rotation that interchanges the a and b axes; as a consequence of this sign change, the order-parameter phases sampled along the a and b directions differ by π, i.e., δ_{ab} = π. This relation (d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} ↔ δ_{ab}=π) is a general property of the d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} pairing symmetry and underlies phase-sensitive experimental tests that detect a half-flux-quantum (Φ0/2) phase offset between orthogonal crystal faces.

extra.notations:
- Δ(k_x,k_y): superconducting order parameter as a function of in-plane wavevector components k_x and k_y.
- δ_{ab}: intrinsic phase difference between order-parameter values sampled along orthogonal crystallographic directions labeled a and b.
- d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} pairing symmetry: a gap symmetry that changes sign between orthogonal directions in the crystal plane (equivalently, under a 90° rotation).
- Φ0: superconducting flux quantum, $h/2e$, referenced here only to indicate how a π phase difference manifests in phase-sensitive flux measurements (half-flux quantum offsets).

title: MgB2 specific-heat signatures of two superconducting gaps
content: In magnesium diboride (MgB2), measurements of the electronic specific heat C(T) reveal thermodynamic features incompatible with a single isotropic BCS gap: (i) an excess in the electronic specific heat at intermediate reduced temperatures (around T ≈ T_c/4), and (ii) an exponential temperature dependence at low temperature consistent with a smaller-than-BCS superconducting energy gap. These observations are jointly accounted for by a two-gap superconductivity picture in which there exist two distinct superconducting gap magnitudes, denoted Δ1 and Δ2, associated with different sheets of the Fermi surface; the presence of two gap scales explains why one observes both a large specific-heat contribution near T_c and a separate small-gap-like low-temperature exponential behavior.

extra.notations:
- MgB2: Magnesium diboride, the superconducting compound under discussion.
- T: absolute temperature; T_c: superconducting critical temperature.
- t: reduced temperature, t = T/T_c.
- C(T): electronic specific heat as a function of temperature.
- Δ1, Δ2: two distinct superconducting energy gaps (gap magnitudes) present in MgB2, associated with different Fermi-surface sheets.
- "exponential low-temperature behavior": C(T) ∝ exp(−Δ_small/k_B T) form indicating thermally activated quasiparticle excitations governed by the smaller gap.

title: Superconductivity in mercury-based cuprates
content: Compounds in the mercury–barium–(calcium)–copper–oxide family (the mercury‑based cuprate superconductors, e.g., compounds with formulas of the type HgBa2CuO4+δ, HgBa2CaCu2O6+x, HgBa2Ca2Cu3O8+x) are superconductors: each such compound exhibits a superconducting transition at a characteristic critical temperature Tc, where Tc denotes the superconducting transition temperature determined from low‑temperature transport (onset of zero resistance) or magnetic measurements (onset of diamagnetism).

extra.notations:
- Tc: superconducting transition temperature (critical temperature) measured as the temperature at which a superconducting transition is observed by resistivity or magnetic susceptibility.
- Hg‑based cuprates / Hg–Ba–(Ca)–Cu–O family: mercury‑containing copper‑oxide compounds such as HgBa2CuO4+δ (commonly called Hg‑1201), HgBa2CaCu2O6+x (Hg‑1212), and HgBa2Ca2Cu3O8+x (Hg‑1223); δ and x denote non‑stoichiometric oxygen content.

title: Dependence of inter-stripe Josephson coupling on transverse spacing
content: In an array of parallel conducting stripes labeled by integer index $j$, let $Y_j(x)$ be the transverse displacement (in the $y$-direction) of stripe $j$ at longitudinal coordinate $x$, and define the local transverse spacing between neighboring stripes as $\Delta_j Y \equiv Y_{j+1}(x)-Y_j(x)$. There exists a Josephson tunneling matrix element $J$ describing Cooper-pair tunneling between superconducting order on adjacent stripes; this matrix element depends on the instantaneous local transverse spacing $\Delta_j Y$ and is parametrized by a constant amplitude $J_0$ and a decay parameter $\alpha$.
