In other words, $J$ is a function of $\Delta_j Y$ that can be written using parameters $J_0$ and $\alpha$, so that the inter-stripe Josephson coupling is controlled by the local transverse geometry of the stripe array.

extra.notations:
- $j$: integer index of a stripe in a parallel stripe array.
- $Y_j(x)$: transverse displacement (along $y$) of stripe $j$ at longitudinal coordinate $x$.
- $\Delta_j Y \equiv Y_{j+1}(x)-Y_j(x)$: local transverse spacing between neighboring stripes.
- $J(\Delta_j Y)$: Josephson tunneling matrix element for Cooper-pair transfer between stripe $j$ and $j+1$, viewed as a function of $\Delta_j Y$.
- $J_0$: constant prefactor (amplitude) parametrizing the Josephson coupling.
- $\alpha$: decay parameter that parametrizes how the coupling depends on transverse spacing.

title: m=3 Hg cuprate: onset Tc ≈164 K at ≈31 GPa
content: Under quasihydrostatic pressure conditions, the optimally doped mercury cuprate HgBa2Ca2Cu3O8+δ (chemical formula HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+\delta}, the $m=3$ member of the HgBa_{2}Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+\delta}$ series) exhibits an onset superconducting transition temperature $T_{co}\approx 164\ \text{K}$ at a pressure of approximately $31\ \text{GPa}$. This statement refers to the measured onset temperature of the superconducting transition under quasihydrostatic compression of an optimally doped sample.

extra.notations:
- $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+\delta}$: mercury-based cuprate with $m=3$ CuO2 layers per unit cell; $\delta$ denotes oxygen non-stoichiometry. 
- $T_{co}$: onset superconducting transition temperature (temperature where resistive onset of superconductivity is observed). 
- GPa: gigapascal, unit of pressure; "quasihydrostatic" indicates the applied pressure environment approximates hydrostatic conditions in a DAC (diamond anvil cell).

title: Some lead-substituted Hg-1223 compositions are optimally doped
content: Some lead-substituted mercury cuprate compositions with formula $Hg_{1-x}Pb_{x}Ba_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+\delta}$ are optimally doped, i.e., their oxygen content $\delta$ corresponds to the doping level that maximizes the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ for those compositions.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted that both $x=0.1$ and $x=0.2$ compositions were reported as optimally doped. Verification showed a union error: child 586 only supports optimal doping for $x=0.1$, child 587 only for $x=0.2$, while child 585 supports both. To satisfy the requirement that every child individually entails the parent, I removed the combined claim about both specific substitution levels and retained only the intersectional, substantive claim present in all children: that lead-substituted Hg$_{1-x}$Pb$_x$Ba$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+\delta}$ has compositions which are optimally doped. This revised parent is self-contained, factual (states the physical property of optimal doping), and is entailed by each child because each child reports at least one optimally doped composition of this formula.

extra.notations:
- $Hg_{1-x}Pb_{x}Ba_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+\delta}$: lead-substituted variant of the $m=3$ mercury cuprate; $x$ is the Pb substitution fraction on Hg sites.
      - "Optimally doped": oxygen stoichiometry adjusted to maximize the observed superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$.

title: Two-gap α-model decomposition of MgB₂ electronic specific heat
content:
For magnesium diboride (MgB$_2$) in zero magnetic field, the electronic contribution to the superconducting specific heat can be represented by a phenomenological two-band, two-gap model in which the total electronic specific heat $C_{\\mathrm{total}}$ is the sum of two independent band contributions,
$$C_{\\mathrm{total}} = C_1 + C_2,$$
where each band $i\\in\\{1,2\\}$ is characterized by a superconducting gap $\\Delta_i(T)$ and a partial normal-state Sommerfeld coefficient $\\gamma_i$. The partial coefficients satisfy $\\gamma_1 + \\gamma_2 = \\gamma_{n}$, with $\\gamma_n$ the total normal-state Sommerfeld constant. In this modeling approach each band contribution is computed from the quasiparticle entropy using the standard BCS/alpha‑model integrals (i.e., integrals over the quasiparticle energy with the Fermi function) with the band gap $\\Delta_i(T)$ taken as a temperature-scaled zero‑temperature gap $\\Delta_{i,0}\\,\\delta(t)$ and the gaps and band weights adjusted to fit the measured specific heat data of MgB$_2$.

extra.notations:
- MgB$_2$: magnesium diboride superconductor
- $C_{\\mathrm{total}}$: total electronic specific heat (zero field)
- $C_i$: electronic specific heat contribution from band $i$
- $\\gamma_n$: total normal-state Sommerfeld coefficient (electronic specific heat coefficient)
- $\\gamma_i$: partial Sommerfeld coefficient for band $i$, with $\\gamma_1+\\gamma_2=\\gamma_n$
- $\\Delta_i(T)$: superconducting gap on band $i$ at temperature $T$; $\\Delta_{i,0}=\\Delta_i(T=0)$
- $t$: reduced temperature $t=T/T_c$
- $\\delta(t)$: normalized BCS gap function, so $\\Delta_i(T)=\\Delta_{i,0}\\,\\delta(t)$ in the alpha‑model

title: Distinct LTT and LTO phases in La2CuO4-derived cuprates
content: La2CuO4-derived cuprate oxides (for example La2-xBaxCuO4 and La1.6-xNd0.4SrxCuO4) exhibit two distinct low-temperature structural variants: a low-temperature tetragonal (LTT) phase and a low-temperature orthorhombic (LTO) phase.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent conflated two claims supported by different children: Child 139 provides the mechanistic claim that the LTT structure pins stripe-like charge order while LTO does not, whereas Child 136 provides that the anomalous suppression of superconductivity near x≈1/8 is contingent on an LTO→LTT transition. No single child jointly supports both the stripe-pinning mechanism and its implication for the 1/8 anomaly, so the parent committed a union error. To ensure the parent is entailed by every child, I removed the mechanism-implication linkage and retained only the structural-fact that both LTT and LTO phases occur in La2CuO4-derived cuprates (a claim directly supported by both children). This yields a self-contained, factual, and jointly entailed proposition.

extra.notations:
- LTT: low-temperature tetragonal crystal structure.
      - LTO: low-temperature orthorhombic crystal structure.
      - x: hole concentration per Cu site (doping level).

title: Distinct effects of LTT and LTO structures on electronic order in cuprates
content: In cuprate (copper‑oxide) superconductors, the low‑temperature tetragonal (LTT) and low‑temperature orthorhombic (LTO) structural phases have different effects on electronic order in the CuO$_2$ planes; specifically, the two phases alter tendencies toward spatially modulated electronic states (such as charge or spin stripe order) and other electronic properties.

refine_history.reasoning:
The original parent merged two distinct claims from different children: a mechanistic claim (that the LTT lattice pins horizontal/vertical stripes while LTO does not) present in Node 147, and a phenomenological claim linking an LTO→LTT transition to the 1/8 superconductivity anomaly in specific La‑based compounds present in Node 136. No single child supported both claims, producing a union error.

To ensure every child individually entails the parent, I removed the specific mechanistic detail about orientation‑specific pinning and the explicit link to particular La compounds and the x≈1/8 anomaly. The revised proposition keeps only the intersectional, entailed content: both children assert that LTT and LTO are distinct low‑temperature structures and that this distinction affects electronic order in the CuO$_2$ planes (Node 147 by stating different effects on stripe order; Node 136 by linking the LTO→LTT change to altered electronic/superconducting behavior). The new statement is self‑contained, factual, and modest while remaining substantive.

extra.notations:
- LTT: low‑temperature tetragonal crystal structure in cuprate superconductors
      - LTO: low‑temperature orthorhombic crystal structure in cuprate superconductors
      - Electronic order: charge or spin density modulations in the CuO$_2$ planes and related electronic properties (including superconductivity)

title: Incommensurate magnetic scattering in La-based cuprates
content: In La-based copper-oxide compounds, neutron scattering detects magnetic scattering that is displaced from the commensurate antiferromagnetic wave vector Q_AF = (1/2, 1/2) (expressed in reciprocal-lattice units of $2\pi/a$). The magnetic intensity appears at incommensurate two-dimensional wave vectors of the form $(\tfrac{1}{2}\pm\varepsilon,\tfrac{1}{2})$ and/or $(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2}\pm\varepsilon)$, where $\varepsilon$ is the incommensurability parameter describing the displacement of the peaks from $Q_{AF}$.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent combined separate claims from the two children (a specific static-order result in a particular Nd-doped sample and a broader empirical relation $\varepsilon\approx x$ for LSCO). Verification flagged a union error: each child supports only part of that joint statement, so the parent as written was not entailed by either child individually. To fix this, I removed claims not common to both children — specifically the explicit empirical relation $\varepsilon\approx x$ and the singled-out Nd-doped sample — and retained only the intersection: that neutron scattering in La-based cuprates shows magnetic peaks displaced from $Q_{AF}$ to $(\tfrac{1}{2}\pm\varepsilon,\tfrac{1}{2})$ and/or $(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2}\pm\varepsilon)$, with $\varepsilon$ defined as the incommensurability. This revised parent is self-contained, substantive, and is entailed by each child individually.

extra.notations:
- $a$: in-plane lattice constant of the CuO$_2$ plane (sets units $2\pi/a$)  
      - $\varepsilon$: incommensurability parameter (displacement of magnetic peaks from $Q_{AF}$)  
      - $Q_{AF}$: commensurate antiferromagnetic wave vector $Q_{AF}=(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2})$ in units of $2\pi/a$

title: Broad damped-oscillator nuclear scattering response in YBa2Cu3O6.92
content: In the superconducting compound YBa2Cu3O6.92 (yttrium barium copper oxide with oxygen content 6.92), neutron scattering measurements reveal a broad, non-magnetic (nuclear) spectral response that spans the measured energy range and is well described by a damped harmonic-oscillator (DHO) spectral function. This broad component is characterized by a resonance energy $\hbar\omega \approx 21.5\ \text{meV}$ and a linewidth (damping) $\Gamma_{\omega} \approx 26\ \text{meV}$. The DHO parameterization captures the lineshape of this extended nuclear scattering contribution in the measured spectra.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: Yttrium barium copper oxide with oxygen stoichiometry 6.92 (a near-optimally doped cuprate superconductor). 
- $\hbar$: reduced Planck constant. 
- $\omega$: angular frequency of the excitation; $\hbar\omega$ is the energy transfer. 
- DHO (damped harmonic oscillator): a spectral response model with a characteristic energy $\hbar\omega$ and a linewidth/damping parameter $\Gamma_{\omega}$ that describes a broadened oscillator-like excitation in inelastic scattering.

title: Pseudospin representation of environmental charge
content: In low-energy effective models of a one-dimensional electron gas (1DEG) (a metallic stripe) coupled to an environment with low-energy charge excitations, the environment's charge sector can be represented as a lattice of pseudospin-1/2 operators τ_R, where R labels sites on the effective pseudospin lattice. The pseudospin operator τ_R^z has eigenvalues ±1/2 such that τ_R^z = +1/2 denotes the presence of a holon pair localized at site R and τ_R^z = −1/2 denotes its absence. This pseudospin mapping is used to construct low-energy Hamiltonians of the form H_pseudo = H_{1DEG} + (local pseudospin terms involving τ_R) + (couplings between τ_R and long-wavelength stripe charge or spin fields), with the precise form of the τ_R couplings determined by whether environmental spin degrees of freedom are retained or are integrated out.

extra.notations:
- 1DEG: one-dimensional electron gas (metallic stripe) whose low-energy charge and spin fields are bosonized into fields such as φ_c, φ_s and their duals.
- τ_R: pseudospin-1/2 operator at pseudospin-lattice site R; τ_R^z = +1/2 indicates a localized holon pair at R, τ_R^z = −1/2 its absence.
- H_{1DEG}: low-energy Hamiltonian of the stripe (charge and spin sectors).
- H_pseudo: effective low-energy Hamiltonian in the pseudospin representation.
- ρ_R or ∂_x φ_c: long-wavelength stripe charge density at site R (these are equivalent up to the conventional bosonization prefactor used in the specific formulation).

title: Intersheet Tl–O bond length in thallium-based cuprate oxides
content: In thallium-containing layered cuprate oxides (examples include Tl2Ba2CuO6 and Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8), the chemical bonds that connect adjacent thallium–oxygen (Tl–O) sheets — i.e., the intersheet Tl–O bonds joining neighboring Tl–O layers that are stacked along the crystallographic $c$-axis — have a bond length on the order of 2.0 angstroms (≈2.0 Å). This statement refers specifically to the distance between a thallium atom in one Tl–O sheet and an oxygen atom in the adjacent Tl–O sheet that together form the intersheet linkage.

extra.notations:
- Å: angstrom = $10^{-10}$ meters; bond lengths are reported in Å.
- Tl–O intersheet bond: the chemical contact between a thallium (Tl) atom in one Tl–O layer and an oxygen (O) atom in an adjacent Tl–O layer.
- $c$-axis: crystallographic axis perpendicular to the (001) plane; layers are stacked along this axis.

title: Layered metal–oxygen sheet structure of Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8
content: The compound Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (thallium–barium–calcium copper oxide, a 2212 cuprate) has a layered crystal structure in which atomic planes composed of metal and oxygen atoms ("metal–oxygen sheets" or layers) are stacked along the crystallographic $c$-axis. Equivalently, the principal atomic planes lie parallel to the (001) crystallographic plane, so the structure is organized as a sequence of metal–oxygen sheets separated along the $c$-direction.

extra.notations:
- Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8: thallium–barium–calcium copper oxide (2212 phase).
- (001) plane: crystallographic plane perpendicular to the $c$-axis; "parallel to the (001) plane" indicates an atomic layer lying in the $ab$ plane.
- $c$-axis: axis along which such layers are stacked.
- "metal–oxygen sheet": an atomic plane or layer whose lattice sites are occupied primarily by metal cations (e.g., Cu, Tl, Ba, Ca) and oxygen anions.

title: Layered nature of thallium-containing cuprate oxides
content: Thallium-containing cuprate oxides (compounds with thallium, barium and copper in their formulas) are characterized by a layered crystal architecture formed by metal–oxygen sheets (atomic planes containing metal cations and oxygen anions) that are stacked along the crystallographic $c$-axis. In this structural motif, atomic planes lie parallel to the (001) plane and different metal–oxygen layers (for example, copper–oxygen planes and thallium–oxygen layers) occur as distinct stacked sheets in the unit cell.

extra.notations:
- "Thallium-containing cuprate oxides": oxide materials whose formulas include Tl, Cu and typically alkaline-earth metals (e.g., Ba, Ca).
- (001) plane and $c$-axis: as defined above; stacking along $c$ means layers are separated in the direction perpendicular to (001).
- "metal–oxygen sheet": an atomic plane principally composed of metal cations and oxygen anions (e.g., CuO planes, Tl–O layers).

title: Crystal structure of Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 in I4/mmm
content: The thallium–barium–calcium–copper oxide with nominal composition Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 crystallizes in the tetragonal space group I4/mmm (International Tables space-group No. 139).

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent combined two distinct claims: (A) that refinements produced atomic fractional coordinates (supported by child 1390) and (B) that refinements produced explicit interatomic bond distances characterizing cation–oxygen coordination (supported by child 1388). That union failed verification because neither child individually asserted both A and B. To satisfy the requirement that every child individually entails the parent, I retained only the claim common to both children: that the compound Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 is reported with a crystal structure in space group I4/mmm (No. 139). All other details (fractional coordinates, numerical bond lengths, refinement methodology) were removed because each is present only in a subset of the children. The revised parent is therefore entailed by every child and remains a substantive, self-contained structural claim.

extra.notations:
- Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8: nominal formula for the thallium–barium–calcium–copper oxide "2212" phase  
      - I4/mmm: tetragonal space group number 139 in the International Tables for Crystallography

title: Interatomic bond distances in Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (2212)
content: In the Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (2212) thallium cuprate phase, crystallographic structure refinements yield numerical interatomic bond distances between cation sites and oxygen anions; those distances are reported in ångströms (Å).

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent conjoined multiple distinct claims (numerical bond lengths and multiplicities; site occupancies and disorder descriptors) that were supported by different children, producing a union error: Child 1388 supplies explicit bond lengths and coordination multiplicities but not occupancies/disorder, while Child 1396 supplies occupancies/disorder and an average Tl–O distance but not the full set of multiplicities. To ensure every child individually entails the parent, I removed claims not common to all children and kept only the intersection: both children report numerical cation–oxygen bond lengths for the Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (2212) phase and use ångström units. The revised parent therefore states only that refinements yield numerical interatomic bond distances (in Å), which is entailed by each child individually. This preserves a substantive, self-contained factual claim while avoiding unsupported conjuncts (coordination multiplicities, site occupancies, disorder descriptors).

extra.notations: - \AA: ångström, $1\ \AA = 10^{-10}\\ \text{meters}$

title: DFT prediction: LaH10 forms an fcc clathrate and is predicted to be a high-Tc superconductor
content: Density functional theory (DFT) and systematic global-structure searches predict that lanthanum decahydride, LaH$_{10}$ (the compound with approximately ten hydrogen atoms per lanthanum atom), adopts a clathrate-like face-centered cubic crystal structure (space group Fm$\overline{3}$m) under megabar-class compression and is predicted by electron–phonon calculations to be a high–critical-temperature superconductor under those high-pressure conditions. This statement summarizes the shared theoretical conclusion that LaH$_{10}$ is a clathrate-type phase that is predicted to exhibit superconductivity when compressed to pressures of order 100 GPa and above.

extra.notations:
- LaH$_{10}$: lanthanum decahydride (lanthanum hydride with ~10 H per La). 
- Fm$\overline{3}$m: face-centered cubic space group notation. 
- DFT: density functional theory; "global-structure searches" denotes systematic searches over candidate crystal structures.

title: LaH$_{10}$ shows abrupt resistance drops at megabar pressures
content: Lanthanum superhydride (LaH$_{10}$ or LaH$_{10\pm x}$) compressed to megabar-class pressures exhibits abrupt decreases in electrical resistance that are interpreted as superconducting-like transitions.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent committed a union error by combining two distinct claims that no single child fully supported: (a) specific resistance-anomaly temperatures/pressure span (248–275 K and ~170–202 GPa) and (b) optical reflectivity/absorption evidence that the normal state is optically metallic. Children 298 and 302 support the electrical-resistance/anomaly interpretation (including $T_c$-level claims), while child 831 supplies the optical observation but not the transport details. To ensure every child individually entails the parent, I removed the optical-metallicity claim and the specific numeric ranges, retaining only the intersectional, factual claim that all children support: that LaH$_{10}$ at megabar pressures exhibits abrupt resistance decreases interpreted as superconducting-like transitions. This revised parent is self-contained, substantive, and entailed by each child individually.

extra.notations:
- LaH$_{10}$ / LaH$_{10\pm x}$: lanthanum superhydride stoichiometry near ten H per La.
      - GPa: gigapascal, unit of pressure (megabar-class pressures $\sim$100+ GPa).
      - "Resistance anomaly": abrupt decrease in measured electrical resistance, interpreted as a superconducting transition.

title: Definition and experimental identification of Tc in high-pressure LaH hydride transport measurements
content: In high-pressure transport experiments on lanthanum hydrides (compounds with stoichiometry LaH$_{x}$, including LaH$_{10}$ and LaH$_{10\pm x}$), the superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ is operationally identified from electrical-resistance measurements as the temperature at which the measured resistance departs from the linear temperature dependence expected for a normal metal; experimentally this departure typically appears as an abrupt resistance anomaly (a sharp decrease) on cooling or heating and is reported as the transition temperature in those studies.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature. 
- "Departure from linear temperature dependence": a clear deviation from the normal-metal R vs T trend used as the experimental onset criterion for superconductivity.

title: Superconductivity in thallium–barium cuprates
content: Members of the thallium–barium–copper–oxide family (abbreviated "Tl–Ba–Cu–O") — layered thallium–barium copper oxide cuprate compounds — form superconducting phases: each such superconducting phase has a material-specific superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$, and below $T_{c}$ the material exhibits zero electrical resistance. This statement applies to Tl–Ba–Cu–O compositions generally, including related phases that additionally contain calcium (Tl–Ba–Ca–Cu–O).

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature, the temperature below which a material shows zero electrical resistance.
- Tl–Ba–Cu–O: shorthand for thallium–barium–copper–oxide layered cuprate compounds.
- Tl–Ba–Ca–Cu–O: shorthand for thallium–barium–calcium–copper–oxide layered cuprate compositions (a subset of Tl–Ba–Cu–O compounds that include Ca).

title: Superconductivity with nonzero critical current in single-layer FeSe/STO
content: Single-layer iron selenide films grown on niobium-doped strontium titanate (FeSe/STO) exhibit superconductivity at low temperature and show non-linear current–voltage (I–V) characteristics in transport measurements, including a nonzero superconducting critical current: there exists a range of applied current values for which the measured voltage is (within experimental resolution) zero, indicating a dissipationless superconducting state below the film's superconducting transition temperature.

extra.notations:
- FeSe/STO: single atomic-layer iron selenide (FeSe) film grown on Nb-doped SrTiO$_3$ (001) substrate.
- I–V: current versus voltage characteristic measured in a transport experiment (e.g., four-point probe).
- superconducting critical current ($I_{\mathrm{c}}$): the maximum applied current for which the measured voltage across the superconducting film remains (within experimental resolution) zero.
- "low temperature": temperatures below the superconducting transition temperature of the film (i.e., in the superconducting state).

title: Five-orbital Fe-layer basis for LaFeAsO
content: For the iron-pnictide compound LaFeAsO, the low-energy electronic states localized on the iron (Fe) layer can be represented using a basis of five iron 3d-derived orbitals. These five orbitals, labeled here by orbital index $\mu\in\{1,2,3,4,5\}$, correspond to the usual atomic $d$-orbital symmetries: $\mu=1:d_{3Z^{2}-R^{2}}$, $\mu=2:d_{XZ}$, $\mu=3:d_{YZ}$, $\mu=4:d_{X^{2}-Y^{2}}$, and $\mu=5:d_{XY}$. Electronic models of the Fe layer commonly employ this five-orbital basis as the minimal set of Fe-derived degrees of freedom required to represent the low-energy band structure of LaFeAsO.

extra.notations:
- LaFeAsO: The iron-pnictide compound lanthanum iron arsenide oxide.
- Fe layer: The square-planar layer of iron atoms whose $3d$ orbitals dominate the low-energy electronic structure.
- $\mu$: Orbital index running over the five Fe $3d$-derived orbitals, with the mapping given explicitly above.

title: General formula and layer-counting for Hg-based cuprates
content: Mercury–barium–calcium–copper–oxide compounds form a homologous chemical series with general formula HgBa2Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+δ}, where m is an integer equal to the number of CuO2 (copper‑oxide) layers per crystallographic unit cell and δ denotes oxygen non‑stoichiometry (excess or deficiency of oxygen). This notation identifies the family members by their layer count and overall stoichiometry.

extra.notations:
- m: integer number of CuO$_2$ layers per crystallographic unit cell (also written as n in some sources).  
- $\delta$: oxygen non‑stoichiometry (excess oxygen relative to the ideal stoichiometry).

title: Designation of Hg‑1212 and Hg‑1223 by layer count
content: In the mercury‑based cuprate homologous series with general formula HgBa2Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+δ} (m an integer, $\delta$ oxygen non‑stoichiometry), the member with two CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell (m = 2) is conventionally denoted Hg‑1212, and the member with three CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell (m = 3) is conventionally denoted Hg‑1223.

extra.notations:
- Hg‑1212: the Hg‑based cuprate with m = 2 (two CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell).  
- Hg‑1223: the Hg‑based cuprate with m = 3 (three CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell).  
- m: integer layer count as in the general formula.  
- $\delta$: oxygen non‑stoichiometry.

title: SmFeAsO as the undoped parent of the LnOFeAs family
content: Samarium iron arsenide oxide (SmFeAsO) is the undoped (fluorine-free) parent compound of the rare-earth iron oxypnictide family with nominal formula LnOFeAs, where Ln denotes a rare-earth element such as Sm or La. In this context "parent compound" denotes the composition without deliberate fluorine substitution on the oxygen site.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO: chemical formula for samarium iron arsenide oxide, the fluorine-free parent composition in this family.
- LnOFeAs: generic formula for rare-earth iron oxypnictide parent compounds, with Ln = a rare-earth element (examples: La, Sm).
- "Undoped (fluorine-free) parent compound": the compound with no intentional substitution of O by F.

title: Coupling-mediated exchange of electron pairs between a 1DEG stripe and its environment
content: In low-energy effective descriptions of a one-dimensional electron gas (1DEG) constituting a metallic stripe coupled to an adjacent environment, the models include coupling terms that directly couple stripe charge or pair operators to the environment's charge degrees of freedom. These coupling terms can mediate exchange or transfer of charge-2e electron pairs (i.e., paired hole/electron excitations) between the stripe and the environment, thereby providing a channel by which charge/pairing degrees of freedom in the environment can influence pairing correlations on the stripe. Here "1DEG" denotes the low-energy, long-wavelength charge and spin degrees of freedom of the stripe; "environment" denotes the nearby electronic/antiferromagnetic medium that supplies low-energy charge excitations.

extra.notations:
- 1DEG: one-dimensional electron gas (metallic stripe), the low-energy charge and spin fields of the stripe.
- Environment: the adjacent electronic/antiferromagnetic medium supplying low-energy charge excitations (may be represented in different effective variables such as localized pair degrees of freedom).
- "Charge-2e electron pair": a bound/paired charge excitation carrying net charge 2e (a paired hole or electron excitation).
- "Coupling term": any low-energy Hamiltonian term that directly couples stripe charge/pair operators to environment charge degrees of freedom and can change their occupations.

title: Oxygen annealing can modify Tc in layered cuprates
content: Post‑synthesis annealing of layered copper‑oxide (cuprate) superconductors in a continuous flowing oxygen (O$_2$) atmosphere can change the measured superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ and the shape or width of the resistive superconducting transition. "Post‑synthesis annealing in flowing oxygen" means heating a synthesized cuprate sample at elevated temperature while O$_2$ is flowed continuously over the sample for a finite processing time; "modifies" means the treatment produces a change in the measured onset or midpoint of the superconducting transition or alters the resistive-transition profile (direction of change may be increase or decrease depending on compound and processing history).

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (Kelvin), the temperature at which a sample shows a superconducting transition in resistivity or susceptibility.
- "Post‑synthesis annealing in flowing oxygen": heating a synthesized cuprate sample in a continuous O$_2$ gas flow at an elevated temperature for a finite time to adjust oxygen stoichiometry.
- "Layered copper‑oxide (cuprate) superconductors": crystalline materials whose structures contain one or more CuO$_2$ planes per unit cell (examples include Hg‑, Tl‑, and Bi‑based families).

title: Existence of a quasihydrostatic onset Tc report in Hg-based cuprates
content: At least one member of the mercury-based cuprate homologous series $HgBa_{2}Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+\delta}$ has an experimentally reported onset superconducting transition temperature $T_{co}$ measured under quasihydrostatic applied pressure (i.e., onset $T_{co}$ determined under an applied-pressure environment approximating hydrostatic conditions such as in a diamond anvil cell).

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent combined a series-level claim ("the homologous series has experimentally reported onset $T_{co}$ measured as a function of applied pressure") with an example measurement. Child 1621 provides only a single-member measurement and therefore does not entail the broader series-level claim, producing a union error. Both children do support the weaker, existential claim that at least one member of the Hg-based cuprate series has an onset $T_{co}$ reported under quasihydrostatic pressure. The revised parent removes the unsupported series-wide formulation and retains only the intersectional, self-contained factual claim that is entailed by each child individually.

extra.notations:
- $HgBa_{2}Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+\delta}$: mercury-based cuprate homologous series (index $m$ = number of CuO$_2$ layers). 
      - $T_{co}$: onset superconducting transition temperature (resistive onset). 
      - quasihydrostatic: applied-pressure environment approximating hydrostatic conditions (e.g., in a DAC).

title: Pair-hopping couples stripe spin field to environmental degrees and can transmit spin-gap/pair correlations
content:
Consider a system consisting of a one-dimensional metallic stripe (a one-dimensional electron gas, 1DEG) coupled to an adjacent active environment (e.g., spatially confined Mott-insulating/antiferromagnetic regions). Let a singlet-pair creation operator on the stripe be denoted $P^{\dagger}$ and the corresponding singlet-pair operator in the environment be denoted $\bar{\mathcal{P}}$. The low-energy effective Hamiltonian for the coupled system contains singlet-pair transfer (pair-hopping) terms that transfer a spin-singlet pair between stripe and environment. In operator form this coupling can be written as
$$
\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{pair}} \;=\; t_{sp}\bigl[P^{\dagger}\,\bar{\mathcal{P}} \;+\; \mathrm{H.c.}\bigr],
$$
or, in a low-energy pseudospin representation of the environmental charge sector, as a term with amplitude $J_{sp}$ that couples the stripe spin field to environmental spin or pseudospin degrees of freedom (for example, schematically $J_{sp}\,\tau^{x}\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\phi_{s}]\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\bar{\phi}_{s}]$). Such pair-transfer terms provide an explicit coupling channel by which spinful correlations or a spin gap present in the environment can be communicated to the stripe, i.e., by which paired/spin-gapped correlations in the environment couple into the stripe's spin sector.

extra.notations:
- 1DEG: one-dimensional electron gas representing the metallic stripe. 
- $P^{\dagger}$: singlet-pair creation operator on the stripe. 
- $\bar{\mathcal{P}}$: singlet-pair operator in the environment. 
- $t_{sp}$, $J_{sp}$: amplitudes for singlet-pair transfer (pair-hopping) between stripe and environment. 
- $\phi_{s}$, $\bar{\phi}_{s}$: bosonic spin fields of stripe and environment, respectively. 
- $\tau^{x}$: pseudospin operator representing environmental pair (holon-pair) degrees of freedom in a low-energy charge/pseudospin mapping.

title: Cation–oxygen bond distances from Tl‑2212 refinements
content: Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (the thallium–barium–calcium–copper oxide "2212" phase) has a crystallographic unit cell in which metal cation sites (thallium Tl, barium Ba, calcium Ca, copper Cu) are coordinated by oxygen anions; crystallographic structure refinements of this 2212 family produce quantitative numerical interatomic metal–oxygen bond distances, expressed in ångströms (Å), that characterize these local cation coordination environments within the unit cell.

extra.notations:
- Å: ångström, 1 Å = 10^{-10} meters.  
- "Tl‑2212": shorthand for Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8-type thallium cuprates.  
- "metal–oxygen bond distances": distances between specified cation (Tl, Ba, Ca, Cu) atomic sites and neighboring oxygen anions determined by crystallographic refinement.

title: Atomic sites and refinement of Tl‑2212 crystal model
content: The compound Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (Tl‑2212) is represented by a crystallographic model with distinct atomic sites for the metal cations (Tl, Ba, Ca, Cu) and oxygen anions; this model is the subject of structure refinement procedures that determine atomic-site parameters (for example atomic fractional coordinates) describing the unit cell and the local arrangements of these cations and anions.

extra.notations:
- Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (Tl‑2212): thallium–barium–calcium–copper oxide 2212 phase.  
- "atomic fractional coordinates": the fractional coordinates (x,y,z) of atoms within the crystallographic unit cell.  
- "atomic-site parameters": quantities refined in a crystallographic model (positions, occupancies, thermal/disorder descriptors).

title: Metal cations present in Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (Tl‑2212)
content: Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8-type (2212) compounds contain the metal cations thallium (Tl), barium (Ba), calcium (Ca), and copper (Cu) as constituents of the oxide lattice in the 2212 unit cell.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent combined claims from different children (a union error): child 1633 reports quantitative refinement outputs (metal–oxygen distances, occupancies, disorder descriptors), while child 1384 only gives a methodological detail (scattering factors and anomalous terms for the metal ions). To ensure the parent is entailed by every child, I removed statements about refinement outputs and about cation–oxygen coordination that are not asserted by child 1384. The revised parent restricts the claim to the intersection supported by both children: that Tl‑2212 compounds contain the metal cations Tl, Ba, Ca, and Cu in the oxide lattice. This statement is factual, self-contained, and is entailed individually by each child.

extra.notations:
- "2212": shorthand for the Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 structure type.  
      - "metal cations": positively charged metal species (Tl, Ba, Ca, Cu) present in the oxide lattice.

title: Tl–O layers stacked along the crystallographic c-axis in thallium cuprates
content: In thallium-containing cuprate oxides (compounds whose stoichiometry includes thallium, Tl, together with copper and alkaline-earth metals such as barium or calcium), thallium–oxygen (Tl–O) layers are planar atomic sheets that lie parallel to the crystallographic (001) plane and occur as distinct stacked layers in the unit cell. These Tl–O sheets are stacked along the crystallographic $c$-axis (the axis perpendicular to the (001) plane), so the crystal structure contains a sequence of Tl–O atomic planes separated in the $c$-direction.

extra.notations:
- Tl: thallium (chemical element); Cu: copper; Ba, Ca: alkaline-earth/alkaline-earth-like cations commonly present in these oxides.
- (001) plane: the crystallographic plane perpendicular to the $c$-axis; "parallel to (001)" means lying in the $ab$ plane.
- $c$-axis: the crystallographic axis perpendicular to the (001) plane; layers are stacked along this axis.
- "Tl–O layer" or "Tl–O sheet": an atomic plane in the crystal whose lattice sites are occupied predominantly by thallium cations and oxygen anions.

title: Inter-stripe coupling depends on local transverse spacing
content: In a two-dimensional array of parallel conducting stripes labeled by integer j, let Y_j(x) denote the stripe j transverse displacement (in the direction perpendicular to the stripe) at longitudinal coordinate x, and define the nearest-neighbor transverse spacing Δ_jY(x) ≡ Y_{j+1}(x) − Y_j(x). Inter-stripe coupling functions that couple degrees of freedom on stripe j and stripe j+1 (examples include the charge-density-wave coupling prefactor V or the Josephson pair-tunneling matrix element J) depend on this local transverse spacing: the strength or prefactor of such nearest-neighbor coupling terms is a function of Δ_jY (i.e., the coupling is not a constant independent of the stripe transverse geometry but varies as a function of the local spacing between stripes).

extra.notations:
- $j$: integer stripe index labeling parallel stripes in the array.  
- $x$: coordinate along the stripe (longitudinal coordinate).  
- $Y_j(x)$: transverse displacement of stripe $j$ at longitudinal coordinate $x$.  
- $\Delta_j Y(x)\equiv Y_{j+1}(x)-Y_j(x)$: local transverse spacing between neighboring stripes.  
- $V(\Delta_j Y)$: example of an inter-stripe coupling prefactor for charge-density-wave (CDW) order, indicating explicit dependence on Δ_jY.  
- $J(\Delta_j Y)$: example of an inter-stripe Josephson (pair-tunneling) matrix element, indicating explicit dependence on Δ_jY.

title: Nominal target composition for Sm–Fe–As–O–F syntheses
content: The syntheses and samples are targeted at the nominal chemical composition SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$, i.e. samarium iron oxyarsenide in which some oxygen sites are intended to be substituted by fluorine. Here Sm denotes samarium, Fe iron, As arsenic, O oxygen, F fluorine, and $x$ denotes the nominal fraction of oxygen sites substituted by fluorine; the formula expresses the intended (nominal) stoichiometry of the starting formulations used in the reported syntheses.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$: samarium iron oxyarsenide with nominal fluorine substitution on oxygen sites. 
- Sm: samarium; Fe: iron; As: arsenic; O: oxygen; F: fluorine. 
- $x$: nominal fluorine substitution fraction on the oxygen sublattice (0 ≤ $x$ ≤ 1).

title: Nominal target composition for Sm–Fe–As–O–F syntheses
content: The syntheses and samples are targeted at the nominal chemical composition SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$, i.e. samarium iron oxyarsenide in which some oxygen sites are intended to be substituted by fluorine. Here Sm denotes samarium, Fe iron, As arsenic, O oxygen, F fluorine, and $x$ denotes the nominal fraction of oxygen sites substituted by fluorine; the formula expresses the intended (nominal) stoichiometry of the starting formulations used in the reported syntheses.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$: samarium iron oxyarsenide with nominal fluorine substitution on oxygen sites. 
- Sm: samarium; Fe: iron; As: arsenic; O: oxygen; F: fluorine. 
- $x$: nominal fluorine substitution fraction on the oxygen sublattice (0 ≤ $x$ ≤ 1).

title: Nominal SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$ as synthesis target
content: The subject matter is the nominal chemical composition SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$ (samarium iron oxyarsenide with intended partial substitution of oxygen by fluorine). In this notation, Sm = samarium, Fe = iron, As = arsenic, O = oxygen, F = fluorine, and $x$ denotes the intended fluorine substitution fraction on the oxygen sublattice; the formula describes the nominal stoichiometry targeted by the reported syntheses.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$: samarium iron oxyarsenide with nominal fluorine substitution on the oxygen site.
- $x$: nominal fluorine fraction.

title: Nominal target composition for Sm[O$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$]FeAs syntheses
content: The starting formulations and syntheses are intended to produce the quaternary compound SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$ (samarium iron oxyarsenide with nominal fluorine substitution on oxygen sites). Symbols: Sm = samarium, Fe = iron, As = arsenic, O = oxygen, F = fluorine; $x$ denotes the nominal fraction of oxygen sites substituted by fluorine. This statement captures the common target stoichiometry referenced by the syntheses.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$ (or Sm[O$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$]FeAs): quaternary samarium iron oxyarsenide with nominal fluorine substitution on the O sublattice.
- $x$: nominal fluorine substitution fraction.

title: Common synthesis target: SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$
content: Reported synthesis routes (ambient-pressure solid-state and high-pressure preparations) are directed at the nominal quaternary composition SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$, i.e. samarium iron oxyarsenide with intended partial substitution of oxygen by fluorine. Here Sm = samarium, Fe = iron, As = arsenic, O = oxygen, F = fluorine, and $x$ denotes the nominal substitution fraction on the oxygen sites.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$: samarium iron oxyarsenide with nominal fluorine substitution.
- $x$: nominal fluorine fraction on the oxygen sublattice.

title: High-pressure Im-3m / R3m structures in H3S-like hydrogen–sulfur compounds
content: Hydrogen–sulfur compounds with stoichiometry close to H3S (e.g., often described as (H2S)2H2 or H3S) can adopt the Im-3m or R3m crystal structures under high pressure.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted that Im-3m/R3m H3S-like phases are superconducting (nonzero $T_c$ at ~10^2 GPa), but child 1529 did not assert the existence of a nonzero $T_c$ — it described a microscopic electron–phonon mechanism only. That made the parent's existence claim unsupported by all children (a union error). To satisfy the requirement that every child individually entails the parent, I removed the superconductivity and numeric pressure claims and retained only the structural fact that both children state: H3S-like hydrogen–sulfur compounds occur in the Im-3m or R3m high-pressure crystal structures. This revised parent is self-contained, factual, and is entailed by both child nodes (1529 and 1530) without adding claims that only one child supports.

extra.notations:
- H3S-like: hydrogen–sulfur stoichiometries formed under high pressure, e.g., (H2S)2H2 or H3S
      - Im-3m / R3m: specific high-pressure crystal structures reported for H3S-like phases

title: Impurity-induced four-fold LDOS pattern in d-wave superconductors
content: In a superconductor whose order parameter has $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ symmetry, an atomic-scale (point-like) impurity (for example a substitutional Zn on a Cu site) produces a pronounced, four-fold symmetric (cross-shaped) modulation of the local quasiparticle density of states (LDOS) in the impurity vicinity. The arms of this cross-shaped LDOS pattern are aligned with the crystallographic a and b axes, and the spatial anisotropy of the pattern reflects the angular (nodal) anisotropy of the $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ pairing. This spatial LDOS pattern is observable in scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) differential conductance ($dI/dV$) maps as an energy-resolved, four-fold symmetric LDOS modulation centered on the impurity site.

extra.notations:
- LDOS(r,E): local density of quasiparticle states at spatial position r and energy E, measurable by STM differential conductance $dI/dV(r,V)$ with $E\approx eV$.
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: superconducting order parameter with sign-changing angular dependence (rotates by 90° to change sign) and gap nodes (directions of vanishing gap) that produce strong angular anisotropy of low-energy quasiparticles.
- "Atomic-scale impurity": a point-like substitutional or vacancy defect (e.g., Zn substituting for Cu) that scatters quasiparticles locally and can induce bound or resonant states inside the superconducting gap.

title: Interstitial oxygen dopes the CuO2 planes in Bi-2212
content: In the cuprate superconductor Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (Bi-2212), nonstoichiometric interstitial oxygen atoms act as atomic-scale dopants that introduce hole carriers into the CuO2 planes.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent combined two claims: (A) that interstitial oxygens supply hole carriers to the CuO2 planes, and (B) that those oxygens produce localized LDOS/STM impurity-state features usable to locate dopants and infer their role. Verification showed Child 1061 supports only claim (A) while Child 1077 supports both (A) and (B), so the parent committed a union error by including (B). To ensure every child individually entails the parent, I removed the STM/LDOS detection and inference language and retained only the doping statement that is common to both children. The revised proposition is self-contained, factual, and entailed by both Node 1061 and Node 1077.

extra.notations:
- Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (Bi-2212): bismuth-based high-Tc cuprate superconductor.
      - CuO2: copper–oxide planes where superconductivity principally resides.
      - "Interstitial/nonstoichiometric oxygen": extra oxygen atoms introduced beyond the stoichiometric lattice (parameterized by δ).

title: Interstitial oxygen dopants are atomic-scale perturbations of the electronic structure in Bi-2212
content: In Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ, nonstoichiometric interstitial oxygen atoms act as dopant/impurity atoms that produce atomic-scale, point-like perturbations of the local electronic environment and electronic structure.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent combined two distinct claims that were not jointly supported by either child: (a) an explicit electrostatic charge assignment for interstitial oxygens (supported by Node 1061 but not by Node 1538) and (b) the experimental STM/STS identification and gap/LDOS correlations (supported by Node 1538 but not by Node 1061). To ensure the parent is entailed by every child, I removed the explicit statement about ionic charge and the specific STM/STS observational claims, leaving only the intersection: both children identify nonstoichiometric interstitial oxygens in Bi-2212 as dopant/impurity atoms that locally perturb the electronic structure at the atomic scale. The revised proposition is self-contained, substantive, and is individually entailed by each child.

extra.notations:
- Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (Bi-2212): bismuth-based high-Tc cuprate superconductor.
      - "Dopant/impurity": a nonstoichiometric atom that locally alters carrier concentration or electronic potentials relative to the host lattice.

title: Oxygen dopants produce a detectable impurity resonance in Bi-2212
content: In Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ, interstitial oxygen dopant atoms produce a distinct, localized impurity resonance in the local density of states that is detectable by scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STM/STS); individual impurity-resonance features correspond to single dopant atoms and can be used to identify dopant positions in LDOS images.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent included correlation claims (that the spatial density of impurity resonances correlates with the sample-averaged superconducting gap and that mapping them relates local dopant density to gap variations) which are supported by child 1538 but not by child 1077. To ensure every child entails the parent, I removed those correlation statements and retained only the claims common to both children: (1) interstitial oxygen dopants produce a distinct, localized impurity resonance detectable by STM/STS, and (2) each such resonance feature corresponds to a single dopant and can be used to identify dopant positions in LDOS images. This revised parent is fully self-contained and is entailed by both child nodes.

extra.notations:
- Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (Bi-2212): bismuth-based cuprate superconductor.
      - LDOS(r,E): local density of states at position r and energy E measured by dI/dV.
      - STM/STS: scanning tunneling microscopy/spectroscopy.

title: Impurity-state resonances attributable to oxygen dopants correlate with the superconducting gap
content: In Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ, electronic impurity-state resonances observed in STM/STS are attributable to interstitial oxygen dopant atoms, and the spatial density of these resonances correlates with the sample-averaged superconducting energy gap magnitude. This empirical correlation supports the identification of those LDOS features as oxygen dopants and links dopant density to variations in the superconducting gap.

extra.notations:
- Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (Bi-2212): bismuth-based high-Tc cuprate superconductor.
- STM/STS, LDOS(r,E): scanning tunneling microscopy/spectroscopy and local density of states.
- "Impurity-state resonance": a localized LDOS signature associated with a dopant or defect.

title: Common absorptive polarizability from charge- and spin-density excitations in the Cu–O normal state
content: In the normal (non‑superconducting) state of copper–oxide high‑temperature superconductors, collective charge‑density and spin‑density excitations each contribute an absorptive component of the electronic polarizability, denoted $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$, and the charge and spin contributions share the same functional dependence on momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$.

refine_history.reasoning:
The original parent asserted a specific piecewise, temperature‑dependent scaling law for $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ (with $-N(0)(\omega/T)$ for $|\omega|<T$ and $-N(0)\,\mathrm{sgn}\omega$ for $|\omega|>T$). Children 930 and 1584 explicitly state that scaling, but child 914 does not: it only asserts that charge and spin susceptibilities must have the same functional form and comparable magnitudes to fit data, and does not commit to the exact piecewise $\omega/T$ / sgn$(\omega)$ law. Hence the parent contained a union error (it included a quantitative scaling not entailed by every child). 

This rewrite removes the quantitative piecewise scaling and the explicit temperature/$N(0)$ dependence, retaining only the intersection of the children: the existence of an absorptive polarizability contribution from charge and spin density excitations in the normal state, and the claim that the charge and spin contributions share a common functional dependence on $\mathbf{q}$ and $\omega$. That weaker statement is entailed by each child individually (930 and 1584 by explicit restatement, 914 by its requirement of a common functional form) and is a self‑contained, substantive physical claim.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary (absorptive) part of the polarizability contribution from charge‑ and spin‑density excitations, as a function of momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$. 
      - $\mathbf{q}$: momentum vector.
      - $\omega$: frequency.

title: Role of the short-distance cutoff a in the phase-stiffness
content: In superconductors, the zero-temperature phase stiffness $V_{0}$ (the energy scale that controls long-wavelength superconducting phase fluctuations) is defined using a short-distance spatial cutoff denoted $a$. Concretely, $a$ is the short-distance length scale (a spatial cutoff) that appears in the definition or calculation of the zero-temperature phase stiffness $V_{0}$ and represents the typical size of the local region over which the superconducting order parameter is coherently defined.

extra.notations:
- $V_{0}$: zero-temperature phase stiffness (energy scale for long-wavelength superconducting phase fluctuations). 
- $a$: short-distance spatial cutoff (a length scale representing the size of regions over which the superconducting order parameter is locally coherent).

title: Cutoff selection in quasi-two-dimensional superconductors
content: In quasi-two-dimensional (weakly coupled layered) superconductors, the short-distance spatial cutoff $a$ used to compute the zero-temperature phase stiffness $V_{0}$ is taken as the larger of the interlayer spacing $d$ and a length of order the perpendicular superconducting coherence length $\xi_{\perp}$. In other words, $a$ is chosen as whichever is larger: the layer spacing $d$ or a length proportional to $\xi_{\perp}$ (different conventions give slightly different numerical prefactors multiplying $\xi_{\perp}$).

extra.notations:
- $a$: short-distance spatial cutoff used in the $V_{0}$ calculation.
- $d$: average spacing between superconducting layers (interlayer spacing).
- $\xi_{\perp}$: superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers.

title: Magnesium diboride (MgB2) is a superconductor
content: Magnesium diboride (chemical formula MgB2) is a superconductor, i.e., it exhibits a phase below a characteristic transition temperature in which it shows zero electrical resistivity and expels magnetic flux (the Meissner effect). This statement makes no specific numeric claim about the transition temperature or about microscopic interaction parameters.

extra.notations:
- MgB2: magnesium diboride, a binary compound of magnesium and boron.
- Superconductor: a material that, below its superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$, exhibits zero DC electrical resistance and the Meissner effect (expulsion of magnetic flux).
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (not specified numerically in this join).

title: SO(5) superspin as combined SC and AF order-parameter
content: In the SO(5) unification framework for antiferromagnetism and superconductivity, define a five-component "superspin" vector n_a = (n_1,n_2,n_3,n_4,n_5) that encodes the order-parameter degrees of freedom. The two components n_1 and n_5 are the two real degrees of freedom of the superconducting order parameter (equivalently, they correspond to the real and imaginary parts of a complex superconducting order parameter), while the three components n_2, n_3, n_4 encode the three components of the antiferromagnetic (staggered spin-density) order. This identification treats superconducting and antiferromagnetic orders as different orientations of a single five-dimensional order-parameter vector within the SO(5) framework.

extra.notations:
- n_a: five-component "superspin" order-parameter vector, a = 1,...,5
- n_1,n_5: the two real components of the superconducting order parameter (real and imaginary parts of a complex SC order parameter)
- n_2,n_3,n_4: the three components of the antiferromagnetic (staggered spin-density) order parameter
- "SO(5) unification": theoretical framework treating SC and AF as components of a single 5D order parameter

title: Shadow band in Bi2212 exhibits an altered reflection parity in polarization-resolved ARPES
content: In the bilayer cuprate Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (Bi2212), the weaker folded/replica feature known as the shadow band appears in polarization-resolved angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) as a structural replica of the principal CuO2-derived main band but with an altered reflection parity: the initial states that give rise to the shadow-band signal couple to a linear polarization that is orthogonal to the polarization that predominantly couples to the corresponding main-band initial states in at least some azimuthal directions of the Brillouin zone.

refine_history.reasoning: The verification identified a union error: the original parent asserted both (i) the experimental observation of an altered reflection parity for the shadow band and (ii) that this parity difference is reproduced in one-step photoemission simulations. Child 1516 supplies the experimental observation and the simulation result, but Child 1517 supports the altered-parity structural replica observation without mentioning simulations. To ensure the parent is entailed by every child, I removed the simulation-reproduction clause and any attributional claims not common to both children. The revised parent states only the intersectional, factual claim — that the shadow band is a structural replica with altered reflection parity observed in polarization-resolved ARPES in at least some azimuths — which is directly supported by both Child 1516 and Child 1517. The revision is self-contained and substantive while avoiding the unsupported conjunctive detail.

extra.notations:
- Bi2212: Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ, a bilayer cuprate superconductor.
      - ARPES: angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy; MB = main (principal CuO2-derived) band; SB = shadow (weaker folded/replica) band.
      - Reflection parity: symmetry (even/odd) of an electronic initial-state wavefunction under reflection in a specified mirror plane; determines which linear polarization couples in ARPES.

title: Phase-ordering and mean-field scales as upper bounds on Tc; dominance of phase fluctuations when comparable
content:
In a superconducting system, define: $T_{c}$ as the observed superconducting transition temperature at which global long-range phase coherence is established; $T^{MF}$ as the mean-field pairing temperature (the temperature scale at which local pair amplitudes first become significant in a mean-field treatment); and $T_{\theta}^{max}$ as the phase-ordering temperature computed from the zero-temperature phase stiffness (i.e., the temperature at which long-wavelength phase order would disappear if other disordering channels were neglected). For such a system, both $T_{\theta}^{max}$ and $T^{MF}$ act as theoretical upper bounds on the actual ordering temperature $T_{c}$ because neglected disordering effects (including phase disordering and other thermal or quantum degrees of freedom) can only reduce the observed transition temperature. Equivalently,
$$
T_{c}\leq\min\{\,T_{\theta}^{max},\,T^{MF}\,\}.
$$
Moreover, when the phase-ordering scale is comparable to the observed transition temperature (i.e., $T_{\theta}^{max}/T_{c}\sim 1$), phase fluctuations (thermal or quantum) can be the dominant mechanism that depresses $T_{c}$ significantly below the mean-field value $T^{MF}$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence).  
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature (temperature scale for local pair formation in mean-field theory).  
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: phase-ordering temperature computed from phase stiffness while neglecting additional disordering mechanisms; interpreted as a theoretical upper bound on $T_{c}$.  
- "Phase fluctuations": fluctuations of the superconducting order-parameter phase (thermal or quantum) that destroy long-range coherence without necessarily destroying local pairing amplitude.

title: MgB2 undergoes a superconducting phase transition
content: Magnesium diboride (MgB2) undergoes a superconducting phase transition at a well-defined critical temperature $T_{c}$. By definition, below this critical temperature $T_{c}$ the material enters a superconducting state characterized by zero (vanishing) dc electrical resistance and magnetic flux expulsion from the bulk (the Meissner effect). This statement reports the existence of the superconducting transition in MgB2 without specifying the numerical value of $T_{c}$.

extra.notations:
- MgB2: magnesium diboride, a layered binary compound containing magnesium and boron. 
- $T_{c}$: the superconducting critical temperature (the temperature below which the material is in the superconducting state with zero resistance and magnetic flux expulsion). 
- Meissner effect: the expulsion of magnetic flux from the bulk of a material upon entering the superconducting state.

title: Definition of population and excited-fraction for THz-driven hydrogenic donors
content: In a semiconductor containing shallow (hydrogenic) donor impurities with bound electronic levels (the ground state commonly denoted 1s and low-lying excited states such as 2p), consider an ensemble of such donors driven by a spatially uniform rectangular terahertz (THz) electric-field pulse of duration τ and peak field amplitude $E_{THz}$. Let $\rho_{11}(\tau)$ denote the ensemble-averaged population (density-matrix population) of the donor ground (1s) state evaluated at the end of the rectangular pulse (time τ). Then $(1-\rho_{11}(\tau))$ is the fraction of the donor ensemble that has been excited out of the 1s ground state by the end of the pulse (time τ). This statement defines the notation and the direct physical interpretation of $\rho_{11}(\tau)$ and $(1-\rho_{11}(\tau))$ for THz-driven population dynamics of hydrogenic donors.

extra.notations:
- $\rho_{11}(\tau)$: ensemble-averaged population (density-matrix diagonal element) of the donor ground state (1s) evaluated at the end of the rectangular THz pulse of duration $\tau$.  
- $1-\rho_{11}(\tau)$: fraction of the donor ensemble excited out of the ground state by time $\tau$.  
- $\tau$: duration of the applied rectangular THz electric-field pulse (time).  
- $E_{THz}$: peak electric-field amplitude of the applied rectangular THz pulse.  
- "Hydrogenic donor": a shallow impurity in a semiconductor with bound electronic states analogous to hydrogen (e.g., 1s, 2p); statements refer to an ensemble average over many such donors.

title: Charged domain line (stripe) in a CuO2 plane
content: A charged domain line (stripe) in a CuO2 plane is a one-dimensional linear defect in the CuO2 layer that carries and localizes excess hole density.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent asserted an existential HF result (that self-consistent Hartree–Fock solutions accommodate excess holes by forming charged, spinless domain walls which flip the Néel order and alter the electronic structure). Child 1515 did not itself assert that HF calculations produce such stripes (it only derived consequences assuming stripes exist), so the original parent was stronger than the intersection of all children (union error). To ensure the parent is entailed by every child, I removed HF-specific existential claims and other details not present in every child (Néel sign flip, suppressed local spin amplitude, HF origin, and statements about altered midgap electronic structure and orientation dependence). The remaining statement — that a "charged domain line (stripe) is a one-dimensional linear defect in the CuO2 plane that carries and localizes excess hole density" — is a minimal, factual definition that is supported or presupposed by each child (1476, 1513, 1514 explicitly; 1515 references "parallel charged domain lines" and their role in hosting holes). This rewrite therefore eliminates the union error while keeping a substantive, self-contained claim about the physical object shared by the children.

extra.notations:
- CuO2 plane: the square-lattice layer composed of Cu d_{x^2-y^2} orbitals hybridized with O p orbitals.
      - Charged domain line / stripe: a one-dimensional linear defect in the CuO2 plane that hosts excess hole charge.

title: Topotactic CaH2 reduction converts Nd perovskite Ni oxides to infinite-layer NdNiO2
content: Low-temperature topotactic reduction of the neodymium perovskite oxide NdNiO3 (perovskite precursor) by treatment with solid calcium hydride (CaH2) powder effects oxygen deintercalation (removal of lattice oxygen) under soft-chemistry conditions and converts the perovskite structure into the infinite-layer nickelate NdNiO2. Here NdNiO3 denotes the perovskite precursor containing neodymium and NdNiO2 denotes the resulting infinite-layer phase obtained by topochemical oxygen removal induced by CaH2.

extra.notations:
- NdNiO3: Neodymium nickelate in the perovskite structure (chemical precursor). 
- NdNiO2: Neodymium infinite-layer nickelate phase produced by topochemical oxygen removal from NdNiO3.
- CaH2 (calcium hydride): solid reducing agent used in low-temperature topotactic reductions to induce oxygen deintercalation.
- Oxygen deintercalation: removal of lattice oxygen (topochemical oxygen loss) during the perovskite → infinite-layer conversion.
- Low-temperature/topotactic reduction: a soft-chemistry, solid-state reduction performed at relatively low temperatures that preserves the cation lattice while removing oxygen.

title: Broad non-magnetic DHO component in YBa2Cu3O3O6.92
content: In the yttrium-barium-copper-oxide compound YBa2Cu3O6.92, the non-magnetic (nuclear) part of the inelastic neutron-scattering spectrum contains a broad spectral response that spans the measured energy range; the lineshape of this broad, non-magnetic response is well described by a damped harmonic-oscillator (DHO) spectral function. This statement does not assert specific numerical values for the DHO parameters, only that a DHO parameterization provides an appropriate phenomenological description of the broad nuclear scattering contribution.

extra.notations:
- DHO: "damped harmonic oscillator", a phenomenological spectral function used to describe a broadened oscillator-like excitation; it is commonly parameterized by a characteristic energy $\hbar\omega_{0}$ and a linewidth (damping) $\Gamma_{\omega}$. 
- "Non-magnetic (nuclear) component": the part of the measured neutron-scattering intensity not arising from magnetic excitations.
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: yttrium barium copper oxide with oxygen stoichiometry 6.92 (a near-optimally doped cuprate).

title: d_{x2-y2} sign change ⇒ intrinsic π phase difference
content: In a crystalline superconductor, a superconducting pairing state of d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} symmetry is defined by an order parameter whose value changes sign under a 90° rotation that interchanges the two orthogonal in-plane crystallographic axes labeled here as "a" and "b". Denote by Δ(k_x,k_y) the complex superconducting order-parameter amplitude as a function of in-plane wavevector components k_x and k_y, and denote by δ_{ab} the intrinsic phase difference between order-parameter values sampled along directions parallel to the crystal a and b axes. For d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} symmetry the sign change under a 90° rotation implies that the complex phases of the order-parameter amplitudes sampled along the a and b directions differ by π; equivalently, the intrinsic phase shift between those directions is δ_{ab} = π.

extra.notations:
- Δ(k_x,k_y): complex superconducting order-parameter amplitude as a function of in-plane wavevector components k_x and k_y.
- δ_{ab}: intrinsic phase difference between order-parameter values sampled along the crystal a and b directions (two orthogonal in-plane axes).
- d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}: pairing symmetry whose order parameter changes sign under a 90° rotation that interchanges the a and b axes.

title: Existence of superconductors with T_c ≈ 26 K
content: Some materials exhibit superconducting transition temperatures of order 20–30 kelvin (K); here $T_{c}$ denotes the superconducting transition temperature (the temperature below which a material shows dissipationless electrical conduction). In particular, experimentally observed superconducting transition temperatures on the order of $26\,$K have been reported.

extra.notations: - $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature, measured in kelvin (K), defined as the temperature below which a material shows zero (or negligible) electrical resistance in standard bulk measurements.

title: High-pressure thermodynamic favorability of hydrogen-rich yttrium hydrides
content: Under static pressures on the order of several hundred gigapascals (GPa), theoretical and/or thermodynamic analyses indicate that hydrogen-rich yttrium hydrides — i.e., yttrium hydride compounds with very high hydrogen-to-yttrium ratios (examples include compositions with H:Y ≈ 9–10 such as YH9 and YH10) — form thermodynamically favored crystalline phases. Here "thermodynamically favored" means that, under the specified high static pressure and relevant temperature conditions, these hydrogen-rich stoichiometries lie on or near the convex hull of formation enthalpy (are predicted or found to be stable or the most hydrogen-rich stable compositions).

extra.notations:
- YH_n: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium stoichiometry n (e.g., YH9 has nine H atoms per Y). 
- GPa: gigapascal, unit of pressure (1 GPa = 10^9 pascals); "several hundred GPa" denotes static pressures on the order of 10^2–10^3 GPa. 
- "Thermodynamically favored": a phase/stoichiometry predicted or determined to be stable (on the convex hull of formation enthalpy) under the stated pressure–temperature conditions.
- fcc: face-centered cubic crystal structure; P6_3/mmc: a hexagonal close-packed space-group designation.

title: Fluorine-doped LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs is a layered iron-based superconductor
content: The compound LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs (lanthanum iron oxyarsenide with a fraction x of oxygen sites substituted by fluorine) is a layered iron-based material that becomes superconducting upon fluorine doping.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent included the numeric claim "T_c ≈ 26 K", which is not entailed by child 715 (that child only asserts T_c ≥ 26 K). To ensure the parent is entailed by every child, I removed the specific numeric characterization of the transition temperature and retained only the intersectional factual content: that LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs is a layered iron-based compound that becomes superconducting upon fluorine doping. This revised parent is entailed by all three children and avoids the previous union error caused by the temperature specificity.

extra.notations:
- LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs: lanthanum iron oxyarsenide with fluorine substituting oxygen at fraction x
      - x: fraction of oxygen sites replaced by fluorine

title: SO(5) generators and microscopic ingredients common to the cited formulations
content: In the SO(5) framework applied to cuprate high-temperature superconductors, the so(5) Lie algebra describing the low-energy AF/SC manifold contains generators that can be identified with the total charge operator $\mathcal{Q}$, the components of total spin $S_\alpha$ (with $\alpha$ labeling spin directions), and six operators denoted $\pi_\alpha$ and $\pi_\alpha^\dagger$ (one complex triplet) that convert or rotate antiferromagnetic order parameters into d-wave superconducting order parameters and vice versa. At the microscopic/operator level, constructions of these $\pi$ operators are built from electronic creation and annihilation operators $c_{p\sigma}^\dagger$ and $c_{p\sigma}$ (momentum $p$, spin $\sigma$), a d-wave pairing form factor $g(p)$ (a momentum-space form factor that changes sign under 90° rotation and is used to build d-wave pairing amplitudes), and the antiferromagnetic ordering wavevector $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$. This statement captures the common, independently supported content of the listed propositions without adding detailed index assignments or particular operator-ordering conventions that appear only in some sources.

extra.notations:
- $c_{p\sigma}^\dagger, c_{p\sigma}$: electron creation and annihilation operators with momentum $p$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $g(p)$: a d-wave pairing form factor (momentum-dependent factor used to construct d-wave pairing); specific analytic forms (e.g., $g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y$) are omitted because not all sources state them identically.
- $Q$: the antiferromagnetic ordering wavevector, $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$.
- $\pi_\alpha,\pi_\alpha^\dagger$: three-component operators (spin triplet) and their Hermitian adjoints that act to transform AF order into d-wave SC order and vice versa.

title: Crystal structure of La[O$_{1-x}$F$_x$]FeAs (including LaFeAsO)
content: La[O$_{1-x}$F$_x$]FeAs (with LaFeAsO the $x=0$ member) crystallizes in a tetragonal P4/nmm-type crystal structure that is described by two lattice parameters: $a$, the in-plane (basal-plane) tetragonal lattice constant, and $c$, the out-of-plane (stacking-direction) tetragonal lattice constant.

refine_history.reasoning: The verification found a union error: the original parent asserted that $x$ denotes F$^{-}$ substituting for O$^{2-}$ (and related family-level substitutional chemistry), but Child 1565 only describes the undoped $x=0$ member and does not support the substitutional interpretation. To ensure every child (including 1565) entails the parent, I removed claims about the substitution chemistry and electron doping and retained only the structural claims common to all children: that the LaOFeAs / La[O$_{1-x}$F$_x$] family adopts a tetragonal P4/nmm-type lattice characterized by two lattice constants $a$ and $c$, and that $x$ is the compositional variable with $x=0$ corresponding to LaFeAsO. This rewrite keeps the proposition self-contained and substantive while ensuring it is individually entailed by each child.

extra.notations:
- La[O$_{1-x}$F$_x$]FeAs: lanthanum oxyfluoride iron arsenide family (composition variable $x$, with $x=0$ = LaFeAsO)
      - $a$: in-plane (basal-plane) tetragonal lattice constant (length)
      - $c$: out-of-plane (stacking direction) tetragonal lattice constant (length)
      - $P4/nmm$: a tetragonal crystallographic space-group designation

title: Magnetic spectral weight in YBa2Cu3O6.92 lies in the multi-tens-of-meV range
content: In the superconductor YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature (a few kelvin), the magnetic (spin) excitation spectrum contains magnetic spectral weight in the multi–tens-of-millielectronvolt energy range, including the region near 41 meV.

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent combined two different claims from the children: (a) a resonance-specific claim (narrow, energy-localized peak) present in child 1508, and (b) a broader spectral-weight / energy-scale claim present in child 1509. Child 1509 does not assert the existence of narrow resonance peaks, so the resonance language caused a union error. To ensure every child individually entails the parent, I removed the resonance/narrowness assertion and retained only the intersectional facts: low temperature, magnetic (spin) channel, and magnetic spectral weight located in the multi–tens-of-meV range (which includes ≈41 meV). The revised proposition is self-contained, factual, and is supported by both child 1508 (peak near 41 meV) and child 1509 (dominant magnetic weight in the ≈25–45 meV window).

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: yttrium–barium–copper oxide with oxygen content per formula unit ≈6.92
      - "magnetic (spin) excitation spectrum"/"magnetic spectral weight": intensity in the magnetic part of the dynamical structure factor S(Q,ℏω) or Im{χ(q,ℏω)} attributable to spin fluctuations
      - meV: millielectronvolt, 1 meV = 1×10⁻³ eV

title: Measurable energy-localized magnetic features in the superconducting state
content: In the superconducting state of YBa2Cu3O6.92 (low temperature, T on the order of a few kelvin), inelastic neutron scattering detects measurable energy-localized spectral features in the multi–tens-of-meV energy range. At least one of these detectable features is a magnetic (spin) excitation; other measurable components (magnetic or non-magnetic) can also occur elsewhere within the same few-10-meV energy window.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: yttrium–barium–copper oxide, oxygen ≈6.92
- "inelastic neutron scattering": measurement of scattering as a function of energy transfer ℏω
- "multi–tens-of-meV": energy scale of order 10–50 meV

title: Energy-localized magnetic excitations in YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature
content: In YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature, the magnetic (spin) component of the neutron-scattering response contains energy-localized resonant magnetic excitations (pronounced peaks in the magnetic part of the dynamical response).

refine_history.reasoning: The original parent included claims of momentum (Q) localization near (1/2,1/2,l) and empirical separability from nuclear background, and it also implied a specific resonance energy. Child 1592 supports those Q-localization and separability claims, but child 1508 does not mention Q-localization or separability and gives a specific energy value that is not stated in child 1592. To ensure the parent is entailed by every child, I removed the momentum-localization/separability claims and the numerical energy value, leaving only the intersection: that at low temperature the magnetic part of the neutron-scattering response in YBa2Cu3O6.92 contains energy-localized resonant magnetic excitations. This revised proposition is self-contained, factual, and is supported individually by both children.

extra.notations:
- $\hbar\omega$: energy transfer (reduced Planck constant times frequency)
      - "magnetic (spin) component": the part of the neutron-scattering signal arising from spin fluctuations (as distinct from nuclear or phonon scattering)

title: Magnetic spin excitations detected in YBa2Cu3O6.92 by inelastic neutron scattering
content: In superconducting YBa2Cu3O6.92, inelastic neutron scattering measurements detect magnetic (spin) excitations as a component of the measured spectral response.

refine_history.reasoning: Verification showed a union error: the original parent combined two claims that appear separately in the children (A: decomposability of the total intensity into separable magnetic vs nuclear/non‑magnetic channels; B: a pronounced ≈41 meV feature lies in the magnetic channel). Child 1508 supports the existence of a pronounced ≈41 meV magnetic resonance but does not assert full decomposition of the total intensity; Child 1593 supports the presence and characterization of nuclear/non‑magnetic contributions and the distribution of magnetic spectral weight but does not mention the ≈41 meV resonance. To make the parent entailed by every child, I removed the decomposability claim and the specific ≈41 meV claim, retaining only the intersection: both children assert that in YBa2Cu3O6.92 the neutron-scattering response includes magnetic (spin) excitations. The revised parent is self-contained, factual, and is supported individually by each child.

extra.notations: - "magnetic (spin) excitations": contributions to the neutron-scattering signal arising from electronic spin fluctuations.

title: Presence of an energy-localized magnetic excitation in the spectrum
content: In YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature, the measured neutron-scattering spectrum contains at least one spectrally narrow, energy-localized excitation in the magnetic channel (a resonance-like peak); this narrow feature coexists with other, less sharply defined spectral contributions in the overall spectrum.

extra.notations:
- "spectrally narrow": confined to a relatively small energy interval compared with broad background
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: yttrium–barium–copper oxide with oxygen ≈6.92

title: Measurable 20–30 meV inelastic scattering with magnetic weight near 25–30 meV in YBa2Cu3O6.92
