is temperature independent (to leading power-law order) because the prefactors scale as $T\\times T^{1/2}/T^{3/2}=\\mathrm{const}$.

extra.notations:
- $\\rho_{cc}(\\omega)$: single-particle density of states in the CC phase.
- $\\rho(0)$: normal-state density of states at the Fermi level (reference value).
- $D$: characteristic CC energy scale (gap amplitude).
- $f(\\omega,T)$: Fermi-Dirac distribution at temperature $T$.
- $C_v(T)$: electronic specific heat at temperature $T$.
- $\\chi(T)$: electronic (Pauli) magnetic susceptibility at temperature $T$.
- $k_B$: Boltzmann constant.

title: Estimated CC energy scale implies high characteristic temperature
content:
Proposition 977 estimates the CC gap amplitude scale $D_0$ to be of order 20 meV (this $D_0$ plays the role of the characteristic energy $D$ appearing in the DOS). Converting this energy to a temperature scale via $T_D\\equiv D_0/k_B$ gives
$$T_D\\approx\\frac{20\\ \\mathrm{meV}}{k_B}\\approx 230\\ \\mathrm{K}.$$
Therefore, if the mean-field estimate $D_0\\sim 20\\,$meV maps directly to the thermal crossover scale $T_p(x)$ that controls validity of the low-$T$ power laws, the CC-related suppression of the density of states and the associated thermodynamic signatures should be observable up to temperatures of order a few $10^2\\,$K (i.e., well above typical superconducting transition temperatures in the cuprates).

extra.notations:
- $D_0$: estimated CC energy amplitude from Proposition 977 (≈ 20 meV).
- $T_D$: characteristic temperature scale defined by $T_D=D_0/k_B$.
- $k_B$: Boltzmann constant.
- $T_p(x)$: crossover/pseudogap temperature scale referenced in the parent node.

title: Existence of an F-doped composition with Tc ≈ 26 K (onset > 30 K)
content: There exists a composition of fluorine-doped lanthanum iron oxyarsenide, written LaFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}, with doping concentration x ≈ 0.11 (≈11 atom %) for which the superconducting transition temperature reaches approximately T_{c} = 26 K, while the resistive onset temperature of the transition satisfies T_{onset} > 30 K. Here T_{c} denotes the characteristic transition temperature reported as the maximum (bulk) transition in the studied doping series and T_{onset} denotes the temperature where the resistivity first departs from the normal-state behaviour on cooling.

extra.notations:
- LaFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}: Fluorine-doped lanthanum iron oxyarsenide with F substituting for O at fraction x.
- x: F doping fraction (dimensionless, e.g., x ≈ 0.11).
- T_{c}: Superconducting transition temperature (bulk/maximum value reported).
- T_{onset}: Temperature at which superconducting onset is observed in resistivity.

title: Broad transition or inhomogeneous/filamentary superconductivity indicated by T_onset > T_c
content: For the F-doped LaFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} composition with reported T_{onset} > 30 K and bulk T_{c} ≈ 26 K, the difference ΔT = T_{onset} - T_{c} exceeds about 4 K. This substantial ΔT plausibly indicates either (a) sample inhomogeneity in doping or phase (regions with higher local T_{c}), (b) filamentary/percolative superconductivity in a minority phase that produces an early resistive onset, or (c) strong superconducting fluctuations above the bulk transition temperature. These explanations are not mutually exclusive.

extra.notations:
- ΔT: Transition width defined as ΔT = T_{onset} - T_{c}.
- T_{c}, T_{onset}: As defined in the deduction above.

title: Tc of some Tl–Ba–Cu–O phases exceeds 77 K
content: At least some bulk samples in the thallium–barium–copper–oxide (Tl–Ba–Cu–O) system have superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ greater than $77\,$K (liquid-nitrogen boiling point), because those samples show zero electrical resistance at temperatures described as "just above $77\,$K".

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature, defined as the highest temperature below which the material exhibits zero electrical resistance (bulk superconductivity).
- Tl–Ba–Cu–O: thallium–barium–copper–oxide compositions.

title: Calcium-containing Tl–Ba–Ca–Cu–O phases likely retain high $T_{c}$ and show more reproducible superconductivity
content: Tl–Ba–Ca–Cu–O phases (thallium–barium–calcium–copper–oxide) are likely to exhibit superconducting critical temperatures $T_{c}$ in the same high range as the related Tl–Ba–Cu–O phases (i.e., at or above $\sim77\,$K) and to show more reproducible bulk superconducting behavior under typical sample preparation, crystal-growth, and annealing conditions because calcium is reported to stabilize the superconducting phases.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (as above).
- "Reproducible superconducting behavior": consistent observation of the superconducting transition and zero resistance across nominally similar samples produced under typical processing/annealing.
- Tl–Ba–Ca–Cu–O: thallium–barium–calcium–copper–oxide compositions (calcium-containing members of the Tl–Ba–Cu–O family).

title: At least two distinct spectral components present, one spectrally narrow
content: Let $Y$ denote YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6+x}$ and let $\hbar\omega$ denote the energy transfer in inelastic neutron scattering. Proposition 1593 asserts that the measured dynamical response of $Y$ contains a measurable nuclear (non-magnetic) contribution distinct from magnetic spin-excitation scattering (therefore both a nuclear and a magnetic component are present). Proposition 1594 asserts that the dynamical response shows at least one energy-localized (spectrally narrow) peak within the studied energy window. Combining these statements implies that the inelastic neutron-scattering intensity of $Y$ contains at least two distinct spectral components (a nuclear/non-magnetic component and a magnetic spin-excitation component), and that at least one of those components is spectrally narrow (energy-localized).

extra.notations:
- $Y$: YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6+x}$ (yttrium barium copper oxide with oxygen content $6+x$)
- $\hbar\omega$: energy transfer in inelastic neutron scattering
- nuclear (non-magnetic) component: scattering intensity arising from nuclear/phonon or other non-spin degrees of freedom
- magnetic spin-excitation component: scattering intensity arising from spin degrees of freedom
- spectrally narrow / energy-localized peak: a feature whose energy width is small compared with the overall inspected energy window

title: The spectrally narrow peak is likely a magnetic spin-excitation
content: In YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6+x}$, the observed energy-localized (spectrally narrow) peak in the dynamical response is plausibly attributable to a magnetic spin-excitation (rather than to a nuclear/phonon mode). Here, "spectrally narrow" means an energy feature whose full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) is small compared with the studied energy window, and "magnetic spin-excitation" means a collective excitation of the spin system that contributes to the magnetic scattering cross section.

extra.notations:
- $Y$: YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6+x}$
- spectrally narrow: small energy width relative to window
- magnetic spin-excitation: spin-origin contribution to neutron scattering intensity

title: Algebraic equivalence of the two forms of $V_{0}$
content:
The zero-temperature phase stiffness $V_{0}$ expressed in particle-density form,
$$
V_{0}=\frac{\hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)a}{4m^{*}},
$$
and in electromagnetic-penetration-depth form,
$$
V_{0}=\frac{(\hbar c)^{2}a}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)},
$$
are mathematically identical provided the identity
$$
4\pi n_{s}(0)=m^{*}\Big(\frac{c}{e\lambda(0)}\Big)^{2}
$$
holds. Here $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant, $n_{s}(0)$ is the zero-temperature superfluid density, $a$ is the short-distance cutoff length, $m^{*}$ is the effective electron mass, $c$ is the speed of light, $e$ is the elementary charge, and $\lambda(0)$ is the London penetration depth at zero temperature.

extra.notations:
- $\hbar$: reduced Planck constant
- $n_{s}(0)$: zero-temperature superfluid density
- $a$: short-distance spatial cutoff (linear dimension)
- $m^{*}$: effective electron mass
- $c$: speed of light
- $e$: elementary charge
- $\lambda(0)$: penetration depth at $T=0$
- $V_{0}$: zero-temperature phase stiffness energy scale

title: Expression for $T_{\theta}^{max}$ when interlayer spacing dominates
content:
For a quasi-two-dimensional superconductor in which the average layer spacing $d$ exceeds the coherence-related cutoff (i.e. $d>\sqrt{\pi\,\xi_{\perp}}$ so $a=d$), the maximum phase-ordering temperature is
$$
T_{\theta}^{max}=A\,V_{0}=A\,\frac{(\hbar c)^{2}d}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)},
$$
where $A$ is a dimensionless constant of order unity, $\xi_{\perp}$ is the superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers, and other symbols are as defined above.

extra.notations:
- $d$: average interlayer spacing
- $\xi_{\perp}$: coherence length perpendicular to layers
- $a$: short-distance cutoff (here $a=d$)
- $A$: dimensionless constant (order unity)
- other symbols as in the previous result

title: Order-unity ambiguity in the $\xi_{\perp}$-based cutoff produces order-unity uncertainty in $V_{0}$ and $T_{\theta}^{max}$
content:
If the short-distance cutoff is chosen on the basis of the perpendicular coherence length as $a=C\,\xi_{\perp}$ (or more generally $a=C'\,\xi_{\perp}$ with $C$ or $C'$ an unknown factor of order unity reflecting different source conventions), then the computed zero-temperature phase stiffness and the derived maximum phase-ordering temperature carry the same multiplicative uncertainty:
$$
V_{0}\propto a \quad\Rightarrow\quad V_{0}\ \text{uncertain by factor }\sim C,
$$
and
$$
T_{\theta}^{max}=A\,V_{0}\ \text{uncertain by the same factor } \sim C.
$$
Here $C$ is an unknown order-unity dimensionless coefficient encapsulating the conventional ambiguity in defining the $\xi_{\perp}$-based cutoff; other symbols are as previously defined.

extra.notations:
- $C$: order-unity dimensionless coefficient parameterizing ambiguity in the $\xi_{\perp}$-based cutoff
- other symbols as in previous results

title: The ≈20 meV spectral feature is predominantly non-magnetic (nuclear) in origin
content: The spectral peak centered near $\hbar\omega\approx 20\ \mathrm{meV}$ (or $\approx 21.5\ \mathrm{meV}$ in one report) corresponds to a contribution to the measured inelastic-scattering intensity arising from nuclear (non-magnetic) scattering, i.e. it is not predominantly magnetic in origin. Here $\hbar\omega$ is the energy transfer and "nuclear" means scattering arising from nuclear (non-spin) processes rather than from electronic spin excitations.

extra.notations:
- $\hbar\omega$: energy transfer in inelastic neutron scattering (meV)
- "nuclear": non-magnetic (nuclear scattering) component of neutron scattering

title: Measured inelastic response contains at least two spectrally distinct components of different origin
content: The inelastic-scattering response in YBa2Cu3O6.92 within the investigated energy range includes (at minimum) a spectrally narrow, predominantly magnetic resonance near $\hbar\omega\approx 41\ \mathrm{meV}$ and a separate, broader, predominantly nuclear spectral component peaked near $\hbar\omega\approx 20\ \mathrm{meV}$ (together with an energy-independent background and a broad continuous response). Symbols: $\hbar\omega$ is energy transfer.

extra.notations:
- $\hbar\omega$: energy transfer in inelastic neutron scattering (meV)
- "magnetic resonance": spectrally narrow spin-excitation feature
- "nuclear spectral component": non-magnetic contribution to scattering intensity

title: Narrow q-width at the 41 meV feature implies enhanced in-plane magnetic correlation length at that energy
content: The reduced in-plane q-width (full width) of $\Delta q \approx 0.18\pm0.02$ r.l.u. observed at the $\hbar\omega\approx 41\ \mathrm{meV}$ resonance indicates a longer real-space in-plane magnetic correlation length at that energy than the average correlation length inferred from broader features (average $\Delta q\approx 0.27\pm0.02$ r.l.u.). Here "r.l.u." denotes reciprocal-lattice units; the correlation length $\xi$ (in real-space units) is inversely related to the momentum-space width $\Delta q$ (roughly $\xi\sim 1/\Delta q$ up to geometry-dependent factors).

extra.notations:
- $\Delta q$: measured momentum-space full width of the spectral feature (r.l.u.)
- r.l.u.: reciprocal-lattice unit
- $\xi$: real-space correlation length (qualitative, proportional to $1/\Delta q$)

title: L-dependence (intensity modulation along (001)) signals bilayer-related (odd/even) character of the 41 meV resonance
content: The reported intensity modulation of the $\hbar\omega\approx 41\ \mathrm{meV}$ peak along the (0,0,L) direction (crystallographic $c$-axis) likely reflects the bilayer structure of YBa2Cu3O6.92 and implies that the resonance has a particular interlayer (odd or even) symmetry and is sensitive to interlayer coupling. "L" is the reciprocal-lattice coordinate along the $c$-axis.

extra.notations:
- (001) / L: scattering vector component along the crystallographic $c$-axis (reciprocal-lattice coordinate)
- "odd/even" character: symmetry of spin excitations under exchange of the two CuO2 layers in a bilayer

title: Numerical decomposition of total electron–phonon coupling into H and non-H parts
content:
Let $\lambda$ be the total (integrated) electron–phonon coupling parameter, and let $\lambda_{H}$ denote the contribution to $\lambda$ arising from hydrogen vibrational modes and $\lambda_{\neg H}$ the remainder (non-hydrogen contribution, e.g. sulfur). From Proposition 408 the fractions of $\lambda$ due to H vibrations are 82.6% for the Im-3m phase and 66.9% for the R3m phase. Using the total $\lambda$ values reported in Proposition 404, the following exact arithmetic follows:
- Im-3m at 200 GPa: $\lambda=2.19$, hence $\lambda_{H}=0.826\times 2.19\approx 1.81$ and $\lambda_{\neg H}=2.19-1.81\approx 0.38$.
- R3m at 130 GPa: $\lambda=2.07$, hence $\lambda_{H}=0.669\times 2.07\approx 1.39$ and $\lambda_{\neg H}=2.07-1.39\approx 0.68$.
These values necessarily follow from the reported percentages and total $\lambda$.

extra.notations:
- $\lambda$: total integrated electron–phonon coupling parameter (dimensionless).
- $\lambda_{H}$: portion of $\lambda$ arising from hydrogen vibrational modes (dimensionless).
- $\lambda_{\neg H}$: remainder of $\lambda$ arising from non-hydrogen modes (dimensionless).
- Im-3m, R3m: crystallographic phases (as in the propositions).

title: Large hydrogen isotope effect expected on T_c
content: If superconductivity in the Im-3m (200 GPa) and R3m (130 GPa) phases is phonon-mediated and hydrogen vibrations supply the majority of the electron–phonon coupling ($\lambda_{H}\gtrsim 1.4$ as above), then substituting hydrogen (H) by its heavier isotope deuterium (D) is expected to produce a substantial reduction in the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$. Under the standard harmonic-phonon approximation the characteristic phonon frequencies scale as $M^{-1/2}$ (mass $M$ of the vibrating atom); for H$\to$D (mass roughly doubled) this gives a frequency scale factor $\approx 1/\sqrt{2}\approx 0.707$. Because $\omega_{log}$ enters roughly linearly in the prefactor of Allen–Dynes-type formulas and hydrogen modes dominate the pairing interaction, a large isotope-induced downshift of $T_{c}$ is plausible unless countervailing changes in $\lambda$ or the electronic structure occur.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (K).
- $\omega_{log}$: logarithmic average phonon frequency (K).
- $\lambda_{H}$: contribution to $\lambda$ from hydrogen vibrational modes.
- H$\to$D: isotopic substitution of hydrogen by deuterium.

title: Scattering below 25 meV is non-magnetic in origin
content: In superconducting YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature (T = 5 K), any measurable scattering intensity in the energy-transfer window ℏω ∈ [20,25] meV is necessarily from a non-magnetic (nuclear/phonon-like) broad response rather than from spin excitations. Here ℏω is the neutron energy transfer. This follows because the spin (magnetic) spectrum is gapped with gap energy E_G = 28 ± 1 meV and shows no sizeable magnetic scattering below 25 meV, while a broad, ungapped nuclear-scattering component centered at ℏω = 21.5 meV with damping Γ = 26 meV is present in the superconducting state.

extra.notations:
- ℏω: energy transfer (reduced Planck constant times angular frequency)
- E_G: energy gap of spin excitations
- Γ: damping (width) of the damped-harmonic-oscillator (DHO) nuclear response

title: Broad nuclear component likely overlaps magnetic threshold near 28 meV
content: The broad nuclear (DHO) response centered at ℏω = 21.5 meV with damping Γ = 26 meV likely has spectral tails that extend into the 25–30 meV range and therefore plausibly contributes measurable spectral weight at or near the magnetic excitation threshold (E_G ≈ 28 meV). Consequently, intensity measured around ℏω ≈ 28 meV may be a superposition of the onset of magnetic excitations and the high-energy tail of the nuclear response unless the two channels are separated (for example by polarization analysis).

extra.notations:
- ℏω: energy transfer
- E_G = 28 ± 1 meV: magnetic gap energy from spin excitations
- Γ = 26 meV: damping (broadening) parameter of the nuclear DHO response
- DHO: damped harmonic oscillator spectral component

title: Quantified lattice-parameter and unit-cell volume contraction at $x=0.1$
content:
Using the lattice parameters reported for the undoped and $x=0.1$ samples, the fluorine-doped sample $Sm[O_{0.9}F_{0.1}]FeAs$ has reduced basal and axial lattice parameters and a reduced tetragonal unit-cell volume. Numerically:
- Undoped (from Prop. 1430): $a_{0}=3.933(5)\,$\AA, $c_{0}=8.495(4)\,$\AA.
- Doped $x=0.1$ (from Prop. 1430): $a_{0.1}=3.915(4)\,$\AA, $c_{0.1}=8.428(7)\,$\AA.
Define the tetragonal unit-cell volume $V=a^{2}c$. Then
- $\Delta a = a_{0.1}-a_{0} = -0.018\,$\AA,
- $\Delta c = c_{0.1}-c_{0} = -0.067\,$\AA,
- $V_{0}=a_{0}^{2}c_{0}\approx 131.405\,$\AA$^{3}$,
- $V_{0.1}=a_{0.1}^{2}c_{0.1}\approx 129.178\,$\AA$^{3}$,
- $\Delta V = V_{0.1}-V_{0}\approx -2.227\,$\AA$^{3}$, i.e. a volume decrease of $\approx 1.70\%$.
All numerical statements follow directly from the reported lattice parameters and the definition $V=a^{2}c$.

extra.notations:
- $a$: tetragonal basal-plane lattice parameter (in \AA).
- $c$: tetragonal axial lattice parameter (in \AA).
- $V$: tetragonal unit-cell volume, $V=a^{2}c$ (in \AA$^{3}$).
- $\Delta a,\Delta c,\Delta V$: changes upon doping ($x=0.1$) relative to undoped.

title: Observed lattice shrinkage likely reflects combined electron doping and chemical-pressure effects that favor superconductivity
content: The measured contraction of both $a$ and $c$ on going to nominal $x=0.1$ ($\Delta a\approx -0.018\,$\AA, $\Delta c\approx -0.067\,$\AA, $\Delta V\approx -1.70\%$) plausibly reflects (i) substitution of the smaller fluorine for oxygen (a smaller covalent/ionic radius) and (ii) concurrent electron doping by F$^{-}$ substituting for O$^{2-}$. These two effects combine to produce a modest chemical (internal) pressure and carrier concentration change that, in iron pnictides, is commonly associated with the emergence or enhancement of superconductivity. Therefore it is likely that the observed lattice contraction at $x\approx0.1$ contributes to the superconducting behavior of the doped sample via lattice-compression-induced modifications of the FeAs electronic structure and by increasing carrier concentration.

extra.notations:
- F-for-O substitution: replacement of oxygen by fluorine at the anion site.
- "Chemical pressure": lattice compression produced by isovalent/heterovalent substitution (no external hydrostatic pressure).
- "Electron doping": increase in itinerant electron count due to heterovalent substitution (here O$^{2-}\rightarrow$F$^{-}$).

title: Sign-change form implies a π phase difference between orthogonal crystal directions
content:
If the superconducting order parameter in the $a$-$b$ plane has the form
$$\Delta(k_x,k_y)=\Delta_0\,[\cos(k_x a)-\cos(k_y a)],$$
then sampling the order parameter along wavevectors that lie purely along the $a$ direction versus purely along the $b$ direction gives values equal in magnitude and opposite in sign. Opposite sign of complex order-parameter values corresponds to a phase difference of exactly $\\pi$. Therefore the intrinsic phase shift between the $a$ and $b$ directions, $\\delta_{ab}$, is $\\pi$.

extra.notations:
- $\\Delta(k_x,k_y)$: superconducting order parameter as a function of in-plane wavevector components $k_x,k_y$.
- $\\Delta_0$: amplitude scale of the order parameter.
- $a$: in-plane lattice constant.
- $\\delta_{ab}$: intrinsic phase shift between order-parameter values sampled along orthogonal $a$ and $b$ directions.

title: Experimental corner-SQUID data strongly support d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} pairing in YBa2Cu3O6.8
content: The measured intercepts of the dynamic-resistance versus applied flux for seven corner dc SQUIDs on YBa2Cu3O6.8 lie in the range $0.3\\Phi_0$ to $0.6\\Phi_0$ and cluster near $\\Phi_0/2$, where $\\Phi_0=h/2e$ is the superconducting flux quantum. Given that the d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} pairing form $\\Delta(k_x,k_y)=\\Delta_0[\\cos(k_x a)-\\cos(k_y a)]$ implies an intrinsic phase shift $\\delta_{ab}=\\pi$ between orthogonal crystal faces, the experimental observation of an approximately $\\Phi_0/2$ phase offset is strong evidence that the superconducting order parameter in these YBa2Cu3O6.8 samples has predominantly d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} symmetry.

extra.notations:
- $\\Phi_0$: superconducting flux quantum ($h/2e$).
- $\\delta_{ab}$: intrinsic phase shift between orthogonal $a$ and $b$ directions.
- $\\Phi$: flux intercept observed in SQUID interference (reported as fraction of $\\Phi_0$).

title: MgB2 superconductivity is likely anisotropic / multi-band and requires band-selective strong electron–phonon coupling
content: Given that the Eliashberg framework in proposition 702 is written with momentum-dependent quantities $ \Delta(\vec{k},i\omega_n)$ and $ \lambda(\vec{k},\vec{k}',n-n')$ (i.e. explicitly allowing anisotropy / band dependence) and the Coulomb pseudopotential is fixed at $ \mu^*(\omega_c)=0.12$, and given the experimentally observed transition temperature $T_c=39\,$K from proposition 1321, it is plausible that reproducing $T_c=39\,$K requires substantially larger electron–phonon pairing strength on some parts of the Fermi surface than on others. In other words, a multi-band or strongly anisotropic Eliashberg solution — with one (or more) Fermi-surface sheets having significantly stronger effective electron–phonon coupling $ \lambda(\vec{k},\vec{k}')$ than others — is likely necessary to account for the high $T_c$ while using $ \mu^*=0.12$.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature.
- $ \Delta(\vec{k},i\omega_n)$: superconducting gap as a function of momentum and Matsubara frequency.
- $ \lambda(\vec{k},\vec{k}',n-n')$: momentum- and frequency-dependent electron–phonon interaction kernel.
- $ \mu^*(\omega_c)$: dimensionless Coulomb pseudopotential at cutoff frequency $ \omega_c$.

title: Significant phonon-mediated pairing is required if $ \mu^*(\omega_c)=0.12$ is used
content: If the Coulomb pseudopotential is fixed at $ \mu^*(\omega_c)=0.12$ in the Eliashberg equations (as stated in node 702) and the material exhibits $T_c=39\,$K (node 1321), then the electron–phonon spectral function (entering $ \lambda(\vec{k},\vec{k}',\omega)$) must provide sufficiently large net pairing strength (an appreciable electron–phonon coupling) so that the net attractive pairing overcomes the Coulomb repulsion and yields $T_c=39\,$K. Thus, it is likely that phonon-mediated interactions play a dominant role in producing the observed superconductivity under the stated modeling choice.

extra.notations:
- $ \mu^*(\omega_c)=0.12$: fixed dimensionless Coulomb pseudopotential.
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature.
- $ \lambda(\vec{k},\vec{k}',\omega)$: electron–phonon coupling spectral function/kernel.

title: Existence of a zero-voltage range up to milliampere currents below ~109 K
content: Combining the reported superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}\\approx 109\\,$K (Proposition 238) with the reported low-temperature critical current $I_{c}\\sim 3$–$4\\,$mA (Proposition 1528) implies that for temperatures $T<T_{c}\\approx 109\\,$K there exists a range of applied currents $0\\le I \\le I_{c}\\approx 3$–$4\\,$mA for which the measured voltage is (within experimental resolution) zero. Thus the dissipationless superconducting state persists up to currents of order a few milliampere at low temperature.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (K)  
- $T$: temperature (K)  
- $I_{c}$: superconducting critical current (A)  
- $I$: applied transport current (A)

title: Implied large volumetric current density for a monolayer if current flows through mm-scale width
content:
If the reported critical current $I_{c}\\sim 3$–$4\\,$mA flows through a continuous single FeSe monolayer of thickness $d\\approx 0.55\\,$nm (typical monolayer thickness for FeSe) and through a lateral width $W$ of order $10^{-4}$–$10^{-3}\\,$m (100\\,$\\mu$m to 1\\,mm, plausible probe/sample scales in 4PP transport), then the implied volumetric critical current density
$$j_{c}=\\dfrac{I_{c}}{W\\,d}$$
lies roughly in the range $5\\times10^{9}$ to $5\\times10^{10}\\,$A/m$^{2}$ (equivalently $5\\times10^{5}$ to $5\\times10^{6}\\,$A/cm$^{2}$). The sheet (per-width) critical current $K_{c}=I_{c}/W$ would lie in the range $3$–$40\\,$A/m for the assumed widths.

extra.notations:
- $I_{c}$: critical current (A)  
- $d$: monolayer thickness (m), assumed $d\\approx 0.55\\,$nm $=5.5\\times10^{-10}\\,$m  
- $W$: lateral width over which current flows (m)  
- $j_{c}$: volumetric critical current density (A/m$^{2}$)  
- $K_{c}$: sheet critical current per unit width (A/m)

title: Unit-norm enforces quantitative trade-off between AF and SC components
content:
Let $n_a=(n_1,n_2,n_3,n_4,n_5)$ be the five-component superspin with fixed magnitude $\sum_{a=1}^5 n_a^2=1$. Denote the superconducting (SC) subvector magnitude by $n_{\mathrm{SC}}^2\equiv n_1^2+n_5^2$ and the antiferromagnetic (AF) subvector magnitude by $n_{\mathrm{AF}}^2\equiv n_2^2+n_3^2+n_4^2$. Then the following identity holds:
$$n_{\mathrm{SC}}^2 = 1 - n_{\mathrm{AF}}^2.$$
Therefore any increase (decrease) in the magnitude of the AF order $n_{\mathrm{AF}}$ necessarily implies a decrease (increase) in the magnitude of the SC order $n_{\mathrm{SC}}$, and vice versa; in particular a purely AF state has $n_{\mathrm{SC}}=0$ and a purely SC state has $n_{\mathrm{AF}}=0$.

extra.notations:
- $n_a$: five-component superspin (unit vector), $a=1,\dots,5$. 
- $n_{\mathrm{SC}}$: magnitude of SC components, $n_{\mathrm{SC}}^2\equiv n_1^2+n_5^2$. 
- $n_{\mathrm{AF}}$: magnitude of AF components, $n_{\mathrm{AF}}^2\equiv n_2^2+n_3^2+n_4^2$.

title: SO(5) rotations imply generators that mix particle-hole and particle-particle channels (the ``π'' operators)
content: Given (i) AF components $n_{2,3,4}$ are expressible as particle–hole bilinears at the staggered wavevector $Q$ [fermionic form $n_{a+1}=\sum_{p,\alpha,\beta} c_{p+Q,\alpha}^\dagger\sigma^a_{\alpha\beta}c_{p,\beta}$] and (ii) SC components $n_1,n_5$ correspond to the two real components of a particle–particle (pair) condensate $\Delta$, it is plausible that the SO(5) algebra contains generators that rotate AF components into SC components. These generators must be two-fermion operators which convert a particle–hole bilinear into a particle–particle bilinear (and vice versa); in the SO(5) literature such operators are commonly denoted "π operators". Concretely, one expects operators $ \pi^\dagger_\alpha \sim \sum_p g(p)\, c_{p+Q,\alpha}^\dagger c_{-p,\beta}^\dagger (\cdots)$ (schematic) that carry the appropriate spin and momentum quantum numbers to map AF order into SC order.

extra.notations:
- $c_{p\sigma}^{(\dagger)}$: electron annihilation (creation) operator with momentum $p$ and spin $\sigma$. 
- $\Delta$: complex superconducting pair field, with real components $n_1,n_5$. 
- $Q$: AF ordering wavevector (e.g. $(\pi,\pi,\pi)$). 
- $\pi$ (schematic): generator that mixes AF and SC sectors (two-fermion operator).

title: Both siblings describe the same cubic LaH10-type phase at high pressure
content: Both propositions assert that, at pressures in the range 150–170 GPa, the observed phase has face-centred cubic symmetry with space group $Fm\\overline{3}m$ and a stoichiometry close to LaH$_{10}$. Therefore they necessarily refer to the same crystalline phase: the cubic (Fm-3m) LaH$_{10}$-type phase observed in lanthanum hydrides at these pressures. This identification is independent of the precise numerical stoichiometry within the stated uncertainties.

extra.notations:
- LaH$_{10}$: Lanthanum decahydride (ten H per La)  
- $Fm\\overline{3}m$: Face-centred cubic space group notation (cubic symmetry)  
- GPa: Gigapascal, unit of pressure

title: Per-formula-unit volume implied by the reported cubic cell
content: Given the reported cubic cell volume $V_{cell}=132.80(4)$ Å$^3$ for the $Fm\\overline{3}m$ unit cell and that the conventional fcc ($Fm\\overline{3}m$) cell contains four La atoms (i.e., four LaH$_{x}$ formula units), the volume per formula unit is $$V_{fu}=V_{cell}/4=33.20(1)\\ \\text{Å}^3\\ \\text{per LaH}_{x}\\text{.}$$ If $x\\approx10$, this is the volume per LaH$_{10}$ formula unit under the reported conditions.

extra.notations:
- $V_{cell}$: reported conventional cubic unit-cell volume (Å$^3$)  
- $V_{fu}$: volume per formula unit (Å$^3$ per LaH$_x$)  
- LaH$_x$: formula unit containing one La atom and $x$ H atoms

title: Stoichiometry of synthesized samples is likely tightly clustered near LaH10 (≈9.6–11)
content: Given the experimentally refined stoichiometry LaH$_{9.6}$ at 150 GPa (from the measured cell volume) and the synthesis-report range LaH$_{10\\pm x}$ with $x\\in[-1,+2]$ at 170 GPa, it is plausible that samples synthesized in the 150–170 GPa window have true hydrogen contents that lie close to ten per La, most likely within approximately LaH$_{9.6}$–LaH$_{11}$. Small deviations arise from synthesis conditions and measurement uncertainty, but large deviations (e.g., LaH$_{12}$) are less likely under the stated compressibility/volume constraints.

extra.notations:
- LaH$_{9.6}$: stoichiometry inferred from the 150 GPa cell volume in Proposition 312  
- LaH$_{10\\pm x}$, $x\\in[-1,+2]$: stoichiometry range reported in Proposition 818

title: Existence of a P1 → Cccm phase boundary at 37 GPa
content: There is a thermodynamic phase boundary at pressure $P=37\ \mathrm{GPa}$ separating a triclinic $P1$ phase (stable for $P<37\ \mathrm{GPa}$) from an orthorhombic $Cccm$ phase (stable for $37\ \mathrm{GPa}\le P\le 111\ \mathrm{GPa}$). Here $P$ denotes applied pressure and GPa is gigapascal.

extra.notations:
- $P$: applied pressure (in GPa)
- $P1$: triclinic space-group label
- $Cccm$: orthorhombic space-group label

title: Persistence of molecular H2 up to the 111 GPa dissociation point
content: Molecular hydrogen ($\mathrm{H}_{2}$) exists as discrete H$_2$ molecules in the low-pressure $P1$ phase and, by inference from the stated reconstructive dissociation at 111 GPa, persists in molecular form through the intermediate $Cccm$ phase until the transition at $P=111\ \mathrm{GPa}$ where those $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ molecules dissociate to form $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{S}$ units.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{H}_{2}$: molecular hydrogen
- $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{S}$: tri-hydrogenated sulfur unit formed after dissociation
- $P$: pressure (GPa)

title: 111 GPa transition is thermodynamically first-order with a 9.2% volume discontinuity
content: The structural transition at $P=111\ \mathrm{GPa}$ from $Cccm$ to $R3m$ is a first-order (discontinuous) phase transition, accompanied by a volume collapse of 9.2% (i.e., a discontinuous decrease in molar volume by 9.2% across the transition).

extra.notations:
- $P$: applied pressure (GPa)
- $R3m$: trigonal space-group label
- "volume collapse 9.2%": a discontinuous relative decrease in unit-cell volume of 9.2% at the transition

title: High-pressure metallic phases with $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{S}$ units are plausible candidates for strong electron–phonon (high-Tc) superconductivity
content: Following dissociation of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ at $P\ge 111\ \mathrm{GPa}$, the system contains $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{S}$ units and becomes metallic (band gap closure). These structural and electronic ingredients make the high-pressure $R3m$ (and the higher-symmetry $Im\text{-}3m$ above 180 GPa) phases plausible candidates for strong electron–phonon coupling and high superconducting critical temperatures ($T_c$) mediated by phonons, analogous to previously reported high-$T_c$ behavior in related $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{S}$-rich phases.

extra.notations:
- $P$: pressure (GPa)
- $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{S}$: tri-hydrogenated sulfur structural unit
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature
- $R3m$, $Im\text{-}3m$: space-group labels for trigonal and cubic phases respectively

title: Experimental Tc (203 K at 155 GPa) falls within the Allen–Dynes predicted range for the Im-3m phase
content: The measured superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}=203\\,$K (measured onset at 155 GPa) lies within the theoretical range $T_{c}=191\\text{–}204\\,$K predicted by Allen–Dynes–McMillan for the $Im\\text{-}3m$ phase (for Coulomb pseudopotential $\\mu^{*}=0.10\\text{–}0.13$). Here $T_{c}$ denotes the superconducting critical temperature in kelvin (K).

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (K)
- $Im\\text{-}3m$: crystallographic space group designation for a predicted metallic phase of hydrogen–sulfur
- $\\mu^{*}$: Coulomb pseudopotential (dimensionless)

title: Calculated electron–phonon coupling parameter implies strong-coupling superconductivity
content: The electron–phonon coupling parameter is $\\lambda=2.19$ for the $Im\\text{-}3m$ phase (as reported), and $\\lambda>1$ is the conventional criterion for strong electron–phonon coupling; therefore the calculated pairing interaction for that phase lies in the strong-coupling regime (electron–phonon mediated strong-coupling superconductivity in the sense of Eliashberg theory).

extra.notations:
- $\\lambda$: electron–phonon coupling constant (dimensionless)
- $Im\\text{-}3m$: crystallographic space group of the predicted metallic phase

title: Observed 203 K superconductivity is likely the Im-3m H3S phase with electron–phonon pairing
content: It is likely (probability ~0.85) that the superconducting state observed at $T_{c}=203\\,$K and $P\\approx155\\,$GPa corresponds to the $Im\\text{-}3m$ crystalline phase of hydrogen–sulfur (stoichiometry close to H$_{3}$S) and that the pairing mechanism is predominantly conventional electron–phonon coupling as captured by the Allen–Dynes–McMillan/Eliashberg framework, with parameters similar to $\\lambda\\approx2.19$ and $\\omega_{\\log}\\approx1335\\,$K (which reproduce the high $T_{c}$).

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (K)
- $P$: pressure (GPa)
- $Im\\text{-}3m$: candidate high-pressure crystal structure
- $\\lambda$: electron–phonon coupling constant
- $\\omega_{\\log}$: logarithmic average phonon frequency (K)

title: Effective hopping amplitudes are second order in the Cu–O hybridization
content:
The effective hopping matrix element $t_{ij}$ that appears as the coefficient of the projected hopping operator in the single-band kinetic term $H_{t}$ is produced by second-order processes in the Cu–O hybridization and therefore scales as the square of the Cu–O hybridization amplitude. Concretely, if the Cu–O hybridization matrix elements entering $H'$ are of order $t_0$, then each term in the perturbative expression
$$t_{ij}=\sum_{\{w\}}\frac{\langle \psi_j^{-} d_{i\sigma} | H' | w \rangle \langle w | H' | (\psi_i^{-} d_{j\sigma})^{\dagger} \rangle}{\Delta E_w}$$
contains two factors of $H'$ and so $t_{ij}=O(t_0^2/\Delta E)$ (with $\Delta E$ a characteristic intermediate-state energy). Hence the hopping part $H_t=\sum_{i\neq j,\sigma} t_{ij}(1-n_{i,-\sigma})d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}d_{j\sigma}(1-n_{j,-\sigma})$ is parametrically second order in the microscopic Cu–O hybridization amplitude.

extra.notations:
- $t_{ij}$: effective hopping amplitude for the local Cu–O singlet between Cu sites $i$ and $j$. 
- $t_0$: microscopic Cu–O hybridization (hopping) amplitude entering $H'$. 
- $\Delta E$ ($\Delta E_w$): characteristic energy denominator(s) of intermediate states $w$ in second-order perturbation theory.
- $H_t$: effective single-band kinetic term acting on projected Cu operators.
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger},d_{i\sigma}$: Cu hole creation/annihilation operators; $n_{i\sigma}=d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}d_{i\sigma}$.

title: Nearest-neighbour Cu–Cu hopping mediated by a single intervening O dominates $t_{ij}$
content: Given that $t_{ij}$ arises from second-order Cu–O hopping processes, the dominant contribution to the effective hopping between copper sites is likely the process that uses the shortest Cu–O–Cu path (one oxygen between nearest-neighbour Cu sites). Consequently, the largest magnitude effective hopping is expected for nearest-neighbour Cu–Cu pairs and scales as $t_{\mathrm{nn}}\sim O(t_0^2/\Delta)$ where $\Delta$ is the relevant intermediate-state energy (e.g. a charge-transfer or on-site energy difference). Longer-range Cu–Cu hoppings (next-nearest, etc.) arise from less favorable or higher-order paths and are parametrically smaller.

extra.notations:
- $t_{\mathrm{nn}}$: effective nearest-neighbour Cu–Cu hopping amplitude.
- $t_0$: microscopic Cu–O hybridization amplitude.
- $\Delta$: characteristic energy denominator for the dominant intermediate state(s) (could be $\epsilon_p-\epsilon_d$, $U-\ldots$, or a similar charge-transfer/Mott scale depending on details).
- Cu–O–Cu path: a minimal two-step virtual process Cu$\to$O$\to$Cu producing $t_{ij}$ at second order.

title: Effective singlet hopping is second order in Cu–O hybridization
content: The effective hopping matrix element $t_{ij}$ for moving a local Cu–O singlet $\psi_i^{-}$ from site $i$ to site $j$ is quadratic in the Cu–O hybridization amplitude $t_0$: schematically $t_{ij}\propto t_0^2/\Delta E_w$, where $\Delta E_w$ denotes the energy denominator of the virtual intermediate state $w$ appearing in second-order perturbation theory.

extra.notations:
- $t_{ij}$: effective hopping of the local singlet between Cu sites $i$ and $j$.  
- $t_0$: amplitude (hopping integral) of the Cu–O hybridization entering $H'$.  
- $\Delta E_w$: energy difference between the initial singlet configuration and intermediate state $w$.

title: Zhang–Rice singlet contains exactly one Cu $d$-hole (no Cu double occupancy)
content: The local Zhang–Rice singlet $\psi_i^{-}=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\big(\phi_{i\uparrow}d_{i\downarrow}-\phi_{i\downarrow}d_{i\uparrow}\big)$ contains one Cu $3d_{x^2-y^2}$ hole (created/annihilated by $d_{i\sigma}^{(\dagger)}$) and one symmetric oxygen Wannier hole (created/annihilated by $\phi_{i\sigma}^{(\dagger)}$). Therefore, the Cu site $i$ in this two-hole local state is singly occupied by a hole and the state does not involve double occupancy of the Cu $d$ orbital.

extra.notations:
- $d_{i\sigma}$: annihilation operator for a Cu $3d_{x^2-y^2}$ hole at site $i$ with spin $\sigma$.  
- $\phi_{i\sigma}$: annihilation operator for the symmetric oxygen Wannier hole centered on Cu site $i$.  
- $\psi_i^{-}$: local Cu–O spin singlet at site $i$.

title: Low-energy dynamics plausibly map to an effective single-band model of mobile Zhang–Rice singlets
content: If the local Cu–O singlets $\psi_i^{-}$ form a well separated low-energy manifold (i.e., excitations out of the singlet sector are costly compared with typical $t_{ij}$), then the low-energy charge dynamics of doped holes on the Cu lattice can be described by an effective single-band model in which the mobile degrees of freedom are the Zhang–Rice singlet quasiparticles with hopping amplitudes $t_{ij}$ and residual interactions (exchange, nearest-neighbour repulsion) induced by virtual processes.

extra.notations:
- $\psi_i^{-}$: local Cu–O spin singlet at Cu site $i$ (Zhang–Rice singlet).  
- $t_{ij}$: effective singlet hopping between Cu sites $i$ and $j$ as in Proposition 430.

title: Composition-dependent sign of annealing effect
content: Given two Tl-containing layered cuprates subjected to the same annealing treatment (450°C in flowing O₂), one composition (Tl–Ba–Cu–O) exhibits depression or loss of the superconducting transition while a related composition containing Ca (Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O) shows slight improvement of superconducting properties. Therefore, the direction of change in superconducting behaviour produced by this anneal is dependent on the sample composition: specifically, inclusion of Ca (or the difference between the two chemical formulations) alters whether the anneal improves or degrades superconductivity. Here $T_{c}$ denotes the superconducting critical temperature.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition (critical) temperature. 
- Tl–Ba–Cu–O, Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O: chemical compositions of the layered thallium cuprates under discussion.

title: Likely role of oxygen stoichiometry and Tl volatility in contrasting responses
content: It is plausible that the opposite responses to the same 450°C O₂ anneal arise because the two compositions differ in how the anneal changes oxygen stoichiometry, carrier concentration and/or Tl cation stability. Concretely: let $p$ denote the hole (carrier) concentration in the CuO₂ planes. In Tl–Ba–Cu–O the 450°C O₂ anneal plausibly increases oxygen content or induces Tl loss/secondary phases in a way that moves $p$ away from the optimal range (reducing or destroying $T_{c}$), whereas in Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O the same anneal likely adjusts oxygen content and charge balance toward optimal $p$, improving $T_{c}$ or the resistive transition. Thus differing oxygen uptake kinetics, Tl volatility, or charge-reservoir chemistry (modified by Ca) likely control the opposite outcomes.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature.
- $p$: hole (charge carrier) concentration in the CuO₂ planes (qualitative variable: underdoped/optimal/overdoped).
- O₂ anneal: heat treatment at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in flowing oxygen.

title: Resistance transition is electronic, not a bulk structural (volume) transition
content: The abrupt, reversible drop in four-probe electrical resistance by a factor $>10^{3}$ beginning at $\sim260\ \text{K}$ (cooling) and persisting to lower temperatures cannot be caused by a temperature-induced structural phase transition that changes unit-cell volume, because low-temperature X-ray diffraction shows no volume change or structural transition across the same temperature range. Therefore the transition is a change in the electronic state controlling charge transport (an electronic phase transition) rather than a bulk, volume-changing structural phase transition.

extra.notations:
- $T$: temperature in kelvin (K)
- $R$: electrical resistance measured in a four-probe geometry
- LaH$_{10\pm x}$: lanthanum superhydride sample under 180–200 GPa

title: Transition exhibits thermal hysteresis and is reversible (not irreversible damage)
content: The observed transition temperature depends on thermal path: onset at $\approx260\,$K on cooling and reversal at $\approx245\,$K on warming (a $\sim15\,$K offset). This behavior demonstrates thermal hysteresis and shows the resistance change is a reversible, reproducible thermally driven transition rather than irreversible sample degradation.

extra.notations:
- $T_{\rm cool}\approx260\,$K: onset temperature on cooling
- $T_{\rm warm}\approx245\,$K: reverse onset temperature on warming
- Hysteresis: $T_{\rm cool}\ne T_{\rm warm}$

title: Transition is likely superconductivity with characteristic $T_c\approx260\,$K
content: The abrupt, large ($>10^{3}$) drop in bulk four-probe resistance at $T\approx260\,$K (cooling), its persistence to lower temperatures, the reversibility with modest hysteresis, and the absence of any detectable structural change in X-ray diffraction together make it likely that the sample undergoes a superconducting transition with a superconducting critical temperature $T_c\approx260\,$K. Here $T_c$ denotes the temperature below which a superconducting condensate forms and dissipationless charge transport is expected.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: putative superconducting critical temperature (kelvin)
- LaH$_{10\pm x}$: the compressed lanthanum superhydride sample (180–200 GPa)

title: Thermal hysteresis magnitude suggests first-order or metastable character of the transition
content: The observed $\sim15\,$K hysteresis between cooling and warming ($T_{\rm cool}\approx260\,$K vs $T_{\rm warm}\approx245\,$K) plausibly indicates that the transition has first-order character or involves metastability/phase coexistence (e.g., nucleation and growth, pinning, or kinetic barriers) in the compressed LaH$_{10\pm x}$ sample.

extra.notations:
- $T_{\rm cool}$, $T_{\rm warm}$: defined as in the preceding result
- Hysteresis width $\Delta T\approx T_{\rm cool}-T_{\rm warm}\approx15\,$K

title: Overdamped gauge mode with dynamical exponent z = 3 in the clean limit
content:
Under the clean-limit assumption (mean free path $l$ satisfies $q l>1$) the transverse conductivity is $\sigma(q)\approx k_{0}/q$ with $k_{0}$ a wavevector-scale constant (of order $k_F$ or inverse lattice spacing). Using the propagator $D^{T}(q,\omega)=[\,i\omega\sigma(q)-\chi_{d}q^{2}\,]^{-1}$ and substituting $\sigma(q)=k_{0}/q$ gives
$$D^{T}(q,\omega)=\frac{q}{\,i\omega k_{0}-\chi_{d}q^{3}\,}.$$
The pole of the propagator (setting the denominator to zero) occurs at
$$\omega = -\,i\,\frac{\chi_{d}}{k_{0}}\,q^{3}.$$
Thus the long-wavelength, low-frequency transverse gauge fluctuations are overdamped with characteristic frequency scaling as $q^{3}$; equivalently, the gauge-field dynamics are characterized by dynamical exponent $z=3$ (i.e. $\omega\!\propto\! q^{z}$ with $z=3$) in the stated clean-limit, low-energy regime.

extra.notations:
- $q$: magnitude of 2D wavevector. 
- $\omega$: real frequency (retarded response conventions assumed).
- $\sigma(q)$: transverse dissipative conductivity at wavevector $q$.
- $k_{0}$: constant with dimensions of wavevector appearing in the clean-limit conductivity $\sigma(q)\approx k_{0}/q$.
- $\chi_{d}$: total diamagnetic susceptibility, $\chi_{d}=\chi_{F}+\chi_{B}$.
- $l$: electronic mean free path.
- $z$: dynamical critical exponent defined by $\omega\propto q^{z}$.

title: Breakdown of Landau quasiparticles (non-Fermi-liquid) at low energies
content:
Proposition 502 gives the fermion (spinon) zero-temperature imaginary self-energy near the Fermi level as
$$\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(k,\epsilon_F)\approx -C\,\xi_{k}^{2/3},$$
where $\xi_{k}\equiv\epsilon_{k}-\epsilon_F$ is the excitation energy measured from the Fermi level and $C>0$ is a constant. For a Landau quasiparticle the decay rate must satisfy $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}\ll \xi_{k}$ as $\xi_{k}\to 0$. Here
$$\frac{\Sigma^{\prime\prime}}{\xi_{k}} \sim \xi_{k}^{-1/3}\to +\infty\quad(\xi_{k}\to 0),$$
so the decay rate dominates the excitation energy at low energies. Therefore single-particle fermionic excitations are not long-lived and the Landau quasiparticle description fails: the system is a non-Fermi liquid (no well-defined quasiparticles) at $T=0$ in the regime where the stated self-energy applies.

extra.notations:
- $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(k,\epsilon_F)$: imaginary part of the fermion self-energy evaluated at the Fermi energy.
- $\xi_{k}=\epsilon_{k}-\epsilon_F$: single-particle energy relative to the Fermi level.
- $C$: positive constant prefactor.
- "Landau quasiparticle": excitation with decay rate much smaller than its energy, i.e. $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}\ll\xi_{k}$ as $\xi_{k}\to 0$.

title: Non-percolating high-Tc superconducting regions (sample inhomogeneity)
content: Let $T_{c}$ denote the superconducting transition temperature; "diamagnetism onset at $133.5\\,$K" means a measurable bulk magnetic susceptibility signal consistent with superconducting screening appears at $133.5\\,$K; "zero resistance at $95\\,$K" means the measured electrical resistivity becomes indistinguishable from zero only below $95\\,$K. If both measurements on the same specimen are correct, then the specimen contains superconducting regions that appear (become superconducting) at temperatures as high as $133.5\\,$K but these regions do not form a continuous, sample-spanning (percolating) electrical path until $95\\,$K. Equivalently, the superconducting volume fraction or connectivity above $95\\,$K is insufficient for macroscopic zero resistance, implying spatial inhomogeneity (phase separation, granular connectivity, or incomplete percolation) of superconductivity in the sample.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature.
- Diamagnetism onset: temperature where magnetic susceptibility indicates superconducting screening.
- Zero resistance: temperature below which measured resistivity is effectively zero (macroscopic superconducting connectivity).
- Percolation / percolating path: a continuous superconducting path across the sample enabling zero DC resistance.

title: Positive correlation between number of CuO2 layers $n$ and $T_{c}$ in Hg-based cuprates (from $n=1$ to $n=3$)
content: Let $n$ be the number of $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell and $T_{c}$ the superconducting transition temperature. The data given are: for Hg-1201 ($n=1$) $T_{c}\\approx 94\\,$K, and for a Hg–Ba–Ca–Cu–O specimen (consistent with the Hg-1223, $n=3$, phase in the Hg family) an onset of superconductivity at $\\approx 133.5\\,$K. A plausible inference is that, within the mercury-based cuprates and under comparable sample conditions (similar doping level, oxygen content, and ambient pressure), $T_{c}$ increases with $n$ from $n=1$ to $n=3$. Mechanistically, this trend may reflect enhanced interlayer coupling, modified charge transfer to the $CuO_{2}$ planes, or other multilayer effects that raise $T_{c}$ as $n$ increases up to $3$.

extra.notations:
- $n$: number of $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (onset of diamagnetism or resistive transition onset as specified).
- Hg-1201: $HgBa_{2}CuO_{4+\\delta}$ (here $n=1$).
- Hg-1223 (implicit in 1339): $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+\\delta}$ (here $n=3$), associated with $T_{c}\\approx 133.5\\,$K onset in the cited measurement.

title: Projected BCS state in 1089 is an explicit realization of the RVB superposition of mobile singlets
content:
The wavefunction given in Proposition 1089,
$$\Psi_{\mathrm{RVB}} = P_{N/2}\prod_{k}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+a(k)^2}}+\frac{a(k)}{\sqrt{1+a(k)^2}}\,c_{k\uparrow}^{\dagger}c_{-k\downarrow}^{\dagger}\right]\Psi_{0},$$
is (after expansion of the product and application of the fixed-pair-number projector $P_{N/2}$) a coherent quantum superposition of many terms each containing $N/2$ singlet pair creation operators. Each such term corresponds to a particular pattern of singlet pairs (expressible in real space by Fourier transform of the momentum-space pair amplitudes). Therefore the state of 1089 is an explicit instance of the Resonating Valence Bond (RVB) type state described in Proposition 1105 — i.e., a coherent superposition of mobile singlet pairs appropriate to low-$S$ (spin-$1/2$), low-dimensional, frustrated systems.

extra.notations:
- $P_{N/2}$: projector onto the subspace with $N/2$ electron pairs (fixed particle number).
- $c_{k\sigma}^{\dagger}$: creation operator for an electron with momentum $k$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $a(k)$: momentum-space pair amplitude controlling weight and phase of a given $k$ pair.
- $\Psi_{0}$: vacuum (no electrons) reference state.

title: Gutzwiller-/number-projected BCS is an appropriate variational ansatz for $S=\tfrac{1}{2}$, 2D frustrated systems
content: For low-spin ($S=1/2$), two-dimensional, magnetically frustrated lattices (the conditions identified in Proposition 1105), the number-projected BCS form of Proposition 1089 — when supplemented by correlation projection operators that suppress double occupancy (Gutzwiller-type projection) or when considered at half-filling — provides a physically appropriate variational trial state to capture the RVB physics (short-range singlets, resonance, and strong quantum fluctuations). In other words, a projected-pairing wavefunction with variational amplitudes $a(k)$ is likely to produce low variational energy and capture the magnetic (spin-liquid) correlations favored in those systems.

extra.notations:
- $a(k)$: variational momentum-space pair amplitude to be optimized.
- $P_{N/2}$: projector onto fixed number of pairs; in practice one often augments this with a Gutzwiller projector $P_G$ to suppress double occupancy at large $U$.
- $S=1/2$, 2D, frustrated: conditions (from 1105) favoring RVB physics.

title: Doping a projected-RVB insulator plausibly yields superconductivity described by mobile singlet pairs
content: If the insulating RVB state (as in 1089/1105) is realized at or near half-filling, introducing a small hole (or electron) concentration $\delta$ (doping) will likely make charge carriers mobile while leaving the short-range singlet (RVB) correlations largely intact; this mobilization of preformed singlet pairs can lead to a superconducting state whose pairing amplitude and symmetry are set by the variational pair amplitudes $a(k)$. Consequently, the superconducting energy gap $\Delta$ and coherence properties (e.g., coherence length $\xi_{0}$, Fermi wave vector $k_{F}$) will be determined by the interplay of $a(k)$, doping $\delta$, and residual correlations.

extra.notations:
- $\delta$: fractional doping (holes per site) away from half-filling.
- $a(k)$: momentum-space pair amplitude controlling internal pairing symmetry.
- $\Delta$: superconducting gap magnitude (energy scale).
- $\xi_{0}$: superconducting coherence length; $k_{F}$: Fermi wave vector of doped carriers.

title: Momentum-space Bloch Hamiltonian is a 5×5 Hermitian matrix
content:
The real-space five-orbital tight-binding Hamiltonian
$$
H_{0}=\sum_{i,j}\sum_{\mu,\nu}\sum_{\sigma}\big[\,t(x_{i}-x_{j},y_{i}-y_{j};\mu,\nu)\,c^{\dagger}_{i\mu\sigma}c_{j\nu\sigma}+\text{H.c.}\,\big]
+\sum_{i,\mu,\sigma}\varepsilon_{\mu}\,n_{i\mu\sigma},
$$
with orbital index $\mu\in\{1,\dots,5\}$ corresponding to the five Fe $3d$-derived orbitals, implies that the Bloch (momentum-space) single-particle Hamiltonian is a $5\times5$ Hermitian matrix $H_{\mu\nu}(\mathbf{k})$ acting in orbital space. Explicitly,
$$
H_{0}=\sum_{\mathbf{k},\sigma}\sum_{\mu,\nu} H_{\mu\nu}(\mathbf{k})\,c^{\dagger}_{\mathbf{k}\mu\sigma}c_{\mathbf{k}\nu\sigma},
$$
where
$$
H_{\mu\nu}(\mathbf{k})=\sum_{R} t(R;\mu,\nu)\,e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot R} \;+\; \delta_{\mu\nu}\,\varepsilon_{\mu},
$$
with $R=(\Delta x,\Delta y)$ lattice displacements on the unfolded square Brillouin zone. Hermiticity follows from the real-space hopping Hermiticity $t(R;\mu,\nu)=t^{*}(-R;\nu,\mu)$ and real $\varepsilon_{\mu}$.

extra.notations:
- $H_{\mu
u}(\mathbf{k})$: Bloch Hamiltonian matrix element between orbitals $\mu,
u$ at crystal momentum $\mathbf{k}$. 
- $t(R;\mu,
u)$: hopping integral between orbitals $\mu,
u$ separated by lattice vector $R$.
- $\varepsilon_{\mu}$: on-site energy of orbital $\mu$.

title: Off-diagonal (interorbital) terms in $H_{\mu\nu}(\mathbf{k})$ are quantitatively significant
content:
Given that the five Fe $3d$ bands are described as "heavily entangled" and that As atoms form a tetrahedral environment around Fe, it is likely that the off-diagonal (interorbital) Bloch Hamiltonian elements $H_{\mu
eq
u}(\mathbf{k})$ are non-negligible for a number of orbital pairs. Concretely, interorbital hoppings connecting $d_{XZ}$/$d_{YZ}$ with $d_{XY}$ and with $d_{X^{2}-Y^{2}}$ (orbitals $\mu\in\{2,3\}$ and $
u\in\{4,5\}$) will have amplitudes comparable to some intraorbital hoppings for near-Fermi-energy bands, leading to momentum-dependent orbital mixing (orbital entanglement) on the Fermi surfaces.

extra.notations:
- $H_{\mu\nu}(\mathbf{k})$: Bloch Hamiltonian matrix element between orbitals $\mu,\nu$.
- "Non-negligible": amplitudes that have the same order of magnitude as at least some intraorbital hopping matrix elements relevant near the Fermi level.
- Orbital labeling as in the parent: $1:d_{3Z^{2}-R^{2}},\;2:d_{XZ},\;3:d_{YZ},\;4:d_{X^{2}-Y^{2}},\;5:d_{XY}$.

title: Orthorhombicity selectively breaks the Γ–Y mirror parity
content:
If the weaker shadow Fermi-surface (SFS) feature in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (Bi2212) is a structural replica whose altered reflection parity arises from bulk orthorhombic distortions (as stated in Proposition 1517), and the measured parity relation between the main band (MB) and shadow band (SB) is opposite for the mirror plane containing the Γ–Y azimuth but identical for the orthogonal Γ–X mirror plane (Proposition 1516), then the orthorhombic distortion necessarily changes (i.e. does not preserve) the reflection parity of the CuO2-derived initial states with respect to the Γ–Y mirror plane while it does not change (i.e. preserves) their reflection parity with respect to the Γ–X mirror plane. 

Here Γ denotes the Brillouin-zone center, Γ–Y the nodal azimuth toward (π,π), Γ–X the orthogonal azimuth, "reflection parity" means the eigenvalue ±1 under reflection in the specified mirror plane, MB denotes the principal CuO2-derived band and SB its weaker structural replica (shadow band).

extra.notations:
- Γ: Brillouin-zone center (0,0).
- Γ–Y: azimuth from Γ toward (π,π) (nodal direction).
- Γ–X: azimuth orthogonal to Γ–Y.
- MB: main (primary) CuO2-derived band / main Fermi surface feature.
- SB: shadow (replica) band / shadow Fermi surface feature.
- "reflection parity": eigenvalue ±1 of an initial-state wavefunction under reflection in a specified mirror plane.

title: Principal orthorhombic axis likely aligned with the Γ–Y direction
content:
Given that (i) the MB↔SB parity is inverted for reflections in the mirror plane containing Γ–Y but not for the orthogonal Γ–X plane (Proposition 1516), and (ii) the parity inversion is attributed to BiO-layer-driven orthorhombic distortions that modify CuO2-derived initial-state parity (Proposition 1517), it is plausible that the dominant orthorhombic distortion has its principal axis (or the symmetry-breaking displacement pattern) oriented so as to break the mirror plane containing Γ–Y. In other words, the microscopic distortion that produces the SFS parity change is likely oriented (or projects) along the Γ–Y (nodal) direction rather than along Γ–X.

Symbols: "principal orthorhombic axis" = direction in the CuO2/BiO plane along which the lattice distortion most strongly breaks tetragonal symmetry; other symbols as in the deduction.

extra.notations:
- "principal orthorhombic axis": direction in-plane along which orthorhombic lattice parameters or displacements are largest.
- Γ–Y, Γ–X, MB, SB as in the deduction.

title: Annealing increases Hg‑1223 Tc by ≈16 K
content: For HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{8+x}$ (denoted Hg‑1223) the reported maximum superconducting transition onset after post‑synthesis annealing in flowing O$_2$ is approximately $133\,$K, while the as‑sintered maximum is reported as about $117\,$K; therefore the annealing treatment produces an increase in the observed maximum $T_{c}$ of approximately $133-117\approx 16\,$K (all temperatures approximate as reported).

extra.notations: - Hg‑1223: HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{8+x}$; $T_{c}$: superconducting transition onset temperature in Kelvin.

title: Optimal annealing temperature for Tc is compound‑dependent
content: The annealing temperature required to produce a beneficial modification of the superconducting transition is not universal across the cited cuprates: Hg‑based Hg‑1223 (and related Hg‑Ba‑Ca‑Cu‑O compounds) require post‑synthesis oxygen annealing at about $300^{\circ}$C to reach their reported maximum $T_{c}$, whereas Tl‑containing layered cuprates (Tl‑Ba‑Cu‑O / Tl‑Ca/Ba‑Cu‑O) are reported to show improvements after annealing at $450^{\circ}$C. Therefore, the temperature that produces the desired modification of $T_{c}$ depends on the specific cuprate compound/family.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature; temperature values in degrees Celsius.
- "Hg‑based": Hg‑Ba‑Ca‑Cu‑O family; "Tl‑containing": Tl‑Ba‑Cu‑O / Tl‑Ca/Ba‑Cu‑O families.

title: Oxygen uptake during annealing likely adjusts hole concentration and thereby alters Tc
content: Post‑synthesis annealing in flowing O$_2$ plausibly changes the oxygen non‑stoichiometry (denoted $x$ in formulas like HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{8+x}$), which in turn modifies the hole concentration in the CuO$_2$ planes; changes in hole concentration commonly move cuprates along the superconducting dome and therefore alter the measured $T_{c}$ and the resistive transition shape/width. Thus the observed modifications of $T_{c}$ upon oxygen annealing are likely mediated by changes in oxygen content and resultant carrier (hole) doping.

extra.notations:
- O$_2$ annealing: heating in flowing oxygen gas.
- $x$: oxygen non‑stoichiometry parameter in formulas HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{8+x}$, etc.
- "hole concentration": number of mobile holes per Cu in CuO$_2$ planes (doping level).

title: Tl‑based cuprates may require higher annealing temperatures due to different oxygen incorporation kinetics or thermochemistry than Hg‑based cuprates
content: The observation that Tl‑containing cuprates are annealed at about $450^{\circ}$C to produce improvements while Hg‑based cuprates achieve maximum $T_{c}$ after annealing at about $300^{\circ}$C suggests that the two families differ in either oxygen incorporation kinetics (diffusion rates, activation energies) or in thermochemical stability of oxygen sites; such differences would lead to different temperatures being necessary to reach the target oxygen stoichiometry and optimal doping.

extra.notations:
- "oxygen incorporation kinetics": rate and temperature dependence of oxygen entering and equilibrating in the solid.
- "thermochemistry": relative stability/formation energy of oxygen‑containing defect configurations in the lattice.

title: Numeric determination of the isotope coefficient from measured Tc(H) and Tc(D)
content:
Using the mass-scaling relation $T_{c}=A\\,m^{-\\alpha}$ (equivalently $\\alpha=-\\dfrac{\\ln(T_{c,2}/T_{c,1})}{\\ln(m_{2}/m_{1})}$), the reported critical temperatures for fcc-LaH_{10} with hydrogen ($T_{c,H}\\approx 249\\,$K) and deuterium ($T_{c,D}\\approx 180\\,$K) at 150 GPa, together with the atomic masses $m_{H}\\approx 1\\,$u and $m_{D}\\approx 2\\,$u, imply
$$\\alpha = -\\dfrac{\\ln(T_{c,D}/T_{c,H})}{\\ln(m_{D}/m_{H})}
= -\\dfrac{\\ln(180/249)}{\\ln 2}\\approx 0.468\\approx 0.47.$$
This numeric value is consistent with the reported $\\alpha\\approx 0.46$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (K)
- $m$: isotopic mass (atomic mass units, u)
- $\\alpha$: isotope coefficient defined by $T_{c}\\propto m^{-\\alpha}$
- Subscripts $H,D$ denote hydrogen and deuterium isotopologues respectively

title: Phonon-mediated pairing is strongly indicated for fcc-LaH_{10} at 150 GPa
content: The measured isotope coefficient $\\alpha\\approx 0.46$ for fcc-LaH_{10} (H→D substitution at 150 GPa) is very close to the canonical BCS/harmonic value $\\alpha=0.5$. If $T_{c}\\propto m^{-\\alpha}$ holds and lattice vibrations (phonons) dominate the pairing interaction, then an isotope exponent near $0.5$ is expected. Therefore it is likely that the superconducting pairing in fcc-LaH_{10} at 150 GPa is primarily phonon-mediated (conventional electron–phonon mechanism).

extra.notations:
- $\\alpha$: isotope coefficient
- BCS: Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer phonon-mediated pairing theory
- H→D: substitution of hydrogen by deuterium

title: Small deviation of $\\alpha$ below 0.5 suggests modest non-ideal effects (strong coupling or anharmonicity)
content: The measured isotope coefficient $\\alpha\\approx 0.46$ is slightly below the canonical harmonic BCS value $\\alpha=0.5$. A plausible explanation is that non-ideal effects — for example strong electron–phonon coupling, finite Coulomb pseudopotential ($\\mu^{*}$) corrections, or anharmonic lattice dynamics common in dense hydrides — reduce the effective isotope exponent below $0.5$. Thus fcc-LaH_{10} at 150 GPa likely exhibits modest strong-coupling and/or anharmonic corrections to the simple harmonic BCS prediction.

extra.notations:
- $\\alpha$: isotope coefficient
- $\\mu^{*}$: Coulomb pseudopotential parameter (dimensionless)
- "Anharmonicity": deviations of lattice vibrations from the harmonic approximation

title: Observed Tc is bounded above by the smaller of the phase-ordering and mean-field scales
content:
Let $T_{c}$ be the observed superconducting transition temperature, $T^{MF}$ the mean-field pairing temperature (the temperature where, in mean-field theory, a nonzero local pair amplitude first appears), and $T_{\theta}^{max}$ the phase-ordering temperature computed from the zero-temperature phase stiffness (the temperature at which long-wavelength phase order would be lost if other disordering channels were neglected). If both $T^{MF}$ and $T_{\theta}^{max}$ are theoretical upper bounds on the true ordering temperature (because each neglects disordering effects that can only reduce $T_{c}$), then it follows necessarily that
$$
T_{c}\leq\min\{\,T_{\theta}^{max},\,T^{MF}\,\}.
$$
This inequality holds without additional assumptions beyond the siblings' claims that each of $T^{MF}$ and $T_{\theta}^{max}$ individually bounds $T_{c}$ from above.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence established). 
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature (local-pair amplitude scale in mean-field theory).
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: maximum phase-ordering temperature computed from the zero-temperature phase stiffness.

title: Plausible existence of a spectroscopic pseudogap (preformed pairs) for $T_{c}<T<T^{MF}$ when phase-ordering is the limiting scale
content: Assume $T^{MF}$ is the temperature at which a local pair amplitude becomes significant in a mean-field sense, while $T_{\theta}^{max}$ (and hence the observed $T_{c}$ when phase fluctuations dominate) is substantially lower: $T_{c}\ll T^{MF}$. Then it is plausible that there exists a temperature window $T_{c}<T\lesssim T^{MF}$ in which local pair amplitude (pairing correlations) is sizable but global phase coherence is absent. In spectroscopic probes sensitive to single-particle excitations (e.g., angle-resolved photoemission or tunneling), this regime is expected to show a suppression of low-energy spectral weight or an energy gap-like feature (a "pseudogap") despite the absence of zero-resistance superconductivity.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence).
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature (local-pair amplitude appearance).
- "pseudogap": suppression of single-particle density of states or spectral weight at low energies without global phase coherence.

title: Heavy 3D $\Gamma$ pocket provided roughly half of the Fermi-level DOS in undoped LaFeAsO (GGA)
content: Let $N(E_F)$ denote the total electronic density of states (DOS) at the Fermi level $E_F$. Proposition 727 reports that filling of the heavy three-dimensional (3D) hole pocket near $\Gamma$ upon electron doping reduces the total $N(E_F)$ by a factor of two (within GGA calculations). Proposition 722 states that this heavy 3D hole pocket is emptied (filled by electrons) by $x\approx 0.04$–$0.05$. Taken together, within the same GGA/DFT framework the heavy 3D hole pocket that is removed between the undoped case and the doped case accounts for approximately 50% of the total $N(E_F)$ of undoped LaFeAsO.

extra.notations:
- $N(E_F)$: total electronic density of states at the Fermi level per formula unit (as computed in GGA DFT).
- $\Gamma$: Brillouin-zone center ($\mathbf{k}=0$).
- "heavy 3D hole pocket": the three-dimensional holelike Fermi-surface sheet centered at $\Gamma$ described in the siblings.

title: Stoner criterion crosses from satisfied to unsatisfied between undoped and $x=0.1$ (GGA)
content: Let $I$ be the Stoner parameter (exchange interaction strength) and $N(E_F)$ the total DOS at the Fermi level (per spin). The Stoner criterion for a ferromagnetic instability is $I\,N(E_F)\ge 1$. Proposition 727 reports that the undoped material (within GGA) shows a ferromagnetic solution while the doped material at $x=0.1$ shows no ferromagnetic solution; the lack of ferromagnetism at $x=0.1$ is attributed to the filling of the heavy 3D $\Gamma$ pocket which reduces $N(E_F)$ by a factor of two. Therefore, within the GGA calculations referenced, the product $I\,N(E_F)$ was (effectively) $\ge 1$ in the undoped calculation and $<1$ at $x=0.1$; the removal of the 3D pocket is the operative cause of this change.

extra.notations:
- $I$: Stoner exchange parameter (material-specific constant entering the Stoner criterion).
- $N(E_F)$: total electronic density of states at the Fermi level per spin (GGA value).
- $I\,N(E_F)$: Stoner product; ferromagnetic instability when $\ge 1$.

title: Magnetic instability is likely suppressed as soon as the 3D pocket is filled (near $x\approx0.04$–$0.05$)
content: Given that the heavy 3D $\Gamma$ pocket is filled by $x\approx0.04$–$0.05$ (Proposition 722) and that its removal is credited with halving $N(E_F)$ and eliminating a ferromagnetic solution by $x=0.1$ (Proposition 727), it is likely that the ferromagnetic instability (within GGA) is already suppressed once the pocket is removed — i.e., the crossover from a magnetic to a non-magnetic solution occurs at or near the pocket-filling concentration $x\approx0.04$–$0.05$. This requires the additional assumption that the Stoner parameter $I$ and the contributions to $N(E_F)$ from the remaining Fermi-surface sheets do not change in a compensating way between $x\approx0.05$ and $x=0.1$.

extra.notations:
- $x$: electron doping concentration (fluorine content) in LaFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}.
- "suppressed": absence of a ferromagnetic self-consistent solution in GGA DFT.
- "pocket filled": the heavy 3D hole pocket at $\Gamma$ no longer crosses $E_F$.

title: Measured isotope coefficient is numerically consistent with canonical BCS value
content: Let $T_c$ be the superconducting critical temperature, $m$ the isotopic mass, and $\\alpha$ the isotope coefficient defined by $T_c\\propto m^{-\\alpha}$. Proposition 1581 states the canonical BCS/harmonic value $\\alpha=0.5$. Proposition 316 reports a measured value $\\alpha=0.46$ for fcc-LaH_{10} vs fcc-LaD_{10} at 150 GPa. The measured value differs from the canonical value by $0.04$, i.e. an 8% relative deviation from $0.5$. Therefore, numerically, the measurement does not contradict the canonical BCS/harmonic expectation $\\alpha=0.5$: the measured isotope coefficient is close to the canonical value.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature (K)
- $m$: isotopic mass
- $\\alpha$: isotope coefficient in $T_c\\propto m^{-\\alpha}$
- fcc-LaH_{10}, fcc-LaD_{10}: lanthanum hydride and its deuteride in the face-centered cubic phase

title: Phonon-mediated (BCS-like) pairing is the likely origin of superconductivity in fcc-LaH_{10} at 150 GPa
content: For fcc-LaH_{10} (hydrogenated lanthanum, $T_c\\approx 249\\,$K at 150 GPa) and its deuterated analogue fcc-LaD_{10} ($T_c\\approx 180\\,$K at 150 GPa), the isotope coefficient computed from $T_c\\propto m^{-\\alpha}$ is $\\alpha=0.46$. Under the harmonic BCS expectation $\\alpha=0.5$, an isotope exponent close to $0.5$ is taken as strong evidence that lattice (phonon) degrees of freedom mediate pairing. Hence it is plausible (probability 0.85) that the superconducting pairing in fcc-LaH_{10} at 150 GPa is predominantly phonon-mediated and well described, at least qualitatively, by BCS-like electron-phonon coupling within the harmonic approximation.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature (K)
- $m$: isotopic mass
- $\\alpha$: isotope coefficient in $T_c\\propto m^{-\\alpha}$
- fcc-LaH_{10}, fcc-LaD_{10}: lanthanum hydride and deuteride in the face-centered cubic phase
- "BCS-like": pairing mechanism dominated by electron-phonon interactions as in Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer theory

title: SmAs and Fe2O3 are common, viable precursors for SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}
content: Both sibling propositions report synthesis routes for SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} in which samarium arsenide (SmAs) and iron(III) oxide (Fe_{2}O_{3}) are included among the starting solid precursors. Therefore, if both siblings are true, it is necessarily true that SmAs and Fe_{2}O_{3} have been used as viable starting materials for preparing polycrystalline SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} by solid-state sintering reactions.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}: fluorine-doped samarium iron oxyarsenide, $x$ is nominal F content
- SmAs: samarium arsenide (solid precursor)
- Fe_{2}O_{3}: iron(III) oxide (solid precursor)

title: Different fluoride sources indicate alternative chemical incorporation pathways for F dopant
content: The use of samarium(III) fluoride (SmF_{3}) in one route and iron(II) fluoride (FeF_{2}) in the other implies that fluorine can be introduced into SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} from chemically distinct fluoride precursors. This suggests different local chemical pathways for F incorporation (e.g., Sm-bound F versus Fe-bound F in precursors) that plausibly affect fluorine incorporation efficiency, control of nominal $x$, and the identity/concentrations of impurity phases formed during sintering.

title: High-pressure sintering likely reduces As volatility and may improve phase purity compared with unspecified solid-state routes
content: Synthesis performed at 6 GPa and 1250°C (high-pressure sintering) is expected to suppress arsenic volatilization and stabilize volatile stoichiometry during high-temperature reaction. Therefore, the high-pressure route in proposition 1426 likely yields samples with higher phase purity and better control of nominal composition (including fluorine content) than a generic solid-state route that does not invoke such pressure; this could, in turn, affect functional properties (e.g., superconducting transition temperature) although those are not specified here.

extra.notations:
- GPa: gigapascal, unit of pressure (1 GPa = 10^9 Pa)
- 1250°C: sintering temperature reported in 1426
- As: arsenic (volatile component in oxyarsenide synthesis)

title: Normalization trade-off between AF and SC components
content:
Define the squared magnitudes of the superconducting (SC) and antiferromagnetic (AF) sectors by
$$n_{\mathrm{SC}}^{2}\equiv n_{1}^{2}+n_{5}^{2},\qquad 
n_{\mathrm{AF}}^{2}\equiv n_{2}^{2}+n_{3}^{2}+n_{4}^{2}.$$
The fixed‑magnitude constraint $\sum_{a=1}^{5}n_{a}^{2}=1$ then implies
$$n_{\mathrm{SC}}^{2}+n_{\mathrm{AF}}^{2}=1.$$
Thus the SC and AF magnitudes are mutually constrained: increasing one necessarily decreases the other.

extra.notations:
- $n_{a}$: components of the SO(5) superspin ($a=1,\dots,5$). 
- $n_{\mathrm{SC}}^{2}$: combined squared magnitude of SC components ($n_{1},n_{5}$).
- $n_{\mathrm{AF}}^{2}$: combined squared magnitude of AF components ($n_{2},n_{3},n_{4}$).

title: With only the AF‑favoring potential (μ = 0), the minimum is a purely AF superspin
content:
Given the symmetry‑breaking potential
$$V(n)=-\tfrac{g}{2}\bigl(n_{2}^{2}+n_{3}^{2}+n_{4}^{2}\bigr)=-\tfrac{g}{2}\,n_{\mathrm{AF}}^{2}$$
with $g>0$, and the constraint $n_{\mathrm{SC}}^{2}+n_{\mathrm{AF}}^{2}=1$, the energy is minimized by maximizing $n_{\mathrm{AF}}^{2}$. The maximum allowed value is $n_{\mathrm{AF}}^{2}=1$, achieved when $n_{\mathrm{SC}}^{2}=0$. Therefore, in the absence of a chemical‑potential coupling ($\mu=0$) and other competing terms, the classical ground state is purely antiferromagnetic ($n_{2}^{2}+n_{3}^{2}+n_{4}^{2}=1$).

extra.notations:
- $V(n)$: symmetry‑breaking potential, $g>0$.
- $n_{\mathrm{AF}}^{2}$, $n_{\mathrm{SC}}^{2}$: defined as above.
- $\mu$: chemical potential (set to zero in this deduction).

title: Chemical potential coupling to $L_{15}$ biases the superspin into the superconducting sector
content: Let $\mu$ be the chemical potential that enters the effective theory through a term proportional to $-2\mu L_{15}$ (where $L_{15}$ is an SO(5) generator). Since $n_{1}$ and $n_{5}$ are the d‑wave superconducting components ($n_{1}=\Delta^{\dagger}+\Delta$, $n_{5}=-i(\Delta^{\dagger}-\Delta)$), a nonzero $\mu$ that couples to the charge generator identified with $L_{15}$ acts as a symmetry‑breaking bias that tends to favor nonzero expectation values of the SC components (finite $\langle\Delta\rangle$) over the AF components. Thus doping (nonzero $\mu$) plausibly drives the superspin toward the SC plane ($n_{\mathrm{SC}}^{2}$ increases).

extra.notations:
- $\mu$: chemical potential entering as $-2\mu L_{15}$.
- $L_{15}$: SO(5) generator mixing/relating charge and SC directions in the superspin.
- $\Delta$: d‑wave pair operator; $n_{1},n_{5}$ defined in 1127.

title: Mean‑field relation between the complex d‑wave order parameter and SC superspin components
content:
Let $\phi\equiv\langle\Delta\rangle$ be the c‑number d‑wave pairing amplitude (mean‑field expectation). Using $n_{1}=\Delta^{\dagger}+\Delta$ and $n_{5}=-i(\Delta^{\dagger}-\Delta)$, at the level of expectations one has
$$n_{1}= \phi^{*}+\phi = 2\Re\phi,\qquad n_{5}= -i(\phi^{*}-\phi)=2\Im\phi.$$
Hence
$$n_{1}^{2}+n_{5}^{2}=4|\phi|^{2},\quad\text{or}\quad |\phi|^{2}=\tfrac{1}{4}\bigl(n_{1}^{2}+n_{5}^{2}\bigr).$$
Thus the usual mean‑field SC amplitude is directly proportional to the length of the SC projection of the superspin.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta$: d‑wave pair creation operator, $\Delta^{\dagger}=\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{p}g(p)c_{p\uparrow}^{\dagger}c_{-p\downarrow}^{\dagger}$.
- $\phi\equiv\langle\Delta\rangle$: mean‑field complex order parameter.
- $n_{1},n_{5}$: SC superspin components as in 1127.

title: Exact stoichiometric formulas for Hg-1212 and Hg-1223 follow from the general formula
content:
Using the parent general formula $HgBa_{2}Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+\\delta}$ with integer $m$ and oxygen non‑stoichiometry $\\delta$, the compound labels Hg-1212 and Hg-1223 correspond to $m=2$ and $m=3$, respectively. Substituting:
- For Hg-1212 ($m=2$): formula is $HgBa_{2}Ca_{1}Cu_{2}O_{2\\cdot 2 + 2 + \\delta}=HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+\\delta}$.
- For Hg-1223 ($m=3$): formula is $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{2\\cdot 3 + 2 + \\delta}=HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+\\delta}$.
These expressions explicitly display the Ca and Cu stoichiometries and retain the oxygen non‑stoichiometry parameter $\\delta$.

extra.notations:
- $m$: integer number of CuO$_2$ layers per crystallographic unit cell.
- $\\delta$: oxygen non‑stoichiometry (excess or deficiency of oxygen relative to the integer stoichiometry).
- Hg-1212, Hg-1223: conventional short names for the $m=2$ and $m=3$ members of the homologous series.

title: Hg-1212 and Hg-1223 are very likely superconducting members of the Hg‑cuprate family
content: Let $T_{c}$ denote the superconducting transition temperature (onset of superconductivity).
