Proposition 1522 states that the Hg–Ba–Ca–Cu–O homologous family contains superconducting phases (some members exhibit $T_{c}>0$ under standard or sample-dependent conditions). Proposition 1548 identifies Hg-1212 and Hg-1223 as the $m=2$ and $m=3$ members of this family. It is therefore highly plausible that Hg-1212 and Hg-1223 themselves are superconducting compounds (i.e., possess a nonzero $T_{c}$ under appropriate synthesis/oxygenation conditions), though the siblings do not explicitly assert superconductivity for these specific labels.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (onset).
- Hg-1212, Hg-1223: $m=2$ and $m=3$ members of the HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{m-1}$Cu$_{m}$O$_{2m+2+\\delta}$ series.
- $\\delta$: oxygen non‑stoichiometry affecting charge carrier concentration and thus superconducting properties.

title: Hg‑1212 and Hg‑1223 are superconducting members of the homologous series
content: The compounds denoted Hg‑1212 (the m=2 member of HgBa2Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+δ}) and Hg‑1223 (the m=3 member) are superconductors: each exhibits a superconducting transition at a characteristic critical temperature Tc, where Tc is the temperature at which zero electrical resistance and/or the onset of diamagnetism is observed by low‑temperature transport or magnetic measurements.

extra.notations:
- Hg‑1212: the m=2 member of HgBa2Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+δ} (two CuO2 layers per unit cell). 
- Hg‑1223: the m=3 member of HgBa2Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+δ} (three CuO2 layers per unit cell). 
- Tc: superconducting transition (critical) temperature determined from resistivity (zero resistance) or magnetic susceptibility (onset of diamagnetism).

title: Hg‑1223 likely has a higher optimal Tc than Hg‑1212 under comparable doping
content: Under the assumption of comparable oxygen content (doping level) and similar sample quality, the m=3 member Hg‑1223 (three CuO2 layers per unit cell) is likely to exhibit a higher optimal superconducting critical temperature Tc than the m=2 member Hg‑1212 (two CuO2 layers per unit cell).

extra.notations:
- Hg‑1212: m=2 member (two CuO2 layers). 
- Hg‑1223: m=3 member (three CuO2 layers).
- Tc: superconducting critical temperature at optimal doping/oxygenation.

title: High-temperature limit: gauge propagator set by holon susceptibility; fermion scattering prefactor determined by χ_B
content:
Using the notations: $D^{T}(q,\omega)$ is the retarded transverse gauge propagator, $\chi_{F}$ and $\chi_{B}$ are the fermion (spinon) and boson (holon) diamagnetic susceptibilities, $\chi_{d}\equiv\chi_{F}+\chi_{B}$, $\sigma(q)$ the transverse dissipative response, and $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(k,\epsilon_{F})$ the fermion on-shell imaginary self-energy. In the strange-metal regime with $T>T_{BE}$, proposition 527 asserts $\chi_{B}\gg\chi_{F}$ so that $\chi_{d}\approx\chi_{B}$. Combining this with the propagator form $D^{T}(q,\omega)=[i\omega\sigma(q)-\chi_{d}q^{2}]^{-1}$ (propositions 1579 and 502) yields the exact simplification
    $$D^{T}(q,\omega)\approx\big[i\omega\,\sigma(q)-\chi_{B}\,q^{2}\big]^{-1}\quad (T>T_{BE}).$$
    Since proposition 502 derives the non-Fermi-liquid on-shell scaling $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(k,\epsilon_{F})\propto -\xi_{k}^{2/3}$ from coupling to this transverse gauge propagator, it follows necessarily that the $2/3$ energy exponent remains in the high-$T$ regime while the numerical prefactor $C$ entering 
    $$\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(k,\epsilon_{F})\approx -C\,\xi_{k}^{2/3}$$
    is controlled parametrically by $\chi_{B}$ (and by $\sigma(q)$ and the microscopic gauge-fermion coupling). Thus, at $T>T_{BE}$ the amplitude of the gauge-induced non–Fermi-liquid scattering of spinons is set by the holon diamagnetic susceptibility $\chi_{B}$.

extra.notations:
- $D^{T}(q,\omega)$: retarded transverse propagator of the emergent U(1) gauge field. 
- $\chi_{F},\chi_{B}$: fermion and boson diamagnetic susceptibilities. 
- $\chi_{d}=\chi_{F}+\chi_{B}$. 
- $\sigma(q)$: transverse dissipative response of matter. 
- $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(k,\epsilon_{F})$: imaginary part of fermion self-energy evaluated at the Fermi level; $\xi_k$ is energy measured from Fermi level.

title: Approach to holon condensation reduces gauge-mediated spinon scattering (quasiparticle sharpening)
content:
Let $T\downarrow T_{BE}$ denote approaching the holon Bose-condensation temperature from above. I conjecture that as $T\to T_{BE}^{+}$ the effective low-frequency transverse gauge fluctuations felt by spinons become progressively suppressed (and for $T<T_{BE}$ are gapped/Higgsed by the holon condensate), which leads to a reduction of the gauge-induced fermion scattering prefactor $C$ in 
    $$\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(k,\epsilon_{F})\approx -C\,\xi_{k}^{2/3}.$$
    Consequently, spinon quasiparticle lifetimes lengthen (spectral peaks sharpen) on cooling toward $T_{BE}$ from above, and a qualitative change to less singular or gapped gauge dynamics occurs for $T<T_{BE}$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{BE}$: holon Bose–Einstein condensation temperature. 
- $\Sigma^{\prime\prime}(k,\epsilon_{F})$, $\xi_{k}$: as above. 
- "Higgsed": gauge fluctuations acquire a mass due to holon condensation, suppressing low-frequency transverse modes.

title: Temperature-driven crossover of the Hall constant near holon condensation
content: Using $R_{H}=(R_{H}^{F}\chi_{B}+R_{H}^{B}\chi_{F})/(\chi_{B}+\chi_{F})$ with $\chi_{d}=\chi_{F}+\chi_{B}$, I conjecture that the Hall constant exhibits a temperature-driven crossover: for $T\gg T_{BE}$, $\chi_{B}\gg\chi_{F}$ and $R_{H}\approx R_{H}^{B}=1/x$ (as stated). On cooling toward and below $T_{BE}$ the holon contribution to the effective transverse response changes (condensation modifies $\chi_{B}$ and the coupling of the gauge field to the external $H$), so $R_{H}$ will cross over away from $1/x$ toward a value influenced by $R_{H}^{F}$ (or a mixed/vanishing Hall response depending on the condensate dynamics). Thus one should expect measurable deviations from strict $1/x$ scaling of $R_{H}$ as $T$ approaches and drops below $T_{BE}$.

extra.notations:
- $R_{H}$: physical Hall constant. 
- $R_{H}^{F},R_{H}^{B}$: fermion and boson Hall constants. 
- $x$: hole doping (holon density per site). 
- $T_{BE}$: holon condensation temperature.

title: Monotonic and unbounded growth of the disorder-averaged Josephson coupling with transverse variance
content:
Let J(Δ) denote the instantaneous inter-stripe Josephson matrix element, with Δ≡Δ_jY = Y_{j+1}-Y_j the local transverse spacing, J_0>0 a constant prefactor, and α a constant. If the disorder-averaged coupling is given by
$$\langle J\rangle = J_0\exp\!\biggl(\tfrac{\alpha^2}{2}\langle[\Delta Y]^2\rangle\biggr),$$
where σ^2 ≡ ⟨[ΔY]^2⟩ is the mean-squared transverse relative displacement, then (i) ⟨J⟩ is a strictly increasing (monotone) function of σ^2, and (ii) for any target coupling J_* > 0 there exists a finite σ^2 such that ⟨J⟩ ≥ J_* (i.e., ⟨J⟩ can be made arbitrarily large by increasing σ^2).

extra.notations:
- J(Δ): instantaneous Josephson matrix element as a function of transverse spacing Δ.
- J_0: positive constant prefactor.
- α: decay parameter (real constant).
- ΔY: shorthand for Δ_jY = Y_{j+1}-Y_j.
- σ^2 = ⟨[ΔY]^2⟩: mean-squared transverse relative displacement (variance).
- ⟨J⟩: ensemble (or thermal) average of J over transverse fluctuations.

title: Transverse fluctuations plausibly drive a crossover to inter-stripe superconducting coherence
content:
Consider an array of metallic stripes with local Josephson coupling per bond J(Δ) ≈ J_0 exp[-αΔ] and mean-squared transverse relative displacement σ^2 = ⟨[ΔY]^2⟩. It is plausible that, as σ^2 increases sufficiently, the disorder-averaged inter-stripe Josephson energy scale
$$\langle J\rangle = J_0\exp\!\biggl(\tfrac{\alpha^2}{2}\sigma^2\biggr)$$
becomes large enough to (a) overcome phase-disordering effects (thermal and quantum) and (b) dominate competing inter-stripe CDW coupling channels that are sensitive to longitudinal arc-length differences. Under these conditions — for example, in the presence of a nonzero spin gap Δ_s and stripe charge Luttinger parameters that do not strongly suppress Josephson relevance — the system is likely to undergo a crossover (or transition, depending on dimensionality and temperature) from predominantly one-dimensional fluctuating superconducting correlations on individual stripes to global (inter-stripe) superconducting phase coherence across the array, thereby favoring SC order over CDW order.

extra.notations:
- J(Δ), J_0, α, ΔY, σ^2 as in the deduction.
- ⟨J⟩: disorder-averaged Josephson coupling.
- Δ_s: spin gap (energy); its presence stabilizes singlet pairing on a stripe.
- χ_SC, χ_CDW: superconducting and CDW susceptibilities (response functions).

title: Exact discrete formula for the isotope coefficient from two isotope Tc measurements
content:
Let $T_{c,H}$ and $T_{c,D}$ be the measured critical temperatures for the protium-substituted ($^{1}$H) and deuterium-substituted ($^{2}$H) samples respectively, and let $m_{H}$ and $m_{D}$ be the corresponding isotopic masses. If $T_{c}=A\\,m^{-\\alpha}$ with the same $A$ and constant $\\alpha$ for both isotopes, then the isotope coefficient $\\alpha$ is given exactly (for the two-point discrete change) by
$$
\\alpha \;=\; -\\frac{\\ln\\bigl(T_{c,D}/T_{c,H}\\bigr)}{\\ln\\bigl(m_{D}/m_{H}\\bigr)}.
$$
In particular, for $m_{D}=2m_{H}$ (deuterium versus hydrogen) this reduces to the simple form
$$
\\alpha \;=\; \\frac{\\ln\\bigl(T_{c,H}/T_{c,D}\\bigr)}{\\ln 2}.
$$
Using the reported values $T_{c,H}\\approx 249\\ \\mathrm{K}$ and $T_{c,D}\\approx 180\\ \\mathrm{K}$ gives the numerical value $\\alpha\\approx\\dfrac{\\ln(249/180)}{\\ln 2}\\approx 0.467\\approx 0.46$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c,H}$: critical temperature of the hydrogen (protium) sample.  
- $T_{c,D}$: critical temperature of the deuterium sample.  
- $m_{H},m_{D}$: isotopic masses of H and D respectively.  
- $A$: material-specific constant in $T_{c}=A\\,m^{-\\alpha}$.  
- $\\alpha$: isotope coefficient defined by $\\alpha=-d\\ln T_c/d\\ln m$.

title: Measured isotope coefficient near the BCS canonical value supports phonon-mediated pairing
content: The measured isotope coefficient for fcc-LaH_{10} at 150 GPa, $\\alpha\\approx 0.46$ (obtained from comparing $T_{c}\\approx 249\\,$K for LaH_{10} and $T_{c}\\approx 180\\,$K for LaD_{10}), is close to the canonical BCS/harmonic value $\\alpha=0.5$. This proximity makes it likely that the superconducting pairing in fcc-LaH_{10} at 150 GPa is predominantly mediated by electron–phonon interactions (a conventional phonon-mediated mechanism) rather than by a purely electronic pairing mechanism that would produce little or no isotope dependence.

extra.notations:
- $\\alpha$: isotope coefficient (dimensionless).  
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (K).  
- BCS: Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer theory of phonon-mediated superconductivity.

title: Ordering of maximum onset temperatures across m=1,2,3
content:
The maximum observed onset superconducting transition temperatures $T_{co}$ for the optimally doped Hg 1:2:m-1:m compounds satisfy
$$T_{co}(m=3)\approx 164\ \text{K} > T_{co}(m=2)\approx 154\ \text{K} > T_{co}(m=1)\approx 118\ \text{K}.$$
Here $T_{co}(m)$ denotes the maximum observed onset transition temperature for the compound with $m$ CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell under quasihydrostatic pressure.

extra.notations:
- $T_{co}(m)$: maximum observed onset superconducting transition temperature for HgBa$_2$Ca$_{m-1}$Cu$_m$O$_{2m+2+\delta}$ under quasihydrostatic pressure. 
- $m$: number of CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell (integer 1,2,3 in these siblings).

title: Ordering of pressures at which maxima occur across m=1,2,3
content:
The pressures at which the maximum onset temperatures are reported satisfy
$$P_{\mathrm{max}}(m=3)\approx 31\ \text{GPa} > P_{\mathrm{max}}(m=2)\approx 29\ \text{GPa} > P_{\mathrm{max}}(m=1)\approx 24\ \text{GPa}.$$
Here $P_{\mathrm{max}}(m)$ denotes the pressure (under quasihydrostatic conditions) at which the maximum $T_{co}$ is observed for the compound with $m$ CuO$_2$ layers.

extra.notations:
- $P_{\mathrm{max}}(m)$: pressure at which the maximum observed $T_{co}$ occurs for HgBa$_2$Ca$_{m-1}$Cu$_m$O$_{2m+2+\delta}$ under quasihydrostatic pressure.
- GPa: gigapascal.

title: Higher layer count correlates with larger pressure-required maxima and higher attainable $T_{co}$
content: The reported data for $m=1,2,3$ (optimally doped HgBa$_2$Ca$_{m-1}$Cu$_m$O$_{2m+2+\delta}$) are consistent with the conjecture that increasing the number of CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell ($m$) both (a) raises the maximum attainable onset superconducting temperature $T_{co}$ under quasihydrostatic compression and (b) shifts the pressure required to reach that maximum to higher values. Formally: it is plausible that for this homologous series, $dT_{co}^{\max}/dm>0$ and $dP_{\mathrm{max}}/dm>0$ over the range $m=1\to3$, and that microscopic causes may include enhanced interlayer coupling or differing pressure-driven charge transfer with increasing $m$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{co}^{\max}(m)$: maximum observed onset superconducting temperature for given $m$ under quasihydrostatic pressure.
- $P_{\mathrm{max}}(m)$: pressure at which $T_{co}^{\max}(m)$ is observed.
- $m$: number of CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell.

title: Resonance energy decreases with temperature within the superconducting state
content: Given the reported peak energy at $T=5\\,$K of approximately $41\\,$meV and the reported peak energy at $T=81\\,$K (still below $T_{c}=91\\,$K) of approximately $25\\,$meV, the magnetic resonant feature in $YBa_{2}Cu_{3}O_{6.92}$ shifts to lower energy as temperature is increased from $5\\,$K to $81\\,$K within the superconducting state. Symbols: $T$ = absolute temperature; $\\hbar\\omega_{res}(T)$ = resonance energy (meV) at temperature $T$.

extra.notations:
- $T$: absolute temperature (K)
- $\\hbar\\omega_{res}(T)$: energy (meV) of the magnetic resonant peak at temperature $T$
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature (K)

title: Conserved energy-integrated magnetic spectral weight implies redistribution to lower energies
content: Because the energy-integrated magnetic scattering intensity is reported to remain approximately constant between $T=5\\,$K (where the peak is at $\\approx41\\,$meV) and $T=81\\,$K (where the peak is at $\\approx25\\,$meV), the magnetic spectral weight present near $41\\,$meV at low temperature is redistributed to lower energies by $81\\,$K rather than being lost from the measured magnetic channel. Symbols: $I_{E}(T)=\\int_{E_1}^{E_2} S(\\vec{Q},\\hbar\\omega;T)\\,d(\\hbar\\omega)$ denotes the energy-integrated magnetic intensity over the relevant energy window $[E_1,E_2]$ at temperature $T$.

extra.notations:
- $S(\\vec{Q},\\hbar\\omega;T)$: magnetic dynamical structure factor at momentum $\\vec{Q}$, energy $\\hbar\\omega$, and temperature $T$
- $I_{E}(T)$: energy-integrated magnetic intensity over the measured energy window at temperature $T$

title: Resonance likely tracks the superconducting gap (pairing energy) and thus is linked to superconducting correlations
content: It is plausible (probability 0.85) that the observed downward shift of the magnetic resonance energy with increasing temperature within the superconducting state reflects a temperature-dependent superconducting energy scale (e.g., the superconducting gap amplitude $\\Delta(T)$). In this conjecture the resonance energy $\\hbar\\omega_{res}(T)$ is tied to $\\Delta(T)$ so that a reduction of the gap magnitude on warming leads to a lower $\\hbar\\omega_{res}$. Symbols: $\\Delta(T)$ = superconducting gap magnitude at temperature $T$; $\\hbar\\omega_{res}(T)$ = resonance energy at $T$.

extra.notations:
- $\\Delta(T)$: superconducting gap amplitude (energy) at temperature $T$
- $\\hbar\\omega_{res}(T)$: resonance energy at temperature $T$

title: Ordering of maximum observed onset $T_{co}$ for $m=1,2,3$
content:
Using the reported maximum onset superconducting temperatures ($T_{co}$) under quasihydrostatic pressure for the three compounds with $m=1,2,3$, the maxima satisfy
$$T_{co}(m=3)\approx 164\ \text{K} \;>\; T_{co}(m=2)\approx 154\ \text{K}\;>\; T_{co}(m=1)\approx 118\ \text{K}.$$
Here $T_{co}(m)$ denotes the experimentally observed onset superconducting transition temperature for the Hg-based cuprate with $m$ CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell under the cited pressure conditions.

extra.notations:
- $T_{co}(m)$: onset superconducting transition temperature for the compound with layer count $m$.
- $m$: number of CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell (values considered: 1,2,3).

title: Ordering of pressures at which maximum $T_{co}$ are observed
content:
The pressures at which the reported maximum onset temperatures occur satisfy the ordering
$$P_{\max}(m=1)\approx 24\ \text{GPa}\;<\;P_{\max}(m=2)\approx 29\ \text{GPa}\;<\;P_{\max}(m=3)\approx 31\ \text{GPa},$$
where $P_{\max}(m)$ denotes the pressure (quasihydrostatic) at which the maximum observed onset $T_{co}$ for layer count $m$ was reported.

extra.notations:
- $P_{\max}(m)$: pressure at which the maximum observed onset $T_{co}$ for the compound with $m$ layers was reported.
- GPa: gigapascal, unit of pressure.

title: Hg-layer composition and coordination likely set the large pressure sensitivity of $T_c$
content: Let $dT_c/dP$ denote the pressure derivative of the superconducting transition temperature. Given (a) the large, reproducible pressure-induced increases of onset $T_{co}$ in the Hg-based series (Propositions 575 and 578) and (b) the observation that Pb substitution (which alters the Hg layer) suppresses the pressure enhancement while leaving the CuO$_2$ planes intact (Proposition 593), it is plausible that the structural/chemical state of the Hg layer (its linear oxygen coordination and associated voids) is the dominant determinant of the large $dT_c/dP$ in this family. Consequently, targeted chemical modification of the Hg layer (for example partial substitution, modification of oxygen coordination or controlled vacancies) should systematically modulate $dT_c/dP$ and the attainable maximum $T_{co}$ under pressure.

extra.notations:
- $dT_c/dP$: derivative of superconducting transition temperature with respect to pressure.
- Pb substitution: partial replacement of Hg by Pb in the Hg layer.
- Hg layer: the structural layer containing Hg ions and its oxygen coordination in the Hg-based cuprates.

title: Interplay of Hg-layer properties with layer count $m$ likely amplifies achievable $T_{co}$ under pressure
content: The reported data for $m=1,2,3$ show both higher maximum onset $T_{co}$ and slightly higher $P_{\max}$ with increasing $m$. Combining this trend with the attribution of the large pressure response to Hg-layer structural/chemical features (Proposition 593) suggests the plausible conjecture that the effect of the Hg layer on $dT_c/dP$ couples to the number of CuO$_2$ layers: increasing $m$ enhances or amplifies the Hg-layer-driven pressure tuning (for example via modified charge reservoir behavior, altered interlayer coupling, or different strain transfer to the CuO$_2$ planes). Therefore, preserving Hg-layer coordination features while increasing $m$ is likely to produce higher attainable $T_{co}$ under pressure than changing $m$ or the Hg layer independently.

extra.notations:
- $m$: number of CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell.
- $P_{\max}(m)$ and $T_{co}(m)$: pressure of maximum observed onset $T_c$ and that maximum onset $T_c$, respectively.
- Interlayer coupling: electronic or structural coupling between CuO$_2$ layers mediated by intervening layers (including the Hg layer).

title: MgB$_2$ exhibits the Meissner effect for T < 39 K
content: Using $T_{c}$ to denote the superconducting critical temperature, MgB$_{2}$ exhibits magnetic flux expulsion (the Meissner effect) for all temperatures $T$ satisfying $T < 39\ \text{K}$. Here "Meissner effect" means macroscopic expulsion (or strong suppression) of magnetic flux from the bulk when the material is in its superconducting state.

extra.notations:
- $T$: temperature (in kelvin)
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature
- Meissner effect: expulsion of magnetic flux from the bulk upon entering the superconducting state

title: MgB$_2$ has vanishing dc electrical resistance for T < 39 K
content: With $T_{c}=39\,$K as above, magnesium diboride (MgB$_{2}$) has (practically) zero direct-current (dc) electrical resistance for all temperatures $T$ satisfying $T<39\,$K — i.e., it is in the superconducting (zero-resistance) state below $39\,$K.

extra.notations:
- $T$: temperature (in kelvin)
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature
- Zero (vanishing) dc electrical resistance: electrical resistivity indistinguishable from zero on macroscopic dc measurements in the superconducting state

title: Quantum numbers of the superspin components
content: The superconducting components $n_{1},n_{5}$ correspond to the real and imaginary parts of the d‑wave pair creation operator $\Delta^{\dagger}$ and thus carry electric charge $+2$ (and their adjoints carry $-2$); they are spin singlets (total spin $S=0$). The antiferromagnetic components $n_{2},n_{3},n_{4}$ are constructed from particle–hole operators $c_{p+Q,\alpha}^{\dagger}\,\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{a}\,c_{p,\beta}$ and therefore transform as an $S=1$ spin triplet and carry no net electric charge.

extra.notations:
- $n_a$: superspin components ($a\in\{1,\dots,5\}$).
- $\Delta^{\dagger}=\tfrac{1}{2}\sum_p g(p)\,c_{p\uparrow}^{\dagger}c_{-p\downarrow}^{\dagger}$: d‑wave pair creation operator.
- $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$: AF ordering vector; $\sigma^{a}$: Pauli matrices.
- $\mathcal{Q}$: total charge operator; $S_{\alpha}$: total spin operators.

title: Chemical potential coupling breaks SO(5) down to SO(3)×U(1) and biases the charge sector
content: A chemical potential term coupling to the SO(5) generator $L_{15}$ (e.g. a term proportional to $-2\mu L_{15}$) explicitly breaks the full SO(5) symmetry. The remaining symmetry that commutes with this coupling is rotations of the spin triplet ($SO(3)$ acting on $n_{2,3,4}$) together with the $U(1)$ phase rotations generated by $L_{15}$ acting on the superconducting doublet $(n_{1},n_{5})$. Consequently a nonzero $\mu$ biases the charge (superconducting) sector relative to the AF sector.

extra.notations:
- $L_{15}$: SO(5) generator that effects rotations in the $(n_{1},n_{5})$ plane (the charge / SC plane).
- $\mu$: chemical potential coupling (doping parameter).
- $SO(3)$: spin rotation subgroup acting on $(n_{2},n_{3},n_{4})$.
- $U(1)$: phase rotations of the superconducting order parameter.

title: Classical ground state of the simple SO(5) potential with fixed-length superspin is purely antiferromagnetic
content:
For the model with a fixed‑magnitude superspin $\sum_{a=1}^{5}n_{a}^{2}=1$ and a symmetry‑breaking potential
$$V(n)=-\tfrac{g}{2}\bigl(n_2^2+n_3^2+n_4^2\bigr)\quad(g>0),$$
the classical energy (with no other competing terms) is minimized by maximizing $n_2^2+n_3^2+n_4^2$. Hence the minimizing configuration satisfies $n_2^2+n_3^2+n_4^2=1$ and $n_1=n_5=0$, i.e. a pure antiferromagnetic state with vanishing superconducting components.

extra.notations:
- $n_a$: superspin components with fixed length $\sum_{a}n_a^2=1$.
- $V(n)=-\tfrac{g}{2}(n_2^2+n_3^2+n_4^2)$: explicit symmetry‑breaking potential with $g>0$ favoring AF.

title: La[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs with x>4% is in a superconducting (zero dc resistivity) state at low temperature
content: Let La[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs denote lanthanum iron oxypnictide with nominal fluorine fraction x (atomic fraction). "Zero resistivity" means the measured dc electrical resistivity ρ(T) falls to the instrumental zero, which by the authors' usage signals a superconducting state. Then, for samples with nominal x>4 at.% (x>0.04), there exist experimentally observed temperatures in the low-T interval 5–22 K at which ρ(T)=0; hence those samples are in the superconducting state (vanishing dc resistivity) at temperatures within 5–22 K.

extra.notations:
- La[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs: lanthanum iron oxypnictide with nominal fluorine substitution fraction x.
- x: nominal atomic fraction of F substituting O (dimensionless; x>0.04 means >4 at.%).
- ρ(T): dc electrical resistivity as a function of temperature T.

title: Fluorine substitution likely provides electron doping and lattice contraction that correlate with superconductivity in RFeAsO (LnFeAsO) compounds
content: For RFeAsO-type compounds (R = La, Sm, etc.), substitution of F^- for O^{2-} at nominal fraction x likely donates extra electrons into the FeAs layers (electron doping). In the closely related SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_x system, increasing x produces a contraction of lattice parameters a and c (tetragonal P4/nmm structure). Combining that structural trend with the observed emergence of zero resistivity for La[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs at x>4 at.% implies it is plausible that fluorine substitution both (i) electron-dopes the FeAs conduction layers and (ii) contracts the lattice; these changes are correlated with, and plausibly contribute to, the appearance of superconductivity in the RFeAsO family.

extra.notations:
- x: nominal F fraction substituting O.
- a, c: tetragonal lattice parameters (basal plane edge and unit-cell height).
- Electron doping: increase in conduction electron count in FeAs layers per formula unit due to substitution.

title: Superconductivity likely emerges as F-doping suppresses the 150 K / 100 K normal-state anomalies in LaOFeAs
content: In undoped LaOFeAs there are normal-state anomalies: a sudden resistivity decrease (T_ann) near ≈150 K and a resistivity minimum (T_min) near ≈100 K. Proposition 12 reports that T_min decreases with F-doping, and propositions 12 and 1572 report that samples with nominal x>4 at.% exhibit zero resistivity at low temperature (superconductivity). It is therefore plausible that increasing F content suppresses or shifts the high-temperature anomalies (structural and/or magnetic transitions often associated with those features) and that superconductivity appears once those anomalies are sufficiently weakened or removed.

extra.notations:
- T_ann ≈ 150 K: temperature of a sudden resistivity decrease in undoped LaOFeAs (associated in the literature with structural/magnetic ordering).
- T_min ≈ 100 K: temperature of a resistivity minimum in undoped LaOFeAs.
- x>4 at.%: nominal fluorine content greater than 4 atomic percent.

title: Unit-cell volume of tetragonal SmFeAsO from reported lattice parameters
content:
Given the in-plane lattice parameter a and the out-of-plane lattice parameter c for a tetragonal unit cell,
the primitive (conventional) unit-cell volume is V = a^2 c. Using a = 0.3940 nm and c = 0.8501 nm (values reported for SmFeAsO),
the unit-cell volume is V = (0.3940 nm)^2 × 0.8501 nm = 0.131966... nm^3 ≈ 0.13197 nm^3, which equals 131.97 Å^3.
Here V denotes the conventional tetragonal unit-cell volume.

extra.notations:
- V: conventional tetragonal unit-cell volume (nm^3 or Å^3)
- a: in-plane lattice parameter (nm)
- c: out-of-plane lattice parameter (nm)
- 1 nm^3 = 1000 Å^3

title: Fluorine substitution on oxygen is likely to induce superconductivity in SmFeAsO
content:
Let LnOFeAs denote the rare-earth iron oxypnictide family and SmFeAsO the fluorine-free parent (x = 0).
Proposition 1533 reports that La[O_{1-x}F_x]FeAs (Ln = La) with nominal fluoride fraction x > 4% shows zero dc resistivity (a superconducting state) between 5 K and 22 K.
Propositions 177 and 1573 establish that SmFeAsO shares the same tetragonal ZrCuSiAs-type structure with lattice parameters a = 0.3940 nm and c = 0.8501 nm.
Because SmFeAsO and LaFeAsO are members of the same structural family (same parent structure) and fluorine substitution on O is an established electron-doping route in this family,
it is plausible that substituting F for O in SmFeAsO (forming Sm[O_{1-x}F_x]FeAs with sufficiently large x) will likewise induce a superconducting state with vanishing dc resistivity for some range of temperatures.
The exact critical fluoride concentration x_c and the resulting superconducting transition temperature T_c may differ from the La-based material due to differences in ionic size and electronic structure of Sm vs La.

extra.notations:
- LnO_{1-x}F_xFeAs: rare-earth iron oxypnictide with nominal fluoride fraction x on the oxygen site
- x: nominal atomic fraction of F substituting for O (dimensionless, e.g., x = 0.04 corresponds to 4 at.%)
- "zero dc resistivity": measured dc resistivity falls to instrumental zero within the stated temperature range
- T_c: superconducting transition temperature

title: Shared stoichiometric precursors: SmAs, Fe, Fe₂O₃
content: Both sibling propositions explicitly identify samarium arsenide (SmAs), elemental iron (Fe), and iron(III) oxide (Fe₂O₃) as components of the stoichiometric precursor mixtures used in solid-state syntheses of SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}. Therefore, if those siblings are true, it is necessarily true that SmAs, Fe and Fe₂O₃ constitute a common set of viable precursors for producing SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} by solid-state methods (including the high‑pressure variant mentioned in one sibling).

extra.notations:
- $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$: fluorine‑doped samarium iron oxyarsenide, $x$ is nominal fluorine content
- $SmAs$: samarium arsenide (precursor)
- $Fe$: elemental iron (precursor)
- $Fe_{2}O_{3}$: iron(III) oxide (precursor)

title: SmF₃ as the fluorine source can be incorporated into stoichiometric mixtures (including high‑pressure routes) to set nominal $x$
content: SmF₃ (samarium(III) fluoride) is a plausible and likely fluorine source that can be included in the stoichiometric precursor mixture (together with SmAs, Fe, Fe₂O₃) used to synthesize SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} by solid‑state methods. By varying the amount of SmF₃ in the precursor mixture, the nominal fluorine content $x$ is likely adjustable. This inclusion is also plausibly compatible with the high‑pressure solid‑state variant mentioned in one sibling.

extra.notations:
- $SmF_{3}$: samarium(III) fluoride, proposed fluorine source in precursor mixture
- $x$: nominal fluorine fraction in SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}

title: LTT is a necessary condition for the 1/8 superconductivity suppression; absence of LTT implies absence of suppression
content: If the anomalous suppression of bulk superconductivity near hole concentration $x\\approx 1/8$ is contingent upon (i.e., requires) a structural transition from the low-temperature orthorhombic (LTO) phase to the low-temperature tetragonal (LTT) phase, then any specimen of a La2CuO4-derived cuprate that does not undergo an LTO→LTT transition at low temperature will not exhibit that anomalous suppression at $x\\approx 1/8$. Here, "contingent upon" is taken to mean a necessary condition: LTT (the low-temperature tetragonal structural environment) must be present for the suppression to occur.

extra.notations:
- $x$: hole concentration per Cu site (doping level), unitless (e.g., $x\\approx 1/8$). 
- LTO: low-temperature orthorhombic crystal phase.
- LTT: low-temperature tetragonal crystal phase.

title: Suppression at $x\\approx 1/8$ likely involves stabilization of spatially modulated electronic (stripe-like) order by the LTT phase
content: Given that the LTO and LTT structural phases have different effects on electronic order in the CuO$_2$ planes (they alter tendencies toward spatially modulated electronic states such as charge or spin stripe order) and that the anomalous suppression of superconductivity at $x\\approx 1/8$ requires the LTT environment, it is plausible that the suppression arises because the LTT phase stabilizes or enhances spatially modulated electronic order (charge and/or spin stripes) that competes with and weakens bulk superconductivity.

extra.notations:
- LTT: low-temperature tetragonal crystal phase.
- LTO: low-temperature orthorhombic crystal phase.
- CuO$_2$: copper–oxygen planes where electronic order (including superconductivity and stripes) resides.
- "Spatially modulated electronic order": periodic modulations of charge density or spin density within the CuO$_2$ planes (commonly referred to as "stripes").

title: High-Tc (~200 K) superconductivity in compressed H2S samples is attributable to an Im-3m H3S phase, not pristine H2S
content: If the theoretical result that under high static pressure a van-der-Waals compound related to H2S transforms to a stable Im-3m crystal of stoichiometry H$_3$S with $T_{c}\!\approx\!190$–$200\,$K (Proposition 199) and the experimental conclusion that the observed $T_{c}\!\approx\!203\,$K phase in compressed H$_2$S experiments is H$_3$S with Im-3m symmetry formed by pressure-induced decomposition/annealing (Proposition 214) are both true, then the superconductivity observed near $200\,$K in those high-pressure H$_2$S experiments necessarily originates from a chemically distinct Im-3m H$_3$S phase produced under pressure, and not from pristine H$_2$S. This conclusion identifies the superconducting carrier phase (Im-3m H$_3$S) as distinct in stoichiometry and crystal symmetry from H$_2$S.

extra.notations:
- H$_3$S: sulfur trihydride (three hydrogen atoms per sulfur atom)  
- Im-3m: body-centered-cubic space-group symmetry (crystal structure)  
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (onset of zero resistance), in kelvin (K)  
- GPa: gigapascal (10$^{9}$ pascal), unit of pressure

title: Stabilization of Im-3m H3S with $T_{c}\!\approx\!200\,$K likely requires pressures on the order of or above ~180 GPa and an annealing step
content: Combining the theoretical threshold that the transformation to Im-3m H$_3$S occurs at pressures exceeding about $180\,$GPa (Proposition 199) with the experimental statement that formation of the high-$T_{c}$ phase requires annealing (heating to room temperature at high pressure) to overcome kinetic barriers (Proposition 214) yields the plausible inference that producing the Im-3m H$_3$S phase with $T_{c}\!\approx\!200\,$K in practice typically requires applying pressures on the order of $\gtrsim 180\,$GPa together with a high-pressure annealing (thermal) step.

extra.notations:
- $P$: pressure in gigapascals (GPa)  
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (K)  
- Annealing: heating the compressed sample (e.g., to room temperature) while under high pressure to allow kinetically hindered transformations

title: Formation of Im-3m H3S from H2S under pressure likely involves hydrogen redistribution and may produce sulfur- or hydrogen-containing byproducts
content: Given the theoretical description of a van-der-Waals compound written as $(\mathrm{H_{2}S})_{2}\mathrm{H_{2}}$ that transforms to H$_3$S (Proposition 199) and the experimental statement that H$_2$S decomposes under pressure to yield the H$_3$S phase (Proposition 214), a plausible chemical pathway is that hydrogen atoms/molecules migrate under pressure to change the local stoichiometry from H$_2$S to H$_3$S. This hydrogen redistribution implies that the transformation may be accompanied by the formation of sulfur-rich (elemental S) or hydrogen-rich (H$_2$) byproducts or rearranged host structures, depending on the exact local stoichiometry and starting sample composition.

extra.notations:
- H$_2$S: hydrogen sulfide (starting material)  
- $(\mathrm{H_{2}S})_{2}\mathrm{H_{2}}$: a van-der-Waals assembled stoichiometry referenced in theoretical work (two H$_2$S units plus an H$_2$ unit per formula grouping)  
- Byproducts: elemental sulfur (S) and/or molecular hydrogen (H$_2$), depending on reaction pathway

title: BCS/harmonic scaling: $T_c$ varies as $m^{-1/2}$
content: Given the mass-scaling relation $T_{c}=A\,m^{-\alpha}$ (where $T_{c}$ is the critical temperature, $m$ the isotopic mass, $A$ a material-specific constant, and $\alpha$ the isotope coefficient) and the BCS/harmonic canonical value $\alpha=0.5$, it follows necessarily that for a material satisfying the BCS assumptions in the harmonic approximation the critical temperature scales as $T_{c}=A\,m^{-1/2}$ (equivalently $T_{c}\propto m^{-1/2}$).

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (K)
- $m$: isotopic mass (same units used consistently)
- $A$: material-specific constant independent of $m$
- $\alpha$: isotope coefficient defined by $T_{c}=A\,m^{-\alpha}$

title: Deviations of measured $\alpha$ from $0.5$ imply additional physics beyond BCS/harmonic
content: If an experimental determination of the isotope coefficient $\alpha$ for a superconducting material differs substantially from the BCS/harmonic value $\alpha=0.5$, then it is likely that at least one of the BCS/harmonic assumptions is violated. Plausible causes include (a) significant anharmonicity of lattice vibrations, (b) strong-coupling electron-phonon effects that modify the simple mass scaling, (c) multiband superconductivity with differing band-resolved isotope shifts, (d) non-phononic pairing mechanisms (e.g., electronic/magnetic pairing), or (e) important Coulomb pseudopotential or retardation corrections that alter the effective isotope exponent.

extra.notations:
- $\alpha$: experimentally measured isotope coefficient defined by $T_{c}=A\,m^{-\alpha}$
- $\alpha_{\text{BCS}}=0.5$: canonical value under BCS theory with harmonic phonons

title: Doped compound in ThCr2Si2-type has Z = 2 formula units per cell
content: If a compound crystallizes in the ThCr$_{2}$Si$_{2}$ structure type with space group $I4/mmm$ (the standard ThCr$_{2}$Si$_{2}$ / 14/mmm setting), the conventional unit cell for that structure contains $Z=2$ formula units. Proposition 791 reports that $BaFe_{1.8}Co_{0.2}As_{2}$ crystallizes in the ThCr$_{2}$Si$_{2}$ structure (space group $I4/mmm$); therefore $BaFe_{1.8}Co_{0.2}As_{2}$ has $Z=2$ formula units per conventional unit cell. (Proposition 780 explicitly states $Z=2$ for the parent $BaFe_{2}As_{2}$; the deduction here is that the same $Z$ applies to the Co-doped material because it shares the same structure type and space group.)

extra.notations:
- ThCr$_{2}$Si$_{2}$ / $I4/mmm$: the ThCr$_{2}$Si$_{2}$ structure type in the body-centered tetragonal setting (space group No. 139).  
- $Z$: number of formula units per conventional unit cell.
- $BaFe_{1.8}Co_{0.2}As_{2}$: Co-doped barium iron arsenide with nominal composition given.

title: Co substitution produces an anisotropic lattice response (c-axis contraction, a nearly unchanged)
content: The observation that $BaFe_{1.8}Co_{0.2}As_{2}$ retains the ThCr$_{2}$Si$_{2}$ ($I4/mmm$) structure while its refined lattice constants show a $0.26\%$ decrease in the $c$-axis and an $a$-axis value unchanged within experimental uncertainty implies an anisotropic structural response to Co substitution: the primary crystallographic effect of Co doping at the Fe site (10% substitution) is a small contraction along the interlayer ($c$) direction with negligible change to the in-plane ($a$) lattice parameter. This suggests that Co incorporation perturbs interlayer spacing or bonding (along $c$) more than the in-plane Fe–Fe/Fe–As network.

extra.notations:
- $a$, $c$: tetragonal lattice parameters (in-plane and out-of-plane, respectively).  
- $BaFe_{1.8}Co_{0.2}As_{2}$: nominal Co-doped composition (10% of Fe replaced by Co).
- "Anisotropic lattice response": a change in one crystallographic direction (here $c$) not matched by a comparable change in another direction ($a$).

title: Resonance energy is well separated above the low-energy spin gap
content: Given the reported spin gap energy $E_{G}=28\pm1$ meV (no sizeable magnetic scattering observed below 25 meV) and a pronounced magnetic peak at $\sim 41$ meV, the peak at 41 meV lies at least $12$ meV above the upper edge of the reported gap. Quantitatively, the separation $\Delta E \equiv 41\ \text{meV} - E_{G} = 13\pm1\ \text{meV}$, so $\Delta E \geq 12\ \text{meV}$.

extra.notations:
- $E_{G}$: energy of the low-energy spin gap (in meV). 
- $\Delta E$: energy separation between the 41 meV peak and $E_{G}$ (in meV).

title: 41 meV feature is more sharply localized in momentum than typical excitations in the spectrum
content: The magnetic feature centered near 41 meV has an in-plane q-width of $0.18\pm0.02$ r.l.u., which is significantly narrower than the reported average q-width of $0.27\pm0.02$ r.l.u. elsewhere in the spectrum. Therefore the 41 meV feature is a distinctly narrower (more sharply momentum-localized) magnetic excitation than the spectrum average.

extra.notations: - q-width: full width in reciprocal-lattice units (r.l.u.) of the magnetic peak in momentum space.

title: 41 meV peak is the superconductivity-induced spin resonance (bilayer odd channel)
content: The pronounced, relatively sharp magnetic peak at $\sim 41$ meV, which is absent at energies below $\sim 25$–28 meV and shows intensity modulation along the (001) direction (the crystallographic $c$-axis), is plausibly the superconductivity-induced spin resonance (a collective spin exciton) in $Y$Ba$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6.92}$. The (001) intensity modulation is consistent with bilayer-odd symmetry of the excitation in the $CuO_2$ bilayers.

extra.notations:
- "spin resonance": a superconductivity-enhanced collective magnetic excitation appearing below $T_c$ at a well-defined energy.
- "bilayer-odd": an interlayer (along $c$) symmetry where adjacent CuO$_2$ layers oscillate out of phase, producing characteristic (001) modulation.

title: Low-energy spectral weight is likely redistributed into the 41 meV resonance upon entering the superconducting state
content: Because there is negligible magnetic scattering below $\sim25$ meV at $T=5$ K while a pronounced 41 meV peak carries significant magnetic intensity, it is plausible that spectral weight removed from low energies in the superconducting state has been redistributed to the resonance near 41 meV (i.e., partial spectral-weight transfer from $<E_G$ to the resonance energy).

extra.notations: - "spectral weight": integrated magnetic intensity over a specified energy window (e.g., $\int \mathrm{Im}\,\chi(\omega)\,d\omega$).

title: Bulk superconductivity is intrinsic to the Co-doped FeAs planes
content: BaFe1.8Co0.2As2 (BaFe2As2 with approximately 0.2 Co substituted on Fe sites) is a bulk superconductor with onset transition temperature $T_{c}^{onset}=22\\,$K, and this superconductivity persists despite substitutional (in-plane) disorder in the FeAs layers introduced by Co. Therefore the superconducting state is an intrinsic property of the Co-doped BaFe2As2 system and is realized within the FeAs planes (the layers that host the substitutional disorder), i.e., superconductivity originates in and survives disorder within the FeAs planes.

extra.notations:
- BaFe1.8Co0.2As2: barium iron arsenide with nominal substitution of 0.2 Co per formula unit on Fe sites.
- $T_{c}^{onset}$: onset superconducting transition temperature.
- "in-plane disorder": substitutional disorder located on the Fe sites within the FeAs planes.

title: Constraint on pairing symmetry from tolerance to in-plane Co disorder
content: The persistence of bulk superconductivity in BaFe1.8Co0.2As2 despite appreciable substitutional disorder in the FeAs planes (nominal 0.2 Co per formula unit) and the retention of the parent tetragonal I4/mmm structure with only a small ($\\sim0.26\\%$) reduction of the $c$-axis suggest that the superconducting order parameter is relatively robust to the type of impurity scattering introduced by Co. Plausibly, this favors either (a) a sign-preserving (conventional-like) $s$-wave order parameter in which nonmagnetic scattering is weakly pair-breaking, or (b) a sign-changing state (e.g., $s_{\\pm}$) only if the effective interband (pair-breaking) scattering from Co is small. Thus the observed robustness constrains candidate pairing symmetries: strong pair-breaking by nonmagnetic in-plane impurities (as predicted for some sign-changing states under strong interband scattering) is disfavored unless scattering is weak or other mitigating mechanisms operate.

extra.notations:
- Co: cobalt substituting Fe sites (nominal concentration 0.2 per formula unit).
- $s$-wave: superconducting order parameter with the same sign on all Fermi-surface sheets.
- $s_{\\pm}$: sign-changing superconducting order parameter with opposite signs on different Fermi-surface sheets.
- "interband scattering": impurity scattering processes that transfer quasiparticles between different Fermi-surface sheets and can be strongly pair-breaking for sign-changing order parameters.

title: THz-induced conductivity necessarily depends on dephasing and population-damping rates
content:
Let $\\rho_{11}(\\tau)$ be the donor ground-state (1s) population at the end of a rectangular THz pulse of duration $\\tau$, and let the THz-induced change in electrical conductivity be denoted $\\Delta\\sigma_{THz}$. From the siblings we have (i) $\\Delta\\sigma_{THz}\\propto(1-\\rho_{11}(\\tau))$ (the conductivity signal is proportional to the fraction excited out of 1s) and (ii) $\\rho_{11}(\\tau)$ evolves under the transverse dephasing rate $\\gamma_{2}$ and the population/damping rate $\\gamma_{3}$. Therefore
$$\\Delta\\sigma_{THz}\\propto 1-\\rho_{11}(\\tau;E_{THz},\\tau,\\gamma_{2},\\gamma_{3}),$$
i.e. the observed THz-induced conductivity change at time $\\tau$ necessarily depends on the dephasing rate $\\gamma_{2}$ and the population/damping rate $\\gamma_{3}$ (as well as on the pulse parameters $E_{THz}$ and $\\tau$).

extra.notations:
- $\\rho_{11}(\\tau)$: population of the donor ground (1s) state at time $\\tau$.  
- $1-\\rho_{11}(\\tau)$: fraction of donors excited out of 1s by time $\\tau$.  
- $\\Delta\\sigma_{THz}$: change in electrical conductivity induced by the THz pulse.  
- $\\gamma_{2}$: transverse dephasing rate (coherence decay).  
- $\\gamma_{3}$: population/damping rate (population relaxation and/or ionization).  
- $E_{THz}$, $\\tau$: peak field amplitude and pulse duration, respectively.

title: In the coherent-drive regime, increasing transverse dephasing reduces the THz-induced conductivity signal
content: Under the assumption that the THz pulse duration $\\tau$ is short enough that coherent Rabi-like dynamics govern excitation (pulse shorter or comparable to population relaxation times) and that $\\gamma_{2}$ primarily reduces coherence without directly producing free carriers, increasing the transverse dephasing rate $\\gamma_{2}$ will reduce the population transfer out of the 1s state during the pulse. Since the THz-induced conductivity change is proportional to the excited fraction $1-\\rho_{11}(\\tau)$, larger $\\gamma_{2}$ in this coherent regime is expected to decrease $\\Delta\\sigma_{THz}$.

extra.notations:
- $\\tau$: pulse duration; "coherent-drive regime" means $\\tau$ is short relative to inverse population-damping rates and Rabi periods.  
- $\\gamma_{2}$: transverse dephasing (coherence decay) that suppresses coherent population transfer.  
- $\\Delta\\sigma_{THz}\\propto(1-\\rho_{11}(\\tau))$: conductivity signal.

title: When $\\gamma_{3}$ represents photoionization, increasing $\\gamma_{3}$ tends to increase the measured conductivity
content: If the population/damping rate $\\gamma_{3}$ is dominated by photoionization (i.e., transfer of bound electrons to the conduction band producing mobile carriers), then a larger $\\gamma_{3}$ reduces the bound 1s population $\\rho_{11}(\\tau)$ more rapidly during/after the pulse and simultaneously produces free carriers that contribute directly to electrical conductivity. Under these conditions, an increase in $\\gamma_{3}$ is expected to increase the THz-induced conductivity change $\\Delta\\sigma_{THz}\\propto(1-\\rho_{11}(\\tau))$.

extra.notations:
- $\\gamma_{3}$: population/damping rate; here assumed to be dominated by ionization (bound→continuum transitions producing mobile carriers).  
- $\\Delta\\sigma_{THz}$: change in conductivity proportional to excited/ionized fraction.

title: Channel decomposition of the single-particle self-energy
content:
The single-particle self-energy $\Sigma$ (a functional of the dressed Green's function $G$) decomposes as a sum of contributions originating from each diagram class included in the Luttinger–Ward functional $\Phi$:
$$\Sigma = \Sigma_{\rm HF} + \Sigma_{\rm long} + \Sigma_{\rm trans} + \Sigma_{\rm pp},$$
where $\Sigma_{\rm HF}$ is the Hartree–Fock contribution, $\Sigma_{\rm long}$ arises from longitudinal (density and longitudinal spin) fluctuation (ring) diagrams, $\Sigma_{\rm trans}$ from transverse spin-fluctuation diagrams, and $\Sigma_{\rm pp}$ from particle–particle (pairing) fluctuation diagrams. Each term is given explicitly by the functional derivative of the corresponding class contribution to $\Phi$ with respect to $G$.
(All symbols are defined in Notations.)

extra.notations:
- $\Sigma$: single-particle self-energy (matrix in space/time or momentum/frequency).
- $G$: dressed single-particle Green's function.
- $\Phi$: Luttinger–Ward free-energy functional, here written as a sum of diagram-class contributions.
- $\Sigma_{\rm HF}$: Hartree–Fock self-energy contribution.
- $\Sigma_{\rm long}$: self-energy from longitudinal (density and longitudinal spin) fluctuation diagrams.
- $\Sigma_{\rm trans}$: self-energy from transverse spin fluctuation diagrams.
- $\Sigma_{\rm pp}$: self-energy from particle–particle fluctuation diagrams.

title: Conservation laws and Ward identities hold within FLEX
content:
Because the FLEX approximation is derived from a Luttinger–Ward free-energy functional $\Phi$, it is a conserving approximation: the approximation satisfies the continuity equations for conserved quantities (particle number, energy, momentum) and the corresponding Ward identities that relate the single-particle self-energy $\Sigma$ and the two-particle vertex/response functions $L$. Consequently, quantities computed self-consistently from $\Phi$ (e.g., $G$, $\Sigma$, and $L$) obey the same conservation constraints implied by the underlying symmetries of the Hubbard Hamiltonian and are consistent with Luttinger's theorem within the approximation.
(All symbols are defined in Notations.)

extra.notations:
- $\Phi$: Luttinger–Ward free-energy functional from which the approximation is derived.
- $\Sigma$: single-particle self-energy.
- $G$: dressed single-particle Green's function.
- $L$: two-particle response function (particle-hole or particle-particle).
- Ward identities: exact relations following from continuous symmetries that connect variations of $\Sigma$ and the two-particle vertices / response functions.

title: FLEX generically yields spin-fluctuation–mediated d-wave pairing on the 2D square-lattice Hubbard model
content:
For the two-dimensional repulsive Hubbard model on a square lattice (nearest-neighbor hopping $t$, on-site repulsion $U$) near half-filling ($n\lesssim 1$), the FLEX approximation — because it includes both spin-fluctuation (longitudinal and transverse) and particle–particle channels in $\Phi$ and treats them self-consistently — typically produces an effective pairing interaction peaked at the antiferromagnetic wavevector $\mathbf{Q}=(\pi,\pi)$. Such a momentum structure of the pairing kernel favors a sign-changing gap on the Fermi surface with $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ symmetry. Therefore FLEX commonly predicts a finite d-wave superconducting transition temperature $T_{d}$ when $U$ and doping place the system in a regime with sufficiently strong low-energy AF spin fluctuations.
Definitions: $T_{d}$ is the superconducting transition temperature for the $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ order parameter; $\mathbf{Q}=(\pi,\pi)$ is the nesting/AF wavevector on the square lattice.

extra.notations:
- $t$: nearest-neighbor hopping amplitude (kinetic energy scale).
- $U$: on-site repulsive interaction.
- $n$: electronic filling (average electrons per site).
- $\mathbf{Q}=(\pi,\pi)$: antiferromagnetic wavevector on the square lattice.
- $T_{d}$: superconducting transition temperature for $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ pairing.

title: Momentum- and frequency-dependent complex self-energy with quasiparticle renormalization and finite lifetime
content: The single-particle self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ produced by the fluctuation-exchange (FLEX) approximation is a complex-valued function of momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and real frequency $\omega$ (or Matsubara frequency $i\omega_n$). Its real part $\Re\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ necessarily renormalizes the single-particle dispersion (shifts and reshapes the quasiparticle band), while its imaginary part $\Im\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ necessarily yields a finite quasiparticle scattering rate (inverse lifetime). These statements follow because FLEX is defined by a Luttinger–Ward functional $\Phi[G]$ built from diagram classes and the self-energy is given by the functional derivative $\Sigma=\delta\Phi/\delta G$, together with the explicit statement that the scheme is solved self-consistently treating full momentum and frequency dependence.

extra.notations:
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: single-particle self-energy as a function of momentum and real frequency. 
- $\Re\Sigma$, $\Im\Sigma$: real and imaginary parts of $\Sigma$.
- $G$: dressed single-particle Green's function.
- $\Phi$: Luttinger–Ward free-energy functional.

title: Conservation laws, Ward identities, and thermodynamic consistency follow from $\Phi$-derivability
content: Because the FLEX approximation is derived from a Luttinger–Ward functional $\Phi[G]$ (i.e. it is $\Phi$-derivable), it necessarily yields a conserving approximation: particle-number, energy and momentum conservation laws are satisfied at the level of the approximate dynamics; associated Ward identities relating one- and two-particle quantities hold; and thermodynamic consistency (agreement between conserved-density susceptibilities computed from functional derivatives of thermodynamic potentials and from two-particle correlation functions) follows.

extra.notations:
- $\Phi$: Luttinger–Ward functional generating the approximation.
- $\Sigma=\delta\Phi/\delta G$: self-energy functional relation.
- Ward identities: relations following from global/local symmetries that connect variations of $\Sigma$ and vertex functions.

title: FLEX permits construction of a pairing kernel and can predict a d-wave transition temperature $T_d$
content: Because FLEX includes particle–particle (pairing) fluctuation diagrams in the $\Phi$ functional and yields two-particle kernels via functional differentiation, one can construct an effective pairing interaction (Bethe–Salpeter kernel $K$ in the particle–particle channel) and solve the linearized Bethe–Salpeter / eigenvalue problem to obtain a superconducting instability. Thus FLEX can be used to predict a d-wave superconducting transition temperature $T_d$ (the temperature where the leading eigenvalue reaches unity), if a d-wave pairing eigenfunction is favored by the computed kernel.

extra.notations:
- $K$: Bethe–Salpeter kernel in the particle–particle channel (effective pairing interaction).
- $T_d$: superconducting transition temperature for d-wave symmetry (temperature where the linearized gap equation has a nontrivial solution).
- $\Phi$: Luttinger–Ward functional including particle–particle diagrams.

title: Numerical results (spectra, susceptibilities, $T_d$) are sensitive to Matsubara discretization and analytic continuation choices
content: Practical implementations of FLEX that discretize the Brillouin zone and Matsubara frequencies and use Padé (or other) analytic continuation to obtain real-frequency spectra will generally produce numerical results (spectral functions, susceptibilities, and any extracted $T_d$) that depend non-negligibly on the number of Matsubara points, frequency cutoff, momentum grid density, and the Padé approximant parameters (e.g. number of poles $N$). Consequently, quantitative predictions carry numerical uncertainties that must be assessed by convergence checks.

extra.notations:
- $N$: number of points used in a Padé approximant for analytic continuation.
- Matsubara frequencies: discrete imaginary frequencies $i\omega_n$ used in finite-temperature Green's-function calculations.
- Padé approximant: rational-function analytic-continuation method from Matsubara to real frequencies.

title: FLEX is most reliable in the weak-to-intermediate coupling regime and can be unreliable at strong coupling
content: Because FLEX is constructed by summing selected perturbative diagram classes (Hartree–Fock, ring diagrams, transverse spin, and particle–particle fluctuations) around the dressed Green's function, its controlled regime is the weak-to-intermediate interaction strength $U$ (comparable to or smaller than the electronic bandwidth). At large $U$ (strong coupling) where local-moment physics and higher-order vertex corrections become essential, FLEX is likely to give qualitatively incorrect results (e.g., underestimate Mott localization, over- or under-estimate ordering tendencies).

extra.notations:
- $U$: on-site Hubbard interaction energy.
- bandwidth: characteristic noninteracting electronic energy scale set by hopping $t$ (e.g., $W\sim 8t$ on a square lattice with nearest-neighbor hopping).

title: Temperature-driven loss of low-energy spin spectral weight is an amplitude (not a linewidth/correlation-length) effect in the high-T regime
content: If the imaginary part of the dynamical magnetic susceptibility $\operatorname{Im}\{\chi(\vec{q},\hbar\omega)\}$ at $\hbar\omega=10\ \text{meV}$ for YBa$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{6.92}$ shows a maximum near $T^{*}\approx 120$–$130\ \text{K}$ (i.e. low-energy spectral weight decreases on warming above $T^{*}$), and in the same high-temperature regime the characteristic energy scale $\Gamma_{\omega}=30\ \text{meV}$ and the antiferromagnetic correlation length $\zeta$ remain constant while the correlated scattering intensity decreases, then the observed temperature-induced weakening of the correlated low-energy magnetic response (the reduction of $\operatorname{Im}\{\chi(\vec{q},10\ \text{meV})\}$ for $T\gtrsim T^{*}$) must be accounted for by an overall reduction of the amplitude (spectral-weight normalization) of the spin-fluctuation spectrum at those $\vec{q}$ and $\omega$, rather than by systematic broadening of the energy linewidth $\Gamma_{\omega}$ or by shortening of the spatial correlation length $\zeta$.

extra.notations:
- $\operatorname{Im}\{\chi(\vec{q},\hbar\omega)\}$: imaginary part of the dynamical magnetic susceptibility at wave vector $\vec{q}$ and energy $\hbar\omega$. 
- $\Gamma_{\omega}$: characteristic energy (damping/linewidth) of the spin-fluctuation spectrum. 
- $\zeta$: antiferromagnetic correlation length (spatial extent of magnetic correlations). 
- $T^{*}$: temperature of the observed maximum in low-energy spectral weight (pseudo-gap onset), here $\approx 120$–$130\ \text{K}$.

title: Pseudogap corresponds to suppression of low-energy correlated spin spectral weight without destroying AF correlations
content: Let $T^{*}\approx 120$–$130\ \text{K}$ denote the temperature where $\operatorname{Im}\{\chi(\vec{q},10\ \text{meV})\}$ is maximal. It is plausible that the pseudogap onset at $T^{*}$ suppresses correlated spin spectral weight predominantly at low energies ($\hbar\omega\lesssim 10\ \text{meV}$), while leaving the characteristic energy scale $\Gamma_{\omega}\approx 30\ \text{meV}$ and the antiferromagnetic correlation length $\zeta$ essentially unchanged. This implies the pseudogap is realized primarily as a redistribution or loss of low-energy correlated spectral weight (reduction of amplitude of the correlated channel) rather than as a breakdown (loss) of short-range antiferromagnetic correlations or a softening/broadening of the spin-fluctuation energy scale.

extra.notations:
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap onset temperature (here $\approx 120$–$130\ \text{K}$).
- $\operatorname{Im}\{\chi(\vec{q},\hbar\omega)\}$: imaginary part of dynamical susceptibility at wave vector $\vec{q}$ and energy $\hbar\omega$.
- $\Gamma_{\omega}$: characteristic energy scale of spin fluctuations (here $\approx 30\ \text{meV}$).
- $\zeta$: antiferromagnetic correlation length.

title: Measured isotope exponent for LaH10 respects the BCS/harmonic upper bound
content: The isotope exponent α (defined by the scaling T_c ∝ m^{-α}, where T_c is the superconducting critical temperature and m is the relevant atomic mass) measured for fcc-LaH10 is α = 0.46. Under the BCS theory in the harmonic approximation the canonical (maximum for a purely harmonic, phonon-mediated pairing) value is α = 0.5. Since 0.46 ≤ 0.5, the measured α does not violate the BCS/harmonic upper bound.

title: LaH10 superconductivity is likely phonon-mediated with small anharmonic corrections at 150 GPa
content: Given a measured isotope exponent α ≈ 0.46 for fcc-LaH10 (comparing LaH10 and LaD10 at 150 GPa) and the BCS expectation α ≈ 0.5 for phonon-mediated, harmonic superconductors, it is plausible that superconductivity in LaH10 at 150 GPa is primarily mediated by electron–phonon interactions and that anharmonic (non‑harmonic lattice) corrections to the isotope effect are modest.

extra.notations:
- T_c: superconducting critical temperature
- m: isotope mass (hydrogen mass for H→D substitution)
- α: isotope-effect exponent defined by T_c ∝ m^{-α}
- LaH10 / LaD10: lanthanum decahydride with hydrogen / deuterium

title: Average pressure coefficient for x=0.1 between ambient and peak
content:
For the optimally doped composition Hg_{1-x}Pb_{x}Ba_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+\\delta} with x=0.1, the onset superconducting transition temperature T_{co} increases from approximately 134 K at ambient pressure (0 GPa) to approximately 151 K at P ≈ 10 GPa. The average pressure coefficient of T_{co} over this interval is therefore
$$\\frac{\\Delta T_{co}}{\\Delta P}=\\frac{151\\ \\text{K}-134\\ \\text{K}}{10\\ \\text{GPa}-0\\ \\text{GPa}}\\approx 1.7\\ \\text{K/GPa}.$$
Here T_{co} is the onset superconducting transition temperature and P is pressure.

title: Average pressure coefficient and peak behavior for x=0.2
content:
For the optimally doped composition Hg_{1-x}Pb_{x}Ba_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+\\delta} with x=0.2, the onset superconducting transition temperature T_{co} increases from approximately 134 K at ambient pressure (0 GPa) to approximately 145 K at P ≈ 8 GPa, and beyond P ≈ 8 GPa T_{co} decreases. The average pressure coefficient of T_{co} from 0 to 8 GPa is therefore
$$\\frac{\\Delta T_{co}}{\\Delta P}=\\frac{145\\ \\text{K}-134\\ \\text{K}}{8\\ \\text{GPa}-0\\ \\text{GPa}}\\approx 1.375\\ \\text{K/GPa}.$$
The reported decline of T_{co} for P>8 GPa follows directly from the statement that T_{co} "decreases as pressure is further increased" past the 8 GPa peak.

title: Pb substitution level alters both peak T_{co} and peak pressure
content: The reported peaks for the optimally doped lead-substituted Hg-1223 compositions differ: for x=0.1 the peak onset temperature is T_{co} ≈ 151 K at P ≈ 10 GPa, while for x=0.2 the peak is T_{co} ≈ 145 K at P ≈ 8 GPa. Therefore, the substitution level x (comparing x=0.1 and x=0.2) changes both the maximum T_{co} reached under pressure and the pressure at which that maximum occurs: x=0.1 yields a higher peak T_{co} and a peak at higher pressure than x=0.2.

title: Pressure-induced dome-like T_{co}(P) with a finite-pressure maximum for optimally doped Pb-substituted Hg-1223
content:
For optimally doped Hg_{1-x}Pb_{x}Ba_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+\\delta} with x=0.1 and x=0.2, the dependence of the onset superconducting transition temperature T_{co} on pressure P is plausibly dome-shaped (increasing with P up to a finite-pressure maximum and decreasing beyond that pressure). Specifically, x=0.2 shows an unambiguous peak at P ≈ 8 GPa followed by a decrease for higher P; x=0.1 shows an increase up to P ≈ 10 GPa where a peak value is reported, which plausibly implies a subsequent decrease at yet higher pressures.
Symbols: T_{co} is onset superconducting transition temperature (K); P is pressure (GPa); x is Pb substitution level.

extra.notations:
- T_{co}: onset superconducting transition temperature (K)
- P: pressure (GPa)
- x: Pb substitution level

title: Increasing Pb substitution (from 0.1 to 0.2) tends to lower the pressure of the T_{co} maximum and reduce its magnitude
content:
Comparing the two optimally doped compositions, increasing the Pb substitution from x=0.1 to x=0.2 is associated with a decrease in the pressure at which T_{co} attains its maximum (from ≈10 GPa to ≈8 GPa) and a reduction in the maximum onset temperature (from ≈151 K to ≈145 K). Thus, it is plausible that further increasing x would continue to shift the T_{co} peak to lower pressure and lower peak T_{co}, at least over the neighborhood of x considered.
Symbols: x is Pb substitution level; T_{co} is onset superconducting transition temperature; P is pressure.

title: Quantitative gap magnitudes, gap ratio, partial Sommerfeld coefficients, and nodeless small gap
content:
From the quoted dimensionless gap ratios one obtains
$$\Delta_{1} \;=\; \frac{4.0}{2}\,k_{\mathrm{B}}T_{c}=2.0\,k_{\mathrm{B}}T_{c},\qquad
  \Delta_{2} \;=\; \frac{1.2}{2}\,k_{\mathrm{B}}T_{c}=0.6\,k_{\mathrm{B}}T_{c}.$$
Hence the gap-magnitude ratio is
$$\frac{\Delta_{1}}{\Delta_{2}}\approx\frac{2.0}{0.6}\approx 3.33.$$
Using the reported total normal-state Sommerfeld coefficient $\gamma_{n}\approx 2.65\ \mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}}$ and the band weight range $x=\gamma_{1}/\gamma_{n}\approx 0.50\text{--}0.55$ gives the partial (band) Sommerfeld coefficients
$$\gamma_{1}=x\gamma_{n}\approx 1.33\text{--}1.46\ \mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}},\qquad
  \gamma_{2}=(1-x)\gamma_{n}\approx 1.19\text{--}1.33\ \mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}}.$$
The reported exponential low-temperature dependence of the electronic specific heat (thermally activated behavior governed by the smaller gap) implies a finite minimum excitation energy on the small-gap Fermi-surface sheet; therefore the smaller gap $\Delta_{2}$ is a full (nodeless) gap on its sheet (i.e., there is a finite gap minimum $\Delta_{2}>0$ and low-T $C(T)\propto\exp(-\Delta_{2}/k_{\mathrm{B}}T)$).

extra.notations:
- $\Delta_{1}$: larger superconducting gap magnitude (energy).
- $\Delta_{2}$: smaller superconducting gap magnitude (energy).
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature.
- $k_{\mathrm{B}}$: Boltzmann constant.
- $x=\gamma_{1}/\gamma_{n}$: fractional contribution of band 1 to total Sommerfeld coefficient.
- $\gamma_{n}$: total normal-state Sommerfeld coefficient; $\gamma_{1},\gamma_{2}$: band contributions.

title: Band-dependent pairing strength and weak interband scattering as origin of two distinct gaps
content: The coexistence of two well-separated gap magnitudes ($\Delta_{1}\approx 2.0\,k_{\mathrm{B}}T_{c}$ and $\Delta_{2}\approx 0.6\,k_{\mathrm{B}}T_{c}$) together with comparable band weights ($x\approx0.50$–$0.55$) plausibly indicates (i) substantially different effective pairing strengths (for example different electron–phonon coupling constants) on the separate Fermi-surface sheets, and (ii) relatively weak elastic interband scattering in the bulk sample so that the two gaps remain distinct rather than being averaged to a single value by scattering-induced gap homogenization.

extra.notations:
- "pairing strength": effective interaction (e.g., electron–phonon coupling) producing a superconducting gap on a given band.
- "interband scattering": elastic scattering processes mixing quasiparticles between different Fermi-surface sheets.

title: Pair-hopping term expressed in pseudospin language
content:
Given that the environment's low-energy charge sector is represented by pseudospin-1/2 variables $\tau_R$ with $\tau_R^z=+1/2$ indicating the presence of a localized holon pair at site $R$ and $\tau_R^z=-1/2$ its absence, the environment singlet-pair annihilation operator $\bar{\mathcal{P}}(R)$ (which destroys a holon pair at environment site $R$) must be proportional to the pseudospin lowering operator $\tau_R^-\equiv\tau_R^x-i\tau_R^y$. Hence the singlet pair-transfer term of Proposition 1520
$$
\mathcal{H}_{pair} \;=\; t_{sp}\,\big[P^{\dagger}\,\bar{\mathcal{P}} + \mathrm{H.c.}\big]
$$
can be written (up to a nonuniversal normalization factor $\alpha_R$ that depends on the microscopic mapping) in the pseudospin representation as the local coupling
$$
\mathcal{H}_{pair} \;=\; \sum_R \, t_{sp}\,\big[\,P^{\dagger}(x_R)\,\big(\alpha_R\,\tau_R^-\big) + \mathrm{H.c.}\big],
$$
where $x_R$ denotes the stripe position coupled to pseudospin site $R$. More compactly one may absorb $\alpha_R$ into a renormalized amplitude $t_{sp}^R\equiv t_{sp}\alpha_R$ and write $\mathcal{H}_{pair}=\sum_R\big[t_{sp}^R P^\dagger(x_R)\tau_R^-+\mathrm{H.c.}\big]$.

extra.notations:
- $P^\dagger(x_R)$: singlet-pair creation operator on the stripe at position coupled to pseudospin site $R$. 
- $\bar{\mathcal{P}}(R)$: environment singlet-pair annihilation operator at environment site $R$.
- $\tau_R^\pm=\tau_R^x\pm i\tau_R^y$: pseudospin raising/lowering operators at site $R$.
- $\alpha_R$: nonuniversal mapping constant relating $\bar{\mathcal{P}}(R)$ to $\tau_R^-$.

title: Effective attractive pair interaction on the stripe from virtual pair-hopping
content:
If the environment pseudospin sites $R$ have a finite excitation energy (gap) $\Delta_p$ for creating or removing a holon pair (i.e., the local pseudospin $+\tfrac12\leftrightarrow -\tfrac12$ flip costs energy $\Delta_p$), then second-order processes in the pair-hopping amplitude $t_{sp}$ generate an effective local attractive interaction for stripe pair degrees of freedom. In perturbation theory (Schrieffer–Wolff) the leading induced term on the stripe has the form
$$
\mathcal{H}_{\rm eff}^{(2)} \simeq -\sum_{R}\frac{|t_{sp}^R|^2}{\Delta_p}\;P^\dagger(x_R)P(x_R)\;+\;\cdots,
$$
where $t_{sp}^R$ is the (possibly renormalized) pair-hopping amplitude to pseudospin site $R$. This term lowers the energy of pair occupancy on the stripe (effective attraction) and therefore plausibly enhances pairing correlations (increasing tendency toward superconducting/paired ordering) in parameter regimes where this induced coupling dominates competing repulsive interactions or when the stripe is proximate to pairing instability.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta_p$: energy cost to flip a pseudospin (create/destroy an environmental holon pair).
- $t_{sp}^R$: renormalized pair-hopping amplitude between stripe position $x_R$ and pseudospin site $R$ (as in the deduction).
- $P^\dagger(x_R)P(x_R)$: local pair-number operator on the stripe at $x_R$ (projects onto pair occupancy).

title: Algebraic expression for $V_{0}$ in terms of the penetration depth $\lambda(0)$
content:
If $V_{0}$ is the zero-temperature phase stiffness defined by $V_{0}=\dfrac{\hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)a}{4m^{*}}$, and the zero-temperature superfluid density $n_{s}(0)$ satisfies $4\pi n_{s}(0)=m^{*}\!\left(\dfrac{c}{e\lambda(0)}\right)^{2}$, then
$$V_{0}=\frac{(\hbar c)^{2}\,a}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)}.$$
Here $V_{0}$ is an energy, $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant, $a$ is the short-distance cutoff length entering the phase-stiffness definition, $m^{*}$ is the effective electron mass, $c$ is the speed of light, $e$ is the elementary charge, and $\lambda(0)$ is the London penetration depth at zero temperature.

extra.notations:
- $V_{0}$: zero-temperature phase stiffness (energy scale for phase fluctuations)  
- $\hbar$: reduced Planck constant  
- $n_{s}(0)$: zero-temperature superfluid density  
- $m^{*}$: effective electron mass  
- $a$: short-distance spatial cutoff (length)  
- $c$: speed of light  
- $e$: elementary charge  
- $\lambda(0)$: zero-temperature magnetic penetration depth

title: Value of the cutoff $a$ in typical quasi-two-dimensional systems
content:
In quasi-two-dimensional superconductors the short-distance cutoff $a$ entering the phase-stiffness $V_{0}$ is chosen as the larger of the interlayer spacing $d$ and a length of order the perpendicular coherence length $\xi_{\perp}$. Therefore, in systems described as "typical" with $d>\xi_{\perp}$, one has $a=d$. Consequently the phase-stiffness expression in such typical quasi-2D systems reduces to
$$V_{0}=\frac{\hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)\,d}{4m^{*}},$$
or, using the penetration-depth form,
$$V_{0}=\frac{(\hbar c)^{2}\,d}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)}.$$
Symbols: $d$ is the interlayer spacing and other symbols are as defined above.

extra.notations:
- $a$: short-distance cutoff length  
- $d$: average inter-layer spacing in a quasi-two-dimensional superconductor  
- $\xi_{\perp}$: superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers  
- Other symbols as in previous result

title: Order-unity uncertainty in numerical value of $V_{0}$ due to cutoff choice
content:
The numerical value of the zero-temperature phase stiffness $V_{0}$ obtained from $V_{0}=\dfrac{\hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)a}{4m^{*}}$ (or its $\lambda$-form) carries an uncertainty of order unity because the short-distance cutoff $a$ is specified only up to an order-unity factor (different sources use slightly different $\xi_{\perp}$-based definitions and there may be $O(1)$ numerical prefactors associated with the coarse-graining). Therefore, computed values of $V_{0}$ should be regarded as uncertain multiplicatively by a factor of order unity (e.g., a factor ~2 is plausible depending on conventions).
Symbols as previously defined.

extra.notations:
- $V_{0}$: zero-temperature phase stiffness  
- $a$: cutoff length whose precise definition can vary by order-unity factors  
- $\xi_{\perp}$: perpendicular coherence length

title: Low-energy dynamics are a single-band constrained hopping model with quantitatively known hoppings
content:
Combining the explicit effective hopping matrix elements t_{ij} derived for the local Cu–O singlet (the Zhang–Rice–type singlet) with the form of the effective carrier Hamiltonian H_t shows that the low-energy carriers are described exactly by a single-band model of constrained fermions (no double occupancy) hopping with matrix elements t_{ij}. In particular, using the notation below, the effective kinetic term acting in the singlet subspace is
$$H_{t}=\sum_{i\neq j,\sigma} t_{ij}\,(1-n_{i,-\sigma})\,d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}d_{j\sigma}\,(1-n_{j,-\sigma}),$$
with the nearest-neighbour hopping numerically t_{nn}\approx -1.5\,t and next-nearest terms \approx -0.16\,t (and further hoppings smaller by an order of magnitude). Consequently, the low-energy band dispersion is dominated by nearest-neighbour hopping of magnitude |t_{nn}|\sim 1.5\,|t| and the kinetic bandwidth is O(z|t_{nn}|) (z the in-plane coordination).

extra.notations:
- d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger},d_{i\sigma}: creation/annihilation operators for the effective singlet carrier at Cu site i with spin σ (these are the projected Cu-site fermions). 
- n_{i,-\sigma}: number operator for spin −σ at site i.
- t_{ij}: effective hopping matrix element for the singlet between sites i and j; t is the hybridization energy scale entering the microscopic second-order calculation.
- t_{nn}: nearest-neighbour t_{ij}; t_{nn}\approx -1.5\,t.

title: Superexchange J is antiferromagnetic and of order t_eff^2/Δ (leading to a t–J description)
content:
Given (i) the strong binding energy of the local singlet (singlet energy ≈ −8(t_1+t_2) well below triplet and nonbonding states), (ii) the projection of low-energy dynamics onto the singlet subspace (single-band effective Hamiltonian H_eff=H_t+H_s), and (iii) the derived effective hopping scale t_{ij} (t_{nn}≈−1.5t), it is plausible that the residual spin interaction H_s is an antiferromagnetic superexchange of order
$$J\sim \frac{\alpha\,t_{\rm eff}^2}{\Delta_{\rm ct}},$$
where t_eff is the dominant effective hopping scale (|t_{nn}|\sim1.5\,t), Δ_{ct} is the relevant charge-transfer (or high-energy) excitation energy separating the singlet subspace from the virtual intermediate states, and α is a numerical factor O(1) (for a standard second-order superexchange α≈4). Thus the low-energy model can be usefully approximated by a t–J type Hamiltonian with antiferromagnetic J of order t^2/Δ_{ct}.

extra.notations:
- t_{nn}: nearest-neighbour effective hopping (≈ −1.5\,t) entering H_t.
- t_eff: shorthand for the dominant effective hopping scale (|t_{nn}|).
- Δ_{ct}: charge-transfer or energy denominator for virtual excitations connecting singlet sector to higher-energy states (e.g. energy difference appearing in second-order perturbation denominators).
- J: effective antiferromagnetic superexchange coupling in H_s.

title: Layering sequence: Ba layers flank strictly planar Cu–O sheets, separated by double Tl–O layers
content: Within the tetragonal I4/mmm unit cell of Tl2Ba2CuO6, the stacking along the crystallographic $c$ axis is (double Tl–O sheet) / Ba layer / planar Cu–O sheet / Ba layer / (double Tl–O sheet). Equivalently, each single Cu–O (CuO$_2$-like) plane is sandwiched by alkaline-earth Ba layers (forming a Ba–Cu–Ba slab) and these Ba–Cu–Ba slabs alternate with double Tl–O sheets along $c$. The copper atoms occupy $D_{4h}$ (4/mmm) sites and are coordinated in-plane to four equivalent oxygen atoms at bond distance $1.9330(5)\ \AA$, so the Cu–O sheet is strictly planar and lies on the crystallographic fourfold axis and mirror plane.

extra.notations:
- Tl–O: thallium–oxygen layer (double sheet)
- Ba: barium (alkaline-earth) layer
- Cu–O or CuO$_2$-like plane: single copper–oxygen sheet with planar coordination
- $D_{4h}$ (4/mmm): point/group symmetry indicating a fourfold rotation axis plus horizontal mirror plane
- $c$: crystallographic $c$ axis (stacking direction)

title: Tl-2201 likely has a more ideal (unbuckled) CuO2 plane than Bi-2201, with implications for electronic homogeneity
content: Given that Tl2Ba2CuO6 (Tl-2201) has strictly planar Cu–O sheets with Cu at $D_{4h}$ sites (no buckling) while Bi2Sr2CuO6 (Bi-2201) shares the same general stacking motif (alkaline-earth–Cu–alkaline-earth slabs alternating with double Bi–O sheets), it is plausible that Tl-2201 presents a structurally more ideal, unbuckled CuO$_2$ plane than Bi-2201. This structural ideality likely reduces structural scattering sources tied to CuO$_2$ buckling and may promote greater in-plane electronic homogeneity in Tl-2201 compared to Bi-2201.

extra.notations:
- Tl-2201: shorthand for Tl2Ba2CuO6
- Bi-2201: shorthand for Bi2Sr2CuO6
- CuO$_2$ plane: copper–oxygen planar sheet
- Buckling: out-of-plane displacement of O (or Cu) that breaks perfect planarity

title: Both gaps vanish at the same bulk $T_{c}$ (single superconducting transition)
content: Under the assumptions of the two-band alpha‑model used here, each band $i\in\{1,2\}$ has a gap $\Delta_i(T)=\Delta_{i,0}\,\delta(t)$ where $\delta(t)$ is a single normalized BCS-like gap function with $t=T/T_{c}$ and $\delta(1)=0$. Therefore both $\Delta_1(T)$ and $\Delta_2(T)$ necessarily go to zero at the same reduced temperature $t=1$, i.e. at the same bulk superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$. Because the specific heat is a bulk thermodynamic probe, the successful two-gap fit of the measured bulk $C(T)$ implies that the two-gap structure exists throughout the sample and that the superconducting transition is a single, bulk transition at $T_{c}$ in which both gaps close.

extra.notations:
- $T$: absolute temperature
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature
- $t$: reduced temperature $t=T/T_{c}$
- $\Delta_{i}(T)$: superconducting gap on band $i$
- $\Delta_{i,0}$: zero‑temperature gap on band $i$
- $\delta(t)$: normalized BCS-like gap function with $\delta(1)=0$

title: Low-temperature specific heat is dominated by the smaller gap and yields its magnitude
