content: In the two-gap alpha‑model each band contributes independently to the electronic specific heat; at temperatures $T\ll T_{c}$ the quasiparticle excitation contribution from a band with gap $\Delta$ is exponentially suppressed as ~$\exp(-\Delta/k_{\mathrm{B}}T)$. Therefore the total electronic specific heat $C_{\mathrm{total}}(T)=C_1(T)+C_2(T)$ at sufficiently low $T$ is dominated by the band with the smaller zero‑temperature gap $\Delta_{\mathrm{min}}=\min(\Delta_{1,0},\Delta_{2,0})$, and the activated temperature dependence of $C_{\mathrm{total}}$ can be used to extract $\Delta_{\mathrm{min}}$ (within the alpha‑model framework).

extra.notations:
- $C_{\mathrm{total}}(T)$: total electronic specific heat in the superconducting state
- $C_i(T)$: contribution from band $i$
- $\Delta_{i,0}$: zero‑temperature gap on band $i$
- $\Delta_{\mathrm{min}}$: smaller of $\Delta_{1,0}$ and $\Delta_{2,0}$
- $k_{\mathrm{B}}$: Boltzmann constant

title: Partial Sommerfeld coefficients approximate fractional band densities of states
content: The fitted relative weight $x\equiv\gamma_{1}/\gamma_{\mathrm{n}}$ (and $1-x\equiv\gamma_{2}/\gamma_{\mathrm{n}}$) used in the two‑gap specific‑heat fits plausibly corresponds to the fraction of the total electronic density of states (DOS) at the Fermi level contributed by each band, i.e. $\gamma_i/\gamma_{\mathrm{n}}\approx N_i(0)/[N_1(0)+N_2(0)]$, provided that band‑dependent many‑body renormalizations (for example electron–phonon mass enhancement factors $1+\lambda_i$) do not differ significantly between the bands.

extra.notations:
- $\gamma_i$: partial normal‑state Sommerfeld coefficient of band $i$
- $\gamma_{\mathrm{n}}$: total normal‑state Sommerfeld coefficient ($\gamma_1+\gamma_2=\gamma_{\mathrm{n}}$)
- $N_i(0)$: electronic density of states at the Fermi level for band $i$
- $\lambda_i$: electron–phonon mass‑enhancement (coupling) parameter on band $i$

title: MgB2 is a bulk superconductor with Tc = 39 K
content: The sample of magnesium diboride (MgB2) exhibits a superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}=39\,$K, and below $T_{c}$ shows magnetic flux expulsion (the Meissner effect) as observed in magnetization measurements (including field-cooling measurements) together with the onset of zero electrical resistance. Therefore the material undergoes a thermodynamic superconducting transition that involves the bulk of the sample: below $T_{c}$ the bulk supports dissipationless (zero-resistance) current and expels magnetic flux from its interior (Meissner state).

extra.notations:
- MgB2: magnesium diboride (sample under test)
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (Kelvin)
- Meissner effect: expulsion of magnetic flux from the bulk when entering the superconducting state

title: Large superconducting volume fraction / non-filamentary superconductivity
content: Given that the onset of zero electrical resistance and the onset of a measurable Meissner effect (observed during field-cooling) occur at the same temperature ($T_{c}=39\,$K) in the reported MgB2 sample, it is likely that a large fraction of the sample volume becomes superconducting near $T_{c}$ rather than superconductivity arising from a small, percolating or filamentary minority phase.

extra.notations:
- Superconducting volume fraction: fraction of sample volume in the superconducting state
- FC: field-cooling measurement (cooling through $T_{c}$ in an applied magnetic field)

title: Siblings are logically compatible (different compounds / conditions)
content: Proposition 1544 reports that the mercury cuprate HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+x}$ (abbreviated Hg-1223) has a superconducting transition onset near $T_c\approx 133\,$K. Proposition 1557 reports that annealing Tl‑containing layered cuprates at $450^\circ$C in flowing O$_2$ alters their superconducting transition temperature $T_c$ and/or the resistive transition profile. These two claims refer to different chemical families (Hg‑based versus Tl‑based cuprates) and therefore can both be true without logical conflict.

extra.notations:
- Hg-1223: HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+x}$, $x$ is oxygen non-stoichiometry. 
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature (onset), in Kelvin.
- "450°C in flowing oxygen": thermal treatment at $450^\circ$C with continuous O$_2$ flow.

title: Hg‑1223 $T_c$ is plausibly tunable by post‑synthesis O$_2$ annealing via oxygen stoichiometry / hole doping
content: It is plausible that the superconducting onset temperature $T_c$ of HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+x}$ (Hg-1223, with reported onset $T_c\approx133\,$K) can be modified by post‑synthesis annealing in flowing O$_2$ (potentially including treatments near $450^\circ$C), because oxygen incorporation/removal changes the oxygen non‑stoichiometry $x$, thereby altering the hole concentration $p$ in the CuO$_2$ planes and moving the material along the universal cuprate $T_c(p)$ dome.

extra.notations:
- Hg-1223: HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+x}$, $x$ denotes excess/deficient oxygen.
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature (onset), in Kelvin.
- $p$: hole concentration per Cu (doping level) in CuO$_2$ planes.
- "post‑synthesis O$_2$ annealing": heating a synthesized sample while flowing O$_2$ over it for some time.

title: CaH2 topotactic reduction of Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO3 on SrTiO3 produces Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2 with a 0.40 Å c-axis contraction and preserved epitaxy
content: For a single-crystal thin film of composition Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO3 (perovskite precursor) grown on a SrTiO3 (STO) substrate, treatment with solid CaH2 at low temperature converts the film to the infinite-layer phase Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2 and this transformation (1) removes apical oxygens (producing NiO2 planes) and (2) reduces the crystallographic lattice parameter along the out-of-plane (c) direction from approximately 3.77 Å (perovskite) to approximately 3.37 Å (infinite-layer), i.e. a contraction Δc ≈ 0.40 Å (≈10.6%), while preserving epitaxial clamping to the STO substrate.

extra.notations:
- Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO3: perovskite precursor film containing neodymium and 20% Sr substitution.
- Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2: infinite-layer nickelate product after oxygen deintercalation (NiO2 planes, no apical O).
- CaH2: solid calcium hydride used as low-temperature reducing/topotactic reagent.
- STO or SrTiO3: SrTiO3 single-crystal substrate; "epitaxial clamping" means the film remains coherently (or coherently strained) bound to the substrate lattice.
- c (c-axis): lattice parameter perpendicular to the substrate (out-of-plane spacing).
- Å: angstrom, 1 Å = 10^-10 m.

title: c-axis contraction is primarily attributable to removal of apical oxygens
content: The observed out-of-plane lattice contraction Δc ≈ 0.40 Å (from ≈3.77 Å to ≈3.37 Å) upon CaH2-induced topotactic reduction of Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO3 to Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2 is plausibly dominated by the removal of apical oxygen atoms (the oxygen sites between NiO2 planes and the rare-earth layers). Here Δc denotes the change in the c-axis lattice parameter associated with removal of those apical oxygens.

extra.notations:
- Δc: change in out-of-plane lattice parameter c (Δc = c_perovskite − c_infinite-layer).
- Apical oxygen: oxygen atom in the perovskite that bonds along the c direction to the Ni site (coordinate between NiO2 planes and the rare-earth layer); removal yields the infinite-layer NiO2 stacking.

title: In typical quasi-2D materials the short-distance cutoff equals the interlayer spacing and V0 simplifies
content:
In systems where the average interlayer spacing $d$ exceeds the perpendicular coherence length $\xi_{\perp}$ (the typical configuration asserted in Parent 101), the short-distance cutoff $a=\max(d,\sqrt{\pi\,\xi_{\perp}})$ reduces to $a=d$. Substituting $a=d$ into the phase-stiffness expression gives the simplified form
$$
V_{0}=\frac{(\hbar c)^{2}d}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)},
$$
where $V_{0}$ is the zero-temperature phase-stiffness energy scale, $\lambda(0)$ is the zero-temperature magnetic penetration depth, $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant, $c$ is the speed of light, and $e$ is the elementary charge.

extra.notations:
- $V_{0}$: zero-temperature phase stiffness (energy scale for classical phase fluctuations)
- $a$: short-distance spatial cutoff (linear length)
- $d$: average spacing between superconducting layers
- $\xi_{\perp}$: superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers
- $\lambda(0)$: magnetic penetration depth at $T=0$
- $\hbar,c,e$: standard physical constants

title: Order-unity uncertainty in the $\xi_{\perp}$-based cutoff produces only order-unity uncertainty in V0
content:
When the $\xi_{\perp}$-based contribution to the cutoff $a$ is defined with differing order-unity numerical prefactors (for example $a\sim\sqrt{\pi\,\xi_{\perp}}$ versus $a\sim\xi_{\perp}$), the resulting estimate of the phase stiffness
$$
V_{0}=\frac{(\hbar c)^{2}a}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)}
$$
changes by at most an order-unity multiplicative factor equal to the ratio of the definitions of $a$. Thus the numerical uncertainty in $a$ due to choice of $\xi_{\perp}$-based convention maps directly to an order-unity uncertainty in $V_{0}$ (and hence in $T_{\theta}^{max}=A\,V_{0}$).

extra.notations:
- $V_{0}$: zero-temperature phase stiffness
- $a$: short-distance cutoff (may be defined with differing order-unity prefactors)
- $\xi_{\perp}$: coherence length perpendicular to layers
- $\lambda(0)$: penetration depth at $T=0$
- $A$: dimensionless constant of order unity

title: Phase stiffness can limit Tc: when $T_{\theta}^{max}<T^{MF}$ superconducting $T_{c}$ is likely set by phase ordering
content:
Let $T^{MF}$ denote the mean-field pairing temperature (BCS/Eliashberg estimate) and $T_{\theta}^{max}=A\,V_{0}$ ($A\sim 1$) the maximum phase-ordering temperature set by the zero-temperature phase stiffness $V_{0}$. If phase fluctuations are the dominant mechanism limiting global phase coherence, then the actual superconducting transition temperature satisfies approximately
$$
T_{c}\lesssim \min\big(T^{MF},\,T_{\theta}^{max}\big).
$$
In particular, in materials for which $T_{\theta}^{max}<T^{MF}$ (for example, systems with large $\lambda(0)$ and/or small $a$ so that $V_{0}$ is small), one expects $T_{c}$ to be suppressed below the mean-field value and to be controlled by phase-ordering physics.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature predicted by BCS–Eliashberg theory
- $T_{\theta}^{max}=A\,V_{0}$: maximum phase-ordering temperature with $A\sim 1$
- $V_{0}=\dfrac{(\hbar c)^{2}a}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)}$: zero-temperature phase stiffness
- $\lambda(0),a$: as above

title: Low-temperature specific heat is governed by the smaller gap
content:
Let Δ_{1,0} and Δ_{2,0} be the two zero-temperature gaps (in energy units) and define Δ_min = min(Δ_{1,0},Δ_{2,0}). Under the two-band alpha‑model assumptions (each band contribution is computed from BCS-type quasiparticle integrals and the total electronic specific heat is C_total = C_1 + C_2 with C_i set by Δ_{i}(T) = Δ_{i,0} δ(t) and partial Sommerfeld coefficients γ_i), the asymptotic low-temperature electronic specific heat (T ≪ Tc) satisfies
$$C_{\\mathrm{total}}(T) \\sim A\\,T^{-n}\\,\\exp\\big(-\\Delta_{\\mathrm{min}}/(k_{\\mathrm{B}}T)\\big),$$
where A depends on the band weight of the band with the smaller gap and n is a positive exponent determined by the quasiparticle density-of-states prefactor (for isotropic gaps in 3D n=1/2 in the usual BCS approximation). Thus the band with the smaller gap controls the leading exponential temperature dependence of the low-T electronic specific heat.

extra.notations:
- Δ_{i,0}: zero-temperature superconducting gap on band i (i=1,2). 
- Δ_min: the smaller of Δ_{1,0} and Δ_{2,0}.
- C_total(T): total electronic specific heat at temperature T.
- γ_i: partial Sommerfeld coefficient of band i.
- k_{\\mathrm{B}}: Boltzmann constant.
- Tc: superconducting critical temperature.

title: Single superconducting transition (common Tc) implies thermodynamic coupling between bands
content: The two-gap alpha‑model as stated uses the same reduced temperature t = T/Tc and the same normalized gap function δ(t) for both bands, so both band gaps satisfy Δ_i(T) = Δ_{i,0} δ(t) and vanish at the same Tc. Therefore the observed single superconducting transition temperature Tc for the bulk sample implies that the two bands are not two completely independent, decoupled superconductors with distinct transition temperatures. Thermodynamically, a single Tc requires nonzero coupling between the bands (e.g., nonzero interband pairing matrix elements or an induced (proximity-like) pairing from one band onto the other) so that the superconducting order on both bands appears at the same temperature.

extra.notations:
- Δ_i(T): temperature-dependent gap on band i, Δ_i(T)=Δ_{i,0} δ(t).
- δ(t): normalized BCS-like gap function with δ(0)=1 and δ(1)=0.
- Tc: the single observed superconducting critical temperature for the bulk sample.
- γ_i: partial Sommerfeld coefficients (γ_1+γ_2=γ_n).

title: Conductivity amplitude cannot distinguish 2p versus continuum excitations
content: Let Δσ denote the change in electrical conductivity induced by the rectangular THz pulse and let ρ_{11}(τ) denote the donor ground-state (1s) population at pulse end time τ. Both siblings state Δσ ∝ (1 - ρ_{11}(τ)) and that (1 - ρ_{11}(τ)) counts donors excited out of the 1s state into either the 2p bound state or the continuum (ionized) states. Therefore the measured amplitude Δσ(τ) only determines the total excited fraction (the aggregate population removed from 1s) and, by itself, cannot resolve how that excited population is partitioned between the 2p bound state and the continuum.

extra.notations:
- Δσ: change in electrical conductivity induced by the THz pulse
- ρ_{11}(τ): population of the 1s ground state at time τ
- (1 - ρ_{11}(τ)): fraction of donors excited out of 1s by time τ (includes both 2p and continuum)

title: RWA analytic fits to Δσ(τ,E_{THz}) can yield dephasing and decay rates
content: If analytic rotating-wave-approximation (RWA) solutions give an explicit functional form ρ_{11}(τ; E_{THz}, γ_{2}, γ_{3}, ...) for the ground-state population as a function of pulse parameters and relaxation rates, then measured conductivity traces Δσ(τ, E_{THz}) that satisfy Δσ ∝ (1 - ρ_{11}(τ)) can be fitted to that analytic form to extract estimates of the transverse dephasing rate γ_{2} and the longitudinal/damping rate γ_{3} (and the effective Rabi frequency or dipole coupling). This requires sufficiently rich data (varying τ and/or E_{THz}), adequate signal-to-noise, and validity of the RWA model for the experimental parameter range.

extra.notations:
- Δσ(τ, E_{THz}): measured conductivity change as a function of pulse duration τ and field amplitude E_{THz}
- ρ_{11}(τ; ...): analytic RWA expression for ground-state population parameterized by γ_{2}, γ_{3}, etc.
- γ_{2}: dephasing (transverse relaxation) rate
- γ_{3}: population decay / damping rate (including ionization coupling)

title: Existence of a member with onset $T_{co}\approx 164\ \mathrm{K}$ under quasihydrostatic pressure
content: There exists a member of the mercury-based cuprate series HgBa$_2$Ca$_{m-1}$Cu$_m$O$_{2m+2+\delta}$ (specifically the $m=3$ compound HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+\delta}$) for which the onset superconducting transition temperature $T_{co}$, defined as the temperature of resistive onset of superconductivity, is approximately $164\ \mathrm{K}$ when measured under quasihydrostatic pressure of approximately $31\ \mathrm{GPa}$ in a diamond anvil cell (DAC) environment.

extra.notations:
- $T_{co}$: onset superconducting transition temperature (temperature where resistive onset of superconductivity is observed). 
- GPa: gigapascal, unit of pressure. 
- "quasihydrostatic": applied pressure environment approximating hydrostatic conditions (e.g., in a DAC).
- HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+\delta}$: the $m=3$ member of HgBa$_2$Ca$_{m-1}$Cu$_m$O$_{2m+2+\delta}$.

title: Plausible high-$T_c$ pressure response across the Hg-cuprate series
content: Given that members of the HgBa$_2$Ca_{m-1}$Cu$_m$O$_{2m+2+\delta}$ series show pronounced, systematic increases of onset $T_c$ under quasihydrostatic pressure and that the $m=3$ member reaches $T_{co}\approx 164\ \mathrm{K}$ at $\sim 31\ \mathrm{GPa}$, it is plausible that other members of the series (e.g., $m=1$ and $m=2$) will also exhibit substantial pressure-induced increases of $T_{co}$ under quasihydrostatic compression; with sufficiently high quasihydrostatic pressures and appropriate oxygen/doping states, some of these other members could attain onset $T_{co}$ values significantly above their ambient-pressure maxima and potentially exceed $150\ \mathrm{K}$.

extra.notations:
- $m$: number of CuO$_2$ layers per formula unit (integer 1,2,3 for the common members). 
- $T_{co}$: onset superconducting transition temperature.
- "sufficiently high quasihydrostatic pressures": pressures in the several-tens of GPa range (e.g., $\sim$10–40 GPa), as exemplified by the $m=3$ case.

title: Antiphase Néel order period is twice the stripe spacing
content:
Let d be the real-space spacing (distance) between adjacent parallel charged domain lines (stripes) measured along the direction perpendicular to the stripes. Proposition 1513 states that each stripe is an antiphase domain wall (the Néel order flips sign across the stripe). Proposition 1515 states that the stripe spacing d is set by the domain-line density. From these two facts it follows necessarily that the antiferromagnetic (Néel) order acquires a spatial period P_m equal to twice the stripe spacing,
$$P_m = 2d.$$
Here P_m is the smallest distance after which the local Néel vector repeats its orientation pattern in the direction perpendicular to the stripes.

extra.notations:
- d: distance between adjacent parallel charged domain lines (stripe spacing).
- P_m: spatial period of the Néel (antiferromagnetic) order perpendicular to the stripes.

title: Stripe spacing inversely related to global hole concentration (under fixed linear charge per stripe)
content:
Let x be the global excess hole concentration per Cu (hole doping). Let ρ_DL be the area density of domain-line unit cells (number of domain-line unit cells per unit area) and let q_L be the average excess hole charge per domain-line unit cell (holes per domain-line unit cell). If each stripe-localized domain-line unit cell carries the same amount of excess hole charge q_L (assumption: approximately fixed linear charge per stripe unit cell), then the global hole concentration is
$$x = q_L \, \rho_{DL}.$$
Since Proposition 1515 states that stripe spacing d is set by the domain-line density, with d decreasing as ρ_DL increases (roughly \(d\propto 1/\rho_{DL}\)), it follows plausibly that, under the fixed-charge assumption,
$$d \propto \frac{1}{x}.$$
Thus stripe spacing is expected to decrease approximately inversely with the global hole concentration.

extra.notations:
- x: global excess hole concentration (holes per Cu).
- ρ_DL: density of domain-line unit cells (number per unit area).
- q_L: excess hole charge per domain-line unit cell (holes per unit cell along stripe).
- d: stripe spacing (distance between adjacent parallel stripes).

title: Magnetic incommensurability scales with stripe density (and, with the fixed-charge assumption, with doping)
content:
Define the magnetic incommensurability δ as the magnitude of the shift of antiferromagnetic Bragg peaks away from the commensurate Néel wavevector in reciprocal-space units measured perpendicular to the stripes. If the stripe spacing is d (in lattice-spacing units), Proposition 1513 implies antiphase boundaries and Proposition 1515 ties d to stripe density. A Néel order with spatial period P_m = 2d (deduced above) produces an additional modulation of the antiferromagnetic structure with wavevector magnitude
$$k_m = \frac{2\pi}{P_m} = \frac{\pi}{d}.$$
Measured as a shift δ from the commensurate position along the corresponding reciprocal-space direction (in units of the reciprocal lattice spacing), this implies that δ scales approximately as the inverse stripe spacing,
$$\delta \propto \frac{1}{d} \propto \rho_{DL}.$$
Under the extra assumption that each domain-line unit cell carries a roughly constant hole charge q_L (as in the previous conjecture), this further implies
$$\delta \propto x.$$
Thus one expects the magnitude of magnetic incommensurability to increase with stripe density and, plausibly, with hole doping.

extra.notations:
- d: stripe spacing (lattice units) perpendicular to stripes.
- P_m: magnetic period perpendicular to stripes, P_m = 2d.
- k_m: modulation wavevector magnitude, k_m = 2π/P_m.
- δ: magnetic incommensurability (reciprocal-lattice units), proportional to k_m.
- ρ_DL: domain-line unit-cell density.
- x: global hole concentration (holes per Cu).

title: Pseudogap is absent at nodal momenta
content: The pseudogap magnitude as a function of momentum and temperature can be written as $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)=\Phi(T)\,g(\mathbf{k})$ with the form factor $g(\mathbf{k})\propto|\cos k_x-\cos k_y|$ (d-wave). At nodal momenta $\mathbf{k}_{\rm node}$ one has $g(\mathbf{k}_{\rm node})=0$, hence $\Delta(\mathbf{k}_{\rm node},T)=0$ for all $T$. Therefore the low-energy suppression of spectral weight that defines the pseudogap (suppression near the Fermi level) is necessarily absent at the nodal Fermi crossings: nodal excitations remain gapless (no pseudogap) while antinodal momenta show the suppression.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$: pseudogap (or superconducting gap) magnitude at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and temperature $T$.
- $g(\mathbf{k})$: d-wave form factor, $g(\mathbf{k})\propto|\cos k_x-\cos k_y|$.
- $\mathbf{k}_{\rm node}$: a momentum on the Fermi surface where $g(\mathbf{k})=0$ (nodal direction).

title: Momentum-dependent crossover temperature $T^{*}(\mathbf{k})$ with antinode controlling the observed $T^{*}$
content:
Define a momentum-resolved crossover temperature $T^{*}(\mathbf{k})$ as the temperature where the local pseudogap is effectively removed at momentum $\mathbf{k}$. Using the criterion from Proposition 675, $T^{*}(\mathbf{k})$ satisfies
$$\Delta(\mathbf{k},T^{*}(\mathbf{k}))\sim\Gamma_{0}(T^{*}(\mathbf{k})).$$
If the gap factorizes as $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)=\Delta_{0}(T)\,g(\mathbf{k})$ with $g(\mathbf{k})\propto|\cos k_x-\cos k_y|$ (Proposition 1265) and if $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ is approximately momentum independent, then $T^{*}(\mathbf{k})$ is a decreasing function of $g(\mathbf{k})$: momenta with larger $g(\mathbf{k})$ (antinodes) maintain a nonzero local gap to higher $T$ than momenta with smaller $g(\mathbf{k})$ (closer to nodes). The globally reported crossover temperature $T^{*}$ therefore corresponds operationally to the temperature where the antinodal pseudogap is filled in.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta_{0}(T)$: temperature-dependent amplitude of the gap prefactor.
- $g(\mathbf{k})$: d-wave form factor proportional to $|\cos k_x-\cos k_y|$.
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime (assumed approximately momentum independent for this conjecture).
- $T^{*}(\mathbf{k})$: momentum-resolved pseudogap crossover temperature.
- Antinode: momentum where $g(\mathbf{k})$ is maximal (e.g., $(\pi,0)$-like).

title: Pseudogap likely originates from pairing correlations (preformed pairs / pair fluctuations)
content: The pseudogap exhibits the same d-wave momentum structure as the superconducting gap (Proposition 1265). Proposition 675 relates the pseudogap magnitude $\Delta(T)$ and its disappearance to the inverse pair lifetime $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ via $\Delta(T)\sim\Gamma_{0}(T)$. Taken together, these statements are consistent with an interpretation in which the pseudogap reflects a nonzero pairing amplitude (gap magnitude) above $T_c$ but with finite pair lifetime: i.e., pairing correlations (preformed pairs or strong pair fluctuations) produce a spectroscopic gap that is recovered/filled when pair lifetimes become too short.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta(T)$: pairing amplitude (local gap magnitude) as a function of temperature.
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime; smaller lifetime $\Rightarrow$ larger $\Gamma_{0}$.
- d-wave: momentum structure given by $|\cos k_x-\cos k_y|$.

title: Zero‑temperature superfluid stiffness (λ⁻²(0)) provides an upper bound on Tc in the low‑δ regime
content:
Let $T_{c}$ be the thermodynamic superconducting transition temperature and $\lambda(0)$ the London penetration depth at $T=0$. Proposition 132 asserts that the phase‑ordering temperature $T_{\theta}^{max}$ is an upper bound on $T_{c}$ and that $T_{\theta}^{max}$ is directly proportional to $\lambda^{-2}(0)$ in the low superfluid‑density (doped insulator / underdoped) regime. Therefore there exists a material‑dependent positive constant $C$ (set by short‑distance cutoff, layer spacing $d$, etc.) such that
$$T_{c} \le T_{\theta}^{max} = C\,\lambda^{-2}(0).$$
Thus the zero‑temperature superfluid stiffness $\lambda^{-2}(0)$ necessarily bounds the observable transition temperature from above in underdoped cuprates: increasing $\lambda^{-2}(0)$ (stronger phase stiffness) is a prerequisite for increasing $T_{c}$, and no sample in the low‑$\delta$ regime can have $T_{c}$ exceeding the corresponding $C\,\lambda^{-2}(0)$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence).
- $\lambda(0)$: London penetration depth at zero temperature; $\lambda^{-2}(0)\propto n_{s}(0)/m^{*}$ is the superfluid stiffness.
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: theoretical maximum phase‑ordering temperature (upper bound on $T_{c}$).
- $C$: positive constant absorbing numerical factors, layer spacing, units.

title: Sublinear scaling: $T_{c}\propto\big(\lambda^{-2}(0)\big)^{2/3}$ in the dilute (low‑δ) limit
content:
Denote the hole doping by $\delta\ll1$, the zero‑temperature superfluid stiffness by $\lambda^{-2}(0)\propto n_{s}(0)/m^{*}$, and the transition temperature by $T_{c}$. Proposition 1422 gives $T_{c}\sim \delta^{2/3}$ in the dilute bosonic (holon) limit. If, in this regime, the zero‑temperature superfluid density scales proportionally to the doped hole density ($n_{s}(0)\propto\delta$) and the effective boson mass $m^{*}$ is only weakly dependent on $\delta$, then eliminating $\delta$ yields
$$T_{c}\propto \big(\lambda^{-2}(0)\big)^{2/3}.$$
Hence the empirically observed approximately linear Uemura plot ($T_{c}\propto\lambda^{-2}(0)$) should be viewed, in the asymptotic low‑$\delta$ limit, as an upper bound: the microscopic dilute‑boson prediction is a sublinear power law with exponent $2/3$.

extra.notations:
- $\delta$: hole doping per CuO2 unit (dimensionless, $\delta\ll1$).
- $n_{s}(0)$: superfluid carrier density at $T=0$.
- $\lambda^{-2}(0)$: zero‑temperature superfluid stiffness, $\propto n_{s}(0)/m^{*}$.
- $m^{*}$: effective mass of the charged bosonic carriers (holons).

title: Experimental extraction of an effective boson mass $m^{*}$ from $T_{c}(\delta)$ is feasible and likely yields strong mass renormalization at low δ
content:
For a dilute gas of charged bosonic holes of density $n\propto\delta$ and effective mass $m^{*}$, the Bose‑Einstein condensation temperature (in three dimensions or weakly coupled layers) scales as
$$k_{B}T_{c}\sim\frac{\hbar^{2}}{m^{*}}\,n^{2/3}.$$
Rearranging gives an operational estimate of the effective mass,
$$m^{*}\sim\frac{\hbar^{2}n^{2/3}}{k_{B}T_{c}}.$$
Thus, using measured $T_{c}(\delta)$ and an estimate of the mobile boson density $n(\delta)$ (e.g., $n\propto\delta$ per CuO$_2$ plane and known interlayer spacing), one can extract an experimental $m^{*}(\delta)$. Given the small observed $T_{c}$ at low $\delta$ and the small carrier density, this procedure plausibly yields $m^{*}$ substantially larger than the bare electron mass and possibly increasing as $\delta\to0$, reflecting strong interaction/renormalization effects in the underdoped regime.

extra.notations:
- $k_{B}$: Boltzmann constant.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature.
- $n$: boson (holon) density (number per unit volume), $n\propto\delta$ assumed.
- $m^{*}$: effective mass of the bosonic carriers to be extracted.

title: Quantitative relation between $T^{*}$, $\Delta$ and linear $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ above $T_{c}$
content:
Assume for $T>T_{c}$ the inverse pair lifetime has the form $\Gamma_{0}(T)=a\,(T-T_{c})$ with constant $a>0$, and define $T^{*}$ by the condition $\Delta(T^{*})\approx\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$. If the pairing amplitude $\Delta(T)$ is approximately temperature-independent across $T_{c}$ in the antinodal region (so $\Delta(T)\approx\Delta$ for $T\lesssim T^{*}$), then
$$T^{*}=T_{c}+\dfrac{\Delta}{a}\,. $$
Here $T^{*}$ is the pseudogap crossover temperature, $\Delta$ is the (approximately constant) pairing amplitude in the antinodal region, and $a$ is the slope of $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ above $T_{c}$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap crossover temperature defined by $\Delta(T^{*})\sim\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$
- $\Delta$: pairing amplitude (assumed $\approx$ constant in the relevant $T$ range)
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime; here modeled as $\Gamma_{0}(T)=a(T-T_{c})$
- $a$: positive proportionality constant

title: Momentum-selective origin of the pseudogap: antinodal preformed pairs vs nodal gap closing
content: In underdoped cuprates, the pseudogap observed in ARPES likely has different microscopic character in different momentum regions of the Brillouin zone. Specifically, at antinodal momenta (near $(\pi,0)$) the spectral suppression above $T_{c}$ is dominated by a nonzero pairing amplitude $\Delta$ with a short pair lifetime (finite $\Gamma_{0}$) so that the gap "fills in" as $\Gamma_{0}$ grows; near the nodal region the gap magnitude $\Delta(T)$ itself diminishes and closes above $T_{c}$. Thus the antinodal pseudogap is plausibly associated with incoherent (short-lived) pair correlations (a "preformed-pair" or pair-fluctuation dominated regime), while the nodal pseudogap reflects a conventional superconducting gap that vanishes at $T_{c}$.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta$: pairing amplitude (may be nonzero above $T_c$ in antinodal region)
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime (broadens pair pole; increases above $T_c$)
- Antinode: momentum near $(\pi,0)$; Node: momentum near the $d$-wave node

title: Temperature evolution of the Fermi arc set by local $\Delta(T)\sim\Gamma_{0}(T)$ condition
content: The extent (length) of the Fermi arc observed in ARPES as temperature increases is plausibly determined by the locus of momenta where the local pairing amplitude $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$ becomes comparable to or smaller than the local inverse pair lifetime $\Gamma_{0}(\mathbf{k},T)$. Concretely, at a given temperature $T$ the arc comprises momenta satisfying $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)\lesssim\Gamma_{0}(\mathbf{k},T)$; as $T$ increases and $\Gamma_{0}$ grows (especially near antinodes) the arc length expands until $T\approx T^{*}$ where antinodal suppression is lost by $\Delta\sim\Gamma_{0}$.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$: momentum- and temperature-dependent pairing amplitude
- $\Gamma_{0}(\mathbf{k},T)$: momentum- and temperature-dependent inverse pair lifetime
- Fermi arc: contiguous segment of the Fermi surface showing gapless (or near-gapless) spectral weight

title: Projected singlet hopping maps to holon hopping times spinon overlap in slave-boson variables
content:
Using the slave-boson decomposition of the electron operator and neglecting double occupancy in the large-$U$ limit, the projected single-band hopping operator
    $H_t=\sum_{i\neq j,\sigma}t_{ij}(1-n_{i,-\sigma})d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}d_{j\sigma}(1-n_{j,-\sigma})$
    can be written as an operator product of a holon (charged boson) hopping term and a spinon (neutral fermion) overlap operator. Concretely, with the identification (for hole language or with appropriate particle-hole mapping) $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}\!\simeq\! e_i S_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$ and suppression of doublons ($d_i\approx0$), each projected hopping term becomes
    $t_{ij}(1-n_{i,-\sigma})d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}d_{j\sigma}(1-n_{j,-\sigma}) \;\to\; t_{ij}\,e_i e_j^{\dagger}\,S_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}S_{j\sigma}$,
    i.e. holon hopping $e_i e_j^{\dagger}$ multiplied by the spinon hopping/overlap operator $S_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}S_{j\sigma}$. Because the slave-boson current splits as $\mathbf j_c=\mathbf j_e+\mathbf j_d$ and spinons carry no charge, the charge current and charge transport are carried by the bosonic holon operators $e_i$. Thus the effective kinetic motion of the local Cu–O singlet in the slave-boson representation is the motion of charged holons dressed by the spinon overlap.

extra.notations:
- $t_{ij}$: effective hopping matrix element between Cu sites $i,j$ (as in 434). 
- $d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}$: Cu-hole creation operator (singlet carrier representation).
- $e_i$: slave-boson annihilation operator for an empty/holon state (charge $+e$) at site $i$ (441).
- $S_{i\sigma}$: auxiliary fermion (spinon) annihilation operator at site $i$ (441).
- $n_{i,-\sigma}$: number operator for the opposite spin at site $i$.

title: Mean-field doping renormalization: effective hopping amplitude scales as $t_{ij}\,\delta$
content:
Under the standard mean-field substitution that replaces the projected correlated hopping by its expectation value, the projected kinetic term
    $t_{ij}(1-n_{i,-\sigma})d_{i\sigma}^{\dagger}d_{j\sigma}(1-n_{j,-\sigma})$
    is approximated as an ordinary hopping with amplitude $t_{ij}\,\delta$, where $\delta$ is the hole doping (fraction of empty sites). Using the numerical result for the effective single-band nearest-neighbor matrix element from Proposition 423, the mean-field nearest-neighbor hopping amplitude becomes approximately
    $$t_{\mathrm{nn}}^{\mathrm{mf}}\approx (-1.5\,t_{\mathrm{hyb}})\,\delta,$$
    where $t_{\mathrm{hyb}}$ is the hybridization energy scale used in Proposition 423. More generally, each effective matrix element $t_{ij}$ from the microscopic reduction is renormalized by the same factor $\delta$ in the mean-field replacement of the projection operators.

extra.notations:
- $t_{ij}$: effective single-band hopping matrix element between Cu sites $i,j$ as computed in 423.
- $t_{\mathrm{hyb}}$: hybridization (Cu–O) energy scale denoted by $t$ in 423.
- $\delta$: doping fraction (fractional density of empty sites) as in 1417.
- $t_{\mathrm{nn}}^{\mathrm{mf}}$: mean-field nearest-neighbor hopping amplitude.

title: Bandwidth and low-doping charge response scale with doping, suppressing coherent charge transport at small $\delta$
content:
If the mean-field substitution $(1-n_{i,-\sigma})\!\to\!\delta$ is applied (1417) and the microscopic effective nearest-neighbor hopping is $t_{\mathrm{nn}}\!\approx\!-1.5\,t_{\mathrm{hyb}}$ (423), then the low-energy single-particle bandwidth $W$ of the projected single-band carrier band will scale approximately linearly with doping:
    $$W \propto |t_{ij}|\,\delta.$$
    Because, in the slave-boson picture (441), charge is carried by bosonic holons whose kinetic energy is controlled by these renormalized hopping amplitudes, the coherent Drude weight (low-frequency optical spectral weight associated with mobile charge) and the charge kinetic energy should be strongly suppressed as $\delta\to 0$, roughly proportional to $\delta$ aside from coherence and scattering factors. Consequently, at low doping the superexchange energy scale $J\sim 4t^2/U$ remains finite while the kinetic energy is reduced, enhancing the relative importance of magnetic correlations and interaction-driven localization tendencies.

extra.notations:
- $W$: single-particle bandwidth of the effective carrier band.
- $t_{ij}$: effective hopping matrix element (423).
- $\delta$: hole doping fraction (1417).
- $J$: antiferromagnetic exchange $J\approx 4t^2/U$ (1417), where $t$ in $J$ is the underlying hopping used to define $J$.

title: Phase stiffness in quasi-2D superconductors scales with the larger of layer spacing and perpendicular coherence length and admits a simple lower bound
content:
Let $V_{0}$ be the zero-temperature phase stiffness, $n_{s}(0)$ the zero-temperature superfluid density, $m^{*}$ the effective electron mass, $a$ the short-distance cutoff, $d$ the inter-layer spacing, $\xi_{\perp}$ the coherence length perpendicular to the layers, $\lambda(0)$ the zero-temperature penetration depth, $\hbar$ the reduced Planck constant, $c$ the speed of light, and $e$ the elementary charge. For quasi-two-dimensional superconductors where the cutoff is chosen as
$$
a = \max\{d,\;\xi_{\perp}\},
$$
the phase stiffness is
$$
V_{0} \;=\; \frac{\hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)\,a}{4m^{*}}
      \;=\; \frac{(\hbar c)^{2}\,a}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)} .
$$
Hence $V_{0}$ is proportional to $\max\{d,\xi_{\perp}\}$, and in particular it satisfies the lower bounds
$$
V_{0} \;\ge\; \frac{\hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)\,d}{4m^{*}}
\quad\text{and}\quad
V_{0} \;\ge\; \frac{(\hbar c)^{2}\,d}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)} ,
$$
since $a\ge d$. (Analogous inequalities with $\xi_{\perp}$ in place of $d$ also hold because $a\ge\xi_{\perp}$.)

extra.notations:
- $V_{0}$: zero-temperature phase stiffness (energy scale for long-wavelength phase fluctuations)  
- $n_{s}(0)$: zero-temperature superfluid density  
- $m^{*}$: effective electron mass  
- $a$: short-distance cutoff used when computing $V_{0}$  
- $d$: inter-layer spacing in a quasi-2D superconductor  
- $\xi_{\perp}$: superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers  
- $\lambda(0)$: zero-temperature magnetic penetration depth  
- $\hbar,c,e$: standard physical constants (reduced Planck constant, speed of light, elementary charge)

title: Phase ordering temperature in quasi-2D materials likely scales with the larger of $d$ and $\xi_{\perp}$
content:
Let $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$ denote the temperature (neglecting other degrees of freedom) at which phase order is lost and let $A$ be a dimensionless constant of order unity. If the phase-only ordering temperature is approximately proportional to the phase stiffness according to
$$
T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}} \approx A\,V_{0},
$$
then for quasi-two-dimensional superconductors with cutoff $a=\max\{d,\xi_{\perp}\}$ one expects
$$
T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}} \;\approx\; A\,\frac{\hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)}{4m^{*}}\,\max\{d,\xi_{\perp}\}
\;=\; A\,\frac{(\hbar c)^{2}}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)}\,\max\{d,\xi_{\perp}\}.
$$
Thus, other factors held fixed, increasing the larger of the inter-layer spacing and the perpendicular coherence length should raise the phase-ordering temperature and conversely a small cutoff (small $a$) enhances phase fluctuations and can reduce the phase-ordering temperature relative to mean-field expectations.

extra.notations:
- $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$: temperature where superconducting phase order vanishes in a phase-only description  
- $A$: dimensionless numerical prefactor (order unity) relating $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$ and $V_{0}$ in phase-only/XY-like models  
- other symbols as in the deduction result

title: Nonzero gap amplitude at Tc and long-lived pairs in the superconducting state
content:
Using the notation below, the following necessarily holds: the gap amplitude is nonzero at the superconducting transition temperature,
i.e. $\Delta(T_{c})>0$, and in the superconducting state ($T<T_{c}$) the inverse pair lifetime $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ becomes much smaller than the gap magnitude, i.e. $\Gamma_{0}(T)\ll\Delta(T)$ for $T<T_{c}$. Consequently the superconducting transition at $T_{c}$ is primarily the onset of long-range phase coherence (long-lived pairs) rather than the appearance of a nonzero gap amplitude.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta(T)$: gap (superconducting gap or pseudogap) magnitude as a function of temperature. 
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime (pair decoherence rate) as a function of temperature.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (onset of global phase coherence).
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap temperature where the low-energy spectral weight suppressed by the pseudogap is recovered (gap vanishes or is filled in).

title: Pseudogap as short-lived (preformed) pairs in the regime Tc < T < T*
content: Using the notation below, it is likely that the pseudogap observed for temperatures $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$ is due to the presence of pair amplitude (finite $\Delta$) without long-range phase coherence — i.e. short-lived or fluctuating Cooper pairs (preformed pairs) with a finite inverse lifetime $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ that is nonzero but smaller than $\Delta(T)$.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta(T)$: gap (pair amplitude) magnitude.
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime; finite $\Gamma_{0}$ indicates pairs are not phase-coherent over long times.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature.
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap closing (or filling-in) temperature defined by $\Delta(T^{*})\sim\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$.

title: Spectral sharpening at Tc arises mainly from increased pair lifetime rather than only reduced single-particle scattering
content: Using the notation below, it is plausible that the observed sharpening of ARPES spectral lines at $T_{c}$ is dominated by a strong reduction in the inverse pair lifetime $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ (increase of pair lifetime / onset of phase coherence) rather than solely by a reduction in single-particle scattering rate $\Gamma_{1}(T)$.

extra.notations:
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime (pair decoherence rate).
- $\Gamma_{1}(T)$: single-particle scattering rate (contributes to ARPES linewidth).
- $\Delta(T)$: gap magnitude.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature.

title: MgB2 will exhibit Meissner flux expulsion below its $T_{c}$
content: Given that magnesium diboride (MgB$_{2}$) is a superconductor with transition temperature $T_{c}=39\,$K, a field-cooling (FC) measurement that cools an MgB$_{2}$ sample from above $39\,$K to below $39\,$K in the presence of a small external magnetic field $H$ will show magnetic flux expulsion (the Meissner effect). Concretely, the measured magnetization $M$ (or magnetic susceptibility $\chi=dM/dH$) will become negative below $T=39\,$K consistent with diamagnetic screening of the applied field.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (here $39\,$K). 
- $H$: externally applied magnetic field during the field-cooling measurement. 
- $M$: magnetization of the sample; $\chi=dM/dH$ is magnetic susceptibility.

title: Field-cooled diamagnetic signal may be reduced by flux trapping in MgB$_{2}$
content: While an FC measurement on MgB$_{2}$ cooled below $T_{c}=39\,$K will show flux expulsion, the magnitude of the diamagnetic signal observed during field cooling may be smaller than the zero-field-cooled (ZFC) diamagnetic response because of partial flux penetration and pinning. In other words, $|M_{\mathrm{FC}}(T<T_{c})|$ may be noticeably less than $|M_{\mathrm{ZFC}}(T<T_{c})|$ due to trapped vortices in a type-II superconducting state.

extra.notations:
- $M_{\mathrm{FC}}$: magnetization measured after cooling the sample in an applied field (field-cooled).
- $M_{\mathrm{ZFC}}$: magnetization measured after cooling in zero field then applying the field at low temperature (zero-field-cooled).
- $T_{c}=39\,$K: MgB$_{2}$ superconducting transition temperature.

title: Upper bound on temperature for static stripe order set by the LTT→LTO transition
content: For La2CuO4-derived cuprates (e.g., La_{2-x}Ba_xCuO_4, La_{1.6-x}Nd_{0.4}Sr_xCuO_4), let T_stripe be the highest temperature at which static (long-lived, diffraction-detectable) charge or spin stripe order is present, and let T_LTT-LTO denote the temperature of the structural transition from the low-temperature tetragonal (LTT) to the low-temperature orthorhombic (LTO) phase. From the siblings, T_stripe ≤ T_LTT-LTO; equivalently, static stripe order cannot persist at temperatures above the LTT→LTO transition temperature (≈ 70 K in the cited systems).

extra.notations:
- T_stripe: highest temperature with static stripe order present (K)
- T_LTT-LTO: temperature of the structural LTT→LTO transition (K)
- LTT: low‑temperature tetragonal structural phase
- LTO: low‑temperature orthorhombic structural phase

title: Presence of orientation‑locked static stripes strongly indicates the LTT structural symmetry (or equivalent symmetry breaking)
content: In La‑based cuprates, the observation of static stripe order that is orientation‑locked to the crystallographic axes (i.e., stripes running strictly horizontal or vertical in the CuO_2 planes, producing satellite Bragg peaks aligned with principal reciprocal‑lattice directions) strongly indicates that, at the measurement temperature, the lattice is in the LTT phase or otherwise exhibits the same in‑plane symmetry breaking that permits lattice pinning of horizontal/vertical stripes. Here "strongly indicates" means the probability that such orientation‑locked static stripes occur without the LTT (or an equivalent symmetry‑breaking distortion) is low but nonzero, because other pinning sources (quenched disorder, twin boundaries, externally applied strain) could in principle lock stripe orientation.

extra.notations:
- "Orientation‑locked": stripe modulation wavevector constrained to lie along principal CuO_2 lattice axes (horizontal/vertical)
- LTT: low‑temperature tetragonal structural phase (favors horizontal/vertical pinning)
- LTO: low‑temperature orthorhombic structural phase (does not provide comparable lattice pinning)

title: When $\xi_{\perp}>d$, $V_{0}$ (and $T_{\theta}^{max}$) scale with $\xi_{\perp}$ (up to an order‑unity factor)
content:
Let $a$ denote the short-distance cutoff used to compute the zero-temperature phase stiffness $V_{0}$. Both siblings define
$$
V_{0}=\frac{(\hbar c)^{2}a}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)} .
$$
Both specify that $a$ is the larger of the inter-layer spacing $d$ and a length proportional to the perpendicular coherence length $\xi_{\perp}$. Therefore, whenever $\xi_{\perp}>d$ the cutoff $a$ equals the $\xi_{\perp}$-based length and $V_{0}$ is proportional to that $\xi_{\perp}$-based length. Consequently the maximum phase-ordering temperature
$$
T_{\theta}^{max}=A\,V_{0}
$$
is also proportional to the same $\xi_{\perp}$-based length (with the same order-unity prefactor $A$).

extra.notations:
- $\hbar$: reduced Planck constant
- $c$: speed of light in vacuum
- $e$: elementary charge
- $\lambda(0)$: magnetic penetration depth at zero temperature
- $a$: short-distance spatial cutoff
- $d$: inter-layer spacing
- $\xi_{\perp}$: superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers
- $V_{0}$: zero-temperature phase stiffness
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: maximum phase-ordering temperature computed from $V_{0}$
- $A$: dimensionless constant of order unity

title: Order‑unity ambiguity in the $\xi_{\perp}$-based cutoff produces comparable uncertainty in $V_{0}$ and $T_{\theta}^{max}$
content:
The two siblings differ only by an order-unity prescription for the $\xi_{\perp}$-based cutoff (one writing the $\xi_{\perp}$-based length with a different unit-factor). Therefore the numerical value of $a$ when determined by $\xi_{\perp}$ is uncertain by an order-unity multiplicative factor $C\sim O(1)$. Since
$$
V_{0}=\frac{(\hbar c)^{2}a}{16\pi e^{2}\lambda^{2}(0)},
$$
a fractional uncertainty of order unity in $a$ maps directly to a comparable fractional uncertainty in $V_{0}$ and hence in $T_{\theta}^{max}=A\,V_{0}$. As a result, comparisons that seek to determine whether phase fluctuations (as estimated by $T_{\theta}^{max}$) suppress the mean-field scale $T^{MF}$ or the observed $T_{c}$ can be uncertain by factors of order unity unless the precise definition (numerical factor) of the $\xi_{\perp}$-based length is specified.

extra.notations:
- $C$: unknown order-unity multiplicative factor relating different $\xi_{\perp}$-based cutoff prescriptions, i.e. $a=\max(d,\,C\,\xi_{\perp})$
- other symbols as in the deduction

title: LTT is required for static, pinned stripe order in La2CuO4‑derived cuprates at $x\approx 1/8$
content: In La2CuO4‑derived cuprates (examples: La2-xBaxCuO4, La1.6-xNd0.4SrxCuO4) at hole concentration $x\approx 1/8$, static (time‑independent) stripe order of charge and/or spin in the CuO$_2$ planes exists only while the crystal is in the low‑temperature tetragonal (LTT) phase; when the crystal is in the low‑temperature orthorhombic (LTO) phase the stripe order ceases to be static (it is absent or dynamic/unpinned). Here LTT = low‑temperature tetragonal crystal structure, LTO = low‑temperature orthorhombic crystal structure, and "stripe order" = spatially modulated charge or spin density in the CuO$_2$ planes.

extra.notations:
- LTT: low‑temperature tetragonal structure (see content)
- LTO: low‑temperature orthorhombic structure (see content)
- $x$: hole concentration per Cu site; $x\approx 1/8$ denotes the 1/8 doping region
- stripe order: static spatial modulation of charge/spin density in CuO$_2$ planes

title: Suppressing the LTT distortion should restore superconductivity near $x\approx 1/8$
content: For La2CuO4‑derived cuprates with hole concentration $x\approx 1/8$, experimental or chemical perturbations that suppress or remove the low‑temperature tetragonal (LTT) distortion (for example, hydrostatic pressure or substitutions that favor the LTO structure) are likely to eliminate the static, pinned stripe order and thereby restore or increase the bulk superconducting transition temperature $T_c$ relative to the suppressed value observed when the LTT phase and pinned stripes are present. Symbols: LTT = low‑temperature tetragonal structure; LTO = low‑temperature orthorhombic structure; $T_c$ = superconducting transition temperature.

extra.notations:
- LTT: low‑temperature tetragonal structure
- LTO: low‑temperature orthorhombic structure
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature
- $x\approx 1/8$: the 1/8 hole‑doping region

title: Concurrent three-component decomposition with specified DHO parameters for YBa2Cu3O6.92
content: For YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6.92}$, the non-magnetic (nuclear) part of the inelastic neutron-scattering spectrum can be represented as the sum of (i) a flat background, (ii) a spectral feature peaked near $\hbar\omega\approx 20\ \mathrm{meV}$, and (iii) a broad damped-harmonic-oscillator (DHO) component whose characteristic energy and linewidth are $\hbar\omega\approx 21.5\ \mathrm{meV}$ and $\Gamma_{\omega}\approx 26\ \mathrm{meV}$ respectively. All symbols are defined below.

extra.notations:
- $S(\vec{Q},\hbar\omega)$: dynamical structure factor at momentum transfer $\vec{Q}$ and energy transfer $\hbar\omega$. 
- $\hbar\omega$: energy transfer (meV). 
- DHO: damped harmonic-oscillator spectral component with resonance energy $\hbar\omega$ and linewidth (damping) $\Gamma_{\omega}$.
- YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6.92}$: the specific compound considered.

title: Broad DHO component is strongly damped (overdamped) for the given parameters
content: Given the quoted DHO parameters for YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6.92}$, the damping parameter exceeds the resonance energy: $\Gamma_{\omega}\approx 26\ \mathrm{meV} > \hbar\omega\approx 21.5\ \mathrm{meV}$. Therefore the DHO describes a strongly damped (overdamped or very heavily broadened) excitation; the spectral contribution is broad and does not correspond to a sharp, underdamped mode.

extra.notations:
- $\hbar\omega$: DHO resonance energy (meV).
- $\Gamma_{\omega}$: DHO linewidth / damping (meV).
- "Overdamped" here denotes damping of the same order or larger than the resonance frequency, producing a broad, weakly peaked or non-oscillatory spectral response.

title: The ~20 meV peaked feature may be largely accounted for by the broad DHO
content: The spectral feature reported near $\sim20\,$meV (a component separate from the DHO in the general decomposition) could be substantially or entirely produced by the broad DHO component with $\hbar\omega\approx21.5\,$meV and $\Gamma_{\omega}\approx26\,$meV; i.e., when fitting experimental spectra for YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6.92}$ a single broad DHO might reproduce a broad maximum centered near 20–22\,meV without requiring an additional distinct narrow peak.

extra.notations:
- "Peaked feature near $\sim20\,$meV": the component identified in 1443 distinct from the DHO in that general decomposition.
- DHO parameters from 1627 as above.

title: Strong-coupling / non-BCS superconducting regime (algebraic consequence)
content: Given the relations k_{F}\,\xi_{0}=E_{F}/\Delta \ll 1 and m^{*}\approx m/\delta (with 0<\delta\ll1), it follows algebraically that the superconducting gap \Delta is parametrically larger than the Fermi energy E_{F} (i.e. \Delta\gg E_{F}) and the pair size (coherence length) \xi_{0} is much smaller than the inter-particle spacing set by 1/k_{F} (i.e. \xi_{0}\ll 1/k_{F}). Consequently, the superconducting state lies outside the weak-coupling Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) regime: the usual BCS assumption E_{F}\gg\Delta is violated, so a weak-coupling BCS quasiparticle-pairing description cannot consistently describe the low-energy superconductivity in this regime.

extra.notations:
- k_{F}: Fermi wave vector (sets inverse inter-particle spacing).
- \xi_{0}: superconducting coherence length (pair size).
- E_{F}: Fermi energy.
- \Delta: superconducting energy gap.
- m^{*}: quasiparticle effective mass; m: bare electron mass.
- \delta: fractional hole doping (0<\delta\ll1).

title: Optimal doping and effective mass at optimality: \delta_{opt}\sim t/U and m^{*}_{opt}\sim m\,U/t
content: Using the author's statement that the maximum transition temperature T_{c} occurs when the kinetic-energy scale matches the pair-binding scale (t/U\sim\delta), the optimal doping is plausibly \delta_{\mathrm{opt}}\sim t/U. Combining this with m^{*}\approx m/\delta implies an estimate for the effective mass at optimal doping: m^{*}_{\mathrm{opt}}\sim m/\delta_{\mathrm{opt}}\sim m\,(U/t). Thus at the putative optimal doping the quasiparticle mass grows in proportion to U/t, becoming large when correlations are strong (U\gg t).

extra.notations:
- \delta_{\mathrm{opt}}: fractional doping at which T_{c} is maximal.
- t: single-particle hopping integral (kinetic energy scale).
- U: on-site Coulomb repulsion.
- m^{*}_{\mathrm{opt}}: effective mass evaluated at \delta_{\mathrm{opt}}.
- m: bare electron mass.

title: Realization of the theoretical impurity-induced four-fold DOS pattern in Zn-doped BSCCO
content: If theoretical models predict that an atomic-scale impurity in a superconductor with d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} pairing produces a four-fold symmetric local density of states (DOS) pattern whose arms align with the crystallographic a- and b-axes, and high-resolution STM measurements of the differential conductance dI/dV around Zn impurity sites in BSCCO show precisely such a four-fold symmetric cross aligned with the a- and b-axes, then the specific theoretical prediction about the spatial symmetry and orientation of the impurity-induced quasiparticle DOS is empirically realized in Zn-doped BSCCO. Here DOS denotes the local quasiparticle density of states as probed by spatially resolved dI/dV, Zn denotes zinc atoms substituting copper sites, and BSCCO denotes Bi2Sr2Ca(Cu1-xZnx)2O8+δ.

extra.notations:
- dI/dV: differential tunneling conductance (local probe of DOS).
- DOS: local quasiparticle density of states.
- BSCCO: Bi2Sr2Ca(Cu1-xZnx)2O8+δ, the material studied.
- Zn: zinc impurity atom substituted on Cu sites.
- d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}: superconducting order parameter symmetry with nodes along the crystallographic a and b axes.

title: Experimental observation supports d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} pairing symmetry in BSCCO
content: The observation that impurity-induced local DOS around Zn in BSCCO forms a four-fold symmetric cross whose arms align with the crystallographic a- and b-axes, together with the theoretical result that such a pattern is a distinctive consequence of a d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} superconducting gap (nodes along a and b), makes it highly likely that the superconducting order parameter in BSCCO is of d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} symmetry (i.e., has gap nodes along the a- and b-axes). Symbols: BSCCO = Bi2Sr2Ca(Cu1-xZnx)2O8+δ; DOS = local quasiparticle density of states; d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} = order parameter symmetry with four-fold angular dependence and nodes along a and b.

extra.notations:
- BSCCO: Bi2Sr2Ca(Cu1-xZnx)2O8+δ.
- DOS: local quasiparticle density of states measured by dI/dV.
- d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}: superconducting order parameter symmetry with nodes along crystallographic a and b axes.

title: YBa2Cu3O6.8 (YBCO) has d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} pairing symmetry
content: Let YBCO denote the cuprate superconductor YBa2Cu3O6.8. Let Δ(k_x,k_y) be the complex superconducting order parameter as a function of in-plane wavevector components k_x,k_y, and let δ_{ab} be the intrinsic phase difference between order-parameter values sampled along the orthogonal in-plane crystallographic directions labeled a and b. Proposition 1504 reports that phase-sensitive Josephson experiments on YBCO measure an intrinsic phase difference of approximately π between tunneling along the a and b directions. Proposition 1617 states the general equivalence that a d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} pairing symmetry is characterized by, and therefore ↔, an intrinsic phase difference δ_{ab}=π between orthogonal crystal directions. Combining these two statements yields the necessary conclusion that the superconducting pairing symmetry of YBCO is d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} (i.e., the order parameter changes sign under a 90° rotation interchanging a and b).

extra.notations:
- YBCO: YBa2Cu3O6.8, the cuprate high-Tc superconductor.
- Δ(k_x,k_y): complex superconducting order parameter as a function of in-plane wavevector.
- δ_{ab}: intrinsic phase difference between order-parameter values sampled along the a and b crystal directions.
- d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}: pairing symmetry with a sign change under 90° rotation (equivalently δ_{ab}=π).

title: YBCO order parameter is well approximated by a $\Delta_{0}[\cos(k_{x}a)-\cos(k_{y}a)]$ form with little isotropic s-wave admixture
content: Let Δ(k_x,k_y) be the superconducting order parameter, Δ_0 its maximum amplitude, and a the in-plane lattice constant. Given (i) phase-sensitive measurements in YBCO that indicate an intrinsic phase difference δ_{ab}≈π between a- and b-direction tunneling (proposition 1504) and (ii) the form Δ(k_x,k_y)=Δ_{0}[\cos(k_{x}a)-\cos(k_{y}a)] as a canonical realization of d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} symmetry (proposition 960), it is plausible that the superconducting gap in YBCO is well approximated by this $\Delta_{0}[\cos(k_{x}a)-\cos(k_{y}a)]$ momentum dependence and that any spatially uniform (isotropic) s-wave component is small compared with the d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} component.

extra.notations:
- Δ(k_x,k_y): superconducting order parameter as a function of in-plane momentum.
- Δ_0: maximum gap amplitude.
- a: in-plane lattice constant.
- δ_{ab}: intrinsic phase difference between a and b directions.
- s-wave admixture: a momentum-independent (isotropic) component added to the dominant d-wave gap.

title: Stripe is a Luther–Emery liquid with finite zero-temperature Drude weight
content: Given that (i) a spin gap $\Delta_s>0$ is induced on the one-dimensional electron gas (1DEG, the stripe) by pair-hopping to an active environment (Propositions 605 and 1519) and (ii) the stripe charge sector remains a gapless Gaussian Luttinger liquid with unrenormalized charge velocity $v_c$ and unrenormalized zero-temperature Drude weight (Proposition 612), the low-energy state of the isolated stripe is necessarily a Luther–Emery liquid: the spin sector is gapped while the charge sector is gapless. Consequently the stripe at $T=0$ has a nonzero charge stiffness (Drude weight), supports gapless charge modes with velocity $v_c$, and exhibits power-law (algebraically decaying) superconducting singlet-pairing and charge-density-wave correlations rather than true long-range superconducting order.

extra.notations:
- 1DEG: one-dimensional electron gas (metallic stripe)  
- $\Delta_s$: spin gap (energy to create lowest spinful excitation on the stripe)  
- $v_c$: charge propagation velocity of the stripe (Gaussian Luttinger-liquid sound velocity)  
- Drude weight: zero-temperature charge stiffness (measure of dissipationless charge response)

title: Induced spin gap corresponds to preformed singlet pairs; global superconducting coherence requires inter-stripe Josephson coupling
content: The induced spin gap $\Delta_s$ on each stripe (a measure of the energy to break a singlet) can be interpreted as the formation of local (preformed) singlet pairs on the stripe. However, because an isolated 1D stripe with a gapless charge sector cannot sustain true long-range superconducting order, macroscopic superconducting phase coherence (finite superconducting transition temperature $T_c$ of an array or plane of stripes) will plausibly require Josephson coupling between stripes. Therefore one expects a separation of energy scales: a higher pairing (spin-gap) crossover scale $T_{pair}\sim\varepsilon^*$ (from Proposition 605) at which singlets form on stripes, and a lower phase-ordering scale $T_c$ controlled by the inter-stripe Josephson coupling; typically $T_c \ll T_{pair}$ when inter-stripe coupling is weak.

extra.notations:
- $T_{pair}$: crossover temperature for spin-gap / local pairing on a stripe  
- $\Delta_s$: spin gap on a stripe (pair-binding energy scale)  
- Josephson coupling: effective inter-stripe pair-hopping that establishes phase coherence between stripes  
- $T_c$: global superconducting transition temperature (phase coherence onset)

title: π† creates a charge-2, spin-1, center-of-mass momentum Q, d-wave Cooper pair
content:
The operator $\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$ is a two-fermion (pair) creation operator defined by
$$\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}=\sum_{p} g(p)\, c_{p+Q,\sigma}^{\dagger}\,(\sigma^{\alpha}\sigma^{y})_{\sigma\sigma'}\,c_{-p,\sigma'}^{\dagger},$$
where $c_{p\sigma}^\dagger$ creates an electron of momentum $p$ and spin $\sigma$, $g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y$ is the d-wave form factor, and $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$ is the antiferromagnetic ordering vector. From this definition it follows necessarily that:
- $\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$ creates two electrons (thus carries electric charge $+2e$ relative to the vacuum): it is a bilinear in two creation operators.
- The two single-particle momenta are $p+Q$ and $-p$, so the center-of-mass (total) momentum of the created pair is $(p+Q)+(-p)=Q$; hence the pair carries total momentum $Q$.
- The spin part of the pair wavefunction is given by the matrix $(\sigma^{\alpha}\sigma^{y})_{\sigma\sigma'}$, which is the standard triplet (spin-1) pairing structure; therefore the pair has total spin $S=1$.
- The internal orbital (relative momentum) structure is weighted by $g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y$, so the internal orbital symmetry of the pair is d-wave.

extra.notations:
- $\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$: SO(5) generator written as a two-electron creation operator (index $\alpha\in\{x,y,z\}$ labels spin component). 
- $c_{p\sigma}^\dagger$: electron creation operator with momentum $p$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y$: d-wave form factor (relative-momentum dependence).
- $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$: antiferromagnetic ordering (center-of-mass) momentum.
- $\sigma^\alpha$: Pauli matrices.

title: π operators transform as spin vectors and change charge by ±2
content:
Given the identification of the SO(5) generators $L_{ab}$ with physical operators (with $L_{15}=\mathcal{Q}$ the total charge, $L_{\alpha\beta}=\epsilon_{\alpha\beta\gamma}S_{\gamma}$ the spin block, and $L_{1\alpha},L_{5\alpha}$ the six $\pi_{\alpha},\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$ operators), the following operator commutation relations necessarily hold (up to the conventional choice of factors of $i$ in the Lie algebra representation):
- The $\pi_{\alpha}$ form a vector under spin rotations:
  $$[\,S_{\gamma},\,\pi_{\alpha}\,]= i\,\varepsilon_{\gamma\alpha\beta}\,\pi_{\beta},$$
  where $\varepsilon_{\gamma\alpha\beta}$ is the Levi-Civita symbol.
- The charge operator $\mathcal{Q}$ counts particle number, and because $\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$ creates two electrons,
  $$[\,\mathcal{Q},\,\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}\,]=2\,\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger},\qquad [\,\mathcal{Q},\,\pi_{\alpha}\,]=-2\,\pi_{\alpha}.$$
These relations express that the $\pi$'s transform as SO(3)-vectors under spin and carry charge $\pm2$ under the U(1) generated by $\mathcal{Q}$.

extra.notations:
- $L_{ab}$: antisymmetric SO(5) generators, $a,b\in\{1,\dots,5\}$.
- $\mathcal{Q}=L_{15}$: total charge (electron number) operator.
- $S_{\gamma}$: total spin component (mapped from $L_{\alpha\beta}$).
- $\pi_{\alpha},\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$: SO(5) generators $L_{1\alpha},L_{5\alpha}$ that rotate AF and SC order.
- $\varepsilon_{\gamma\alpha\beta}$: Levi-Civita symbol.

title: Explicit expression for the maximum phase-ordering temperature
content:
Combining the proportionality T_{\theta}^{max} = A V_{0} (with A a dimensionless constant) and the definition V_{0} = \hbar^{2} n_{s}(0) a / 4m^{*} yields the identity
$$T_{\theta}^{max} = A \frac{\hbar^{2} n_{s}(0) a}{4 m^{*}}\;.$$
Here T_{\theta}^{max} is the upper-bound temperature for phase order, A is an O(1) dimensionless constant determined by short-distance physics and cutoff choice, \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, n_{s}(0) is the zero-temperature superfluid density, a is the short-distance spatial cutoff (length), and m^{*} is the electronic effective mass.

extra.notations:
- T_{\theta}^{max}: temperature at which phase order disappears (upper bound on T_{c})
- A: dimensionless constant of order unity
- V_{0}: zero-temperature phase stiffness energy scale
- \hbar: reduced Planck constant
- n_{s}(0): zero-temperature superfluid density
- a: short-distance spatial cutoff (length)
- m^{*}: effective mass

title: Inverse-square dependence of T_{\theta}^{max} on penetration depth
content:
Under the usual London relation between superfluid density and the zero-temperature penetration depth, the maximum phase-ordering temperature is expected to scale proportionally to the short-distance cutoff and inversely with the square of the penetration depth:
$$T_{\theta}^{max} \propto a\,\lambda(0)^{-2}\;.$$
More explicitly, using \lambda(0)^{-2}\propto n_{s}(0)/m^{*} (London electrodynamics), and V_{0}\propto \hbar^{2} a\,(n_{s}(0)/m^{*}), one obtains T_{\theta}^{max}=A V_{0}\propto A\,a\,\lambda(0)^{-2}. Symbols: \lambda(0) is the zero-temperature London penetration depth and other symbols are as defined above.

extra.notations:
- \lambda(0): London (magnetic) penetration depth at zero temperature
- other symbols: as in the siblings (A, a, n_{s}(0), m^{*}, V_{0})

title: Below Tc the inverse pair lifetime is much smaller than the gap (Γ0 << Δ)
content:
Let Γ0(T) denote the inverse pair lifetime (pairing-related broadening) and Δ(T) the superconducting gap or pseudogap magnitude. If, as stated in Proposition 1536, global phase coherence appears at the superconducting transition temperature Tc and ARPES shows sharpening of low-energy spectral features into resolution-limited quasiparticle peaks below Tc, and if, as stated in Proposition 675, the pairing-related suppression (pseudogap) is filled in when Δ(T) ≈ Γ0(T), then for the superconducting-state spectra to show sharp, well-defined quasiparticle peaks one must have, at T < Tc,
$$\Gamma_{0}(T) \ll \Delta(T).$$
That is, the pairing-related broadening must be negligible compared with the gap magnitude in the coherent superconducting state.

extra.notations:
- Γ0(T): inverse pair lifetime (pairing-related broadening) at temperature T.
- Δ(T): superconducting gap (or pseudogap) magnitude at temperature T.
- Tc: superconducting critical temperature (onset of global phase coherence).
- ARPES peak "resolution-limited": observed linewidth is limited by instrumental resolution, implying intrinsic lifetime broadening is very small.

title: In underdoped Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8−δ the pseudogap temperature T* exceeds Tc (T* > Tc), so the pseudogap coexists with superconducting coherence
content: Let T* be the temperature at which the pairing-related pseudogap is effectively closed (Δ(T*) ≈ Γ0(T*)). For underdoped Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8−δ, the observation that the superconducting transition at Tc produces sharpening of low-energy ARPES lineshapes into resolution-limited quasiparticle peaks (Proposition 1536) does not imply that the pairing gap (pseudogap) has closed at Tc. Combining this with Proposition 675 (which defines T* via Δ ≈ Γ0) makes it likely that the pseudogap temperature T* lies above Tc (T* > Tc), so that a pairing-related suppression of spectral weight persists through Tc and coexists with the phase-coherent superconducting state for T < Tc.

extra.notations:
- T*: pseudogap closing/filling-in temperature defined by Δ(T*) ≈ Γ0(T*).
- Tc: superconducting transition temperature where global phase coherence appears.
- Δ(T), Γ0(T): as defined above.
- "Coexistence": presence of a pairing-related suppression (pseudogap) in the single-particle spectrum while long-lived, phase-coherent quasiparticles (sharp ARPES peaks) also exist.

title: Phase-ordering sets an upper bound proportional to superfluid stiffness
content:
If the phase-ordering temperature $T_{\theta}^{max}$ is proportional to the zero-temperature phase stiffness (equivalently to the inverse-square penetration depth $\lambda^{-2}(0)$) and $T_{c}\le T_{\theta}^{max}$, then there exists a material-independent constant $C$ (set by microscopic short-distance physics and $A$ in the siblings) such that
$$T_{c}\le T_{\theta}^{max}=C\,\lambda^{-2}(0).$$
Here $T_{c}$ is the observed superconducting transition temperature, $\lambda(0)$ is the London penetration depth at $T=0$, and $C$ collects universal factors (including $A$, $d$, $\xi_{\perp}$ and fundamental constants) appropriate to the quasi-two-dimensional cuprates.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: Observed superconducting transition temperature. 
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: Maximum phase-ordering temperature (upper bound on $T_c$ obtained from phase stiffness). 
- $\lambda(0)$: Magnetic penetration depth at zero temperature. 
- $C$: Proportionality constant depending on short-distance cutoff and geometric factors.

title: Doping controls whether $T_c$ is phase-ordering or mean-field limited
content: For cuprate materials, there are two distinct regimes controlled by doping: (i) when underdoped so that $T_{\theta}^{max}/T_{c}\approx 1$, phase fluctuations (phase-ordering physics) determine and limit the observed $T_{c}$, i.e. $T_{c}\approx T_{\theta}^{max}\ll T^{MF}$; (ii) when overdoped so that $T_{\theta}^{max}/T_{c}\gtrsim 2$, mean-field pairing sets the transition, i.e. $T_{c}\approx T^{MF}$. Optimally doped samples lie near the crossover between these regimes.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: Observed superconducting transition temperature.
- $T^{MF}$: Mean-field pairing temperature (local-pair-formation scale).
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: Maximum phase-ordering temperature computed from phase stiffness.

title: At extremely low doping, phase-ordering may force a linear-in-doping $T_c$ instead of the BEC $\,\delta^{2/3}$ law
content:
Let $\delta\ll1$ be the hole concentration per CuO2 unit. If the charged carriers behave as bosonic holes of density $n\propto\delta$ with Bose–Einstein condensation temperature $T_{BEC}\propto n^{2/3}\propto\delta^{2/3}$ (as in propositions 123 and 1422), while the phase-ordering upper bound scales with zero-temperature stiffness $T_{\theta}^{max}\propto\lambda^{-2}(0)\propto n_s/m^*$ and if the superfluid density scales roughly as $n_s\propto\delta$ with an effective mass $m^*$ that does not increase faster than $\delta^{-1/3}$ as $\delta\to0$, then for sufficiently small $\delta$ one expects $T_{\theta}^{max} < T_{BEC}$ and therefore the observed transition temperature will be limited by phase-ordering and scale approximately as
$$T_c \approx T_{\theta}^{max} \propto \delta\quad\text{(rather than }\delta^{2/3}\text{).}$$
Symbols: $\delta$ is hole doping fraction; $n\propto\delta$ boson density; $m^*$ effective mass of bosons; $\lambda^{-2}(0)$ proportional to superfluid stiffness.

extra.notations:
- $\delta$: Hole doping fraction (dimensionless, $\delta\ll1$). 
- $T_{BEC}$: Bose–Einstein condensation temperature of charged bosons, $T_{BEC}\propto n^{2/3}$. 
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: Phase-ordering upper bound, $T_{\theta}^{max}\propto\lambda^{-2}(0)\propto n_s/m^*$. 
- $n_s$: Superfluid density at $T=0$. 
- $m^*$: Effective mass of the carriers.

title: Unit hole per domain-line unit cell (linear charge density of stripes)
content: From the counting rule "the number of domain-line unit cells equals the number of charge carriers" (denote the number of excess holes by $N_{h}$ and the number of domain-line unit cells by $N_{dl}$), it follows that $N_{dl}=N_{h}$. Therefore each domain-line unit cell hosts exactly one excess hole on average; equivalently, the linear charge density along a straight domain line equals one hole per domain-line unit cell. Symbols: $N_{h}$ = number of excess holes (carriers), $N_{dl}$ = number of elementary unit cells participating in domain lines.

extra.notations:
- $N_{h}$: number of excess holes (carriers) in the supercell
- $N_{dl}$: number of elementary domain-line unit cells in the supercell
- $V$: hybridization (hopping) energy scale used in the siblings

title: Bandwidth-driven kinetic contribution partially explains orientation preference
content: Let the midgap band bandwidth be $W_{(1,0)}\approx 0.3V$ for stripes oriented along $(1,0)$ and $W_{(1,1)}\approx 0.1V$ for $(1,1)$ (as reported). If the midgap band is (partially or fully) occupied by the excess holes that reside on the stripe, the kinetic (band) energy per carrier is lower for the larger-bandwidth orientation because a broader dispersive band allows a greater downward shift of occupied states. Therefore the larger $W_{(1,0)}$ provides a kinetic-energy-based stabilization of $(1,0)$ stripes relative to $(1,1)$. However, the reported energetic preference of only $0.005V$ per carrier for $(1,0)$ (Proposition 1483) is much smaller than the raw bandwidth difference $W_{(1,0)}-W_{(1,1)}\approx 0.2V$, so other energy terms (interaction energy, Hartree potentials, exchange, lattice/elastic energy, or differences in localization energy) must partially cancel the kinetic advantage. In short: kinetic (bandwidth) effects are a plausible primary driver of the orientation anisotropy, but significant compensation by non-kinetic terms is required to yield the small net energy difference.

extra.notations:
- $W_{(1,0)}$: midgap band bandwidth for stripes oriented along $(1,0)$ (given $\approx 0.3V$)
- $W_{(1,1)}$: midgap band bandwidth for stripes oriented along $(1,1)$ (given $\approx 0.1V$)
- $V$: hybridization (hopping) energy scale used in the siblings
- Energy differences are reported per excess carrier

title: Single-particle scattering rate linear in max(|ω|,T)
content:
If electrons scatter dominantly from the charge- and spin-density excitations whose absorptive polarizability has the universal form
$$\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim\begin{cases}
-N(0)\,(\omega/T), & |\omega|<T,\\[4pt]
-N(0)\,\mathrm{sgn}(\omega), & |\omega|>T,
\end{cases}$$
then the leading (inelastic) contribution to the retarded single-particle self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ has an imaginary part
$$\mathrm{Im}\,\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)\propto\max\{|\omega|,\,T\},$$
up to a (weakly momentum dependent) dimensionless coupling constant and an ultraviolet cutoff. Here $\omega$ is energy measured from the chemical potential and $T$ is the temperature.

extra.notations:
- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: contribution of the density excitations to the polarizability. 
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level.
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy; $\mathrm{Im}\,\Sigma$ is the single-particle scattering rate.
- $T$: temperature (energy units).

title: Approximate linear-in-temperature electrical resistivity in the metallic phase
content:
Given that the dominant low-energy electronic scattering rate satisfies $\mathrm{Im}\,\Sigma(\omega)\propto\max\{|\omega|,T\}$ because of the specified density excitations, the electrical resistivity $\rho(T)$ of the metallic (normal) phase of the Cu–O materials will be approximately proportional to temperature:
$$\rho(T)\propto T$$
for temperatures and frequencies below the ultraviolet cutoff where these excitations control scattering. Here $\rho$ is the dc resistivity.

extra.notations:
- $\rho(T)$: electrical dc resistivity at temperature $T$.
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ and $\mathrm{Im}\,\Sigma$: as in the deduction above.
- $T$: temperature (energy units).

title: Midgap band supplies one spinless single-particle state per domain-line unit cell
content: Let $N_{dl}$ be the number of domain-line unit cells and let $N_{h}$ be the number of excess holes (carriers). Proposition 1490 states the counting rule $N_{dl}=N_{h}$ and that the line is optimally stable when the midgap band is completely filled with those excess holes; it also characterizes the domain line as spin-zero (local magnetic moment strongly suppressed at the wall). Therefore the midgap band must provide exactly one available single-particle state per domain-line unit cell that is occupied by one hole. Concretely: the total number of single-particle states in the midgap band equals $N_{dl}$, and each such state is singly occupied by one hole; the midgap band is not spin-degenerate (i.e., it supplies one state per unit cell in total, not two spin states per unit cell).

extra.notations:
- $N_{dl}$: number of domain-line unit cells in the supercell (from Proposition 1490). 
- $N_{h}$: number of excess holes/carriers (doped holes). 
- "midgap band": the electronic band inside the antiferromagnetic gap associated with the domain line (Proposition 1490).
- "spin-zero / spinless": local magnetic moment suppressed at the domain line (Proposition 1490 and parent).

title: Stripe (domain-line) density (spacing) is set by hole doping: inverse relation
content: Let $x$ denote the concentration of excess holes per CuO$_2$ unit cell (doping). Using the counting rule $N_{dl}=N_{h}$ (Proposition 1490) and assuming straight, single-unit-thickness domain lines that each contain one domain-line unit cell per elementary translation along the direction perpendicular to the line, the areal density of domain-line unit cells equals the hole density. Therefore the linear density of domain lines (number per unit length across the plane) and hence the average spacing between adjacent straight domain lines scale inversely with the hole concentration: the spacing between domain lines (measured in elementary CuO$_2$ unit-cell units) is approximately $1/x$.

extra.notations:
- $x$: excess hole concentration per CuO$_2$ unit cell (doping fraction). 
- $N_{dl}$: number of domain-line unit cells (Proposition 1490). 
- "spacing": average distance between neighboring straight domain lines measured in units of the elementary CuO$_2$ lattice constant.

title: Orientation preference $(1,0)$ over $(1,1)$ likely driven mainly by larger midgap-band bandwidth (kinetic-energy gain)
content: Proposition 1490 reports midgap-band bandwidths of approximately $0.3V$ for the $(1,0)$ orientation and $0.1V$ for the $(1,1)$ orientation; Proposition 1476 reports an energetic favoring of straight domain lines along $(1,0)$ over $(1,1)$ by about $0.005V$ per carrier.
