It is therefore plausible that the principal microscopic origin of the $(1,0)$ orientation preference is a larger kinetic (band) delocalization energy gained when the midgap band is filled: the broader $(1,0)$ midgap band allows greater lowering of electronic energy per carrier upon filling, producing a net energetic advantage for the $(1,0)$ orientation that is consistent in sign with the observed small preference.

extra.notations:
- $V$: energy unit used for $p$–$d$ hybridization (both propositions). 
- "bandwidth": energy width of the midgap electronic band (Proposition 1490). 
- "kinetic-energy (delocalization) gain": lowering of electronic energy due to band dispersion when carriers occupy extended states, relative to more localized configurations.

title: Symmetry-enforced equality of symmetry-equivalent metal–oxygen bond lengths
content: If Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 is described in space group I4/mmm and its crystallographic refinements report metal–oxygen bond distances (in Å), then any two metal–oxygen pairs that are related by a symmetry operation of the I4/mmm space group must have equal reported bond lengths (within the numerical precision of the refinement). Equivalently, bond distances belonging to the same Wyckoff-equivalence class or generated from the same crystallographic site under I4/mmm operations will appear as repeated, equal values in the refinement output.

extra.notations:
- I4/mmm: tetragonal space group (International Tables No. 139).  
- Wyckoff-equivalence class: set of atomic positions related by the symmetry operations of a space group.  
- Bond length: the reported interatomic distance between a specified metal cation site and an oxygen anion, expressed in ångströms ($\AA$).

title: Likely presence of distinct Cu–O planar and Cu–O apical bond lengths
content: It is likely that the reported metal–oxygen distances for Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 will include at least two distinct Cu–O bond lengths: (1) shorter in-plane Cu–O distances within the CuO$_2$ planes (planar Cu–O) and (2) longer out-of-plane Cu–O distances to apical oxygens (apical Cu–O). Thus the refinement output will show anisotropic Cu coordination with distinguishable planar and apical Cu–O bond lengths (planar < apical).

extra.notations:
- Cu–O (planar): Cu–O bonds lying within the copper-oxide (CuO$_2$) plane.  
- Cu–O (apical): Cu–O bonds connecting a Cu atom to oxygen atoms located above or below the CuO$_2$ plane (out-of-plane).  
- Å: ångström, $1\ \AA = 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$.

title: LTT versus LTO: LTT necessarily favors more strongly pinned spatially modulated electronic order
content: For La2CuO4-derived cuprates (compounds derived from La2CuO4 containing CuO$_2$ planes), the low-temperature tetragonal (LTT) structural phase increases the tendency for spatially modulated electronic order (charge or spin stripe order) to be energetically stabilized and pinned in the CuO$_2$ planes, whereas the low-temperature orthorhombic (LTO) phase is associated with comparatively more dynamic, less-pinned stripe behavior. This statement uses "stabilized/pinned" to mean a greater energetic preference for static (or more slowly fluctuating) spatial modulation of charge or spin density in the CuO$_2$ planes under LTT than under LTO.

extra.notations:
- LTT: low-temperature tetragonal structural phase in La2CuO4-derived cuprates
- LTO: low-temperature orthorhombic structural phase in La2CuO4-derived cuprates
- CuO$_2$: copper–oxygen planes where the electronic order resides
- stripe order: spatially modulated charge and/or spin density in the CuO$_2$ planes

title: Suppression of superconductivity at $x=1/8$ is likely mediated by LTT-stabilized static stripe order
content: In La2CuO4-derived cuprates (e.g., La2-xBaxCuO4, La1.6-xNd0.4SrxCuO4), the pronounced suppression of the superconducting transition temperature observed near hole concentration $x=1/8$ is plausibly caused by the formation of static or strongly pinned stripe order in the CuO$_2$ planes that is promoted by the LTO→LTT structural transition. In other words, the sequence "LTO → LTT" at low temperature promotes pinned stripe order, and such pinned stripe order competes with and reduces bulk superconductivity near $x=1/8$.

extra.notations:
- $x$: hole concentration per Cu site (e.g., $x=1/8$ denotes one-eighth hole per Cu)
- LTO: low-temperature orthorhombic structural phase
- LTT: low-temperature tetragonal structural phase
- stripe order: static or slowly fluctuating spatial modulation of charge and/or spin density in the CuO$_2$ planes

title: Hg-1223 has a superconducting onset near 133 K
content: HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{8+x}$ (abbreviated Hg-1223), the mercury-based cuprate phase with three $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell ($n=3$), exhibits a superconducting transition onset at approximately $133\,$K (i.e., the temperature at which superconducting response first appears on cooling is $\approx 133\,$K).

extra.notations:
- Hg-1223: HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{8+x}$, mercury cuprate with $n=3$ $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition onset temperature (Kelvin).

title: Higher $T_{c}$ of Hg-1223 supports layer-count trend in Hg-based cuprates
content: The observation that Hg-1223 (the $n=3$ member) has $T_{c}\approx 133\,$K plausibly supports the empirical trend that, within the homologous Hg-based cuprate series HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{n-1}$Cu$_{n}$O$_{2n+2+\delta}$, the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ increases with the number of $CuO_{2}$ layers $n$ (at least for $n$ up to 3). In particular, Hg-1223 is likely to have a higher $T_{c}$ than the corresponding $n=1$ and $n=2$ members under comparable doping/oxygenation conditions.

extra.notations:
- $n$: number of $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell in the homologous series.
- Hg-1223: HgBa$_{2}$Ca$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{8+x}$ ($n=3$).
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition onset temperature (Kelvin).

title: Magnetic incommensurability equals domain-line density; inverse proportionality to real-space spacing
content:
Combining the counting rule that the number density of domain-line unit cells equals the density of excess holes and the relation ΔQ = Q_{0} n_{h} yields
$$\Delta Q = Q_{0} n_{dl},$$
where ΔQ is the magnetic incommensurability (shift in reciprocal-space units), Q_{0} is the antiferromagnetic wave number, n_{h} is the areal density of excess holes (per elementary CuO_{2} unit cell), and n_{dl} is the areal density of domain-line unit cells (per elementary CuO_{2} unit cell). If the real-space spacing between parallel domain lines is s (measured in units of elementary CuO_{2} spacing along the relevant direction), then n_{dl}=1/s and therefore
$$\Delta Q = \frac{Q_{0}}{s}.$$
Thus the magnetic incommensurability is exactly proportional to the domain-line density and inversely proportional to the real-space period (spacing) of the parallel charged domain lines.

extra.notations:
- \Delta Q: magnetic incommensurability (reciprocal-space shift from AF wavevector). 
- Q_{0}: antiferromagnetic wave number (reciprocal-space unit corresponding to AF ordering). 
- n_{h}: density of excess holes (per elementary CuO_{2} unit cell). 
- n_{dl}: density of domain-line unit cells (per elementary CuO_{2} unit cell). 
- s: real-space spacing between adjacent parallel domain lines (in units of elementary CuO_{2} spacing).

title: Orientation-dependent stability may produce doping-dependent orientation selection and small deviations in ΔQ versus doping when orientation changes
content: Let the charged domain lines exist in two principal orientations with midgap-band bandwidths W_{(1,0)}≈0.3V and W_{(1,1)}≈0.1V (V is the Cu–O hybridization scale). If the midgap band is partially occupied by excess holes (i.e., not yet completely filled), the kinetic (band) energy per added hole is lower for the larger-bandwidth orientation. Consequently, for a range of dopings where midgap bands are partially filled, the (1,0) orientation (larger bandwidth) will be energetically favored over the (1,1) orientation. If the system changes orientation as doping increases, the proportionality ΔQ = Q_{0} n_{h} (derived assuming a fixed domain-line structure) will remain true in each orientation but the observed slope or apparent linearity in experimental ΔQ versus doping plots could show kinks or orientation-dependent offsets where orientation transitions occur.

extra.notations:
- W_{(1,0)}, W_{(1,1)}: midgap-band bandwidths for domain lines oriented along (1,0) and (1,1), respectively. 
- V: hybridization energy scale entering the bandwidth estimates. 
- Other symbols as in the deduction result.

title: Continuity and preservation of the gap function across Tc
content: Let Δ(k,T) denote the magnitude of the low-energy electronic excitation gap at crystal momentum k in the CuO2 planes and temperature T. The combination of the siblings implies that Δ(k,T) is continuous at T = Tc for every k: there is no discontinuous opening or closing of the gap at Tc, and the momentum structure (d-wave form and nodal positions where Δ=0) is the same just above and just below Tc. Equivalently, the superconducting transition at Tc does not produce a new discontinuous gap function; instead the gap that vanishes only at T* (> Tc) persists through Tc.

extra.notations:
- Δ(k,T): excitation gap magnitude at momentum k and temperature T
- Tc: superconducting transition temperature
- T*: pseudogap closing (crossover) temperature, T* > Tc

title: Superconductivity arises from phase coherence of a pre-existing pairing amplitude (preformed-pair scenario)
content: Define the (complex) pair amplitude Ψ(k,T) = |Ψ(k,T)| e^{iφ(T)} where |Ψ| represents a local pairing amplitude at momentum k and φ the global phase. The conjecture is that for T between Tc and T* the system has a nonzero local pairing amplitude |Ψ(k,T)| ≠ 0 (responsible for the pseudogap Δ(k,T) > 0) but lacks long-range phase coherence (no uniform φ), and that at T = Tc long-range phase coherence (global locking of φ) is established without creating a new gap. Thus superconductivity below Tc is the onset of phase coherence of preformed pairs that already produced the pseudogap.

extra.notations:
- Ψ(k,T): complex pair amplitude at momentum k and temperature T
- |Ψ|: magnitude of pairing amplitude
- φ: superconducting phase (global coherence corresponds to a well-defined φ across the sample)
- Tc, T*: as above

title: Same k-space electronic states participate in pseudogap and superconducting coherence
content: Let A(k,ω,T) be the single-particle spectral function at momentum k, energy ω, and temperature T. The conjecture is that the k-regions where A(k,ω≈0,T) is suppressed by the pseudogap for Tc < T < T* are the same k-regions that acquire coherent, resolution-limited Bogoliubov-like peaks below Tc. In other words, the electronic states that are gapped in the pseudogap evolve into the coherent superconducting quasiparticle states once phase coherence sets in at Tc.

extra.notations:
- A(k,ω,T): single-particle spectral function
- ω: energy (measured from the chemical potential)
- coherent peaks: sharp, resolution-limited features in A indicative of long-lived quasiparticles

title: Impurity-state at -0.96 V co-locates with enhanced superconducting gap and suppressed low-energy LDOS
content: Let O_imp(r) denote the spatial image constructed by locating the broad differential-conductance peak at sample bias V = -0.96 V (the impurity-state identified as an interstitial oxygen dopant, located ≈1.9 Å from the nearest Bi atom). Let Δ(r) be the local superconducting gap magnitude and LDOS(r,E) the local density of states at energy E. Then, necessarily given the siblings, the positions marked by O_imp(r) coincide with locations where Δ(r) is locally enhanced relative to the surrounding background and where LDOS(r,E) is reduced for energies |E| < 60 meV (i.e., dopant sites inhabit minima of the low-energy LDOS modulations and fix their spatial phase).

extra.notations:
- O_imp(r): spatial map of the impurity-state peak at V = -0.96 V (identifies interstitial oxygen dopants).
- Δ(r): local superconducting energy-gap magnitude.
- LDOS(r,E): local density of electronic states at position r and energy E.
- |E| < 60 meV: low-energy range relevant to superconducting coherence peaks and QPI.

title: Dopant-added holes are not strongly localized at the impurity site; local gap enhancement arises from a mechanism other than local hole accumulation
content: Let p denote hole density per CuO2 unit and let n denote the spatial density of the impurity-state features (interstitial oxygens). Proposition 1060 gives an empirical relation for the spatially averaged gap Δ̄ ≈ [75 − 600(±15%) × n] meV and interprets each impurity as contributing ≈2 holes to the CuO2 planes (i.e., increasing global p and thereby reducing Δ̄). Proposition 1065 shows that locally Δ(r) is increased near dopant sites. To reconcile a global reduction of Δ̄ with a local increase of Δ(r) at the dopant site, it is plausible that the holes donated by each dopant are not confined to the immediate vicinity of the impurity-state (i.e., they delocalize over length scales larger than the region of local Δ(r) enhancement), and that the local increase of Δ(r) is caused by a non-charge mechanism (for example, local modifications of the pairing interaction, strain, or structural/electronic rearrangement) rather than by local hole accumulation.

extra.notations:
- n: spatial density of impurity-state features (interstitial oxygens) per unit area.
- Δ̄: spatial average of the superconducting gap magnitude.
- p: hole density per CuO2 unit (global carrier concentration).
- Δ(r): local superconducting gap magnitude.

title: Dopants simultaneously scatter quasiparticles (suppress coherence peaks) and modify local pairing, implying decoupling of quasiparticle lifetime and pairing amplitude at dopant sites
content: Define "coherence-peak amplitude" as the magnitude of LDOS features at energies ≈ ±Δ(r). The combined observations — (i) dopants correlate with local suppression of coherence-peak LDOS for |E| < 60 meV (Proposition 1078), and (ii) dopants correlate with locally increased gap magnitude Δ(r) (Proposition 1065) — suggest that at impurity sites the quasiparticle spectral weight (coherence-peak height) and the superconducting pairing amplitude (gap magnitude) are affected in opposite ways. Therefore, it is plausible that interstitial oxygen dopants act as strong scattering centers that reduce quasiparticle lifetime/coherence while simultaneously modifying the local pairing interaction (enhancing Δ) through a separate channel (e.g., local lattice distortion, modification of electronic correlations, or change of local pairing interaction).

extra.notations:
- Δ(r): local superconducting gap magnitude (pairing amplitude proxy).
- LDOS(r,E): local density of states; "coherence-peak amplitude" refers to LDOS at energies ≈ ±Δ(r).
- O_imp(r): positions of impurity-state at V = -0.96 V (interstitial oxygens).

title: Odd symmetry of Im\tilde{P} and logarithmic Re\tilde{P} at intermediate frequencies
content:
The absorptive polarizability contribution $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ is an odd function of frequency and vanishes linearly at $\omega=0$ (i.e. $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},-\omega)=-\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ and $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\propto -N(0)\,\omega/T$ for $|\omega|\ll T$). By causality (analyticity of the retarded polarizability) the real part $\mathrm{Re}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ is the Hilbert transform of $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$; therefore, for temperatures and frequencies satisfying $T\ll|\omega|\ll\omega_c$ (with $\omega_c$ a high-frequency cutoff of the phenomenology), $\mathrm{Re}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ contains a logarithmic dependence on frequency of the form
$$\mathrm{Re}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\;\sim\; -C\,N(0)\,\ln\!\frac{\omega_c}{|\omega|}\quad\text{for }T\ll|\omega|\ll\omega_c,$$
where $C$ is an order-unity positive constant set by the precise cutoff convention.

extra.notations:
- $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: contribution of charge- and spin-density excitations to the retarded polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$. 
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the Fermi level (or chemical potential). 
- $T$: absolute temperature. 
- $\omega_c$: ultraviolet (high-frequency) cutoff of the phenomenological spectral form.
- $C$: order-unity constant depending on cutoff convention.

title: Linear-in-energy electronic scattering rate (marginal Fermi liquid self-energy) from coupling to the density excitations
content:
If quasiparticles couple to the charge- and spin-density excitations whose absorptive polarizability is $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ with the universal frequency scaling given above, and if the electron–density-excitation coupling matrix element is approximately momentum-independent (or dominated by small-$q$ averages) and vertex corrections are negligible, then the electronic retarded self-energy $\Sigma(\omega,T)$ acquires an imaginary part
$$\mathrm{Im}\,\Sigma(\omega,T)\;\approx\; -\lambda\,\max\{|\omega|,\,T\},$$
for $|\omega|\ll\omega_c$, where $\lambda$ is a dimensionless coupling constant proportional to the squared coupling matrix element times $N(0)$. Consequently the single-particle scattering rate is linear in energy at $T\ll|\omega|$ and linear in temperature at $|\omega|\ll T$ (the marginal-Fermi-liquid phenomenology).

extra.notations:
- $\Sigma(\omega,T)$: retarded electronic self-energy (frequency $\omega$, temperature $T$). 
- $\mathrm{Im}\,\Sigma(\omega,T)$: electronic single-particle scattering rate (negative for loss). 
- $\lambda$: effective dimensionless electron–density-excitation coupling constant (proportional to coupling^2\times N(0)).
- $\omega_c$: ultraviolet cutoff of the bosonic spectrum.

title: Hg-1223 (n=3) has higher Tc than Hg-1212 and Hg-1201 in the Hg-Ba-Ca-Cu-O series
content: If in the Hg-Ba-Ca-Cu-O homologous series the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ increases with the number of $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell $n$, and the observed superconducting transition at approximately $133\,$K is attributed to the $n=3$ phase Hg-1223, then necessarily the $n=3$ phase (Hg-1223) has a larger $T_{c}$ than the $n=2$ (Hg-1212) and $n=1$ (Hg-1201) phases in the same series. In symbols, letting $T_{c}(n)$ denote the transition temperature of the phase with $n$ CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell, the siblings imply $T_{c}(3) > T_{c}(2)$ and $T_{c}(3) > T_{c}(1)$, with $T_{c}(3)\approx 133\,$K.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}(n)$: superconducting transition temperature of the homologous-phase having $n$ CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell. 
- Hg-1201: $HgBa_{2}CuO_{4+\delta}$ ($n=1$).
- Hg-1212: $HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+x}$ ($n=2$).
- Hg-1223: $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ ($n=3$), observed transition $\sim 133\,$K.

title: Possible further increase of Tc for Hg-based phases with n>3
content: Given the empirical trend that superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ tends to increase with the number of CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell $n$ in layered cuprates, and that Hg-1223 ($n=3$) exhibits $T_{c}\approx 133\,$K, it is plausible that synthesizing Hg-based homologous phases with $n>3$ (if structurally and chemically stable) would yield phases with $T_{c}$ exceeding $133\,$K, i.e., $T_{c}(n>3) > 133\,$K.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}(n)$: superconducting transition temperature of the homologous-phase having $n$ CuO$_2$ layers per unit cell.
- Hg-1223: $n=3$ phase with $T_{c}\approx 133\,$K.

title: Necessary existence of a local-pair, phase-disordered temperature window (pseudogap)
content: If $T^{MF}$ denotes the temperature scale at which local pairing (nonzero pair amplitude) develops in mean-field theory, and the experimentally observed superconducting transition occurs at $T_{c}<T^{MF}$ because phase-ordering is the controlling energy scale, then there necessarily exists a temperature interval $(T_{c},T^{MF})$ in which local pairs (or a nonzero pairing amplitude) are present but global phase coherence is absent. In other words, a phase-disordered but amplitude-ordered regime (commonly referred to as a pseudogap regime) must occur whenever $T^{MF}>T_{c}$ and phase fluctuations suppress the transition.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence). 
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature (temperature for local pair formation/finite pair amplitude).
- $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$: maximum phase-ordering temperature (phase stiffness scale).

title: Doping-driven crossover toward BEC-like behavior in the very underdoped limit
content: In the very underdoped limit ($\delta\ll 1$) the charge carriers are plausibly better described as tightly bound charged bosonic holes with effective mass $m^{*}$, so that the superconducting transition is set by Bose–Einstein condensation (BEC) of those bosons and scales approximately as $T_{c}\sim \delta^{2/3}$. Consequently, as hole concentration $\delta$ is reduced one expects a crossover from a BCS-like (amplitude-driven) regime at higher doping to a BEC-like (phase-ordering / condensation-driven) regime at very low doping.

extra.notations:
- $\delta$: hole doping fraction (dimensionless, $\delta\ll 1$ in the very underdoped limit).
- $m^{*}$: effective mass of the charged bosonic holes.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature set by BEC in this conjecture.

title: Enhancing superfluid stiffness (e.g., by increasing doping) should raise $T_{c}$ toward $T^{MF}$
content: If the experimentally observed $T_{c}$ in underdoped cuprates is suppressed relative to $T^{MF}$ by phase fluctuations because the phase-ordering temperature $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$ is comparable to $T_{c}$, then increasing the zero-temperature superfluid stiffness (for example by increasing carrier density/doping $\delta$ or by reducing disorder that depletes $n_{s}(0)$) will increase $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$ and therefore raise the observed $T_{c}$ toward $T^{MF}$ until the regime $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}\gg T_{c}$ is reached and mean-field behavior is restored.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature.
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature.
- $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$: maximum phase-ordering temperature; an increasing function of zero-temperature superfluid stiffness (proportional to $n_{s}(0)/m^{*}$ in standard phase-only descriptions).
- $n_{s}(0)$: zero-temperature superfluid density.
- $\delta$: hole doping fraction.

title: Suppression at x = 1/8 co‑occurs with an LTT lattice that stabilizes stripe‑like modulations
content: For the cited lanthanum‑based cuprates (examples: La$_{2-x}$Ba$_x$CuO$_4$ and La$_{1.6-x}$Nd$_{0.4}$Sr$_x$CuO$_4$), the experimentally observed anomalous suppression of superconductivity at hole concentration $x=1/8$ occurs only in the low‑temperature tetragonal (LTT) structural phase. Proposition 147 states that the LTT phase stabilizes (pins) horizontal or vertical stripe‑like charge and/or spin modulations in the CuO$_2$ planes, whereas the low‑temperature orthorhombic (LTO) phase does not provide comparable lattice pinning. Therefore, whenever the anomalous suppression at $x=1/8$ is observed in these compounds, the crystal is in a structural state (LTT) that stabilizes stripe‑like modulations in the CuO$_2$ planes. In other words, the suppression at $x=1/8$ necessarily co‑occurs with lattice conditions that pin stripe‑like electronic order.

extra.notations:
- $a$: in‑plane lattice constant (defined for context; not used explicitly in the deduction)
- $x$: hole concentration per Cu site (here $x=1/8$)
- LTT: low‑temperature tetragonal structural phase
- LTO: low‑temperature orthorhombic structural phase
- CuO$_2$: copper–oxygen planes where electronic order occurs

title: Pinned stripe order is the likely proximate cause of the 1/8 superconductivity anomaly
content: It is plausible that the anomalous suppression of superconductivity at hole concentration $x=1/8$ in the cited La‑based cuprates is caused (proximate mechanism) by the emergence of stripe‑like charge and/or spin order that becomes stabilized (pinned) by the LTT lattice distortion. Specifically, when the LTT phase appears it pins stripe modulations in the CuO$_2$ planes; pinned static or quasi‑static stripe order both reduces the superconducting order parameter amplitude in regions of the plane and frustrates inter‑region phase coherence, leading to a substantial suppression of the superconducting transition temperature $T_c$ at $x=1/8$.

extra.notations:
- $x$: hole concentration per Cu site (here $x=1/8$)
- LTT: low‑temperature tetragonal structural phase
- "Pinned stripe order": spatially modulated charge and/or spin density waves that are statically or quasi‑statically locked to the lattice
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature

title: Weak electron–phonon coupling (λ ≈ 0.2, ω_log ≈ 250 K) cannot produce Tc ≥ 26 K within Eliashberg theory
content: Within phonon-mediated Eliashberg theory, a material whose electron–phonon interaction is characterized by a dimensionless coupling constant $\\lambda\\approx 0.2$ and a logarithmically averaged boson frequency $\\omega_{\\log}\\approx 250\\,$K will have a superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ that is insufficient to reach $26\\,$K; therefore, such a set of parameters cannot account for an observed $T_{c}\\ge 26\\,$K.

extra.notations:
- $\\lambda$: dimensionless electron–phonon coupling constant
- $\\omega_{\\log}$: logarithmically averaged boson (phonon) frequency measured in kelvin
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature measured in kelvin

title: LaFeAsO's ~26 K superconductivity is unlikely to be explained solely by weak phonon-mediated pairing
content: LaFeAsO (lanthanum iron arsenide) is a superconductor with experimentally observed $T_{c}$ values of order $\\sim 26\\,$K. If LaFeAsO's electron–phonon interaction were characterized by parameters similar to $\\lambda\\approx0.2$ and $\\omega_{\\log}\\approx250\\,$K, then, by Eliashberg theory, phonon-mediated pairing would be unable to produce $T_{c}\\simeq26\\,$K. Therefore, it is likely that LaFeAsO's superconductivity is not due solely to weak conventional electron–phonon coupling and instead requires either substantially stronger electron–phonon coupling, coupling to higher-frequency bosons, or a non-phononic (e.g., electronic/magnetic) pairing mechanism.

extra.notations:
- LaFeAsO: lanthanum iron arsenide compound (superconducting)
- $\\lambda$: electron–phonon coupling constant
- $\\omega_{\\log}$: logarithmically averaged boson frequency
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature

title: Reported cation–oxygen distances arise from refinements that include anomalous scattering for the metal cations
content: The numerical interatomic bond distances between cation sites (thallium Tl, barium Ba, calcium Ca, and copper Cu) and oxygen anions reported for Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (the 2212 phase) were obtained from crystallographic structure refinements that employed neutral-atom X-ray scattering-factor curves supplemented by anomalous-scattering terms for the metal cations. Therefore the published distances are the output of that specific refinement model and thus incorporate the influence of the anomalous-scattering terms applied to Tl, Ba, Ca and Cu.

extra.notations:
- Tl, Ba, Ca, Cu: metal cations in the Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (2212) unit cell.
- "scattering-factor curves": X-ray atomic form factors (real part without anomalous corrections).
- "anomalous-scattering terms": corrections to the atomic scattering factors (dispersion corrections often denoted f' and f'').
- Distances reported in angstroms (Å).

title: Anomalous-scattering corrections likely materially affected refined cation–oxygen distances
content: It is likely (probability ~0.8) that inclusion of anomalous-scattering terms for the heavy cations (particularly Tl and Ba) meaningfully influenced the refined cation–oxygen bond lengths reported for Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8. In other words, omitting those anomalous-dispersion corrections (or using a different X-ray wavelength with different dispersion) would plausibly change the numerical bond distances and some derived structural parameters.

extra.notations:
- "meaningfully influenced": changes larger than typical statistical uncertainty in refined bond lengths for quality single-crystal X-ray data.
- "anomalous-dispersion corrections": the real and imaginary dispersion terms commonly written f' and f'' added to the atomic scattering factor.

title: Metals treated with complex (anomalous) scattering factors; anions treated with neutral factors
content:
The refinement model uses atomic scattering factors f that for metal cations (Tl, Ba, Ca, Cu) include anomalous (energy-dependent complex) corrections, commonly written as
$$f = f_0 + f' + i f''$$
while the oxygen anions are represented by neutral-atom scattering curves (i.e. using $f_0$ only, with no anomalous $f'$ or $f''$ terms). Consequently, the calculated structure factors $F(hkl)$ used in least-squares refinement contain complex-valued contributions from the metal sites and purely real contributions (within the chosen approximation) from the oxygen sites; the refinement formalism must therefore handle these complex contributions when computing intensities and gradients.

extra.notations:
- $f$: atomic scattering factor (complex when anomalous terms included)  
- $f_0$: neutral-atom (real, non-resonant) scattering factor  
- $f', f''$: real (dispersive) and imaginary (absorptive) anomalous correction terms, respectively  
- $F(hkl)$: crystallographic structure factor for Miller indices $(h,k,l)$

title: Refinement parameters are symmetry-reduced and anomalous parameters are assigned per symmetry-unique metal site
content: Because Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 is refined in the tetragonal space group I4/mmm, atomic positions and site occupancies are constrained by the space-group symmetry: only symmetry-independent (Wyckoff) positional parameters and displacement parameters need to be refined. The anomalous scattering corrections ($f',f''$) introduced for the metal cations are therefore specified and refined (or fixed) per symmetry-unique metal site (i.e. per independent Wyckoff position), not independently for every symmetry-equivalent atom in the unit cell.

extra.notations:
- I4/mmm: centrosymmetric tetragonal space group (No. 139)  
- Wyckoff position: symmetry-unique site within the space group; atoms related by symmetry share coordinates determined by the same independent parameters

title: Significant anomalous scattering from heavy Tl and Ba likely affects intensities and improves refinement when modeled
content: Given the presence of heavy metal cations (thallium and barium) in Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 and the explicit inclusion of anomalous scattering terms for metal ions, it is likely that anomalous (resonant) scattering contributions are non-negligible at typical X-ray wavelengths used for single-crystal or powder diffraction (e.g. Cu Kα, Mo Kα). Therefore including $f'$ and $f''$ for metal sites likely improves the fit to measured intensities, can produce measurable Bijvoet (Friedel) differences in this centrosymmetric I4/mmm structure, and may allow refinement (or empirical adjustment) of anomalous parameters for the symmetry-unique metal sites, leading to more accurate metal-site occupancies and displacement parameters.

extra.notations:
- Bijvoet (Friedel) differences: intensity differences between reflections $I(hkl)$ and $I(\bar h\bar k\bar l)$ arising from anomalous scattering  
- $f', f''$: anomalous dispersion corrections (see above)

title: Spectral sharpening at Tc does not entail pseudogap closure at Tc
content: Let $T_{c}$ be the superconducting transition temperature, $T^{*}$ the pseudogap recovery temperature, $\Delta(T)$ the single-particle gap magnitude, and $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ the inverse pair lifetime. Proposition 675 defines $T^{*}$ by the condition $\Delta(T^{*})\sim\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$. Proposition 1542 reports that ARPES shows (i) a pseudogap-like suppression of low-energy spectral weight above $T_{c}$ and (ii) sharpening of spectral features (narrower single-particle linewidths and emergence of quasiparticle peaks) at $T_{c}$. Combining these facts with the given definition of $T^{*}$ and the empirical statement that $T^{*}>T_{c}$ yields the necessary conclusion: the sharpening of ARPES spectral features at $T_{c}$ cannot, by itself, imply $\Delta(T_{c})=0$ or that the pseudogap has been recovered at $T_{c}$. The pseudogap closure is governed by the balance $\Delta\sim\Gamma_{0}$ and occurs only at $T^{*}>T_{c}$; therefore the spectral sharpening at $T_{c}$ must be a signature of increased phase coherence and longer-lived excitations rather than of the disappearance of the gap magnitude.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (onset of long-range phase coherence)
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap recovery (crossover) temperature defined by $\Delta(T^{*})\sim\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$
- $\Delta(T)$: single-particle gap or pseudogap magnitude at temperature $T$
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime (pair decoherence rate) at temperature $T$

title: Temperature window Tc < T < T* characterized by incoherent pairs with a finite single-particle gap
content: For underdoped cuprates, in the temperature range $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$ there exists a finite single-particle gap $\Delta(T)>0$ while pairs are short-lived so that the inverse pair lifetime satisfies $\Gamma_{0}(T)\gtrsim\Delta(T)$ (approaching equality at $T^{*}$). As a result, ARPES in this window shows a suppression of low-energy spectral weight (a pseudogap) without the sharp, coherent quasiparticle peaks that appear below $T_{c}$. Below $T_{c}$, $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ drops sufficiently (pairs live long enough) that coherent quasiparticles appear even though $\Delta$ may remain comparable in magnitude across $T_{c}$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap recovery temperature defined by $\Delta(T^{*})\sim\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$
- $\Delta(T)$: single-particle gap magnitude at temperature $T$
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime at temperature $T$

title: Physical resistivity linear in temperature when bosons dominate
content:
Let $1/\tau_{\mathrm{tr}}^{B}\propto T$ be the boson (holon) transport scattering rate in the two-dimensional diffusive limit, $n_{B}$ the boson density (number of charge carriers per unit area) which is not strongly temperature-dependent for $T>T_{BE}$, $m_{B}$ the boson effective mass, and $e$ the elementary charge. Using a Drude-like form for the boson conductivity,
$$\sigma_{B}=\frac{n_{B}e^{2}\tau_{\mathrm{tr}}^{B}}{m_{B}},$$
it follows that $\sigma_{B}\propto 1/T$. If the Ioffe–Larkin composition rule for the physical conductivity holds,
$$\sigma^{-1}=\sigma_{F}^{-1}+\sigma_{B}^{-1},$$
and the boson contribution is the limiting one ($\sigma_{B}\ll\sigma_{F}$), then the physical resistivity satisfies
$$\rho=\sigma^{-1}\approx\sigma_{B}^{-1}\propto T.$$
Thus, under these conditions the physical resistivity is necessarily linear in temperature, $\rho\propto T$.

extra.notations:
- $1/\tau_{\mathrm{tr}}^{B}$: boson (holon) transport scattering rate. 
- $n_{B}$: boson (holon) areal density.
- $m_{B}$: boson effective mass.
- $\sigma_{B}$, $\sigma_{F}$: boson and fermion subsystem conductivities.
- $\sigma$: physical electrical conductivity; $\rho=\sigma^{-1}$ physical resistivity.
- $T_{BE}$: boson Bose–Einstein condensation temperature; statement assumes $T>T_{BE}$.

title: Microscopic boson–spinon scattering and emergent gauge-field scattering describe the same T-linear channel
content: The two descriptions of the scattering channel—(A) a microscopic hopping-term induced scattering between holons and spinons represented schematically by terms like $e_{k}^{\dagger}e_{k}S_{-k+q,\sigma}^{\dagger}S_{-k'+q,\sigma}$, and (B) scattering of holons by transverse fluctuations of an emergent gauge field with transverse polarization $\Pi^{T}$—are plausibly two representations of the same physical process in the slave-particle formulation. Concretely, in a path-integral or slave-particle gauge theory one can (under standard approximations) perform a Hubbard–Stratonovich/gauge transformation of the hopping term and/or integrate out high-energy spinon degrees of freedom; this generates effective gauge-field dynamics and a holon–gauge coupling whose dominant transverse fluctuations produce the same $1/\tau_{\mathrm{tr}}^{B}\propto T$ scaling for holon transport. Therefore the hopping-term picture (prop. 469) and the gauge-fluctuation calculation (prop. 511) likely capture the same T-linear scattering channel rather than two independent mechanisms.

extra.notations:
- $e_{k}^{\dagger},e_{k}$: holon creation/annihilation operators in momentum space.
- $S_{p,\sigma}^{\dagger},S_{p,\sigma}$: spinon creation/annihilation operators.
- $a_{\mu}$: emergent gauge field mediating holon–spinon coupling.
- $\Pi^{T}$: transverse polarization (gauge propagator denominator) generated by fermionic (spinon) loops.
- $1/\tau_{\mathrm{tr}}^{B}$: holon transport scattering rate.

title: Zn in BSCCO produces a localized, subgap, fourfold-symmetric impurity bound state
content: Combining the reported resonance energy $\Omega=-1.5\pm0.5\,$meV with the superconducting gap $\Delta_{0}=45\,$meV and the STM spatial imaging shows that zinc (Zn) substitutional impurities on Cu sites in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ produce a sharply localized quasiparticle bound state whose energy lies well inside the superconducting gap ($|\Omega|\ll\Delta_{0}$) and whose local density of states (LDOS) is four-fold symmetric and aligned with the crystal $a$- and $b$-axes. Here $\Omega$ is the impurity resonance energy and $\Delta_{0}$ is the bulk superconducting gap.

extra.notations:
- $\Omega$: impurity-induced quasiparticle resonance energy (measured from the Fermi level). 
- $\Delta_{0}$: superconducting energy gap (magnitude).
- LDOS: local density of states measured by STM/STS.

title: Zn acts as a near-unitary (strong) nonmagnetic potential scatterer that predominantly scatters nodal quasiparticles
content: The Zn impurities likely act as near-unitary (strong) potential scatterers (scattering phase shift $\delta_{0}\approx0.48\pi\lesssim\pi/2$), are effectively nonmagnetic on Cu sites, and therefore strongly scatter low-energy quasiparticles originating from the $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ gap nodes. Consequently, the impurity-induced resonance will have pronounced LDOS tails extending along the nodal directions and Zn substitution should produce an outsized contribution to low-energy scattering and to suppression of superconducting coherence (e.g., $T_{c}$) per impurity concentration.

extra.notations:
- $\delta_{0}$: scattering phase shift of the impurity potential; unitary limit corresponds to $\delta_{0}\to\pi/2$.
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: superconducting order-parameter symmetry with gap nodes (zeroes) along the Brillouin-zone diagonals.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature.

title: Mutual exclusivity of thermodynamic favorability for more hydrogen-rich stoichiometries at the same conditions
content: If, under a specified static pressure–temperature condition, the phase YH9 (yttrium hydride with H:Y = 9) in space group P6_3/mmc is the hydrogen-richest thermodynamically favored yttrium hydride (i.e., no yttrium hydride with greater H:Y ratio lies on the convex hull of formation enthalpy at those same conditions), then YH10 (yttrium hydride with H:Y = 10) cannot be thermodynamically favored at those identical pressure–temperature conditions.

extra.notations:
- YH9: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio 9.
- YH10: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio 10.
- "thermodynamically favored at given conditions": lies on (or is the hydrogen-richest composition on) the convex hull of formation enthalpy at the specified pressure and temperature.

title: YH10 (fcc, high-Tc) is likely metastable or favored at different (probably higher) pressures than the YH9 stability field
content: Given that YH9 in P6_3/mmc is reported as the hydrogen-richest thermodynamically favored yttrium hydride at a certain high-pressure window, while theoretical calculations predict an fcc YH10 with a high superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$, it is plausible that the fcc YH10 phase is not thermodynamically favored in the same pressure–temperature window where YH9 is the hydrogen-richest favored phase. Instead, YH10 is likely (a) metastable at those conditions (kinetically accessible but not on the convex hull), or (b) stabilized (thermodynamically favored) only at different conditions, plausibly at higher static pressures or different temperatures.

extra.notations:
- YH9: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio 9.
- YH10: yttrium hydride with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio 10.
- fcc: face-centered cubic crystal structure.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature.
- "metastable": a phase that is not on the convex hull (not thermodynamically favored) but can exist due to kinetic barriers.

title: Pairing amplitude in the self-energy must carry d-wave momentum dependence in underdoped Bi2212 pseudogap
content:
If (a) the normal-state pseudogap in underdoped Bi2212 below a crossover temperature $T^{*}>T_{c}$ exhibits the same d-wave momentum dependence as the superconducting gap (proposition 1265), and (b) the ARPES pseudogap is represented within the three-parameter self-energy model 
$$\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)=-\,i\Gamma_{1}(\mathbf{k},T)+\dfrac{\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)^{2}}{\omega+\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}+i\Gamma_{0}(\mathbf{k},T)}\,, $$
(proposition 1535), then the pairing amplitude entering that self-energy, $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$, must be nonzero for $T<T^{*}$ (i.e. in the pseudogap regime) and must carry the same d-wave $\mathbf{k}$-dependence (to lowest symmetry order) as the superconducting gap. Concretely one may write $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)=\Delta_{0}(T)\,\phi(\mathbf{k})$ with $\phi(\mathbf{k})\propto(\cos k_{x}-\cos k_{y})$ (or its absolute-value for spectral-weight comparisons) for underdoped Bi2212 in the pseudogap regime.

extra.notations:
- $k_x,k_y$: in-plane momentum components in the CuO$_2$ Brillouin zone. 
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap crossover temperature; $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature.
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: electronic self-energy used in ARPES spectral function.
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$: pairing amplitude (superconducting gap or pseudogap amplitude).
- $\Gamma_{0}(\mathbf{k},T)$: inverse pair lifetime (pair-pole broadening).
- $\Gamma_{1}(\mathbf{k},T)$: single-particle scattering rate.
- $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$: band dispersion measured from the chemical potential.

title: “Filling” (increasing $\Gamma_{0}$) explains loss of spectral coherence across $T_{c}$ while gap anisotropy persists
content: For underdoped Bi2212 in the temperature range $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$, the ARPES pseudogap is likely to show a "filling" behavior: the momentum-dependent gap magnitude inferred from peak positions (set by $\Delta_{0}(T)\,\phi(\mathbf{k})$ with $\phi(\mathbf{k})\propto\cos k_{x}-\cos k_{y}$) remains approximately present, while low-energy spectral weight increases and quasiparticle peaks lose coherence because the inverse pair-lifetime $\Gamma_{0}(\mathbf{k},T)$ grows with increasing $T$. Experimentally this appears as reduced peak amplitude, increased FWHM, and a partial recovery of weight near the chemical potential without a strong shift of the peak energy that defines the gap.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta_{0}(T)$: overall pairing amplitude scale (temperature dependent).
- $\phi(\mathbf{k})$: normalized d-wave form factor, $\phi(\mathbf{k})\propto(\cos k_{x}-\cos k_{y})$.
- $\Gamma_{0}(\mathbf{k},T)$: inverse pair lifetime; larger values "fill in" low-energy suppression.
- FWHM: full-width at half-maximum of ARPES spectral peaks (measures coherence/broadening).

title: Magnetic ordering is not required to produce the observed parity swap
content: Let MB denote the main CuO2-derived band and SB the shadow (folded) band observed in ARPES. The experimental observation (Proposition 1029) is that along the $\Gamma$–$Y$ nodal azimuth MB and SB have opposite reflection parity (MB odd, SB even), and along $\Gamma$–$X$ they share parity. The DFT one-step photoemission calculations (Proposition 1040) reproduce the same polarization dependence using the averaged distorted (orthorhombic) crystal structure. Therefore the observed parity swap along $\Gamma$–$Y$ can be produced by a non-magnetic, structural origin encoded in the one-electron photoemission matrix element computed for the distorted orthorhombic lattice. Concretely: the parity swap does not require invoking magnetic (e.g., antiferromagnetic) order or time-reversal-breaking electronic order as a necessary cause.

extra.notations:
- MB: Main (primary) CuO2-derived band / main Fermi-surface feature.
- SB: Shadow (folded/replica) band / shadow Fermi-surface feature.
- $\Gamma$–$Y$: High-symmetry nodal azimuth in the Brillouin zone where the reflection plane considered contains the $\Gamma$–$Y$ direction.
- Reflection parity: Eigenvalue ($\pm1$) under reflection in the mirror plane containing the indicated azimuth.
- DFT one-step photoemission: First-principles simulation of photoemission intensity including initial-state, matrix-element and final-state effects on a specified crystal structure.

title: Shadow band arises from orthorhombic distortion–induced backfolding with parity inversion
content: It is plausible that the SB observed in Bi2212 is the structural backfold (replica) of the MB produced by the orthorhombic distortion of the Bi2212 lattice. Denote the undistorted (Bloch) initial-state wavefunction by $\phi(x,y)$ and the distortion along $x$ by a function $D(x)$; the distorted-system wavefunction can be written schematically as $\phi_D(x,y)=\phi(x,y)+\alpha\,D(x)\,\phi(x,y)+\dots$ where $\alpha$ is a coupling amplitude. The distortion couples Bloch components at $k$ and $k+Q$ (folding vector) and can produce a folded component (the SB) whose transformation under reflection in the $\Gamma$–$Y$ plane has the opposite eigenvalue to the original MB component, yielding the observed parity swap. This structural backfolding mechanism is consistent with the DFT one-step photoemission result that the parity alteration is intrinsic to the matrix element derived from the distorted orthorhombic structure and with the ARPES polarization data.

extra.notations:
- $\phi(x,y)$: Undistorted (initial) electronic wavefunction in the CuO$_2$ plane coordinates $(x,y)$.
- $D(x)$: Spatial distortion profile along $x$ producing the orthorhombic distortion (real-space modulation that breaks tetragonal symmetry).
- $\phi_D(x,y)$: Total (distorted) initial-state wavefunction including distortion-induced admixtures.
- $k$, $Q$: Crystal momentum and folding (reciprocal) vector introduced by the structural distortion.

title: Phase fluctuations cannot be the limiting mechanism when $T_{\theta}^{max}\gg T_{c}$
content: Let $T_{c}$ be the observed superconducting transition temperature and $T_{\theta}^{max}$ the maximum possible phase-ordering temperature computed ignoring non-phase disordering effects. If $T_{\theta}^{max}/T_{c}\gg 1$, then phase fluctuations (which can at best suppress ordering only down to $T_{\theta}^{max}$) cannot be the dominant mechanism that reduces $T_{c}$; consequently, whatever suppresses $T_{c}$ below other theoretical scales must be due to non-phase degrees of freedom or pairing-amplitude limitations (i.e. constraints on $T^{MF}$), not phase disordering.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence). 
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: maximal phase-ordering temperature computed when ignoring disordering effects of non-phase degrees of freedom.
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature (upper bound on local-pair formation scale).

title: Raising superfluid density in underdoped cuprates will increase $T_{c}$ up to the pairing scale
content: In underdoped cuprates (low carrier concentration $\delta$) the observed $T_{c}$ is often set by phase-ordering rather than pairing, i.e. $T_{c}\lesssim T_{\theta}^{max}<T^{MF}$. Increasing $\delta$ (or otherwise increasing the zero-temperature superfluid density $n_{s}(0)$) will typically increase $T_{\theta}^{max}$; therefore, unless the mean-field pairing scale $T^{MF}$ decreases faster with doping, increasing superfluid density will raise $T_{c}$ until $T_{c}$ becomes limited by $T^{MF}$ (the pairing amplitude scale) rather than by phase fluctuations.

extra.notations:
- $\delta$: carrier (hole) concentration (underdoped when small).
- $n_{s}(0)$: zero-temperature superfluid density.
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: maximal phase-ordering temperature.
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature.

title: Strong quasi-two-dimensionality enhances the likelihood of phase-fluctuation-limited $T_{c}$
content: For layered (quasi-two-dimensional) superconductors, increasing anisotropy — characterized by larger interlayer spacing $d$ or shorter perpendicular coherence length $\xi_{\perp}$ — reduces interlayer phase stiffness and thus lowers the phase-ordering temperature $T_{\theta}^{max}$. Therefore systems with strong quasi-2D anisotropy are more likely to have $T_{\theta}^{max}$ comparable to the observed $T_{c}$, making phase fluctuations the dominant mechanism that suppresses $T_{c}$ below $T^{MF}$.

extra.notations:
- $d$: average spacing between superconducting layers.
- $\xi_{\perp}$: superconducting coherence length perpendicular to the layers.
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: maximal phase-ordering temperature (depends on phase stiffness which is reduced by anisotropy).
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature.

title: Existence of a fluorine-doped LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs superconductor with T_c ≈ 26 K
content: There exists at least one fluorine substitution level $x$ for which the compound LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs is superconducting with superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ reported at or near $26\ \mathrm{K}$ (experimentally stated as $T_{c}\ge 26\ \mathrm{K}$ and as $T_{c}\approx 26\ \mathrm{K}$ in independent reports).

extra.notations:
- LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs: lanthanum iron oxyarsenide with fraction $x$ of oxygen sites replaced by fluorine. 
- $x$: fluorine doping fraction (dimensionless).
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature measured in kelvin.

title: Fluorine substitution likely electron-dopes FeAs layers and induces superconductivity
content: It is likely that substituting fluorine for oxygen in LaO_{1-x}F_{x}FeAs supplies additional electrons (electron doping) to the FeAs conducting layers (the Fe^{2+} square-lattice sheets), increasing carrier concentration in those layers and thereby inducing superconductivity with $T_{c}\approx 26\ \mathrm{K}$ for the reported doping range.

extra.notations:
- Electron doping: increase in conduction electron density caused by substitutional impurities that donate electrons.
- FeAs layers / Fe^{2+} square-lattice sheets: the approximately planar iron-arsenic layers that host the conducting electrons.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature in kelvin.

title: Tl–O sheets are chemically connected along the c-axis (not purely van der Waals stacked)
content: Given that thallium–oxygen (Tl–O) sheets occur as distinct stacked atomic planes lying parallel to the (001) plane and stacked along the crystallographic $c$-axis, and that adjacent Tl–O sheets are joined by intersheet Tl–O chemical bonds with a Tl–O separation on the order of $2.0\ \text{\AA}$, it follows necessarily that the Tl–O layers are chemically bonded to one another along the $c$-axis (i.e., the stacking includes real Tl–O chemical contacts rather than only weak van der Waals gaps). Here $c$-axis denotes the crystallographic axis perpendicular to the (001) plane and Å = angstrom ($10^{-10}\,$m).

extra.notations:
- Å: angstrom, $1\,\text{\AA}=10^{-10}\,$m  
- Tl–O intersheet bond: chemical contact between a thallium atom in one Tl–O sheet and an oxygen atom in an adjacent Tl–O sheet  
- $c$-axis: crystallographic axis perpendicular to the (001) plane

title: Short Tl–O intersheet bond length suggests appreciable interlayer electronic coupling
content: The presence of Tl–O intersheet bonds with a Tl–O separation on the order of $2.0\ \text{\AA}$ between adjacent Tl–O sheets stacked along the crystallographic $c$-axis plausibly implies significant orbital overlap between Tl and O across layers; therefore, Tl–O derived electronic states are likely to have non-negligible hopping matrix elements between neighboring Tl–O sheets. As a consequence, one expects measurable band dispersion along the out-of-plane direction (finite $k_z$ dispersion) for electronic bands with Tl–O character and a nonzero contribution from Tl–O states to c-axis electrical transport. Symbols: Å = angstrom ($10^{-10}\,$m); $k_z$ = crystal momentum along the $c$-axis.

extra.notations:
- Å: angstrom, $1\,\text{\AA}=10^{-10}\,$m  
- $k_z$: crystal momentum component along the $c$-axis  
- Tl–O derived states: electronic states with dominant thallium and oxygen orbital character

title: Nodes (zero-gap directions) persist in the pseudogap regime
content: Let $T_{c}$ be the superconducting transition temperature and $T^{*}$ the pseudogap crossover temperature with $T_{c}<T^{*}$. Let $k=(k_{x},k_{y})$ be a momentum in the 2D Brillouin zone of the CuO2 planes. If the pseudogap for $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$ has the same d-wave momentum dependence as the superconducting gap, i.e. the gap magnitude is proportional to $|\cos k_{x}-\cos k_{y}|$, then there exist momentum directions for which the pseudogap magnitude vanishes (nodes). Concretely, along the zone-diagonal directions where $\cos k_{x}=\cos k_{y}$ (the nodal lines $k_{x}=\pm k_{y}$), the pseudogap is zero for all $T$ with $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$.

extra.notations:
- $k_{x},k_{y}$: in-plane crystal momentum components. 
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature. 
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap crossover temperature (pseudogap appears for $T<T^{*}$). 
- d-wave dependence: gap $\propto |\cos k_{x}-\cos k_{y}|$.

title: Pseudogap likely reflects a pairing amplitude (preformed pairs) with suppressed phase coherence
content: The pseudogap observed for $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$ in underdoped Bi$_{2}$Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8-\delta}$ shares the superconducting d-wave momentum dependence ($\propto |\cos k_{x}-\cos k_{y}|$). Given also that $T^{*}$ grows as $T_{c}$ falls with underdoping (e.g. $T^{*}=170\,$K at $T_{c}=83\,$K, and $T^{*}>301\,$K at $T_{c}=10\,$K), it is plausible that the pseudogap reflects an electronic pairing amplitude (a gap in single-particle spectra produced by pair formation) that persists to temperatures well above the bulk superconducting transition, with superconductivity lost at $T_{c}$ because global phase coherence is suppressed rather than because the pairing amplitude vanishes.

extra.notations:
- "Pairing amplitude / preformed pairs": a nonzero expectation value of a local (short-range) pair amplitude that opens a single-particle gap, but without long-range phase coherence that produces zero-resistance superconductivity.
- $T_{c}$: temperature for onset of phase-coherent superconductivity. 
- $T^{*}$: temperature where the pseudogap (pairing-like spectral gap) closes.

title: Superconducting transition corresponds to onset of phase coherence, not gap formation
content: Let $T^{*}$ be the pseudogap crossover temperature and $T_{c}$ the superconducting transition temperature with $T^{*}>T_{c}$. The combination of the two propositions implies that a momentum-dependent energy gap with d-wave symmetry (i.e. with the same $|\cos k_{x}-\cos k_{y}|$ form) is already present for $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$ (the pseudogap state), while at $T_{c}$ the low-energy ARPES lineshape sharpens into resolution-limited quasiparticle peaks indicating the appearance of global phase coherence. Therefore the formation (opening) of the momentum-dependent gap and the establishment of long-range phase coherence are distinct events: the gap exists without coherence above $T_{c}$, and $T_{c}$ marks primarily the onset of phase coherence rather than the initial opening of the gap.

extra.notations:
- $k_{x},k_{y}$: momentum components in the CuO2 plane Brillouin zone.
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap crossover temperature (normal state).
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (onset of global phase coherence).
- ARPES: angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy.

title: Pseudogap likely reflects a pairing amplitude (preformed pairs) without phase coherence
content: A plausible interpretation of the two siblings is that, in underdoped Bi2Sr2CaCu2−δ, the pseudogap regime ($T_{c}<T<T^{*}$) hosts a nonzero pairing amplitude with d-wave momentum structure (i.e. a finite magnitude of a pairing gap that follows $|\cos k_{x}-\cos k_{y}|$) but lacks global phase coherence; at $T_{c}$ the system achieves long-range phase coherence and the incoherent gapped excitations become coherent Bogoliubov-like quasiparticles observed as sharp ARPES peaks. Here "pairing amplitude" denotes a local or short-range superconducting pair amplitude whose phase is not globally ordered until $T_{c}$.

extra.notations:
- "Pairing amplitude": a local (possibly fluctuating) superconducting order-parameter magnitude.
- $T^{*}$, $T_{c}$, $k_{x},k_{y}$ as above.

title: Phase-stiffness (superfluid-density) control of Tc in the underdoped regime
content: If phase fluctuations dominate so that the maximum phase-ordering temperature $T_{\theta}^{max}$ is comparable to the observed superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$, then the observed $T_{c}$ cannot exceed the phase-ordering scale and is therefore controlled by the phase stiffness (also called phase-ordering energy) rather than by the mean-field pairing scale. Denoting the zero-temperature superfluid density by $n_{s}(0)$ and the effective mass by $m^{*}$, the phase-stiffness energy satisfies $V_{0}\propto \hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)/m^{*}$ (standard relation for long-wavelength phase stiffness). Since $T_{\theta}^{max}\propto V_{0}$ and $T_{c}\leq T_{\theta}^{max}$, it follows necessarily that $T_{c}$ is bounded above by, and in the fluctuation-dominated regime is set by, a temperature proportional to $\hbar^{2}n_{s}(0)/m^{*}$. Consequently, $T_{c}$ must correlate with measures of superfluid density (for example $n_{s}(0)$ or $\lambda^{-2}(0)$) in underdoped cuprates.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: experimentally observed superconducting transition temperature (onset of global phase coherence).
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: maximum phase-ordering temperature estimated from phase-stiffness energy, ignoring non-phase disordering effects.
- $V_{0}$: phase stiffness (phase-ordering energy scale) at $T=0$.
- $n_{s}(0)$: superfluid density at $T=0$.
- $m^{*}$: effective mass of the superfluid carriers.
- $\lambda(0)$: magnetic penetration depth at $T=0$; note $\lambda^{-2}(0)\propto n_{s}(0)/m^{*}$.

title: Existence of a pseudogap window of local pairing without global coherence
content: In underdoped cuprates (low hole concentration $\delta\ll 1$ and low superfluid density), there should exist a temperature interval $T_{c}<T<T^{MF}$ in which a substantial local pairing amplitude (a pairing gap in single-particle spectra) persists despite the absence of global phase coherence. Here $T^{MF}$ denotes the mean-field pairing temperature (scale for local pair formation) and $T_{c}$ denotes the temperature for global phase coherence. Experimentally this would appear as a spectroscopic gap (pseudogap) and other local-pair signatures above $T_{c}$ but below $T^{MF}$, while transport and magnetic response remain non-superconducting until $T_{c}$.

extra.notations:
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature (temperature scale for local pair formation).
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence).
- $\delta$: hole-doping (carrier concentration); low $\delta$ denotes underdoped regime.

title: Numerical value of the tetragonal unit-cell volume
content:
For a tetragonal unit cell the volume is V = a^2 c, where a and c are the lattice parameters. Using the reported values a = 3.8550(6) Å and c = 29.318(4) Å yields
$$V = a^{2}c \approx (3.8550\ \text{\AA})^{2}\times 29.318\ \text{\AA} \approx 435.75\ \text{\AA}^{3}.$$
Propagating the reported standard uncertainties (assuming independent, small relative errors) gives an approximate uncertainty of ±0.20 Å^3, i.e. V = 435.75(20) Å^3.

extra.notations:
- a: lattice constant along the a-axis (Å). 
- c: lattice constant along the c-axis (Å).
- V: conventional tetragonal unit-cell volume (Å^3).

title: Layered, quasi-two-dimensional electronic character centered on CuO2 planes
content: The structure contains double Cu–O sheets (CuO2 planes) separated by Tl–O and Ba layers, and the Cu(1) atoms are described as square-planar coordinated by four in-plane O(1) atoms with only a weak apical interaction to an O(2) atom (square-pyramidal tendency). These features imply that the electronic states relevant to conduction and superconductivity are strongly confined to the CuO2 planes (quasi-two-dimensional electronic structure), with reduced interlayer orbital overlap via the apical oxygen.

extra.notations:
- CuO2 plane: the square-planar network of Cu(1) atoms coordinated by in-plane O(1) oxygens.
- O(2): apical oxygen atom weakly bonded to Cu(1).

title: Likely number of formula units per conventional unit cell (Z ≈ 2)
content: Given the reported space group I4/mmm (body-centered tetragonal) and the nominal stoichiometry Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (per formula unit), it is likely that the conventional I-centered unit cell contains two formula units (Z = 2), i.e. two copies of Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 per conventional cell.

extra.notations:
- Z: number of chemical formula units per conventional unit cell.
- I4/mmm: body-centered tetragonal space group (space-group no. 139).

title: Measured incommensurability equals hole concentration for the $x=0.12$ sample
content: For the sample La1.48Nd0.4Sr0.12CuO4 (hole concentration per Cu site $x=0.12$) the reported incommensurability parameter is $\varepsilon=0.12$, hence $\varepsilon = x$ for this specimen (where $\varepsilon$ is the displacement of magnetic peaks from $Q_{AF}=(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2})$ in reciprocal-lattice units $2\pi/a$ and $x$ is the Sr concentration). This equality is a factual numeric relation for that sample.

extra.notations:
- $\varepsilon$: incommensurability parameter (displacement of magnetic peaks from $Q_{AF}$ in units of $2\pi/a$).
- $x$: hole concentration per Cu site (Sr content) in La-based cuprates.
- $a$: in-plane lattice constant.

title: Incommensurability tracks hole density across static and dynamic cases
content: Because dynamical incommensurate correlations in superconducting La2-xSrxCuO4 follow $\varepsilon\approx x$ (Proposition 154), and the static incommensurate order observed in La1.48Nd0.4Sr0.12CuO4 has $\varepsilon=0.12$ at $x=0.12$ (Proposition 140), it is plausible that the same primary control — the hole concentration $x$ — sets the incommensurability $\varepsilon$ regardless of whether the magnetism is dynamical or statically pinned (and despite Nd-doping or structural differences). Thus the incommensurability appears to be governed predominantly by carrier density rather than the precise structural phase or static vs dynamic character of the order.

extra.notations:
- $\varepsilon$: incommensurability parameter (displacement of magnetic peaks from $Q_{AF}$ in units of $2\pi/a$).
- $x$: hole concentration per Cu site.
- $Q_{AF}$: commensurate antiferromagnetic wave vector $(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2})$.

title: Implied real-space stripe period and coherence for the $x=0.12$ sample
content: If magnetic incommensurability $\varepsilon$ arises from a unidirectional stripe modulation, then the magnetic peak displacement $\varepsilon=0.12$ implies a stripe-related real-space modulation period of order $1/(2\varepsilon)\approx 4.17$ in-plane lattice spacings (where $a$ is the in-plane lattice constant). Combining this with the reported in-plane magnetic correlation length $>\!150\,$Å (Proposition 140) implies that many such stripe periods are coherent (i.e., the static modulation extends over dozens of lattice spacings), consistent with reasonably long-range stripe-like order in La1.48Nd0.4Sr0.12CuO4.

extra.notations:
- $\varepsilon$: incommensurability parameter (reciprocal units $2\pi/a$).
- $a$: in-plane lattice constant.
- stripe period: real-space spacing between equivalent features of the modulation, estimated here as $1/(2\varepsilon)$ lattice units for the usual stripe interpretation.

title: Both theoretical scales bound the observed transition: Tc ≤ min(T_theta^{max}, T^{MF})
content:
If $T_{c}$ denotes the experimentally observed superconducting transition temperature, $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$ denotes the theoretical maximum phase-ordering temperature, and $T^{MF}$ denotes the mean-field pairing temperature, then
$$T_{c}\leq \min\big(T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}},\,T^{MF}\big).$$
That is, the observed $T_{c}$ cannot exceed either theoretical scale; it is bounded above by the smaller of the two.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence). 
- $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$: maximum phase-ordering temperature (theoretical scale set by phase stiffness). 
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing (local-pair formation) temperature.

title: In underdoped, low-superfluid-density cuprates, Tc is set by the phase-ordering scale and scales with superfluid stiffness
content:
For cuprate superconductors in the underdoped (low carrier concentration $\delta$) regime with low zero-temperature superfluid density $n_{s}(0)$, the experimentally observed transition temperature $T_{c}$ is primarily determined by phase-ordering physics and is therefore approximately equal to the phase-ordering scale:
$$T_{c}\approx T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}<T^{MF}.$$
Moreover, because the phase-stiffness energy scale $V_{0}$ (and hence $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$) depends parametrically on the superfluid density divided by effective mass, one expects, in this regime, an approximate proportionality
$$T_{c}\propto V_{0}\propto \frac{n_{s}(0)}{m^{*}},$$
i.e., increasing $n_{s}(0)$ (for example by increased doping) raises $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$ and thus raises $T_{c}$ toward $T^{MF}$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence). 
- $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$: theoretical maximum phase-ordering temperature (set by phase stiffness). 
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature (local-pair formation scale).
- $n_{s}(0)$: zero-temperature superfluid density.
- $m^{*}$: effective electron mass.
- $V_{0}$: phase stiffness energy scale (proportional to $n_{s}(0)/m^{*}$ up to system-dependent prefactors).

title: Unit-cell volume determined from reported lattice parameters
content:
The conventional tetragonal unit-cell volume $V$ is given by $V=a^{2}c$, where $a$ and $c$ are the lattice parameters. Using $a=3.8550(6)\,\text{\AA}$ and $c=29.318(4)\,\text{\AA}$ (reported for the $I4/mmm$ cell), the volume is
$$V = (3.8550\,\text{\AA})^{2}\times 29.318\,\text{\AA}\approx 435.70\,\text{\AA}^{3}.$$
Propagating the quoted uncertainties ($\delta a=0.0006\,\text{\AA}$, $\delta c=0.004\,\text{\AA}$) gives $\delta V \approx 0.20\,\text{\AA}^{3}$, so $V=435.70(20)\,\text{\AA}^{3}$.

extra.notations:
- $a$: lattice parameter along the tetragonal $a$ axis (in \AA).
- $c$: lattice parameter along the tetragonal $c$ axis (in \AA).
- $V$: conventional unit-cell volume (in \AA$^{3}$).
- Parentheses after a number denote the uncertainty in the last digits (standard crystallographic notation).

title: Cu coordination implies predominantly Cu(II) (3d^9) square-planar character
content: The reported local geometry that each Cu(1) is coordinated primarily by four in-plane O(1) atoms in a square-planar arrangement, with only a weak apical interaction to an O(2) atom (square-pyramidal distortion), is characteristic of Cu in a nominal $+\!2$ oxidation state (electronic configuration $3d^{9}$) found in cuprate superconductors. Therefore it is plausible that the copper sites in Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 are predominantly Cu(II) with strong planar crystal-field splitting and a Jahn–Teller–type tendency favoring the planar coordination; the weak apical bond indicates reduced out-of-plane hybridization compared with a regular octahedron.

extra.notations:
- Cu(1): copper atom at the specified crystallographic site.
- O(1), O(2): oxygen atoms at the in-plane and apical positions, respectively.
- Cu(II): copper in formal oxidation state +2, nominal $3d^{9}$ electronic configuration.

title: Localized 20 meV component does not follow the Bose population factor
content: The localized spectral component centered near $\hbar\omega\approx 20\ \mathrm{meV}$ (a nuclear/non-magnetic contribution) exhibits an intensity that "decreases slightly with increasing temperature", while the broad damped-harmonic-oscillator (DHO) component is explicitly stated to "follow the Bose population factor" $(1-\exp(-\hbar\omega/kT))^{-1}$. Therefore the localized $\approx 20\ \mathrm{meV}$ component does not follow the Bose population temperature dependence and so cannot be described solely as scattering from a thermally populated harmonic mode whose intensity scales as the Bose factor.

extra.notations:
- $\hbar\omega$: energy transfer (in meV). 
- $k$: Boltzmann constant. 
- $T$: absolute temperature. 
- "Bose population factor": $(1-\exp(-\hbar\omega/kT))^{-1}$.

title: Energy overlap forces a two-component fit; single-DHO fits are insufficient
content: The two nuclear components are centered at nearby energies ($\approx 20\,$meV and $\hbar\omega=21.5\,$meV) while the DHO has a large linewidth $\Gamma_{\omega}=26\,$meV that produces a broad response across the measured range. Hence the two components necessarily overlap in energy; fitting the nuclear contribution near 20–22 meV requires both a localized feature and the broad DHO term. A model containing only a single DHO (or only the localized peak) cannot, in general, reproduce both the observed slight decrease-with-$T$ of the localized feature and the Bose-factor temperature dependence of the broad component simultaneously.

extra.notations:
- $\hbar\omega$: center energy of the DHO (21.5 meV). 
- $\Gamma_{\omega}$: DHO linewidth (26 meV). 
- "Localized feature": peak near 20 meV with weakly decreasing intensity vs $T$.

title: Broad DHO likely arises from phononic (nuclear) excitations or multiphonon continuum
content: Because the broad nuclear response is described as following the Bose population factor and is parameterized by a damped harmonic oscillator with center $\hbar\omega=21.5\,$meV and large linewidth $\Gamma_{\omega}=26\,$meV, this component plausibly represents heavily damped phonon excitations or a multiphonon (continuum) contribution to the nuclear inelastic scattering rather than a sharp localized vibration.

extra.notations:
- "DHO": damped harmonic oscillator parameterization of inelastic scattering intensity. 
- "Phononic": arising from lattice vibrations (nuclear scattering), whose populations follow the Bose factor.

title: Apparent disagreement can be reconciled by different operational definitions, k-resolution and dominant momentum weighting
content: Both propositions can be simultaneously true if they refer to different operational definitions of "gap" and to ARPES measurements with different momentum resolution or momentum weighting. Let $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$ be the intrinsic single-particle excitation gap at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and temperature $T$, and let $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ denote an inverse pair-lifetime (or pair-breaking broadening) that primarily affects antinodal spectra. If 1282's statement uses a k-averaged or antinodal-dominated measure of the gap (or uses peak position averaged over a broad k-window) then the antinodal $\Delta(\mathbf{k}\approx(\pi,0),T)$ that is approximately $T$-independent but "fills in" (increasing spectral weight at low energy due to growing $\Gamma_{0}(T)$) will appear as a gap that is smooth across $T_{c}$ and only disappears near $T^{*}$. Simultaneously, 676's more momentum-resolved observation that $\Delta(\mathbf{k})$ near the node decreases with $T$ and goes to zero at $T_{c}$ can be true because the nodal region is differently affected by pair-lifetime broadening and has a distinct $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$ that closes at $T_{c}$. Thus the two propositions describe different facets of a single k-dependent phenomenology: antinodal behavior dominated by gap "filling" (increase in $\Gamma_{0}$) and nodal behavior showing genuine gap-closing (decrease of $\Delta$), and different experimental analysis/weighting can emphasize one behavior over the other.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$: momentum- and temperature-dependent single-particle excitation gap magnitude. 
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime or pair-breaking broadening parameter that fills spectral weight without reducing $\Delta$.
- $\mathbf{k}$: electron crystal momentum; "node" denotes momenta along the $d$-wave node (gap zero in ideal $d$-wave), "antinode" denotes momenta near $(\pi,0)$.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature; $T^{*}$: pseudogap closing temperature.

title: Existence of nodal gapless excitations in the normal state if the pseudogap follows a d-wave form
content: If the normal-state pseudogap has the same d-wave momentum dependence as the superconducting gap, i.e. its magnitude is proportional to $|\cos k_{x}-\cos k_{y}|$, then the gap value is zero on the d-wave node lines ($k_{x}=k_{y}$ modulo reciprocal-lattice vectors). Therefore, for temperatures $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$ where the pseudogap is present with that momentum dependence, single-particle excitations at momenta on the nodal lines are gapless (their excitation energy vanishes at the Fermi momentum along the node). Here $k_{x},k_{y}$ are the in-plane crystal-momentum components, $T_{c}$ is the superconducting transition temperature, and $T^{*}$ is the pseudogap crossover temperature.

extra.notations:
- $k_{x},k_{y}$: in-plane momentum components in the $CuO_{2}$ Brillouin zone. 
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature. 
- $T^{*}$: crossover temperature below which the pseudogap is present. 
- $|\cos k_{x}-\cos k_{y}|$: d-wave form factor (zero on nodal lines).

title: Onset of superconductivity at Tc corresponds to increased single-particle coherence (reduced spectral linewidth)
content: The observed sharpening of ARPES spectral features and emergence of sharper quasiparticle peaks at $T_{c}$ implies a reduction in the low-energy single-particle scattering rate (the imaginary part of the electronic self-energy). Concretely, the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the spectral peaks is proportional to twice the quasiparticle scattering rate; narrowing of FWHM at $T_{c}$ therefore indicates increased quasiparticle lifetime and enhanced single-particle coherence coincident with the onset of superconducting phase coherence.

extra.notations:
- FWHM: full width at half maximum of an ARPES energy-distribution curve (EDC) peak, proportional to quasiparticle inverse lifetime.
- Im Σ(ω,k): imaginary part of the electronic self-energy (scattering rate).

title: Pseudogap as pairing amplitude without global phase coherence (preformed-pair scenario)
content: Combining (a) that the normal-state pseudogap has the same d-wave momentum dependence as the superconducting gap (suggesting it occupies the same pairing channel) and (b) that the superconducting transition at $T_{c}$ produces sharpening of spectral features and emergent long-lived quasiparticles (consistent with onset of phase coherence), it is plausible that the pseudogap for $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$ reflects a nonzero pairing amplitude (local or short-range Cooper pairing) without long-range phase coherence. Under this scenario, $T^{*}$ marks the temperature where the pairing amplitude appears (pair formation) and $T_{c}$ marks the lower temperature where global phase coherence is established.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (phase-coherence onset). 
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap onset temperature (putative pair-formation temperature). 
- "Pairing amplitude": a nonzero magnitude of a Cooper-pair order parameter locally or in amplitude fluctuations, independent of long-range phase order.

title: π† creates a d-wave pair with center-of-mass momentum Q
content: The operator $\boldsymbol{\pi}_{\alpha}^{\dagger}=\sum_{p} g(p)\,c_{p+Q,\sigma}^{\dagger}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}^{\alpha}\boldsymbol{\sigma}^{y})_{\sigma\sigma'} c_{-p,\sigma'}^{\dagger}$, with $g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y$ and $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$, necessarily creates a two-electron (Cooper) pair whose total (center-of-mass) crystal momentum is $Q$ and whose relative momentum dependence is weighted by the d-wave form factor $g(p)$. In symbols, each term in the sum creates a pair with momenta $(p+Q)$ and $(-p)$ so the pair momentum is $(p+Q)+(-p)=Q$.

extra.notations:
- $\boldsymbol{\pi}_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$: π creation operator (sums over electron pairs). 
- $c_{k,\sigma}^{\dagger}$: electron creation operator with momentum $k$ and spin $\sigma$. 
- $g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y$: d-wave form factor. 
- $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$: antiferromagnetic ordering vector (pair center-of-mass momentum).

title: π, Q and S close under so(5) commutators (structure constraints)
content:
Identifying the physical operators $\mathcal{Q}$ (total charge), $S_{\gamma}$ (total spin components), and the six operators $\pi_{\alpha},\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$ with the antisymmetric so(5) generators $L_{ab}$ implies that these physical operators satisfy the so(5) Lie algebra commutation relations. Consequently, commutators among the π operators must be linear combinations of $\mathcal{Q}$ and $S_{\gamma}$ with the so(5) structure constants. In particular (up to normalization convention), the commutator of a lowering and raising π operator takes the form
$$[\pi_{\alpha},\pi_{\beta}^{\dagger}]=C\big(\delta_{\alpha\beta}\,\mathcal{Q}+i\epsilon_{\alpha\beta\gamma}S_{\gamma}\big),$$
where $C$ is a real constant fixed by the choice of normalization of the generators and $\epsilon_{\alpha\beta\gamma}$ is the Levi-Civita symbol.

extra.notations:
- $\mathcal{Q}$: total charge operator. 
- $S_{\gamma}$: total spin operator (component $\gamma$). 
- $\pi_{\alpha},\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$: so(5) generators rotating AF and SC sectors. 
- $[\,,\,]$: commutator. 
- $C$: normalization-dependent real constant.

title: π operators are spin-1, charge-2, momentum-$Q$ collective pair excitations (π-resonance interpretation)
content: The microscopic form $\boldsymbol{\pi}_{\alpha}^{\dagger}=\sum_{p} g(p)\,c_{p+Q,\sigma}^{\dagger}(\boldsymbol{\sigma}^{\alpha}\boldsymbol{\sigma}^{y})_{\sigma\sigma'} c_{-p,\sigma'}^{\dagger}$ plausibly represents collective excitations that (i) change charge by $+2$ (they create two electrons), (ii) carry spin quantum number corresponding to a vector index $\alpha$ (spin-1 character), and (iii) carry center-of-mass momentum $Q$. Therefore, these π operators can be interpreted as spin-1, charge-2, momentum-$Q$ collective pair modes (the so-called "π resonance") that couple the antiferromagnetic and d-wave superconducting sectors.

extra.notations:
- spin-1: total spin angular momentum quantum number $S=1$ for the excitation.
