- charge-2: increments the system charge by two units when acting as a creation operator. 
- momentum-$Q$: center-of-mass crystal momentum $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$.

title: Oddness of the absorptive polarizability and vanishing at zero frequency
content:
The absorptive part of the polarizability $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ is an odd function of frequency and vanishes at $\omega=0$. Concretely, for all momenta $\mathbf{q}$ and temperatures $T$,
$$\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},-\omega)=-\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega),\qquad 
\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},0)=0.$$

extra.notations: - $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary (absorptive) part of the polarizability at momentum $\mathbf{q}$ and frequency $\omega$.

title: Logarithmic frequency dependence of the real part via Kramers–Kronig (for $T\ll|\omega|\ll\omega_c$)
content:
Given a high-frequency cutoff $\omega_c$ for the scaling form of $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$, the real part $\mathrm{Re}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ acquires a logarithmic frequency dependence at intermediate frequencies. For temperatures $T$ satisfying $T\ll|\omega|\ll\omega_c$,
$$\mathrm{Re}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\simeq -\,N(0)\,\ln\!\bigg(\frac{\omega_c}{|\omega|}\bigg)+\text{(const.)},$$
up to additive, weakly $\mathbf{q}$-dependent regular terms.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Re}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: real part of the polarizability.
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized one-particle density of states at the chemical potential (per spin).
- $\omega_c$: soft ultraviolet cutoff frequency for the validity of the scaling form.

title: Implication for low-frequency nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate $T_1^{-1}$
content:
Using the standard relation for nuclear spin-lattice relaxation in metals,
$$\frac{1}{T_1}\;\propto\; T\sum_{\mathbf{q}}|A(\mathbf{q})|^2\;\lim_{\omega\to 0}\frac{\operatorname{Im}\chi(\mathbf{q},\omega)}{\omega},$$
where $A(\mathbf{q})$ is the hyperfine form factor and $\chi$ is the spin susceptibility, the scaling
$\operatorname{Im}\chi(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim -N(0)\,(\omega/T)$ at low $\omega$ implies that the factor $\lim_{\omega\to0}\operatorname{Im}\chi(\mathbf{q},\omega)/\omega$ is $\sim -N(0)/T$. Multiplying by the prefactor $T$ yields a temperature-independent nuclear relaxation rate (up to $\mathbf{q}$-dependent weights):
$$\frac{1}{T_1}\;\propto\;\text{const. (independent of }T\text{)}.$$

extra.notations:
- $T_1^{-1}$: nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate.
- $A(\mathbf{q})$: hyperfine form factor coupling nucleus to electronic spins at momentum $\mathbf{q}$.
- $\chi(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: dynamical spin susceptibility (its imaginary part is the spin contribution to $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}$).
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized density of states at the Fermi level.

title: Marginal-Fermi-liquid form of the one-particle self-energy and transport consequences
content:
If electrons couple with non-negligible matrix elements to the charge- and spin-density excitations whose spectral weight is given by the stated $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$, then the electron retarded self-energy $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$ will acquire the "marginal Fermi liquid" frequency and temperature dependence:
$$\operatorname{Im}\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)\;\simeq\;-\,\lambda\,\max\{|\omega|,\,T\},$$
and
$$\operatorname{Re}\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)\;\simeq\;\lambda\,\omega\ln\!\bigg(\frac{\omega_c}{\max\{|\omega|,T\}}\bigg),$$
where $\lambda$ is a (weakly $\mathbf{k}$-dependent) coupling constant and $\omega_c$ is the ultraviolet cutoff of the fluctuation spectrum. As a result, single-particle scattering rates measured by ARPES are expected to be approximately linear in energy (for $|\omega|>T$) and transport scattering rates (and hence resistivity) are expected to display an approximately linear-in-$T$ dependence over the temperature range where the scaling applies.

extra.notations:
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: retarded electron self-energy at momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and frequency $\omega$.
- $\operatorname{Im}\Sigma$, $\operatorname{Re}\Sigma$: imaginary and real parts of $\Sigma$.
- $\lambda$: effective coupling constant between electrons and the density fluctuations.
- $\omega_c$: ultraviolet cutoff frequency for the fluctuation spectrum (as in Proposition 936).
- ARPES: angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy.

title: Numerical equivalence of lattice constants under unit conversion
content: The lattice constants reported in Proposition 8 for undoped LaOFeAs, given as $a=0.403552(8)\\,$nm and $c=0.87393(2)\\,$nm, are numerically identical (within the quoted precision) to the lattice constants reported in Proposition 747 given in angstroms: $a=4.03552\\,$\\AA\\ and $c=8.7393\\,$\\AA. Here $1\\,$nm$=10\\,$\\AA, so $0.403552\\,$nm$=4.03552\\,$\\AA\\ and $0.87393\\,$nm$=8.7393\\,$\\AA.

extra.notations:
- $a$: in-plane tetragonal lattice constant (units indicated in the content). 
- $c$: out-of-plane tetragonal lattice constant (units indicated in the content).
- nm: nanometre; \\AA: angstrom ($1\\,$\\AA$=10^{-10}\\,$m, $1\\,$nm$=10\\,$\\AA).

title: Quantified anisotropic lattice contraction and unit-cell volume decrease for 5 at.% F substitution
content:
Let $a_0$ and $c_0$ denote the lattice constants of undoped LaOFeAs and $a_{5\\%}$ and $c_{5\\%}$ those for La[O_{0.95}F_{0.05}]FeAs. From Proposition 8:
$a_0=4.03552\\,$\\AA, $c_0=8.7393\\,$\\AA; $a_{5\\%}=4.03200\\,$\\AA, $c_{5\\%}=8.72630\\,$\\AA.
Define the tetragonal unit-cell volume $V=a^2 c$. Then
$V_0\\approx a_0^2 c_0\\approx 142.323\\,$\\AA$^{3}$ and $V_{5\\%}\\approx a_{5\\%}^2 c_{5\\%}\\approx 141.864\\,$\\AA$^{3}$.
Therefore 5 at.% F substitution produces absolute lattice changes
$\\Delta a = a_0 - a_{5\\%} \\approx 0.00352\\,$\\AA\\ (a fractional decrease of $\\approx 0.087\\%$),
$\\Delta c = c_0 - c_{5\\%} \\approx 0.0130\\,$\\AA\\ (a fractional decrease of $\\approx 0.149\\%$),
and a unit-cell volume decrease $\\Delta V \\approx 0.459\\,$\\AA$^{3}$ (a fractional decrease of $\\approx 0.32\\%$).
The fractional contraction along $c$ is about $1.7$ times the fractional contraction along $a$.

extra.notations:
- $a_0, c_0$: lattice constants for undoped LaOFeAs (in \\AA).
- $a_{5\\%}, c_{5\\%}$: lattice constants for La[O_{0.95}F_{0.05}]FeAs (in \\AA).
- $V=a^2 c$: tetragonal unit-cell volume (in \\AA$^{3}$).
- $\\Delta X$: change $X_{undoped}-X_{doped}$ for $X\\in\\{a,c,V\\}$.

title: Vegard-like linear decrease and interpretation as chemical pressure affecting Fe–As bonding
content: If the nominal fluoride substitution $x$ is small and the samples are predominantly single-phase (as stated in Proposition 15), then the observed systematic decrease of $a$ and $c$ with increasing $x$ (Proposition 16) and the measured values at $x\\approx0$ and $x\\approx0.05$ (Proposition 8) are consistent with an approximately linear (Vegard-like) dependence of lattice parameters and unit-cell volume on $x$ for small $x$. This lattice contraction acts as an effective chemical pressure: i.e., reduced interatomic distances (notably Fe–As) that plausibly increase Fe $3d$–As $p$ hybridization and electronic bandwidth, and therefore can modify the electronic structure (density of states at the Fermi level) and superconducting properties.

extra.notations:
- $x$: nominal F substitution fraction in La[O_{1-x}F_x]FeAs.
- Vegard-like: approximately linear dependence of lattice parameters/volume on composition $x$ in a limited composition range.
- Fe $3d$–As $p$ hybridization: qualitative descriptor of orbital overlap affecting bandwidth and electronic structure.

title: Coexisting static spin and charge stripes with related periods (ε = 0.12)
content:
Both a static spin modulation and a static charge modulation exist in La1.48Nd0.4Sr0.12CuO4 with the same incommensurability parameter $\varepsilon = 0.12$ (measured in reciprocal-lattice units of $2\pi/a$). The spin modulation has wavevector displacement $\varepsilon$ and therefore a real-space period
$$\lambda_{\text{spin}}=\frac{a}{\varepsilon}=\frac{1}{0.12}\,a\approx 8.33\,a.$$ 
The charge modulation is observed at twice the spin displacement (wavevector component $2\varepsilon$) and therefore has a real-space period
$$\lambda_{\text{charge}}=\frac{a}{2\varepsilon}=\frac{1}{0.24}\,a\approx 4.17\,a.$$
Thus the charge period is exactly half the spin period (charge stripes occur every half spin period), and both orders share the same incommensurability parameter $\varepsilon=0.12$.

extra.notations:
- $a$: in-plane lattice constant (real-space lattice spacing).
- $\varepsilon$: incommensurability parameter (displacement of peaks in units of $2\pi/a$).
- $\lambda_{\text{spin}}$: real-space period of the spin modulation (distance between equivalent spin-phase features).
- $\lambda_{\text{charge}}$: real-space period of the charge modulation (distance between charge stripes).

title: Static stripes likely reconstruct the electronic structure (Brillouin-zone folding at $2\varepsilon$)
content: The observed static charge modulation with wavevector component $2\varepsilon=0.24$ (rlu of $2\pi/a$) produces a periodic potential with period $\lambda_{\text{charge}}=a/(2\varepsilon)\approx 4.17\,a$. Such a periodic potential along the Cu–O bond directions generically folds the electronic bands by the modulation wavevector and can reconstruct the Fermi surface (for example producing new replica bands, band gaps at reconstructed Brillouin-zone boundaries, and possibly small electron or hole pockets). Therefore, in La1.48Nd0.4Sr0.12CuO4 in the LTT-pinned static-stripe state one should expect observable signatures of Fermi-surface reconstruction in spectroscopic and transport probes (ARPES, quantum oscillations, Hall effect), arising from scattering at wavevector $2\varepsilon$.

extra.notations:
- $\varepsilon$: incommensurability parameter (0.12 in the reported system).
- $2\varepsilon$: charge-order wavevector component in rlu ($=0.24$ here).
- $\lambda_{\text{charge}}$: real-space period of the charge modulation $=a/(2\varepsilon)$.
- ARPES: angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy.

title: Monotonic decrease of lattice parameters and unit-cell volume with nominal F content
content:
For the tetragonal La[O$_{1-x}$F$_x$]FeAs system (space group P4/nmm) with nominal substitution parameter $x\ge 0$,
the in-plane lattice constant $a(x)$ and the out-of-plane lattice constant $c(x)$ satisfy
$a(x)<a(0)=4.03552\ \text{\AA}$ and $c(x)<c(0)=8.7393\ \text{\AA}$ for any nominal $x>0$.
Consequently the tetragonal unit-cell volume defined by $V(x)=a(x)^2\,c(x)$ decreases with increasing $x$,
i.e. $V(x)<V(0)=4.03552^2\times 8.7393\ \text{\AA}^3$ for any nominal $x>0$.

extra.notations:
- $a(x)$: in-plane tetragonal lattice constant for nominal composition $x$.
- $c(x)$: out-of-plane tetragonal lattice constant for nominal composition $x$.
- $V(x)=a(x)^2 c(x)$: conventional tetragonal unit-cell volume (assumes $a=b$).
- $a(0)=4.03552\ \text{\AA}$, $c(0)=8.7393\ \text{\AA}$: values for undoped ($x=0$) LaFeAsO.

title: Fluoride substitution likely provides electron doping and chemical-pressure effects
content: In La[O$_{1-x}$F$_x$]FeAs, nominal substitution of F$^{-}$ for O$^{2-}$ (i) likely introduces extra electrons into the conducting FeAs layers (electron doping) because replacing an O$^{2-}$ by F$^{-}$ reduces the anionic negative charge by one unit per substitution and (ii) produces a chemical-pressure effect because the measured decreases of lattice constants $a(x)$ and $c(x)$ compress the unit cell. Together, these effects are likely to modify the electronic band structure (carrier concentration, bandwidth, and interlayer coupling) and therefore to influence electronic properties such as the tendency toward superconductivity.

extra.notations:
- $x$: nominal molar fraction of F$^{-}$ substituting for O$^{2-}$.
- "Electron doping": increase in conduction-electron count per formula unit in the FeAs layers.
- "Chemical pressure": changes in interatomic distances and band parameters caused by lattice contraction rather than externally applied pressure.

title: Observed superconducting transition width for Tl2Ba2CuO6 (900°C samples)
content: For single crystals of Tl2Ba2CuO6 synthesized at 900°C, the superconducting transition onset temperature $T_{c}^{onset}$ is approximately $90\ \mathrm{K}$ and the temperature of zero resistivity $T_{c}^{0}$ is $83\ \mathrm{K}$; therefore the observed transition width $\Delta T = T_{c}^{onset}-T_{c}^{0}$ for those samples is approximately $7\ \mathrm{K}$. (Here $T_{c}^{onset}$ denotes the temperature where superconductivity first appears in the sample and $T_{c}^{0}$ denotes the temperature where resistivity falls to zero.)

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}^{onset}$: superconducting transition onset temperature (K)
- $T_{c}^{0}$: temperature of zero resistivity (K)
- $\Delta T$: transition width $=T_{c}^{onset}-T_{c}^{0}$ (K)

title: Tc sensitivity to thermal history likely reflects changes in oxygen stoichiometry / carrier concentration
content: For Tl2Ba2CuO6, the reported strong dependence of the superconducting transition onset $T_{c}^{onset}$ on synthesis temperature (examples: $T_{c}^{onset}\approx90\ \mathrm{K}$ for synthesis at $900^\circ\mathrm{C}$ versus $T_{c}^{onset}\approx55\ \mathrm{K}$ for synthesis at $850^\circ\mathrm{C}$) plausibly indicates that processing-dependent variables — most likely oxygen content and hence hole carrier concentration $p$ — change with thermal history and are responsible for the observed variation in $T_{c}$. (Here $p$ denotes the hole doping per Cu site.)

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}^{onset}$: superconducting transition onset temperature (K)
- $p$: hole carrier concentration per Cu site (dimensionless)

title: Marked difference in Tc between Tl- and Bi-based single-layer cuprates implies differing electronic/structural factors
content: The two single-layer cuprates reported here, Tl2Ba2CuO6 (reported $T_{c}^{onset}\sim90\ \mathrm{K}$ for well-prepared crystals) and Bi2Sr2CuO6 (reported $T_{c}$ slightly above $9\ \mathrm{K}$), show a very large difference in superconducting transition temperature. This disparity plausibly reflects substantial differences in their optimal carrier concentrations, intrinsic electronic structure (band structure, correlation strength), and/or interlayer coupling arising from different A-site cations (Tl vs Bi) and their associated oxide layers (Tl–O vs Bi–O). Symbols: $T_{c}$ denotes superconducting transition temperature (K).

extra.notations: - $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (K)

title: Mandatory subtraction of nuclear contributions near 20–22 meV
content: If the total measured inelastic neutron scattering intensity S(⃗Q,ℏω) at the specified momentum (e.g. ⃗Q=(1/2,1/2,5.2)) contains (a) a constant flat background, (b) a localized spectral component centered near ℏω≈20 meV, and (c) a broad damped-harmonic-oscillator (DHO) component with ℏω=21.5 meV and Γω=26 meV that follows the Bose population factor, then any analysis that attributes the measured intensity in the 20–22 meV region entirely to magnetic excitations is necessarily incorrect unless those three nuclear (non-magnetic) contributions are modeled and subtracted. In symbols: the measured S_total(⃗Q,ℏω) = S_nuclear(⃗Q,ℏω) + S_magnetic(⃗Q,ℏω), where S_nuclear contains the flat background plus the localized ~20 meV peak and the broad DHO (ℏω=21.5 meV, Γω=26 meV); therefore S_magnetic can only be obtained by S_total − S_nuclear.

extra.notations:
- S(⃗Q,ℏω): measured dynamical structure factor at momentum transfer ⃗Q and energy transfer ℏω.
- S_total: total measured scattering intensity (nuclear + magnetic + background).
- S_nuclear: modeled nuclear (non-magnetic) contribution = flat background + localized ~20 meV peak + broad DHO (ℏω=21.5 meV, Γω=26 meV) following the Bose factor.
- S_magnetic: remaining (magnetic) contribution after subtraction.
- ℏω: energy transfer (meV).
- Γω: damping (meV) of the DHO.

title: Broad DHO component is predominantly phononic (thermal) in origin
content: The broad spectral response parameterized by a damped harmonic oscillator with characteristic energy ℏω = 21.5 meV and linewidth Γω = 26 meV, which follows the Bose population factor (1 − e^{−ℏω/kT})^{-1}, is plausibly dominated by phonon (nuclear) scattering rather than magnetic excitations. Here ℏ is the reduced Planck constant, k is the Boltzmann constant, and T is temperature. The large damping (Γω comparable to or larger than ℏω) makes the feature broad in energy, consistent with strongly damped lattice vibrations or multi-phonon contributions that scale with the Bose occupation.

extra.notations:
- ℏω = 21.5 meV: characteristic energy of the broad component.
- Γω = 26 meV: linewidth (damping) of the broad component.
- (1 − e^{−ℏω/kT})^{-1}: Bose population (detailed-balance) factor.

title: Localized ~20 meV peak likely corresponds to a weakly temperature-dependent phonon or defect mode
content: The localized spectral feature centered near ℏω ≈ 20 meV, whose intensity decreases only slightly with increasing temperature, is plausibly an optical phonon mode or a localized lattice/defect vibration (nuclear scattering) rather than a magnetic excitation strongly tied to superconductivity. Denote this localized contribution as S_loc(ℏω≈20 meV); the weak temperature dependence suggests modest anharmonic broadening or population changes rather than the stronger temperature dependence often seen in magnetic resonances across Tc.

extra.notations:
- S_loc: localized nuclear spectral contribution centered near ℏω≈20 meV.
- Tc: superconducting transition temperature.
- ℏω: energy transfer (meV).

title: Overlap of nuclear components complicates identification of magnetic features near 20–22 meV
content: Because the modeled nuclear contributions include both a localized peak at ≈20 meV and a broad DHO centered at 21.5 meV with large Γω, the nuclear spectral weight substantially overlaps the 20–22 meV window. Therefore, the unambiguous identification and quantitative extraction of any magnetic excitation or resonance within this energy range will be difficult without high-precision Q-dependent measurements and robust modeling of the nuclear contributions. Let S_overlap denote the combined nuclear spectral weight in 20–22 meV that must be separated from any magnetic signal.

extra.notations:
- S_overlap: combined nuclear (localized + broad DHO + flat background) spectral weight in the 20–22 meV energy window.
- Q: momentum transfer (reciprocal space coordinate).

title: Existence of a temperature window with local pairs but no global phase coherence (pseudogap / preformed-pair regime)
content:
If $T^{MF}$ denotes the temperature scale for local pair formation (pairing amplitude) and $T_{c}$ denotes the experimentally observed superconducting transition temperature (the onset of global phase coherence), then in the regime where phase fluctuations suppress $T_{c}$ below $T^{MF}$ there necessarily exists a temperature interval $T_{c}<T<T^{MF}$ in which local pair amplitude is nonzero (pairs are formed locally) but long-range phase coherence is absent. This interval corresponds to a pseudogap or preformed-pair regime in which spectroscopic signatures of pairing can persist above the temperature where global superconductivity (zero-resistance, Meissner response) appears.
Symbols:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (temperature for global phase coherence).
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature (temperature scale for local pair formation or pair amplitude onset).
- $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$: phase-ordering temperature (energy scale for establishing global phase coherence).

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: defined above.
- $T^{MF}$: defined above.
- $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$: defined above.

title: Tc in the low-superfluid-density limit is determined by the phase stiffness and therefore scales with superfluid density over effective mass
content:
In the low-doping / low-superfluid-density regime where the observed transition temperature $T_{c}$ is set by phase-ordering physics (i.e. $T_{c}\approx T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$), $T_{c}$ is controlled by the phase stiffness (phase-ordering energy) $J$ which, in standard continuum or lattice descriptions of a charged superfluid, is proportional to the superfluid density divided by an effective mass. Consequently
$$T_{c}\propto T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}\sim \frac{\hbar^{2} n_{s}}{k_{B} m^{*}}\,, $$
where $n_{s}$ is the superfluid (pair) density and $m^{*}$ is the effective mass of the paired carriers. Thus in this regime $T_{c}$ scales approximately as $n_{s}/m^{*}$ (the Uemura-like scaling).
Symbols:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature.
- $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}$: phase-ordering temperature (phase stiffness energy divided by $k_{B}$).
- $J$: phase stiffness (energy per unit area or per site controlling phase-ordering).
- $n_{s}$: superfluid (pair) density.
- $m^{*}$: effective mass of the paired carriers.
- $k_{B}$: Boltzmann constant.

extra.notations:
- $J$: phase stiffness; in common microscopic derivations $J\sim \hbar^{2} n_{s}/(4m^{*})$ (dimensionally an energy).
- $T_{\theta}^{\mathrm{max}}\sim J/k_{B}$: phase-ordering temperature scale.

title: FeF2 is the intended fluorine source for nominal $x=0.15$
content: The starting reagent list includes iron(II) fluoride ($FeF_{2}$) and the target nominal composition is $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$ with $x=0.15$. Therefore the fluorine atoms in the intended nominal composition (the fraction $x=0.15$ of oxygen sites substituted by fluorine) are supplied by the fluorine-bearing reagent $FeF_{2}$ present in the starting mixture.

extra.notations:
- $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$: target nominal composition with fluorine fraction $x$ on oxygen sites
- $FeF_{2}$: iron(II) fluoride, a fluorine-containing starting reagent
- $x$: nominal fraction of O sites substituted by F

title: Stoichiometric balancing likely required because $FeF_{2}$ also supplies Fe
content: Iron(II) fluoride ($FeF_{2}$) introduces both fluorine and iron into the starting mixture. To achieve the intended nominal formula $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$ with $x=0.15$, the amounts of elemental Fe, $Fe_{2}O_{3}$, and $FeF_{2}$ must be chosen to yield the correct net Fe and O occupancy per formula unit. If the reagent amounts are not compensated for the additional Fe brought in by $FeF_{2}$, the result is likely Fe-rich stoichiometry locally, which plausibly promotes formation of iron-rich secondary phases (e.g., FeAs or elemental Fe) or requires adjustment of oxygen-bearing reagents to maintain the intended O/F occupancy.

extra.notations:
- $FeF_{2}$: iron(II) fluoride (provides both F and Fe)
- $Fe_{2}O_{3}$: iron(III) oxide (oxygen source and Fe source)
- $x$: nominal F fraction ($x=0.15$)

title: Nonzero magnetic response at ℏω ≈ 41 meV
content: There exists a momentum transfer q (a wavevector in the Brillouin zone) such that the imaginary part of the dynamical spin susceptibility, Im{χ(q,ℏω)}, is strictly positive at energy transfer ℏω ≈ 41 meV in YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature (T on the order of a few kelvin). In other words, the spin-excitation spectrum has nonzero magnetic spectral weight at ℏω ≈ 41 meV.

extra.notations:
- ℏω: energy transfer (ℏ is the reduced Planck constant).
- χ(q,ℏω): dynamic spin susceptibility as a function of momentum q and energy ℏω.
- Im{χ(q,ℏω)}: imaginary part of the susceptibility, proportional to magnetic inelastic scattering intensity.

title: Resonance as a dominant feature of the multi–tens‑of‑meV magnetic spectral weight
content: At T ≈ 5 K in YBa2Cu3O6.92, the resonant magnetic peak near ℏω ≈ 41 meV constitutes a substantial (non-negligible and often dominant) fraction of the total magnetic spectral weight in the multi–tens-of-meV energy window; therefore measurements integrating magnetic spectral weight over that energy range will be strongly influenced by the resonance centered at ≈41 meV.

extra.notations:
- "magnetic spectral weight": integrated intensity of Im{χ(q,ℏω)} over selected q and ℏω ranges attributable to spin fluctuations.
- "multi–tens-of-meV energy window": energies of order 20–80 meV (i.e., several tens of meV).

title: Nuclear scattering at Q=(1/2,1/2,5.2) decomposes into background + localized ~20 meV peak + DHO term
content:
The nuclear (non-magnetic) contribution to the measured inelastic neutron scattering intensity at momentum transfer $\vec{Q}=(1/2,1/2,5.2)$ in YBa$_{2}$Cu$_{3}$O$_{6.92}$ is described as the sum of three parts:
(i) a constant, energy-independent background term; (ii) a localized spectral feature centered near an energy transfer $\hbar\omega\approx 20\ \mathrm{meV}$ whose intensity shows a weak decrease with increasing temperature; and (iii) a broad spectral component extending across the measured energy range that is parameterized by a damped harmonic oscillator (DHO) with characteristic energy $\hbar\omega_0 = 21.5\ \mathrm{meV}$ and damping (linewidth) $\Gamma_{\omega}=26\ \mathrm{meV}$ and whose intensity follows the Bose population factor.
All symbols: $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant, $\omega$ the angular frequency (energy transfer $\hbar\omega$), $k$ the Boltzmann constant, and $T$ the absolute temperature.

extra.notations:
- $\vec{Q}$: momentum transfer (wave vector) where the spectrum is measured.
- $\hbar\omega$: energy transfer.
- $\hbar\omega_{0}$: characteristic (resonant) energy of the DHO.
- $\Gamma_{\omega}$: damping (linewidth) of the DHO.

title: Broad DHO component is likely phononic (lattice) in origin
content: The broad spectral response parameterized by a damped harmonic oscillator with $\hbar\omega_{0}=21.5\ \mathrm{meV}$ and $\Gamma_{\omega}=26\ \mathrm{meV}$, whose intensity follows the Bose population factor $(1-\exp(-\hbar\omega/kT))^{-1}$, is plausibly attributable to phonon (lattice vibration) scattering (including single-phonon or multi-phonon contributions) rather than to magnetic excitations. Symbols: $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant, $\omega$ the angular frequency, $k$ Boltzmann's constant, and $T$ absolute temperature.

extra.notations:
- Bose population factor: $(1-\exp(-\hbar\omega/kT))^{-1}$.
- DHO: damped harmonic oscillator model used to parameterize broad inelastic response.

title: Localized ~20 meV feature is a separate localized mode (e.g., optical phonon or defect mode)
content: The spectral feature centered at $\hbar\omega\approx 20\ \mathrm{meV}$ represents a distinct, localized excitation (for example an optical phonon or a defect/local-mode) that is not simply the narrow part of the broad DHO continuum parameterized by $\hbar\omega_{0}=21.5\ \mathrm{meV}$ and $\Gamma_{\omega}=26\ \mathrm{meV}$. Here $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant and $\omega$ the angular frequency (energy transfer $\hbar\omega$).

extra.notations:
- Localized feature: spectral peak with a center near $20\,$meV and temperature dependence differing from the broad DHO term.
- DHO parameters: $\hbar\omega_{0}=21.5\,$meV, $\Gamma_{\omega}=26\,$meV.

title: Hexagonal unit-cell volume from lattice parameters
content:
The volume V of the hexagonal unit cell (conventional/principal cell) with basal-plane lattice constant $a$ and axial lattice constant $c$ is
$$V = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,a^{2}c.$$
Using the reported values $a=3.086\ \text{\AA}$ and $c=3.524\ \text{\AA}$ gives
$$V \approx \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,(3.086\ \text{\AA})^{2}(3.524\ \text{\AA}) \approx 29.06\ \text{\AA}^{3}.$$
All symbols: $V$ = unit-cell volume (cubic ångströms), $a$ = basal-plane lattice constant (Å), $c$ = axial lattice constant (Å).

extra.notations:
- V: unit-cell volume (Å^3)
- a: basal-plane lattice constant (Å)
- c: lattice constant along hexagonal axis (Å)

title: Boron nearest-neighbour (in-plane) distance from basal lattice parameter
content:
In the graphite-like honeycomb boron layer the nearest-neighbour B–B bond length $d_{BB}$ is related to the hexagonal lattice parameter $a$ by
$$d_{BB} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{3}}.$$
With $a=3.086\ \text{\AA}$ this gives
$$d_{BB} \approx \frac{3.086\ \text{\AA}}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.78\ \text{\AA}.$$
All symbols: $d_{BB}$ = in-plane nearest-neighbour distance between boron atoms (Å), $a$ as above.

extra.notations:
- d_{BB}: nearest-neighbour B–B bond length in the boron honeycomb (Å)
- a: basal-plane lattice constant (Å)

title: Estimated crystalline mass density assuming one formula unit per cell
content:
Assuming the conventional MgB2 unit cell contains one formula unit (one Mg atom + two B atoms per unit cell, $Z=1$), the crystalline mass density $\rho$ can be estimated from
$$\rho = \frac{Z\,M}{N_{\mathrm{A}}\,V},$$
where $M$ is the molar mass of MgB2, $N_{\mathrm{A}}$ is Avogadro's number, and $V$ is the unit-cell volume in cm^3. Using $M\approx 24.305+2\times 10.811=45.927\ \mathrm{g\,mol^{-1}}$, $N_{\mathrm{A}}=6.022\times 10^{23}\ \mathrm{mol^{-1}}$, and $V\approx 29.06\ \text{\AA}^{3}=2.906\times 10^{-23}\ \mathrm{cm}^{3}$ yields
$$\rho \approx \frac{1\times 45.927\ \mathrm{g\,mol^{-1}}}{6.022\times 10^{23}\ \mathrm{mol^{-1}}\times 2.906\times 10^{-23}\ \mathrm{cm}^{3}} \approx 2.62\ \mathrm{g\,cm^{-3}}.$$
All symbols: $\rho$ = mass density (g cm^{-3}), $Z$ = number of formula units per unit cell (assumed 1), $M$ = molar mass of MgB2 (g mol^{-1}), $N_{\mathrm{A}}$ = Avogadro's number, $V$ = unit-cell volume (cm^3).

extra.notations:
- \rho: estimated crystalline density (g cm^{-3})
- Z: formula units per unit cell (dimensionless, assumed 1)
- M: molar mass of MgB2 (g mol^{-1})
- N_{\mathrm{A}}: Avogadro's number (mol^{-1})
- V: unit-cell volume (cm^{3})

title: Derived crystallographic ratios, unit-cell volume and theoretical density
content:
From the reported basal-plane lattice constant $a=3.086\,$\AA\ and vertical lattice constant $c=3.524\,$\AA\ for hexagonal $MgB_{2}$ one obtains the following necessary numerical consequences.
- The axial ratio is $$\frac{c}{a}=\frac{3.524}{3.086}\approx 1.142\;.$$
- The hexagonal unit-cell volume (conventional hexagonal cell) is
  $$V=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,a^{2}c\approx \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}(3.086^{2})(3.524)\approx 29.09\ \text{\AA}^{3}\;.$$
- Assuming one formula unit of $MgB_{2}$ per unit cell (standard structural assignment for $MgB_{2}$ in $P6/mmm$), the theoretical crystal density is
  $$\rho=\frac{M_{\mathrm{fu}}/N_{\mathrm{A}}}{V}$$
  where $M_{\mathrm{fu}}$ is the molar mass of $MgB_{2}$ and $N_{\mathrm{A}}$ is Avogadro's number. With $M_{\mathrm{fu}}=45.927\ \mathrm{g\,mol^{-1}}$ and $V=29.09\ \text{\AA}^{3}=2.9089\times10^{-29}\ \mathrm{m^{3}}$ one obtains
  $$\rho\approx 2.623\ \mathrm{g\,cm^{-3}}\ \ (\approx 2623\ \mathrm{kg\,m^{-3}})\;.$$

extra.notations:
- $a$: basal-plane lattice constant (in \AA).
- $c$: vertical lattice constant (in \AA).
- $V$: conventional hexagonal unit-cell volume (in \AA$^{3}$).
- $M_{\mathrm{fu}}$: molar mass of one formula unit (here $MgB_{2}$), $M_{\mathrm{fu}}=45.927\ \mathrm{g\,mol^{-1}}$.
- $N_{\mathrm{A}}$: Avogadro's number ($6.02214076\times10^{23}\ \mathrm{mol^{-1}}$).
- $\rho$: mass density (in g cm$^{-3}$ or kg m$^{-3}$).

title: Hot isostatic pressing (HIP) likely preserves stoichiometry and produces low average lattice strain
content: The report that $MgB_{2}$ synthesized by hot isostatic pressing (HIP) has the same space group $P6/mmm$ and identical lattice parameters $a=3.086\,$\AA, $c=3.524\,$\AA\ as the canonical values suggests that the HIP process, for this sample, did not introduce a large average change in stoichiometry (e.g., substantial Mg deficiency) nor produce large bulk lattice strains that would shift the mean lattice parameters beyond measurement uncertainty.

extra.notations:
- HIP: hot isostatic pressing (a densification/synthesis technique).
- "Stoichiometry": relative numbers of Mg and B atoms per formula unit in the crystalline phase.
- "Average lattice strain": shift of the mean lattice parameters measurable by diffraction.

title: Spin-gap (pairing) in the stripe does not renormalize the zero-temperature Drude weight
content:
If the charge sector of the one-dimensional electron gas (1DEG) is described by a Gaussian Luttinger model with soundlike dispersion and an unrenormalized charge velocity $v_c$, then the zero-temperature Drude weight (the superfluid phase stiffness), which depends only on the charge-sector parameters, is unchanged by interactions that act exclusively in the spin sector. Concretely: let $D_0$ denote the bare zero-temperature Drude weight of the isolated stripe and let $D$ denote the Drude weight when the stripe is coupled to the environment. Given that (i) the unitary decoupling separates spin and charge and leaves the charge Hamiltonian Gaussian with the same $v_c$ (proposition 637), and (ii) the environment coupling induces a spin gap $\Delta_s$ in the spin sector of the stripe (proposition 618), it follows necessarily that
$$D = D_0$$
at zero temperature: the formation of the spin gap (pairing in the spin sector) cannot change $D$ because $D$ is determined solely by the decoupled charge-sector fields.

extra.notations:
- $D$: zero-temperature Drude weight (superfluid phase stiffness) of the stripe. 
- $D_0$: bare Drude weight of the isolated stripe. 
- $v_c$: charge velocity of the stripe charge sector (unchanged by coupling). 
- $\Delta_s$: induced spin gap in the stripe.

title: Crossover between two asymptotic scalings of the induced spin gap as charge-transfer energy varies
content:
The two presented asymptotic forms for the induced spin gap $\Delta_s$ correspond to distinct limiting regimes of the renormalized charge-transfer energy $\varepsilon^*$. For vanishing $\varepsilon^*$ (environment strongly resonant with stripe pair states) proposition 618 gives a power-law (nonperturbative) scaling
$$\Delta_s \sim \frac{v_s}{a}\Big(\frac{\mathcal{J}_{sp}a}{v_s}\Big)^{\alpha},$$
with $\alpha = \dfrac{2}{4-K_s}$. For finite but small $\varepsilon^*$ in the window stated in proposition 645, mean-field analysis yields a perturbative-like scaling
$$\Delta_s(0) \sim \frac{\mathcal{J}_{sp}^2}{\varepsilon^*}.$$
Equating these two expressions gives an estimate for the crossover charge-transfer energy $\varepsilon^*_c$ at which the dominant scaling of $\Delta_s$ switches:
$$\varepsilon^*_c \approx v_s^{\,\alpha-1}\,a^{\,1-\alpha}\,\mathcal{J}_{sp}^{\,2-\alpha}.$$
Here $\varepsilon^*_c$ depends on the spin velocity $v_s$, lattice cutoff $a$, the effective pair-tunneling amplitude $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$, and the exponent $\alpha$ (hence on $K_s$). Therefore one expects a controlled crossover from the power-law regime (small or zero $\varepsilon^*$) to the $ \mathcal{J}_{sp}^2/\varepsilon^*$ regime as $\varepsilon^*$ increases through $\varepsilon^*_c$.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta_s$: zero-temperature induced spin gap in the stripe. 
- $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: effective pair-hopping coupling between stripe and environment. 
- $\varepsilon^*$: renormalized charge transfer energy (energy to move a pair of holes). 
- $v_s$: spin velocity of the stripe. 
- $a$: short-distance cutoff (lattice constant). 
- $K_s$: spin Luttinger parameter; $\alpha = 2/(4-K_s)$.
- $\varepsilon^*_c$: estimated crossover value of $\varepsilon^*$ between the two scalings.

title: Pairing amplitude (spin gap) can grow without increasing stripe phase stiffness — implications for superconducting transition
content: Because the decoupling keeps the stripe charge-sector stiffness (Drude weight) unrenormalized (proposition 637) while the environment coupling can enhance the pairing amplitude (spin gap) in the stripe (proposition 645), it is plausible that increasing the pairing scale $\Delta_s$ (or raising the pairing crossover temperature $T_{pair}$) need not increase the zero-temperature phase stiffness of an isolated stripe. Consequently, in an array of stripes the superconducting transition temperature $T_c$ may become limited by phase-ordering (inter-stripe Josephson coupling and the finite superfluid stiffness) rather than by the pairing amplitude itself: i.e., stronger local pairing does not automatically raise global $T_c$ unless it is accompanied by increased charge coherence (larger inter-stripe Josephson coupling or enhanced effective stiffness).

extra.notations:
- $\Delta_s$: induced spin gap (local pairing amplitude) on a stripe. 
- $T_{pair}$: crossover temperature where spin-gap/pairing develops. 
- $D$: zero-temperature Drude weight (stripe phase stiffness), unaffected by spin-sector renormalizations in decoupling limit. 
- $T_c$: global superconducting transition temperature (requires phase coherence between stripes).

title: Existence of at least two distinct measurable spectral features separated in energy
content: There exist at least two distinct, measurable energy-localized spectral features in superconducting YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature: (i) a pronounced magnetic (spin) resonance at an energy transfer $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 41\\,$meV, and (ii) at least one other measurable scattering feature with energy transfer in the window $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 20\\text{–}30\\,$meV. Because the highest energy in the 20–30 meV window is 30 meV and the resonance is at ~41 meV, these two measurable features are spectrally separated by at least $\\approx 11\\,$meV.

extra.notations:
- $\\hbar\\omega$: energy transfer measured in inelastic neutron (or other) scattering (\\hbar is the reduced Planck constant). 
- "magnetic resonance": a pronounced feature in the magnetic part of the dynamical response (Im\\{\\chi(q,\\,\\hbar\\omega)\\}) detected by inelastic neutron scattering.

title: Multi-component magnetic excitation spectrum (resonant mode plus lower-energy magnetic spectral weight)
content: The magnetic excitation spectrum of superconducting YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature plausibly comprises two magnetic components: (A) a high-energy collective resonant mode centered at $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 41\\,$meV, and (B) lower-energy magnetic spectral weight forming a gapped continuum or broadened spin excitations with onset near $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 25\\text{–}28\\,$meV (coexisting with an ungapped, broad nuclear/phononic scattering contribution near $\\approx 21.5\\,$meV). Thus the total inelastic response in the few-10-meV window is a superposition of a dominant magnetic resonance plus additional lower-energy magnetic and non-magnetic components.

extra.notations:
- $\\hbar\\omega$: energy transfer in inelastic scattering.
- "gapped continuum": a spread of magnetic spectral weight with little intensity below a threshold energy (here suggested near 25–28 meV).

title: True transition temperature is bounded by the smaller phase-ordering or mean-field scale
content:
Let $T_{c}$ be the observed superconducting transition temperature, $T_{\theta}^{max}$ the theoretical maximum phase-ordering temperature, and $T^{MF}$ the mean-field pairing temperature. If both $T_{\theta}^{max}$ and $T^{MF}$ are upper bounds on $T_{c}$, then necessarily
$$T_{c} \le \min\!\big( T_{\theta}^{max},\, T^{MF} \big).$$
This relation holds without further assumptions beyond the statements that each named temperature is an upper bound on the true transition temperature.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence). 
- $T_{\theta}^{max}$: theoretical maximum phase-ordering temperature (upper bound from phase stiffness). 
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field transition (pairing) temperature.

title: Under low doping, $T_{c}$ should scale with superfluid stiffness ($n_{s}/m^{*}$)
content:
In the regime where phase fluctuations control the transition (low doping $\delta\ll1$, low superfluid density), the observed $T_{c}$ is expected to scale proportionally to the phase stiffness, which in typical models is proportional to the superfluid density divided by effective mass. Denoting $n_{s}(0)$ the $T=0$ superfluid density and $m^{*}$ the effective mass, one expects (up to dimensionless constants and a short-distance cutoff length $a$)
$$T_{c}\ \propto\ \frac{\hbar^{2}}{k_{B}}\frac{n_{s}(0)}{m^{*}} \times (\text{geometry-dependent factor}).$$
Hence, for quasi-two-dimensional cuprates in the phase-fluctuation-dominated underdoped regime, $T_{c}$ will approximately track $n_{s}(0)/m^{*}$ (the empirical "Uemura-like" scaling), modulo prefactors set by interlayer coupling and short-distance physics.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature.
- $n_{s}(0)$: superfluid density at $T=0$.
- $m^{*}$: effective mass.
- $k_{B}$: Boltzmann constant, $\hbar$: reduced Planck constant.
- $a$: short-distance cutoff (e.g., coherence length or layer spacing).

title: Pseudogap (pairs without coherence) is expected above $T_{c}$ in underdoped cuprates
content: When the mean-field pairing temperature $T^{MF}$ exceeds the phase-ordering/phase-coherence temperature (i.e., $T^{MF}>T_{c}$) in the underdoped, low-superfluid-density regime, the system can host locally paired (gapped) electronic states without global phase coherence for a temperature window $T_{c}<T<T^{MF}$. This manifests as a pseudogap: a suppression of single-particle spectral weight or a gap-like feature in spectroscopic probes above the resistive superconducting transition.

extra.notations:
- $T^{MF}$: mean-field pairing temperature (local pair formation scale).
- $T_{c}$: observed superconducting transition temperature (global coherence).

title: Superconducting LaH_x phase is LaH9.6 (Fm-3m) and occurs near 150–170 GPa with Tc ≈ 250 K
content: The reported superconducting transition with critical temperature $T_{c}\approx 250\,$K occurs in the lanthanum hydride phase that crystallizes in the face-centered cubic space group $Fm\overline{3}m$ with refined lattice parameter $a=5.1019(5)\,$Å and cell volume $V=132.80(4)\,$Å$^{3}$, corresponding to stoichiometry approximately $LaH_{9.6}$ (i.e. close to $LaH_{10}$). The superconducting observations span pressures reported at $\sim 150\,$GPa (structural identification) and $\sim 170\,$GPa (electrical zero-resistance), so the superconducting LaH$_{x}$ phase with $x\approx 9.6$ exists in the megabar pressure range near $150$–$170\,$GPa and exhibits $T_{c}\approx 250\,$K.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (K). 
- $Fm\overline{3}m$: face-centered cubic space group. 
- $a$: cubic lattice parameter (Å). 
- $V$: unit-cell volume (Å$^{3}$). 
- $LaH_{x}$: lanthanum hydride with stoichiometry $x$.

title: Superconductivity is intrinsic bulk superconductivity of the Fm-3m LaH9.6 phase with conventional (spin-singlet, orbital-limited) pairing likely
content: The superconducting behavior observed (zero electrical resistance, suppression of transition temperature by applied magnetic field, large extrapolated upper critical field $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(0)\approx 95$–$136\,$T with short Ginzburg–Landau coherence length $\xi\approx 1.56$–$1.86\,$nm, and current-dependent transition temperature) is most plausibly explained by intrinsic, bulk superconductivity of the $Fm\overline{3}m$ $LaH_{9.6}$ ($\approx LaH_{10}$) phase. The magnetic-field dependence reported is consistent with an orbital pair-breaking mechanism and a spin-singlet superconducting state (total Cooper-pair spin $S=0$); therefore, pairing mediated by lattice vibrations (phonons), as predicted for high-hydrogen-content superhydrides, is a plausible mechanism for the observed $T_{c}\approx 250\,$K.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (K). 
- $\mu_{0}H$: applied magnetic field (T). 
- $H_{\mathrm{c2}}(0)$: upper critical magnetic field extrapolated to $0\,$K (T). 
- $\xi$: Ginzburg–Landau coherence length (nm). 
- $S$: total spin of a Cooper pair.

title: Fluctuation-averaged Josephson coupling equals the ensemble average of the distance-dependent matrix element
content:
Let $J(\Delta_jY)$ be the instantaneous Josephson matrix element for Cooper-pair tunneling between stripes $j$ and $j+1$, with $\Delta_jY\equiv Y_{j+1}(x)-Y_j(x)$. If transverse stripe fluctuations are present and an effective (coarse-grained) inter-stripe coupling is obtained by averaging over those fluctuations, then the effective Josephson coupling is
$$J_{\mathrm{eff}}(j,x)=\big\langle J(\Delta_jY(x))\big\rangle,$$
where $\langle\cdot\rangle$ denotes the expectation value over the thermal/quantum ensemble of stripe configurations. In the common parametrization $J(\Delta)=J_0 e^{-\alpha\Delta}$ (with $J_0>0$ and $\alpha>0$) this implies
$$J_{\mathrm{eff}}(j,x)=J_0\big\langle e^{-\alpha\Delta_jY(x)}\big\rangle.$$
Because $f(\Delta)=e^{-\alpha\Delta}$ is convex ($f''(\Delta)=\alpha^2 e^{-\alpha\Delta}>0$), Jensen's inequality gives the rigorous bound
$$J_{\mathrm{eff}}(j,x)\ge J_0 e^{-\alpha\langle\Delta_jY(x)\rangle}.$$
Moreover, since Josephson (pair) tunneling is a $k=0$ order parameter, $J$ does not couple to arc-length mismatch $\Delta_jL$ and the averaging involves only transverse-position fluctuations $\Delta_jY$.

extra.notations:
- $J(\Delta_jY)$: instantaneous Josephson matrix element between stripes $j$ and $j+1$.  
- $\Delta_jY\equiv Y_{j+1}(x)-Y_j(x)$: local transverse spacing.  
- $\langle\cdot\rangle$: ensemble expectation over stripe fluctuations.  
- $J_{\mathrm{eff}}$: fluctuation-averaged (effective) Josephson coupling.  
- $J_0,\alpha$: parameters in the exponential parametrization.

title: Transverse/arc-length fluctuations cannot increase the magnitude of CDW inter-stripe coupling; they produce dephasing suppression
content:
Consider the inter-stripe CDW coupling term on stripes $j$ and $j+1$,
$$H_c \supset V(\Delta_jY)\cos\!\big[\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta_j\phi(x)\;-\;2k_F\Delta_jL(x)\big],$$
where $\Delta_jL(x)\equiv L_{j+1}(x)-L_j(x)$ and $\Delta_j\phi\equiv\phi_{j+1}-\phi_j$. Averaging over transverse/arc-length fluctuations yields an effective prefactor
$$V_{\mathrm{eff}}(j,x)=V(\Delta_jY)\,\big\langle\cos\!\big[\sqrt{2\pi}\,\Delta_j\phi-2k_F\Delta_jL\big]\big\rangle.$$
The magnitude of the fluctuation-averaged cosine factor satisfies
$$\big|\big\langle\cos[\cdots]\big\rangle\big|\le 1,$$
so transverse/arc-length fluctuations cannot increase the absolute amplitude of the CDW coupling and, when $\Delta_jL$ fluctuates, generically reduce (suppress) the effective CDW coupling relative to its instantaneous/static value.

extra.notations:
- $V(\Delta_jY)$: instantaneous CDW inter-stripe coupling prefactor depending on transverse spacing.  
- $\Delta_jL(x)=L_{j+1}(x)-L_j(x)$: arc-length mismatch.  
- $k_F$: Fermi wavevector.  
- $V_{\mathrm{eff}}$: fluctuation-averaged effective CDW coupling prefactor.

title: Transverse fluctuations tend to favor inter-stripe superconducting coherence over CDW ordering
content: Let $V_{\mathrm{eff}}$ denote the fluctuation-averaged amplitude of the inter-stripe CDW coupling and $J_{\mathrm{eff}}$ the fluctuation-averaged Josephson coupling between stripes. Because CDW inter-stripe coupling contains a $2k_F\Delta L$ phase factor, transverse/arc-length fluctuations generically suppress $|V_{\mathrm{eff}}|$ (dephasing). In contrast, Josephson coupling is a $k=0$ order and its effective value is $J_{\mathrm{eff}}=\langle J(\Delta_jY)\rangle$, which does not suffer dephasing from $\Delta_jL$ and, for an exponential $J(\Delta)=J_0 e^{-\alpha\Delta}$, may even be enhanced relative to $J_0 e^{-\alpha\langle\Delta\rangle}$ by variance (convexity). Therefore, in parameter regimes where transverse-position fluctuations are significant, the balance of inter-stripe couplings can shift in favor of superconducting inter-stripe coherence relative to CDW order.

extra.notations:
- $V_{\mathrm{eff}}$: fluctuation-averaged CDW inter-stripe coupling amplitude.  
- $J_{\mathrm{eff}}$: fluctuation-averaged Josephson coupling amplitude.  
- Other symbols as defined previously ($\Delta_jY,\Delta_jL,k_F,J_0,\alpha$).

title: Gaussian-fluctuation formulas: explicit Debye–Waller factors for $J_{\mathrm{eff}}$ and $V_{\mathrm{eff}}$
content:
Under the additional (common) assumption that the fluctuations of $\Delta_jY$ and $\Delta_jL$ are Gaussian with means $\mu_Y\!=\!\langle\Delta_jY\rangle,\ \mu_L\!=\!\langle\Delta_jL\rangle$ and variances $\sigma_Y^2=\mathrm{Var}(\Delta_jY),\ \sigma_L^2=\mathrm{Var}(\Delta_jL)$ (and independent of higher cumulants), the fluctuation-averaged couplings take closed forms:
- For an exponential Josephson matrix element $J(\Delta)=J_0 e^{-\alpha\Delta}$,
  $$J_{\mathrm{eff}}=J_0\exp\!\Big(-\alpha\mu_Y+\tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2\sigma_Y^2\Big).$$
- For the CDW coupling with phase $-2k_F\Delta_jL$, the ensemble average of the cosine factor yields a Debye–Waller factor,
  $$\big\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\Delta\phi-2k_F\Delta_jL]\big\rangle
  =\cos\!\big[\sqrt{2\pi}\langle\Delta\phi\rangle-2k_F\mu_L\big]\,
  \exp\!\big(-2k_F^2\sigma_L^2\big).$$
Thus under Gaussian statistics $J_{\mathrm{eff}}$ acquires a variance-induced multiplicative enhancement factor $e^{\frac12\alpha^2\sigma_Y^2}$ relative to $J_0 e^{-\alpha\mu_Y}$, while $V_{\mathrm{eff}}$ is multiplicatively suppressed by $e^{-2k_F^2\sigma_L^2}$.

extra.notations:
- $\mu_Y,\mu_L$: means of $\Delta_jY,\Delta_jL$.  
- $\sigma_Y^2,\sigma_L^2$: variances of $\Delta_jY,\Delta_jL$.  
- Other symbols as previously defined.

title: Existence of syntheses targeting SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} with x = 0.15
content: Given the sibling propositions, at least one reported synthesis targets the nominal composition SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} with the fluorine substitution fraction set to $x=0.15$. Here Sm = samarium, Fe = iron, As = arsenic, O = oxygen, F = fluorine, and $x$ is the intended fraction of O sites substituted by F.

extra.notations:
- $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$: nominal composition with fluorine substituting oxygen sites  
- $x$: intended fluorine substitution fraction (dimensionless, between 0 and 1)

title: Likely discrepancy between nominal $x=0.15$ and actual fluorine occupancy
content: Although the syntheses are reported with a nominal fluorine substitution $x=0.15$ in SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$, it is likely (probability ~0.80) that the actual fluorine occupancy on the oxygen sublattice is lower than the nominal value after typical solid-state synthesis. Therefore, structural or physical-property data reported for a sample labelled as $x=0.15$ should be interpreted as for a nominal composition unless direct compositional analysis (e.g., neutron/ X-ray diffraction refinement with site occupancies, electron probe microanalysis, or nuclear reaction analysis) confirms the true F content.

extra.notations:
- "nominal $x$": the targeted substitution fraction declared for the starting composition/recipe  
- "actual fluorine occupancy": the real fraction of O sites occupied by F in the synthesized material (may differ from nominal $x$)

title: Broad DHO component is temperature independent after Bose-factor correction
content: If the measured intensity of the broad nuclear component follows the Bose population factor $(1-\exp(-\hbar\omega/kT))^{-1}$, then dividing the measured dynamical structure factor $S(\vec{Q},\hbar\omega)$ by that Bose factor removes the explicit temperature dependence. Therefore the Bose-factor–corrected spectral weight of the broad damped harmonic-oscillator (DHO) component is temperature independent over the measured temperature range.

extra.notations:
- $S(\vec{Q},\hbar\omega)$: measured dynamical structure factor at momentum transfer $\vec{Q}$ and energy transfer $\hbar\omega$.  
- $k$: Boltzmann constant.  
- $T$: absolute temperature.  
- Bose factor: $(1-\exp(-\hbar\omega/kT))^{-1}$.

title: Broad DHO is strongly damped (overdamped) because $\Gamma_{\omega}>\hbar\omega$
content: Given the DHO parameterization with characteristic energy $\hbar\omega = 21.5\ \text{meV}$ and linewidth (damping) $\Gamma_{\omega} = 26\ \text{meV}$, the linewidth exceeds the mode energy ($\Gamma_{\omega}>\hbar\omega$). Therefore the oscillator is in the strongly damped (overdamped) regime rather than a narrow underdamped resonance: the spectral response is broad and lacks a sharp, well-resolved peak at $\hbar\omega$.

extra.notations:
- $\hbar\omega$: characteristic energy of the oscillator (in meV).  
- $\Gamma_{\omega}$: full linewidth / damping parameter of the oscillator (in meV).

title: Broad DHO component is phonon-like and likely arises from oxygen vibrational fluctuations in Cu(1) planes
content: The broad, Bose-factor–obeying nuclear spectral response parameterized by a DHO with $\hbar\omega\approx 21.5\ \text{meV}$ and $\Gamma_{\omega}\approx 26\ \text{meV}$ is plausibly a phononic (lattice vibration) excitation. In particular, the combination of Bose-population scaling (characteristic of bosonic lattice excitations) and the explicit attribution in one sibling to "large fluctuations of oxygen atoms in Cu(1) planes" makes it likely that this broad DHO reflects oxygen-related vibrational dynamics in the Cu(1) planes rather than a magnetic excitation.

extra.notations:
- Phonon-like: an excitation whose occupation follows the Bose factor and which originates from lattice vibrations (nuclear scattering).  
- Cu(1): copper sites in the chain/plane environment (notation as used in the siblings).

title: Localized ~20 meV feature likely corresponds to a local/defect vibrational mode or weakly coupled phonon
content: The localized spectral contribution centered near $20\ \text{meV}$ whose intensity decreases slightly with increasing temperature is plausibly a localized vibrational (nuclear) mode — for example, a defect- or impurity-related local phonon, an oxygen-vacancy mode, or a weakly dispersing optical phonon associated with a specific site (e.g., Cu(2) or nearby oxygens). The small temperature dependence suggests either modest thermal population effects or weak coupling to electronic degrees of freedom.

extra.notations:
- Localized spectral contribution: a feature confined in energy and weakly dispersive in momentum, centered near $20\ \text{meV}$.  
- Cu(2): copper sites in the superconducting CuO2 planes.

title: Pseudogap in underdoped Bi2212 corresponds to a nonzero pairing amplitude above Tc
content:
Given the symbols: $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$ is the pairing (superconducting or pseudogap) amplitude; $T_c$ is the superconducting critical temperature; $T^{*}$ is the temperature where the pseudogap closes. The combination of the empirical statement that the energy gap remains smooth across $T_c$ and vanishes only at $T^{*}$ (hence does not open at $T_c$) together with the self-energy model which represents a pseudogap as arising from a nonzero $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$ (possibly broadened by lifetime effects) implies that, in underdoped Bi2212, the ARPES pseudogap for temperatures in the range $T_c < T < T^{*}$ is associated with a nonzero pairing amplitude:
$$\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)>0\quad\text{for }T_c<T<T^{*}.$$
Thus the pseudogap observed in ARPES is consistent with (and in the model is produced by) a finite pairing amplitude above $T_c$.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$: pairing (superconducting or pseudogap) amplitude as a function of momentum $\mathbf{k}$ and temperature $T$. 
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature. 
- $T^{*}$: temperature at which the pseudogap closes (Fermi surface recovered).

title: Emergence of superconducting coherence at Tc requires a reduction of spectral-broadening parameters
content:
Using the self-energy parametrization
$$\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -\,i\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)\;+\;\dfrac{\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)^2}{\omega+\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}+i\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)},$$
where $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$ is the single-particle scattering rate (broadens quasiparticle peaks) and $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$ is the inverse pair lifetime (broadens the pair pole and causes filling): the observed sharpening of spectral linewidths at $T_c$ (broad features in the pseudogap state become resolution-limited peaks in the superconducting state) necessarily implies that at least one broadening parameter decreases upon cooling through $T_c$. Concretely, for relevant $\mathbf{k}$:
$$\text{At }T\downarrow T_c:\quad \Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)\text{ and/or }\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)\ \text{decreases significantly,}$$
while $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$ remains smooth across $T_c$ (per Proposition 1282).

extra.notations:
- $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$: single-particle scattering rate (broadens quasiparticle peaks).
- $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$: inverse pair lifetime (pair-pole broadening; controls "filling" of suppressed spectral weight).
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$: pairing amplitude.

title: Superconducting transition predominantly reflects a collapse of the inverse pair-lifetime ($\Gamma_0$) — i.e., onset of long-lived pairs (phase coherence)
content:
Define $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$ as the inverse pair lifetime (pair-pole broadening). A plausible interpretation of the empirical statement that the superconducting transition is "not an opening of an energy gap but the emergence of phase coherence" together with the self-energy model is that the dominant change at $T_c$ is a rapid decrease of $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$ (pairs become long-lived), producing a sharp pair pole and coherent quasiparticle peaks, while the pairing amplitude $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$ remains nonzero across $T_c$ and only goes to zero at $T^{*}$. In other words:
$$\text{For }T\downarrow T_c:\quad \Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)\to \text{(small)}\quad\text{(dominant)}\,,\quad \Delta(\mathbf{k},T)\approx\text{continuous}.$$
This conjecture interprets "phase coherence" as the formation of long-lived pairs (small $\Gamma_0$), rather than primarily a reduction of single-particle scattering $\Gamma_1$.

extra.notations:
- $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$: inverse pair lifetime (pair-pole broadening).
- $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$: single-particle scattering rate.
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$: pairing amplitude.

title: Oxygen annealing is necessary to realize the Hg-1223 133 K onset
content: Given the two siblings: (1) the superconducting onset at approximately $133\\,$K is attributed to the Hg-1223 ($HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$) phase, and (2) post-synthesis annealing in flowing O$_{2}$ at $300^{\\circ}$C is required to raise the system's maximum $T_{c}$ from the as-sintered value $\\sim117\\,$K to $\\sim133\\,$K, it follows necessarily that observing the $\\sim133\\,$K onset associated with Hg-1223 requires the post-synthesis oxygen annealing treatment (or an equivalent oxygenation procedure). In other words, the Hg-1223 phase only exhibits the $\\sim133\\,$K transition when the sample has been oxygen-annealed as specified.

extra.notations:
- $Hg$-1223: $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ (mercury cuprate, $n=3$). 
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (onset).
- as-sintered: sample state immediately after sintering, prior to the reported O$_{2}$ anneal.

title: Oxygen annealing likely optimizes Hg-1223 hole doping and/or phase fraction
content: It is plausible (probability ~0.80) that the mechanism by which post-synthesis O$_{2}$ annealing at $300^{\\circ}$C increases the observed $T_{c}$ from $\\sim117\\,$K to $\\sim133\\,$K is one or both of the following for the Hg-1223 phase: (A) increasing the oxygen stoichiometry $x$ (thereby raising the hole concentration in the CuO$_{2}$ planes toward optimal doping), and/or (B) increasing the superconducting volume fraction or crystallographic quality of the Hg-1223 phase (e.g., by promoting phase formation or healing defects). Either effect (or their combination) would raise the transition temperature and the field-cooling susceptibility attributed to Hg-1223.

extra.notations:
- $x$: oxygen non-stoichiometry in $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$.
- "hole concentration": number of mobile holes per Cu in the CuO$_{2}$ planes (doping level).
- superconducting volume fraction: fraction of sample volume that is superconducting/phase-pure.

title: Spectral gap is broadened/fills in at $T^{*}$ (no sharp coherence peaks)
content: At the pseudogap onset temperature $T^{*}$ the characteristic gap energy $ \Delta(T^{*}) $ is comparable to the pair-decay broadening $ \Gamma_{0}(T^{*}) $, $ \Delta(T^{*}) \approx \Gamma_{0}(T^{*}) $. Under the usual relation between lifetime broadening and spectral lineshape (where a quasiparticle or pair lifetime $ \tau $ produces an energy broadening $ \Gamma_{0}\sim\hbar/\tau $), a broadening of order the gap energy implies that any coherence peaks or well-resolved Bogoliubov-like features associated with a long-lived paired state are washed out. Therefore ARPES spectra at $T^{*}$ will not display sharp superconducting-like coherence peaks; instead the gap region is expected to be broadened or to be filled in by spectral weight, yielding a suppressed but nonzero low-energy spectral weight rather than sharp gapped quasiparticle peaks.

extra.notations:
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap crossover temperature (above $T_{c}$). 
- $ \Delta(T) $: magnitude of the momentum-dependent gap at temperature $T$. 
- $ \Gamma_{0}(T) $: pair-decay broadening (inverse pair lifetime expressed as an energy).
- $ \tau $: pair lifetime (related by $ \Gamma_{0}\sim\hbar/\tau $ under standard units).

title: Pseudogap is consistent with short-lived (preformed) Cooper pairing
content: The pseudogap observed below $T^{*}$ is plausibly produced by short-lived Cooper-pair correlations (preformed pairs) without long-range phase coherence: the pseudogap shares the same d-wave momentum structure as the superconducting gap, and at $T^{*}$ the gap magnitude satisfies $ \Delta(T^{*}) \approx \Gamma_{0}(T^{*}) $, indicating that pair decay occurs on the same energy/time scale as the gap. This scenario implies finite local pair amplitude (hence a suppression of single-particle spectral weight with the superconducting momentum form) but insufficient pair lifetime or phase stiffness to establish superconducting coherence.

extra.notations:
- $ \Delta(k,T) $: momentum-dependent gap (d-wave form as in proposition 1265). 
- $ \Gamma_{0}(T) $: energy scale of pair decay (inverse lifetime). 
- "Preformed pairs": short-lived bound electron pairs lacking global phase coherence.

title: Origin of Fermi arcs via momentum-dependent competition between $ \Delta(k) $ and $ \Gamma_{0} $
content: Let $ \Delta(k,T) $ denote the momentum-dependent pseudogap with d-wave form (so $ \Delta(k,T)\rightarrow 0 $ along the nodal directions where $ \cos k_{x}=\cos k_{y} $). At $T^{*}$, because $ \Delta(T^{*})\approx\Gamma_{0}(T^{*}) $ as a characteristic scale, momenta for which the local gap $ \Delta(k,T^{*}) \lesssim \Gamma_{0}(T^{*}) $ will not show a resolved gap in ARPES (spectral weight fills in), whereas momenta with $ \Delta(k,T^{*}) \gg \Gamma_{0}(T^{*}) $ will retain a pronounced suppression. Consequently, the Fermi surface will appear gapless (an arc) near the nodal region where $ \Delta(k) $ is small, while antinodal regions remain gapped—providing a natural mechanism for the emergence of Fermi arcs in the pseudogap regime.

extra.notations:
- $ \Delta(k,T) $: momentum-dependent pseudogap magnitude (d-wave form implied by proposition 1265).
- $ \Gamma_{0}(T) $: pair-decay broadening at temperature $T$ (energy units).
- "Fermi arc": segment of the nominal Fermi surface where low-energy spectral weight is restored (gapless or weakly gapped).

title: Computed conventional-cell volume, Fe–Fe in-plane spacing, and vertical layer separations for undoped LaFeAsO; volume change on 5 at% F doping
content:
Using the tetragonal lattice parameters $a$ and $c$ and the internal coordinates $z_{La}$ and $z_{As}$ reported in the siblings, the following numerical structural quantities follow directly.

- Conventional tetragonal cell volume: $V = a^{2}c$. For the undoped cell ($a=4.03552\ \text{\AA}$, $c=8.7393\ \text{\AA}$) $V \approx 142.32\ \text{\AA}^{3}$.
- Nearest-neighbor in-plane Fe–Fe distance on the square Fe sublattice (effective lattice rotated by $45^\circ$): $d_{\mathrm{Fe-Fe}} = a/\sqrt{2} \approx 2.854\ \text{\AA}$ (using $a=4.03552\ \text{\AA}$).
- Vertical distance between the Fe plane (conventional Fe $z$ position $=1/2$ in the standard $P4/nmm$ setting) and the As atom: $h_{\mathrm{As}} = |z_{As}-\tfrac{1}{2}|\;c = (0.6512-0.5)\times 8.7393\ \text{\AA} \approx 1.32\ \text{\AA}$.
- Vertical distance between the O plane (conventional O $z=0$) and the La atom: $h_{\mathrm{La-O}} = z_{La}\;c = 0.1415\times 8.7393\ \text{\AA} \approx 1.24\ \text{\AA}$.
- For the 5 at% F-doped sample reported ($a=4.0320\ \text{\AA}$, $c=8.7263\ \text{\AA}$) the conventional cell volume is $V_{\mathrm{5\%F}}\approx 141.86\ \text{\AA}^{3}$. The volume change on 5 at% F substitution is
  $$\frac{\Delta V}{V}=\frac{V_{\mathrm{5\%F}}-V_{\mathrm{undoped}}}{V_{\mathrm{undoped}}}\approx -0.32\%\,. $$

All numerical values above follow from the quoted lattice parameters and internal coordinates together with the standard $P4/nmm$ conventional-cell conventions (Fe at $z=1/2$, O at $z=0$).

extra.notations:
- $a$: tetragonal in-plane lattice constant (conventional cell), in \AA.
- $c$: tetragonal out-of-plane lattice constant (conventional cell), in \AA.
- $V$: conventional-cell volume, $V=a^{2}c$.
- $z_{La}, z_{As}$: fractional $c$-axis coordinates of La and As respectively.
- $d_{\mathrm{Fe-Fe}}$: nearest-neighbor Fe–Fe in-plane distance.
- $h_{\mathrm{As}}$: vertical As–Fe-plane distance.
- $h_{\mathrm{La-O}}$: vertical La–O separation.

title: Small-volume contraction from 5 at% F likely acts as modest chemical pressure altering Fe–As hybridization
content: The reported 5 at% F substitution reduces the conventional cell volume by approximately $0.32\%$. If the internal coordinate $z_{As}$ changes little on this level of doping (a common approximation for small substitutions), the As height above the Fe plane $h_{\mathrm{As}}$ will scale roughly with $c$ and decrease by a similar fraction (order $10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}\ \text{\AA}$, here $\sim 0.004\ \text{\AA}$). Such a reduction constitutes a small chemical-pressure-like perturbation that plausibly increases Fe–As orbital overlap (hybridization) and electronic bandwidth, and therefore can shift the balance between magnetism and superconductivity in LaFeAsO-derived compounds.

extra.notations:
- Chemical pressure: effective effect on electronic structure produced by volume (lattice parameter) changes due to substitution, treated qualitatively like applied hydrostatic pressure.
- $h_{\mathrm{As}}$: As height above the Fe plane (defined in the deduction result).

title: Induced spin gap grows with effective pair-hopping
content: Both sibling propositions imply that the induced spin gap in the 1DEG, denoted $\Delta_s$, is an increasing function of the effective pair-hopping strength ($J_{sp}$ or the renormalized $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$) when other parameters are held fixed. In Proposition 618 $\Delta_s\propto (\mathcal{J}_{sp})^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha>0$, and in Proposition 645 $\Delta_s(0)\propto J_{sp}^2/\varepsilon^*$ (within the stated regime), which is an increasing function of $J_{sp}$. Therefore, irrespective of the detailed regime, strengthening the effective pair-hopping coupling increases the induced spin gap.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta_s$: Induced spin gap in the 1DEG (zero-temperature unless otherwise specified). 
- $J_{sp}$, $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: Bare and renormalized (effective) pair-hopping strengths between stripe and environment.
- $\varepsilon^*$: Renormalized charge-transfer energy.
- $\alpha$: Positive exponent (here $\alpha=2/(4-K_s)$).

title: Crossover between mean-field inverse-$\varepsilon^*$ scaling and decoupling power-law scaling of $\Delta_s$
content:
There exists a crossover charge-transfer scale $\varepsilon^*_c$ separating two regimes for the zero-temperature induced spin gap $\Delta_s(0)$ as a function of $\varepsilon^*$ at fixed effective pair-hopping: (i) a mean-field regime at intermediate small $\varepsilon^*$ where $\Delta_s(0)\approx C_{MF}\,J_{sp}^2/\varepsilon^*$ (Proposition 645), and (ii) a decoupling/strong-coupling regime approaching $\varepsilon^*\to 0$ where $\Delta_s(0)\approx C_{dec}\,(\mathcal{J}_{sp})^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha=2/(4-K_s)$ (Proposition 618). The crossover scale is of order
      $$\varepsilon^*_c \sim \frac{C_{MF}}{C_{dec}}\;J_{sp}^{\,2-\alpha},$$
    where $C_{MF}$ and $C_{dec}$ are nonuniversal prefactors set by velocities, cutoffs and expectation values (e.g. $v_s/a$, factors of $a$, and $\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\tilde\phi_s]\rangle$). For $\varepsilon^*>\varepsilon^*_c$ the mean-field inverse-$\varepsilon^*$ behavior is expected to hold; for $\varepsilon^*<\varepsilon^*_c$ the decoupling power-law (bounded) behavior controls $\Delta_s(0)$. Consequently, as $\varepsilon^*$ is reduced from values ≳$J_{sp}$ toward zero, $\Delta_s(0)$ will crossover from the mean-field form and approach the finite power-law value rather than diverging.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta_s(0)$: Zero-temperature induced spin gap in the 1DEG.
- $J_{sp},\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: Bare and renormalized pair-hopping couplings.
- $\varepsilon^*$, $\varepsilon^*_c$: Renormalized charge-transfer energy and the conjectured crossover scale.
- $\alpha$: Exponent $2/(4-K_s)$ (positive for physical $K_s<4$).
- $C_{MF},C_{dec}$: Nonuniversal prefactors from the two asymptotic formulas.

title: Negative linear dependence of average gap on impurity-state density
content:
The average superconducting gap magnitude $\bar{\Delta}$ is a decreasing linear function of the spatial density $n$ of the identified impurity-state features (one feature per $CuO_2$ plaquette scale): specifically,
$$\bar{\Delta}\approx 75\ \text{meV} - 600\ \text{meV}\times n,$$
with the specified empirical fractional uncertainty of about $\pm 15\%$ on the slope. Thus increasing $n$ necessarily reduces $\bar{\Delta}$ (negative correlation) and the instantaneous slope $d\bar{\Delta}/dn$ is $-600\ \text{meV per (impurity per plaquette)}$ (within the reported uncertainty).

extra.notations:
- $\bar{\Delta}$: average superconducting gap magnitude (meV).
- $n$: spatial density of the impurity-state features per $CuO_2$ plaquette (dimensionless).

title: Approximate proportionality between hole density and impurity-state density
content:
The hole density per $CuO_2$ unit, $p$, is approximately proportional to the measured impurity-state density $n$ according to
$$p \approx 2\,n.$$
Here $p$ is the number of doped holes per $CuO_2$ unit and $n$ is the density of $-0.96$ eV impurity-state features per $CuO_2$ plaquette.

extra.notations:
- $p$: hole density (holes per $CuO_2$ unit).
- $n$: spatial density of the impurity-state features per $CuO_2$ plaquette.

title: Empirical expression of $\bar{\Delta}$ as a function of hole density
content:
Combining the empirical gap–density relation with the approximate proportionality $p\approx 2n$ yields an approximate relation between the average gap and hole density:
$$\bar{\Delta}\approx 75\ \text{meV} - 300\ \text{meV}\times p.$$
Here $p$ is the hole density per $CuO_2$ unit and $\bar{\Delta}$ is the average superconducting gap magnitude in meV.

extra.notations:
- $\bar{\Delta}$: average superconducting gap magnitude (meV).
- $p$: hole density (holes per $CuO_2$ unit).

title: Linear-extrapolation estimate for doping where the fitted average gap would vanish
content:
Extrapolating the linear empirical relation $\bar{\Delta}\approx75-600\,n$ meV to $\bar{\Delta}=0$ predicts a critical impurity-state density
$$n_{\mathrm{crit}}\approx \frac{75}{600}=0.125\ \text{(features per $CuO_2$ plaquette)},$$
which, using $p\approx2n$, corresponds to an estimated hole density
$$p_{\mathrm{crit}}\approx 0.25\ \text{holes per }CuO_2.$$
This is an extrapolated estimate of the doping level at which the fitted average gap would vanish under the linear model.

extra.notations:
- $n_{\mathrm{crit}}$: impurity-state density at which fitted $\bar{\Delta}=0$.
- $p_{\mathrm{crit}}$: corresponding hole density per $CuO_2$ unit.

title: Direct-absorption contribution vanishes in the DC limit if $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$ is nonsingular
content:
If the imaginary part of the polarizability at zero momentum, $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$, is finite (nonsingular) as $\omega\to 0$, then the direct absorption contribution
$$\sigma_{2}(\omega)\sim -\omega\,\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$$
obeys $\sigma_{2}(\omega)\to 0$ as $\omega\to 0$. Consequently the DC (zero-frequency) optical conductivity is entirely given by the Drude-like term,
$$\sigma(0)=\sigma_{1}(0)\,,$$
with $\sigma_{1}(\omega)\sim\operatorname{Im}\bigl[\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}/(\omega+i\Sigma(\omega))\bigr]$ evaluated at $\omega\to 0$.

extra.notations:
- $\omega$: frequency
- $\sigma(\omega)$: total complex optical conductivity
- $\sigma_{1}(\omega)$: Drude-like contribution (renormalized by the retarded one-particle self-energy)
- $\sigma_{2}(\omega)$: direct absorption contribution from polarizability excitations
- $\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$: polarizability at zero momentum
- $\Sigma(\omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy
- $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$: plasma frequency

title: DC Drude conductivity is inversely proportional to the low-frequency scattering rate
content:
Define the low-frequency quasiparticle scattering rate by $\Gamma\equiv -\operatorname{Im}\Sigma(0)>0$ (the imaginary part of the retarded self-energy at $\omega=0$). In the DC limit ($\omega\to 0$) the Drude-like contribution satisfies the proportionality
$$\sigma_{1}(0)\propto \frac{\omega_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}}{\Gamma}\,.$$
Thus, for a finite scattering rate $\Gamma$ the DC conductivity is finite and scales inversely with $\Gamma$; reductions of the scattering rate increase $\sigma_{1}(0)$ proportionally.

extra.notations:
- $\Gamma\equiv -\operatorname{Im}\Sigma(0)$: low-frequency quasiparticle scattering rate
- $\omega_{\mathrm{p}}$: plasma frequency
- $\sigma_{1}(0)$: DC value of the Drude-like conductivity
- $\Sigma(\omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy

title: Peaks in $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$ produce distinct finite-frequency absorption features in $\sigma(\omega)$
content:
If the polarizability at zero momentum, $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$, contains pronounced spectral peaks at finite frequencies $\omega_{0}>0$ (for example due to collective charge or spin excitations that couple at $q\approx 0$), then the direct absorption term
$$\sigma_{2}(\omega)\sim -\omega\,\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$$
will show corresponding absorption peaks near those $\omega_{0}$ (their amplitude weighted by the prefactor $\omega$). Hence experimentally observed finite-frequency peaks in $\sigma(\omega)$ that cannot be accounted for by the renormalized Drude term are plausibly attributable to such $q\approx 0$ polarizability excitations.

extra.notations:
- $\omega_{0}$: characteristic finite frequency of a polarizability peak
- $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$: imaginary part of the polarizability at zero momentum
- $\sigma_{2}(\omega)$: direct absorption contribution to optical conductivity

title: Optical conductivity necessarily encodes the one-particle self-energy but decomposition is non-unique
content:
Given the two siblings' identical decomposition
$$\sigma(\omega)=\sigma_1(\omega)+\sigma_2(\omega)$$
with
$$\sigma_1(\omega)\sim\operatorname{Im}\frac{\omega_p^2}{\omega+i\Sigma(\omega)}$$
and
$$\sigma_2(\omega)\sim-\omega\,\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega),$$
it follows as a logical necessity that (i) the frequency-dependent one-particle self-energy $\Sigma(\omega)$ enters the optical response through $\sigma_1(\omega)$, so measurements of $\sigma(\omega)$ contain information about $\Sigma(\omega)$; and (ii) because the observable $\sigma(\omega)$ is the sum of two distinct, frequency-dependent contributions ($\Sigma(\omega)$-dependent $\sigma_1$ and $\tilde{P}$-dependent $\sigma_2$), the decomposition of a measured $\sigma(\omega)$ into a unique $\Sigma(\omega)$ and a unique $\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$ is not determined by $\sigma(\omega)$ alone without further independent constraints or assumptions (for example, independent knowledge of $\omega_p$, symmetry or model constraints on $\tilde{P}$, separate probes of two-particle spectra, or assumptions that one term is negligible in a frequency range).

extra.notations:
- $\sigma(\omega)$: complex optical conductivity (frequency $\omega$). 
- $\sigma_1(\omega)$: Drude-like (one-particle) contribution. 
- $\sigma_2(\omega)$: direct absorption (two-particle/collective) contribution. 
- $\omega_p$: plasma frequency. 
- $\Sigma(\omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy (complex function of $\omega$). 
- $\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$: polarizability of specific density excitations at zero momentum.

title: Direct absorption term likely represents collective two-particle excitations producing finite-frequency spectral weight beyond the Drude peak
content: Interpreting $\sigma_2(\omega)\sim -\omega\,\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$ together with the statement that $\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ is the contribution of specific charge and spin density excitations, it is plausible that $\sigma_2(\omega)$ corresponds to absorption by collective (two-particle) excitations — e.g., particle–hole continua, spin fluctuations, or charge-density modes — and therefore produces spectral weight at finite frequencies distinct from the low-frequency coherent Drude response governed by $\Sigma(\omega)$. Consequently, experimentally observed mid-infrared (or other finite-frequency) absorption features that cannot be fitted by a single renormalized Drude form are likely attributable to a nonzero $\operatorname{Im}\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$.

extra.notations:
- $\sigma_2(\omega)$: direct absorption contribution associated with two-particle/collective polarizability. 
- $\tilde{P}(0,\omega)$: polarizability of the relevant excitations at momentum $\mathbf{q}=0$. 
- "Spectral weight": frequency-integrated contribution of a term to $\operatorname{Re}\sigma(\omega)$.

title: Zero-temperature spin gap parametrically exceeds pairing crossover temperature in the specified regime
content:
In the regime specified by Proposition 645, the zero-temperature spin gap on the stripe, $\Delta_s(0)$, and the pairing crossover temperature, $T_{pair}$, scale as
      $$
        T_{pair}\sim \varepsilon^*,\qquad \Delta_s(0)\sim \frac{[\mathcal{J}_{sp}]^2}{\varepsilon^*},
      $$
    where $\varepsilon^*$ is the renormalized charge-transfer energy and $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ the effective pair-tunneling coupling. Hence the dimensionless ratio
      $$
        \frac{\Delta_s(0)}{T_{pair}} \;=\; \frac{[\mathcal{J}_{sp}]^2}{\varepsilon^{*2}} \;=\; \left(\frac{\mathcal{J}_{sp}}{\varepsilon^*}\right)^2.
      $$
    Because the quoted regime requires $\varepsilon^*\ll\mathcal{J}_{sp}$, this ratio is parametrically large and therefore $\Delta_s(0)\gg T_{pair}$.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta_s(0)$: zero-temperature spin gap induced on the 1DEG (stripe). 
- $T_{pair}$: crossover temperature where the spin gap $\Delta_s(T)$ begins to develop.
- $\varepsilon^*$: renormalized charge transfer energy.
- $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: effective pair-tunneling coupling.

title: Charge-sector stiffness (Drude weight) remains set by bare charge velocity; pairing physics confined to spin sector
content: Under the decoupling/unitary-transformation limit described in Proposition 637, the charge sector of the 1DEG is a Gaussian Luttinger model with charge velocity $v_c$ and Luttinger parameter $K_c$ that are not renormalized by coupling to the environment. Consequently the zero-temperature Drude weight (superfluid phase stiffness) $D$ of the stripe is set by the bare charge parameters (scales as $D\propto v_c K_c$ in conventional bosonization units) and is not reduced by the spin-sector processes that generate the pairing scale.
