Combining this with Proposition 645, which places the pairing mechanism in the spin sector (spin-gap formation mediated by $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ and $\varepsilon^*$), implies that the "heavy" renormalizations are confined to spin degrees of freedom while the charge stiffness relevant for phase coherence remains of order the bare charge parameters and is independent (to leading order in the decoupling limit) of $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ and $\varepsilon^*$.

extra.notations:
- $v_c$: charge velocity of the 1DEG.
- $K_c$: Luttinger parameter for the charge sector.
- $D$: zero-temperature Drude weight / superfluid phase stiffness of the stripe.
- $\mathcal{J}_{sp},\varepsilon^*$: as above.

title: Crossover from a strong-gap (preformed-pair–like) regime to a BCS-like regime as $\varepsilon^*$ is increased toward $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$
content:
Using the scalings $T_{pair}\sim\varepsilon^*$ and $\Delta_s(0)\sim[\mathcal{J}_{sp}]^2/\varepsilon^*$ (Proposition 645), the ratio $\Delta_s(0)/T_{pair}=(\mathcal{J}_{sp}/\varepsilon^*)^2$ interpolates from $\gg 1$ for $\varepsilon^*\ll\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ to $\sim 1$ when $\varepsilon^*\sim\mathcal{J}_{sp}$. Combined with Proposition 637 (which keeps the charge stiffness large and unrenormalized), this suggests two distinct qualitative regimes: 
      - For $\varepsilon^*\ll\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: a strong-gap regime where the zero-temperature gap is parametrically larger than the crossover temperature (large $\Delta/T$), behavior reminiscent of preformed-pair or strong-coupling phenomenology. 
      - For $\varepsilon^*\sim\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: a regime where $\Delta_s(0)$ and $T_{pair}$ become comparable, i.e. more BCS-like behavior.
    Thus one plausibly expects a crossover in phenomenology (gap-to-onset ratio, temperature dependence of spectral gaps, etc.) as $\varepsilon^*$ is tuned through $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$.

extra.notations: - All symbols as defined above: $\Delta_s(0),T_{pair},\varepsilon^*,\mathcal{J}_{sp},v_c,K_c$.

title: Tc corresponds to onset of global phase coherence as seen in ARPES sharpening
content: Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on underdoped Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8−δ show that at the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ the low-energy single-particle spectral features become markedly sharper (linewidths narrow and sharper quasiparticle peaks appear). Both cited propositions identify this sharpening as coincident with the onset of superconducting phase coherence. Therefore, the superconducting transition at $T_{c}$ is experimentally identified primarily with the development of global phase coherence (long-lived, well-defined quasiparticle states), rather than with the sudden formation of a large new single-particle excitation gap at $T_{c}$.

extra.notations:
- ARPES: angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (measures single-particle spectral function vs momentum and energy).
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (onset of global phase coherence).
- spectral linewidth / quasiparticle peak: width/intensity of low-energy features in the ARPES single-particle spectrum; narrowing indicates increased lifetime/coherence.

title: Existence of a normal-state pseudogap persisting above Tc up to T*
content: The ARPES data reported in the siblings establish that underdoped Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8−δ exhibits a suppression of low-energy single-particle spectral weight (a "pseudogap") in the normal state for temperatures $T>T_{c}$, and that this suppression persists until a higher temperature $T^{*}$ where the suppression disappears (the Fermi surface is recovered). Hence there exists a temperature window $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$ in which the pseudogap is present.

extra.notations:
- pseudogap: suppression/depletion of low-energy single-particle spectral weight observed in ARPES for $T>T_{c}$.
- $T^{*}$: temperature at which the pseudogap closes / the normal-state Fermi surface is restored ($T^{*}>T_{c}$).

title: Transition at Tc is likely limited by phase stiffness (phase-fluctuation-driven Tc)
content: It is plausible that in underdoped Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8−δ the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ is set by the temperature at which global phase coherence of preexisting incoherent pairing correlations (or other gapped electronic order) is established, i.e., $T_{c}$ is limited by phase stiffness (phase fluctuations) rather than by the appearance of a pairing amplitude. Concretely: the pseudogap for $T>T_{c}$ reflects a nonzero single-particle gap or strong pairing-related correlations, while the abrupt ARPES spectral sharpening at $T_{c}$ marks the locking of the phase of those correlations across the sample.

extra.notations:
- phase stiffness / phase fluctuations: the energy scale controlling stability of long-range phase coherence of the superconducting order parameter.
- pairing amplitude: magnitude of the superconducting order parameter (pairing gap) as a single-particle energy scale.

title: Stoichiometric amounts of FeF$_2$ and Fe$_2$O$_3$ required per nominal formula unit
content:
Given the nominal target composition SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$ with $x=0.15$ (i.e. 0.15 F atoms per formula unit) and the listed starting reagents which include iron(II) fluoride FeF$_2$ as the fluorine-bearing reagent and iron(III) oxide Fe$_2$O$_3$ as an oxygen-bearing reagent, the stoichiometric amounts (moles of reagent per mole of nominal target compound) required if those reagents are taken as the sole sources of F and O are:
- FeF$_2$: $x/2 = 0.15/2 = 0.075$ mol FeF$_2$ per mol SmFeAsO$_{0.85}$F$_{0.15}$ (because each FeF$_2$ supplies 2 F atoms).
- Fe$_2$O$_3$: $(1-x)/3 = 0.85/3 \approx 0.28333$ mol Fe$_2$O$_3$ per mol SmFeAsO$_{0.85}$F$_{0.15}$ (because each Fe$_2$O$_3$ supplies 3 O atoms).
These numerical relations follow directly from atomic counts in the reagent formulas and the nominal target formula.

extra.notations:
- $x$: nominal fluorine fraction in SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_x$ (here $x=0.15$)
- FeF$_2$: iron(II) fluoride, supplies 2 fluorine atoms per mole
- Fe$_2$O$_3$: iron(III) oxide, supplies 3 oxygen atoms per mole
- "per mol SmFeAsO$_{0.85}$F$_{0.15}$": per mole of the nominal target formula unit

title: High reagent purity makes impurity-driven shifts of nominal $x$ negligible
content: The reagents are reported at purities >99.99%, i.e. impurity fraction <1.0e-4 (0.01%) by amount in each reagent. If FeF$_2$ is used as the intended sole fluorine source at the stoichiometric level of 0.075 mol FeF$_2$ per mol target (see deduction above), a maximum shortfall in supplied fluorine due to reagent impurity would be on the order of $1\times10^{-4}\times0.15\,$mol F = $1.5\times10^{-5}$ mol F per mol target, corresponding to an absolute change in $x$ of $\sim1.5\times10^{-5}$ (i.e. a relative change $\sim10^{-4}$ of $x$). Therefore, under typical stoichiometric mixing, the reported reagent purities imply that impurity-related uncertainty in the nominal fluorine content $x=0.15$ is negligibly small (much smaller than 1% of $x$).

extra.notations:
- Purity >99.99%: reagent impurity fraction < $1.0\times10^{-4}$
- $x$: nominal fluorine fraction in SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_x$ ($x=0.15$)
- "shortfall in supplied fluorine": reduction in mol F actually provided relative to ideal stoichiometry

title: Observed superconducting onsets lie in the ≈110–120 K interval; some bulk samples reach zero resistance above 100 K
content: Let $T_c$ denote the superconducting transition (critical) temperature. "Onset $T_c$" means the temperature where superconducting behavior first appears (e.g., first deviation in resistivity or flux exclusion), and "zero resistance" means measured electrical resistance falls below the experimental detection limit. Propositions 73 and 1411 report superconducting onsets of 120 K (bulk resistance criterion) and ≈110 K (single-crystal flux-exclusion criterion), respectively, and Proposition 73 reports that some bulk samples achieve zero resistance above 100 K. Therefore, it is necessarily true that members of the Tl–Ba–Ca–Cu–O family have reported superconducting onsets within the approximate range 110–120 K, and that some bulk samples in this family exhibit zero electrical resistance at temperatures >100 K.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature (onset defined above).
- "onset $T_c$": temperature where superconducting signal first appears by the specified measurement.
- "zero resistance": resistance below the measurement detection threshold.

title: Higher onset (≈120 K) in bulk samples plausibly arises from a minor higher-$T_c$ phase (e.g., Tl-2223) whose fraction or connectivity is enhanced by Ca and O2 annealing
content: Let Tl-2212 denote the phase Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (reported onset ≈110 K) and Tl-2223 denote Tl2Ba2Ca2Cu3O10 (a known higher-$T_c$ phase in Tl–Ba–Ca–Cu–O). The conjecture is: the bulk onset at ≈120 K reported in Proposition 73 is likely produced by a minority higher-$T_c$ phase (for example Tl-2223) or by improved inter-grain connectivity of such a phase within multi-phase bulk specimens; the presence of Ca stabilizes superconducting phases against annealing and annealing in O2 at 450°C (Proposition 88) promotes either the formation, growth, or oxygenation of the higher-$T_c$ phase or its connectivity, thereby producing or enhancing the ≈120 K onset while single-phase Tl-2212 crystals show the lower ≈110 K onset (Proposition 1411).

extra.notations:
- Tl-2212: Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 (2212 cation ratio; $T_c\approx110\,$K in Prop.1411).
- Tl-2223: Tl2Ba2Ca2Cu3O10 (2223 cation ratio; historically known as a higher-$T_c$ member of the family).
- Annealing at 450°C in flowing O2: a thermal/oxygenation processing step referenced in Prop.88.

title: Hole density per CuO2 equals twice the impurity-state density
content: Let p be the hole density (number of holes) per CuO2 unit and n the spatial density of the identified impurity states per CuO2 plaquette. Both siblings assert that each identified impurity state contributes approximately two holes to the CuO2 system. If each impurity supplies two holes, then the total holes per CuO2 unit equals 2 times the number of impurity states per CuO2 unit; hence p = 2 n.

extra.notations:
- p: hole density (holes per CuO2 unit)  
- n: spatial density of impurity states (impurity atoms) per CuO2 plaquette

title: STM -0.96 eV impurity state is the charged nonstoichiometric oxygen dopant (charge -2e)
content: The electronic impurity state observed at energy E = -0.96 eV and identified in STM/SI-STM images is the spectroscopic signature of the nonstoichiometric oxygen dopant atom introduced by δ. Combining the siblings' identification of those states as the oxygen dopants (each contributing ≈2 holes to the CuO2 plane) with the parent statement that each nonstoichiometric oxygen dopant carries electrostatic charge -2e implies the observed -0.96 eV state corresponds to a charged oxygen dopant with charge -2e.

extra.notations:
- E = -0.96 eV: energy of the observed impurity-state peak relative to the Fermi level  
- "impurity state": the localized electronic resonance detected by STM attributed to the dopant  
- -2e: electrostatic charge of each nonstoichiometric oxygen dopant (elementary charges)

title: Local Coulomb potential of -2e dopants plausibly drives gap inhomogeneity
content: Conjecture: The empirical relation \bar{\Delta} ≈ 75 − 600 (±15%) × n (meV) arises because the electrostatic (Coulomb) potential produced by the charged -2e oxygen dopants locally modifies carrier density and pairing interactions in the CuO2 planes. Regions with higher local impurity density n(r) produce a larger local Coulomb potential, reducing local pairing strength and hence the local superconducting gap magnitude Δ(r), leading to the observed decrease of the average gap \bar{\Delta} with increasing n.

extra.notations:
- n(r): local density of impurity states per CuO2 plaquette at position r  
- Δ(r): local superconducting gap magnitude at r  
- \bar{\Delta}: spatial average of Δ(r)

title: Intersheet Tl–O bond length ≈ 2.0 Å in the cited Tl cuprates
content: The intersheet thallium–oxygen (Tl–O) bond length in the cited thallium-containing layered cuprates is on the order of $2.0\ \AA$. Specifically, Proposition 487 gives a measured Tl–O(2) bond length $1.995(9)\ \AA$ in $Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CuO_{6}$ and Proposition 1400 reports a $2.0\ \AA$ Tl–O intersheet bond length in $Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8}$. Therefore, for these compounds the intersheet Tl–O linkage length is $ \approx 2.0\ \AA$ (i.e., within experimental uncertainty of $2.0\ \AA$).

extra.notations:
- Tl–O bond length: distance between a thallium atom in one Tl–O sheet and an oxygen atom in the adjacent Tl–O sheet, in angstroms ($\AA$).
- $1.995(9)\ \AA$: measured value with standard uncertainty in the last digits (i.e., $1.995\ \AA \pm 0.009\ \AA$).

title: Short Tl–O intersheet distances imply chemical interlayer bonding and non-negligible c-axis coupling
content: The intersheet Tl–O distance of approximately $2.0\ \AA$ is characteristic of a chemical Tl–O bond (as opposed to a purely van-der-Waals interlayer gap). Therefore it is plausible that adjacent Tl–O sheets are chemically bonded, producing appreciable structural and electronic coupling along the crystallographic $c$-axis in these Tl-based cuprates. Concretely, this suggests (i) the interlayer interaction is not purely weak van-der-Waals contact, (ii) interlayer charge transfer or hybridization involving Tl and O orbitals can be significant, and (iii) these materials may show reduced electronic anisotropy (smaller in-plane vs. out-of-plane property ratios) than comparable cuprates whose intersheet separations are substantially larger.

extra.notations:
- $c$-axis: crystallographic axis normal to the CuO$_2$ planes along which Tl–O sheets stack.
- "Chemical bond": interatomic distance comparable to established single-bond lengths in oxides (order $1.8$–$2.2\ \AA$ for Tl–O), implying orbital overlap beyond dispersion forces.
- "Electronic anisotropy": ratio of in-plane to out-of-plane electronic/transport properties (e.g., resistivity anisotropy).

title: Measured inelastic-neutron intensity is a superposition of magnetic and nuclear contributions
content:
For a given momentum transfer q and energy transfer $\hbar\omega$ (e.g. $\hbar\omega\approx 41\,$meV), the total inelastic-neutron scattering intensity $I_{\rm tot}(q,\hbar\omega)$ measured without spin analysis equals the sum of at least a magnetic (spin) contribution $I_{\rm mag}(q,\hbar\omega)$ and a nuclear (non-magnetic) contribution $I_{\rm nuc}(q,\hbar\omega)$, plus any instrumental/background term $I_{\rm bg}$:
$$I_{\rm tot}(q,\hbar\omega)=I_{\rm mag}(q,\hbar\omega)+I_{\rm nuc}(q,\hbar\omega)+I_{\rm bg}.$$
Therefore, when both a pronounced magnetic resonance at $\hbar\omega\approx 41\,$meV (Proposition 1508) and a measurable nuclear contribution (Proposition 1593) are present in YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6.92}$, the raw unpolarized INS spectrum at those $(q,\hbar\omega)$ values necessarily contains contributions from both channels and cannot be treated as purely magnetic without further separation.

extra.notations:
- $I_{\rm tot}(q,\hbar\omega)$: total measured inelastic-neutron scattering intensity at momentum transfer $q$ and energy transfer $\hbar\omega$. 
- $I_{\rm mag}(q,\hbar\omega)$: intensity arising from magnetic (spin) excitations. 
- $I_{\rm nuc}(q,\hbar\omega)$: intensity arising from nuclear (non-magnetic) scattering, e.g., phonons or other lattice-related processes.
- $I_{\rm bg}$: instrumental and other background contributions.

title: Unpolarized spectra near 41 meV may include a non-negligible nuclear (phonon) contribution
content: In YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6.92}$, the unpolarized inelastic-neutron spectrum observed near the reported magnetic resonance energy $\hbar\omega\approx 41\,$meV likely contains a non-negligible nuclear (e.g., phononic or multi-phonon) contribution $I_{\rm nuc}(q,\hbar\omega)$ at some measured $q$. Consequently, correct quantification of the magnetic-resonance spectral weight and lineshape from unpolarized data typically requires either spin-polarized neutron measurements, subtraction based on independent phonon measurements, or exploitation of differing temperature and momentum-transfer dependences of $I_{\rm mag}$ and $I_{\rm nuc}$.

extra.notations:
- $I_{\rm nuc}(q,\hbar\omega)$: nuclear (non-magnetic) contribution, possibly from phonons or multi-phonon scattering.
- "Non-negligible": large enough to affect inferred magnetic spectral weight or lineshape in typical unpolarized experiments.

title: Starting materials supply all elemental constituents of SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$
content: Given the listed starting materials (pre‑sintered SmAs, elemental As, elemental Fe, Fe$_{2}$O$_{3}$, FeF$_{2}$) the reagents collectively contain the elements samarium (Sm), iron (Fe), arsenic (As), oxygen (O) and fluorine (F) required for the nominal target composition SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$. Therefore the high‑pressure synthesis explicitly provides sources for every elemental species present in the nominal quaternary phase.

extra.notations:
- Sm: samarium (element)
- Fe: iron (element)
- As: arsenic (element)
- O: oxygen (element)
- F: fluorine (element)
- Fe$_{2}$O$_{3}$: iron(III) oxide (source of O and Fe)
- FeF$_{2}$: iron(II) fluoride (source of F and Fe)
- SmAs: samarium arsenide (source of Sm and As)

title: High‑pressure route and FeF$_{2}$ reagent are intended to supply and retain fluorine during synthesis
content: The inclusion of an iron fluoride reagent (FeF$_{2}$) together with a reported high‑pressure synthesis implies the intent to (a) introduce fluorine into the nominal SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$ composition via FeF$_{2}$ and (b) use high pressure to reduce fluorine loss (volatile F‑containing species) during the high‑temperature synthesis, so that the nominal substitution fraction $x$ is more closely approached than would be expected under ambient‑pressure routes.

extra.notations:
- $x$: nominal fluorine substitution fraction on the oxygen sites in SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$
- FeF$_{2}$: iron(II) fluoride, the fluorine‑containing reagent listed

title: Nominal fluorine fraction set by FeF2 amount (if FeF2 is sole F source)
content:
If iron(II) fluoride (FeF$_2$) is the only fluorine-containing reagent present in the starting mixture and no fluorine is lost during processing, then the nominal fluorine substitution fraction $x$ in SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$ equals the number of fluorine atoms supplied by FeF$_2$ per formula unit divided by the number of oxygen sites per formula unit. Quantitatively, letting $n_{\mathrm{FeF_2}}$ be the moles of FeF$_2$ added per mole of target SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$ product, the nominal $x$ is
$$
x = 2\,n_{\mathrm{FeF_2}}
$$
because each FeF$_2$ molecule contains two F atoms and the formula unit has one oxygen site that can be occupied by at most one F atom.

extra.notations:
- Sm, Fe, As, O, F: chemical elements samarium, iron, arsenic, oxygen, fluorine respectively.
- $x$: nominal fraction of oxygen sites substituted by fluorine in SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$.
- FeF$_2$: iron(II) fluoride reagent; $n_{\mathrm{FeF_2}}$: moles of FeF$_2$ per mole of target formula units.

title: Purpose of pre-sintered SmAs and excess As: control of As stoichiometry and volatility
content: The inclusion of pre-sintered SmAs powder together with elemental arsenic (As) as starting reagents indicates an intent to control the Sm:As stoichiometry in the product and to compensate for arsenic loss during high-temperature synthesis. Concretely, using pre-sintered SmAs reduces direct handling and high-temperature reactions of metallic Sm with As vapor and, when combined with additional elemental As, allows the preparer to apply an arsenic excess (or compensating As source) to offset As volatility and obtain a near 1:1 Sm:As ratio in the final SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$ material.

extra.notations:
- SmAs: pre-sintered samarium arsenide.
- As (elemental): elemental arsenic added as powder.
- SmFeAsO$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$: target quaternary phase; $x$ is defined as above.

title: Superconducting transition corresponds to a reduction in inverse pair lifetime, not to opening of a gap
content: The superconducting transition at $T_{c}$ in underdoped Bi2212 is driven by a decrease in the inverse pair lifetime $\Gamma_{0}$ (i.e., an increase of pair lifetime) rather than by an increase in the gap magnitude $\Delta$. Concretely: the ARPES-observed sharpening of spectral lines into resolution-limited coherence peaks below $T_{c}$ implies $\Gamma_{0}(T<T_{c})\ll\Delta(T\lesssim T_{c})$, while the statement that the gap magnitude is smooth across $T_{c}$ (vanishing only at $T^{*}>T_{c}$) means $\Delta$ does not change appreciably at $T_{c}$. Therefore the spectral-change at $T_{c}$ is necessarily caused by a change in $\Gamma_{0}$ rather than in $\Delta$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap closing temperature
- $\Delta(T)$: gap (pseudogap/superconducting gap) magnitude at temperature $T$
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime (spectral broadening) at temperature $T$

title: Pseudogap closing at T* coincides with pair lifetime becoming comparable to the gap
content: At the pseudogap temperature $T^{*}$ the gap magnitude and the inverse pair lifetime are approximately equal: $\Delta(T^{*})\approx\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$. Hence $T^{*}$ marks the temperature where the pair lifetime is marginal for producing a spectrally resolvable gap; for $T\gtrsim T^{*}$ the condition $\Gamma_{0}(T)\gtrsim\Delta(T)$ holds and the gap is no longer a well-resolved feature in ARPES.

extra.notations:
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap closing temperature
- $\Delta(T)$: gap magnitude at temperature $T$
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime at temperature $T$

title: Intermediate (preformed-pair) regime with finite gap but short pair lifetime between Tc and T*
content: In underdoped cuprates there exists an intermediate temperature regime $T_{c}<T<T^{*}$ in which a finite gap magnitude $\Delta>0$ persists (the pseudogap) while pair lifetimes are short ($\Gamma_{0}\gtrsim\Delta$), i.e., pairs are locally formed but lack long-range phase coherence. This regime produces broad, incoherent ARPES spectral features above $T_{c}$ and collapses at $T^{*}$ when $\Gamma_{0}$ becomes comparable to $\Delta$ and the gap is no longer resolvable.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap closing temperature
- $\Delta$: gap magnitude
- $\Gamma_{0}$: inverse pair lifetime

title: Oxygen deintercalation (apical O removal) is the cause of the c-axis contraction
content: The observed reduction of the $c$-axis lattice constant from about $3.77\ \AA$ (perovskite $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{3}$) to $3.37\ \AA$ (infinite-layer $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$) is a direct consequence of oxygen deintercalation that removes the apical oxygen atoms during the CaH$_2$ topotactic reduction. Here $c$ denotes the lattice vector perpendicular to the NiO$_2$ planes, and "apical oxygen" denotes the oxygen atoms that complete the NiO$_6$ octahedral coordination in the perovskite and occupy positions between NiO$_2$ planes and the rare-earth (Nd/Sr) layers.

extra.notations:
- $c$: lattice constant along the crystallographic axis perpendicular to NiO$_2$ planes (Angstroms, $\AA$)
- "apical oxygen": oxygen atoms occupying the positions axial to the NiO$_2$ planes in the perovskite (forming NiO$_6$ octahedra)

title: Low-temperature CaH$_2$ topotactic reduction conditions for single-crystal films
content: The perovskite single-crystal thin-film precursor $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{3}$ can be converted to the infinite-layer $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ phase by a low-temperature CaH$_2$ topotactic reduction performed in a vacuum-sealed environment at temperatures in the range $260^\circ\mathrm{C}$–$280^\circ\mathrm{C}$. This procedure effects oxygen deintercalation while preserving the film's single-crystal epitaxy on a SrTiO$_3$ substrate.

extra.notations:
- CaH$_2$: calcium hydride reducing agent
- $260^\circ\mathrm{C}$–$280^\circ\mathrm{C}$: temperature window of the reduction reaction
- "vacuum-sealed": reaction environment used to avoid external contamination/oxidation

title: Formal average Ni oxidation state in Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2
content: For the infinite-layer composition $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$, formal charge neutrality implies an average nickel oxidation state of $+1.2$. Explicitly, using formal ionic charges $Nd^{3+}$ and $Sr^{2+}$ and oxygen $O^{2-}$, the average Ni valence $v_{\mathrm{Ni}}$ satisfies $0.8\cdot(+3)+0.2\cdot(+2)+v_{\mathrm{Ni}}+2\cdot(-2)=0$, giving $v_{\mathrm{Ni}}=+1.2$.

extra.notations:
- $v_{\mathrm{Ni}}$: formal average oxidation state of Ni in the compound
- $Nd^{3+}$, $Sr^{2+}$, $O^{2-}$: formal ionic charges assumed for charge-counting

title: Epitaxial clamping likely preserves in-plane strain and alters NiO$_2$ electronic structure
content: Because the CaH$_2$ topotactic reduction is performed at low temperature ($260^\circ\mathrm{C}$–$280^\circ\mathrm{C}$) and explicitly "preserves epitaxial clamping" to the SrTiO$_3$ substrate, the resulting infinite-layer $Nd_{0.8}Sr_{0.2}NiO_{2}$ films are likely to retain the in-plane lattice parameters imposed by the substrate (biaxial strain) rather than relax to bulk lattice constants. This retained epitaxial strain will plausibly modify in-plane Ni–O bond lengths and Ni–O–Ni bond angles, thereby changing the Ni $d$-bandwidth and electronic correlations relative to an unstrained bulk sample.

extra.notations:
- "epitaxial clamping": the film's in-plane lattice parameters are constrained by the substrate
- "in-plane strain": biaxial strain within the film plane produced by lattice mismatch with SrTiO$_3$
- "bandwidth": energy spread of the Ni $d$-derived conduction band(s)

title: 41 meV magnetic resonance is observable by Q-scans across the antiferromagnetic ridge
content: Given that YBa2Cu3O6.92 exhibits a magnetic (spin) resonance at energy transfer $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 41\\,$meV (a magnetic feature in the dynamical response) and that the magnetic part of the neutron signal varies with momentum transfer $\\mathbf{Q}$ along the antiferromagnetic ridge at wave vectors of the form $(1/2,1/2,l)$ (r.l.u.) and can be separated from the more slowly varying nuclear background by performing $\\mathbf{Q}$-space scans, it follows that performing momentum scans across the ridge $(1/2,1/2,l)$ at fixed energy transfer $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 41\\,$meV will reveal the resonant magnetic peak as a magnetic intensity peak (distinct from nuclear/phonon background). In other words, the 41-meV resonance is accessible and separable experimentally by $\\mathbf{Q}$-scans across the antiferromagnetic ridge.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: Yttrium–barium–copper oxide with oxygen stoichiometry 6.92. 
- $\\hbar\\omega$: neutron energy transfer (reduced Planck's constant times angular frequency).
- $\\mathbf{Q}$: momentum-transfer (scattering) vector in reciprocal space.
- r.l.u.: reciprocal-lattice units.
- "magnetic resonance"/"resonant magnetic peak": pronounced feature in the magnetic part of the dynamical structure factor or Im$\\{\\chi(\\mathbf{Q},\\hbar\\omega)\\}$.

title: Resonance is centered at the in-plane antiferromagnetic wavevector and shows $\\ell$-modulation from bilayer coupling
content: It is plausible that the $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 41\\,$meV magnetic resonance in YBa2Cu3O6.92 is centered in momentum space at the in-plane antiferromagnetic wavevector $(h,k)=(1/2,1/2)$ (r.l.u.), i.e., the resonance intensity peaks at $\\mathbf{Q}=(1/2,1/2,l)$ for some values of $l$, and that its intensity varies with the out-of-plane index $l$ (modulation in $l$) due to the bilayer CuO2 structure of YBa2Cu3O6.92 (leading to stronger intensity for one symmetry channel in $l$). Thus, energy scans at fixed in-plane $\\mathbf{Q}=(1/2,1/2)$ and varying $l$, or $\\mathbf{Q}$-scans at fixed $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 41\\,$meV, will show a momentum-localized peak in the planar $(1/2,1/2)$ position and a characteristic $l$-dependence of its amplitude.

extra.notations:
- $(h,k,l)$: components of $\\mathbf{Q}$ in reciprocal-lattice units (r.l.u.); $(1/2,1/2,l)$ denotes the antiferromagnetic ridge in-plane coordinates $(1/2,1/2)$ with variable out-of-plane index $l$.
- "bilayer coupling": the coupling between the two CuO2 planes in the crystallographic unit cell of YBa2Cu3O6.92, which can produce $l$-modulation (odd/even symmetry) of magnetic excitations.

title: Multiple distinct fluorine-containing starting reagents are reported
content: At least two different fluorine-containing starting reagents have been reported in published syntheses of the fluorine-doped samarium oxyarsenide SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}: iron(II) fluoride (FeF_{2}) and samarium(III) fluoride (SmF_{3}). Here SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} denotes samarium iron oxyarsenide with fluorine substitution on the oxygen site; FeF_{2} and SmF_{3} denote the indicated fluoride compounds used as sources of F in the starting mixture.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}: samarium iron oxyarsenide doped with fluorine (oxygen site partially substituted by F). 
- FeF_{2}: iron(II) fluoride, reported as a starting reagent in at least one synthesis. 
- SmF_{3}: samarium(III) fluoride, reported as a starting reagent in at least one synthesis.

title: Common non-fluoride starting reagents across reports
content: Both reported syntheses include pre-sintered samarium arsenide (SmAs), elemental iron (Fe), and iron(III) oxide (Fe_{2}O_{3}) among their starting materials for producing SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}.

extra.notations:
- SmAs: samarium arsenide, a pre-sintered powder used as a starting reagent.
- Fe: elemental iron.
- Fe_{2}O_{3}: iron(III) oxide.
- SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}: samarium iron oxyarsenide doped with fluorine.

title: Different fluorine reagents correlate with different synthesis conditions
content: Published procedures suggest a plausible association between the choice of fluorine-containing reagent and the synthesis conditions: FeF_{2} is reported in contexts consistent with high-pressure synthesis of Sm[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs, whereas SmF_{3} is reported in a conventional evacuated-quartz-tube solid-state reaction at ambient pressure and high temperature. Thus the choice of fluoride source likely depends on the synthesis route (pressure/temperature and equipment) used to prepare SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}.

extra.notations:
- FeF_{2}: iron(II) fluoride, reported in starting mixtures associated with high-pressure syntheses in the parent generalization.
- SmF_{3}: samarium(III) fluoride, reported in a conventional evacuated-quartz-tube high-temperature synthesis.
- "Synthesis route": the combination of pressure, temperature, and apparatus used (e.g., high-pressure cell versus evacuated quartz tube).

title: Equivalence of the non-interacting five-orbital Hamiltonian and explicit parameter assignment
content:
The non-interacting tight-binding Hamiltonian $H_{0}$ written in Proposition 766 is the same mathematical object as the $H_{0}$ of Proposition 752 (same orbital basis, same unfolded-square-lattice convention and same hopping notation). Consequently, the on-site energies $\varepsilon_{\mu}$ appearing in Proposition 766 can be instantiated with the numeric values reported in Proposition 752, namely
$$ (\varepsilon_{1},\varepsilon_{2},\varepsilon_{3},\varepsilon_{4},\varepsilon_{5}) = (10.75,\;10.96,\;10.96,\;11.12,\;10.62)\ \text{eV}, $$
and the hopping integrals $t(\Delta x,\Delta y;\mu,\nu)$ in Proposition 766 are those computed and tabulated in Proposition 752 (Table I). Because Proposition 752 asserts that a minimal microscopic description requires all five Fe $3d$ bands, the five-orbital $H_{0}$ in Proposition 766 is therefore the minimal non-interacting electronic Hamiltonian consistent with the first-principles derivation in Proposition 752.

extra.notations:
- $H_{0}$: non-interacting tight-binding Hamiltonian on the unfolded square lattice.
- $\varepsilon_{\mu}$: on-site energy of orbital $\mu$; $\mu\in\{1,\dots,5\}$ corresponds to $(d_{3Z^{2}-R^{2}},d_{XZ},d_{YZ},d_{X^{2}-Y^{2}},d_{XY})$.
- $t(\Delta x,\Delta y;\mu,\nu)$: hopping integral between orbital $\mu$ at a site and orbital $\nu$ at a site displaced by $(\Delta x,\Delta y)$ on the effective square lattice.

title: Likely emergence of spin-fluctuation driven sign-changing $s$-wave pairing in RPA using the five-orbital parameters
content: If the five-orbital non-interacting Hamiltonian $H_{0}$ (with on-site energies and hoppings taken from Proposition 752) is supplemented by physically reasonable interaction parameters $U$ (intraorbital), $U'$ (interorbital), $J$ (Hund's coupling) and $J'$ (pair-hopping) of intermediate strength and one considers electron doping relevant to $LaFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$, then applying the Random Phase Approximation (RPA) spin-fluctuation framework to the resulting multiorbital model will likely produce a leading superconducting eigenfunction $\hat{\phi}$ of the linearized Eliashberg equation with positive eigenvalue $\lambda>0$ that has a nodeless or nearly nodeless sign change between hole and electron Fermi surface pockets (the so-called $s_{\pm}$-type gap). Here $\hat{\phi}$ is the gap matrix in orbital (or band) space and $\lambda$ is the corresponding eigenvalue indicating instability toward superconductivity.

extra.notations:
- $H_{0}$: non-interacting five-orbital Hamiltonian (from Propositions 752 and 766).
- $U$: intraorbital Coulomb repulsion.
- $U'$: interorbital Coulomb repulsion.
- $J$: Hund's rule exchange.
- $J'$: pair-hopping term.
- RPA: Random Phase Approximation used to compute spin and charge susceptibilities and the effective pairing interaction.
- $\hat{\phi}$: superconducting gap function matrix (orbital or band basis).
- $\lambda$: eigenvalue of the linearized Eliashberg equation; $\lambda>0$ signals a pairing instability (larger values indicate stronger tendency).

title: Electron–phonon coupling computed for stoichiometric LaFeAsO cannot by itself produce the observed $T_{c}$ in F-doped samples (conditional statement made explicit)
content: If the electron–phonon coupling in F-doped LaOFeAs equals the coupling computed for stoichiometric LaFeAsO (specifically $\lambda\approx 0.2$ and $\omega_{\log}\approx 250\,$K, where $\lambda$ is the electron–phonon coupling constant and $\omega_{\log}$ the logarithmically averaged boson frequency), then conventional phonon-mediated pairing using those parameters cannot produce the experimentally observed superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}\ge 26\,$K in the F-doped compound. Here $T_{c}$ denotes the superconducting transition temperature.

extra.notations:
- $\lambda$: electron–phonon coupling constant (dimensionless)  
- $\omega_{\log}$: logarithmically averaged boson (phonon) frequency (in K)  
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (in K)  
- $\chi_{mol}$: molar magnetic susceptibility

title: Superconductivity in F-doped LaOFeAs is likely non–phonon-mediated, plausibly due to electronic (spin) fluctuations
content: The superconductivity observed in F-doped LaOFeAs (bulk superconducting fraction ~50% at 5 at.% F and $T_{c}\gtrsim 26\,$K) is likely not primarily mediated by conventional electron–phonon interaction. Instead, a pairing mechanism based on electronic interactions—most plausibly spin fluctuations originating from Fe-derived local moments or itinerant magnetism—provides a more probable explanation for the superconducting state in the doped material. Here "spin fluctuations" denotes bosonic spin-excitation modes that can mediate attractive pairing between electrons.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (in K)  
- $\lambda$, $\omega_{\log}$: as above for electron–phonon coupling (dimensionless and K respectively)  
- $\chi_{mol}$: molar magnetic susceptibility (indicates paramagnetic response)

title: LDOS -0.96 eV features count maps directly to total dopant charge per unit cell
content: If each localized LDOS feature at approximately $-0.96\\,$eV corresponds to a single nonstoichiometric oxygen dopant atom, and each such dopant carries an electrostatic charge of $-2e$, then the total electric charge introduced by dopants in one crystallographic unit cell is $Q_{dop} = -2e\\times N_{feat}$, where $N_{feat}$ is the number of $-0.96\\,$eV LDOS features counted per unit cell. Counting these LDOS features therefore provides a direct, local measurement of the dopant charge distribution (in units of $-2e$ per counted feature).

extra.notations:
- $N_{feat}$: number of LDOS features at $-0.96\\,$eV per crystallographic unit cell (counted in an STM/LDOS image). 
- $e$: elementary charge (positive constant).
- $Q_{dop}$: net electrostatic charge introduced by dopants in one unit cell (SI units of charge).

title: Feature count provides a proxy for hole density per CuO2 plaquette under full charge transfer
content:
Let $\\delta$ be the number of nonstoichiometric oxygen atoms per crystallographic unit cell and let $n_{CuO_2}$ be the number of $CuO_2$ plaquettes (Cu sites) per unit cell (for Bi-2212, $n_{CuO_2}=2$). If each dopant carries charge $-2e$ and that negative charge is compensated by withdrawing two electrons from the $CuO_2$ planes (i.e., full charge transfer, no additional compensating defects), then the total number of holes added to the $CuO_2$ planes per unit cell is $2\\delta$ and the hole concentration per $CuO_2$ plaquette is
$$p = \\dfrac{2\\delta}{n_{CuO_2}}.$$
Under the common case $n_{CuO_2}=2$ this simplifies to $p=\\delta$. Because Proposition 1077 equates each $-0.96\\,$eV LDOS feature with one dopant, the spatial density of those features provides a direct proxy for the local hole density $p$ provided the stated full-transfer assumption holds.

extra.notations:
- $\\delta$: nonstoichiometric oxygen atoms per unit cell (equals number of $-0.96\\,$eV features per unit cell by Proposition 1077).
- $n_{CuO_2}$: number of $CuO_2$ plaquettes (Cu sites) per crystallographic unit cell (for Bi-2212, $n_{CuO_2}=2$).
- $p$: hole density per $CuO_2$ plaquette.
- $e$: elementary charge.

title: π triplet exists only in the superconducting phase and disappears at or above Tc
content: The spin-triplet π collective mode (the "π triplet") is a gapped magnetic excitation that exists only while the system has superconducting long-range order; it is present in the superconducting (SC) phase at the antiferromagnetic wavevector $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$ and vanishes for temperatures $T\ge T_c$ where superconducting order is lost. Here $T_c$ is the superconducting transition temperature.

extra.notations:
- π triplet: the spin-1 collective excitation that rotates AF into SC components of the SO(5) superspin.
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature.
- $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$: the commensurate antiferromagnetic wavevector.

title: π resonance energy controlled by the SO(5) chemical-potential parameter and likely tracks doping/$T_c$
content: The energy (gap) of the π triplet excitation, denoted $E_\pi$, is set parametrically by the SO(5) chemical-potential-like parameter $\mu$ in the effective theory. Consequently, $E_\pi$ should vary with control parameters (e.g., doping) that shift $\mu$ and is therefore likely to correlate with the superconducting transition temperature $T_c$ (and with the superconducting gap scale) across samples.

extra.notations:
- $E_\pi$: energy (gap) of the π triplet excitation as measured by neutron scattering.
- $\mu$: the chemical potential (or SO(5) symmetry-breaking parameter) appearing in the effective equations that sets the π mass.
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature.

title: Spectral weight of the π resonance is tied to the superconducting order parameter amplitude
content: The intensity (spectral weight) of the π triplet measured in spin-flip neutron scattering is expected to be strongly coupled to the superconducting order-parameter amplitude $|\Delta|$ and to decrease continuously to zero as $|\Delta|\to 0$ at $T\to T_c$. Quantitatively, the low-temperature integrated intensity I_\pi is plausibly a nonzero function of the superconducting amplitude (e.g., scaling like $|\Delta|^2$ under simple coupling assumptions).

extra.notations:
- $I_\pi$: integrated spectral weight (intensity) of the π resonance in neutron scattering.
- $|\Delta|$: amplitude of the d-wave superconducting order parameter.
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature.

title: Pseudospin eigenvalues represent quantized transfer of a charge pair between stripe and environment
content: The unitary operator U = exp(-i√{2π} ∑_R τ_R^z θ_c) (node 622) acts by translating the charge-phase/dual field θ_c conditional on the pseudospin configuration τ_R^z. Because the stripe charge density ρ_c is the field canonically conjugate to θ_c (in bosonization conventions used in the siblings, the long-wavelength stripe charge density is proportional to ∂_x φ_c and conjugate to θ_c), the eigenvalue τ_R^z = +1/2 (presence of a holon pair in the environment) implies that the stripe has lost a quantized pair of charge compared to the τ_R^z = −1/2 state. Equivalently, τ_R^z eigenstates label discrete, quantized transfers of a charge-2e pair between stripe and environment, and the local term [τ_R^z+1/2] appearing in the Hamiltonians (node 638) is an operator that counts whether a pair has been transferred and therefore couples as a source to the stripe charge density. Consequently, the mapping enforces conservation of total charge (stripe + environment) at the operator level: a change in τ_R^z is accompanied by a compensating change in stripe charge.

extra.notations:
- τ_R^z: pseudospin operator with eigenvalues +1/2 (holon pair present in environment) and −1/2 (absent).
- θ_c, φ_c: dual bosonic fields for stripe charge; ρ_c ∝ ∂_x φ_c is the stripe long-wavelength charge density.
- U: unitary transformation coupling τ_R^z and θ_c.

title: Gapped / slowly fluctuating pseudospin sector preserves stripe charge velocity and Drude weight
content: If the pseudospin sector has a large excitation gap (pseudospin flip energy δ ≫ low-energy stripe scales) or the pseudospin kinetics are negligible (environmental charge degrees frozen on stripe timescales), then τ_R^z can be treated as (nearly) static. In that limit the coupling terms of the form [ε^* + const·ρ_c][τ_R^z+1/2] reduce to a c-number shift (static potential/chemical potential) for the stripe charge sector. Consequently, the stripe charge Hamiltonian remains a Gaussian Luttinger liquid with its charge velocity v_c and zero-temperature Drude weight unrenormalized by the environment, consistent with the decoupling statement in node 612.

extra.notations:
- δ: pseudospin flip (creation) energy scale.
- ρ_c: long-wavelength stripe charge density.
- v_c: stripe charge (sound) velocity; Drude weight: zero-temperature charge stiffness of the stripe.

title: Dynamic pseudospin fluctuations generically renormalize the stripe charge Luttinger parameters and Drude weight
content: If pseudospin flips and dynamics occur at energies comparable to or below the stripe's low-energy scales (pseudospin gap δ ≲ stripe cutoff or temperature), the coupling terms of the form [ε^* + const·∂_x φ_c][τ_R^z+1/2] (node 613) or [ε + 2V_c ρ_R][τ_R^z+1/2] (node 638) will induce temporal and spatial fluctuations in the forward-scattering potential acting on the stripe charge field. Integrating out dynamic pseudospin fluctuations produces retarded/instantaneous contributions to the stripe charge action that renormalize the Gaussian coefficients — i.e., the Luttinger parameter K_c and the charge velocity v_c — and therefore will generically change the temperature- and frequency-dependent Drude weight.

extra.notations:
- K_c: charge Luttinger parameter (controls power laws and compressibility).
- v_c: stripe charge velocity.
- δ: pseudospin excitation energy (flip gap).
- V_c, ΔV_c: forward-scattering coupling strengths between stripe charge and pseudospin.

title: F−-doped superconducting samples have reduced lattice parameters and unit-cell volume
content:
Given that $F^{-}$ substitution in La[O$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$]FeAs produces samples with smaller lattice constants ($a$ and $c$) relative to the undoped material, and given that superconductivity in LaOFeAs is induced by $F^{-}$ (electron) doping, it follows that there exist superconducting compositions of La[O$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$]FeAs whose lattice parameters $a$ and $c$ — and therefore their crystallographic unit-cell volume $V$ — are reduced compared with the undoped parent compound.

Symbols:
- $a,c$: tetragonal lattice parameters (lengths of cell edges) of La[O$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$]FeAs.
- $V$: unit-cell volume for the tetragonal cell, $V = a^{2}c$ (using conventional setting for P4/nmm).
- $x$: molar fraction of substituted fluoride, $0<x\leq 0.05$ (example value reported at 5 at.%).
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature; superconductivity is present for some $x>0$ via $F^{-}$ substitution.

extra.notations:
- $F^{-}$: fluoride anion substituting for oxide anion O$^{2-}$ in La[O$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$]FeAs.
- P4/nmm: tetragonal crystallographic space group in which LaOFeAs crystallizes.

title: Chemical-pressure (lattice contraction) likely contributes to superconductivity in F-doped LaOFeAs
content:
It is plausible that the lattice contraction produced by $F^{-}$ substitution (a reduction of lattice constants $a$ and $c$ and unit-cell volume $V$) contributes to the emergence or enhancement of superconductivity in La[O$_{1-x}$F$_{x}$]FeAs by providing a chemical-pressure effect that alters electronic structure (bandwidths, Fermi surface nesting, or electronic correlations) in a way favorable to superconducting pairing.

Symbols:
- $a,c,V$ as defined above.
- "Chemical pressure": the effect on electronic structure produced by substitution-driven reduction of lattice parameters, analogous to applied hydrostatic pressure.

extra.notations: - $F^{-}$, $x$, $T_{c}$ as defined in the deduction.

title: Finite CDW inter-stripe correlation length at nonzero temperature
content:
Using the notation of Proposition 42, at non-zero temperature the mean-squared arc-length difference grows as
$$\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle \sim A T |x|,$$
and the effective inter-stripe CDW coupling is
$$\tilde{V}(x)=\langle V(\Delta Y)\rangle\exp\{-(2k_{\mathrm{F}}^{2})\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle\}.$$
Therefore $\tilde{V}(x)$ decays exponentially with $|x|$ at non-zero $T$, with a characteristic decay length (correlation length)
$$\xi_{\mathrm{CDW}} \simeq \frac{1}{2k_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}A T}.$$
Hence inter-stripe CDW correlations are short-ranged at any non-zero temperature (no true long-range CDW locking across stripes).

extra.notations:
- $k_{\mathrm{F}}$: Fermi wavevector
- $A$: model-dependent positive constant appearing in $\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle\sim A T|x|$
- $T$: temperature
- $\xi_{\mathrm{CDW}}$: CDW inter-stripe correlation length (longitudinal)

title: Algebraic (power-law) suppression of CDW coupling at zero temperature
content:
Using Proposition 42, at zero temperature the mean-squared arc-length difference grows as
$$\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle \sim A\hbar\omega\log|x|.$$
Then the effective inter-stripe CDW coupling behaves as
$$\tilde{V}(x)\propto |x|^{-2k_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}A\hbar\omega},$$
i.e. $\tilde{V}(x)$ decays as a power law in $|x|$ at $T=0$. Consequently, true long-range CDW locking across infinitely long stripes is absent at $T=0$ (inter-stripe CDW coupling vanishes algebraically at long separations).

extra.notations:
- $\hbar\omega$: characteristic frequency scale appearing in the zero-temperature fluctuation amplitude
- other symbols as in Proposition 42

title: Transverse fluctuations favor inter-stripe superconducting (Josephson) coupling over CDW locking
content:
Let $\langle[\Delta Y]^{2}\rangle$ denote the mean-squared transverse relative displacement between neighbouring stripes and $\langle[\Delta L]^{2}\rangle$ the corresponding arc-length difference squared. From Proposition 47,
$$\langle J\rangle \approx J_{0}\exp\{(\alpha^{2}/2)\langle[\Delta Y]^{2}\rangle\},$$
while Proposition 42 gives for the CDW coupling at longitudinal separation $x$
$$\tilde{V}(x)\propto\exp\{-2k_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle\}.$$
If $\langle[\Delta L]^2\rangle$ and $\langle[\Delta Y]^2\rangle$ scale proportionally (plausible for small stripe slopes so that arc-length differences track transverse separations), then the ratio of Josephson to CDW inter-stripe couplings grows exponentially with the mean-squared relative displacement:
$$\frac{\langle J\rangle}{\tilde{V}(x)}\sim\frac{J_{0}}{\langle V(\Delta Y)\rangle}\exp\Big\{\Big(\tfrac{\alpha^{2}}{2}+2k_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}C\Big)\langle[\Delta Y]^{2}\rangle\Big\},$$
with $C\!>\!0$ the proportionality constant defined by $\langle[\Delta L]^2\rangle\approx C\langle[\Delta Y]^2\rangle$. Therefore, for sufficiently large transverse fluctuations the Josephson coupling will dominate over the CDW coupling, favoring transverse superconducting phase locking between stripes rather than CDW locking.

extra.notations:
- $J_{0}$: bare Josephson prefactor
- $\alpha$: parameter entering the Josephson exponential enhancement
- $k_{\mathrm{F}}$: Fermi wavevector
- $C$: proportionality constant such that $\langle[\Delta L]^2\rangle\approx C\langle[\Delta Y]^2\rangle$ (valid for small slopes)
- other symbols as in Propositions 42 and 47

title: Nonzero environmental spin coherence is necessary for induced stripe spin gap and pairing
content: Let $\mathcal{J}_{sp}=J_{sp}\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\tilde{\phi}_s]\rangle$ (definition from Proposition 613). Proposition 605 asserts that the induced spin-gap scale on the stripe, $\overline{\Delta}_s$ (and the pairing crossover temperature $T_{pair}$), are controlled by $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ with $\overline{\Delta}_s(0)\sim \mathcal{J}_{sp}^2/\varepsilon^*$ and $T_{pair}\sim\varepsilon^*$ in the quoted regime. Therefore if the environmental spin expectation value vanishes, $\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\tilde{\phi}_s]\rangle=0$, then $\mathcal{J}_{sp}=0$, implying $\overline{\Delta}_s=0$ and no induced pairing scale on the stripe. Hence a nonzero environmental spin coherence (nonzero $\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\tilde{\phi}_s]\rangle$) is a necessary condition for the pair-tunneling channel to induce a spin gap and pairing on the stripe.

extra.notations:
- $J_{sp}$: bare pair-hopping coupling (Proposition 613). 
- $\tilde{\phi}_s$: environmental spin boson field (Proposition 613).
- $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: effective pair-tunneling coupling, $J_{sp}\langle\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\tilde{\phi}_s]\rangle$ (Proposition 613).
- $\overline{\Delta}_s$: induced spin gap on the stripe (Proposition 613 and 605).
- $\varepsilon^*$: renormalized charge-transfer energy (Proposition 605 and 613).

title: Charge velocity and zero-temperature Drude weight of the stripe remain unrenormalized by the pair-transfer coupling
content: Proposition 613 contains a coupling term to the stripe charge field of the form $\propto \{\varepsilon^* - 2\sqrt{2/\pi}\,\Delta V_c\,\partial_x\phi_c\}\,[\tau_R^z+1/2]$, i.e. a term linear in $\partial_x\phi_c$ (a forward-scattering / density-shift coupling). Proposition 612 states that, after the unitary transformation that decouples spin and charge, the stripe charge sector is a Gaussian Luttinger liquid with charge velocity $v_c$ unrenormalized by the coupling to the environment; consequently the zero-temperature Drude weight (charge stiffness) is unrenormalized. Combining these, the $\Delta V_c$ coupling acts only to shift the charge zero mode / local chemical potential depending on the pseudospin configuration $\tau^z$, but it does not modify the quadratic coefficient of the Gaussian charge action that sets $v_c$ and the Drude weight. Therefore $v_c$ and the zero-temperature Drude weight remain unchanged by the pair-transfer/pseudospin coupling.

extra.notations:
- $\phi_c$: stripe charge boson field (Proposition 613).
- $\partial_x\phi_c$: proportional to stripe charge density fluctuations.
- $\Delta V_c$: coupling coefficient between stripe charge and environment (Proposition 613).
- $v_c$: stripe charge velocity; Drude weight is the associated zero-temperature charge stiffness (Proposition 612).
- $\tau_R^z$: pseudospin operator indicating presence/absence of a holon pair at site $R$ (Proposition 613).

title: Maximum induced stripe spin gap within the stated regime occurs near the lower bound of $\varepsilon^*$
content:
Proposition 605 gives the zero-temperature induced spin gap scaling $\overline{\Delta}_s(0)\sim \mathcal{J}_{sp}^2/\varepsilon^*$, valid in the regime
$$
  \mathcal{J}_{sp}\big[\mathcal{J}_{sp}/W\big]^{1/3} \ll \varepsilon^* \ll \mathcal{J}_{sp},
$$
where $W$ is a bandwidth. Treating $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ and $W$ as fixed parameters, this scaling implies $\overline{\Delta}_s(0)$ increases as $\varepsilon^*$ is reduced within the regime. Extrapolating to the smallest allowed $\varepsilon^*$ (the lower bound), the largest gap estimate inside the regime is
$$
  \overline{\Delta}_{s,\mathrm{max}}\;\sim\;\frac{\mathcal{J}_{sp}^2}{\mathcal{J}_{sp}(\mathcal{J}_{sp}/W)^{1/3}}
  \;=\;\mathcal{J}_{sp}^{2/3}\,W^{1/3}.
$$
Thus, within the regime of validity of the mean-field scaling, the maximal induced gap scales as $\overline{\Delta}_{s,\mathrm{max}}\sim \mathcal{J}_{sp}^{2/3}W^{1/3}$. Reaching gaps parametrically larger than $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$ would require leaving the quoted regime and so is not supported by the given scalings.

extra.notations:
- $\mathcal{J}_{sp}$: effective pair-tunneling coupling (Proposition 613 and 605).
- $\varepsilon^*$: charge-transfer excitation energy (Proposition 605 and 613).
- $W$: electronic bandwidth scale entering the renormalization analysis (Proposition 605).
- $\overline{\Delta}_s(0)$: induced spin gap at $T=0$ on the stripe (Proposition 605).

title: Formal Ni oxidation-state change on perovskite → infinite-layer topotactic reduction
content: For the general composition Nd1−xSrxNiO3 converted to Nd1−xSrxNiO2 by removal of one O per formula unit (apical-oxygen deintercalation), the formal Ni oxidation state changes from (3 + x) in the perovskite to (1 + x) in the infinite-layer phase — i.e., a reduction by exactly two oxidation units per Ni. Equivalently, the formal Ni valence decreases by 2 when O3 → O2 while A-site charges (Nd3+, Sr2+) are unchanged.

extra.notations:
- x: Sr doping fraction in Nd1−xSrxNiOx (0 ≤ x ≤ 1)
- Ni oxidation state: formal ionic charge on Ni (in units of +)
- Nd1−xSrxNiO3 → Nd1−xSrxNiO2: stoichiometric removal of one O2− per formula unit

title: 260–280°C CaH2 reduction likely preserves epitaxial clamping while producing c-axis contraction to ≈3.37 Å
content: The soft-chemistry CaH2 topotactic reduction of epitaxial perovskite Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO3 films performed at 260–280 °C in a vacuum-sealed environment likely yields the infinite-layer Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2 phase with an out-of-plane lattice constant of ≈3.37 Å while maintaining clamping (in-plane lattice registry) to the SrTiO3 substrate — i.e., the structural change is accommodated predominantly by c-axis contraction rather than by relaxation of the in-plane lattice.

extra.notations:
- CaH2: calcium hydride reducing agent
- 260–280 °C: reported reduction temperature range
- c-axis: lattice parameter perpendicular to the film plane
- clamping / epitaxial clamping: in-plane lattice matching to the substrate (SrTiO3)

title: Nominal Ni d-electron count in Nd1−xSrxNiO2 is 9 − x (e.g., d9 for x=0, d8.8 for x=0.2)
content: Using formal-charge bookkeeping, the nominal d‑electron count per Ni in the infinite-layer Nd1−xSrxNiO2 is 10 − (1 + x) = 9 − x. Thus undoped NdNiO2 corresponds to a nominal Ni d9 configuration, while Nd0.8Sr0.2NiO2 corresponds nominally to d8.8.

extra.notations:
- dN: nominal number of electrons in Ni 3d shell
- x: Sr doping fraction
- Ni formal charge in Nd1−xSrxNiO2: 1 + x

title: Temperature dependence of the low-energy slope of Im χ matched to the observed relaxation law
content:
Let $T_{1}^{-1}$ denote the nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate, $T$ the temperature, and
$\chi_{m}^{+-}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ the transverse magnetic susceptibility. Proposition 909 gives the formal low-frequency relation
$$T_{1}^{-1}\propto -\lim_{\omega\to0}\frac{T}{\omega}\sum_{\mathbf{q}}\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{m}^{+-}(\mathbf{q},\omega).$$
Proposition 929 reports the empirical fit
$$T_{1}^{-1}=aT + b,$$
with constants $a$ and $b$. Combining these two statements and rearranging yields the necessary relation for the q-summed low-energy slope of the susceptibility:
$$\lim_{\omega\to0}\frac{1}{\omega}\sum_{\mathbf{q}}\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{m}^{+-}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\propto a + \frac{b}{T}.$$
Thus the temperature dependence of the q-summed low-$\omega$ limit of $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi/\omega$ is necessarily a temperature-independent constant plus a term proportional to $1/T$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{1}^{-1}$: nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate (units s$^{-1}$)  
- $T$: absolute temperature (units K)  
- $\chi_{m}^{+-}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: transverse magnetic susceptibility (complex)  
- $\mathrm{Im}$: imaginary part of a complex quantity  
- $\mathbf{q}$: wave vector summed over the Brillouin zone  
- $a,b$: empirical constants appearing in $T_{1}^{-1}=aT+b$

title: Origin of the $b/T$ term: a relaxational mode with Curie-like static weight
content:
If the temperature-independent contribution $b$ in $T_{1}^{-1}=aT+b$ arises from a distinct excitation spectrum encoded in $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$ (as stated in 929), then a plausible microscopic interpretation is:
the $\tilde{P}$-mode is a relaxational (overdamped) excitation with characteristic time $\tau_{m}$ (reported $\tau_{m}\sim 1\ \mathrm{eV}^{-1}$). For a relaxational form at each $\mathbf{q}$,
$$\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\tilde{P}}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\approx \chi_{0}(\mathbf{q})\frac{\omega\tau_{m}}{1+(\omega\tau_{m})^{2}},$$
so in the low-$\omega$ limit $\mathrm{Im}\,\chi_{\tilde{P}}(\mathbf{q},\omega)/\omega\approx \chi_{0}(\mathbf{q})\tau_{m}$. To produce the observed $b$ which gives a $b/T$ contribution in $\mathrm{Im}\chi/\omega$ (see deduction), the static weight $\chi_{0}(\mathbf{q})$ of this mode must scale approximately as $1/T$ when summed over $\mathbf{q}$ (i.e., a Curie-like temperature dependence of the mode's amplitude). Therefore it is plausible that $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}$ represents local or quasi-local fluctuating moments (or nearly-critical modes) whose static susceptibility is $\sim C/T$, with $C$ a constant, combined with a temperature-independent $\tau_{m}$.

extra.notations:
- $\mathrm{Im}\,\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)$: imaginary part of the proposed excitation spectrum contribution to the polarizability  
- $\tau_{m}$: characteristic fluctuation time of the mode (units eV$^{-1}$)  
- $\chi_{0}(\mathbf{q})$: static (zero-frequency) susceptibility weight of the mode at wave vector $\mathbf{q}$

title: Sharpening at Tc implies reduction of at least one broadening rate
content:
Given the phenomenological self-energy model
$$\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -\,i\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)\;+\;\dfrac{\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)^2}{\omega+\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}+i\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)}\,, $$
and the observation that ARPES lineshapes in underdoped Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8−δ sharpen from broad pseudogap-like spectra above $T_c$ to resolution-limited quasiparticle peaks below $T_c$, it necessarily follows that at least one of the two temperature-dependent broadening rates decreases across $T_c$: either the single-particle scattering rate $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$ decreases for relevant $\mathbf{k}$, or the inverse pair-lifetime $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$ decreases, or both. This change is required because, within the model, only reductions in these rates can produce the observed sharpening of low-energy spectral features.

extra.notations:
- $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$: single-particle scattering rate (broadens quasiparticle peaks). 
- $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$: inverse pair lifetime (broadens the pairing pole; causes filling of a pairing-related suppression).
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$: pairing amplitude (superconducting gap or pseudogap).
- $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$: band dispersion measured from chemical potential.

title: Sharpening primarily reflects a decrease in inverse pair-lifetime ($\Gamma_0$) associated with phase coherence
content: It is plausible that the dominant change at $T_c$ responsible for the sharpening of ARPES lineshapes is a reduction of the inverse pair-lifetime $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$ (i.e., an increase in pair lifetime), reflecting the onset of global phase coherence of preformed (or fluctuating) pairs, while the pairing amplitude $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$ remains nonzero across $T_c$. In this scenario the pseudogap above $T_c$ is largely a "filling" phenomenon caused by large $\Gamma_0$, and below $T_c$ the decrease of $\Gamma_0$ removes the filling and yields sharper quasiparticle peaks without requiring a large increase in $|\Delta|$.

extra.notations:
- $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$: inverse pair lifetime (pair-pole broadening).
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$: pairing amplitude.

title: Resolution-limited quasiparticle peaks imply intrinsic single-particle scattering is reduced below instrumental resolution
content: The observation of resolution-limited quasiparticle peaks in ARPES below $T_c$ implies that the intrinsic single-particle linewidth contribution from $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$ (and any other intrinsic broadening channels) at the relevant momenta $\mathbf{k}$ is reduced to a value smaller than the experimental energy resolution. Therefore, $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T<T_c)$ is plausibly below the instrument's resolution limit for those states.

extra.notations:
- $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$: single-particle scattering rate contributing to quasiparticle linewidth.
- "Resolution-limited": measured peak width is dominated by instrument resolution rather than intrinsic broadening.

title: Single-particle scattering rate linear in max(|ω|,T) and logarithmic vanishing of quasiparticle residue
content:
The single-particle scattering rate (inverse quasiparticle lifetime) defined by Γ( k, ω, T ) ≡ -Im Σ( k, ω ) follows directly from the given self-energy
$$\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)\sim g^{2}N^{2}(0)\Big(\omega\ln\frac{x}{\omega_{c}}-i\frac{\pi}{2}x\Big),\quad x=\max(|\omega|,T).$$
Hence
$$\Gamma(\mathbf{k},\omega,T)= -\mathrm{Im}\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)\simeq \frac{\pi}{2}\,g^{2}N^{2}(0)\;x.$$
Therefore (i) at zero frequency the scattering rate is linear in temperature, $\Gamma(\mathbf{k},0,T)\propto T$, and (ii) at zero temperature the scattering rate is linear in frequency, $\Gamma(\mathbf{k},\omega,0)\propto|\omega|$ (with proportionality constants given above). Furthermore the quasiparticle residue
$$z_{k}^{-1}=1-\left.\frac{\partial\operatorname{Re}\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)}{\partial\omega}\right|_{\omega=E_k} \sim g^{2}N^{2}(0)\ln\frac{\omega_c}{|E_k|},$$
so $z_k\to 0$ logarithmically as the quasiparticle pole energy $E_k\to 0$, i.e. coherent quasiparticle weight vanishes at the Fermi level in the low-energy limit.

extra.notations:
- Γ( k, ω, T ): single-particle scattering rate (inverse lifetime) = $-\mathrm{Im}\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$
- x: $\max(|\omega|,T)$
- other symbols as in node 907

title: DC electrical resistivity likely linear in temperature (under standard transport assumptions)
content:
Let ρ(T) denote the DC electrical resistivity. If the momentum dependence of the scattering is such that the transport relaxation rate τ_tr^{-1}(T) tracks the single-particle scattering rate Γ(ω=0,T) (i.e., vertex corrections and strong forward-scattering do not render transport lifetime parametrically different from the single-particle lifetime), then from Γ(0,T)∝T one expects the Drude relation
$$\rho(T)=\frac{m^*}{n e^2}\,\tau_{\rm tr}^{-1}(T)\propto T.$$
Thus, under the usual assumption that τ_tr^{-1}\sim Γ(0,T), the DC resistivity is expected to be linear in T at low temperatures.

extra.notations:
- ρ(T): DC electrical resistivity
- τ_tr: transport relaxation time
- Γ(0,T): single-particle scattering rate at ω=0
- m^*, n, e: effective mass, carrier density, electron charge

title: Nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate 1/T1 expected to be approximately temperature independent
content:
Define the nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate 1/T1 given (in the electronic-fluctuation mechanism) by
$$\frac{1}{T_1}\propto T\sum_{\mathbf{q}}|A(\mathbf{q})|^2\left.\frac{\mathrm{Im}\,\chi(\mathbf{q},\omega)}{\omega}\right|_{\omega\to0},$$
where $A(\mathbf{q})$ is the hyperfine form factor and $\chi$ is the relevant (spin) susceptibility. The Marginal Fermi Liquid form in node 908 gives, for $|\omega|\ll T$,
$$\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)\sim -N(0)\,\frac{\omega}{T},$$
so $\mathrm{Im}\tilde{P}(\mathbf{q},\omega)/\omega\sim -N(0)/T$. Substituting into the expression for $1/T_1$ yields $1/T_1\propto T\times(1/T)\sim\text{const}$ (up to weak $T$-dependence from the $q$-sum and form factors). Thus, modulo assumptions about the $q$-dependence of $A(\mathbf{q})$ and absence of additional temperature-dependent channels, 1/T1 is expected to be roughly temperature independent.

extra.notations:
- 1/T1: nuclear spin-lattice relaxation rate
- A(q): hyperfine form factor
- χ(q,ω): electronic spin susceptibility (related to $\tilde{P}$)
- N(0), T as above

title: Identification of the spectrally narrow dynamical peak as the 41 meV magnetic resonance
content: The energy-localized (spectrally narrow) peak present in the dynamical response of YBa2Cu3O6.92 (as asserted by node 1594) is the same feature described in node 1508: a pronounced magnetic (spin) resonance at energy transfer $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 41\\ \\text{meV}$ measured at low temperature. Here $\\hbar\\omega$ is the neutron energy transfer ($\\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant, $\\omega$ the angular frequency). The statement identifies the narrow dynamical feature with a magnetic resonant excitation at $\\approx 41\\,$meV in YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: yttrium-barium-copper oxide with oxygen stoichiometry 6.92. 
- $\\hbar\\omega$: energy transfer in inelastic neutron scattering.
- "magnetic resonance": a pronounced, spectrally narrow feature in the magnetic part of the dynamical response (Im$\\{\\chi(q,\\,\\hbar\\omega)\\}$ or magnetic structure factor).

title: Resonance likely a superconductivity-induced collective spin excitation
content: The spectrally narrow magnetic peak at $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 41\\,$meV in YBa2Cu3O6.92, observed at low temperature (e.g., $T\\sim 5\\,$K), is likely a collective spin excitation that is induced or enhanced by the superconducting state (a superconductivity-related spin resonance). Here "superconducting state" means the phase below the superconducting critical temperature $T_c$ of optimally doped YBa2Cu3O6.92 (about 90–93 K).

extra.notations:
- $\\hbar\\omega$: energy transfer in neutron scattering.
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature.

title: Resonance plausibly centered near the antiferromagnetic wavevector
content: The spectrally narrow magnetic resonance at $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 41\\,$meV in YBa2Cu3O6.92 is plausibly peaked in momentum space near the antiferromagnetic wavevector (commonly indexed as $\\mathbf{Q}_{\\mathrm{AF}}=(\\pi,\\pi)$ in the CuO2-plane Brillouin zone), i.e., its magnetic spectral weight is concentrated near that wavevector.

extra.notations:
- $\\mathbf{Q}_{\\mathrm{AF}}$: antiferromagnetic ordering wavevector in the CuO2-plane, often written as $(\\pi,\\pi)$ in cuprate reciprocal-lattice units.
- $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 41\\,$meV: energy of the resonance.

title: Leading instability at n = 0.85 is singlet d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} and even-frequency
content: For the two-dimensional Hubbard model with $U/t=4$ and filling $n=0.85$, the leading particle-particle Bethe–Salpeter eigenfunction is a singlet with $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ spatial symmetry and is even in Matsubara frequency. Concretely: the dominant eigenfunction $\phi(\mathbf{K},\omega_n)$ satisfies singlet spin symmetry, changes sign under a $\pi/2$ lattice rotation (d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}), and obeys $\phi(\mathbf{K},\omega_n)=\phi(\mathbf{K},-\omega_n)$.

extra.notations:
- $\mathbf{K}$: cluster/lattice momentum variable used in Bethe–Salpeter equation
- $\omega_n$: fermionic Matsubara frequency
- $\phi(\mathbf{K},\omega_n)$: particle-particle Bethe–Salpeter eigenfunction

title: Robustness of d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} dominance across approximation methods
content: The identification of a singlet $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ leading particle-particle instability at dopings around $n=0.85$ is likely robust with respect to the two distinct approximation schemes reported (fluctuation-exchange / Bethe–Salpeter analysis in 872 and 24-site DCA in 1006). That is, the dominance of the $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ pairing channel at 15% hole doping is probably not an artifact of a single computational method.

extra.notations:
- FLEX: fluctuation-exchange approximation (used in analyses like in 872)
- DCA: Dynamic Cluster Approximation (used in 1006)

title: Existence of a finite superconducting transition temperature at n = 0.85
content: It is plausible that for $U/t=4$ and filling $n=0.85$ the d_{x^{2}-y^{2}} pairing eigenvalue reaches unity at a finite temperature $T_c>0$ (i.e., the model exhibits a superconducting transition at a nonzero temperature in these approximations). Here "reaches unity" refers to the Bethe–Salpeter eigenvalue criterion for an instability.

extra.notations:
- $\lambda$: leading Bethe–Salpeter eigenvalue in the particle-particle channel
- $T_c$: temperature where $\lambda(T_c)=1$, the transition temperature by the Bethe–Salpeter criterion

title: Filling of ARPES gap does not imply vanishing pairing amplitude
content:
Observing that a gap-like depletion of low-energy spectral weight in ARPES is "filled in" on warming through $T_c$ (i.e., the low-energy intensity increases without a clear shift of peak energies) does not logically imply that the pairing amplitude $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$ becomes zero at $T_c$. In the minimal self-energy model
$$\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -\,i\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)\;+\;\dfrac{\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)^2}{\omega+\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}+i\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)}\,, $$
an increase of the inverse pair-lifetime $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$ broadens the pair pole and can restore low-energy spectral weight ("fill" the gap) even while $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)\neq 0$. Thus, the ARPES observation of filling is insufficient by itself to conclude $\Delta$ vanishes at $T_c$.

extra.notations:
- $I(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: ARPES intensity (not required explicitly here).
- $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: one-particle spectral function entering $I$.
- $\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}$: band dispersion measured from chemical potential.
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$: pairing amplitude (superconducting/pseudogap).
- $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$: inverse pair-lifetime (pair-pole broadening).
- $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$: single-particle scattering rate.

title: Sharpening at Tc requires reduction of effective imaginary self-energy
content:
The observed narrowing of ARPES linewidths and emergence of sharper quasiparticle peaks on cooling through $T_c$ implies a decrease in the effective imaginary part of the electronic self-energy at energies near the Fermi level. In the model
$$\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -\,i\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)\;+\;\dfrac{\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)^2}{\omega+\epsilon_{\mathbf{k}}+i\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)}\,, $$
this implies a decrease in $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$ and/or a decrease in $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$ on entering the superconducting/coherent state (i.e., reduced single-particle scattering and/or longer pair lifetimes).

extra.notations:
- $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$: single-particle scattering rate (broaden quasiparticle peaks).
- $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$: inverse pair-lifetime (broadens pair pole; can affect low-energy linewidths).
- Other symbols as in the previous result.

title: Pseudogap predominantly reflects a persistent pairing amplitude with coherence loss driven by pair dephasing
content: It is plausible that in underdoped cuprates the pseudogap observed above $T_c$ predominantly corresponds to a nonzero pairing amplitude $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$ that persists across $T_c$ up to a higher crossover temperature $T^*$, while the loss of superconducting order at $T_c$ primarily reflects an increase in inverse pair-lifetime $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$ (pair dephasing) and finite single-particle scattering $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$ which destroy long-range phase coherence and broaden spectral features. In this picture the transition at $T_c$ is mainly a coherence (lifetime) transition rather than an abrupt collapse of the pairing amplitude.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature (onset of phase coherence).
- $T^*$: pseudogap crossover temperature where $\Delta$ may finally vanish.
- $\Delta(\mathbf{k},T)$, $\Gamma_0(\mathbf{k},T)$, $\Gamma_1(\mathbf{k},T)$ as above.

title: Degeneracy of the dXZ and dYZ on-site energies
content: From the tabulated on-site energies (ε1, ε2, ε3, ε4, ε5) = (10.75, 10.96, 10.96, 11.12, 10.62) eV one has ε2 = ε3 = 10.96 eV. Therefore, in the five-orbital tight-binding model the on-site energies of the d_{XZ} and d_{YZ} Wannier orbitals are equal, implying these two orbitals are degenerate at the level of on-site energies in the model (before inclusion of hopping and symmetry-breaking perturbations).

extra.notations:
- ε_μ: on-site energy of orbital μ (units: eV)
- orbital 2: d_{XZ}; orbital 3: d_{YZ}

title: Hermiticity of the hopping matrix and k-space 5×5 Hamiltonian form
content:
Because the real-space Hamiltonian is written with both the hopping term and its Hermitian conjugate (or equivalently with explicit t(x_i-x_j,y_i-y_j;μ,ν) and t(x_j-x_i,y_j-y_i;ν,μ) terms), the hopping amplitudes satisfy the Hermiticity condition
      $$t(\Delta x,\Delta y;\mu,
u)=t^{*}(-\Delta x,-\Delta y;
u,\mu).$$
      Consequently, after Fourier transform the non-interacting Hamiltonian can be written in momentum space as a 5×5 Hermitian matrix h(k) with elements
      $$h_{\mu
u}(k)=\sum_{\Delta} t(\Delta x,\Delta y;\mu,
u)\,e^{i k\cdot \Delta}+\varepsilon_{\mu}\,\delta_{\mu
u},$$
      where Δ runs over lattice displacements and δ denotes the Kronecker delta. Thus h(k)=h(k)† for all k and defines five energy bands given by the eigenvalues of h(k).

extra.notations:
- t(Δx,Δy;μ,ν): hopping integral from orbital μ at a site to orbital ν at a site displaced by (Δx,Δy)
- ε_μ: on-site energy of orbital μ
- h_{μν}(k): μν matrix element of the Bloch Hamiltonian at crystal momentum k
- Δ: lattice displacement vector (Δx,Δy)
- †: Hermitian conjugate (complex conjugate transpose)

title: Likely sign-changing pairing (s± or d) driven by inter-pocket spin fluctuations in the five-band model
content: Given the five-band tight-binding description with hole pockets (α1, α2) centered at k=(0,0) and electron pockets (β1, β2) centered at k=(π,0) and (0,π), together with local interactions (U, U', J, J') treated within RPA, the leading superconducting instabilities are likely to have a sign change of the gap function between Fermi pockets connected by the characteristic wavevectors (near (π,0) or (0,π)). In practice this commonly manifests as either (i) an s± state (gap changes sign between hole and electron pockets but retains overall A1g symmetry) or (ii) a d_{x^2-y^2}-type state (gap changes sign between the two electron pockets β1 and β2). Both gap types are compatible with spin-fluctuation pairing mediated by inter-pocket scattering in this multi-orbital model.

extra.notations:
- α_{1}, α_{2}: hole Fermi-surface pockets centered at Γ=(0,0)
- β_{1}, β_{2}: electron Fermi-surface pockets centered at (π,0) and (0,π)
- U, U', J, J': local Coulomb and Hund interactions
- RPA: random phase approximation used to compute spin/charge susceptibilities and pairing vertex
- s±: sign-changing s-wave pairing (A1g) with opposite sign on hole vs electron pockets
- d_{x^2-y^2}: pairing symmetry with sign change between β1 and β2

title: Electron–phonon coupling in Im-3m/R3m H3S-like phases suffices to produce Tc ~10^2 K
content: If (a) first-principles Eliashberg/Allen–Dynes calculations using the electron–phonon spectral function $\\alpha^{2}F(\\omega)$ predict high superconducting transition temperatures $T_{c}$ for the Im-3m/R3m hydrogen–sulfur phases because of a non-negligible integrated electron–phonon coupling parameter $\\lambda$, and (b) independent electrical/magnetic measurements observe $T_{c}$ values on the order of $10^{2}\\,$K (roughly $150$–$205\\,$K) in the same pressure range ($\\sim 100$–$200\\,$GPa), then the electron–phonon interaction present in those Im-3m/R3m H3S-like phases is sufficient (i.e., capable) to account for superconducting $T_{c}$ on the order of $10^{2}\\,$K under those pressures.

extra.notations:
- $\\alpha^{2}F(\\omega)$: Eliashberg electron–phonon spectral function (frequency-dependent pairing kernel). 
- $\\lambda$: integrated electron–phonon coupling parameter (dimensionless), $\\lambda=2\\int_0^{\\infty}\\!\\frac{\\alpha^{2}F(\\omega)}{\\omega}\\,d\\omega$. 
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (kelvin). 
- GPa: gigapascal, unit of pressure.

title: Superconductivity in Im-3m/R3m H3S-like phases is likely conventional (phonon-mediated) with an observable isotope effect and predominantly s-wave gap
content: It is likely that the superconducting state in the high-pressure Im-3m/R3m H3S-like compounds is predominantly phonon-mediated (i.e., "conventional" pairing arising from electron–phonon interactions described by $\\alpha^{2}F(\\omega)$), that the superconducting gap has mainly isotropic (s-wave) character, and that a measurable isotope effect on $T_{c}$ should be present (for example, partial substitution of hydrogen by deuterium will reduce $T_{c}$ by a substantial, detectable amount).

extra.notations:
- $\\alpha^{2}F(\\omega)$: Eliashberg electron–phonon spectral function. 
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature. 
- "Isotope effect": change in $T_{c}$ when atomic masses (here H $\\to$ D) are changed, expected for phonon-mediated pairing.
- "s-wave": superconducting gap symmetry that is nodeless and (approximately) isotropic on the Fermi surface.

title: Existence of a phase in the sample with superconducting onset at or above 133.5 K
content: The synthesized Hg–Ba–Ca–Cu–O sample exhibits an onset of superconducting diamagnetism at $133.5\ \text{K}$ (magnetic susceptibility) and a resistive drop beginning at $132.5\ \text{K}$. Therefore the sample contains at least one superconducting phase whose superconducting transition onset temperature $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}$ satisfies $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}\ge 133.5\ \text{K}$. Here $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}$ denotes the temperature at which diamagnetism or the initial resistive downturn associated with superconductivity is first observed.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}$: temperature where superconducting onset (diamagnetism or initial resistive drop) is observed
- Hg-1212: $HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+x}$
- Hg-1223: $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$

title: High-temperature onset likely attributable to the Hg‑1223 phase; lower zero-resistance temperature due to phase inhomogeneity or percolation
