content: Given the sample contains two identified Hg-based superconducting phases (Hg-1212 and Hg-1223) and the bulk measurements show diamagnetic onset near $133.5\ \text{K}$ with zero electrical resistance only reached at $95\ \text{K}$, it is plausible that the higher onset ($\approx133.5\ \text{K}$) arises from the Hg-1223 phase while the lower temperature for zero resistance reflects either the lower superconducting percolation threshold of the sample (e.g., filamentary connectivity, weak intergranular coupling) or the contribution of the lower-$T_c$ phase (Hg-1212) to the global transport path.

extra.notations:
- Hg-1212: $HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+x}$ (two CuO$_2$ layers)
- Hg-1223: $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ (three CuO$_2$ layers)
- $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}$: temperature of diamagnetic or initial resistive transition onset
- $T_{c,\mathrm{zero}}$: temperature where bulk zero electrical resistance is observed

title: Bloch tight-binding Hamiltonian is a 5×5 Hermitian matrix
content:
Given five maximally localized Wannier orbitals per Fe site labeled by orbital index μ∈{1,...,5}, the in-plane tight-binding Hamiltonian H₀ expressed in momentum space is a 5×5 Hermitian matrix H₀(k) with matrix elements
$$
[H_{0}(k)]_{\mu\nu} \equiv t_{\mu\nu}(k)=\sum_{R} t(R;\mu,\nu)\,e^{i k\cdot R}+\delta_{\mu\nu}\varepsilon_{\mu},
$$
where the sum is over lattice vectors R. Here k lies in the Brillouin zone (BZ). Thus the low-energy electronic problem is represented by five Bloch bands obtained by diagonalizing this 5×5 Hermitian matrix for each k.

extra.notations:
- H₀(k): Bloch (momentum-space) tight-binding Hamiltonian.
- μ,ν: orbital indices running from 1 to 5 corresponding to the Fe 3d symmetries.
- t(R;μ,ν): hopping integral between orbitals μ and ν separated by lattice vector R.
- ε_μ: on-site energy of orbital μ.
- k: crystal momentum in the BZ.

title: Accurate low-energy descriptions require retaining all five Fe 3d orbitals
content: Because the low-energy basis is five Fe-centered Wannier orbitals and the Fe 3d-derived bands are heavily entangled with strong interorbital hybridization (driven by the tetrahedral As coordination), any quantitatively accurate low-energy model that preserves Fermi-surface topology and orbital character will need to retain all five Fe 3d orbitals. Truncating to a model with fewer than five orbitals (e.g., 2- or 3-orbital models) without a clear large energy separation between retained and discarded degrees of freedom will generally distort the orbital composition of Bloch states near the Fermi level and thereby alter interaction matrix elements and pairing tendencies (e.g., the RPA/Eliashberg eigenvalue λ).

extra.notations:
- Wannier orbital: localized basis function centered on Fe representing one Fe 3d symmetry.
- "Entangled bands": bands near the Fermi level that cannot be separated into independent subsets without significant mixing.
- λ: leading eigenvalue of the linearized Eliashberg equation (measure of pairing strength).

title: Inelastic intensity in 20–30 meV contains distinct magnetic and non-magnetic components
content: In superconducting YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature (T ≈ 5 K), the measurable inelastic-scattering intensity in the energy-transfer window $\\hbar\\omega\\approx 20$–$30\\,$meV necessarily includes at least two distinct contributions: (i) a non-magnetic (nuclear/phonon) component centered near $\\approx 21.5\\,$meV (reported as a broad, ungapped nuclear-scattering feature) and (ii) magnetic (spin) spectral weight concentrated toward the upper part of the window, near $\\approx 25$–$30\\,$meV, with little magnetic weight below $\\approx 25\\,$meV. Therefore, the total observed intensity in that window cannot be attributed solely to a single magnetic excitation.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: Yttrium–barium–copper oxide with oxygen stoichiometry 6.92.
- $\\hbar\\omega$: energy transfer in inelastic scattering ($\\hbar$ is reduced Planck's constant).
- "Magnetic (spin) spectral weight": intensity in the magnetic part of $S(\\mathbf{Q},\\hbar\\omega)$ or $\\mathrm{Im}\\{\\chi(\\mathbf{q},\\hbar\\omega)\\}$ attributable to spin fluctuations.
- "Nuclear (non-magnetic) component": scattering intensity arising from nuclear/phonon processes rather than spin fluctuations.

title: Presence of a magnetic excitation gap (or strong suppression) below ≈25 meV in the superconducting state
content: Given that the magnetic (spin) spectral weight in superconducting YBa2Cu3O6.92 at low temperature is observed to be concentrated toward $\\approx 25$–$30\\,$meV and that little magnetic weight is observed below $\\approx 25\\,$meV, it is plausible that the superconducting-state spin excitation spectrum features a magnetic gap or strong suppression of magnetic spectral weight for $\\hbar\\omega\\lesssim 25\\,$meV. Here "magnetic gap" means a substantial reduction of low-energy spin excitation spectral density compared with energies above the gap.

extra.notations:
- "Magnetic gap": an energy range below which spin-excitation spectral weight is strongly reduced relative to higher energies (not necessarily a sharp excitation gap; may be a suppression).
- $\\hbar\\omega\\lesssim 25\\,$meV: energies approximately below 25 meV.

title: Numerical ordering of key interatomic distances in Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8
content:
In Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 the reported bond lengths imply the following strict numerical ordering of representative interatomic distances (all distances in angstroms, Å):
$$\text{Cu–O (intralayer)} = 1.928\ \text{Å} < \text{Tl–O (intersheet)} \approx 2.00\ \text{Å} < \text{Ca–O} = 2.48\ \text{Å} < \text{Cu–O (weak fifth/apical)} = 2.70\ \text{Å}.$$
Symbols:
- Cu–O (intralayer): the copper–oxygen bond within the square-planar CuO4 units lying in the (001) plane.
- Tl–O (intersheet): the bond joining a Tl atom in one Tl–O sheet to an O atom in the adjacent Tl–O sheet (stacked along the $c$-axis).
- Ca–O: typical calcium–oxygen coordination distance reported for Ca in this structure.
- Cu–O (weak fifth/apical): the longer, weakly interacting fifth oxygen coordinated to Cu at ~2.70 Å.

extra.notations:
- Å: angstrom, $1\ \text{Å}=10^{-10}\ \text{m}$.
- Distances taken from Propositions 1391, 1400 and 1629.

title: Weak apical Cu–O interaction implies enhanced two-dimensionality of the CuO2 electronic states
content:
The relatively long distance of the weakly interacting fifth (apical) oxygen from copper (apical Cu–O ≈ 2.70 Å) implies reduced overlap between Cu 3d orbitals with apical O 2p orbitals. Consequently, the electronic states near the Fermi level are likely dominated by the in-plane Cu–O (Cu d_{x^2-y^2} – O p_{x,y}) antibonding bands, producing a more two-dimensional (layer-confined) electronic structure for the CuO2 planes.
Notations:
- Cu d_{x^2-y^2}: the copper 3d orbital lying in the CuO2 plane.
- O 2p_{x,y}: the in-plane oxygen 2p orbitals.
- Two-dimensionality: electronic states and charge transport predominantly confined to the CuO2 planes, with weak interplane hybridization.

extra.notations:
- "Apical Cu–O ≈ 2.70 Å" from Proposition 1391.
- "Layered CuO2 planes" geometry from Proposition 1631.

title: Tl–O intersheet bonds likely provide appreciable structural cohesion and a pathway for interlayer coupling
content:
The reported Tl–O intersheet distance of ≈2.0 Å is comparable to typical Tl–O bond lengths in oxides, indicating a chemically significant interlayer linkage between adjacent Tl–O sheets stacked along the $c$-axis. Therefore the Tl–O intersheet contacts likely contribute meaningfully to structural cohesion of the layered stack and can provide a pathway for electronic or ionic coupling between Tl–O layers, which may influence c-axis transport and interlayer hopping.
Notations:
- Tl–O (intersheet): distance between a thallium atom in one Tl–O layer and an oxygen atom in the neighboring Tl–O layer (stacked along $c$).
- Interlayer coupling: any structural or electronic interaction that links adjacent metal–oxygen sheets and can affect transport or electronic dispersion along the $c$-axis.

extra.notations:
- Tl–O ≈ 2.0 Å from Propositions 1400 and 1629.
- Layered architecture from Proposition 1631.

title: Single-particle scattering rate linear in x and logarithmically vanishing quasiparticle weight
content:
Let x = max(|ω|,T). From the marginal-Fermi-liquid self-energy
$$\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)\sim g^{2}N^{2}(0)\Big(\omega\ln\frac{x}{\omega_{c}}-i\frac{\pi}{2}x\Big),$$
the single-particle scattering rate Γ(ω,T) (defined as Γ(ω,T) ≡ −2 Im Σ(ω,T)/ħ) satisfies
$$\Gamma(\omega,T)\propto x.$$
Hence the quasiparticle lifetime τ(ω,T) ∝ 1/x. Moreover the quasiparticle residue (weight)
$$z_{k}^{-1}=1-\left.\frac{\partial\mathrm{Re}\,\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)}{\partial\omega}\right|_{\omega=E_{k}}
\sim \ln(\omega_{c}/E_{k}),$$
so z_k → 0 (logarithmically) as the excitation energy E_k → 0.

extra.notations:
- x: max(|ω|,T), energy scale controlling low-energy dynamics
- ω: frequency
- T: absolute temperature
- Σ(𝑘,ω): retarded one-particle self-energy
- g: coupling constant to density fluctuations
- N(0): unrenormalized DOS at the Fermi level
- ω_c: ultraviolet cutoff
- Γ(ω,T): single-particle scattering rate = −2 Im Σ/ħ
- τ(ω,T): quasiparticle lifetime ∼ ħ/Γ
- z_k: quasiparticle residue at momentum k

title: DC resistivity linear in temperature under weak momentum dependence
content:
If the single-particle scattering rate controls transport (i.e., the scattering is only weakly momentum dependent and vertex corrections are negligible), then the DC resistivity ρ(T) is proportional to the transport scattering rate 1/τ_tr(T). With Γ(ω≈0,T) ∝ x = T, the transport relaxation time τ_tr ∝ 1/T and therefore
$$\rho(T)\propto T$$
for T within the MFL regime (T ≪ ω_c but large compared with any lower cutoffs).

extra.notations:
- ρ(T): DC electrical resistivity at temperature T
- τ_tr: transport relaxation time (≈ quasiparticle lifetime if momentum dependence is weak)
- other symbols as in the deduction result

title: Optical conductivity with a non-Drude high-frequency tail (roughly σ1(ω) ∝ 1/ω for ω≫T)
content:
Using an extended-Drude form for a single-band metal with plasma frequency ω_p and a frequency-dependent scattering rate Γ(ω) ∝ x = max(|ω|,T),
$$\sigma_1(\omega)\approx\frac{\omega_p^2}{4\pi}\frac{\Gamma(\omega)}{\omega^2+\Gamma(\omega)^2}.$$
For frequencies in the window T ≪ ω ≪ ω_c where Γ(ω)∝ω, this yields approximately
$$\sigma_1(\omega)\sim \mathrm{const}\times\frac{1}{\omega}.$$
Thus the optical conductivity should show a non-Drude broad high-frequency tail decaying roughly as 1/ω (below interband onset).

extra.notations:
- σ1(ω): real part of optical conductivity
- ω_p: plasma frequency
- Γ(ω): frequency-dependent scattering rate ∝ max(|ω|,T)
- other symbols as above

title: High‑pressure sintering conditions for Sm[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs
content: There exists a high‑pressure (HP) synthesis protocol for preparing polycrystalline Sm[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs (fluorine‑doped samarium iron oxyarsenide) in which a stoichiometric mixture of SmAs (samarium arsenide), elemental As (arsenic), Fe (iron), Fe_{2}O_{3} (iron(III) oxide), and FeF_{2} (iron(II) fluoride) is sintered at a pressure of 6 GPa and a temperature of 1250°C for two hours while contained in boron nitride (BN) crucibles.

extra.notations:
- Sm[O_{1-x}F_{x}]: samarium iron oxyarsenide with nominal fluorine doping level x  
- SmAs: samarium arsenide  
- Fe: elemental iron  
- Fe_{2}O_{3}: iron(III) oxide  
- FeF_{2}: iron(II) fluoride  
- GPa: gigapascal (unit of pressure)  
- °C: degrees Celsius (temperature)  
- BN: boron nitride

title: FeF_{2} and SmF_{3} are alternative fluoride sources
content: In solid‑state syntheses of SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}, both FeF_{2} (iron(II) fluoride) and SmF_{3} (samarium(III) fluoride) are used as fluoride‑containing precursors; therefore it is plausible that FeF_{2} and SmF_{3} can act as interchangeable fluorine sources for introducing nominal fluorine content $x$, provided stoichiometry and redox/balance adjustments are made in the precursor mix.

extra.notations:
- SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}: fluorine‑doped samarium iron oxyarsenide, nominal fluorine fraction x  
- FeF_{2}: iron(II) fluoride (used as F source in HP protocols 1426/1437)  
- SmF_{3}: samarium(III) fluoride (used as F source in protocol 162)

title: Elemental arsenic is likely added to compensate As volatility during HP sintering
content: The inclusion of elemental arsenic (As) alongside pre‑sintered SmAs in the HP precursor mixtures likely serves to compensate for arsenic loss (volatilization) at the high sintering temperature (1250°C), thereby preserving the intended As stoichiometry in the final Sm[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs product.

extra.notations:
- As: elemental arsenic (added to precursor mix)  
- SmAs: samarium arsenide (pre‑sintered in 1437)  
- Volatilization: loss of a volatile element (As) from the reacting mixture at high temperature

title: Decrease of low-energy electronic scattering rate below Tc
content: If the ARPES low-energy lineshape at momentum k and energy ω sharpens on cooling below the superconducting transition temperature Tc (as stated in the siblings), then the imaginary part of the electronic self-energy, Im Σ(k,ω), at the energies where the sharpening occurs must decrease below Tc. Here Σ(k,ω) is the single-particle self-energy that enters the retarded Green's function G(k,ω) = [ω - ε_k - Σ(k,ω)]^{-1}, and the ARPES spectral linewidth (full width at half maximum, Γ) is proportional to the inverse quasiparticle lifetime and to −2 Im Σ(k,ω) (up to factors of the quasiparticle residue and instrumental broadening). Resolution-limited quasiparticle peaks imply that −Im Σ(k,ω) becomes smaller than the instrumental resolution at those (k,ω).

extra.notations:
- Σ(k,ω): single-particle electronic self-energy (retarded).
- Im Σ(k,ω): imaginary part of Σ, which determines quasiparticle inverse lifetime.
- Γ: ARPES spectral linewidth (FWHM) at given (k,ω), Γ ∝ −2 Im Σ(k,ω) (modulo residue and resolution).
- G(k,ω): retarded single-particle Green's function.

title: Emergence of a coherent quasiparticle pole and transfer of spectral weight at Tc
content: The sharpening of low-energy ARPES features below Tc plausibly corresponds to the appearance (or substantial growth) of a coherent quasiparticle pole in the single-particle Green's function at energies near the gap edge and at momenta where peaks appear. In practical terms, the spectral function A(k,ω) = −(1/π) Im G(k,ω) can be usefully decomposed as a narrow Lorentzian-like coherent contribution with weight Z(k) and small width Γ_coh plus a broad incoherent background. The siblings' observations suggest that, at Tc, spectral weight is transferred from the broad incoherent background above Tc into the narrow coherent peak below Tc (i.e., Z(k) increases and Γ_coh decreases for those k and ω).

extra.notations:
- G(k,ω): retarded single-particle Green's function.
- A(k,ω): single-particle spectral function, A = −(1/π) Im G.
- Z(k): quasiparticle residue (weight of coherent pole).
- Γ_coh: width of the coherent quasiparticle contribution (∝ −Im Σ evaluated at the pole).

title: Oxygen in the nominal SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} must come from the oxidic starting reagent Fe2O3
content: Given the listed starting materials for the syntheses (pre-sintered SmAs, elemental As, Fe, and the only oxygen-bearing reagent Fe2O3, or in the alternative recipe SmF3/Fe and Fe2O3), the oxygen atoms required to build the nominal SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x} lattice (where $x$ is the nominal fluorine fraction) must be supplied by the iron(III) oxide reagent $Fe_{2}O_{3}$. Here $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$ denotes the intended product with one formula-unit worth of the anion site occupied by $1-x$ oxygen atoms and $x$ fluorine atoms.

extra.notations:
- $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$: target oxyarsenide with oxygen and fluorine on the same anion site, $0\le x\le1$. 
- $Fe_{2}O_{3}$: iron(III) oxide, the only listed oxygen-containing starting reagent in the given recipes.

title: High-temperature annealing around 1160–1200°C for extended time is likely required to form the ZrCuSiAs-type phase
content: To obtain the ZrCuSiAs-type ($P4/nmm$) tetragonal phase of $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$ (with nominal $x=0.15$), it is likely necessary to perform high-temperature solid-state reactions with annealing temperatures in the range ~1160–1200°C for long dwell times (e.g., 40 hours) under controlled (evacuated) atmosphere. Here $T_c$ denotes superconducting transition temperature and the ZrCuSiAs-type structure refers to the layered tetragonal arrangement targeted for superconductivity.

extra.notations:
- $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$: target compound with nominal fluorine fraction $x=0.15$.
- ZrCuSiAs-type ($P4/nmm$): the tetragonal crystal structure characteristic of the 1111 iron pnictides.

title: Choice of fluorine precursor (SmF3 vs FeF2) will likely affect secondary phases and chemical byproducts
content: Using different fluorine-containing starting reagents (e.g., $SmF_{3}$ as in node 167 versus $FeF_{2}$ as in nodes 1423/1425) to reach the same nominal fluorine level $x=0.15$ is likely to produce different reaction byproducts and impurity-phase distributions (for example, differing amounts of residual Sm–F–O phases, iron fluorides, or unreacted fluoride), because the cation accompanying the fluoride determines which elements are locally in excess or deficit during solid-state reaction and therefore which secondary phases are thermodynamically/kinetically accessible.

extra.notations:
- $SmF_{3}$: samarium trifluoride, a Sm-bearing fluoride reagent.
- $FeF_{2}$: iron(II) fluoride, an Fe-bearing fluoride reagent.
- Secondary phases: unintended crystalline compounds (e.g., SmOF, SmF3 remnants, FeF2/FeAs impurities) formed during synthesis.

title: Use of >99.99% pure starting reagents likely reduces impurity-driven disorder and improves superconducting phase quality
content: When all starting reagents (pre-sintered SmAs, As, Fe, $Fe_{2}O_{3}$, $FeF_{2}$) are of very high purity (>99.99%), it is likely that the resulting $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$ samples will contain fewer extrinsic impurity phases and less chemical inhomogeneity, which plausibly improves superconducting phase fraction, sharpness of the superconducting transition $T_c$, and reproducibility between batches.

extra.notations:
- Purity >99.99%: mass fraction of desired chemical species in the reagent is at least 99.99%.
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature; sharpness refers to width of resistive/magnetic transition.

title: Zn-induced low-energy LDOS in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ must be four-fold (cross-shaped)
content:
Let LDOS(r,E) denote the local quasiparticle density of states at position r and energy E.
Let Zn denote a substitutional zinc impurity on a Cu site in the CuO2 plane of Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (Bi-2212).
Proposition 1539 states that Zn impurities in Bi-2212 produce intense low-energy (intra-gap) quasiparticle resonances, i.e. enhanced LDOS(r,E) in the impurity vicinity for energies E well within the superconducting gap.
Proposition 1562 states that in a superconductor whose order parameter has $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ symmetry, quasiparticle scattering from an atomic-scale impurity produces a spatial LDOS pattern that is four-fold symmetric (cross-shaped) with arms aligned along the crystallographic a and b axes (the nodal directions).
Therefore, for Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ (assumed to have $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ pairing), the low-energy impurity-induced resonance described in 1539 must exhibit the four-fold symmetric cross-shaped spatial modulation predicted in 1562: i.e., LDOS(r,E_res) measured at the resonance energy $E_{\rm res}$ will show a pronounced cross-shaped pattern centered at the Zn site with arms along the a and b crystal axes.

extra.notations:
- LDOS(r,E): local density of states at position r and energy E.
- Zn: substitutional zinc on a Cu site in the CuO2 plane.
- Bi-2212: Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ, the cuprate superconductor.
- $E_{\rm res}$: energy of the Zn-induced intra-gap quasiparticle resonance (a low/intra-gap energy).
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: superconducting order parameter symmetry with nodes along the crystal a and b axes.

title: Observed cross-shaped Zn LDOS would serve as strong local evidence for $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ pairing in Bi-2212
content:
If STM differential conductance ($dI/dV$) maps of Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ at energies near the Zn-induced resonance energy $E_{\rm res}$ show a pronounced four-fold symmetric (cross-shaped) LDOS modulation centered on the Zn site with arms aligned along the crystallographic a and b axes, then this observation constitutes strong local, phase-sensitive evidence that the superconducting order parameter has $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ symmetry.
Here "strong" means the observation is highly indicative but not strictly definitive because alternative causes of spatial anisotropy (e.g., anisotropic impurity potentials, surface reconstruction, or other forms of electronic nematicity) could in principle produce anisotropic LDOS.

extra.notations:
- LDOS(r,E): local density of states at position r and energy E.
- $E_{\rm res}$: energy of the Zn-induced intra-gap resonance.
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: superconducting order parameter symmetry with nodes along the crystal a and b axes.
- STM $dI/dV$: differential conductance measured by scanning tunneling microscopy, approximately proportional to LDOS.

title: Numerical values for the partial Sommerfeld coefficients
content: Let γn denote the total normal-state Sommerfeld coefficient and γ1, γ2 the partial Sommerfeld coefficients for bands 1 and 2 with γ1+γ2=γn. Given γn ≈ 2.65 mJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻² and the band-weight x ≡ γ1/γn in the interval 0.50–0.55, the partial coefficients satisfy γ1 = x·γn ≈ 1.33–1.46 mJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻² and γ2 = γn−γ1 ≈ 1.19–1.32 mJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻². These ranges follow directly from the reported γn and x.

extra.notations:
- γn: total normal-state Sommerfeld coefficient (mJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻²)
- γi: partial Sommerfeld coefficient of band i (mJ·mol⁻¹·K⁻²)
- x: γ1/γn, relative weight of band 1

title: Nonzero interband coupling is required to have both gaps share the same Tc
content: Let Δ1(T) and Δ2(T) be the temperature-dependent superconducting gaps on two distinct bands and Tc the single measured superconducting critical temperature. The siblings state (i) the smaller-gap ratio 2Δ2/kB Tc ≈ 1.2 (anomalously small) and (ii) in the two-gap α-model both gaps follow the same normalized gap function δ(t) with t = T/Tc so that both gaps close at the same Tc. These two facts imply that, unless the two independent bands coincidentally have identical intrinsic transition temperatures, a nonzero interband coupling (pair-transfer term) is necessary to raise the intrinsic closing temperature of the small-gap band to the common Tc. Therefore interband coupling between the bands is required.

extra.notations:
- Δi(T): superconducting gap on band i as a function of temperature
- Δi,0: gap on band i at T=0
- Tc: superconducting critical temperature
- δ(t): normalized gap function with t = T/Tc
- kB: Boltzmann constant

title: Large-gap band (2D) supplies the dominant pairing strength (σ-band driven pairing)
content: Denote the larger gap by Δ1 (reported 2Δ1/kB Tc ≈ 4.0) and the smaller gap by Δ2 (reported 2Δ2/kB Tc ≈ 1.2). The siblings associate Δ1 with two-dimensional (2D) Fermi-surface sheets and Δ2 with three-dimensional (3D) sheets, and they report a gap ratio for Δ1 exceeding the weak-coupling BCS value (3.52). It is therefore plausible that the 2D (σ-like) band responsible for Δ1 has substantially stronger electron–phonon pairing interactions and provides the principal contribution to the superconducting pairing strength, while the 3D (π-like) band with Δ2 is a more weakly paired band whose gap is elevated to Tc by interband coupling. This allocation explains the large jump at Tc being dominated by Δ1 and the low-temperature exponential tail being dominated by Δ2.

extra.notations:
- Δ1, Δ2: larger and smaller superconducting gaps (at T=0) respectively
- Tc: superconducting critical temperature
- σ-band: quasi-two-dimensional band (2D) commonly identified in MgB2 band structure
- π-band: three-dimensional band (3D) commonly identified in MgB2 band structure

title: ARPES leading-edge shift around the Fermi surface follows a d-wave form and vanishes at nodes for Tc < T < T*
content: For underdoped Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8−δ (Bi2212), at any temperature T in the normal-state window Tc < T < T*, Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy (ARPES) leading-edge midpoint measurements as a function of crystal momentum k = (k_x,k_y) along the Fermi surface will exhibit a momentum-dependent leading-edge energy shift Δ_LE(k) (defined as the shift of the spectrum's leading-edge midpoint relative to a metallic reference) that (i) is nonzero where the pseudogap is present, (ii) follows the same angular/momentum dependence as the superconducting gap, i.e. is proportional to |cos k_x − cos k_y| to leading order on the Fermi surface, and (iii) vanishes on the nodal lines defined by cos k_x = cos k_y. Thus ARPES will show maximal leading-edge shifts near the antinodal momenta and zero shift along the nodal (diagonal) directions for Tc < T < T*.

extra.notations:
- k = (k_x,k_y): in-plane crystal momentum in the CuO2 Brillouin zone.  
- T_c: superconducting transition temperature.  
- T*: pseudogap crossover (closing) temperature, with T* > T_c.  
- Δ_LE(k): leading-edge midpoint shift at momentum k measured relative to a metallic reference; Δ_LE(k)>0 indicates a pseudogap at k.  
- ARPES: Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy.

title: Pseudogap likely reflects pairing correlations (preformed pairs) with d-wave symmetry
content: The observation that the normal-state pseudogap in underdoped Bi2212 (present for T<T* with T*>T_c) exhibits the same d-wave momentum dependence as the superconducting gap plausibly indicates that the pseudogap arises from pairing correlations (preformed Cooper pairs) that share the superconducting order-parameter symmetry but lack global phase coherence above T_c. In other words, the pseudogap may be a precursor superconducting gap with d_{x^2−y^2}-like symmetry rather than (or in addition to) a distinct competing order with different symmetry.

extra.notations:
- d_{x^2−y^2}-like symmetry: gap function ∝ (cos k_x − cos k_y) on the CuO2 Fermi surface.  
- Preformed pairs: local or short-range pairing correlations that do not establish long-range phase coherence (hence no zero-resistance superconductivity).

title: Numeric ordering of reported bond lengths and layer separations
content:
Using the reported distances from the two siblings, the following strict numerical ordering holds:
$$
d_{\mathrm{Cu\text{-}O(plane)}} = 1.928\ \AA \;<\; d_{\mathrm{Tl\text{-}O(intersheet)}} = 1.995\ \AA \;<\; s_{\mathrm{Tl\text{-}Tl\ layer}} = 2.20\ \AA \;<\; d_{\mathrm{Ca\text{-}O}} = 2.48\ \AA \;<\; d_{\mathrm{Cu\text{-}O(apical)}} = 2.70\ \AA.
$$
Here each symbol denotes the distance value reported in the cited siblings (numbers taken directly from Propositions 1391 and 487).

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathrm{Cu\text{-}O(plane)}}$: in-plane Cu–O bond length in $Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8}$ (given as $1.928\ \AA$). 
- $d_{\mathrm{Tl\text{-}O(intersheet)}}$: Tl–O intersheet bond length in $Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CuO_{6}$ (given as $1.995\ \AA$).
- $s_{\mathrm{Tl\text{-}Tl\ layer}}$: physical separation between adjacent thallium metal layers in $Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CuO_{6}$ (given as $2.20\ \AA$).
- $d_{\mathrm{Ca\text{-}O}}$: Ca–O coordination distance in $Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8}$ (given as $2.48\ \AA$).
- $d_{\mathrm{Cu\text{-}O(apical)}}$: distance to the fifth (apical) oxygen for Cu in $Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8}$ (given as $2.70\ \AA$).

title: Short Tl–O intersheet bonds imply significant chemical/interlayer coupling through Tl–O layers
content: The reported Tl–O intersheet bond length $d_{\mathrm{Tl\text{-}O}} \approx 1.995\ \AA$ and the small Tl–Tl layer separation $s_{\mathrm{Tl\text{-}Tl}} \approx 2.20\ \AA$ in $Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CuO_{6}$ are consistent with substantial chemical bonding and non-negligible orbital overlap involving Tl and O across the Tl-containing layers; this structural compactness plausibly increases electronic (and possibly vibrational) coupling between adjacent structural blocks compared with materials where the corresponding charge-reservoir layers are thicker or contain longer M–O separations.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathrm{Tl\text{-}O}}$: Tl–O intersheet bond length (approx.\ $1.995\ \AA$ from Proposition 487).
- $s_{\mathrm{Tl\text{-}Tl}}$: separation between adjacent Tl metal layers (approx.\ $2.20\ \AA$ from Proposition 487).
- "Interlayer coupling": overlap-mediated electronic hopping or hybridization through atoms in the intervening (Tl–O) layers.

title: Cu in $Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8}$ is effectively square-planar with weak apical coupling, favoring quasi-two-dimensional electronic structure
content: The copper atoms in $Tl_{2}Ba_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{8}$ have four short in-plane Cu–O bonds of length $1.928\ \AA$ and a much longer "fifth" (apical) Cu–O distance of $2.70\ \AA$, indicating that the Cu coordination is dominated by the planar $CuO_4$ unit and that hybridization with the apical oxygen is weak. Consequently, the low-energy electronic states are likely concentrated in the CuO$_2$ planes, consistent with a predominantly quasi-two-dimensional electronic structure.

extra.notations:
- $d_{\mathrm{Cu\text{-}O(plane)}}$: in-plane Cu–O bond length ($1.928\ \AA$).
- $d_{\mathrm{Cu\text{-}O(apical)}}$: apical Cu–O distance ($2.70\ \AA$).
- "Low-energy electronic states": states near the Fermi level that govern transport and pairing.

title: Hg-1223 phase must have a higher superconducting transition temperature than Hg-1212 in the mixed sample
content: The sample described contains two superconducting phases: Hg-1212 (formula $HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+x}$, with $m=2$) and Hg-1223 (formula $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$, with $m=3$). From proposition 576, for the mercury-based homologous series $HgBa_{2}Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+\delta}$ the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ at ambient pressure increases with the number of $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell $m$ (for $m=1,2,3$). Therefore, in this sample the Hg-1223 phase ( $m=3$ ) necessarily has a higher superconducting transition temperature than the Hg-1212 phase ( $m=2$ ). Consequently, measurements sensitive to superconducting onset (e.g., electrical resistivity $\rho(T)$ or magnetic susceptibility $\chi(T)$) on the mixed-phase sample must exhibit two distinct superconducting onsets (or transitions), with the higher-temperature onset attributable to the Hg-1223 phase and the lower-temperature onset to the Hg-1212 phase.

extra.notations:
- $HgBa_{2}Ca_{m-1}Cu_{m}O_{2m+2+\delta}$: mercury-based cuprate with $m$ $CuO_{2}$ layers per cell and oxygen non-stoichiometry $\delta$. 
- $m$: number of $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (onset).
- Hg-1212: shorthand for $HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+x}$ ($m=2$).
- Hg-1223: shorthand for $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ ($m=3$).

title: Extra $c$-axis length between Hg-1212 and Hg-1223 corresponds to one additional Ca+CuO$_2$ block thickness (~3.4 Å)
content: The reported tetragonal lattice parameters are $c\!=\!12.7(2)\,\text{\AA}$ for Hg-1212 and $c\!=\!16.1(3)\,\text{\AA}$ for Hg-1223. The difference $\Delta c \approx 16.1-12.7 = 3.4\,\text{\AA}$ is plausibly the thickness of a single additional Ca$+$CuO$_2$ structural block that is present when going from $m=2$ to $m=3$. Thus the added structural unit (one Ca layer plus one CuO$_2$ layer and their immediate interlayer spacings) has an effective thickness of approximately $3.4\,\text{\AA}$ in these compounds.

extra.notations:
- $c$: lattice parameter along the crystallographic $c$-axis (vertical stacking direction), in angstroms ($\text{\AA}$).
- $\Delta c$: difference in $c$ between Hg-1223 and Hg-1212, $\Delta c = c(\mathrm{Hg\text{-}1223}) - c(\mathrm{Hg\text{-}1212})$.
- Ca+CuO$_2$ block: the structural unit added when $m$ increases by 1.

title: Long-period $c\approx86.4\,$Å supercell likely arises from ordered stacking or modulation of Hg-1212 and Hg-1223 units
content: The reported ordered superstructure with a supercell period $c\approx86.4\,\text{\AA}$ plausibly reflects a long-period modulation or ordered stacking sequence along the $c$-axis involving the two distinct unit cells (Hg-1212 with $c\approx12.7\,\text{\AA}$ and Hg-1223 with $c\approx16.1\,\text{\AA}$). Such a supercell could arise from periodic repetition of a mixed-stack motif (for example, a sequence combining several Hg-1212 and Hg-1223 unit cells, or regular faulting/ordering of additional oxygen/hg-oxide layers) that produces an overall repeat near $86.4\,\text{\AA}$.

extra.notations:
- supercell $c$: observed long-period repeat along the $c$-axis, $c_{\mathrm{super}}\approx86.4\,\text{\AA}$.
- $c_{1212}=12.7\,\text{\AA}$, $c_{1223}=16.1\,\text{\AA}$: unit-cell $c$ values for Hg-1212 and Hg-1223 respectively.

title: Variational family ψ_λ = exp(−λK)P_Gψ_0 can represent AF, AF+SC and SC phases
content: The family of trial wavefunctions ψ_λ = exp(−λ K) P_G ψ_0 (where $K$ is the kinetic operator, $P_G$ the Gutzwiller projection and $ψ_0$ a one-particle reference state) is variationally flexible enough to produce, under appropriate microscopic parameters and hole density $x$, (i) an antiferromagnetic insulating state at low $x$, (ii) a coexisting antiferromagnetic-plus-superconducting state at intermediate $x$, and (iii) a pure superconducting state at larger $x$. Here $x$ denotes the hole concentration per CuO2 unit. This follows because both Proposition 1255 (Hubbard model on a $10\times10$ lattice with $U/t=18$, $t'=0$) and Proposition 1260 (three-band d–p model on an $8\times8$ lattice) used the same exponential kinetic renormalization ansatz and obtained AF stability at low $x$ and superconducting tendencies near the AF–metal boundary (including coexistence in at least one study).

extra.notations:
- $ψ_λ$: variational wavefunction $ψ_λ=\exp(-\lambda K)P_G ψ_0$.
- $K$: kinetic part of the Hamiltonian (hopping operator).
- $P_G$: Gutzwiller projection operator.
- $ψ_0$: one-particle reference state (e.g. AF mean-field or BCS state).
- $x$: hole concentration (doping level).

title: Pairing symmetry depends on model details and effective local interactions
content: Within microscopic lattice descriptions of the CuO2 plane, the symmetry of the superconducting order parameter (e.g., $d$-wave versus $s$-wave) is likely determined by short-range microscopic parameters: in the single-band Hubbard realization studied in Proposition 1255 the optimized superconducting state is $d$-wave, whereas in the three-band d–p model of Proposition 1260 an effective on-site attraction ("negative-$U$" region for large $\Delta_{dp}$) can favor charge order or an $s$-wave pairing state. Therefore, it is plausible that (i) the robust $d$-wave pairing found in single-band Hubbard-type parameter regimes reflects dominant nearest-neighbor pairing channels generated by strong on-site repulsion and kinetic renormalization, while (ii) parameter regimes of the multi-band model that produce effective on-site attraction (large $\Delta_{dp}$ and suitable $U_d,U_p$) can stabilize $s$-wave or on-site paired states instead.

extra.notations:
- $d$-wave: superconducting gap changing sign under 90° rotation (nearest-neighbor sign structure).
- $s$-wave: superconducting gap with full on-site/extended s symmetry (no sign change under 90° rotation).
- $\Delta_{dp}=\varepsilon_p-\varepsilon_d$: energy-level separation between O $p$ and Cu $d$ orbitals.
- "negative-$U$ region": parameter regime where effective local attraction binds two holes on the same Cu site.

title: Kinetic-energy renormalization (exp(−λK)) plausibly enhances pairing near the AF–metal boundary
content: Because both studies obtain superconducting tendencies (or phases) near the antiferromagnetic–metallic boundary while employing the exponential kinetic renormalization factor $\exp(-\lambda K)$ in the variational wavefunction, it is plausible that the kinetic-energy renormalization encoded by the variational parameter $\lambda$ amplifies pairing correlations (by improving kinetic energy gain of paired states) and thus plays an active role in stabilizing superconductivity adjacent to antiferromagnetism. Here $K$ denotes the kinetic hopping operator and $\lambda$ is the real variational parameter controlling its weight in the ansatz.

extra.notations:
- $K$: kinetic part of the Hamiltonian (hopping operator).
- $\lambda$: real variational parameter in $ψ_λ=\exp(-\lambda K)P_G ψ_0$ that modifies kinetic correlations.
- "AF–metal boundary": parameter/doping boundary separating antiferromagnetically ordered and paramagnetic/metallic regions.

title: Existence of bulk superconductivity near 203 K in H–S compounds at ~155 GPa
content: At pressure P ≈ 155 GPa, hydrogen–sulfur compounds with stoichiometry close to H3S exhibit bulk superconductivity with superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}\approx 203\,$K, as demonstrated by (i) vanishing electrical resistivity (reported minimum resistivity $\rho\sim 10^{-11}\ \Omega\cdot\mathrm{m}$) and (ii) a sharp superconducting magnetic response (diamagnetic-to-paramagnetic change) in susceptibility measurements. This conclusion refers to the same class of compressed H–S materials for which first-principles calculations and measurements indicate $T_c$ values on the order of $10^2\,$K in the 100–200 GPa range.

extra.notations:
- $P$: pressure (GPa)
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature (K)
- $\rho$: electrical resistivity ($\Omega\cdot$m)

title: High-$T_c$ phase arises from H-rich (H3S-like) stoichiometry produced from H2S under pressure
content: The experimentally observed high critical temperature $T_c\approx 203\,$K at P≈155 GPa most plausibly originates from an H-rich phase (stoichiometry close to H3S) formed from the starting H2S sample under compression (for example by decomposition or reassembly to H3S + S). Calculations that assume H2S stoichiometry predict a much lower $T_c\approx 80\,$K at similar pressures; therefore the much higher experimental $T_c$ implies formation of a different, hydrogen-richer superconducting phase.

extra.notations:
- H2S: hydrogen sulfide (initial sample molecule)
- H3S: hydrogen trihydride (H-rich stoichiometry proposed at high pressure)
- $P$: pressure (GPa)
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature (K)

title: Superconductivity is likely phonon-mediated with strong electron–phonon coupling involving hydrogen vibrations
content: The superconducting state observed at $T_c\approx203\,$K at high pressure is likely conventional (phonon-mediated) superconductivity arising from strong electron–phonon coupling, with substantial contribution from high-frequency hydrogen vibrational modes. In this statement, $\alpha^{2}F(\omega)$ denotes the Eliashberg electron–phonon spectral function and $\lambda$ its integrated electron–phonon coupling; "phonon-mediated" means Cooper pairing is primarily due to electron–phonon interaction as described by Eliashberg theory.

extra.notations:
- $\alpha^{2}F(\omega)$: Eliashberg electron–phonon spectral function (frequency-dependent)
- $\lambda$: integrated electron–phonon coupling parameter (dimensionless)
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature (K)

title: Magnetic spectral weight at tens of meV is localized in Q near the antiferromagnetic ridge and is directly observable in Q‑scans
content: At temperature T ≈ 5 K, the magnetic (spin) spectral weight that appears at energy transfers in the multi–tens-of-meV range (i.e., at energy ℏω on the order of tens of meV) is also concentrated in momentum space near the antiferromagnetic ridge wave vectors of the form Q = (1/2,1/2,l) (reciprocal‑lattice units). Consequently, performing momentum-transfer (Q) scans across the ridge at those energy transfers will reveal a magnetic signal (an excess in the magnetic part of S(Q,ℏω) or in Im{χ(Q,ℏω)}) that is distinguishable from the more slowly varying nuclear scattering background.

extra.notations:
- T: temperature in kelvin (K). 
- meV: millielectronvolt (1 meV = 1×10⁻³ eV). 
- Q: momentum-transfer (scattering) vector in reciprocal space; Q = (h,k,l) in reciprocal‑lattice units (r.l.u.). 
- S(Q,ℏω): dynamical structure factor; Im{χ(Q,ℏω)}: imaginary part of the dynamical susceptibility. 
- "Magnetic spectral weight": intensity in S(Q,ℏω) or Im{χ} attributable to spin fluctuations.

title: Tens-of-meV, Q-localized excitations are likely the superconductivity-associated magnetic resonance
content: The magnetic excitations observed at multi–tens-of-meV energies and localized near Q = (1/2,1/2,l) at T ≈ 5 K are likely the superconductivity-associated magnetic resonance mode: a collective spin excitation that appears below the superconducting transition temperature Tc, is centered at the antiferromagnetic wavevector (1/2,1/2,l), and carries substantial magnetic spectral weight at a characteristic energy ℏω_res on the order of tens of meV.

extra.notations:
- Tc: superconducting critical temperature of YBa2Cu3O6.92 (≈ 90–93 K for near‑optimal oxygenation). 
- ℏω_res: resonance energy (energy of the collective magnetic mode), in meV.

title: Multiple distinct fluoride precursors are used
content: From the cited synthesis protocols it follows that at least two different fluoride-containing precursor compounds have been employed in solid-state syntheses of $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$: samarium(III) fluoride ($SmF_{3}$) and iron(II) fluoride ($FeF_{2}$). Here $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$ denotes fluorine-doped samarium iron oxyarsenide and $x$ the nominal F content.

extra.notations:
- $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$: fluorine-doped samarium iron oxyarsenide (product). 
- $SmF_{3}$: samarium(III) fluoride (fluoride precursor in node 166). 
- $FeF_{2}$: iron(II) fluoride (fluoride precursor in nodes 1426/1437).

title: Fe and Fe2O3 are included in the reported precursor mixtures
content: All provided solid-state synthesis descriptions for $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$ include elemental iron ($Fe$) and iron(III) oxide ($Fe_{2}O_{3}$) as components of the stoichiometric precursor mixtures.

extra.notations:
- $Fe$: elemental iron (precursor). 
- $Fe_{2}O_{3}$: iron(III) oxide (precursor).
- $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_{x}$: fluorine-doped samarium iron oxyarsenide (product).

title: Choice of fluoride precursor correlates with inclusion of elemental As in the mixture
content: Within the provided protocols, routes that use $FeF_{2}$ as the fluoride source (nodes 1426 and 1437) also include elemental arsenic ($As$) in the precursor mixture, whereas the protocol that uses $SmF_{3}$ (node 166) does not list additional elemental $As$. Thus, it is plausible that using $FeF_{2}$ commonly accompanies addition of elemental $As$, while using $SmF_{3}$ may allow omission of separate elemental $As$ in the reported stoichiometries.

extra.notations:
- $FeF_{2}$: iron(II) fluoride (fluoride source that also supplies Fe). 
- $SmF_{3}$: samarium(III) fluoride (fluoride source that also supplies Sm). 
- $As$: elemental arsenic.

title: Interlayer registry of charge stripes differs between the two materials
content: For the monoclinic notation used here, the Miller index $l$ labels the reciprocal-space coordinate along the crystallographic $c$ axis (perpendicular to the transition-metal-oxide planes). A charge-order diffraction peak observed at integer $l$ parity corresponds to a particular phase relation of the charge modulation between adjacent planes: peaks with even $l$ (including $l=0$) indicate the same phase (in‑phase stacking) of the modulation from layer to layer, whereas peaks with odd $l$ (e.g. $l=1$) indicate a phase inversion (staggering) between adjacent layers. Proposition 149 reports a charge peak at $l=0$ for La1.48Nd0.4Sr0.12CuO4, implying in‑phase stacking of charge stripes along $c$. Proposition 153 reports charge superlattice peaks with $l=1$ for La2NiO4, implying that the hole stripes are staggered (phase-shifted by $\pi$) between adjacent $NiO_2$ layers. Therefore, the interlayer registry (stacking pattern along $c$) of charge stripes is different in these two materials: in‑phase in the cited cuprate sample and staggered in the cited nickelate sample.

extra.notations:
- $l$: Miller index along the crystallographic $c$ axis (reciprocal-space coordinate).
- "in‑phase stacking": adjacent planes have the same phase of the charge modulation.
- "staggered"/"phase inversion": adjacent planes have a $\pi$ phase shift of the charge modulation.

title: Atomic displacements associated with the charge modulation are very small (order 10⁻³–10⁻² of the in‑plane lattice)
content: Let $\delta$ be a characteristic amplitude of the atomic displacement pattern (perpendicular or parallel to bonds) associated with the charge-density modulation, and let $a$ be the in‑plane lattice constant. The measured intensity of the charge superlattice reflection in La1.48Nd0.4Sr0.12CuO4 is approximately $10^{-6}$ times the intensity of the fundamental $(020)$ reflection. Under the usual kinematic approximation for scattering from small displacements, superlattice intensity scales roughly as $(\delta / a)^2$ (modulo form-factor and multiplicity factors). Hence $\delta / a \sim \sqrt{10^{-6}} \sim 10^{-3}$, giving $\delta$ of order $10^{-3}a$ (for $a\approx 3.8$–4.0 Å typical in these materials, this implies $\delta \sim 0.004$ Å). Allowing for form-factor, Debye–Waller and structure-factor prefactors, a conservative plausible range is $\delta/a \sim 10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$.

extra.notations:
- $\delta$: characteristic amplitude of lattice displacement associated with charge ordering.
- $a$: in‑plane lattice constant (typical value $\approx 3.8$–4.0 Å in these compounds).
- $I_{\text{super}}/I_{\text{fund}}$: ratio of superlattice to fundamental Bragg intensities.

title: Charge stripes are (or act like) antiphase domain walls of the spin order, explaining the factor-of-two relation between charge and spin modulation periods
content: Let $\varepsilon$ be the spin-order incommensurability (shift from the antiferromagnetic Bragg point) so the spin modulation period is $\lambda_{\text{spin}} \propto 1/\varepsilon$. Both propositions report charge-order peaks at wave-vector shifts of magnitude $2\varepsilon$, i.e. a charge modulation period $\lambda_{\text{charge}} \propto 1/(2\varepsilon)=\lambda_{\text{spin}}/2$. A natural microscopic interpretation is that the charge stripes reside on lines (or narrow regions) that act as antiphase domain walls for the antiferromagnetic spin pattern: each charge stripe reverses the spin-phase, so the spin modulation completes a $2\pi$ cycle only after encountering two neighboring charge stripes, doubling the spin wavelength relative to the charge spacing. Thus the observed $2\varepsilon$ charge wave vector is consistent with charge lines separating antiphase spin domains.

extra.notations:
- $\varepsilon$: spin incommensurability parameter (reciprocal-lattice units).
- $\lambda_{\text{spin}}$: wavelength of spin modulation (proportional to $1/\varepsilon$).
- $\lambda_{\text{charge}}$: wavelength of charge modulation (proportional to $1/(2\varepsilon)$).
- "antiphase domain wall": a linear defect across which the antiferromagnetic spin phase flips by $\pi$.

title: Existence of a cation–oxygen bond ≈ 2.48 Å in the refined structure
content: At least one cation–oxygen interatomic distance in the refined Tl2Ba2CaCu2O8 structure equals approximately $2.48\,$\AA. Specifically, Proposition 1388 reports a Ca(1)–O(1) distance of $2.478(4)\,$\AA\ (where parentheses denote the standard uncertainty in the last digits) and Proposition 1396 reports an average short Tl–O bond of $2.48\,$\AA; therefore the structure contains a cation–oxygen bond whose measured length is $2.478(4)\,$\AA\ (numerically consistent with $2.48\,$\AA\ within the stated uncertainties).

extra.notations:
- \AA: angstrom, $1\,$\AA$=10^{-10}\,$m.
- Ca(1)–O(1): the bond between the Ca site labelled Ca(1) and the oxygen site labelled O(1).
- Tl–O: a thallium–oxygen bond.

title: Short Tl–O bonds reported may correspond to (or be averaged into) the reported Ca(1)–O(1) distance because of Tl-on-Ca site mixing
content: Given the reported partial occupancy of Tl on the Ca site (Proposition 1396) and the measured Ca(1)–O(1) distance $2.478(4)\,$\AA\ (Proposition 1388), it is plausible that the short average Tl–O distance of $2.48\,$\AA\ arises from Tl atoms occupying the Ca(1) site (or from statistical mixing of Tl and Ca on that site). In other words, some fraction of the nominal Ca(1)–O(1) contacts measured in the refinement may actually be Tl–O contacts (or a weighted average of Tl–O and Ca–O) because of the reported Tl substitution on the Ca site and the oxygen disorder in the Tl–O layers.

extra.notations:
- Tl-on-Ca site mixing: partial substitution of thallium atoms onto crystallographic positions nominally assigned to calcium.
- Averaging: in a refinement with mixed occupancy or unresolved disorder, reported interatomic distances can reflect occupancy-weighted averages of different cation–oxygen distances.

title: Charge-sector Drude weight equals the decoupled Gaussian value (unrenormalized)
content: Under the stated decoupling conditions (the unitary transformation separates spin and charge and the effective charge couplings to the environment vanish, i.e. $\Delta V_c=0$ and $\varepsilon^*=0$), the low-energy charge sector of the 1DEG is governed by a Gaussian (Luttinger-liquid) Hamiltonian with charge velocity $v_c$ and Luttinger parameter $K_c$. Consequently the zero-temperature Drude weight $D$ (the zero-frequency charge stiffness) equals the value computed from this decoupled Gaussian theory and does not depend on the spin / pseudospin / environmental couplings. In standard units (and up to model-dependent prefactors), $D$ is proportional to the product $v_c K_c$; since $v_c$ (and $K_c$) are those of the decoupled Gaussian model, $D$ is unrenormalized by the environment or by the strong-coupling physics confined to the spin sector.

extra.notations:
- $v_c$: charge propagation velocity of the 1DEG (stripe) in the decoupled charge theory.
- $K_c$: Luttinger-liquid charge exponent for the 1DEG in the decoupled theory.
- $D$: zero-temperature Drude weight (charge stiffness / superfluid phase stiffness); in Luttinger-liquid theory $D\propto v_c K_c$ up to units-dependent prefactors.
- $\Delta V_c,\varepsilon^*$: effective charge couplings which are set to zero in the decoupling limit.

title: Pairing amplitude / pairing energy scale is set by the spin sector, while phase stiffness is set by the charge sector
content: In the decoupling limit (charge sector Gaussian and unrenormalized; $\Delta V_c=0$, $\varepsilon^*=0$), the energy scale that produces pair binding (the pairing amplitude or pairing gap) is plausibly determined by the spin-sector dynamics (for example an induced spin gap $\overline{\Delta}_s$), whereas the phase stiffness (zero-temperature superfluid stiffness / Drude weight) is fixed by the decoupled charge parameters $v_c$ and $K_c$. Therefore the superconducting transition temperature $T_c$ (or the onset of phase-coherent superconductivity) is likely limited by the smaller of (i) the pairing scale set by the spin sector and (ii) the phase-ordering scale set by the charge-sector stiffness, with the latter not suppressed by heavy renormalization from the environment.

extra.notations:
- $\overline{\Delta}_s$: induced spin gap in the 1DEG (spin-sector energy scale).
- $T_c$: superconducting transition temperature (phase-coherence onset).
- $D$: phase stiffness / Drude weight (see deduction).

title: Experimental signatures: charge transport vs magnetic/thermodynamic probes will appear decoupled
content: If the charge sector is decoupled and its parameters ($v_c,K_c$) are unrenormalized while the spin sector exhibits strong renormalizations, then measurements sensitive to charge stiffness (e.g. low-frequency optical conductivity/Drude weight, London penetration depth in a superconducting stripe array, or charge transport at low temperature) will reflect the "light" decoupled charge parameters. In contrast, probes sensitive to spin or entropy (e.g. static magnetic susceptibility, spin-resolved spectroscopies, low-temperature magnetic contribution to specific heat) can display large renormalizations or heavy-like behavior. Thus one should observe a dichotomy: nominally light charge response together with heavy/strongly-renormalized spin thermodynamics.

extra.notations:
- Optical Drude weight: integrated spectral weight at zero (or low) frequency in optical conductivity linked to charge stiffness $D$.
- Magnetic susceptibility / specific heat: probes primarily sensitive to spin and thermodynamic degrees of freedom.

title: Measured total inelastic intensity at tens of meV contains nonzero non‑magnetic part
content:
Let I_total(Q,ℏω) denote the measured inelastic neutron scattering intensity as a function of momentum transfer Q and energy transfer ℏω, and decompose it as
I_total(Q,ℏω) = I_mag(Q,ℏω) + I_nuc(Q,ℏω) + I_other(Q,ℏω),
where I_mag is the magnetic (spin) contribution, I_nuc is the nuclear (non‑magnetic) contribution, and I_other stands for other small contributions (e.g., background, instrumental). From Proposition 1593 there exists a measurable nuclear contribution I_nuc(Q,ℏω) in YBa2Cu3O6+x (including x≈0.92). From Proposition 1509 there is appreciable magnetic spectral weight I_mag(Q,ℏω) at multi‑tens‑of‑meV energies in YBa2Cu3O6.92. Therefore the measured I_total at those energies necessarily contains a nonzero nuclear (non‑magnetic) component in addition to the magnetic component; it is therefore incorrect to attribute the entirety of I_total at tens of meV to magnetic excitations without accounting for I_nuc.

extra.notations:
- I_total(Q,ℏω): total measured inelastic neutron scattering intensity at momentum transfer Q and energy transfer ℏω.
- I_mag(Q,ℏω): magnetic (spin) contribution to I_total.
- I_nuc(Q,ℏω): nuclear (non‑magnetic) contribution to I_total.
- ℏω: energy transfer (ℏ is the reduced Planck constant).

title: Separation procedures will be needed and will likely confirm significant magnetic weight at tens of meV
content: It is likely that quantitative statements that magnetic (spin) excitations carry appreciable spectral weight at multi‑tens‑of‑meV in YBa2Cu3O6.92 require explicit separation of I_mag and I_nuc (for example by neutron polarization analysis, by exploiting different Q‑dependences, or by temperature dependence). Given Proposition 1509 (an explicit claim of appreciable magnetic spectral weight at ~5 K) and Proposition 1593 (existence of measurable nuclear scattering), applying standard separation methods will probably demonstrate that a substantial fraction of the tens‑of‑meV intensity is magnetic after subtracting the nuclear contribution.

extra.notations:
- "separation methods": experimental or analysis techniques that distinguish magnetic from non‑magnetic scattering (e.g., polarization analysis, Q‑dependence, temperature dependence, phonon modeling).
- "substantial fraction": here meaning a non‑negligible portion of I_total at tens of meV (order tens of percent or more), not strictly quantified.

title: Existence of zero-resistivity superconducting state for x>4 atom %
content: For La[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs samples with nominal fluoride content x>4 atom %, there exists at least one temperature T in the interval 5 K ≤ T ≤ 22 K at which the dc electrical resistivity ρ(T) reaches zero (ρ=0), i.e. the samples enter a superconducting state characterized by vanishing dc resistivity.

extra.notations:
- La[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs: Lanthanum iron oxypnictide with nominal fluoride substitution fraction x.
- x: nominal F^{-} content (atom percent).
- ρ(T): dc electrical resistivity as a function of temperature T.
- ρ=0: vanishing dc resistivity (superconducting state as defined here).

title: F-doping reduces the low-temperature resistivity minimum and correlates with superconductivity
content: Increasing fluorine substitution (increasing x) in La[O_{1-x}F_{x}]FeAs shifts the resistivity minimum temperature T_{min} to lower temperatures and, for x>4 atom %, is associated with the appearance of a zero-resistivity superconducting state at low T. Here T_{min} is the temperature of the low-temperature resistivity minimum (reported near 100 K in the undoped material), and x is the nominal F^{-} content.

extra.notations:
- T_{min}: temperature of the resistivity minimum in ρ(T).
- x: nominal F^{-} content (atom percent).

title: Sample-dependent spread in reported superconducting onset temperatures likely due to composition/inhomogeneity
content: The reported variation in the upper bound of the zero-resistivity interval (some reports up to 26 K, others up to 22 K) likely reflects sample-dependent factors such as differences between nominal and actual F concentration, compositional inhomogeneity, strain, or presence of minor secondary phases, rather than a fundamental contradiction in the existence of superconductivity for x>4 atom %.

extra.notations:
- x: nominal F^{-} content (atom percent).
- "upper bound of zero-resistivity interval": the highest reported temperature at which ρ reaches zero in a given sample.

title: Superconductivity likely appears upon suppression of a higher-temperature normal-state anomaly
content: The emergence of superconductivity (ρ→0 at low T for x>4 atom %) likely occurs after partial suppression of the higher-temperature resistive/structural/magnetic anomaly observed near T_{ann}≈150 K in the undoped compound. That is, F-doping reduces the signatures at T_{ann} and T_{min}, and superconductivity appears in the doping regime where those anomalies are weakened.

extra.notations:
- T_{ann}: temperature of the higher-temperature anomaly in ρ(T) (≈150 K in undoped LaOFeAs).
- x: nominal F^{-} content (atom percent).

title: YH9 (P6_3/mmc) is stable across the overlapping high-pressure range including ~200–410 GPa
content:
If proposition 250 is true (theory predicts the hexagonal close-packed yttrium hydride YH9 with space group P6_3/mmc is stable at high pressure, e.g. ~200 GPa) and proposition 269 is true (experimentally YH10 does not form up to 410 GPa and 2250 K, and the P6_3/mmc YH9 phase remains the hydrogen-richest yttrium hydride stable under those conditions), then it necessarily follows that the P6_3/mmc YH9 phase is a thermodynamically stable, hydrogen-rich yttrium hydride for pressures in the overlapping region that includes approximately 200 GPa up to 410 GPa (at temperatures within the probed range up to 2250 K). 
Symbols: $YH_{9}$ denotes yttrium hydride with H:Y ratio 9:1; $P$ denotes static pressure in gigapascals (GPa); $P6_{3}/mmc$ denotes the stated hexagonal space group.

extra.notations:
- $YH_{9}$: yttrium hydride composition with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio 9:1
- $P$: pressure (GPa)
- $P6_{3}/mmc$: hexagonal close-packed space group identifier

title: Predicted fcc YH10 phase is likely inaccessible in the probed static compression window; theoretical phase diagrams need revision
content:
Given that some theoretical studies predicted a face-centred cubic ($fcc$) YH10 phase (often associated with very high hydrogen content and predicted high superconducting $T_{c}$) but proposition 269 reports that YH10 does not form in the pressure–temperature range explored (up to 410 GPa and 2250 K) while P6_3/mmc YH9 is observed as the hydrogen-richest stable phase, it is plausible that the $fcc$ YH10 phase is not thermodynamically accessible under static compression within that P–T window. Therefore, theoretical predictions that forecast a stable $fcc$ YH10 region at experimentally reachable pressures likely omit important contributions (for example, finite-temperature Gibbs free energy, anharmonic vibrational effects, or kinetic/formation barriers), and the theoretical phase diagrams should be revisited with those effects included.
Symbols: $YH_{10}$ denotes yttrium hydride with H:Y ratio 10:1; $fcc$ denotes face-centred cubic crystal structure; $T_{c}$ denotes superconducting critical temperature.

extra.notations:
- $YH_{10}$: yttrium hydride composition with hydrogen-to-yttrium ratio 10:1
- $fcc$: face-centred cubic crystal structure
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature

title: No resolution-limited pairing peaks at $T^{*}$
content: Let $\Delta(T)$ be the pairing gap magnitude and $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ the inverse pair lifetime (pair-pole broadening) as defined in the parent node. At the pseudogap temperature $T^{*}$ the siblings assert $\Delta(T^{*}) \approx \Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$. Because $\Gamma_{0}$ represents pair-pole broadening that can "fill" pairing-related spectral features, when the broadening is comparable to the gap energy the pairing-induced spectral structure cannot appear as a sharp, resolution-limited peak in ARPES. Therefore, at $T^{*}$ pairing-related ARPES features (gap edges or coherence peaks associated with pairing) are intrinsically strongly broadened and are not resolution-limited quasiparticle peaks.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta(T)$: pairing gap magnitude (superconducting or pseudogap) at temperature $T$. 
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime (pair-pole broadening) at temperature $T$; when large it fills/broadens pairing spectral features.
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap onset temperature.

title: Sharp quasiparticle peaks at $T_{c}$ require pair broadening much smaller than the gap
content: Let $T_{c}$ be the superconducting transition temperature where global phase coherence appears, $\Delta(T)$ the pairing gap magnitude, and $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ the inverse pair lifetime. The observation that broad pseudogap-state lineshapes sharpen into resolution-limited ARPES quasiparticle peaks below $T_{c}$ (node 1536) together with the role of $\Gamma_{0}$ as pair-pole broadening (parent) implies that, at or just below $T_{c}$, the pair broadening must satisfy $\Gamma_{0}(T_{c}) \ll \Delta(T_{c})$ (pair lifetime long compared with the inverse gap energy) for pairing-related spectral features to be sharp and resolution-limited.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature (global phase coherence onset).
- $\Delta(T)$: pairing gap magnitude at temperature $T$.
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime (pair-pole broadening) at temperature $T$.

title: Likely thermal evolution between $T^{*}$ and $T_{c}$: increasing gap coherence via reduced $\Gamma_{0}$ and/or growing $\Delta$
content: Let $\Delta(T)$ and $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ be as above. Given that at $T^{*}$ $\Delta\approx\Gamma_{0}$ (node 1552) and that clear, resolution-limited quasiparticle peaks appear only below $T_{c}$ (node 1536), it is plausible that on cooling from $T^{*}$ toward $T_{c}$ either (i) $\Gamma_{0}(T)$ decreases (pair lifetime lengthens), (ii) $\Delta(T)$ increases (gap deepens), or (iii) both occur, so that by $T_{c}$ the condition $\Gamma_{0}(T_{c})\ll\Delta(T_{c})$ (or at least $\Gamma_{0}(T_{c})<\Delta(T_{c})$ with reduced single-particle scattering) is reached and coherence peaks become resolvable.

extra.notations:
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap temperature.
- $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature.
- $\Delta(T)$: pairing gap magnitude at temperature $T$.
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime at temperature $T$.

title: Numerical ranges for partial Sommerfeld coefficients
content:
Using the definitions $x=\\gamma_1/\\gamma_n$ and $\\gamma_1+\\gamma_2=\\gamma_n$, with the reported values $\\gamma_n\\approx 2.65\\ \\mathrm{mJ\\,mol^{-1}\\,K^{-2}}$ and $x\\in[0.50,0.55]$, the partial normal-state Sommerfeld coefficients satisfy
$$\\gamma_1 = x\\,\\gamma_n \\in [0.50\\times2.65,\\;0.55\\times2.65]\\ \\mathrm{mJ\\,mol^{-1}\\,K^{-2}} = [1.325,\\;1.4575]\\ \\mathrm{mJ\\,mol^{-1}\\,K^{-2}},$$
$$\\gamma_2 = \\gamma_n-\\gamma_1 \\in [1.1925,\\;1.325]\\ \\mathrm{mJ\\,mol^{-1}\\,K^{-2}}.$$
Equivalently, the two partial coefficients are comparable: $\\gamma_1/\\gamma_2\\in[1.00,1.222\\dots]$.

extra.notations:
- $\\gamma_n$: total normal-state Sommerfeld coefficient (mJ mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-2}$)
- $\\gamma_1,\\gamma_2$: partial Sommerfeld coefficients for bands 1 and 2
- $x$: fractional weight $\\gamma_1/\\gamma_n$

title: Zero-temperature gap magnitudes expressed in units of $k_{\\mathrm{B}}T_c$
content:
The reported dimensionless gap ratios are $2\\Delta_1/(k_{\\mathrm{B}}T_c)\\approx 4.0$ and $2\\Delta_2/(k_{\\mathrm{B}}T_c)\\approx 1.2$. Solving for the zero-temperature gaps $\\Delta_{i,0}$ gives
$$\\Delta_{1,0} \\approx 2.0\\,k_{\\mathrm{B}}T_c,\qquad \\Delta_{2,0}\\approx 0.6\\,k_{\\mathrm{B}}T_c.$$
Thus, in units of energy these gaps are fixed fractions of $k_{\\mathrm{B}}T_c$ as shown above (numerical values follow directly when $T_c$ is specified).

extra.notations:
- $\\Delta_{i,0}$: zero-temperature superconducting gap on band $i$
- $k_{\\mathrm{B}}$: Boltzmann constant
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature

title: Nonzero interband coupling is required to produce a single bulk $T_c$ with two distinct gaps
content: Let the two bands be labelled 1 and 2 with gaps $\\Delta_1(T)$ and $\\Delta_2(T)$. The siblings model both gaps as sharing the same reduced-temperature dependence $\\delta(t)$ and thus vanishing at the same critical temperature $T_c$ (i.e., $\\Delta_i(T)=\\Delta_{i,0}\\,\\delta(t)$ with the same $t=T/T_c$). Combined with the thermodynamic observation that the two-gap behaviour arises from the bulk of the sample, the most plausible mechanism to produce a single bulk transition temperature while maintaining distinct gap magnitudes is that the two bands are coupled by a nonzero interband pairing interaction. Therefore it is highly likely that MgB$_2$ exhibits nonzero interband coupling between the bands that host the two gaps.

extra.notations:
- $\\Delta_i(T)$: superconducting gap on band $i$ at temperature $T$
- $T_c$: common superconducting transition temperature
- "interband coupling": pairing interaction term that couples superconducting order parameters on different bands

title: Leading-order pairing vertex is the magnetic particle–hole exchange
content:
Using the decomposition
$$\Gamma_{\mathrm{even}}^{pp}(K,K')=\Lambda_{\mathrm{irr}}(K,K')+\tfrac{1}{2}\Phi_{d}(K,K')+\tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_{m}(K,K'),$$
and the stated facts that $\Phi_{m}$ (the magnetic $S=1$ particle–hole exchange contribution) is the dominant, momentum-dependent term while $\Phi_{d}$ (the $S=0$ charge channel) and $\Lambda_{\mathrm{irr}}$ (the fully irreducible vertex) are small and effectively flat in momentum, it follows necessarily that to leading order the irreducible particle–particle pairing vertex is given by
$$\Gamma_{\mathrm{even}}^{pp}(K,K')\approx \tfrac{3}{2}\,\Phi_{m}(K,K').$$
Here $K=(\mathbf{K},i\omega_n,\sigma)$ and $K'$ likewise; $\Gamma_{\mathrm{even}}^{pp}$ denotes the even-momentum part of the irreducible particle–particle vertex.

extra.notations:
- $\Gamma_{\mathrm{even}}^{pp}(K,K')$: even-momentum irreducible particle–particle vertex. 
- $\Lambda_{\mathrm{irr}}(K,K')$: fully irreducible two-fermion vertex. 
- $\Phi_{d}(K,K')$: charge-density ($S=0$) particle–hole exchange channel contribution. 
- $\Phi_{m}(K,K')$: magnetic ($S=1$) particle–hole exchange channel contribution.
- $K=(\mathbf{K},i\omega_n,\sigma)$: cluster 4-vector (momentum, Matsubara frequency, spin).

title: Magnetic-channel-dominated vertex enforces a sign-changing (d-wave) leading pairing eigenfunction
content: Let $(\lambda_\alpha,\phi_\alpha(K))$ be an eigenpair of the Bethe–Salpeter equation in the particle–particle channel with $\lambda_\alpha$ the eigenvalue and $\phi_\alpha(K)$ the corresponding eigenfunction (gap form factor). Given that (i) $\Gamma^{pp}(K,K')$ increases with large momentum transfer $q=K-K'$ (i.e., is peaked for transfers near $(\pi,\pi)$ in the lattice Brillouin zone), and (ii) the magnetic particle–hole exchange channel $\Phi_{m}$ provides the dominant, momentum-dependent contribution to $\Gamma^{pp}$, it follows necessarily that the leading eigenfunction $\phi_{\mathrm{lead}}(K)$ changes sign under a momentum translation by the dominant transfer (promoting a sign change between regions connected by that transfer). For a square-lattice Hubbard model where the dominant transfer is near $\mathbf{Q}=(\pi,\pi)$, this implies the leading pairing form factor has $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ symmetry (a sign-changing gap under 90° rotation and opposite signs on $(\pi,0)$ and $(0,\pi)$ lobes).

extra.notations:
- $\lambda_{\alpha}$: eigenvalue of the Bethe–Salpeter equation in the particle–particle channel. 
- $\phi_{\alpha}(K)$: corresponding eigenfunction (pairing form factor) dependent on cluster momentum and frequency. 
- $\mathbf{Q}$: dominant momentum transfer (for the square lattice typically $(\pi,\pi)$).
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: d-wave symmetry with sign change between $k=(\pi,0)$ and $k=(0,\pi)$.

title: Effective spin-fluctuation representation of the pairing interaction
content:
It is plausible that the dominant magnetic contribution $\Phi_{m}(K,K')$ (and hence, to leading order, $\Gamma^{pp}(K,K')\approx \tfrac{3}{2}\Phi_{m}(K,K')$) can be modeled by an effective spin-fluctuation mediated interaction of the form
$$V_{\mathrm{eff}}(\mathbf{q},i\Omega_m)\simeq g^{2}\,\chi(\mathbf{q},i\Omega_m),$$
where $\chi(\mathbf{q},i\Omega_m)$ is the spin (magnetic) susceptibility and $g$ is an effective coupling (dimension of energy). In many weak-to-intermediate coupling treatments one further approximates $g\sim U$ (or a renormalized $U_{\mathrm{eff}}$), yielding the familiar ansatz $V_{\mathrm{eff}}\sim U^{2}\chi$.

extra.notations:
- $V_{\mathrm{eff}}(\mathbf{q},i\Omega_m)$: effective pairing interaction as a function of momentum transfer $\mathbf{q}$ and bosonic Matsubara frequency $i\Omega_m$. 
- $\chi(\mathbf{q},i\Omega_m)$: spin (magnetic) susceptibility. 
- $g$: effective electron–spin-fluctuation coupling (may be of order $U$ or a renormalized value).
- $U$: on-site Hubbard interaction.

title: Pseudogap disappears (is filled) at $T^{*}$ because $\Delta(T^{*})\approx\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$
content: Given the minimal self-energy parameterization in which the pseudogap (energy scale) is $\Delta$ and the inverse pair lifetime is $\Gamma_{0}$, and given that the disappearance (filling) of the pseudogap is controlled by increases in $\Gamma_{0}$ (as stated in the parent), the equality $\Delta(T^{*})\approx\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$ implies that at the pseudogap temperature $T^{*}$ the pair-lifetime broadening is comparable to the gap energy. When broadening on the order of the gap energy occurs, spectral weight suppression at low energy is smeared out and the gap is no longer resolved in ARPES: the pseudogap is thereby filled (i.e., the spectral gap vanishes as a distinct feature) at $T^{*}$.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta(T)$: pseudogap (or superconducting gap) magnitude as a function of temperature $T$.
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime (pair-breaking / pair-dephasing rate) as a function of $T$.
- $T^{*}$: pseudogap (crossover) temperature where the pseudogap disappears.

title: Superconducting coherence onset at $T_{c}$ is mainly a reduction of single-particle scattering ($\Gamma_{1}$); pseudogap persists until $T^{*}$ where $\Gamma_{0}\sim\Delta$
content: It is plausible that the sharpening of spectral features observed at the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ (i.e., emergence of sharper quasiparticle peaks and narrowed linewidths) is primarily caused by a reduction in the single-particle scattering rate $\Gamma_{1}$ rather than by a closure of the gap $\Delta$. Meanwhile the pseudogap energy scale $\Delta$ remains nonzero above $T_{c}$ and only becomes unresolved at $T^{*}$ where the inverse pair lifetime $\Gamma_{0}$ grows to be comparable to $\Delta$, i.e., $\Delta(T^{*})\approx\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$, producing the filling of the pseudogap.

extra.notations:
- $\Gamma_{1}(T)$: single-particle scattering rate (controls single-particle linewidth).
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime (controls pair-induced broadening / gap filling).
- $\Delta(T)$: gap magnitude; $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature; $T^{*}$: pseudogap temperature.

title: Algebraic relation between dopant count and hole density per CuO2 plaquette
content:
Let δ be the number of nonstoichiometric interstitial oxygen dopant atoms per crystallographic unit cell in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ, and let p be the hole density (number of holes) per CuO2 plaquette. If each interstitial oxygen dopant carries an electrostatic charge of −2e, then charge neutrality requires that the CuO2 layers accommodate compensating positive charge equal to +2eδ per unit cell. Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ contains two CuO2 plaquettes (two Cu atoms) per crystallographic unit cell; therefore the hole density per CuO2 plaquette is
$$p=\frac{2\delta}{2}=\delta.$$
Hence, under the stated assumptions, p = δ (holes per CuO2 plaquette equals the oxygen over-stoichiometry parameter δ).

extra.notations:
- Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ: Bi-2212 cuprate superconductor (two CuO2 plaquettes per unit cell).
- δ: number of interstitial oxygen dopants per crystallographic unit cell.
- p: hole density (holes per CuO2 plaquette).
- e: elementary charge.

title: SI-STM impurity-state features should spatially coincide with charged dopant positions and correlate with local superconducting-gap variations
content:
Let f(r) be a two-dimensional SI-STM image that highlights the localized impurity-state spectral features produced by interstitial oxygen dopants (for example, a map of LDOS amplitude at the impurity resonance energy), and let g(r) be a map of the local superconducting energy gap magnitude Δ(r) (or any spatial map of gap-related spectroscopic signature). Then, given that (i) each interstitial oxygen is a point-like charged impurity (charge −2e) and (ii) interstitial oxygens produce localized impurity-state features detectable by SI-STM, it is plausible that the normalized cross-correlation C_fg(R) between f and g will show a statistically significant peak at small displacement R (in particular R ≈ 0), indicating spatial co-location of impurity-state signatures and modifications of the local gap:
$$C_{fg}(\vec{R})\ \text{is significant and peaked near}\ \vec{R}=0.$$
In words: SI-STM-resolved impurity-state signatures should be spatially coincident with (or closely surrounding) the dopant positions and these sites should show systematic perturbations of the local superconducting gap magnitude.

extra.notations:
- f(⃗r): two-dimensional SI-STM image selecting dopant-related impurity-state spectral weight (e.g., LDOS at resonance energy).
- g(⃗r): two-dimensional map of local superconducting gap magnitude Δ(⃗r) or related spectroscopic measure.
- C_fg(⃗R): normalized cross-correlation between f and g as defined in Proposition 1061 notation.
- δ, e as above.

title: Absence of long-range inter-stripe CDW locking at non-zero temperature
content:
If the effective inter-stripe CDW potential is given by
$$\tilde{V}(x)=\langle V(\Delta Y)\rangle\exp\{-2k_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle\},$$
and at non-zero temperature the mean-squared arc-length difference diverges as
$$\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle\sim A T |x|\quad(\text{with }A>0),$$
then $\tilde{V}(x)$ decays exponentially with distance,
$$\tilde{V}(x)\propto\exp[-(2k_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}A T)\,|x|].$$
Therefore the inter-stripe CDW coupling strength vanishes at long distances in the thermodynamic limit, which forbids true long-range CDW phase locking between different stripes at any non-zero temperature.

extra.notations:
- $k_{\mathrm{F}}$: Fermi wavevector. 
- $\Delta L(x)=L_{j+1}(x)-L_j(x)$: arc-length difference between neighboring stripes at longitudinal coordinate $x$.
- $\tilde{V}(x)$: effective, fluctuation-averaged inter-stripe CDW coupling as a function of separation $x$ along the stripes.
- $A$: positive coefficient characterizing the strength of transverse fluctuations coupling to arc-length variance.
- $T$: temperature.

title: Finite-temperature inter-stripe CDW correlation length
content:
Using the exponential form
$$\tilde{V}(x)\propto\exp[-(2k_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}A T)\,|x|],$$
the CDW inter-stripe coupling is characterized by a finite correlation (locking) length
$$\xi=\frac{1}{2k_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}A T}.$$
Here $\xi$ is the longitudinal distance along the stripes beyond which inter-stripe CDW locking is exponentially suppressed.

extra.notations:
- $\xi$: inter-stripe CDW locking (correlation) length.
- $k_{\mathrm{F}}$: Fermi wavevector.
- $A$: coefficient appearing in $\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle\sim A T |x|$.
- $T$: temperature.

title: Transverse fluctuations preferentially suppress inter-stripe CDW locking relative to Josephson (SC) coupling
content: The geometrical phase factor $-2k_{\mathrm{F}}\Delta L$ appearing in the inter-stripe CDW coupling $H_c=\sum_j\int\mathrm{d}x\,V(\Delta_j Y)\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}(\Delta_j\phi)-2k_{\mathrm{F}}(\Delta_j L)]$ causes strong dephasing when $\Delta L$ fluctuates. By contrast, a conventional inter-stripe Josephson (superconducting) coupling between stripe order parameters does not generically carry a fast oscillatory $2k_{\mathrm{F}}\Delta L$ phase factor and therefore is not subject to the same exponential dephasing mechanism. Consequently, transverse stripe fluctuations are likely to suppress long-range CDW locking more severely than they suppress inter-stripe superconducting phase coherence; this can make two-dimensional superconducting coherence (inter-stripe Josephson locking) more favorable than inter-stripe CDW order in the presence of strong transverse fluctuations.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta_j L$: difference in arc-length between stripes $j+1$ and $j$.
- $H_c$: inter-stripe CDW coupling Hamiltonian.
- Josephson coupling: inter-stripe tunneling term coupling superconducting phases on neighboring stripes (not explicitly given in the siblings).

title: At zero temperature inter-stripe CDW coupling is power-law suppressed; relevance depends on charge-sector scaling
content:
If at zero temperature the mean-squared arc-length difference grows logarithmically,
$$\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle\sim B\log|x|\quad(\text{with }B>0),$$
then the fluctuation-averaged coupling decays algebraically,
$$\tilde{V}(x)\propto |x|^{-2k_{\mathrm{F}}^{2}B}.$$
Consequently, whether inter-stripe CDW locking leads to spontaneous long-range order at $T=0$ depends on the RG scaling dimension of the operator $\cos[\sqrt{2\pi}\Delta\phi]$ (controlled by the charge-sector exponent $K_c$) relative to the power-law suppression induced by geometrical fluctuations: if the combined scaling makes the coupling irrelevant, long-range CDW locking is absent even at $T=0$; if it is relevant, quasi-long-range or true long-range ordering can occur.

extra.notations:
- $B$: coefficient appearing in $\langle[\Delta L(x)]^{2}\rangle\sim B\log|x|$ at $T=0$.
- $\tilde{V}(x)$: fluctuation-averaged inter-stripe CDW coupling.
- $K_c$: charge-sector Luttinger exponent controlling scaling dimension of CDW-phase operators.

title: Marginal Fermi liquid form necessarily implies absence of well-defined Landau quasiparticles
content:
Given the marginal Fermi liquid (MFL) retarded self-energy
$$\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)\sim g^{2}N^{2}(0)\Big(\omega\ln\frac{x}{\omega_{c}}-i\frac{\pi}{2}x\Big),\quad x=\max(|\omega|,T),$$
the quasiparticle renormalization factor at energy $E_{k}$ satisfies
$$z_{k}^{-1}=1-\left.\frac{\partial\operatorname{Re}\Sigma}{\partial\omega}\right|_{\omega=E_{k}}\sim\ln\frac{\omega_{c}}{E_{k}}\to\infty\quad\text{as }E_{k}\to0.$$
