Simultaneously $\operatorname{Im}\Sigma(\omega)\propto x\sim|\omega|$ so the single-particle decay rate is of the same order as the excitation energy. Therefore Landau quasiparticles (finite $z_k$ and decay rate much smaller than excitation energy as $\omega\to0$) do not exist in this regime.

extra.notations:
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: retarded one-particle self-energy.
- $z_{k}$: quasiparticle weight (residue) at momentum $\mathbf{k}$.
- $x$: energy scale $x=\max(|\omega|,T)$.
- $N(0)$: unrenormalized density of states at Fermi level.
- $\omega_{c}$: ultraviolet cutoff.

title: Different low-energy scalings (ω^{2/3} vs ω) produce distinct experimentally testable signatures
content: If the dominant low-energy single-particle scattering obeys Γ(ω)∝ω^{2/3} (gauge-field / spin-charge separated scenario, node 518) rather than the marginal-Fermi-liquid linear form Im Σ(ω)∝ω (nodes 907/1589/917), then one expects experimentally distinct fingerprints: (i) ARPES energy-dependent linewidths at low $|\omega|$ scaling as $|\omega|^{2/3}$ rather than ∝|ω|; (ii) temperature-dependent scattering contributions to transport dominated by the corresponding thermal scaling (either ∝T^{2/3} in the frequency-controlled regime or the stated thermal form ∝(T m_B)^{1/2} in the boson-dominated regime), distinct from the roughly linear-in-T scattering expected from MFL; (iii) tunneling conductance $g(V)$ and low-bias differential conductance slope should show different low-bias dependences (MFL predicts spectra with long ω^{-1} wings and a characteristic linear-in-energy broadening, whereas the gauge-field scenario produces broader, non-linear power-law tails). Observing one set of scalings experimentally would strongly disfavour the other mechanism as the dominant low-energy scattering channel in that material.

extra.notations:
- Γ(ω): single-particle spectral peak width (≈−Im Σ).
- ARPES: angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy.
- $g(V)$: tunneling differential conductance $dI/dV$ at bias $V$.
- $m_B$: effective holon mass appearing in thermal scaling from node 518.

title: Unit-cell volume from reported lattice parameters
content:
The unit-cell volume $V$ of the tetragonal cell (space group $I4/mmm$) is $V=a^{2}c$. Using the reported lattice parameters $a=3.8550(6)$ \AA\ and $c=29.318(4)$ \AA\ gives
$$V = (3.8550\ \text{\AA})^{2}\times 29.318\ \text{\AA} \approx 435.696\ \text{\AA}^{3}.$$
Propagating the stated standard uncertainties ($\sigma_{a}=0.0006$ \AA, $\sigma_{c}=0.004$ \AA) by $dV/V=2(d a/a)+(d c/c)$ yields $\sigma_{V}\approx 0.20\ \text{\AA}^{3}$, so $V=435.696(20)\ \text{\AA}^{3}$.

extra.notations:
- $a$: tetragonal basal lattice parameter (Å). 
- $c$: tetragonal axial lattice parameter (Å).
- $V$: unit-cell volume (Å$^{3}$).
- $\sigma_{x}$: standard uncertainty of quantity $x$.

title: Cu(1) fourfold (square-planar) coordination consistent with CuO$_2$ planes
content: The reported Cu(1)–O(1) distance is $1.9277(3)$ \AA\ with coordination multiplicity 4. In the tetragonal space group $I4/mmm$ four symmetry-equivalent oxygen ligands at equal Cu–O distances imply that the four O(1) atoms occupy the vertices of a square around Cu(1) in the basal plane. Therefore Cu(1) is fourfold coordinated in a planar square geometry, i.e. it forms the CuO$_2$ planar unit characteristic of layered cuprates.

extra.notations:
- Cu(1): the copper site identified in the structural model.
- O(1): the oxygen site with multiplicity 4 bonded to Cu(1).
- "coordination multiplicity 4": four symmetry-equivalent ligands at the given distance.
- CuO$_2$ plane: the square planar unit of one Cu atom and its four in-plane oxygens.

title: Inclusion of anomalous scattering likely improves reliability of heavy-atom positions and derived bond lengths
content: The refinement is reported to use full-matrix least-squares on X-ray diffraction data with neutral-atom scattering factors plus anomalous (dispersive/absorptive) scattering terms for the metal ions (Tl, Ba, Ca, Cu). Including anomalous scattering for heavy/transition elements typically reduces systematic biases in fitted site occupancies and positional parameters caused by energy-dependent scattering factors. Therefore it is likely (high probability) that the positional parameters and hence bond distances involving the heavy metal atoms and their neighbors (particularly distances influenced by Tl and Ba scattering) are more accurate and have reduced systematic error compared with a refinement that omitted anomalous terms.

extra.notations:
- "anomalous scattering terms": energy-dependent corrections $f'$, $f''$ added to atomic scattering factors in X-ray refinement.
- "full-matrix least-squares": refinement algorithm that computes the covariance matrix of parameters (no simplifications).
- "systematic bias": non-random error arising from incorrect scattering models.

title: Presence of a long Cu(1)–O(2) axial distance implies a weak apical interaction (square-pyramidal / elongated-octahedral motif)
content: The reported Cu(1)–O(2) distance is $2.699(10)$ \AA\ (multiplicity 1), substantially longer than the four in-plane Cu(1)–O(1) distances of $1.9277(3)$ \AA. This disparity is consistent with Cu(1) having four strong in-plane bonds and at least one weak axial (apical) bond, i.e. a square-pyramidal local environment or an elongated octahedron with a weak apical interaction on one side. Such an arrangement is typical for layered cuprates where axial Cu–O distances are much longer than in-plane bonds.

extra.notations:
- "axial/apical": the ligand(s) along the crystallographic $c$ axis above/below the CuO$_2$ plane.
- "square-pyramidal": coordination in which four ligands form a square base and a fifth ligand occupies an axial position.
- "elongated octahedron": an octahedron with one or two axial bonds significantly lengthened relative to equatorial bonds.

title: Both superconducting gaps are bulk electronic properties
content: Let Δ1 and Δ2 denote the two superconducting energy gaps and C the electronic specific heat (thermodynamic heat capacity). Because specific heat is an extensive thermodynamic quantity that integrates the electronic excitation spectrum over the entire sample volume, a successful two-gap fit to the zero-field, equilibrium specific heat implies that the excitations characterized by Δ1 and Δ2 make finite contributions to the bulk electronic density of states. Therefore the two gaps are bulk (volume) properties of the superconducting state of MgB2 and are not merely surface-localized spectroscopic features.

extra.notations:
- Δ1, Δ2: superconducting energy gaps (defined throughout the sample volume)
- C: equilibrium electronic specific heat (bulk thermodynamic probe)

title: Both gaps belong to a single superconducting phase with comparable partial densities of states
content: Let T_c denote the superconducting critical temperature, γ_n the total normal-state Sommerfeld coefficient, and γ_1, γ_2 the partial Sommerfeld coefficients (γ_1+γ_2=γ_n) associated with the two bands hosting gaps Δ1 and Δ2. The two-gap α-model used in the specific-heat fits assumes temperature dependences Δ_i(T)=Δ_{i,0}·δ(t) with the same reduced-temperature function δ(t) (hence a common T_c). The reported fits give comparable band weights x = γ_1/γ_n ≈ 0.50–0.55. Therefore (i) both gaps close at the same bulk T_c and are part of the same superconducting phase, and (ii) the two bands contribute comparable fractions (~50% each) of the total normal-state electronic density of states.

extra.notations:
- T_c: superconducting transition temperature (common to both gaps)
- γ_n: total normal-state Sommerfeld coefficient
- γ_i: partial Sommerfeld coefficient for band i (i=1,2)
- x = γ_1/γ_n: relative weight of band 1

title: Small gap is likely induced (partly) by interband coupling rather than strong intraband pairing
content: Let 2Δ_2/k_B T_c ≈ 1.2 denote the dimensionless gap ratio of the smaller gap. In a single-band weak-coupling BCS superconductor the canonical ratio is 2Δ/k_B T_c ≈ 3.53. The anomalously small value for the smaller gap (≈1.2) plausibly indicates that, on its host band, intrinsic intraband pairing would correspond to a much lower standalone T_c; the fact that Δ_2 persists up to the bulk T_c implies that interband pair-transfer (interband coupling) with the band hosting the larger gap forces Δ_2 to remain finite up to the common T_c. Thus MgB2 is plausibly a multiband superconductor in which interband coupling induces superconductivity (or raises the effective closing temperature) on the band with the small gap.

extra.notations:
- Δ_2: smaller superconducting gap
- k_B: Boltzmann constant
- T_c: common superconducting transition temperature

title: Large gap likely reflects stronger electron–phonon coupling (probably on the quasi-2D σ-band)
content: With reported dimensionless ratio 2Δ_1/k_B T_c ≈ 4.0 for the larger gap Δ_1, the gap exceeds the single-band BCS weak-coupling value (≈3.53). Let the σ-band denote the quasi-two-dimensional sheets and the π-band the three-dimensional sheets of the Fermi surface. Given (i) the larger gap magnitude, (ii) the assignment in spectroscopic and band-structure studies of the large gap to the σ-derived sheets, and (iii) the α-model fit weights showing comparable partial DOS, it is plausible that the σ-band has stronger electron–phonon coupling (or stronger effective pairing interaction) than the π-band, producing the larger gap observed.

extra.notations:
- Δ_1: larger superconducting gap (associated with σ-band in band-structure/spectroscopy)
- σ-band, π-band: quasi-2D and 3D Fermi-surface sheets respectively
- 2Δ_1/k_B T_c: dimensionless gap ratio

title: Direct relation between dopant count, LDOS -0.96 eV features, and hole density p
content:
Let $\delta$ be the number of nonstoichiometric interstitial oxygen atoms per Bi-2212 unit cell (Proposition 1061). Each such dopant carries electrostatic charge $-2e$ and — under the parent assumption that these dopants introduce hole carriers into the CuO$_2$ planes — this requires the creation of two holes (total charge $+2e$) in the CuO$_2$ subsystem per dopant to maintain overall charge neutrality. Bi-2212 has two CuO$_2$ plaquettes (two Cu sites) per unit cell (formula unit Bi$_2$Sr$_2$CaCu$_2$O$_{8+\delta}$ contains 2 Cu). Therefore the hole density per CuO$_2$ plaquette is
$$p \;=\; \frac{2\ \text{holes per dopant}\times \delta\ \text{dopants per unit cell}}{2\ \text{CuO}_2\ \text{plaquettes per unit cell}} \;=\; \delta.$$
Combining this with Proposition 1077, which identifies each localized LDOS feature at $\sim -0.96\ \text{eV}$ with a single oxygen dopant atom, implies that the areal (or per-unit-cell) density of the $-0.96\ \text{eV}$ LDOS features equals the hole density per CuO$_2$ plaquette $p$. Equivalently, counting the number of $-0.96\ \text{eV}$ features within an STM field of view and normalizing by the number of unit cells in that field of view yields $p$.

extra.notations:
- $\delta$: number of interstitial (nonstoichiometric) oxygen atoms per Bi-2212 unit cell. 
- $e$: elementary charge. 
- $p$: hole density per CuO$_2$ plaquette (holes per Cu). 
- "$-0.96\ \mathrm{eV}$ feature": localized LDOS resonance at approximately $-0.96\ \mathrm{eV}$ identified with a single dopant (Prop. 1077).

title: Local dopant positions (−0.96 eV features) are likely anticorrelated with local superconducting gap magnitude
content:
Let $\Delta(\vec{r})$ be the local superconducting gap magnitude at position $\vec{r}$ in the CuO$_2$ plane, and let S be the set of positions of localized LDOS $-0.96\ \mathrm{eV}$ features (each identified with one interstitial oxygen dopant). The conjecture is that, statistically, the presence (or local density) of dopants near $\vec{r}$ is associated with a reduced local gap magnitude, i.e. a negative spatial cross-correlation between a dopant-position map and $\Delta(\vec{r})$:
$$C_{\text{dopant},\Delta}(\vec{R}=0)\;=\;\langle D(\vec{r})\,\Delta(\vec{r})\rangle_{\vec{r}} - \langle D\rangle\langle\Delta\rangle \;<\;0,$$
where $D(\vec{r})$ is a localized indicator field (e.g. sum of Gaussians centered at S) and averages are over the imaged area.

extra.notations:
- $D(\vec{r})$: dopant indicator field constructed from positions of $-0.96\ \mathrm{eV}$ LDOS features (high where dopants are present). 
- $\Delta(\vec{r})$: local superconducting gap magnitude. 
- $C_{\text{dopant},\Delta}(\vec{R})$: cross-covariance between dopant map and gap map at displacement $\vec{R}$.

title: Resistive criterion mismatch prevents direct use of 857's Hc2(0) for the 365 definition
content: Proposition 365 defines the resistive upper critical field $\mu_{0}H_{c2}(T)$ using the criterion $f=0.9$, i.e. $R(H_{c2},T)=0.9\times R(T_{c}^{\mathrm{onset}})$. Proposition 857 reports slopes $dH_{c2}/dT|_{T_c}$ obtained with different resistive criteria (50% and 10% of the reference resistance) and uses those slopes to estimate $H_{c2}(0)$. Therefore the numerical estimates of $H_{c2}(0)$ in proposition 857 (80–180 T) refer specifically to $H_{c2}(T)$ curves defined with $f=0.5$ and $f=0.1$, not to the $f=0.9$ definition of proposition 365. Consequently, one cannot substitute the 80–180 T range directly for the $H_{c2}(0)$ that would be obtained under the $f=0.9$ criterion without reprocessing the resistive data using $f=0.9$.

extra.notations:
- $f$: fraction of $R(T_{c}^{\mathrm{onset}})$ used to define resistive $H_{c2}(T)$, $0<f<1$. 
- $H_{c2}(T)$: upper critical field defined by $R(H_{c2},T)=f\cdot R(T_{c}^{\mathrm{onset}})$.
- $H_{c2}(0)$: extrapolated value of $H_{c2}(T)$ at $T=0$.

title: Using a 90% resistive criterion will likely yield a smaller extrapolated H_{c2}(0) than the 50% and 10% based estimates
content: It is plausible that the $f=0.9$ resistive criterion (90% of $R(T_{c}^{\mathrm{onset}})$) used in proposition 365 would produce a slope $dH_{c2}/dT|_{T_c}$ smaller than the 1.3 T/K value reported for the 50% criterion in proposition 857, and therefore an extrapolated $H_{c2}(0)$ below the lower end of the 80–180 T range quoted in 857, under the same weak-coupling extrapolation method used there.

extra.notations:
- $f$: fraction of $R(T_{c}^{\mathrm{onset}})$ used to define resistive $H_{c2}(T)$.
- $dH_{c2}/dT|_{T_c}$: slope of $H_{c2}(T)$ near $T_c$.
- $H_{c2}(0)$: extrapolated upper critical field at $T=0$.

title: Operational definition of Tc from resistance anomalies
content: The superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ for LaH$_{x}$ samples (with $x\\approx 10$) under megabar-class pressures is, in these reports, defined operationally as the temperature at which an abrupt decrease (anomaly) in measured electrical resistance occurs on cooling. Here $T_{c}$ denotes the reported transition temperature at which the resistance anomaly is observed.

extra.notations:
- LaH$_{x}$: lanthanum hydride with stoichiometry parameter $x$ (here $x\\approx 10$).
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature, as operationally measured by an abrupt resistance decrease.

title: Resistance anomalies alone are insufficient to unambiguously establish superconductivity
content: Although LaH$_{x}$ (with $x\\approx 10$) samples under megabar pressures show abrupt resistance drops on cooling (reported as $T_{c}$), these electrical-resistivity-only observations are plausibly insufficient by themselves to unambiguously demonstrate superconductivity. Independent corroborating evidence — for example, a Meissner effect (expulsion of magnetic flux) detected via magnetic susceptibility or magnetization measurements, observation of zero resistance to within measurement limits with controlled contact artifacts, and characteristic magnetic-field-dependent critical behavior — is likely required to confirm superconductivity definitively.

extra.notations:
- LaH$_{x}$: lanthanum hydride with stoichiometry parameter $x$ (here $x\\approx 10$).
- $T_{c}$: reported temperature of abrupt resistance decrease.
- Meissner effect: the expulsion of magnetic flux from a superconductor below $T_{c}$.

title: Narrow q-width at 41 meV is a property of the magnetic scattering component
content: Using Q-scans across the antiferromagnetic ridge $(1/2,1/2,l)$ to separate magnetic from nuclear contributions, the reported reduced q-width of $0.18\pm0.02$ r.l.u. at the $\sim 41\,$meV feature is an intrinsic property of the magnetic scattering (the dynamical structure factor $S(\vec Q,\hbar\omega)$) rather than an artifact of the nuclear background.

extra.notations:
- $S(\vec{Q},\hbar\omega)$: dynamical structure factor at momentum transfer $\vec{Q}$ and energy transfer $\hbar\omega$. 
- r.l.u.: reciprocal lattice units along in-plane directions (H,K) of the crystal.
- q-width: full width (as reported) of the magnetic peak along the in-plane scan direction, expressed in r.l.u.

title: Resonance at ~41 meV corresponds to increased in-plane magnetic correlation length
content: The significantly reduced in-plane q-width of $0.18\pm0.02$ r.l.u. at the $\sim 41\,$meV magnetic feature implies a longer real-space in-plane magnetic correlation length $\xi$ at that energy compared with energies where the width is $\sim 0.27\pm0.02$ r.l.u. (all widths are FWHM-like measures in reciprocal space). Here $\xi$ denotes the characteristic length scale over which magnetic correlations in the CuO$_2$ planes decay.

extra.notations:
- $\Delta H$: in-plane q-width in r.l.u. (full width reported). 
- $\xi$: in-plane magnetic correlation length (real-space), defined approximately as the inverse of the peak half-width in absolute momentum units under standard Fourier-convention assumptions.
- 1 r.l.u. (in-plane) $= 2\pi/a$, where $a$ is the in-plane lattice constant.

title: Observed intensity modulation along (001) indicates bilayer-related interlayer correlations involving CuO2 planes
content: The reported modulation of scattering intensity along the $(0,0,l)$ (or (001)) direction for the $\sim 41\,$meV feature suggests that the magnetic excitation has a nontrivial $l$-dependent structure factor consistent with interlayer correlations within the CuO$_2$ bilayers (i.e., the excitation amplitude varies with the momentum transfer perpendicular to the planes). Consequently, the resonance likely involves the Cu(2) sites in the superconducting CuO$_2$ bilayers rather than purely single-layer, uncorrelated planar moments.

extra.notations:
- $(001)$ or $(0,0,l)$ direction: momentum transfer perpendicular to the CuO$_2$ planes (out-of-plane).
- Cu(2): copper sites in the CuO$_2$ planes (the superconducting layers) in YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{6+x}$.
- "intensity modulation along (001)": variation of measured magnetic scattering intensity as a function of $l$.

title: Existence of a spectrally narrow magnetic excitation at low temperature
content: For YBa2Cu3O6.92 at temperature T ≈ 5 K, the measured dynamical magnetic response (i.e., the magnetic part of the dynamical structure factor $S(\\mathbf{Q},\\hbar\\omega)$ or the imaginary part of the dynamical susceptibility $\\mathrm{Im}\\{\\chi(\\mathbf{q},\\hbar\\omega)\\}$) contains at least one energy-localized (spectrally narrow) feature. In other words, there exists some momentum transfer $\\mathbf{Q}$ and energy transfer $\\hbar\\omega$ within the studied window for which the magnetic signal displays a pronounced, spectrally narrow peak.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: yttrium–barium–copper oxide with oxygen stoichiometry 6.92 (the cuprate under study). 
- $T$: temperature (here $T\\approx 5\\,$K). 
- $\\hbar\\omega$: energy transfer in inelastic scattering (with $\\hbar$ the reduced Planck constant). 
- $S(\\mathbf{Q},\\hbar\\omega)$: dynamical structure factor; $\\mathrm{Im}\\{\\chi(\\mathbf{q},\\hbar\\omega)\\}$: imaginary part of dynamical spin susceptibility. 
- "Spectrally narrow": an energy feature whose width is small compared with the overall investigated energy window and which appears as a localized peak in energy.

title: Identification of the narrow peak with the ≈28 meV magnetic resonance
content: It is likely that the observed energy-localized (spectrally narrow) peak in the dynamical magnetic response of YBa2Cu3O6.92 corresponds to the superconductivity-associated magnetic resonance centered near $\\hbar\\omega_{\\mathrm{res}}\\approx 28\\,$meV (i.e., the narrow feature's central energy $E_{\\rm peak}\\approx 28\\,$meV and the feature represents the well-known resonance mode of this compound).

extra.notations:
- $\\hbar\\omega_{\\mathrm{res}}$: resonance (central) energy of the magnetic mode. 
- $E_{\\rm peak}$: center energy of the spectrally narrow peak.

title: Energy-resolved features in 20–30 meV separate into Q-dependent magnetic and Q-slowly-varying nuclear components
content: In superconducting YBa2Cu3O6.92 (superconducting transition temperature Tc ≈ 91 K), the inelastic-scattering spectral feature centered near ℏω ≈ 28 meV is a magnetic excitation that will exhibit momentum-transfer (Q) dependence along the antiferromagnetic ridge at wave vectors of the form (1/2,1/2,l) (in reciprocal-lattice units), whereas the spectral feature centered near ℏω ≈ 21.5 meV is dominated by a broadly distributed nuclear (non-magnetic) scattering component that varies only slowly with Q. Thus Q-resolved scans across (1/2,1/2,l) in the 20–30 meV energy window will distinguish the magnetic weight concentrated near 28 meV from the more Q‑independent nuclear background near 21.5 meV.

extra.notations:
- YBa2Cu3O6.92: yttrium barium copper oxide with oxygen content 6.92 per formula unit.
- Tc: superconducting critical temperature (≈ 91 K for this sample).
- ℏω: energy transfer in inelastic neutron scattering (ℏ is reduced Planck constant, ω angular frequency).
- Q: momentum-transfer vector in reciprocal space.
- (1/2,1/2,l): location in reciprocal-lattice units (r.l.u.) forming the antiferromagnetic ridge.
- "magnetic component": scattering intensity arising from spin excitations.
- "nuclear scattering background": non-magnetic scattering (e.g., phonons, nuclear incoherent/coherent scattering) that varies slowly with Q.

title: Hydrogen-derived phonons necessarily dominate the predicted pairing interaction
content: If LaH$_{10}$ adopts the predicted clathrate-like Fm$\overline{3}$m structure in which each La is enclosed by a cage of $\sim$32 H atoms (nodes 820,1634) and density-functional electron–phonon calculations predict a very large $T_c$, then the electron–phonon spectral function $\alpha^2F(\\omega)$ must have substantial spectral weight at the high phonon frequencies associated with hydrogen vibrations. Because hydrogen is the lightest atom in the lattice, its vibrational modes occur at substantially higher angular frequencies $\\omega$ than modes of La; therefore the pairing kernel (which in electron–phonon theory scales with $\alpha^2F(\\omega)/\\omega$ and with characteristic frequency moments such as $\\omega_{\\log}$) necessarily receives dominant contributions from H-derived phonons. Consequently, the predicted high $T_c$ in LaH$_{10}$ (under the stated high-pressure conditions) is necessarily mediated predominantly by hydrogen vibrational modes rather than by low-frequency La modes.

extra.notations:
- $\\alpha^2F(\\omega)$: electron–phonon spectral function (electron–phonon coupling strength as a function of phonon frequency $\\omega$).
- $\\omega_{\\log}$: logarithmic average phonon frequency, a measure that enters McMillan / Allen–Dynes formulas for $T_c$.
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature.

title: Substantial isotope effect (Tc reduction) is expected on H→D substitution
content: Under the stated electron–phonon mechanism and the clathrate H-rich structure of LaH$_{10}$, replacing hydrogen (mass $m_{\\mathrm{H}}$) by deuterium (mass $m_{\\mathrm{D}}\\approx2m_{\\mathrm{H}}$) is expected to reduce characteristic hydrogen phonon frequencies approximately by the factor $\\sqrt{m_{\\mathrm{H}}/m_{\\mathrm{D}}}\\approx 1/\\sqrt{2}$. Because $T_c$ in electron–phonon superconductors scales roughly with a characteristic phonon frequency (e.g. $\\omega_{\\log}$) times a function of the coupling strength, one therefore expects a substantial decrease of $T_c$ upon deuteration. A plausible quantitative estimate (allowing for strong-coupling and retardation effects that reduce the simple BCS exponent) is that $T_c$ for LaD$_{10}$ would be tens of percent lower than for LaH$_{10}$ (e.g. $T_{c}^{\\mathrm{D}}\\sim0.6$–$0.8\\,T_{c}^{\\mathrm{H}}$), rather than unchanged.

extra.notations:
- $T_c$: superconducting critical temperature.
- $m_{\\mathrm{H}}$, $m_{\\mathrm{D}}$: masses of hydrogen and deuterium.
- $\\omega_{\\log}$: logarithmic average phonon frequency.

title: Algebraic decomposition of π operators and their exact SO(5) commutation relations with charge and spin generators
content:
Using the identifications of the SO(5) generators with physical operators (the total charge operator $\mathcal{Q}=L_{15}$ and the spin block $L_{\alpha\beta}=\epsilon_{\alpha\beta\gamma}S_\gamma$ for $\alpha,\beta,\gamma\in\{2,3,4\}$), and the linear definitions
$L_{\alpha 1} = -\tfrac{i}{2}(\pi_\alpha^\dagger - \pi_\alpha)$ and $L_{\alpha 5} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\pi_\alpha^\dagger + \pi_\alpha)$,
one obtains the algebraic inverses
$$\pi_\alpha^\dagger = L_{\alpha 5} + i L_{\alpha 1},\qquad \pi_\alpha = L_{\alpha 5} - i L_{\alpha 1}.$$
From the SO(5) commutation relations $[L_{ab},L_{cd}]=i(\delta_{ad}L_{bc}-\delta_{ac}L_{bd}-\delta_{bd}L_{ac}+\delta_{bc}L_{ad})$ it follows exactly that
- the charge generator acts by
  $$[\mathcal{Q},\pi_\alpha] = -\,\pi_\alpha^\dagger,\qquad [\mathcal{Q},\pi_\alpha^\dagger]=\pi_\alpha,$$
- the spin generators act on the $\pi_\alpha$ as a vector (spin-1 triplet):
  $$[S_\gamma,\pi_\alpha]=i\,\epsilon_{\gamma\alpha\beta}\,\pi_\beta\quad\text{for }\gamma,\alpha,\beta\in\{2,3,4\}.$$
These relations are algebraic consequences of the SO(5) Lie algebra together with the stated identification of the ten generators.

extra.notations:
- $\mathcal{Q}=L_{15}$: total charge generator (as identified in the siblings)
- $S_\gamma$: total spin component (from $L_{\alpha\beta}=\epsilon_{\alpha\beta\gamma}S_\gamma$)
- $\pi_\alpha,\pi_\alpha^\dagger$: pi operators rotating AF and SC order (labels $\alpha\in\{2,3,4\}$)
- $L_{ab}$: SO(5) generators with indices $a,b\in\{1,\dots,5\}$
- $\epsilon_{\alpha\beta\gamma}$: Levi-Civita symbol (3D)

title: Microscopic charge action: π† raises physical electron number by two
content:
Let $\mathcal{N}=\sum_{p,\sigma} c_{p\sigma}^\dagger c_{p\sigma}$ be the physical electron number operator with the standard normalization $[ \mathcal{N}, c_{p\sigma}^\dagger ] = c_{p\sigma}^\dagger$. Given the microscopic definition
$$\pi_\alpha^\dagger=\sum_{p} g(p)\,c_{p+Q,\sigma}^{\dagger}(\sigma^\alpha\sigma^y)_{\sigma\sigma'}\,c_{-p,\sigma'}^{\dagger},$$
the operator $\pi_\alpha^\dagger$ creates two electrons and therefore satisfies
$$[\,\mathcal{N},\pi_\alpha^\dagger\,]=2\,\pi_\alpha^\dagger.$$
Equivalently, $\pi_\alpha^\dagger$ carries physical charge $+2e$ (in units where an electron has charge $-e$ this means it creates a charge-$2e$ pair). This statement requires identifying the sibling $\mathcal{Q}=L_{15}$ with the standard electron-number operator (up to no anomalous normalization).

extra.notations:
- $\mathcal{N}$: physical electron-number operator with $[\mathcal{N},c_{p\sigma}^\dagger]=c_{p\sigma}^\dagger$
- $\pi_\alpha^\dagger$: microscopic pi creation operator defined in 1138/1165
- $g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y$: d-wave form factor
- $Q$: total momentum $(\pi,\pi,\pi)$

title: SI-STM-detected ~-0.96 eV LDOS features identify individual interstitial O dopants and therefore measure dopant areal density
content: If (a) individual interstitial oxygen dopant atoms in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ produce localized impurity-state features that are detectable by spectroscopic imaging STM (SI-STM), and (b) every localized impurity-state LDOS feature observed at energy ≈ −0.96 eV corresponds to a single oxygen dopant atom (as asserted from the observed consistency between the spatial density of these features and the doping-dependent hole contribution to the CuO2 plane), then any SI-STM map of LDOS(given energy ≈ −0.96 eV) that resolves these localized features both (i) identifies the positions of individual interstitial oxygen dopants and (ii) yields their areal number density n_O (number of such features per unit area). Under the further, explicitly stated empirical correspondence between feature density and hole contribution, the measured n_O is therefore an estimator for the local/global hole concentration p in the CuO2 plane (modulo the known conversion factor between dopant count and hole count used in the empirical calibration).

extra.notations:
- LDOS( r, E ): local density of states at spatial location r and energy E.
- SI-STM: spectroscopic imaging scanning tunneling microscopy.
- n_O: areal number density of localized LDOS features at E ≈ −0.96 eV (features per unit area).
- p: hole density per CuO2 unit in Bi2Sr2CaCu2O8+δ.

title: Using −0.96 eV impurity resonances as spatial markers to correlate dopant positions with local superconducting gap variations
content: Given that SI-STM detects localized impurity-state LDOS features at ≈ −0.96 eV which correspond to individual interstitial oxygen dopants, it is plausible that one can use the spatial map of those features as precise markers of dopant positions and thereby compute spatial correlations between dopant locations and the local superconducting gap magnitude Δ(r) (measured independently by SI-STM). From such an analysis one can infer how local oxygen dopants affect superconducting properties on nanometer scales (for example, whether dopants tend to coincide with regions of larger or smaller Δ). This is a plausible inference but requires the additional assumptions that (i) the local gap Δ(r) is measured with sufficient spatial resolution and signal-to-noise, (ii) the dopant-induced electronic perturbation is neither completely screened nor completely masked by other disorder, and (iii) any empirical calibration between dopant count and hole concentration is applicable locally.

extra.notations:
- Δ(r): local superconducting gap magnitude at position r as measured by SI-STM (e.g., from coherence-peak separation).
- n_O(r): local indicator function (or local density) marking presence of a −0.96 eV impurity resonance at r.

title: Numerical upper bounds on doped lattice parameters
content:
If $a_0$ and $c_0$ denote the room-temperature lattice parameters of undoped SmFeAsO with $a_0=0.3940\\ \\text{nm}$ and $c_0=0.8501\\ \\text{nm}$, and if for $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_x$ (with $x>0$) fluorine substitution "produces a contraction of the lattice parameters $a$ and $c$ relative to the undoped $SmFeAsO$", then for any doped composition with $x>0$ the lattice parameters satisfy
$$a(x) < a_0 = 0.3940\\ \\text{nm},\\qquad c(x) < c_0 = 0.8501\\ \\text{nm}.$$
Here $a(x)$ and $c(x)$ are the room-temperature in-plane and out-of-plane lattice parameters of $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_x$ respectively.

extra.notations:
- $a_0$: in-plane lattice parameter of undoped SmFeAsO (given value $0.3940\\ \\text{nm}$). 
- $c_0$: out-of-plane lattice parameter of undoped SmFeAsO (given value $0.8501\\ \\text{nm}$). 
- $a(x),c(x)$: corresponding lattice parameters for $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_x$.

title: Unit-cell volume decreases upon fluorine substitution
content:
Let $V_0$ denote the room-temperature unit-cell volume of tetragonal SmFeAsO, $V_0 = a_0^2 c_0$ with $a_0=0.3940\\ \\text{nm}$ and $c_0=0.8501\\ \\text{nm}$. For $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_x$ with $x>0$ and lattice parameters $a(x),c(x)$ that satisfy $a(x)<a_0$ and $c(x)<c_0$, the unit-cell volume satisfies
$$V(x)=a(x)^2 c(x) < a_0^2 c_0 = V_0.$$
Thus fluorine substitution necessarily reduces the room-temperature unit-cell volume relative to the undoped parent.

extra.notations:
- $V_0$: unit-cell volume of undoped SmFeAsO, $V_0=a_0^2 c_0$. 
- $V(x)$: unit-cell volume of $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_x$, $V(x)=a(x)^2 c(x)$.

title: Fluorine-induced lattice contraction acts as chemical pressure with likely electronic consequences
content:
The observed contraction of both $a$ and $c$ on substituting fluorine for oxygen in $SmFeAsO_{1-x}F_x$ implies a decreased unit-cell volume (chemical compression). It is plausible (with probability ~0.75) that this chemical compression (i) increases overlap of Fe 3d and As 4p orbitals and/or modifies Fe–As bond geometry, and (ii) thereby alters the electronic band structure (bandwidths, Fermi-surface topology) in a way that contributes to the emergence or modification of superconducting and transport properties relative to the undoped parent.
Symbols: $a,c$ are lattice parameters; "chemical pressure" denotes the effective internal pressure produced by reduced lattice dimensions due to substitution.

extra.notations:
- chemical pressure: effective internal pressure produced by lattice contraction from chemical substitution.
- Fe–As bond geometry: bond lengths/angles in the FeAs layer that control electronic structure.

title: Existence of spatially inhomogeneous superconducting regions with higher local onset temperature than bulk Tc
content: If a polycrystalline, oxygen‑deficient Ba–La–Cu–O sample shows a pronounced resistivity drop beginning at an onset temperature $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}\approx 35\,$K (proposition 1363) but exhibits bulk superconducting behavior (zero or bulk-like resistivity) only below $13\,$K, and the material is percolative (multiple phases/grain boundaries; proposition 1369), then there necessarily exist spatially localized regions (grains, phases, or filaments) that become superconducting at temperatures near $35\,$K while the sample as a whole does not attain global superconducting connectivity until $\sim 13\,$K. In other words, local superconductivity appears at $T\approx 35\,$K in parts of the sample, and global/percolative superconductivity (sample‑spanning phase coherence and bulk zero resistivity) is only achieved upon further cooling to $\approx 13\,$K.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}$: experimentally identified onset temperature where a pronounced downward change in resistivity $\rho(T)$ begins. 
- $T_{c,\mathrm{bulk}}$: temperature below which bulk (sample‑spanning) superconducting behavior is observed (here $\approx 13\,$K).
- "Percolative": the sample contains distinct regions/phases/grains such that global conduction (including superconducting paths) depends on connectivity between superconducting regions.

title: Higher onset likely due to minority phase, grain‑boundary, or oxygen‑deficient regions rather than a homogeneous new high‑Tc phase
content: Given (a) an onset of resistivity decrease at $T_{c,\mathrm{onset}}\approx 35\,$K that exceeds previously reported homogeneous $T_c$ in comparable materials, and (b) the sample's percolative, multiphase character, it is plausible that the high onset temperature reflects superconductivity in minority phases, oxygen‑deficient grains, or filamentary/grain‑boundary regions with locally enhanced $T_c$, rather than a homogeneous, bulk new superconducting phase with $T_c\approx 35\,$K.

extra.notations:
- "Minority phase": a material phase present only in a small volume fraction of the polycrystalline sample.
- "Filamentary/grain‑boundary superconductivity": current paths confined to narrow, possibly surface or boundary regions that become superconducting at higher $T$ than the bulk grains.
- Oxygen deficiency: local deviation from ideal oxygen stoichiometry that can change carrier concentration $n$ and thus local superconducting properties.

title: Normal-state metallicity of the predicted LaH$_{10}$ phase at high pressure
content: If a phase is predicted to be a superconductor with a finite superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ under given pressure conditions, then the normal (non-superconducting) state at those conditions must possess a nonzero electronic density of states at the Fermi level (i.e. be metallic). Therefore the LaH$_{10}$ clathrate-type phase predicted (by DFT structure searches and electron–phonon calculations) to have $T_{c}\approx 270\,$K at $210\,$GPa is necessarily metallic at those pressures: it has electronic states crossing the Fermi energy and a Fermi surface in the DFT description used to obtain the superconducting prediction.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}$: superconducting critical temperature (Kelvin). 
- "Metallic": nonzero electronic density of states at the Fermi level; bands cross the Fermi energy.
- GPa: gigapascal (unit of pressure).

title: Hydrogen atoms must be shared between La-centered cages to yield LaH$_{10}$ stoichiometry
content: The siblings report both (A) the stoichiometry LaH$_{10}$ (approximately ten hydrogen atoms per lanthanum atom in the crystallographic formula unit) and (B) that each La atom is surrounded by a cage of 32 hydrogen atoms in the clathrate-type Fm$\overline{3}$m structure. These two statements together logically imply that the 32 hydrogen positions that define a La-centered cage are not uniquely assigned to a single La atom: individual hydrogen atoms must be shared between cages (i.e. each hydrogen site is coordinated to more than one La in the crystal), so that the crystallographic count of hydrogen yields an average of about 10 H per La in the chemical formula.

extra.notations:
- LaH$_{10}$: chemical stoichiometry indicating on average ten hydrogen atoms per lanthanum atom in the crystal formula unit.
- "Cage of 32 H": local coordination describing that around each La center the surrounding hydrogen framework contains 32 hydrogen lattice sites (coordination number expressed as H positions forming the cage).

title: High $T_{c}$ plausibly arises from strong H-derived, high-frequency electron–phonon coupling
content: Given that the DFT searches predict LaH$_{10}$ to have H–H interatomic distances of order $1.1\,$\AA\ and a very high $T_{c}\approx 270\,$K at $210\,$GPa, it is plausible (under the additional assumption that the superconductivity is conventional electron–phonon mediated) that the superconducting pairing is dominated by high-frequency phonon modes derived from hydrogen vibrations and by strong electron–phonon coupling to those modes. Short H–H distances at megabar pressures increase phonon frequencies and can enhance the electron–phonon prefactor ($\omega_{\rm log}$) and, if coupling strength $\lambda$ is sufficiently large, produce the very large $T_{c}$ predicted.

extra.notations:
- H–H distance: interatomic separation between nearest hydrogen atoms (angstrom, \AA).
- $\omega_{\rm log}$: logarithmic average phonon frequency entering Allen–Dynes formula.
- $\lambda$: electron–phonon coupling constant.

title: Four-probe electrical transport is feasible for LaH10 at 180–200 GPa using the reported synthesis method
content: Using ammonia borane ($NH_{3}BH_{3}$) as a hydrogen source together with modulated pulsed laser heating (300-ms pulses, $\sim$30-$\mu$m spot) at pressures in the 180–200 GPa range permits the synthesis of cubic lanthanum superhydride ($LaH_{10\pm x}$) while preserving sample–probe electrical contacts sufficiently to perform four-probe electrical transport measurements. Here $LaH_{10\pm x}$ denotes lanthanum hydride with stoichiometry near 10 H per La, GPa is gigapascal, and K is kelvin.

extra.notations:
- $NH_{3}BH_{3}$: ammonia borane, used as a hydrogen source and solid pressure medium\n
- $LaH_{10\pm x}$: lanthanum superhydride phase near stoichiometry 10\n
- GPa: gigapascal (pressure unit)\n
- four-probe: electrical transport measurement geometry where separate current and voltage leads are used

title: Differences in reported $T_c(P)$ trends may stem from different hydrogen-loading/synthesis methods and contact preservation
content: Let $T_c(P)$ denote the superconducting critical temperature as a function of pressure $P$. The reported dome-like $T_c(P)$ with a maximum $T_c\approx 250\text{–}252\,$K at $\sim170\,$GPa and an abrupt decrease by $\sim202\,$GPa (node 303) is plausibly explained by differences in sample preparation and measurement integrity compared to prior reports claiming a continuous increase to $\sim275\,$K. Specifically, synthesis using solid $NH_{3}BH_{3}$ and modulated pulsed laser heating (node 816) reduces electrode damage and may produce more stoichiometric, homogeneous $LaH_{10\pm x}$ regions probed by four-probe transport; by contrast, direct $H_2$ loading and different heating protocols can cause contact degradation, phase inhomogeneity, or altered stoichiometry, which can bias measured $T_c(P)$ upward or produce spurious signals. Therefore, the lower peak $T_c$ and abrupt pressure-induced fall observed in node 303 are plausibly intrinsic to well-contacted, $NH_{3}BH_{3}$-synthesized LaH$_{10}$, while the higher continuous-increasing reports may reflect different sample conditions or measurement artifacts.

extra.notations:
- $T_c(P)$: superconducting critical temperature as a function of pressure $P$\n
- $LaH_{10\pm x}$: lanthanum hydride with stoichiometry near 10 H per La\n
- $NH_{3}BH_{3}$: ammonia borane, solid hydrogen source / pressure medium\n
- $H_2$: molecular hydrogen gas loading

title: Isotropic harmonic electron–phonon models cannot simultaneously reproduce MgB2's Tc and boron isotope exponent
content: Let $T_{c}^{\rm obs}\approx 39\,$K denote the experimentally observed superconducting transition temperature of MgB$_2$. Proposition 692 reports that an isotropic Eliashberg solution using the same material parameters yields $T_{c}^{\rm iso}=19\,$K (i.e. isotropic approximations severely underestimate $T_{c}$). Proposition 706 reports an observed boron isotope-effect exponent $\alpha_{B}=0.32$, while the harmonic expectation for the modes in question is $\alpha_{\rm harm}\approx 0.46$. Therefore any model that is both isotropic (no momentum dependence in the electron–phonon kernel) and harmonic (no anharmonic weakening of electron–phonon coupling) is inconsistent with the two empirical facts ($T_{c}^{\rm obs}\approx 39\,$K and $\alpha_{B}=0.32$): isotropic harmonic calculations give the wrong $T_{c}$ (they yield $19\,$K) and the harmonic assumption predicts the wrong isotope exponent ($\approx0.46$ rather than $0.32$). Hence, an isotropic harmonic electron–phonon model cannot simultaneously reproduce the observed $T_{c}$ and the observed boron isotope-effect exponent of MgB$_2$.

extra.notations:
- $T_{c}^{\rm obs}$: experimentally observed superconducting transition temperature (approx.\ $39\,$K for MgB$_2$). 
- $T_{c}^{\rm iso}$: transition temperature from an isotropic Eliashberg calculation (reported as $19\,$K in node 692).
- $\alpha_{B}$: boron isotope-effect exponent (reported as $0.32$ in node 706).
- $\alpha_{\rm harm}$: isotope exponent expected in the harmonic approximation (quoted $\approx 0.46$ in node 706).

title: Anisotropic, σ-band–dominated coupling compensates anharmonic weakening to produce high Tc
content: Denote by $\lambda_{\rm eff}$ the effective (Fermi-surface averaged) electron–phonon coupling that sets $T_{c}$, and by $\lambda_{\rm harm}$ the coupling one would obtain under harmonic approximations. Proposition 706 implies anharmonicity reduces the effective electron–phonon coupling relative to $\lambda_{\rm harm}$ (i.e. $\lambda_{\rm eff}<\lambda_{\rm harm}$ for the key boron modes). Proposition 692 shows that isotropic treatments (which average momentum-dependent coupling) give a $T_{c}$ far below the observed value ($19\,$K vs.\ $\sim39\,$K). A plausible resolution is that a strongly momentum-dependent (anisotropic) coupling concentrated on the boron-derived $\sigma$ Fermi surfaces — particularly to the in-plane E$_{2g}$ boron phonon — provides a local, large pairing strength that (i) increases $T_{c}$ significantly above the isotropic average and (ii) survives despite anharmonic weakening of the mode-averaged coupling. Therefore it is likely that the high $T_{c}$ of MgB$_2$ arises from strong, k-selective electron–phonon coupling (σ-band → E$_{2g}$) that compensates the reduction of overall coupling due to anharmonicity.

extra.notations:
- $\lambda_{\rm eff}$: effective electron–phonon coupling controlling superconducting $T_{c}$ after including anisotropy and anharmonicity.
- $\lambda_{\rm harm}$: electron–phonon coupling estimated under harmonic phonons.
- E$_{2g}$: in-plane boron optical phonon mode often implicated in MgB$_2$ pairing.
- σ-band: the boron-derived quasi-two-dimensional bands in MgB$_2$ with strong coupling to in-plane phonons.

title: Linear relation between average gap and hole concentration
content:
Define $n$ as the spatial density of the identified impurity states per $CuO_2$ plaquette and let $p$ be the hole concentration (holes per Cu) induced in the $CuO_2$ plane by those impurities. If each impurity contributes approximately two holes, then $p = 2\,n$. Combining this with the empirical relation $\bar{\Delta}\approx[75 - 600\times n]\;\text{meV}$ (using the central coefficient from Prop. 1071) yields the deterministic relation
$\displaystyle \bar{\Delta} \approx 75 - 300\,p\quad\text{(meV)}$,
i.e. the sample-averaged superconducting gap magnitude is a linear, decreasing function of the hole concentration $p$ with slope $-300\;\text{meV per hole}$. All symbols: $n$ = impurity density per $CuO_2$ plaquette; $p$ = holes per Cu; $\bar{\Delta}$ = sample-averaged superconducting gap (meV).

extra.notations:
- $n$: impurity-state density per $CuO_2$ plaquette (dimensionless fraction). 
- $p$: hole concentration (holes per Cu), $p=2n$ under the ~2-holes-per-impurity assumption. 
- $\bar{\Delta}$: sample-averaged superconducting gap magnitude (meV).

title: SI-STM maps of the $-0.96\,$eV resonance as a quantitative proxy for local hole doping
content: Because (i) Prop. 1538 states that SI-STM can detect individual interstitial oxygen dopant atoms as localized impurity-state features, and (ii) Prop. 1071 identifies the $-0.96\,$eV impurity-state as arising from such oxygen dopants and links their density $n$ to hole injection (~2 holes per dopant), it is plausible that spatial SI-STM maps of the $-0.96\,$eV resonance provide a quantitative local map of dopant-induced hole concentration. Under this conjecture the local hole concentration at position $\vec r$ can be estimated as $p(\vec r)\approx 2\,n(\vec r)$ where $n(\vec r)$ is the local density of identified $-0.96\,$eV resonances (per plaquette), and local superconducting gap variations should anti-correlate with $n(\vec r)$ roughly with the sample-averaged slope (order $-600\,$meV per unit-$n$ or equivalently $\sim-300\,$meV per hole).

extra.notations:
- $n(\vec r)$: local density (per plaquette) of $-0.96\,$eV impurity resonances at position $\vec r$ as measured by SI-STM. 
- $p(\vec r)$: local hole concentration (holes per Cu) at $\vec r$, conjectured $p(\vec r)\approx 2\,n(\vec r)$. 
- $\bar{\Delta}$: sample-averaged superconducting gap; local gap $\Delta(\vec r)$ expected to anti-correlate with $n(\vec r)$.

title: Exact expression for low-energy ARPES spectral weight at k_F and its value at $T^{*}$
content:
Using the phenomenological self-energy
$$
\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -\,i\Gamma_{1} \;+\; \dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{\omega+\epsilon(\mathbf{k})+i\Gamma_{0}}\,,
$$
at the Fermi momentum $\mathbf{k}=\mathbf{k}_{F}$ (so $\epsilon(\mathbf{k}_{F})=0$) and at $\omega=0$ one has
$$
\Sigma(\mathbf{k}_{F},0) = -\,i\Gamma_{1} \;+\; \dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{i\Gamma_{0}} = -\,i\Big(\Gamma_{1}+\dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{\Gamma_{0}}\Big).
$$
The single-particle spectral function is
$$
A(\mathbf{k},\omega) = -\dfrac{1}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Im}\, \dfrac{1}{\omega - \epsilon_{\mathbf{k}} - \Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)}.
$$
Evaluated at $\mathbf{k}_{F}$ and $\omega=0$ (and using $\mathrm{Re}\,\Sigma(\mathbf{k}_{F},0)=0$ above), this gives the exact expression
$$
A(\mathbf{k}_{F},0) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\pi\big(\Gamma_{1}+\dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{\Gamma_{0}}\big)}.
$$
Using the parent/sibling condition at the pseudogap temperature $T^{*}$, $\Delta(T^{*})\approx\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$, one obtains the evaluated value
$$
A(\mathbf{k}_{F},0;T^{*}) \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{\pi\big(\Gamma_{1}+\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})\big)}.
$$
Equivalently, the low-energy effective broadening (inverse lifetime) that controls the zero-energy spectral weight is
$$
\Gamma_{\mathrm{eff}}(\mathbf{k}_{F},T)\;=\;\Gamma_{1}(T)\;+\;\dfrac{\Delta(T)^{2}}{\Gamma_{0}(T)},
$$
and at $T^{*}$ one has $\Gamma_{\mathrm{eff}}(T^{*})\approx\Gamma_{1}(T^{*})+\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$.

extra.notations:
- $\mathbf{k}_{F}$: Fermi momentum where $\epsilon(\mathbf{k}_{F})=0$. 
- $\omega$: energy relative to the chemical potential; $\omega=0$ is the Fermi energy.
- $A(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: single-particle spectral function.
- $\Sigma(\mathbf{k},\omega)$: complex self-energy with real and imaginary parts.
- $\Gamma_{1}$: single-particle scattering rate (imaginary part contribution).
- $\Gamma_{0}$: inverse pair lifetime (pair-pole broadening).
- $\Delta$: pairing amplitude (superconducting gap / pseudogap magnitude).
- $\Gamma_{\mathrm{eff}}$: effective low-energy broadening controlling $A(\mathbf{k}_{F},0)$.

title: Interpretation: $T^{*}$ marks a crossover from coherent to incoherent pair response (filling, not closing)
content: Within the three-parameter self-energy model, the condition $\Delta(T^{*})\approx\Gamma_{0}(T^{*})$ implies that the pair-pole in the denominator acquires an imaginary part comparable to its real part at energies of order the gap. Consequently, pair correlations at and above $T^{*}$ are short-lived (lifetime $\sim 1/\Gamma_{0}$) even if the local pairing amplitude $\Delta(T)$ remains nonzero. Therefore it is plausible that $T^{*}$ corresponds to a crossover where the pseudogap in ARPES is erased by lifetime broadening ("filling in") rather than by a vanishing of the pairing amplitude ("closing"). Above $T^{*}$ one expects broad, incoherent low-energy spectral weight with an effective width $\Gamma_{\mathrm{eff}}\gtrsim\Gamma_{0}$ while $\Delta(T)>0$ can persist on short length/time scales (a preformed-pair or incoherent-pairing phenomenology).

extra.notations:
- $\Delta(T)$: pairing amplitude as a function of temperature.
- $\Gamma_{0}(T)$: inverse pair lifetime as a function of temperature.
- $\Gamma_{\mathrm{eff}}$: effective low-energy single-particle broadening (see deduction).

title: Alpha-pocket presence (or absence) determines dominant pairing channel in the five-band RPA analysis
content: Within the same effective five-band model treated by RPA, the presence of the concentric hole pockets $\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2}$ centered at $k=(0,0)$ selects an extended $s$-wave (``s±'') state whose gap changes sign between the hole ($\alpha$) and electron ($\beta$) pockets, whereas if those $\alpha$ pockets are absent or ineffective the favored symmetry in the RPA treatment is $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ (gap changing sign between the electron pockets $\beta_{1}$ and $\beta_{2}$). Thus, the qualitative identity of the dominant superconducting channel (extended $s$ versus $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$) is determined by whether the $\alpha$ pockets participate in the low-energy Fermi-surface topology.

extra.notations:
- $\alpha_{1},\alpha_{2}$: concentric holelike Fermi pockets at $k=(0,0)$.
- $\beta_{1},\beta_{2}$: electronlike Fermi pockets near $(\pi,0)$ and $(0,\pi)$.
- s± (extended $s$-wave): superconducting gap that changes sign between $\alpha$ and $\beta$ pockets.
- $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$: superconducting gap that changes sign between $\beta_{1}$ and $\beta_{2}$.
- RPA: random-phase approximation calculation on the five-band model.

title: Interband (off-diagonal) gap components can lift diagonal nodal lines into finite minima
content: If the diagonal (intraband) components of the superconducting gap matrix $\hat{\phi}$ have nodal lines that intersect a Fermi surface (creating zeros of the diagonal gap on the Fermi surface), non-negligible off-diagonal (interband) elements of $\hat{\phi}$ generically produce a nonzero total gap amplitude everywhere so that the gauge-invariant gap magnitude $G_{a}(\mathbf{k})\equiv\sqrt{(\hat{\phi}\hat{\phi}^{\dagger})_{aa}(\mathbf{k})}$ on band $a$ remains finite across the Brillouin zone; the former diagonal nodes then appear as deep minima (valleys) rather than symmetry-protected zeros of $G_{a}(\mathbf{k})$.

extra.notations:
- $\hat{\phi}$: superconducting gap function matrix in orbital/band space.
- $(\hat{\phi}\hat{\phi}^{\dagger})_{aa}(\mathbf{k})$: $a$-th diagonal element of the positive semi-definite matrix $\hat{\phi}\hat{\phi}^{\dagger}$ at momentum $\mathbf{k}$.
- $G_{a}(\mathbf{k})$: gauge-invariant gap magnitude on band $a$.

title: Parameter-dependent phase diagram with s± (gapped), s± (nodal/minima), and d-wave regions
content: In the space of experimentally tunable or material-dependent parameters (band filling, crystal-field splittings that control the presence/effective weight of the $\alpha$ pockets, and interaction strengths $U,U',J,J'$), the five-band RPA model likely supports a phase diagram with at least three neighboring pairing regimes: (i) an extended s± region where $\alpha$–$\beta$ interactions dominate and the gap is sign-changing but largely nodeless on the Fermi surfaces, (ii) an extended s± region where competing $\beta_{1}$–$\beta_{2}$ interactions induce diagonal nodal lines on the $\beta$ pockets which are converted to deep minima by interband pairing, and (iii) a $d_{x^{2}-y^{2}}$ region reached when the $\alpha$ pockets are removed or are ineffective. Continuous tuning of filling or interaction ratios therefore can produce transitions between these regimes.

extra.notations:
- $n$: band filling (electrons per site); e.g. $n=6.3$ in Proposition 779 corresponds to increased electron doping that suppresses $\alpha$ pockets.
- s± (nodal/minima): extended $s$ with diagonal nodal lines on band-resolved gap components, but finite $G_{a}(\mathbf{k})$ due to interband terms.
- phase diagram: qualitative map in parameter space $(n,U,U',J,J',\ldots)$ of dominant pairing symmetry.

title: Damped Rabi oscillations in the measured conductivity (RWA two-level result)
content: Under the rotating-wave-approximation (RWA) two-level description that accurately models the measured signal, the fraction excited out of the ground state at pulse end, $1-\\rho_{11}(\\tau)$, necessarily exhibits Rabi oscillations at the generalized Rabi angular frequency $\\Omega' = \\sqrt{\\Omega^{2}+\\Delta^{2}}$, where $\\Omega$ is the on-resonance Rabi angular frequency (proportional to the drive amplitude $E_{THz}$) and $\\Delta$ is the detuning. Because the measured conductivity is proportional to $1-\\rho_{11}(\\tau)$ (Proposition 1307) and the experimentally extracted oscillation frequency scales linearly with $E_{THz}$ with an offset from detuning (Proposition 1311), the conductivity versus pulse duration $\\tau$ must show (potentially damped) Rabi oscillations whose oscillation frequency follows the stated linear-in-$E_{THz}$ scaling (with a nonzero intercept set by $\\Delta$).

extra.notations:
- $\\rho_{11}(\\tau)$: ground-state population at pulse end.
- $1-\\rho_{11}(\\tau)$: excited fraction (proportional to measured conductivity).
- $\\Omega$: on-resonance Rabi angular frequency ($\\propto E_{THz}$).
- $\\Delta$: detuning (angular frequency).
- $\\Omega'=\\sqrt{\\Omega^{2}+\\Delta^{2}}$: generalized Rabi angular frequency.
- $\\gamma_{2},\\gamma_{3}$: transverse dephasing and population/damping rates (enter as envelopes).

title: Numerical dipole matrix element implied by the reported Rabi frequency
content:
The reported Rabi angular frequency $\\Omega=4.7\\times10^{11}\\ \\mathrm{rad\\ s^{-1}}$ at $E_{THz}=3.1\\times 10^{4}\\ \\mathrm{V\\ m^{-1}}$ implies an electric-dipole matrix element magnitude
$$\\mu = \\frac{\\hbar\\,\\Omega}{E_{THz}}.$$
Numerically, with $\\hbar\\approx1.055\\times10^{-34}\\ \\mathrm{J\\,s}$, this gives $\\mu\\approx1.6\\times10^{-27}\\ \\mathrm{C\\,m}$, which equals $e\\times(10\\ \\mathrm{nm})$ to within rounding. Thus the experimental numbers are algebraically consistent with a dipole length of order $10\\ \\mathrm{nm}$.

extra.notations:
- $\\Omega$: reported Rabi angular frequency (rad s$^{-1}$).
- $E_{THz}$: terahertz electric-field amplitude (V m$^{-1}$).
- $\\mu$: electric-dipole matrix element (C m).
- $\\hbar$: reduced Planck's constant.
- $e$: elementary charge.

title: Required coherence and pulse-duration scales to resolve Rabi cycles at the reported field
content:
If the reported Rabi angular frequency $\\Omega\\approx4.7\\times10^{11}\\ \\mathrm{rad\\ s^{-1}}$ (at $E_{THz}=3.1\\times10^{4}\\ \\mathrm{V\\ m^{-1}}$) governs the donor dynamics, the Rabi period is
$$T_{R}=\\frac{2\\pi}{\\Omega}\\approx1.34\\times10^{-11}\\ \\mathrm{s}\\approx13.4\\ \\mathrm{ps}.$$
To resolve at least one full Rabi cycle in the measured conductivity one therefore needs pulse durations $\\tau$ of order $T_{R}$ or larger and a transverse coherence time $T_{2}=1/\\gamma_{2}$ of order $T_{R}$ or longer (i.e., $\\gamma_{2}\\lesssim 1/T_{R}\\approx7.5\\times10^{10}\\ \\mathrm{s^{-1}}$). To observe multiple visible oscillations one requires $T_{2}\\gg T_{R}$ (equivalently $\\gamma_{2} \\ll \\Omega$). These numerical scales set experimental constraints on pulse duration and sample dephasing for clear Rabi-oscillation signatures in the conductivity.

extra.notations:
- $T_{R}$: Rabi period (s).
- $T_{2}=1/\\gamma_{2}$: transverse coherence time (s).
- $\\gamma_{2}$: transverse dephasing rate (s$^{-1}$).
- $\\Omega$: Rabi angular frequency (rad s$^{-1}$).

title: Existence and separability of a nuclear (non-magnetic) spectral component near 20 meV
content:
If (a) the nuclear (non-magnetic) contribution to the inelastic-neutron scattering in YBa2Cu3O6.92 is modeled to include a spectral component peaked at ≈20 meV, and (b) Q-dependent scans across the antiferromagnetic (AF) ridge allow empirical definition and isolation of nuclear background from magnetic scattering, and (c) measurable scattering is observed in the superconducting state in the energy window ℏω ≈ 20–30 meV, then it necessarily follows that:
(1) the measured inelastic intensity in YBa2Cu3O6.92 within ℏω ≈ 20–30 meV contains a non-negligible nuclear (non-magnetic) component peaked near ℏω ≈ 20 meV; and
(2) that nuclear component is separable (i.e., can be empirically identified and subtracted) from the magnetic spin-excitation signal by performing Q-scans across the AF ridge, yielding an isolated magnetic spectrum in which magnetic spectral weight below ≈25 meV is substantially reduced relative to the uncorrected total intensity.
All symbols are defined in the notations below.

extra.notations:
- $YBa_{2}Cu_{3}O_{6.92}$: Yttrium barium copper oxide with oxygen content 6.92 per formula unit.
- $\hbar\omega$: energy transfer in inelastic neutron scattering ($\hbar$ is reduced Planck's constant).
- "nuclear": non-magnetic lattice-related scattering contributions (coherent/incoherent nuclear scattering, phonon-related spectral features, or other nuclear-origin intensity).
- "magnetic" / "spin-excitation": scattering arising from electronic spin dynamics concentrated near the AF wavevector $(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2},l)$.
- "Q-scans across the AF ridge": scans in reciprocal space along the $(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2},l)$ rod to probe Q-dependent variation of intensity.

title: Risk of attributing the ≈20–25 meV intensity to magnetism without nuclear-background subtraction
content:
It is plausibly likely (probability ≈ 0.85) that, in published or ongoing analyses of inelastic-neutron-scattering data on YBa2Cu3O6.92 which do not explicitly perform the Q-dependent subtraction described in Proposition 1444, the spectral intensity observed near ℏω ≈ 20–25 meV will be at least partly misattributed to magnetic excitations. Consequently, reanalysis that removes the modeled nuclear components (flat background, a ≈20 meV peak, and broad continuum) will shift the apparent onset of magnetic spectral weight upward (toward ≳25 meV) and clarify the assignment of the magnetic resonance or spin-excitation features (e.g., the concentration of magnetic weight near ≈28 meV).
Definitions: probabilities refer to the conditional plausibility given the sibling propositions; symbols as defined above.

extra.notations:
- "misattributed": the assignment of measured spectral intensity to magnetic (spin) excitations when part or all originates from nuclear scattering.
- "apparent onset of magnetic spectral weight": the lowest energy at which the residual (post-subtraction) magnetic scattering is non-negligible.

title: Charge and spin commutation relations for the $\pi$ generators and their Hermitian combinations
content:
Using the standard electron-number (total charge) operator $\mathcal{Q}$ which counts electrons and the definitions of the $\pi$ operators as two-electron (pair) creation/annihilation operators, the following identities necessarily hold:
1) The $\pi$ operators raise or lower total charge by two:
$$[\mathcal{Q},\pi_\alpha^\dagger]=2\,\pi_\alpha^\dagger,\qquad [\mathcal{Q},\pi_\alpha]=-2\,\pi_\alpha.$$
2) Defining the Hermitian SO(5) generators (given in Proposition 1156) by
$$L_{\alpha 1}=-\frac{i}{2}(\pi_\alpha^\dagger-\pi_\alpha),\qquad 
  L_{\alpha 5}=\frac{1}{2}(\pi_\alpha^\dagger+\pi_\alpha),$$
one obtains exact commutation relations with the charge operator:
$$[\mathcal{Q},L_{\alpha 1}]=-2i\,L_{\alpha 5},\qquad 
  [\mathcal{Q},L_{\alpha 5}]=2i\,L_{\alpha 1}.$$
3) From the explicit spin structure in the definition $\pi_\alpha^\dagger=\sum_p g(p)\,c_{p+Q,\gamma}^\dagger(\sigma^\alpha\sigma^y)_{\gamma\delta} c_{-p,\delta}^\dagger$ and the usual total spin generators $S_\gamma=\sum_{p,\sigma\sigma'} c_{p\sigma}^\dagger(\sigma^\gamma_{\sigma\sigma'}/2)c_{p\sigma'}$, the $\pi_\alpha^\dagger$ transform as a spin-1 (vector) under rotations:
$$[S_\gamma,\pi_\alpha^\dagger]=i\,\epsilon_{\gamma\alpha\beta}\,\pi_\beta^\dagger,$$
with the analogous relation for $\pi_\alpha$.
These relations follow algebraically from the definitions in the two sibling propositions and elementary commutation rules for fermion bilinears.

extra.notations:
- $\mathcal{Q}$: total electron charge operator (counts electrons).
- $\pi_\alpha^\dagger,\pi_\alpha$: pair creation/annihilation operators defined in Prop. 1165.
- $L_{\alpha 1},L_{\alpha 5}$: Hermitian SO(5) generators given in Prop. 1156.
- $S_\gamma$: total spin operator component.
- $g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y$: d-wave form factor.
- $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$: antiferromagnetic ordering momentum.
- $\epsilon_{\gamma\alpha\beta}$: Levi-Civita symbol.

title: Existence of a low-energy $\pi$ collective mode coupling AF and d-wave SC sectors
content: Given the existence of $\pi_\alpha^\dagger$ operators that are spin-1, charge-2, carry total momentum $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$ and have the d-wave form factor $g(p)$, it is plausible that the physical spectrum contains a collective $\pi$-resonance: a relatively sharp, low-energy excitation at momentum $Q$ that connects the antiferromagnetic (AF) and d-wave superconducting (SC) sectors. This mode would correspond to oscillations in the components $L_{\alpha 1},L_{\alpha 5}$ (the Hermitian $\pi$-generator combinations) and can mix with particle-hole spin excitations in the presence of superconducting order, producing an observable resonance in probes sensitive to spin and pair channels (e.g., inelastic neutron scattering or pair-sensitive spectroscopies).

extra.notations:
- $\pi_\alpha^\dagger$: pair creation operator (Prop. 1165) carrying spin-1, charge 2, momentum $Q$.
- $L_{\alpha 1},L_{\alpha 5}$: Hermitian SO(5) generators built from $\pi$ (Prop. 1156).
- $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$: AF ordering vector.
- $g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y$: d-wave form factor.

title: Microscopic $\pi$ operators carry spin-1, charge $\pm 2$, and momentum $Q$
content: Given the microscopic definition $\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}=\sum_{p}g(p)\,c_{p+Q,\sigma}^{\dagger}(\sigma^{\alpha}\sigma^{y})_{\sigma\sigma'}c_{-p,\sigma'}^{\dagger}$ and $\pi_{\alpha}=(\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger})^{\dagger}$, each $\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$ creates two electrons (two fermion creation operators), so it raises the total electric charge by $+2e$ (we set the electron charge unit to $1$ so charge change $+2$). The spin matrix structure $(\sigma^{\alpha}\sigma^{y})_{\sigma\sigma'}$ gives total spin $S=1$ (spin triplet) for the created pair, and the pair's center-of-mass momentum is $(p+Q)+(-p)=Q$. Therefore the microscopic $\pi$ operators carry spin 1, charge $\pm 2$, and momentum $Q$; their Hermitian combinations $L_{\alpha 1}=-\tfrac{i}{2}(\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}-\pi_{\alpha})$ and $L_{\alpha 5}=\tfrac{1}{2}(\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}+\pi_{\alpha})$ are operators that necessarily connect sectors differing by two units of charge.

extra.notations:
- $\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$: operator defined above that creates a spin-triplet, d-wave pair with center-of-mass momentum $Q$.
- $c_{p\sigma}^{\dagger}$: electron creation operator with momentum $p$ and spin $\sigma$.
- $g(p)=\cos p_x-\cos p_y$: d-wave form factor.
- $Q=(\pi,\pi,\pi)$: antiferromagnetic ordering vector (center-of-mass momentum of the created pair).
- $L_{\alpha 1},L_{\alpha 5}$: SO(5) generators expressed as linear combinations of $\pi_\alpha$ and $\pi_\alpha^\dagger$.

title: Exact microscopic SO(5) symmetry is incompatible with strict particle-number conservation
content: Because the microscopic realization of some SO(5) generators (the $\pi_\alpha$ and their Hermitian combinations $L_{\alpha 1},L_{\alpha 5}$) changes the total charge by $\pm 2$, an exact global SO(5) symmetry implemented by these generators would relate eigenstates of the Hamiltonian with different total particle numbers. Therefore, for a Hamiltonian that strictly conserves particle number (commutes with $\mathcal{Q}$) to also be exactly SO(5)-symmetric under these microscopic generators, it would need to exhibit exact degeneracies between sectors with different charges differing by two. In generic interacting electron systems without such fine-tuned degeneracies, SO(5) implemented in this microscopic way cannot be an exact symmetry and must be either broken or emergent/approximate (for example, realized only in a subspace where charge is not fixed, or only when U(1) is spontaneously broken).

extra.notations:
- $\mathcal{Q}$: total charge operator.
- $\pi_\alpha,\pi_\alpha^\dagger$: pair annihilation/creation operators changing charge by $\mp 2$.
- $L_{ab}$: SO(5) generators, with $L_{15}\equiv\mathcal{Q}$ in the mapping of Proposition 1156.

title: Presence of a collective "π" mode that mixes AF and d-wave SC channels
content: Given that $\pi_{\alpha}^{\dagger}$ creates a spin-1, charge-$+2$, momentum-$Q$ d-wave pair and that these operators serve as SO(5) rotations between antiferromagnetic (AF) and superconducting (SC) order parameters, it is plausible that the interacting electron system supports a collective excitation (the ``π'' mode) with these quantum numbers which couples to both AF fluctuations and d-wave pair fluctuations. Near a point or regime where SO(5) symmetry is approximately realized, this mode would appear as a resonance that mixes spin excitations at momentum $Q$ and pair-field fluctuations, and could be observable (e.g., as a neutron scattering or Raman feature) with energy inside the superconducting gap.

extra.notations:
- "π mode": collective excitation associated with $\pi_\alpha$ operators.
- AF: antiferromagnetic order / spin fluctuations at wavevector $Q$.
- SC: d-wave superconducting order / pair fluctuations with form factor $g(p)$.
